name: Denzel Boston
scouting_report:
Background:
Patience defined Denzel Boston's path at Washington more than anything else. He arrived from Emerald Ridge High School in Puyallup as a three-star prospect ranked the 71st receiver nationally by ESPN, having hauled in 105 receptions for 1,572 yards and 23 touchdowns across four varsity seasons with first-team All-South Puget Sound League honors as a senior. But with Rome Odunze, Jalen McMillan, and Ja'Lynn Polk ahead of him, he appeared in just four games as a true freshman and totaled seven catches over his first two seasons combined. Rather than seeking a transfer, he continued developing behind three future NFL Draft picks. That decision paid dividends when his number was finally called in 2024, and he delivered a 63-catch, 834-yard, nine-touchdown campaign that earned him honorable mention All-Big Ten recognition from both coaches and media. His 2025 season built on that foundation with 62 receptions for 881 yards and 11 touchdowns, securing third-team All-Big Ten honors at receiver while also earning honorable mention as a return specialist. Boston's 11 receiving touchdowns tied for sixth in Washington single-season history, and his 20 career scores rank eighth all-time in program history. He capped his collegiate career with a monster performance in the L.A. Bowl against Boise State, catching six passes for 126 yards and a 78-yard touchdown while also completing a pass. Named Washington's Offensive Skill Player of the Year in back-to-back seasons, Boston leaves Seattle having proven himself after two years of dominant production.

Strengths:
Built like a true X receiver. 6-4 with long arms and he knows how to use every inch of that frame to wall off corners and pluck balls outside his body. Gets up to speed quick and carries it through his routes without any wasted steps. Go watch the deep balls. Head comes around early, tracks it over his shoulder like a centerfielder, adjusts to back-shoulders as good as anyone in this class. 20 touchdowns in two years and most of those came in the red zone. This kid just knows how to score. Wins fades, boxes out on slants, finds the soft spot. Hands are legit. Vice grip when he catches through contact. DBs can get a hand in there and he's still coming down with it nine times out of ten. Real crafty at the catch point on those in-breaking routes. Puts his body between the ball and the defender like he's posting up in the paint. Runs angry after the catch for a lanky guy. Breaks arm tackles, falls forward, fights for every extra yard. There's some dog in him. Willing blocker and that matters. Seals off corners on screens, cracks down on edge players. Does the dirty work without pouting about it.

Weaknesses:
Release package needs work. Too straight-line right now and NFL press corners will eat that up. Gonna need more moves off the line. Not a burner - didnt run at the combine or at his proday. Can't just run by guys on outside releases. Wins with positioning and length more than flat-out speed when the ball goes deep. Separation closes on him at the break point. Gets open initially but that cushion shrinks when he plants because the explosion out of cuts isn't there. Numbers dipped against the better corners on the schedule. Ohio State, Oregon, those games weren't his best. Something to watch. Not making anyone miss after the catch. Gets his yards through contact and falling forward, not with feet. Limits the big play upside.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Defense Faced Rating: 76%
  QB Rating When targeted: 111.2
  Hands: 83%
  Short Receiving: 86%
  Intermediate Routes: 89%
  Deep Threat: 86%
  Blocking: 70%

NFL Player Comparisons: CedricTillman - Tennessee (91%), TetairoaMcMillan - Arizona (82%), TreHarris - Ole Miss (79%)

Denzel Boston, 6-4/209 Wide Receiver Washington

Denzel Boston Scouting Report By Charlie Campbell

Strengths: Mismatch size Sure-handed High points the bal Has suddenness and twitch for a big receiver Tracks the ball well High points the ball well Red zone weapon Scoring threat Reliable hands Late hands to make receptions Natural hands catcher Makes contested catches Concentration Ready to contribute immediately Experienced and successful against good college programs Creates mismatches out of the slot

Weaknesses: Lacks elite speed Could be a more physical runner after the catch

Prospect Summary: In 2022 and 2023, Boston was stuck behind Rome Odunze as he was the primary receiver in the Washington offense. Aside from Odunze, there were other early-round talents like Jalen McMillan and Ja’Lynn Polk ahead of Boston. After that trio moved on to the NFL, Boston got an opportunity and had a breakout 2024 season with 63 catches for 834 yards and nine touchdowns.  In 2025, Boston had 62 catches for 881 yards and 11 touchdowns. In the passing-driven NFL, teams are always on the lookout for mismatch receivers, and Boston brings that type of ability. Boston is a tall receiver with a large wingspan who presents a large catch radius for his quarterback. With his height, length, and leaping ability, Boston is a problem for defenses even when he is blanketed in coverage, as he will make big plays with leaping grabs over defensive backs. Boston is a red zone weapon that looks like an outside receiver to attack defenses. In the condensed part of the field, Boston is a real weapon, and his 20 touchdowns over the past two seasons are not a fluke. He can box out, leap over, and uncover to get open for his quarterback. Boston should be a real asset for his team to produce touchdowns. Aside from his size mismatch, Boston has other strengths that make him a viable prospect for the NFL. He shows some nice suddenness and twitch out of his break for a big receiver.  Boston tracks passes very well downfield and attacks the ball with late hands that make it more difficult for defensive backs to break up receptions. Boston high points the ball well and has an excellent feel to finish the reception. Boston flashed some run-after-the-catch ability in college, but he could stand to be more physical as a runner. Boston also has very good hands and has had very few drops during his first season as a starter. In the 2026 NFL Draft, Boston looks like a first-round pick. The success of similar receivers could help Boston rise in the lead-up to the draft.

Prospect Comparison: Drake London. Boston is similar to other receivers like London and Tet McMillan.

2024 NFL Mock Draft : Charlie's | Walt's 2024 NFL Draft Prospect Rankings 2024 NFL Draft Player Previews
bio: Patience defined Denzel Boston's path at Washington more than anything else. He arrived from Emerald Ridge High School in Puyallup as a three-star prospect ranked the 71st receiver nationally by ESPN, having hauled in 105 receptions for 1,572 yards and 23 touchdowns across four varsity seasons with first-team All-South Puget Sound League honors as a senior. But with Rome Odunze, Jalen McMillan, and Ja'Lynn Polk ahead of him, he appeared in just four games as a true freshman and totaled seven catches over his first two seasons combined. Rather than seeking a transfer, he continued developing behind three future NFL Draft picks. That decision paid dividends when his number was finally called in 2024, and he delivered a 63-catch, 834-yard, nine-touchdown campaign that earned him honorable mention All-Big Ten recognition from both coaches and media. His 2025 season built on that foundation with 62 receptions for 881 yards and 11 touchdowns, securing third-team All-Big Ten honors at receiver while also earning honorable mention as a return specialist. Boston's 11 receiving touchdowns tied for sixth in Washington single-season history, and his 20 career scores rank eighth all-time in program history. He capped his collegiate career with a monster performance in the L.A. Bowl against Boise State, catching six passes for 126 yards and a 78-yard touchdown while also completing a pass. Named Washington's Offensive Skill Player of the Year in back-to-back seasons, Boston leaves Seattle having proven himself after two years of dominant production.
strengths: Built like a true X receiver. 6-4 with long arms and he knows how to use every inch of that frame to wall off corners and pluck balls outside his body. Gets up to speed quick and carries it through his routes without any wasted steps. Go watch the deep balls. Head comes around early, tracks it over his shoulder like a centerfielder, adjusts to back-shoulders as good as anyone in this class. 20 touchdowns in two years and most of those came in the red zone. This kid just knows how to score. Wins fades, boxes out on slants, finds the soft spot. Hands are legit. Vice grip when he catches through contact. DBs can get a hand in there and he's still coming down with it nine times out of ten. Real crafty at the catch point on those in-breaking routes. Puts his body between the ball and the defender like he's posting up in the paint. Runs angry after the catch for a lanky guy. Breaks arm tackles, falls forward, fights for every extra yard. There's some dog in him. Willing blocker and that matters. Seals off corners on screens, cracks down on edge players. Does the dirty work without pouting about it.
weaknesses: Release package needs work. Too straight-line right now and NFL press corners will eat that up. Gonna need more moves off the line. Not a burner - didnt run at the combine or at his proday. Can't just run by guys on outside releases. Wins with positioning and length more than flat-out speed when the ball goes deep. Separation closes on him at the break point. Gets open initially but that cushion shrinks when he plants because the explosion out of cuts isn't there. Numbers dipped against the better corners on the schedule. Ohio State, Oregon, those games weren't his best. Something to watch. Not making anyone miss after the catch. Gets his yards through contact and falling forward, not with feet. Limits the big play upside.
player_comparisons: CedricTillman - Tennessee (91%), TetairoaMcMillan - Arizona (82%), TreHarris - Ole Miss (79%)

name: Avieon Terrell
scouting_report:
Background:
Avieon Terrell grew up in Atlanta, Georgia, where football runs deep in the family bloodline. His older brother A.J. Terrell starred at Clemson before becoming a first-round pick of the Atlanta Falcons in 2020, and Avieon followed that same path from Westlake High School to Death Valley. Standing 5-foot-11 and weighing 180 pounds, Terrell arrived as a consensus four-star recruit with an ESPN rating of 80 and a Rivals grade of 5.9 out of 6.1. Born into a football family, the younger Terrell carried expectations from day one. His freshman campaign in 2023 served as a baptism by fire, appearing in all 13 games with five starts while logging 375 snaps. Terrell recorded 18 tackles, six pass breakups, and snagged his first career interception on a one-handed grab against Georgia Tech. The learning curve was steep, but the tools were obvious. By his sophomore season, everything clicked. He started all 14 games and put together a stat line that announced his arrival: 64 tackles, 13 pass breakups (tying the Clemson sophomore record), two interceptions, three forced fumbles, two fumble recoveries, and a sack across 816 defensive snaps. That 2024 campaign earned him second-team All-ACC honors from multiple outlets. His junior year cemented his status as one of the nation's premier corners. Terrell earned Jim Thorpe Award semifinalist recognition and was named a second-team AP All-American, becoming the first Clemson cornerback to receive AP All-American honors since Cordrea Tankersley in 2016. He finished with 46 tackles, 3.0 sacks (the first Clemson defensive back under Dabo Swinney to record 2.0 sacks in a single game came against Florida State), 11 pass breakups, and five forced fumbles that set a school single-season record for a defensive back. His career total of eight forced fumbles tied him for third in Clemson history overall and broke the program record for defensive backs. First-team All-ACC selections from both the conference and AP followed, and in January 2026, Terrell declared for the NFL Draft.

Strengths:
Zone eyes are the best I've seen in this class. Watch the Syracuse tape where he jumped three different routes before the quarterback even finished his drop. Reads concepts, not just receivers. The Peanut Tillman comparisons are lazy but accurate. Eight forced fumbles at Clemson and it's all technique. He times his punch, rips through the ball, and does it legally. That's a learned skill. Fluid hips let him stay in phase through the entire route. His flip at the top is clean and he doesn't open his gate when receivers try to stack him vertically. Just stays connected. Ball tracking is receiver-quality. He finds the football in the air, adjusts his body, and attacks with both hands. Not waiting for the ball to arrive. Going to get it. Nasty competitor who wants all the smoke. Went at it with every big receiver Clemson faced and never backed down. You can't teach that kind of edge. He's got real dog in him. Scheme versatility is legit. Clemson moved him around in press, off, zone match, pattern match. He handled all of it without looking lost or hesitant. Ready for an NFL playbook. Better run defender than his frame suggests. Louisville game he came off the edge and blew up a screen for a three-yard loss. Willing to get his nose dirty and mix it up. Closing speed is a weapon. The 4.4 shows up when quarterbacks think they have a window. He eats up that cushion in a hurry and arrives with bad intentions at the catch point.

Weaknesses:
At 180 pounds, bigger receivers are going to body him on contested catches. That's just physics. He competes hard but sometimes hard isn't enough against a 6-3 target with long arms. Quick releases across his face give him trouble in soft press. He's a half-beat slow getting his hands on speedsters who want to win inside leverage early in the route. Gets grabby when he feels himself losing in trail position. NFL officials won't let that slide, and savvy route runners will bait him into penalties on double moves. Tackling needs work. Too many arm tackles and ankle swipes on tape. NFL backs will run through those attempts and turn five-yard gains into fifteen. Short zone recognition can be inconsistent. He'll jump one route beautifully then get caught flat-footed on the next when offenses flood his area with traffic.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Offense Rating: 79%
  QB Rating When targeted: 85.7
  Tackling: 75%
  Run Defense: 91%
  Coverage: 80%
  Zone: 75%
  Man/Press: 80%

NFL Player Comparisons: JalenPitre - Baylor (94%), CamSmith - South Carolina (87%), EnnisRakestraw Jr. - Missouri (85%)

Avieon Terrell, 5-11/180 Cornerback Clemson

Avieon Terrell Scouting Report By Charlie Campbell

Strengths: Excellent corner to run the route and prevent separation Superb at forcing fumbles Exceptional instincts Very fast Loose hips Great agility, no stiffness Twitchy Can flip his hips with ease Maintains coverage in and out of breaks Tracks the ball well Good ball adjustment Advanced body control Quality ball skills Times breakups well Competitive, battles receivers Effective fighter on 50-50 passes Advanced route recognition Willing tackler Not afraid to get physical Contributes in run defense Ready to compete immediately Experienced and successful against good college competition

Weaknesses: Small, very undersized Short Light Durability? Limited to slot?

Prospect Summary: Clemson enjoyed a great career from cornerback A.J. Terrell. After impressing with the Tigers, Terrell was a first-round pick of the Atlanta Falcons and has turned into a very good pro. With that pedigree, there were big expectations and excitement for Avieon Terrell to turn into a good player for Clemson. Terrell worked his way onto the field as a freshman and had five starts. He recorded 19 tackles with four passes broken up and an interception. In 2024, Terrell started every game and totaled 58 tackles with two interceptions, 12 passes broken up, three forced fumbles, and 4.5 tackles for a loss. While Clemson had a disappointing 2025 season, Terrell played well with 48 tackles, four forced fumbles, a sack, and nine passes batted. In the passing-driven NFL, teams are always looking for cornerbacks who can play man coverage and shut down pro receivers. Some teams feel that some corners aren’t a good fit for man coverage, and that limits the schemes and teams those players can play for. With tremendous man coverage skills, Terrell looks like a future asset as a man-to-man cover corner in the NFL. Terrell is a superb cornerback to run the route and prevent separation. In off-man coverage or press-man coverage, Terrell is very fast and has easy speed to run with wideouts and carry verticals down the field. Terrell is extremely loose, has zero stiffness, and is a very twitchy cornerback. With tremendous agility, Terrell can flip his hips and turn to stay in phase with receivers. When he is in off-man coverage, he shows impressive instincts to read the route and then explode to cover up the receiver after they make their break. Terrell is not strong enough to reroute NFL receivers in press man, but he has no problem turning and running with them downfield, blanketing them, and not allowing them to get open. Terrell’s excellent speed, twitchy athleticism, and instincts make him an excellent zone corner. He reads plays well and shows good vision for diagnosing routes while also reading the eyes of the quarterback. Terrell’s twitch lets him explode to eat up ground and cover up receivers. He is very natural in zone coverage. The big knock on Terrell for the NFL is his size. While he is a little shorter than ideal, Terrell’s weight is a bigger concern, and he is listed at only 180 pounds. Terrell plays stronger than his listed numbers, and he is a willing tackler. Terrell will fly downhill and into the backfield to make tackles, throwing his shoulder into ball carriers. Terrell also battles receivers hard, and he has very good ball skills. He times his breakups extremely well to smack the ball out. While Terrell is not tall or big, he battles receivers really well on 50-50 passes and has a knack for winning with a pass breakup. Terrell has impressive ball skills and is a threat to pick off passes. He is also very instinctive and ball aware to break out a “Peanut Punch” on ball carriers and strip them of the ball. Given his size, there will be some concerns with durability, tackling at the pro level, and whether he will be limited to the slot. However, Terrell looks like a future starter, and he could be a valuable cover corner.

Prospect Comparison: A.J. Terrell. Avieon Terrell is like a smaller version of his brother A.J..

name: Monroe Freeling
scouting_report:
Background:
The top-ranked prospect in South Carolina's 2023 class, Monroe Freeling came out of Oceanside Collegiate Academy in Mount Pleasant, just outside Charleston. A consensus four-star recruit with five-star grades from On3 and PrepStar, he was rated as high as No. 32 nationally and No. 5 among offensive tackles. His senior season anchored an offense that piled up nearly 3,000 rushing yards as the Landsharks went 12-2 and reached the SCHSL Class 2A title game. He also played 56 career basketball games at Oceanside and was selected for the All-American Bowl before enrolling early at Georgia. Freeling appeared in eight games as a true freshman in 2023 on a Georgia line that led the SEC in fewest sacks allowed. His role grew in 2024 with 14 appearances and five starts down the stretch after Earnest Greene went down. The 2025 season was his breakout: 14 appearances, 13 starts, and Second Team All-SEC honors. He earned SEC Offensive Lineman of the Week twice and played through a high ankle sprain suffered against Kentucky that cost him zero starts.

Strengths:
Rare athlete for the position; combine numbers confirmed what the tape already showed in his movement ability. Pass protection was strong in 2025, allowing minimal pressure across 747 snaps against SEC edge talent. Basketball background shows up in his balance, spatial awareness, and ability to redirect through contact. Quick first step off the snap gives him an advantage reaching landmarks on zone concepts and getting to linebackers. Active hands with quick punch resets; recovers well when his initial strike misses rather than conceding the rep. Finishes with intent when he locks onto a defender and gets his feet moving downhill. Showed tangible week-to-week growth throughout 2025 while managing an ankle injury and full-time starter duties. Length at 6-7 with 34-inch arms gives him a wide strike zone that edge rushers have to work around.

Weaknesses:
Run blocking lags behind his pass protection, especially in gap schemes where leverage matters most. Gets tall through his hips too often and loses power at the point of attack against stout defenders. Needs to add 10 to 15 pounds of functional weight to anchor consistently against NFL power rushers. Tendency to lunge and bend at the waist costs him his length advantage and creates missed assignments. Limited starting experience leaves real questions about handling a full NFL workload from the jump.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Defense Faced Rating: 75%
  Pass Blocking: 89%
  Run Blocking: 69%

NFL Player Comparisons: AbrahamLucas - Washington State (94%), TroyFautanu - Washington (80%), RogerRosengarten - Washington (78%)

Monroe Freeling, 6-7/315 Offensive Tackle Georgia

Monroe Freeling Scouting Report By Charlie Campbell

Strengths: Very athletic More athletic than the average LT in the NFL Quick at the point of attack Fast to the second level Can kickslide and cut off the edge Very agile Flexible, long limbs Good length to sustain blocks Excellent fit on zone blocking plays Athletic upside Frame to get bigger Improved as a pass blocker as he gained experience

Weaknesses: Could stand to add more power for the NFL Inconsistent hand placement Lunges Raw Could stand to add more power One-year starter

Prospect Summary: Freeling was a backup in 2023 and 2024 who rotated into the game at times for Georgia. In 2025, he took over as the starting left tackle for the Bulldogs. As the year progressed, Freeling got better and better for the Bulldogs. For the NFL, Freeling is an athletic edge protector who has a natural skill set to protect the quarterback. What stands out about Freeling the most is his quickness and athleticism. Freeling is light on his feet to swiftly get depth in his drop to neutralize speed rushers around the corner. With his agility and smooth movement, Freeling is adept at cutting off the corner from speed rushers. Freeling tangles up defenders well with his length and blocks through the whistle. Freeling could stand to improve his technique as he will lunge after defenders rather than moving his feet at times. His hand placement is inconsistent, and he could get better at handling moves to the inside. Freeling showed improvement throughout the 2026 season as he gained experience, so the upside is there for him to develop. In the ground game, Freeling is impressive in space. He is very quick and agile to get out to the perimeter for blocks on stretch runs or screens. Freeling is also fast firing to the second level and hitting blocks in space. While Freeling is not overpowering at the point of attack against defensive linemen, he can tie up and scrap defenders to keep them from getting to the ball carrier. Freeling will look to punish and pancake defenders at the second level, as a number of linebackers and defensive backs took some punishment from him downfield. He blocks hard through the whistle and is a real fighter. In the 2026 NFL Draft, Freeling could go as high as the back half of the first round, and should not be in for much of a wait if he slips to round two. Check out in my latest 2026 NFL Mock Draft to see where Freeling is being projected.

Prospect Comparison: Paris Johnson. Freeling is a tough player to comp, but he has some athletic similarities to Johnson.

name: Caleb Lomu
scouting_report:
Background:
Before Caleb Lomu was turning heads at the NFL Combine, he was pancaking defenders in the Arizona high school ranks at Highland High in Gilbert. The kid was a dominant prep tackle who helped his team reel off back-to-back state titles in 2021 and 2022, and the recruiting world noticed. A consensus four-star prospect, 247Sports slotted him as the 21st-best tackle in the country and the third-best player in the state of Arizona. Rivals tagged him with a 5.9 grade, ESPN gave him an 81, and invitations to both the All-American Bowl and the Polynesian Bowl followed. He took home the BJ Media and Randall McDaniel Offensive Lineman of the Year award as a senior while anchoring an offense that churned out over 300 yards a game. Michigan, Florida, Oregon, USC, and Tennessee all came knocking, but Lomu picked Utah. His first year in Salt Lake City was a quiet one. Lomu redshirted in 2023, seeing the field in just three games on special teams and mop-up offensive line duty. That changed in a hurry. By 2024, he owned the starting left tackle spot and never let go, starting all 12 games as a redshirt freshman. He surrendered only two sacks and 17 pressures on 425 pass blocking reps, good enough for a 76.0 PFF pass blocking grade. The Athletic named him a Midseason Freshman All-American, and he stacked CSC Academic All-District and Academic All-Big 12 honors on top of that for good measure. The 2025 season was where Lomu separated himself from the pack. Across another full 12-game slate, he cleaned up his pass protection to the tune of zero sacks allowed and just eight pressures on 357 pass blocking snaps, pushing his PFF pass blocking grade up to 82.1. That earned him First Team All-Big 12 recognition and a spot on both the Outland Trophy and Polynesian College Football Player of the Year watch lists. Utah's offensive line collectively earned Big 12 Offensive Line of the Week honors four times and landed on the Joe Moore Award Midseason Honor Roll. Lomu was the anchor of a unit that put up 587 total yards against Colorado (including 422 on the ground), 532 at West Virginia, and 518 against Cal Poly.

Strengths:
Explodes out of his stance with a first step that belongs on a tight end, not a 313-pound tackle. His initial burst consistently beats defensive ends to the landmark in both pass sets and pull schemes. Hands land early and land hard in pass protection. Once he latches onto a rusher's chest plate, the fight is over. Watch the Colorado tape and count how many reps end the moment he gets a grip. Stays square and upright through the entire pass rush sequence, never dropping his eyes or lunging forward, which robs speed rushers of the false movements they depend on to set up counters. Processes twists, stunts, and delayed blitzers with a calm awareness that belies his limited starting experience. His communication with interior linemen on exchanges looks natural, not coached-up. Kick slide is smooth, controlled, and covers serious ground in a hurry. His Combine confirmed it: 4.99 forty, 32.5-inch vertical, a 9-foot-5 broad jump, all top-10 among offensive linemen at the event. Pull blocking footwork is fluid and decisive. He locates second-level targets on the move with the kind of spatial awareness that unlocks gap and pin-and-pull concepts offensive coordinators covet. Lateral agility gives him a recovery gear that most tackles his size simply do not possess. When he loses a half-step, he gathers and redirects fast enough to turn a lost rep into a stalemate. The frame at 6-6 has visible room for another 15 to 20 pounds of functional weight through the chest, shoulders, and lower half. He is a physical projection play with a real foundation already underneath him.

Weaknesses:
Core power is the glaring issue and it showed up at the Combine. In the mirror drill, he shadowed the coach beautifully but drifted backward the whole time, bracing for contact rather than anchoring against it. Run blocking is a full tier below his pass protection right now. He struggles to generate movement at the point of attack and too often stalls out against defensive ends who sit and anchor. Arm length at 33 3/8 inches and hand size at 9 1/2 inches are both below average for the position. He compensates with timing and technique, but longer NFL edge rushers will test those measurements. Finishing through blocks is inconsistent. He wins the initial punch and the positioning battle, then lets defenders shed and make plays that his talent should have prevented outright. Opens his hips early against speed-to-power conversions, giving away the inside lane. Any NFL rusher with a corner-to-counter move in his bag will attack that tendency until it is coached out of him.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Defense Faced Rating: 77%
  Pass Blocking: 88%
  Run Blocking: 70%

NFL Player Comparisons: AbrahamLucas - Washington State (95%), TroyFautanu - Washington (86%), RogerRosengarten - Washington (84%)

Caleb Lomu, 6-6/304 Offensive Tackle Utah

Caleb Lomu Scouting Report By Charlie Campbell

Strengths: Skilled pass protector Very good against speed rushers Can glide with his feet to cut off the edge Gets depth in his drop to neutralize speed Adept at reacting and stopping moves to the inside Good awareness Reacts quickly to stop stunts and DL games Good athlete Quickness Quick feet Bends at the knee Smooth mover Can stop bull rushes Bends at the knee Fights and blocks physical as a run blocker Has the frame to get bigger, stronger Upside

Weaknesses: Needs to get stronger Not powerful as a run blocker Run blocking needs improvement Could use more weight in his anchor Footwork needs development

Prospect Summary: In his second season at Utah, Lomu took over as the starting left tackle with the Utes moving Spencer Fano to the right side. Over two seasons, Lomu locked down left tackle and showed NFL ability. In the passing-driven NFL, Lomu has good athleticism to be an asset as a pass protector. Lomu is a capable pass blocker to neutralize speed rushers around the corner. Lomu is a natural pass blocker who has quick feet that allow him to get depth in his drop and neutralize speed rushers. He is quick out of stance and reacts immediately to cut off the corner and prevent them from running the loop around the edge. With his quick feet and an ability to bend at the knee, Lomu doesn’t have to reach after rushers and is not a waist bender. With impressive awareness and reaction skills, Lomu reacts quickly to handle stunts and games from the defensive line. He reacts very well to rushes to the inside and shows impressive balance. As a pass blocker, Lomu does a nice job of maintaining his blocks and sustaining using his hands and length well to stay engaged on defenders. Lomu uses his athletic ability well and stays in front of defenders. For the NFL, Lomu could be a very valuable starting left tackle to protect his quarterback against edge rushers. As a run blocker, Lomu is not a bull in the ground game. He is not overly strong or physical to blast defenders off the ball and ride them around the field. Lomu has a good mentality as he looks to hit and get after defenders, but Lomu needs to get stronger to create movement in the ground game at the pro level. While he is not as powerful as a run blocker, he shows the athleticism and mobility to execute zone runs. Utah’s scheme did not have him fire off and run a lot of power man, so that will need development for the NFL. Right now, Lomu would be better off in a zone-blocking system to use his athleticism due to not packing a serious punch at the point of attack. There are some raw elements to Lomu that need development. Lomu can stop bull rushes, but he needs to add more weight and strength to handle the power rushers in the NFL. His footwork can be inconsistent. He needs to get stronger to improve his power in the ground game and his ability to sustain blocks as a run blocker. Lomu has a lot of upside, and his best football is ahead of him. If he gets stronger and develops his technique well, he could be an excellent starting left tackle as a pro. He definitely has a shot at being selected on the opening night of the draft .

Prospect Comparison: Taylor Decker. In terms of style of play, Lomu reminds me of Decker as they are both solid athletes with size and good awareness. Like Decker, Lomu is a good player but not overwhelmingly dominant.

2024 NFL Mock Draft : Charlie's | Walt's 2024 NFL Draft Prospect Rankings 2024 NFL Draft Player Previews
bio: Before Caleb Lomu was turning heads at the NFL Combine, he was pancaking defenders in the Arizona high school ranks at Highland High in Gilbert. The kid was a dominant prep tackle who helped his team reel off back-to-back state titles in 2021 and 2022, and the recruiting world noticed. A consensus four-star prospect, 247Sports slotted him as the 21st-best tackle in the country and the third-best player in the state of Arizona. Rivals tagged him with a 5.9 grade, ESPN gave him an 81, and invitations to both the All-American Bowl and the Polynesian Bowl followed. He took home the BJ Media and Randall McDaniel Offensive Lineman of the Year award as a senior while anchoring an offense that churned out over 300 yards a game. Michigan, Florida, Oregon, USC, and Tennessee all came knocking, but Lomu picked Utah. His first year in Salt Lake City was a quiet one. Lomu redshirted in 2023, seeing the field in just three games on special teams and mop-up offensive line duty. That changed in a hurry. By 2024, he owned the starting left tackle spot and never let go, starting all 12 games as a redshirt freshman. He surrendered only two sacks and 17 pressures on 425 pass blocking reps, good enough for a 76.0 PFF pass blocking grade. The Athletic named him a Midseason Freshman All-American, and he stacked CSC Academic All-District and Academic All-Big 12 honors on top of that for good measure. The 2025 season was where Lomu separated himself from the pack. Across another full 12-game slate, he cleaned up his pass protection to the tune of zero sacks allowed and just eight pressures on 357 pass blocking snaps, pushing his PFF pass blocking grade up to 82.1. That earned him First Team All-Big 12 recognition and a spot on both the Outland Trophy and Polynesian College Football Player of the Year watch lists. Utah's offensive line collectively earned Big 12 Offensive Line of the Week honors four times and landed on the Joe Moore Award Midseason Honor Roll. Lomu was the anchor of a unit that put up 587 total yards against Colorado (including 422 on the ground), 532 at West Virginia, and 518 against Cal Poly.
strengths: Explodes out of his stance with a first step that belongs on a tight end, not a 313-pound tackle. His initial burst consistently beats defensive ends to the landmark in both pass sets and pull schemes. Hands land early and land hard in pass protection. Once he latches onto a rusher's chest plate, the fight is over. Watch the Colorado tape and count how many reps end the moment he gets a grip. Stays square and upright through the entire pass rush sequence, never dropping his eyes or lunging forward, which robs speed rushers of the false movements they depend on to set up counters. Processes twists, stunts, and delayed blitzers with a calm awareness that belies his limited starting experience. His communication with interior linemen on exchanges looks natural, not coached-up. Kick slide is smooth, controlled, and covers serious ground in a hurry. His Combine confirmed it: 4.99 forty, 32.5-inch vertical, a 9-foot-5 broad jump, all top-10 among offensive linemen at the event. Pull blocking footwork is fluid and decisive. He locates second-level targets on the move with the kind of spatial awareness that unlocks gap and pin-and-pull concepts offensive coordinators covet. Lateral agility gives him a recovery gear that most tackles his size simply do not possess. When he loses a half-step, he gathers and redirects fast enough to turn a lost rep into a stalemate. The frame at 6-6 has visible room for another 15 to 20 pounds of functional weight through the chest, shoulders, and lower half. He is a physical projection play with a real foundation already underneath him.
weaknesses: Core power is the glaring issue and it showed up at the Combine. In the mirror drill, he shadowed the coach beautifully but drifted backward the whole time, bracing for contact rather than anchoring against it. Run blocking is a full tier below his pass protection right now. He struggles to generate movement at the point of attack and too often stalls out against defensive ends who sit and anchor. Arm length at 33 3/8 inches and hand size at 9 1/2 inches are both below average for the position. He compensates with timing and technique, but longer NFL edge rushers will test those measurements. Finishing through blocks is inconsistent. He wins the initial punch and the positioning battle, then lets defenders shed and make plays that his talent should have prevented outright. Opens his hips early against speed-to-power conversions, giving away the inside lane. Any NFL rusher with a corner-to-counter move in his bag will attack that tendency until it is coached out of him.
player_comparisons: AbrahamLucas - Washington State (95%), TroyFautanu - Washington (86%), RogerRosengarten - Washington (84%)

name: Max Iheanschor
scouting_report:
Max Iheanachor, 6-5/325 Offensive Tackle Arizona State

Max Iheanachor Scouting Report By Charlie Campbell

Strengths: Massive blocker Good skill set Has size, quickness, athleticism Heavy hands Thick build Strong at the point of attack Can generate a push in the ground game Can knock defenders back and reset the line Good in short-yardage Physical and nasty, looks to bury defenders Blocks with a mean streak Good athlete Solid quickness for a big blocker Can get depth in his drop at times Strong anchor Assending player, getting better with experience Upside to be a good starting right tackle

Weaknesses: Inconsistent Needs development Smaller speed rushers give him problems Will set up too high in pass pro at times

Prospect Summary: In 2023, Iheanachor saw some starting action after injuries pushed him onto the field. In 2024 and 2025, Iheanachor was the starting right tackle for the Sun Devils. He showed improvement in his final season in 2025, and then ended the year with an excellent week of practice at the Senior Bowl. As a run blocker, Iheanachor gets after defenders with power at the point of attack. With serious strength and physicality, Iheanachor will knock them back and reset the line of scrimmage with brute physicality. He has heavy hands, and defenders are in trouble when he latches onto them. Iheanachor can flat-out knock defenders off the ball and open up gaps for his back. He can generate movement in the ground game to supply some excellent lanes for his backs. Aside from being able to play power man, Iheanachor is quick to the second level and packs a punch when he gets there. Iheanachor is a nasty run blocker that will be an asset in the ground game at the pro level. As a pass blocker, Iheanachor has talent, but he needs development for the NFL. Despite being such a large offensive lineman, he has quickness and movement ability to match up against speed rushers. His strength and length allow him to sustain blocks well to keep edge defenders from turning the corner. With his weight and strength, Iheanachor is able to anchor and hold up against bull rushes. However, Iheanachor has problems with speed rushers around the corner. They can get a step and leverage on him to beat him to the quarterback. Smaller rushers that can bend and get underneath him give him problems as well. Iheanachor plays too high in pass pro, and that leads to the issues. Iheanachor has problems recovering once beaten. His footwork and knee bend are not consistent, and that leads to him giving up some pressures. Thus, Iheanachor is a work in progress as a pass protector for the NFL. But the skill set is there for him to be developed into a starting right tackle. In the 2026 NFL Draft, Iheanachor could go in the second round, but he has a shot to sneak into the late first round. Check out my latest 2026 NFL Mock Draft to see where we project Iheanachor.

Prospect Comparison: Jawaan Taylor. In terms of style of play and skill set, Iheanachor reminds me of Taylor. Both are talented blockers with good skill sets, but are inconsistent.

2024 NFL Mock Draft : Charlie's | Walt's 2024 NFL Draft Prospect Rankings 2024 NFL Draft Player Previews

name: C.J. Allen
scouting_report:
Background:
Christian Jamar Allen grew up in Barnesville, Georgia, a small town about two hours south of Athens, where he became a local legend at Lamar County High School. Born in March 2004, Allen was a true two-way force for the Trojans, lining up at both linebacker and running back while also competing in basketball and track. His senior season saw him pile up 84 tackles on defense while gashing opponents for over 1,600 rushing yards and 26 touchdowns on the other side of the ball. That versatility earned him 2021 Region 3-AA Player of the Year honors, an invitation to the All-American Bowl, and a consensus four-star rating from every major recruiting service. Allen enrolled early at Georgia in January 2023 and immediately worked his way into the rotation as a true freshman. Injuries opened the door, and when he got his first start against Ole Miss, he responded with nine tackles and a sack to claim SEC Freshman of the Week. He finished that season with 41 stops across 14 games, including five starts, with his performance in the Orange Bowl win over Florida State (six tackles, 1.5 for loss) showing he belonged on the big stage. By year two, he had locked down a starting spot, appearing in all 14 contests and racking up 76 tackles, an interception against Florida, and a career-high 10 stops in that wild eight-overtime finish against Georgia Tech. The 2025 campaign represented Allen's coronation as the heartbeat of Georgia's defense. Standing 6-foot-1 and 235 pounds, he led the Bulldogs in tackles (88), tackles for loss (8.0), and sacks (3.5) while forcing two fumbles that swung games. His 11-tackle effort keyed the overtime win at Tennessee, and he came up with crucial forced fumbles against both Kentucky and Auburn. The accolades piled up accordingly: Butkus Award finalist, Lott IMPACT Trophy finalist, SEC Coaches All-SEC First Team, and a spot on watch lists for the Bednarik Award. Allen also served on both the SEC Student-Athlete Leadership Council and Football Leadership Council, cementing his reputation as much more than just a playmaker.

Strengths:
Put the tape on against any SEC running game and you will see Allen flying downhill with rare decisiveness, filling gaps before blocks can develop and meeting backs in the hole. Wraps up and finishes with textbook technique every single time; his missed tackle rate across three seasons is remarkably low, and he brings real thump at contact. Takes on pulling guards and lead blockers with leverage and proper hand placement, holding his ground at the point of attack rather than getting washed out of his gap. Covers ground sideline to sideline with that 4.55 speed, which shows up when he chases down stretch runs or rallies from the backside to make stops. Reads his keys quickly and trusts his instincts, triggering downhill without hesitation once he diagnoses the play, which allows him to play at full speed. Shows some dog in him as a blitzer, timing up his rushes through the A and B gaps with good burst off the snap and a knack for finding creases. Natural communicator who gets teammates aligned pre-snap and handles the green dot responsibilities with the poise of a ten-year veteran. Ball awareness in zone coverage has improved noticeably; he keeps his eyes on the quarterback while feeling routes around him and closes quickly on throws.

Weaknesses:
Hip stiffness limits his ability to flip and run with athletic tight ends or backs on angle routes, creating separation that better athletes will exploit. Gets caught flat-footed against quality play-action, taking a half-step forward before recognizing the pass and leaving soft spots in the middle of the field. Lacks the pop and physicality to consistently stack and shed bigger blockers; offensive linemen can absorb his contact and ride him out of the play. Man coverage assignments against NFL-caliber receiving threats will be a test; his footwork in transition needs refinement to stick with route breaks. Not a high-value playmaker despite the production; does not create turnovers, explosive hits, or splash plays at a rate that matches his overall production.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Offense Rating: 79%
  Tackling: 91%
  Pass Rush: 74%
  Run Defense: 97%
  Coverage: 58%

NFL Player Comparisons: BarrettCarter - Clemson (78%), KobeKing - Penn State (73%), DaiyanHenley - Washington State (73%)

C.J. Allen,                     LB, Georgia.

name: Jadarian Price
scouting_report:
Background:
After tearing his Achilles during summer workouts as a freshman in 2022, an injury that robbed him of his entire rookie campaign, Jadarian Price has methodically worked his way from special teams contributor to dynamic offensive weapon. The four-star recruit out of Denison High School arrived at Notre Dame with serious credentials, having rushed for 4,990 yards and 55 touchdowns while adding 64 receptions during his prep career, where he twice earned District 7 5A D2 Offensive Player of the Year honors. His collegiate career truly began in earnest during the 2023 season, where he carved out a role behind Audric Estime while the coaching staff carefully managed his workload coming off the devastating injury. That patience paid dividends when Price exploded for 106 yards on 13 carries in the Sun Bowl against Oregon State, his first 100-yard performance and a glimpse of what was to come. The breakthrough continued into his sophomore campaign in 2024, where he accumulated 746 rushing yards and seven touchdowns while averaging an impressive 6.2 yards per carry, including that memorable 111-yard outburst against USC that announced his arrival as a legitimate feature back. Through the first five games of his junior season in 2025, the Denison native kept the momentum rolling, rushing for 360 yards and eight touchdowns while adding another two scores through the air. His four-touchdown demolition of Purdue, three on the ground plus a 100-yard kickoff return, showcased the complete skill set that has NFL scouts circling his name on their boards. Price has earned a spot on the Early Campbell Tyler Rose Watch List and continues to form one half of what many consider college football's most dangerous backfield tandem alongside Jeremiyah Love. Worth noting: Price played behind Love in Notre Dame's rotation and was lightly used across those two seasons, averaging just 8.3 carries and 0.4 catches per game, leaving evaluators with a smaller film sample than most top prospects at the position.

Strengths:
Runs with nasty physicality through contact, consistently churning through arm tackles and falling forward for extra yardage Shows exceptional patience setting up blocks before exploding through creases with sudden acceleration that catches linebackers flat-footed Displays elite contact balance and leg drive, averaging well over four yards after contact per attempt in meaningful action Carries legit second-level speed that shows up on tape, though his 4.49 forty suggest good-not-great straight-line juice rather than top-end burners Natural hands out of the backfield with smooth route-running ability to split out wide and threaten vertically Demonstrates outstanding vision for cutback lanes, often creating opportunities where none initially exist through instinctive feel Shows special teams value as a dangerous kickoff returner with track speed and field vision to house it Big-play ability is real and not just noise: nearly one in five of his career carries went for 15-plus yards, the second-best rate among this year's top running back prospects

Weaknesses:
Measured just 203 pounds at the Combine, dropping seven pounds from his listed weight and landing in the 18th percentile among Combine backs since 1999 Pass protection grades hover around average, and he needs refinement in technique and recognition to earn consistent third-down snaps Limited sample size as a feature back remains the biggest question mark, with only 8.3 carries per game across two seasons of real action Occasionally dances behind the line rather than hitting designated holes when his initial read gets muddy Production context raises fair questions: he averaged a middling +0.8 yards per carry over expected in 2024, suggesting his splash plays may owe something to Notre Dame's scheme and blocking Durability concerns linger after the Achilles tear, and the lighter frame only adds another layer to the workload worry

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Defense Faced Rating: 76%
  Rushing: 81%
  Break Tackles: 89%
  Receiving/Hands: 63%
  Pass Blocking: 37%
  Run Blocking: 57%

NFL Player Comparisons: ZachEvans - Ole Miss (89%), KendreMiller - TCU (76%), BhayshulTuten - Virginia Tech (69%)

Jadarian Price, 5-11/203 Running Back Notre Dame

Jadarian Price Scouting Report By Charlie Campbell

Strengths: Acceleration Quick first step to hit the hole Second gear in the open field Can rip off long gains on any carry Instinctive runner Excellent vision Anticipation Compact build Strength to run through tackles Picks up yards after contact Elusive, has moves in the open field Cutting ability Quick feet Can run in short-yardage situations Receiving skills Solid route-runner Soft hands Excellent body lean to run low Runs behind pads Bends at the knee Keeps feet going after contact Decisive runner Three down starter ability Special teams kick return ability Enters NFL fresh, little mileage, small college workload Ready to compete quickly

Weaknesses: Not overly big Not overly fast Straight line runner

Prospect Summary: At most schools, Price would have been a feature back who produced a big stat line during his collegiate career. However, at Notre Dame, Price was stuck behind a superstar back in Jeremiyah Love, yet Price showed that he is a future NFL starter in his own right.  In 2024, Price averaged 6.2 yards per carry for 746 yards with seven touchdowns. In 2025, Price was very good as the No. 2 back behind Love. Price averaged six yards per carry for 674 yards with 11 touchdowns. He had six catches for 87 yards and a touchdown. He has also been a dynamic kick returner with two returns for touchdowns. There is no doubt that Price has the ability to be an NFL running back with a skill set to be in contention as a three-down starter early in his pro career. The first trait that generates a lot of talk about Price is his acceleration, as he has a quick first step to hit the hole and a second gear to burst to the second level of the defense. In the open field, Price can rip off yards in chunks as a threat to turn ordinary carries into big gains. Price is a one-cut, downhill, straight-line runner with a knack for ripping off long gains and scoring sprints. Aside from his quickness, Price is a natural runner with instincts. He has excellent vision and anticipation to follow his line before bursting downhill as he quickly anticipates where a hole is about to open. On top of his feel for running with the ball, he has a compact build that provides him with some power to run through tackles and pick up yards after contact. Price has plus balance to maintain his feet after getting hit by defenders, and he uses his stature to run through would-be tacklers. In the open field, Price has some moves, quick feet, and elusiveness. His strength and build make him a quality short-yardage runner as well. Price did not get a lot of opportunities, but he functioned well as an outlet receiver and blocker in the passing game in limited chances. For the NFL, Price could use some development in the passing game in protection, as well as contributing as a receiver. In the 2026 NFL Draft, Price is likely to go in the second round. Check out my latest 2026 NFL Mock Draft to see where Price is currently being projected.

Prospect Comparison: J.K. Dobbins. In terms of style of play, Price reminds me of Dobbins. Some team sources said that Price reminded them of a taller version of Clyde Edwards-Helaire.

name: Cashius Howell
scouting_report:
Background:
Before Cashius Howell was rattling SEC quarterbacks, he was a lightly recruited kid out of Rockhurst High School in Kansas City trying to convince someone to take a chance on him. He had 21 career sacks and over 150 tackles at Rockhurst, including a 14-sack junior season that shattered school records. None of it mattered much to the Power Five crowd. ESPN tagged him at 73, 247Sports had him at 0.80, and Rivals gave him two stars. His offer sheet read like a Group of Five travel brochure: Bowling Green, Arkansas State, Wyoming. He picked the Falcons and got to work. Bowling Green turned out to be the perfect incubator. After redshirting and easing in during 2022, Howell went off as a redshirt sophomore in 2023, racking up 9.5 sacks to tie for the MAC lead and rank sixth nationally among linebackers. The strip-sack he put on Ohio's Kurtis Rourke was the kind of play that travels, and it traveled all the way to College Station. Mike Elko's staff at Texas A&M came calling, and Howell made the leap to the SEC. His first year in maroon was a supporting role behind Nic Scourton and Shemar Stewart, but he still put up 40 tackles, four sacks, a pick, and seven passes defended while learning the hybrid "Jack" spot. Nobody was sleeping on him anymore. The 2025 breakout was as emphatic as it gets. Howell posted 31 tackles, 14 tackles for loss, 11.5 sacks, and 41 quarterback pressures on his way to unanimous All-American honors, only the 11th Aggie ever to earn that distinction. The SEC named him its Defensive Player of the Year, and he was a finalist for the Ted Hendricks, Lombardi, Bednarik, and Nagurski awards. Three consecutive sacks against Utah State opened the season with a statement, and a six-game sack streak through the heart of the SEC schedule removed any doubt. The kid who couldn't get a Power Five offer rewrote Texas A&M's record books. Then came Indianapolis, and the tape measure introduced a whole new conversation.

Strengths:
First step off the snap is borderline violent, regularly beating tackles out of their stances and turning the corner before protection can get set. Natural ankle flexibility and hip bend allow him to flatten around the arc like a man half his size, keeping his shoulder pad low and threatening the quarterback's lap. Inside spin counter has become a true weapon, catching tackles who overcommit outside and giving him a reliable plan B on any rush. Intelligent rush spacing keeps him outside the tackle's strike zone, forcing linemen to lunge and punch air rather than locking on. Long hop on vertical rushes creates stacking opportunities that allow him to control the rep and work back toward the pocket with leverage. Quick trigger into the B-gap when he reads slow-developing plays collapses the pocket from the inside and blows up play timing. Backside pursuit and change-of-direction burst are well above average, allowing him to chase down plays that most edge rushers give up on. Gets his hands up at the line when the rush stalls, batting down passes and disrupting throwing windows even on plays where he does not get home.

Weaknesses:
The 30 1/4-inch arms measured at the Combine are historically short for the position, ranking third shortest in combine history for edge defenders. That limited reach compounds every hand-fighting issue: NFL tackles with length will be able to lock onto his chest plate and steer him wherever they want. At 253 pounds with a compact frame and short wingspan (74 1/4 inches), he gets knocked around at the point of attack by power schemes and double teams. Turns his shoulders perpendicular to the line of scrimmage when setting the edge, giving blockers the angle to wash him down inside on perimeter runs. Pass rush currently lacks tempo changes and setup work; too often he fires his best move immediately without disguising intent or setting up counters. Drops his eyes and ducks his helmet into first contact against the run, losing sight of the ball carrier and getting sealed off far too easily at this level.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Offense Rating: 79%
  Tackling: 84%
  Pass Rush: 99%
  Run Defense: 80%

NFL Player Comparisons: BJOjulari - LSU (97%), DonovanEzeiruaku - Boston College (94%), ArnoldEbiketie - Penn State (94%)

Cashius Howell, 6-3/253 Defensive End Texas A&M

Cashius Howell Scouting Report By Charlie Campbell

Strengths: Fast Extremely athletic Explosive Dangerous edge pass rusher Speed to turn the corner Very bendy Excellent agility Can sink his hips and dip low under blockers Closes in a hurry Excellent pursuit skills Run and chase defender Natural in space Makes big plays in the backfield Natural quarterback hunter Plays hard Excellent motor, outstanding effort

Weaknesses: Undersized Lacks height, length, weight for a NFL edge defender Weak in run defense Teams will run at him Could be limited to being a designated pass rusher only

Prospect Summary: Howell started out his career at Bowling Green, playing three seasons and totaling 11.5 sacks, 13 tackles for a loss, and 56 tackles in that time. Howell transferred to Texas A&M in 2024 and started out his career with the Aggies as a backup. In 2025, Howell had a breakout season to close out his collegiate career as he totaled 11.5 sacks, one forced fumble, 31 tackles, 14 tackles for a loss, and six passes batted. There is no doubt Howell is a dangerous pass rusher off the edge. He is fast with a quick first step to get upfield and the ability to accelerate around the corner. To go along with his being fast, Howell is very bendy with no hint of stiffness. He can get low to duck underneath tackles, as Howell is very athletic, showing the skills to sink his hips and redirect. With his speed and fluidity, offensive tackles are in trouble when there is space for Howell to work with, as he is adept at dodging blockers. As a pro, Howell could be a dangerous edge rusher and quarterback hunter with the sheer speed to blow by tackles around the corner. While Howell is an effective rusher, he has problems as a run defender for the NFL. Howell is undersized as he lacks the height and weight to be a pro edge defender. In the ground game, Howell can get covered up and pushed around. He is also not very physical as a defender and needs to get stronger to take on pro offensive linemen. In the NFL, Howell will probably start out as a DPR (designated pass rusher). To become a three-down starter, Howell is going to have to get much tougher defending the run. In obvious passing situations, Howell could be a nice contributing edge rusher to get pressure on the quarterback. In the 2026 NFL Draft, Howell looks like a late first-round or early second-round pick. Check out my latest 2026 NFL Mock Draft to see where Howell is projected.

Prospect Comparison: Dee Ford. Howell reminds me of Ford coming out of Auburn. Ford had a couple of double-digit sack seasons for the Chiefs after being a first-round pick. If Howell pans out in the NFL, he could be an edge rusher similar to Ford.

2024 NFL Mock Draft : Charlie's | Walt's 2024 NFL Draft Prospect Rankings 2024 NFL Draft Player Previews
bio: Before Cashius Howell was rattling SEC quarterbacks, he was a lightly recruited kid out of Rockhurst High School in Kansas City trying to convince someone to take a chance on him. He had 21 career sacks and over 150 tackles at Rockhurst, including a 14-sack junior season that shattered school records. None of it mattered much to the Power Five crowd. ESPN tagged him at 73, 247Sports had him at 0.80, and Rivals gave him two stars. His offer sheet read like a Group of Five travel brochure: Bowling Green, Arkansas State, Wyoming. He picked the Falcons and got to work. Bowling Green turned out to be the perfect incubator. After redshirting and easing in during 2022, Howell went off as a redshirt sophomore in 2023, racking up 9.5 sacks to tie for the MAC lead and rank sixth nationally among linebackers. The strip-sack he put on Ohio's Kurtis Rourke was the kind of play that travels, and it traveled all the way to College Station. Mike Elko's staff at Texas A&M came calling, and Howell made the leap to the SEC. His first year in maroon was a supporting role behind Nic Scourton and Shemar Stewart, but he still put up 40 tackles, four sacks, a pick, and seven passes defended while learning the hybrid "Jack" spot. Nobody was sleeping on him anymore. The 2025 breakout was as emphatic as it gets. Howell posted 31 tackles, 14 tackles for loss, 11.5 sacks, and 41 quarterback pressures on his way to unanimous All-American honors, only the 11th Aggie ever to earn that distinction. The SEC named him its Defensive Player of the Year, and he was a finalist for the Ted Hendricks, Lombardi, Bednarik, and Nagurski awards. Three consecutive sacks against Utah State opened the season with a statement, and a six-game sack streak through the heart of the SEC schedule removed any doubt. The kid who couldn't get a Power Five offer rewrote Texas A&M's record books. Then came Indianapolis, and the tape measure introduced a whole new conversation.
strengths: First step off the snap is borderline violent, regularly beating tackles out of their stances and turning the corner before protection can get set. Natural ankle flexibility and hip bend allow him to flatten around the arc like a man half his size, keeping his shoulder pad low and threatening the quarterback's lap. Inside spin counter has become a true weapon, catching tackles who overcommit outside and giving him a reliable plan B on any rush. Intelligent rush spacing keeps him outside the tackle's strike zone, forcing linemen to lunge and punch air rather than locking on. Long hop on vertical rushes creates stacking opportunities that allow him to control the rep and work back toward the pocket with leverage. Quick trigger into the B-gap when he reads slow-developing plays collapses the pocket from the inside and blows up play timing. Backside pursuit and change-of-direction burst are well above average, allowing him to chase down plays that most edge rushers give up on. Gets his hands up at the line when the rush stalls, batting down passes and disrupting throwing windows even on plays where he does not get home.
weaknesses: The 30 1/4-inch arms measured at the Combine are historically short for the position, ranking third shortest in combine history for edge defenders. That limited reach compounds every hand-fighting issue: NFL tackles with length will be able to lock onto his chest plate and steer him wherever they want. At 253 pounds with a compact frame and short wingspan (74 1/4 inches), he gets knocked around at the point of attack by power schemes and double teams. Turns his shoulders perpendicular to the line of scrimmage when setting the edge, giving blockers the angle to wash him down inside on perimeter runs. Pass rush currently lacks tempo changes and setup work; too often he fires his best move immediately without disguising intent or setting up counters. Drops his eyes and ducks his helmet into first contact against the run, losing sight of the ball carrier and getting sealed off far too easily at this level.
player_comparisons: BJOjulari - LSU (97%), DonovanEzeiruaku - Boston College (94%), ArnoldEbiketie - Penn State (94%)

name: R. Mason Thomas
scouting_report:
R. Mason Thomas, 6-2/249 Edge Rusher Oklahoma

R. Mason Thomas Scouting Report By Charlie Campbell

Strengths: Fast speed rusher Burst off ball Speed to run the arc and get around the corner Speed to power rush ability Strong rip move Impressive set of pass rushing moves Can fire to the inside Has a burst to close on the quarterback Ability to use hands and feet at the same time Gives a second effort Agile, not stiff Easy bending ability Can dip underneath tackles Loose hips Nice ability to redirect Good instincts Advanced rush plan Plays hard Can be a scary presence as a rusher Ready to compete immediately Experienced and successful against quality competition

Weaknesses: Undersized Lacks weight Lacks strength Lacks length Limited as a run defender Could struggle to be an every-down edge defender Can get pushed around in the ground game

Prospect Summary: In his third season, Thomas had a breakout season and was one of the most dangerous pass rushers in the SEC. In 2024, he totaled nine sacks with one forced fumble, one pass batted, and 23 tackles.  In 2025, Thomas had 26 tackles with 6.5 sacks, a forced fumble, and a pass batted while missing some time with injuries. As a pass rusher, Thomas is a nightmare for offensive tackles, and he has some serious rush ability. Thomas is lightning fast around the corner with an explosive burst off the ball. Thomas has the speed to blow by tackles, but also has a developed set of moves with some functional strength to show speed to power and use a rip move to get leverage. After his quick first step to get upfield, Thomas uses his hands and feet at the same time nicely with agility to redirect to the inside or sink his hips while running the loop on the outside. Thomas has a dangerous bending ability, and tall tackles can really struggle with him. He has a dangerous speed cut to the inside and a lightning spin move to fire past tackles. Thomas has excellent closing speed to eat up space in a blur. As a pro, Thomas has the potential to be a dangerous double-digit sack producer. At his size, Thomas has limitations as a run defender, as he can get covered up and pushed around sometimes by offensive tackles. NFL offensive tackles are going to outweigh him by 50-80 pounds, so Thomas could have problems holding up as a three-down edge defender going against pro tackles. That is not surprising considering his lack of height, length, and weight. As a pro, he could get rotated out a fair amount in running situations, and he might end up being a designated pass rusher only. In the 2026 NFL Draft, Thomas could be a late first-round or early second-round pick.

Prospect Comparison: Harold Landry. With his skill set, size, and natural rush ability, Thomas reminds me of Landry. Also like Landry, Thomas has limitations as a run defender because of his size.

name: Jake Slaughter
scouting_report:
Background:
At Trinity Catholic High School in Ocala, Florida, Jake Slaughter earned All-County first-team honors as a senior and helped pave the way for a 2,200-yard rusher. He had been snapping footballs since age six growing up in Sparr, a rural pocket of Marion County. He initially committed to Florida State before flipping to Florida and arrived in Gainesville as a three-star recruit in 2021. He redshirted his first year and spent 2022 on special teams. His breakthrough came in 2023 when he started eight of 12 games at center and locked down the job for the rest of his college career. In 2024, he started all 13 games, led the offensive line with 800 snaps, and allowed just one sack. He earned AP and PFF All-American First Team honors along with AP All-SEC First Team recognition. Slaughter returned for 2025 as a team captain and started all 12 games against one of the toughest schedules in the country. He allowed one sack and one quarterback hit across 422 pass protection reps, earning Rimington Trophy Finalist honors and Second-Team All-American nods from The Athletic and USA Today. He graduated with a degree in Agricultural Education and Communication and was a three-time SEC Academic Honor Roll selection.

Strengths:
Pass protection anchor is outstanding, with serious core strength and clean hip sink against power. Processes the game quickly, identifying stunts pre-snap and adjusting protections without hesitation. Generates real push in the run game when he catches defenders clean with hands inside. Finishes blocks with intent and climbs to the second level with good effort and urgency. Pulls cleaner than expected for a 305-pound center, staying balanced through moving targets. Plays with consistent knee bend and low pad level, understanding leverage throughout the rep. Started every game over two full seasons and never missed time against physical SEC fronts. Two-time captain who handled all line calls and set the standard through preparation and effort.

Weaknesses:
Leans on his hands against quick counters instead of chopping feet to stay square. Lets defenders disengage late in the play when the quarterback extends beyond the design. Struggles reaching quick three-techniques on stretch plays when they shoot gaps hard. Gets too aggressive on stunts and lunges rather than trusting his footwork to stay patient. Could use more mass in his lower half to hold up better against pure power on bull rushes.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Defense Faced Rating: 76%
  Pass Blocking: 93%
  Run Blocking: 88%

NFL Player Comparisons: LukeWypler - Ohio State (91%), SedrickVan Pran - Georgia (84%), ZachTom - Wake Forest (80%)

name: L.T. Overton
scouting_report:
Background:
LT Overton arrived in College Station as one of the most decorated recruits in Texas A&M history, part of the Aggies' 2022 class that featured eight five-star prospects. The Georgia native earned unanimous five-star status from every major service, ranking as the No. 4 defensive lineman and No. 17 overall player nationally. He reclassified to graduate a year early and was a 2022 Under Armour All-American selection. His freshman year at Texas A&M produced 14 total pressures across 323 snaps but no sacks. His sophomore season cratered to just 220 snaps and three pressures, prompting his transfer to Alabama. The move paid off in 2024: Overton logged 503 snaps and generated 39 pressures with three sacks and 32 hurries. His performance against Wisconsin, six tackles, a sack, a forced fumble, and a pass breakup, earned defensive player of the week honors. His 2025 season showed some regression as pressure totals dropped to 24 despite similar snap counts, though he did record five sacks. The inconsistency between the breakout junior year and the senior campaign is a fair question heading into the draft process.

Strengths:
The 2024 tape is convincing: 39 pressures and 32 hurries across 503 snaps shows real pass rush ability. Length and leverage work together at the point of attack to extend blockers and create separation. Versatile alignment history across Alabama's front; took snaps at multiple spots without looking lost. Motor runs hot for four quarters, chasing plays downfield and wearing on tackles with consistent effort. Flashes real bend around the edge when he drops his pad level, surprising for a player pushing 280 pounds. Hurry production means he affects quarterbacks even when sacks do not land, disrupting timing underneath. Can slide inside on passing downs where his functional strength collapses guards who cannot handle his power. Showed up against Wisconsin and Michigan, handling quality SEC offensive tackles in high-leverage moments.

Weaknesses:
Pressure production fell off in 2025, dropping from 39 to 24 despite similar snap counts. Hand accuracy is inconsistent; he misses punch points and lets tackles get into his chest too often. Get-off is late too frequently, reacting instead of attacking and giving tackles time to set their feet. Tackling regressed in 2024 with a 20% miss rate compared to 5.6% the year before. Pad level stays high when rushed inside, allowing guards to get underneath and neutralize his size advantage.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Offense Rating: 80%
  Tackling: 64%
  Pass Rush: 73%
  Run Defense: 75%

NFL Player Comparisons: MarshawnKneeland - Western Michigan (86%), PatrickJones II - Pittsburgh (83%), AdisaIsaac - Penn State (78%)

L.T. Overton, 6-5/280 Defensive Tackle Alabama

L.T. Overton Prospect Preview By Charlie Campbell

Career Recap: Texas A&M produced some excellent recruiting classes in the final years of Jimbo Fisher and Overton was one of those top signees. With the Aggies, he had 31 tackles in 2022 and 17 stops in 2023 while rotating with some other future pro linemen like Walter Nolen.  In 2024, Overton had a breakout season after transferring to Alabama. He was a very active defender at the point of attack for the Crimson Tide as he totaled 42 tackles with two sacks, one forced fumble, and one pass broken up.

2025 Season Outlook: The best games to watch Overton include Georgia (Sep. 27), Tennessee (Oct. 18), South Carolina (Oct. 25), LSU (Nov. 8), Oklahoma (Nov. 15), and Auburn (Nov. 29). In the season finale in the Iron Bowl, Overton could take on some early-round picks in Auburn left tackle Xavier Chaplin and center Conner Lew.

Prospect Summary: The NFL is always looking for athletic, strong, and quick defensive linemen with versatility, and Overton definitely fits that description. In 2024, Alabama lined Overton up at defensive tackle and end, playing a variety of techniques along the defensive line. While Overton is versatile, as a pro, he would fit best as a defensive tackle in the passing-driven pro league. Overton is strong enough to be a power end, but he does not have the speed to be a rush end and is better equipped to try to beat guards with a speed rush rather than offensive tackles. Thus, Overton would fit best as a three technique or 4i in the NFL. As a pass rusher, Overton shows a nice combination of skills that make him tough to block at the point of attack. Overton has good quickness out of his stance to fire upfield and get into the chest of blockers. He shows a strong upper body to shed blocks, and does a nice job of using his hands and feet at the same time. Overton has a strong bull rush, and he can ride blockers back to the quarterback. With quality athleticism, Overton is able to move and is agile to redirect to get on the edge of blockers. Overton has quickness to close and is physical when he gets to the quarterback. Overton has quality speed, but is not fast enough to beat pro tackles as a speed rusher around the corner. Hence, he is better off lining up inside. Overton could stand to refine his pass-rushing moves and add more moves to attack pro blockers. In run defense, Overton is impressive. He is strong at the point of attack and shows the ability to hold his ground against downhill runs coming straight at him. Overton is strong to shed blocks and is capable of flowing down the line to get in on tackles. Overton has good vision and discipline to lock down the backside on misdirection runs. Overton is physical at the point of attack and has a nasty demeanor. Overton is quick, strong, and athletic. He has a well-rounded skill set, but does not have elite speed or athleticism. Overall, his skill set could be that of a first- or second-round pick.

2026 NFL Draft Expectations: Entering the 2025 season, Overton looks like he has top-60 potential for the 2026 NFL Draft.
