name: Derrick Moore
scouting_report:
Background:
Derrick Moore came out of St. Frances Academy in Baltimore, where he recorded 55 tackles, 24 tackles for loss, and 12 sacks as a senior, earning MaxPreps Maryland High School Football Player of the Year. He took MVP honors at the Under Armour All-American Game with a two-sack performance. He was a consensus four-star recruit rated among the top 25 players nationally by ESPN, with a 247Sports grade of 0.95 and a Rivals rating of 6.0. Moore appeared in all 14 games as a true freshman at Michigan in 2022, recording two sacks. His sophomore year coincided with Michigan's national championship run, and he contributed 34 tackles, 6.0 tackles for loss, and 5.0 sacks across 15 games. He earned his first 12 starts as a junior but production dipped to 4.0 sacks while dealing with injuries. Everything came together as a senior. He was voted team captain, named Michigan's Defensive Player of the Year and Bo Schembechler MVP, and posted 10.5 tackles for loss and 10 sacks in 12 starts. He earned first-team All-Big Ten from the coaches and was selected for the East-West Shrine Bowl. Over four years he totaled 95 tackles, 23 tackles for loss, 21 sacks, and three forced fumbles in 53 games.

Strengths:
Bull rush is his best weapon; generates real pocket collapse and drives tackles into the quarterback's space. Keeps his legs churning through contact rather than stalling, converting initial burst into sustained pressure. Good pad level and forward lean at the top of his rush, staying under blockers through the finish. Reliable tackler who wraps up and finishes; does not give ball carriers free yards once he arrives. Michigan used him in standing alignments, three-point stances, and wide-9 looks without drop-off. Carries 255 pounds on a frame that plays lighter, with enough length to hold the point of attack. Hands and feet work together during his rush, creating fluid speed-to-power conversion on his best reps. Senior year production spike to 10 sacks suggests a player who learned how to finish at the quarterback.

Weaknesses:
Run defense effort is inconsistent; does not always play with the urgency needed on every snap. Counter moves remain underdeveloped, leaving him predictable when his initial rush gets stalled. First-step quickness is not in the top tier, forcing him to win with power over pure speed. Struggles to disengage when tackles get into his frame, often riding blocks laterally instead of working through. Pad level rises on contact at times, surrendering the leverage his frame should provide.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Offense Rating: 81%
  Tackling: 77%
  Pass Rush: 95%
  Run Defense: 77%

NFL Player Comparisons: JalyxHunt - Houston Christian (98%), PrincelyUmanmielen - Ole Miss (95%), K.J.Henry - Clemson (92%)

Derrick Moore,                     DE, Michigan.
bio: Derrick Moore came out of St. Frances Academy in Baltimore, where he recorded 55 tackles, 24 tackles for loss, and 12 sacks as a senior, earning MaxPreps Maryland High School Football Player of the Year. He took MVP honors at the Under Armour All-American Game with a two-sack performance. He was a consensus four-star recruit rated among the top 25 players nationally by ESPN, with a 247Sports grade of 0.95 and a Rivals rating of 6.0. Moore appeared in all 14 games as a true freshman at Michigan in 2022, recording two sacks. His sophomore year coincided with Michigan's national championship run, and he contributed 34 tackles, 6.0 tackles for loss, and 5.0 sacks across 15 games. He earned his first 12 starts as a junior but production dipped to 4.0 sacks while dealing with injuries. Everything came together as a senior. He was voted team captain, named Michigan's Defensive Player of the Year and Bo Schembechler MVP, and posted 10.5 tackles for loss and 10 sacks in 12 starts. He earned first-team All-Big Ten from the coaches and was selected for the East-West Shrine Bowl. Over four years he totaled 95 tackles, 23 tackles for loss, 21 sacks, and three forced fumbles in 53 games.
strengths: Bull rush is his best weapon; generates real pocket collapse and drives tackles into the quarterback's space. Keeps his legs churning through contact rather than stalling, converting initial burst into sustained pressure. Good pad level and forward lean at the top of his rush, staying under blockers through the finish. Reliable tackler who wraps up and finishes; does not give ball carriers free yards once he arrives. Michigan used him in standing alignments, three-point stances, and wide-9 looks without drop-off. Carries 255 pounds on a frame that plays lighter, with enough length to hold the point of attack. Hands and feet work together during his rush, creating fluid speed-to-power conversion on his best reps. Senior year production spike to 10 sacks suggests a player who learned how to finish at the quarterback.
weaknesses: Run defense effort is inconsistent; does not always play with the urgency needed on every snap. Counter moves remain underdeveloped, leaving him predictable when his initial rush gets stalled. First-step quickness is not in the top tier, forcing him to win with power over pure speed. Struggles to disengage when tackles get into his frame, often riding blocks laterally instead of working through. Pad level rises on contact at times, surrendering the leverage his frame should provide.
player_comparisons: JalyxHunt - Houston Christian (98%), PrincelyUmanmielen - Ole Miss (95%), K.J.Henry - Clemson (92%)

name: T.J. Parker
scouting_report:
Background:
Tomarrion "T.J." Parker hails from Phenix City, Alabama, where Central High School has become something of a Clemson pipeline in recent years. Born September 19, 2004, Parker terrorized opposing backfields throughout his prep career, racking up 63 tackles and 12 sacks as a senior while helping Central to a 10-3 record. His junior campaign was even more destructive with 75 tackles, 23 tackles for loss, and 9.5 sacks. The 6'4", 263-pound defender earned four-star status from Rivals, ESPN, and 247Sports, with composite rankings placing him among the nation's top 50 overall prospects in the 2023 class. Originally committed to Penn State, Parker flipped to Clemson in November 2022 and enrolled early in January 2023. Parker's freshman season at Clemson announced his arrival in spectacular fashion. He shattered Myles Murphy's freshman record with 12.5 tackles for loss while adding 5.5 sacks and earning Freshman All-American honors from 247Sports, ESPN, the FWAA, The Athletic, and several other outlets. His performance at Syracuse that year, a five-tackle, two-sack demolition, made him the first Clemson true freshman with multiple sacks in a game since Murphy himself in 2020. The accolades kept coming as a sophomore: 64 tackles, 19.5 tackles for loss, 11 sacks, and a school-record six forced fumbles earned him second-team All-ACC recognition and national attention. That Pittsburgh game in 2024 will live in Clemson lore. Parker went nuclear with seven tackles, five tackles for loss, and four sacks, tying the school record held by Keith Adams (1999), Andre Branch (2011), and Austin Bryant (2017). The performance earned him Bednarik Award National Player of the Week, Walter Camp National Defensive Player of the Week, and ACC Defensive Lineman of the Week in one fell swoop. He finished his sophomore year as the only player in the nation ranking top-10 in forced fumbles, tackles for loss, and sacks. His junior season saw a dip in production with five sacks over 12 games, but Parker still flashed dominance, including a three-sack performance against South Carolina that pushed him past Chester McGlockton into 10th place on Clemson's all-time sack list. He earned his degree in sports communication in December 2025 and married former Clemson volleyball player Azyah Dailey that fall.

Strengths:
Weaponizes his hands at the point of attack with violent striking and active counters that regularly leave offensive tackles grasping at air. Ball-stripping ability is elite, not coincidental. Six forced fumbles in 2024 came from precise timing and calculated violence at the catch point. Long-arm rush can absolutely devastate tackles when he times it right, rolling them backward into the quarterback's lap and collapsing the pocket. Sets firm, immovable edges against the run by utilizing his length and upper-body strength to anchor, control, and discard blockers. Reads the backfield while rushing, adjusting his path to stay connected with the quarterback's movements rather than just chasing empty grass. Attacks the blocker's outside hand to open the corner, a subtle technical detail that creates free rushes when executed properly. Shows legitimate flexibility dropping into zone coverage, comfortable carrying backs and tight ends into the flat without looking stiff or lost. Competes with a visible edge and intensity when challenged. After getting chipped or cut, he responds by bringing extra violence to the next snap.

Weaknesses:
Not going to win many footraces to the corner. His get-off is solid but not special, so he needs to time the snap perfectly or rely on power. Down blocks give him trouble when they land clean. Gets pushed off his spot and opens up running lanes he should be squeezing shut. Disappears on plays that break to the opposite side. Limited range means he's a spectator when the action moves away from his gap. Needs more tricks in the bag. When tackles absorb his initial charge and sit down on him, he doesn't have reliable counters to keep them guessing. Tackling in the open field gets loose. Tends to swipe and grab rather than wrapping through ball carriers, which leads to missed opportunities.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Offense Rating: 79%
  Tackling: 69%
  Pass Rush: 82%
  Run Defense: 82%

NFL Player Comparisons: CameronThomas - San Diego State (96%), BrennanJackson - Washington State (90%), MarshawnKneeland - Western Michigan (89%)

T.J. Parker, 6-3/265 Defensive End Clemson

T.J. Parker Prospect Preview By Charlie Campbell

Career Recap: Parker was a top recruit to Clemson, and he made his presence felt immediately for the Tigers. In his freshman campaign, Parker contributed 5.5 sacks with 33 tackles while rotating with other defensive linemen. In 2024, Parker produced an excellent season for the Tigers, totaling 11 sacks with six forced fumbles and 57 tackles.

2025 Season Outlook: Parker will see some good talent during the 2025 season, and it will start with LSU in the opener. However, the Tigers are replacing their starting tackle tandem from last year, so it could be a good matchup for Parker to start his junior season with a high-profile performance. Other notable games to watch, according to Parker, include North Carolina (Oct. 4), Louisville (Nov. 14), and South Carolina (Nov. 29).

Prospect Summary: In the passing-driven NFL, there are always teams looking to get a natural quarterback hunter around the edge, and Parker has that ability. Parker shows a nice skill set with speed around the corner and functional strength to keep tackles from latching onto him. With an advanced ability to use his hands and feet at the same time, Parker keeps climbing upfield while breaking the hands away from tackles. Parker has a developed upper body and impressive strength for an edge defender who is not overly big. While he is not an outstanding bender, he shows some ability to dip and sink his hips to get around the corner. Parker has speed to get upfield, and if a tackle can’t get depth in their drop, they are in big trouble with Parker. Another attribute that really jumps out about Parker is his superb instincts to go for the ball when he gets to the quarterback. Parker is very cognizant of smacking at the ball, and he hits quarterbacks hard. His forced fumble total last year was legit, and he did an outstanding job of creating turnovers for his defense. If Parker shows consistency with creating forced fumbles, that is going to get a ton of pro evaluators very excited about him. Parker showed solid run defense in 2024 as well. His upper body strength translates to effective tackling in the ground game, and his speed makes him a solid defender against pursuit runs. It will be interesting to see his official measurements and if a lack of length is going to be an issue in the NFL. In 2025, there are parts of his game that Parker can stand to upgrade and improve upon. He could stand to react faster to the snap. While Parker is quick, in some plays, he does not seem to react as quickly as possible to the snap of the ball. In other plays, he really fires off the ball, so consistency could improve. He also could stand to refine pass-rushing moves. Parker uses speed around the corner well with his active hands, but spins to the inside, and rip moves would be nice to add to his rush plan for attacking NFL offensive tackles.

2026 NFL Draft Expectations: Entering the 2025 season, Parker looks like he has first-round potential for the 2026 NFL Draft.

name: Gennings Dunker
scouting_report:
Background:
Gennings Dunker grew up in Lena, Illinois, where he was a two-way force who helped his team capture state championships as a freshman and junior while reaching the semifinals as a sophomore. The program went 39-2 during his three seasons before COVID-19 wiped out his senior year entirely. Standing 6'5" and 320 pounds (born May 8, 2003), Dunker was also a track athlete and wrestler who earned all-state honors as a junior and first-team all-conference recognition on both sides of the ball. Despite the raw tools and dominance against smaller-school competition, he flew somewhat under the national radar as a three-star recruit. Iowa brought Dunker into their 2021 class with the understanding that development takes time in their system. He redshirted his true freshman year without seeing game action due to injury, then appeared in 11 games as a redshirt freshman in 2022, earning his first career start at right guard in the Music City Bowl win over Kentucky. From 2023 onward, Dunker owned the right tackle spot, starting 13 games that year and helping the Hawkeyes rush for 254 yards against Western Michigan. The 2024 season saw him earn second-team All-Big Ten honors from the coaches while anchoring a line that averaged 197.2 rushing yards per game, the program's best mark since 2002. His final collegiate season in 2025 was his best. Dunker started all 13 games at right tackle, pushing his career total to 38 starts, and was named first-team All-Big Ten by the media. More importantly, the offensive line won the Joe Moore Award as the nation's best unit. He helped pave the way for 200-plus rushing yard performances in five different games, including a 310-yard explosion against UAlbany in the season opener. Dunker also earned CSC second-team Academic All-America recognition, the Forest Evashevski Academic Achievement Award, and the Hayden Fry Award for offense. His Senior Bowl performance only reinforced his standing as one of the class's premier interior offensive line prospects.

Strengths:
Generates violent movement at the point of attack with exceptional leg drive and upper-body torque that consistently displaces defenders in the run game. Once his hands land clean, defenders are locked in place; his grip strength and ability to steer blocks is among the best in this class. Plays with a nasty disposition that shows up every snap; finishes blocks with bad intentions and keeps grinding well after the whistle. Anchor is sturdy enough to absorb bull rushes without giving ground; rarely gets walked back into the quarterback's lap on power moves. Creates serious knock-back on combination blocks and climbs to linebackers with enough force to jar them off their feet entirely. Footwork timing is refined on angle blocks, allowing him to seal edges and open cutback lanes in gap and duo schemes effectively. Freakish weight room numbers translate to the field; there's legitimate pound-for-pound power advantage against most interior defenders. Four-year starter with 38 career starts and Joe Moore Award pedigree; brings the kind of experience and leadership that translates immediately to NFL locker rooms.

Weaknesses:
Functional length is borderline for the NFL tackle position; his punch range struggles to dictate angles against longer-limbed edge rushers consistently. Lacks the explosive first step to win reach blocks on the backside of outside zone concepts; often arrives late creating run-through lanes. Hip stiffness limits his ability to redirect and mirror sudden change-of-direction moves; quicker rushers can work him into bad angles. Gets caught leaning and overextended when defenders stack and move their feet; pass rushers who can create secondary moves find success. Foot quickness grades as pedestrian for the position; creates shorter corners in pass protection that more athletic rushers will exploit.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Defense Faced Rating: 77%
  Pass Blocking: 83%
  Run Blocking: 91%

NFL Player Comparisons: SeanRhyan - UCLA (94%), LoganBrown - Kansas (92%), TalieseFuaga - Oregon State (90%)

Gennings Dunker, 6-5/319 Guard Iowa

Gennings Dunker Scouting Report By Charlie Campbell

Strengths: Strong Tough Tenacious run blocker Generates movement Can knock defenders back Very good at pulling Will punish and pound linebackers, defensive backs Heavy hands Strong base Can sink his weight to stop bull rushes Adequate length for guard Experienced

Weaknesses: Hand placement needs improvement in pass pro on the edge Short arms for a tackle Lacks some foot quickness as a tackle Could have kick slide and mirror issues versus edge rushers

Prospect Summary: The media is captivated by Dunker’s awesome mullet, and they spend too much time talking about his hair rather than Dunker being a really good football player. As a sophomore, Dunker broke into the starting lineup for a program with a strong pipeline of offensive line talent to the NFL. Dunker settled in at the right tackle position and started 38 games over three seasons with Iowa. While Dunker started at right tackle for Iowa, his best fit in the NFL would come inside at guard. In pass protection, Dunker could have issues with pro speed rushers. His arms are a little short, but his feet and kick slide look better suited to line up inside. His hand placement also needs some development, but Dunker has a strong base to hold his ground against bull rushes. He should be able to handle power nose tackles as well as quick interior pass rushers if he were to line up at guard. As a run blocker, Dunker is absolutely tenacious with some serious heavy hands. He blocks hard through the whistle and looks to really punish defenders. Dunker charges hard and pulls well to hit kick out blocks, and when he gets to linebackers or defensive backs, he brings serious physicality. Dunker will rock them with some bone-rattling hits and sheer violence. Dunker is strong at the point of attack, and he fires into the chest of defensive linemen. With his size and strength, Dunker can generate a push as a run blocker. He also functions well on zone runs, and moving to the inside will make him even better. In the 2026 NFL Draft, Dunker looks like a day-two pick. I think Dunker has the potential to be a Pro Bowl guard in the NFL. He could be a nice backup plan as an emergency tackle as well if there are injuries that force the line to be shuffled around. If Dunker is drafted to be a starting right tackle, I think he could be solid but not above average as an edge blocker. Check out my latest 2026 NFL Mock Draft to see where Dunker is currently being projected.

Prospect Comparison: Wyatt Teller. In terms of skill set and style of play, Dunker reminds me of Teller coming out of Virginia Tech.

name: D’Angelo Ponds
scouting_report:
Background:
Before covering future Ohio State star Jeremiah Smith daily in practice at Chaminade-Madonna Prep, D'Angelo Ponds was winning state titles in the 100 and 200-meter dashes in Hollywood, Florida. Despite 77 tackles and seven pass breakups on an undefeated championship team, the 5-9, 170-pound corner was ranked the No. 170 cornerback in the 2023 class. Syracuse was his only Power 4 offer. James Madison took the shot nobody else would. Ponds started by midseason as a freshman, finishing 2023 with 51 tackles, two picks, and 13 pass breakups, ranking 11th nationally with 15 passes defended. The FWAA named him a Freshman All-American. When Curt Cignetti left for Indiana and 30 programs came calling in the portal, Ponds followed the coach who believed in him first. At Indiana, he earned All-America honors both seasons and first-team All-Big Ten twice while anchoring a defense that set program records in scoring and total yards allowed. He took home Defensive MVP in both the Rose Bowl and the Peach Bowl, picking off Dante Moore on Oregon's opening snap for a touchdown. At the Combine, he posted a 43.5-inch vertical, tied for the best mark in three years. Across 40 career games, Ponds totaled six interceptions, four non-offensive touchdowns, and two blocked punt return scores.

Strengths:
Track-caliber speed provides a recovery cushion that lets him gamble without getting torched over the top. Reads quarterback eyes at a different level and finds throwing lanes before the ball is even released. Two picks in one half against Washington, including a 67-yard house call, put the ball skills on full display. Competes with a nastiness that has no business coming from a frame that size. Threw 6-4 Denzel Boston to the ground and made the tackle. That tells you about his competitive fire. Diagnosed Oregon's RPO pre-snap off the receiver's split in the Peach Bowl and jumped it for six. The 43.5-inch vertical confirms the explosive lower half you see on tape in his plant-and-drive breaks. Two blocked punt return touchdowns and genuine return ability make him a contributor from day one on special teams.

Weaknesses:
At 5-8 and a half, 182 pounds with shorter arms, bigger NFL receivers will bully him at the catch point. Shows a small hitch in his deceleration against comeback routes that sharper NFL timing will punish more consistently. The gambling instincts cut both ways, and the misses against pro-caliber route timing will sting harder. Bigger bodies wall him off in run support, and he resorts to ankle tackles instead of wrapping through contact. Almost zero slot reps, which is where a corner his size likely needs versatility at the next level.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Offense Rating: 80%
  QB Rating When targeted: 55.9
  Tackling: 94%
  Run Defense: 93%
  Zone: 90%
  Man/Press: 97%

NFL Player Comparisons: JahdaeBarron - Texas (82%), KrisAbrams-Draine - Missouri (59%), MikeSainristil - Michigan (58%)

name: Isaiah World
scouting_report:
Background:
Standing at a towering 6-foot-8 and weighing 312 pounds, Isaiah World spent four formative years at Nevada before transferring to Oregon for his final collegiate season. A three-star recruit coming out of high school, World transformed himself from the nation's 211th-ranked player into the No. 1 overall transfer in the portal according to 247Sports ahead of his move to Eugene. At Nevada, World steadily climbed the depth chart, redshirting as a freshman before earning 10 starts at right tackle in 2022. He transitioned to left tackle in 2023, where he started all 12 games, earning All-Mountain West Honorable Mention honors. His development continued in 2024 when he again started all 13 games at left tackle, repeating as an All-Mountain West Honorable Mention while posting an impressive 82.3 pass blocking grade from PFF. His performance against top competition caught the attention of powerhouse programs, ultimately leading to his transfer to Oregon. World's single season at Oregon represented his greatest challenge and opportunity. Tasked with protecting the blindside for a less mobile quarterback in Dante Moore, World stepped into the shoes of NFL draftee Josh Conerly Jr. and performed admirably. Coaches praised his "great spirit" and work ethic, while noting his basketball background had helped him develop the athleticism and body control that complemented his imposing frame. Going against elite edge talent like Matayo Uiagalelei (the Big Ten's sack leader) in practice helped World refine his technique against top-tier competition he hadn't consistently faced in the Mountain West.

Strengths:
Possesses legitimate NFL tackle dimensions with an imposing 6'8" frame that creates immediate problems for edge rushers trying to bend around his massive radius. Shows natural shock in his hands when he lands his punch, displaying the power to stun rushers and reset the line of scrimmage when he connects cleanly. Demonstrates above-average lateral agility for his size, capable of mirroring speed rushers and rarely looking lost in space during pass sets. Created impressive tape against elevated competition after transferring to Oregon, indicating he can handle the significant jump in athleticism at the next level. Pass protection fundamentals have steadily improved each season, reflected in allowing just one sack and two QB hits during his final year at Nevada. Brings a basketball background that manifests in his body control and balance, allowing him to recover when initially beaten by explosive first steps. Displays an understanding of angles and leverage that compensates for occasions when he's beaten by superior athleticism or technique. Shows the mental processing to recognize and pick up stunts and delayed blitzes, rarely appearing confused by defensive schemes or late-developing pressure.

Weaknesses:
Still developing consistency in run blocking, where his pad level tends to rise and negate his natural size advantage, resulting in lost leverage battles. Struggles with penalty discipline, committing 8 penalties in his final Nevada season despite improvement from previous years (11 and 13 respectively). Hand placement remains inconsistent, too often landing wide or allowing defenders into his chest, negating his length advantage. Shows adequate but not exceptional explosion off the snap, limiting his ability to reach defenders on outside zone plays or second-level blocks. Limited experience against elite competition prior to his Oregon transfer raises questions about his learning curve against NFL speed and technique. Tore his ACL playing against Indiana - will miss the NFL combine as a result

  Pass Blocking: 72%
  Run Blocking: 68%

NFL Player Comparisons: LukeTenuta - Virginia Tech (89%), SpencerBrown - Northern Iowa (82%), ChaseLundt - UConn (80%)

Isaiah World, 6-8/318 Offensive Tackle Oregon

Isaiah World Scouting Report By Charlie Campbell

Strengths: Giant size Good length Heavy hands Uses size and length to cause rushers to take extra steps Can sink his weight to stop bull rushes Gets depth in his drop Shows some quickness on the edge Strong upper body in the ground game Can knock defenders back Will bury and pancake defenders Uses length to sustain Has starter upside

Weaknesses: Raw Needs development Coming off a serious knee injury Some times he reaches after rushers Gives up pressure to the inside too frequetnly Will resort to grabbing and holding Needs to use his feet more consistently to block off the edge

Prospect Summary: World was the starting left tackle at Nevada the past two seasons after starting at right tackle as a freshman. After the 2024 season, World entered the transfer portal, and Oregon won his services. In 2025, World had a solid season for the Ducks, but did have some struggles to close out the year in the college football playoff. World also suffered a torn ACL that will impact his rookie NFL season. As a pass blocker, World has a natural build with good size, strength, and length to play on the edge. He does a nice job of sinking his weight to stop bull rushes, and he shows the ability to kill speed to power attempts. World does a nice job of sustaining his blocks with the upper body strength to tie up defenders. With quickness off the ball, World gets some depth in his drop, and he is quick out of his stance. However, World will get in some stretches where he doesn’t move his feet and lunges after rushers. That can get him in trouble, and he was exposed with these issues in the college football playoff game against Texas Tech and their future NFL pass rushers. World was slow to react to moves to the inside, and grabbed rather than moving his feet at times. World has the talent to take on edge rushers, but he needs development for the NFL. In the ground game, World uses his strong upper body to knock defenders back and push them around. He has real power at the point of attack and blocks with a mean streak. With overwhelming mass, he can knock defenders flat on their backs and will bury them with a violent pancake block. Other plays World shows that he can twist, turn, and manipulate his defender to wall them away from his back. World uses his length and strength to sustain blocks and does not give up second effort plays. In the 2026 NFL Draft, World could go on day two or the mid-rounds. Check my latest 2026 NFL Mock Draft to see where World is currently projected to go.

Prospect Comparison: Patrick Paul. In terms of size, quickness, and style of play, World reminds me of Paul coming out of Houston. Paul was a second-round pick and World would have been as well if it weren’t for his injury.

2024 NFL Mock Draft : Charlie's | Walt's 2024 NFL Draft Prospect Rankings 2024 NFL Draft Player Previews
bio: Standing at a towering 6-foot-8 and weighing 312 pounds, Isaiah World spent four formative years at Nevada before transferring to Oregon for his final collegiate season. A three-star recruit coming out of high school, World transformed himself from the nation's 211th-ranked player into the No. 1 overall transfer in the portal according to 247Sports ahead of his move to Eugene. At Nevada, World steadily climbed the depth chart, redshirting as a freshman before earning 10 starts at right tackle in 2022. He transitioned to left tackle in 2023, where he started all 12 games, earning All-Mountain West Honorable Mention honors. His development continued in 2024 when he again started all 13 games at left tackle, repeating as an All-Mountain West Honorable Mention while posting an impressive 82.3 pass blocking grade from PFF. His performance against top competition caught the attention of powerhouse programs, ultimately leading to his transfer to Oregon. World's single season at Oregon represented his greatest challenge and opportunity. Tasked with protecting the blindside for a less mobile quarterback in Dante Moore, World stepped into the shoes of NFL draftee Josh Conerly Jr. and performed admirably. Coaches praised his "great spirit" and work ethic, while noting his basketball background had helped him develop the athleticism and body control that complemented his imposing frame. Going against elite edge talent like Matayo Uiagalelei (the Big Ten's sack leader) in practice helped World refine his technique against top-tier competition he hadn't consistently faced in the Mountain West.
strengths: Possesses legitimate NFL tackle dimensions with an imposing 6'8" frame that creates immediate problems for edge rushers trying to bend around his massive radius. Shows natural shock in his hands when he lands his punch, displaying the power to stun rushers and reset the line of scrimmage when he connects cleanly. Demonstrates above-average lateral agility for his size, capable of mirroring speed rushers and rarely looking lost in space during pass sets. Created impressive tape against elevated competition after transferring to Oregon, indicating he can handle the significant jump in athleticism at the next level. Pass protection fundamentals have steadily improved each season, reflected in allowing just one sack and two QB hits during his final year at Nevada. Brings a basketball background that manifests in his body control and balance, allowing him to recover when initially beaten by explosive first steps. Displays an understanding of angles and leverage that compensates for occasions when he's beaten by superior athleticism or technique. Shows the mental processing to recognize and pick up stunts and delayed blitzes, rarely appearing confused by defensive schemes or late-developing pressure.
weaknesses: Still developing consistency in run blocking, where his pad level tends to rise and negate his natural size advantage, resulting in lost leverage battles. Struggles with penalty discipline, committing 8 penalties in his final Nevada season despite improvement from previous years (11 and 13 respectively). Hand placement remains inconsistent, too often landing wide or allowing defenders into his chest, negating his length advantage. Shows adequate but not exceptional explosion off the snap, limiting his ability to reach defenders on outside zone plays or second-level blocks. Limited experience against elite competition prior to his Oregon transfer raises questions about his learning curve against NFL speed and technique. Tore his ACL playing against Indiana - will miss the NFL combine as a result

name: Kage Casey
scouting_report:
Background:
None of the major recruiting services ranked Kage Casey inside their top 1,000 nationally when he signed with Boise State as a three-star out of Clackamas High School in Happy Valley, Oregon. He had played both offensive tackle and defensive end for the Cavaliers and helped them reach the second round of the Oregon 6A state playoffs as a senior. After a redshirt season with limited action as a true freshman, Casey stepped into the starting left tackle job in 2023 and never gave it back. He started all 14 games that year and earned Second Team All-Mountain West and Freshman All-America recognition from The Athletic. That offensive line ranked 13th nationally in sacks allowed per game. His 2024 season was the real breakout. Casey started every game again, logged a team-high 947 offensive snaps, and did not allow a single sack across 440 pass-blocking opportunities. He picked up multiple All-America nods, including Second Team honors from The Athletic and Walter Camp. In 2025, he was named First Team All-Mountain West for a second straight year and earned a Senior Bowl invite, capping a career that saw him play over 2,700 offensive snaps across 44 games.

Strengths:
Anchor is legitimate; sits down against bull rushes and holds his ground consistently. Strong, active hands that latch and control once he gets into defenders' frames. Powerful drive blocker who creates vertical displacement in the run game. Keeps his legs churning through the whistle and finishes with intent. Recognizes stunts quickly and adjusts his positioning without panic. Firm inside hand shuts down B-gap counters and spin moves effectively. Takes good angles to reach second-level defenders in space. Durable three-year starter with over 2,700 snaps and zero missed games.

Weaknesses:
Below-average arm length forces him to overextend and lets defenders into his chest. Lacks the foot speed and range to stay with NFL-caliber edge speed. Tends to short-set in pass protection, opening himself to outside rushes. Pad level runs high, which lets longer defenders pry underneath him. Recovery quickness is limited once beaten off the snap. Gets over his skis in the run game and can slip off blocks at the perimeter.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Defense Faced Rating: 74%
  Pass Blocking: 81%
  Run Blocking: 75%

NFL Player Comparisons: BlakeFisher - Notre Dame (99%), EmeryJones Jr. - LSU (94%), AjaniCornelius - Oregon (94%)

name: Sam Roush
scouting_report:
Background:
Sam Roush was born in Atlanta but calling him a Georgia kid would be selling his story short. His family moved seven times across three continents before he settled at Lipscomb Academy in Nashville, with stops in Kuala Lumpur and Jakarta along the way. In Indonesia, he played rugby for the Jakarta Komodo Rugby Club, and during high school he competed on a world championship X-Prize Ocean Quest Engineering team, the kind of resume detail that makes you do a double take. Football bloodlines run thick in this family, though: his father Luke played defensive back at Duke, his maternal grandfather Phil Olsen suited up for the Rams, Broncos, and Bills, and his great uncle is Pro Football Hall of Famer Merlin Olsen. Roush earned Tennessee Co-Defensive Player of the Year honors as a senior in 2021 and helped lead Lipscomb to a D2-AA state championship, drawing four-star ratings from ESPN (81/100), 247 Sports (90/100), and Rivals (5.8/95%). Stanford got a prospect who needed time to cook, and Roush never tried to rush the process. He appeared in all 48 games across his four-year Cardinal career, working his way from special teams as a freshman in 2022 to a full-time starter by his junior season. His sophomore year in 2023 produced 28 catches for 288 yards across 10 starts, highlighted by a seven-catch, 61-yard day against Washington State that hinted at what was coming. As a junior in 2024, he started all 12 games and pulled in 40 receptions for 344 yards and two touchdowns while picking up ACC All-Academic Team honors. The numbers climbed every single year, and by his senior campaign the arrow was pointing steeply upward. Roush's 2025 season was comfortably his best. He finished with 49 catches for 545 yards and two touchdowns, ranking second on the team in both receptions and receiving yards while leading all ACC tight ends in receiving yardage. An eight-catch, 104-yard performance against Pittsburgh made him just the eighth Stanford tight end to crack 100 receiving yards in a game since 1995, and a 69-yard catch-and-run touchdown against Boston College showcased a gear that his size would suggest he does not have. He became one of only five FBS tight ends with multiple 50-plus yard receptions during the season. The accolades stacked up accordingly: All-ACC Second Team from the coaches, the Go Bowling Military Bowl STEM Scholar-Athlete award, and both the Al Masters Award and Tommy Vardell Award as a senior. He closed his career with 34 starts, 30 of them consecutive, and earned a Senior Bowl invitation that gave NFL evaluators an extended look at the complete package.

Strengths:
Combine testing was a revelation for a 267-pound man. A 38.5-inch vertical (96th percentile) and 10-foot-6 broad jump (96th percentile) are freakish explosion numbers, the best vertical of any tight end above 265 pounds by four and a half inches. His 87 athleticism score ranked third among all tight ends at the Combine, and his 91.7 SPORQ percentile clears the critical 90th-percentile threshold that separates future contributors from roster fillers at the position. Ran a weight-adjusted 3-cone (7.08) that compares favorably to Sam LaPorta and was faster straight up than Darren Waller and Kyle Pitts. For a guy tipping 267, that kind of agility in tight spaces is genuinely unusual. Understands the full tight end job description and does not flinch at the dirty work. Lines up inline without complaint, gets to the second level on outside zone, and holds his ground in pass protection when edge rushers try to work inside. The intermediate game between the numbers from 12 to 18 yards is where he does his best work. He finds soft spots in zone with a feel that looks natural, gives the quarterback a massive target on third down, and has the awareness to sit or drift based on what the coverage shows. Production climbed every single year in a run-first Stanford offense that never featured him as a primary weapon. Going from 28 catches to 40 to 49 while adding a 69-yard score tells you there is more in the tank when the passing game actually asks for him. Carries himself with maximum effort whether the ball is coming his way or not. Runs off safeties to clear space, finishes blocks 20 yards downfield, competes on every snap without being told. Coaching staffs fall in love with that makeup. Better with the ball in his hands than his testing speed would suggest. Runs through arm tackles, keeps his legs churning, and showed real balance in traffic on that Boston College touchdown that covered 69 yards.

Weaknesses:
Drops are a legitimate concern that showed up consistently on tape. Too many catchable balls hit him in the chest and bounced away, and at times he shortened his arms when defenders closed on him at the catch point. The hands grade needs to improve significantly. Blocking technique gets loose when he widens his base too much at the point of attack. He lunges forward, drops his head, and winds up on the ground on reps he should finish. To his credit, he showed the ability to self-correct at the Combine, adjusting quickly on the sled after getting high initially. Route breaks lack the snap required to create immediate separation at the NFL level. He relies on length and positioning more than crisp footwork to get open, which will be a tougher sell against linebackers and safeties who can match his size. Ball tracking on deeper throws is inconsistent. He misjudges landing spots too often, either pulling up short or running past the ball, which limits how much a coordinator can trust him as a vertical seam threat despite the explosion numbers. The 74.3 passer rating when targeted is a below-average mark that reflects both the drops and some inconsistency in creating genuine separation on his routes. NFL defensive coordinators will test whether that number improves against tighter coverage.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Defense Faced Rating: 76%
  QB Rating When targeted: 74.3
  Hands: 64%
  Short Receiving: 69%
  Intermediate Routes: 78%
  Deep Threat: 66%
  Blocking: 67%

NFL Player Comparisons: AJBarner - Michigan (87%), JoshWhyle - Cincinnati (83%), CadeOtton - Washington (78%)

name: Nick Singleton
scouting_report:
Nick Singleton, 6-0/221 Running Back Penn State

Nick Singleton Scouting Report By Charlie Campbell

Strengths: Strong build runner Physical runner Can pick up yards after contact Power to push through tackles Finishes runs well Falls forward Delivers some hard hits to defenders Will punish tacklers Tough on defensive backs at the second level Enough speed to hit the hole Can accelerate to the second level Good vision One-cut downhill runner Patience Elusive Good hands Contributing receiver Ready to contribute immediately Experienced against good competition

Weaknesses: Lacks elite speed Well-rounded but not exceptional traits

Prospect Summary: Under head coach James Franklin, Penn State has produced a lot of excellent talent for the NFL, and Singleton should carry on that tradition in the 2026 NFL Draft. Singleton combines size, athleticism, quickness, and versatility to look like a potential starting running back at the pro level. In 2024, Singleton ran well for the Nittany Lions, averaging 6.4 yards per carry for 1,099 yards with 12 touchdowns. He was also a nice contributor as a receiver out of the backfield with 41 catches for 375 yards with five touchdowns. Singleton flashed in previous seasons while splitting carries. In 2023, he averaged 4.4 yards per carry for 752 yards with eight scores. That season, he caught 26 passes for 308 yards with two touchdowns. Singleton was very impressive as a freshman when he averaged 6.8 yards per carry for 1,061 yards with 12 touchdowns. He caught 11 passes for 85 yards and a score.  In 2026, the wheels came off for the Penn State football team, and Franklin was fired. Singleton, along with other players, was unable to get into a productive rhythm with the program in turmoil. In 2025, Singleton averaged 4.5 yards per carry for 549 yards with 13 touchdowns. He had 24 receptions for 219 yards and a touchdown. For the NFL, Singleton is a well-rounded back who can attack defenses in a variety of ways. He has a strong build and is a physical runner who shows the strength to pick up yards after contact. Singleton has the strength to push through tacklers and finishes runs well, delivering hits to defenders and falling forward for additional yardage. At the second level, Singleton is a problem for defensive backs as he will run through them and punish them for attempting to get him to the ground. Singleton has good knee bend to run behind his pads and shows enough speed to open it up when he breaks free. While Singleton does not have elite speed for the NFL like Jahmyr Gibbs, Singleton can hit the hole before it closes and accelerate to the second level. Singleton has a natural skill set as a runner with good vision, one-cut downhill ability, patience, and some elusiveness to weave through the defense. Along with being the engine of a rushing attack, Singleton can contribute to the passing offense as well. He has good hands and shows natural ability as a catcher. He runs quality routes, is smooth in space, and is dangerous after the catch. Like any college player, Singleton will need to learn some blitz protection, but the potential is there for him to handle those duties. In the 2026 NFL Draft, Singleton could be a third or fourth-round pick. He does not have exceptional traits but is a well-rounded back. He could potentially develop into an NFL starter. Check out my latest 2026 NFL Mock Draft to see where Singleton is projected.

Prospect Comparison: Ray Davis. In terms of style of play, Singleton reminds me of a taller version of Davis.

name: Dani Dennis-Sutton

name: Demond Claiborne
scouting_report:
Background:
Demond Claiborne arrived at Wake Forest from King William High School in Aylett, Virginia, a four-star recruit by 247Sports and a state champion sprinter who won the Virginia Class AA 100-meter title. He saw limited action as a true freshman in 2022, logging 14 carries for 57 yards. His sophomore season brought a larger workload: 586 yards and five touchdowns on 137 carries, plus a 96-yard kickoff return score against Virginia Tech. The 2024 campaign was a full arrival. Claiborne carried 228 times for 1,049 yards and 11 touchdowns, becoming the first Demon Deacon to rush for 1,000 yards since Cade Carney in 2018. He added 23 catches for 254 yards and two receiving scores, earned Third Team All-ACC at running back and all-purpose, and returned a kickoff 100 yards for a touchdown against Miami. As a senior in 2025, he ran for 909 yards and 10 touchdowns on 179 carries, earning Second Team All-ACC and landing on the Doak Walker, Maxwell, and Walter Camp preseason watch lists. Claiborne finished as Wake Forest's fourth all-time rusher with 2,599 yards and 26 rushing touchdowns. He accepted a Shrine Bowl invite and confirmed his track-star speed at the NFL Combine, posting one of the fastest times among backs.

Strengths:
Combine-confirmed top-end speed is among the best in this running back class and shows up on tape at the corner. Fluid change-of-direction ability allows him to redirect in tight spaces without losing momentum. Reads zone-blocking schemes well, showing patience to let blocks develop before committing to a lane. Contact balance through arm tackles is impressive given his frame, stays upright when hit off-center. Breakaway ability stands out on film, with nearly a quarter of his 2025 carries going for 10-plus yards. Two career kickoff return touchdowns and consistent return production give him immediate special teams value. Subtle hip fakes and shoulder dips behind the line create hesitation in linebackers before he attacks gaps.

Weaknesses:
Listed under 190 pounds and it shows when defenders square him up in the hole. Five fumbles in 2025 are a serious red flag after zero the previous two seasons. Pass protection is not close to NFL-ready, both in technique and in his ability to anchor. Drops spiked to six in his senior year after just two the season before. Not a yards-after-contact runner when defenders get a clean shot, goes down on first solid hit too often. Can hesitate behind the line when his initial read closes, turning potential short gains into losses.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Defense Faced Rating: 74%
  Rushing: 73%
  Break Tackles: 64%
  Receiving/Hands: 52%
  Pass Blocking: 42%
  Run Blocking: 58%

NFL Player Comparisons: MicahBernard - Utah (73%), BhayshulTuten - Virginia Tech (72%), JeromeFord - Cincinnati (70%)

name: DeMonte Capehart
scouting_report:
Background:
DeMonte Capehart grew up in Hartsville, South Carolina, and spent his senior prep season at IMG Academy, where he started all 10 games and piled up 16 tackles for loss and 5.5 sacks. A consensus four-star recruit ranked inside the top 35 nationally by every major recruiting service, he signed with Clemson in the 2020 class and enrolled early that January. Capehart's first three seasons at Clemson were a slow burn. He logged just 20 snaps as a true freshman, then worked his way to 86 defensive snaps by 2022 and 174 by 2023, when he recorded 16 tackles with 5.0 for loss, a sack, and a forced fumble across 12 appearances. His best single-game effort that year came against Georgia Tech, where he picked up a sack and earned the team's defensive player of the game award. The 2024 and 2025 seasons brought more consistent playing time, with Capehart finally earning six starts in each year. He finished 2024 with 27 tackles and notched five against both Georgia and Texas. As a sixth-year senior in 2025, he posted 29 tackles, 3.0 tackles for loss, and a sack over 344 snaps, contributing to another Clemson ACC championship run. He was invited to the Shrine Bowl following the season.

Strengths:
Heavy, violent hands at initial contact that consistently jolt blockers backward. Good anchor strength against double teams; rarely gets pushed off his spot. Lateral quickness is unusual for a 313-pound interior lineman. Active hands at the line of scrimmage; five career pass breakups from the interior. Tested as one of the most athletic defensive tackles in combine history. Plays with solid leverage and pad level in the run game on most snaps. Functional power to walk guards into the pocket when singled up.

Weaknesses:
Started only 12 games across six college seasons; limited starter experience. Pass rush plan lacks variety; too reliant on a straight bull rush. Rarely develops a counter move when his primary rush stalls out. Tackling technique is inconsistent; comes in high and slides off ballcarriers. Gets upright after his initial strike and loses the leverage advantage. Needs to improve recognition of angle and reach blocks on the backside.

DeMonte Capehart,                     DT, Clemson.
bio: DeMonte Capehart grew up in Hartsville, South Carolina, and spent his senior prep season at IMG Academy, where he started all 10 games and piled up 16 tackles for loss and 5.5 sacks. A consensus four-star recruit ranked inside the top 35 nationally by every major recruiting service, he signed with Clemson in the 2020 class and enrolled early that January. Capehart's first three seasons at Clemson were a slow burn. He logged just 20 snaps as a true freshman, then worked his way to 86 defensive snaps by 2022 and 174 by 2023, when he recorded 16 tackles with 5.0 for loss, a sack, and a forced fumble across 12 appearances. His best single-game effort that year came against Georgia Tech, where he picked up a sack and earned the team's defensive player of the game award. The 2024 and 2025 seasons brought more consistent playing time, with Capehart finally earning six starts in each year. He finished 2024 with 27 tackles and notched five against both Georgia and Texas. As a sixth-year senior in 2025, he posted 29 tackles, 3.0 tackles for loss, and a sack over 344 snaps, contributing to another Clemson ACC championship run. He was invited to the Shrine Bowl following the season.

name: Jacob Rodriguez
scouting_report:
Background:
Jacob Rodriguez was a dual-threat quarterback at Rider High School in Wichita Falls, Texas, where he threw for 7,085 yards and 68 touchdowns and added 3,051 rushing yards and 38 scores across three varsity seasons. An ESPN four-star recruit ranked as the nation's No. 3 dual-threat quarterback, Rodriguez signed with Virginia and spent his true freshman year bouncing between quarterback, receiver, tight end, and running back. He transferred to Texas Tech before the 2022 season and made the full-time switch to linebacker. After a promising but injury-shortened 2023 season (five games, Independence Bowl Defensive MVP), Rodriguez broke out in 2024 with 127 tackles, 10.5 tackles for loss, and five sacks to earn All-Big 12 First Team honors. His senior year in 2025 was historic. Rodriguez posted 128 tackles, seven forced fumbles, four interceptions, and two fumble recoveries while anchoring a Texas Tech defense that led the FBS in takeaways and rushing yards allowed. He swept the Bednarik, Butkus, Nagurski, and Lombardi awards, finished fifth in Heisman voting, and helped lead the Red Raiders to their first Big 12 championship.

Strengths:
Elite run defender who keys quickly and fires downhill with real conviction at the point of attack. Sideline-to-sideline range is legitimate; his lateral burst gets him to the perimeter in a hurry. Active, violent hands when working off blocks; stays square and sheds with good leverage. Ball production is absurd: 13 career forced fumbles, six interceptions, five fumble recoveries. Quarterback background shows up in pre-snap reads and coverage instincts against underneath routes. Constant mover who frustrates climbing linemen with his ability to avoid getting locked on. Combine performance confirmed the athleticism: top-tier agility scores and a strong vertical. Two-year captain who clearly raises the play of those around him on film.

Weaknesses:
Undersized frame gets washed out when offensive linemen reach the second level cleanly. Missed tackle numbers are higher than you want, partly from overaggressive angles. Coverage busts showed up too often in 2025 despite strong overall coverage grades. Receivers caught over 80 percent of passes thrown his direction as a senior. Bites hard on misdirection and play-action; the urgency works against him there. Lacks ideal arm length for consistent wrap-up tackles in traffic.

Honors & Awards:
2025 Chuck Bednarik Award 2025 Dick Butkus Award 2025 Bronko Nagurski Award 2025 Lombardi Award

  Offense Rating: 78%
  Tackling: 70%
  Pass Rush: 74%
  Run Defense: 98%
  Coverage: 96%

name: Deion Burks
scouting_report:
Background:
Deion Burks was a three-star recruit out of Belleville High School in Michigan, where he posted 35 catches for 819 yards and 12 touchdowns as a junior and earned AP Division 1-2 All-Michigan honors. He also ran track, and while the recruiting rankings were modest, the athleticism was obvious early. After redshirting at Purdue in 2021, Burks broke out in 2023 as the Boilermakers' lead receiver, starting all 12 games and finishing with 47 receptions, 629 yards, and seven touchdowns. That season included a 152-yard, two-score opener against Fresno State featuring an 84-yard catch. He also landed on Bruce Feldman's Freaks List for physical testing numbers that included a 350-pound bench and GPS-tracked speed above 22 miles per hour. Burks transferred to Oklahoma for 2024 but saw just five games before soft-tissue injuries and a concussion shut him down. He still managed 31 receptions and three touchdowns in that window, including a three-score debut against Temple. Healthy in 2025, he started all 13 games and finished with 57 receptions for 620 yards and four touchdowns, saving his best work for the biggest stages: 107 yards and a score against Alabama in the College Football Playoff and a 101-yard game against Michigan.

Strengths:
Combine-verified top-end speed that shows up on tape as a consistent deep threat off the line. Best vertical jump among all receivers at Indianapolis, confirming rare lower-body explosion. Tied for the most bench reps among wideouts, unusual upper-body strength for his frame. Polished route runner who varies tempo and sells fakes well at the top of his stems. Reliable hands when catching in rhythm, and he tracks the ball naturally over his shoulder. Dangerous after the catch with the wiggle and burst to turn short completions into chunk plays. Spatial awareness near the sideline is advanced; works the boundary with body control beyond his size. Showed his best football in the biggest games, not a player who shrinks from the moment.

Weaknesses:
Size will always be a factor when asked to win contested catches against physical corners. Gets knocked off his route stems by mid-route contact and loses timing against press looks. Blocking grades are well below standard, which limits his ability to stay on the field in the run game. Durability is a genuine concern after missing significant time more than once at Oklahoma. Did not run the three-cone or shuttle at the Combine, leaving short-area agility unconfirmed. Production at Oklahoma was solid but never huge, partly due to quarterback play around him.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Defense Faced Rating: 76%
  QB Rating When targeted: 93.5
  Hands: 82%
  Short Receiving: 74%
  Intermediate Routes: 81%
  Deep Threat: 75%
  Blocking: 51%

name: Carson Beck
scouting_report:
Background:
From Jacksonville's fields to Athens' bright lights, Carson Beck carved a patient path to quarterback stardom that began on the basketball courts of Mandarin High School. His dual-sport background shaped an athlete who would eventually claim Florida's Mr. Football crown, leading Mandarin to its first state championship while completing 136 of 278 passes for 1,843 yards and 20 scores his senior year. Red-shirting at Georgia in 2020, Beck methodically climbed the depth chart beside NCAA champions, absorbing the Bulldogs' culture through four games in 2021 and seven more as Bennett's reliable backup in 2022. The wait proved worthwhile. Beck's junior campaign erupted with remarkable efficiency, completing 72.4% of his attempts for 3,941 yards and 24 touchdowns against six interceptions while piloting Georgia to a 13-1 season and SEC Championship appearance. His professional polish peaked against Kentucky that fall, dissecting the Wildcats for 388 yards and four scores in a dominating performance. College football watched Beck author the largest margin of victory in bowl history when he torched Florida State 63-3 in the Orange Bowl, flashing the precision that had scouts buzzing. Still, 2024 humbled the coronated prospect. Despite launching 3,485 yards and 28 touchdowns, interceptions doubled to twelve as his once-pristine timing wavered behind Georgia's porous protection. The elbow injury finally forced a tough choice - Beck chose revival over draft risk, landing in Miami with a $4 million NIL deal in January 2025. What followed was a season of redemption and resurrection. Beck missed spring practice entirely while his shoulder healed for four months, then guided Miami to a 13-2 record and improbably into the national championship game. Through 15 games, he completed 346 of 494 passes (70.0%) for 3,995 yards with 28 touchdowns against 10 interceptions. The playoff run showcased his growth: a gritty 14-of-20 performance for 103 yards in a 10-3 road win at Texas A&M, followed by 19-of-26 for 138 yards and a touchdown while upsetting second-seeded Ohio State. In the Fiesta Bowl semifinal against Ole Miss, Beck earned MVP honors completing 23 of 37 passes for 268 yards and two touchdowns before scrambling for the game-winning 3-yard touchdown with 18 seconds remaining, punctuating Miami's 31-27 victory. Four-year veteran Beck has now totaled 7,995 yards and 58 touchdowns across 48 games and 35 starts, compiling the resume NFL clubs covet: starting experience, conference championships, and a winning pedigree forged through adversity that proves character matters.

Strengths:
Pocket technician who stands confident behind pressure, sliding laterally without losing downfield focus while displaying remarkable balance through heavy contact Ball placement artist with exceptional anticipatory throws, drops passes perfectly on shoulders and back hips where only receivers can corral contested catches Lightning-quick processing speed translates defensive looks pre-snap into audible adjustments, often identifying mismatches college defenses desperately hide Release mechanics stay compact through different arm angles, whipping sidearm darts over linemen while still firing bullets on deep crossers with proper velocity Decision-making has evolved through playoff experience, now pulling balls back from danger instead of forcing throws into coverage when defense plays well Scrambling ability improved considerably down the stretch, wheeling out of pressure to extend plays and even punching in the game-winning touchdown with his legs Clutch gene shines in critical moments, engineering game-winning drives when everything's on the line rather than wilting under postseason pressure Resilience and mental toughness shine through his ability to rebound from adversity, going from "broken" at Georgia to leading Miami to championship game despite doubters

Weaknesses:
Deep ball accuracy remains inconsistent and problematic, with McShay noting "the deep ball stuff is not where it needs to be right now" despite the strong arm Pocket mobility still limited to shuffles and slides rather than explosive movement, though playoff scrambles showed slight improvement in extending plays beyond structure Trust issues lingered throughout career regarding whether he'd protect the football, make smart decisions in key situations, or handle postgame responsibilities professionally without throwing teammates under bus Susceptible to inside pressure forcing early releases, causing trajectory issues when forced to rush throws while sliding laterally away from collapsing pocket Coverage disguises occasionally create false reads, particularly when defenses show two-high before rotating to single-high after the snap to confuse his progressions

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Defense Faced Rating: 73%
  Release Speed: 90%
  Short Passing: 76%
  Medium Passing: 79%
  Long Passing: 82%
  Rush/Scramble: 52%

NFL Player Comparisons: TannerMcKee - Stanford (81%), CarsonStrong - Nevada (75%), KurtisRourke - Indiana (71%)

name: Oscar Delp
scouting_report:
Background:
Oscar Delp arrived at Georgia as the consensus top tight end recruit in the 2022 class, a four-star prospect out of West Forsyth High School in Cumming, Georgia, where he put up 59 catches for 923 yards and eight touchdowns as a senior. He also earned Forsyth County Offensive Player of the Year and was a 247Sports All-American selection. Beyond football, Delp was a multi-sport athlete who also played lacrosse. His freshman year in 2022 was a limited one, understandably so behind Darnell Washington and Brock Bowers. Five catches for 61 yards in 13 appearances, though his first career reception went for a 28-yard touchdown against South Carolina. As a sophomore in 2023, he started 10 of 14 games and finished with 24 catches for 284 yards and three scores. By 2024, he was Georgia's primary tight end option, posting 21 catches for 248 yards and a career-high four touchdowns across 14 games, earning Coaches Preseason All-SEC Second Team honors and a spot on the John Mackey Award Watch List. In his final season in 2025, Delp started all 14 games and tallied 20 receptions for 261 yards and a touchdown while appearing on the preseason John Mackey Award watchlist. A hairline fracture in his foot, discovered during combine medicals, kept him from on-field drills in Indianapolis. He ran at Georgia's pro day instead, clocking a verified 4.48 in the 40 and drawing favorable comparisons to former teammate Brock Bowers in positional work. His career totals at Georgia: 70 catches, 854 yards, and nine touchdowns across 51 games

Strengths:
Elite straight-line speed for a tight end, verified at his pro day. Reliable hands with a very low drop rate across four college seasons. Versatile alignment history, lining up wide, in the slot, and inline. Consistent 12+ yards per reception average shows ability to work downfield. Good catch rate on targets, converting at nearly 75% for his career. Pass protection improved significantly in his final season at Georgia. Finds soft spots in zone coverage and settles into windows well. Forward lean and acceleration off the line give him a quick first step.

Weaknesses:
Narrow frame shows up when defenders get hands on him at the catch point. Balls thrown into traffic tend to come loose; does not finish well through contact. Gets walked back in the run game by stronger defenders at the point of attack. Four years of tape and the production never matched the athletic tools on display. Physical corners and safeties who jam him at the line can throw off his timing. Does not create much after the catch despite the speed he shows in a straight line.

name: Bud Clark
scouting_report:
Background:
Bud Clark came out of Alexandria, Louisiana as a four-star recruit, the 13th-ranked player in the state and 31st safety nationally, choosing TCU over LSU, Oklahoma, and Ole Miss. He redshirted in 2020 and injuries limited him to just four starts in 2021, costing him the final seven games. His career turned during TCU's 2022 College Football Playoff run. Clark earned the starting job midseason and finished with five interceptions, tied for 11th nationally. He saved his best for the Fiesta Bowl semifinal against Michigan, returning a J.J. McCarthy interception 41 yards for a touchdown and recovering a fumble in the end zone, earning Honorable-Mention All-Big 12 honors. Named team captain in each of his final three seasons, Clark posted three interceptions in 2023 before grading as the third-best Power Four safety nationally in 2024 with a career-high 67 tackles and three picks. His 2025 senior year brought four more interceptions, a career-best seven pass breakups, and Second-Team All-Big 12 recognition, though he missed two late regular-season games due to injury. He finishes his TCU career with 15 interceptions, 21 pass deflections, and 214 tackles across 61 games. He reinforced his stock at the Senior Bowl, collecting multiple interceptions and breakups throughout the week in Mobile.

Strengths:
Fifteen career interceptions over four productive seasons with two pick-sixes. Elite straight-line speed allows him to recover and range over the top. Reads route combinations quickly and triggers downhill with conviction. Comfortable in man or zone, with extensive reps at safety and slot. Uses length well at the catch point to contest and high-point the ball. Willing run defender who navigates traffic and arrives with purpose. Three-year captain with 61 games of experience and Senior Bowl standout week. Strong closing burst to undercut routes and jump passing lanes.

Weaknesses:
Thin frame at just188 pounds leaves him vulnerable absorbing blocks. Over-aggressive reads on the quarterback's eyes can pull him out of position. Opens hips prematurely in man, giving receivers a window to separate. Missed tackle rate jumped in 2025 after a clean 2024 tackling season. Injury history is real: missed significant time in 2021, 2022, and 2025. Can lunge at legs rather than wrap up, especially against bigger ball carriers.

  Offense Rating: 76%
  QB Rating When targeted: 68.1
  Tackling: 81%
  Run Defense: 88%
  Coverage: 89%
  Zone: 89%

weaknesses: Thin frame at just188 pounds leaves him vulnerable absorbing blocks. Over-aggressive reads on the quarterback's eyes can pull him out of position. Opens hips prematurely in man, giving receivers a window to separate. Missed tackle rate jumped in 2025 after a clean 2024 tackling season. Injury history is real: missed significant time in 2021, 2022, and 2025. Can lunge at legs rather than wrap up, especially against bigger ball carriers.

name: Max Klare
scouting_report:
Background:
Max Klare's father Andy was a nose guard at Boston College, a cousin, grandfather, and two uncles all suited up at the college level, and his brother Luke currently plays tight end at Purdue. At St. Xavier in Cincinnati, Klare was a three-sport contributor who played quarterback, tight end, and even handled kicking duties on the football field while also drawing legitimate interest from baseball programs as a left-handed power hitter. As a senior, he posted 39 catches for 637 yards and five touchdowns, earning consensus three-star status and a ranking as the No. 26 prospect in Ohio by 247Sports. He originally committed to Duke before flipping to Purdue. Klare's time in West Lafayette started slow. He appeared in just one game as a true freshman in 2022, then caught 22 balls for 196 yards through five games in 2023 before an ankle injury shut him down. That setback cost him critical development time, but when he came back for 2024, he looked like a different player. He started all 12 games, led Purdue in receiving with 51 catches for 685 yards and four touchdowns, and earned third-team All-Big Ten honors from the coaches. That Illinois game in particular put him on the national radar: six catches, 133 yards, and a 62-yard reception that showcased every bit of his downfield ability. He finished the year ranked ninth nationally among tight ends in receptions and was named to the Comeback Player of the Year Award Watch List. After that breakout campaign, Klare entered the transfer portal in December 2024 and landed at Ohio State. The move was widely viewed as a ticket to a bigger stage, and he delivered a solid if quieter season for the Buckeyes: 43 catches for 448 yards and two touchdowns across 14 games, 11 of which he started. He ranked third on the team in both receptions and receiving yards and earned first-team All-Big Ten recognition from both coaches and media. His career totals across Purdue and Ohio State stand at 116 receptions for 1,329 yards and six touchdowns in 32 games. Despite having another year of eligibility remaining, Klare elected to declare for the 2026 NFL Draft.

Strengths:
Loose, fluid athlete who can run the full route tree and bends with ease at the top of his stems to create natural separation from linebackers and safeties alike. Genuine vertical threat with the speed to climb past second-level defenders on seam routes. He burned safeties at Purdue on those 62-yard and 32-yard chunk plays that changed games. Excellent production on intermediate and deep targets throughout his career, consistently winning at the catch point and delivering reliable yardage on throws beyond ten yards. Attacks the ball after the catch with real intent. He turns upfield quickly, reduces angles on pursuing defenders, and fights through arm tackles to squeeze out extra yards. Reads zone coverage well and has a feel for settling into soft spots rather than drifting through them. Quarterbacks trust where he will be, and he rewards that trust consistently. Versatile alignment piece who lined up roughly evenly from in-line, slot, and wide positions at Ohio State, giving coordinators a moveable chess piece to exploit matchup advantages. Showed legitimate growth as a run blocker at Ohio State compared to his Purdue tape. He became more willing to fit up at the point of attack and improved noticeably when blocking on the move. Multi-sport background and former quarterback experience give him a natural feel for timing, spatial awareness, and how defensive structures try to take away throwing windows.

Weaknesses:
Blocking remains the clear question mark. His hand placement is inconsistent, he gets grabby at the point of attack, and he lacks the anchor strength to sustain against NFL-caliber edge defenders. Light for the position at 246 pounds with a frame that sits below average among tight end prospects. He will need to add functional mass without sacrificing the athleticism that makes him dangerous. Ball skills are uneven. He had a noticeable drop issue at Purdue and while the rate improved at Ohio State, he still fights certain throws rather than plucking them cleanly out of the air. Production and efficiency took a step backward at Ohio State. His yards per route run, average depth of target, and receiving grade all declined, and he failed to stand out at the NFL Combine, where he chose not to run or jump and looked average in pass-catching drills. Durability is worth monitoring. The 2023 ankle injury cost him most of that season, and his relatively light frame raises questions about how he will hold up absorbing contact from NFL defenders over a full 17-game schedule.

  Defense Faced Rating: 75%
  QB Rating When targeted: 100.3
  Hands: 79%
  Short Receiving: 82%
  Intermediate Routes: 88%
  Deep Threat: 87%
  Blocking: 63%

NFL Player Comparisons: ElijahArroyo - Miami (98%), IsaiahLikely - Coastal Carolina (93%), CadeStover - Ohio State (92%)

name: Logan Jones
scouting_report:
Background:
State champion in the shot put and discus before ever snapping a football at the college level, Logan Jones arrived at Iowa as a four-star recruit from Council Bluffs who initially lined up on the defensive line. The coaching staff moved him to center ahead of 2022, and that switch paid off immediately: Jones started all 13 games as a redshirt sophomore and never gave the job back. Over four seasons as Iowa's starting center, Jones racked up 50 career starts and steadily climbed the award lists. He earned third-team All-Big Ten honors in 2023, then first-team All-Big Ten in 2024 while anchoring a rushing attack that averaged nearly 200 yards per game. His unit helped the Hawkeyes rush for 200-plus yards in eight games that season, the program's best mark since 2015. The 2025 season was the crowning year. Jones won the Rimington Trophy, earned unanimous All-American status, and anchored an offensive line that took home the Joe Moore Award. He was a permanent team captain in each of his final three seasons. Bruce Feldman had already named him No. 7 on his annual Freaks list, and Jones backed that up at the combine: fastest 40 among all offensive linemen, second-best 3-cone and shuttle times in the position group.

Strengths:
Pass protection is his calling card; punch timing, hand placement, and mirror ability are all top-shelf. Sits in his base and lets rushers come to him rather than lunging or guessing. Picks up late A-gap blitzers as well as any center in this class, eyes never drop. Over his final two seasons, almost nobody got home clean through his gap in the passing game. Combine backed up the tape: tested as the most athletic center in this class across every drill. Quick enough to reach second-level landmarks and finish on linebackers in space. Fifty career starts and three years as team captain, clearly runs the show at the line. Brings real toughness and a physical edge despite his size limitations.

Weaknesses:
Run blocking is functional but inconsistent; doesn't generate much push at the point of attack. Undersized frame with short arms limits his ability to sustain blocks in tight quarters. Stout nose tackles with active hands can walk him back and control the rep. Struggles to hold ground on double teams when asked to be the anchor side. Age is a factor; he will turn 25 during his rookie season. Center-only value narrows his versatility and limits roster flexibility. Recovery range from gap to gap is restricted by his lack of length.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Defense Faced Rating: 77%
  Pass Blocking: 96%
  Run Blocking: 80%

NFL Player Comparisons: ZachTom - Wake Forest (94%), JaredWilson - Georgia (93%), JakeMajors - Texas (91%)

name: Aaron Anderson
scouting_report:
Background:
Aaron Anderson was a five-star recruit out of Edna Karr High School in New Orleans, rated as the top player in Louisiana for the Class of 2022 by 247Sports Composite. He was also a standout on the track, running a 10.77-second 100-meter dash as a prep athlete. Anderson originally signed with Alabama but injuries limited him to a single appearance as a true freshman, and he transferred to LSU in the spring of 2023. His first season in Baton Rouge was quiet. Anderson caught 12 passes for 59 yards across 11 games while handling return duties. The 2024 season was a different story entirely. He led LSU with 61 receptions for 884 yards and five touchdowns, ranking third in the SEC in catches and seventh in receiving yards. His biggest play came against Ole Miss, hauling in a 23-yard touchdown on fourth-and-five with 27 seconds left to force overtime in a 29-26 win. He also took a kickoff 100 yards to the house against Oklahoma. Anderson's 2025 junior season was cut short by a nagging knee issue that cost him the final three games. He finished with 33 catches for 398 yards and no touchdowns in nine appearances before declaring for the 2026 NFL Draft.

Strengths:
Top-end speed shows up on film with the ability to stack corners vertically. Runs disciplined routes from the slot with clean footwork at his break points. Good tempo and patience in his stems, rarely tipping off when the break is coming. Compact frame absorbs contact well; tough to bring down on initial hit after the catch. Willing worker over the middle who does not hear footsteps in traffic. Strong receiving marks across short, medium, and deep targets throughout his career. Proven kickoff returner with the vision and burst to create chunk plays.

Weaknesses:
Undersized with a limited catch radius that hurts him on contested and high-point throws. Hand-eye coordination is inconsistent; drop rate has been a recurring issue on film. Struggles to separate from physical press corners who get hands on him early. Blocking effort and technique fall well short of what NFL coordinators need. Never scored a touchdown in 2025 and was limited to nine games by knee trouble. Contested catch rate across his career sits well below average for the position.

  QB Rating When targeted: 98.6
  Hands: 69%
  Short Receiving: 82%
  Intermediate Routes: 83%
  Deep Threat: 87%
  Blocking: 60%
