name: Keyron Crawford
scouting_report:
Background:
Keyron Crawford didn't suit up for organized football until his senior year at Briarcrest Christian School. That single season was enough to turn heads: 78 tackles and 14 sacks earned him regional defensive MVP honors, along with invitations to both the AutoZone Liberty Bowl All-Star Game and Nashville All-American Bowl. The recruiting services took notice, with 247 Sports, Rivals, and ESPN all assigning him three-star ratings despite the minimal football resume. Crawford began his college career at Arkansas State, where he spent the 2022 and 2023 seasons developing his craft on the defensive line. After a quiet freshman campaign with 14 tackles across 10 games, he broke out as a sophomore with 44 tackles, 10.5 tackles for loss, 5.5 sacks, and a blocked field goal while starting all 13 contests. His best single-game performance came against Louisiana, where he racked up seven tackles. The production attracted SEC interest, and Crawford entered the transfer portal following that 2023 season. Auburn landed his commitment, and while his first year on the Plains saw him serving as a backup with 24 tackles in a reserve role, his senior season delivered a genuine breakthrough. Named a team captain, Crawford started all 12 games in 2025 and finished with 36 tackles, 9.5 tackles for loss, 5.0 sacks, one interception, and a fumble recovery. He ranked fourth in the SEC with 43 pressures according to Pro Football Focus and earned First Team All-SEC recognition from Phil Steele's Football News along with Third Team honors from the conference coaches. His late-season performance against Alabama was particularly notable, tallying five tackles and five quarterback hurries against the Crimson Tide.

Strengths:
Explosive first step off the snap generates immediate pressure, putting tackles on their heels before they can establish position in pass protection. Fluid hips and ankle flexibility allow him to bend and flatten around the edge, staying low through contact at the top of his rush arc. Outstanding lateral agility creates two-way go potential that makes him difficult to set up against when he's working edge-to-edge. Shows legitimate pop when his hands strike first, capable of jolting blockers backward and creating separation to disengage. Versatility in Auburn's "Buck" position demonstrated comfort dropping into zone coverage and handling responsibility in space. Year-over-year improvement from 2024 to 2025 shows the learning curve is still pointing upward for this late football bloomer. Pursuit speed and closing burst to the ball carrier creates problems for stretch runs and quarterback scrambles headed his direction. Physical at the line of scrimmage against tight ends attempting to release into routes, capable of disrupting timing with aggressive reroutes.

Weaknesses:
Struggles to disengage once offensive linemen get hands locked into his chest plate, lacking the counter moves to work free consistently. Rush approach can become one-dimensional and predictable, needs to develop better pace changes to keep tackles guessing at the snap. Run defense instincts remain raw, often late to recognize blocking schemes and slow to trigger against misdirection looks. Edge-setting ability is inconsistent against power run concepts, occasionally getting washed down when bigger linemen drive into him. Tackling technique needs refinement; tendency to launch from distance rather than breaking down leads to missed tackles in space.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Offense Rating: 78%
  Tackling: 51%
  Pass Rush: 89%
  Run Defense: 82%

NFL Player Comparisons: ColbyWooden - Auburn (96%), AustinBooker - Kansas (91%), ElijahRoberts - SMU (90%)

name: Eli Raridon
scouting_report:
Background:
The son of former Notre Dame offensive lineman Scott Raridon, Eli grew up around Fighting Irish football before following his father's path to South Bend. Valley High School in West Des Moines became his proving ground, where the lanky tight end hauled in 53 catches for 627 yards and 10 touchdowns as a senior while earning Elite All-Iowa Team recognition. He also shined on the hardwood, grabbing Second Team All-State honors as a basketball player following his junior year. The recruiting services couldn't quite agree on his ranking, with 247Sports calling him the 173rd prospect nationally while Rivals slotted him at 198, but all four major services awarded him four stars. His 5.8 Rivals grade and 0.97 247 recruit grade reflected his projection as a legitimate pass-catching weapon at the next level. That projection got delayed when Raridon tore his right ACL during his senior basketball season, then suffered the devastating blow of re-tearing the same ACL during practice in October 2022 as a true freshman. The double knee reconstruction cost him nearly two full seasons, limiting him to just five games before the injury and seven contests in 2023 as he worked his way back. His 2024 campaign finally showed what Notre Dame signed, as he appeared in all 16 games during the Irish's march to the national championship game, starting three times and catching 11 passes for 90 yards with two touchdowns. The 2025 season has become his showcase year, with Raridon starting all 12 games and finishing third on the team with 32 receptions for 482 yards. His career-best performance came against NC State when he torched the Wolfpack secondary for seven catches and 109 yards, and he opened the season by leading Notre Dame with 97 receiving yards at Miami. Across 36 career games and 1,293 offensive snaps, Raridon has evolved from an injury rehab project into one of college football's most intriguing vertical tight end weapons. The 79-inch frame that basketball coaches loved has translated differently to football, creating both advantages and challenges. Raridon carries his 251 pounds across that long frame without the thickness scouts typically want at the position, ranking in just the 24th percentile for tight end weight. What matters more than the stopwatch is how that speed plays on Sundays, and Raridon's tape suggests the straight-line velocity works better than the testing number implies. He entered Notre Dame as the program's highest-rated tight end recruit since 2018, and despite the injury setbacks, he's delivered enough production as a senior to warrant significant draft consideration.

Strengths:
Tears apart zone coverages with his length and feel for finding space, settling into holes between levels and converting short gains into chunk yardage consistently. Forces safeties to respect him vertically, which opens up the entire middle of the field and gives the offense an extra dimension defenses have to account for. Timing at the route break point keeps improving, snapping his head around on schedule and giving quarterbacks clean throwing windows to hit him in rhythm. Brings more physicality as a blocker than that lean build suggests, delivering functional pop at the second level and showing he'll get after people in the trenches. Grip strength catches you off guard, locking onto defenders when blocking and securing passes through contact even when defenders are draped all over him. Works seam routes intelligently by using those long strides and reading coverage shells, knowing when to throttle down versus when to keep pushing vertical. Can handle three-down work with his blend of receiving chops and blocking commitment, the kind of tight end who doesn't come off the field based on situation. Route stems getting more advanced as he figures out how to manipulate defenders with tempo changes and leverage positioning, steering coverage before breaking into open grass.

Weaknesses:
Two torn ACLs on the same knee creates real medical red flags that team doctors need to dig into before anyone commits draft capital to this kid. Gets too tall in his stance when blocking, letting shorter defenders get underneath his pads and knock him backward when they come with power. Route tree remains pretty limited from lack of reps, looks uncomfortable when asked to run option routes or make adjustments on the fly mid-play. Doesn't make much happen after the catch with below-average change of direction and balance through contact, gets tracked down from behind too often for comfort. Needs to pack serious weight onto those legs and hips to hold up blocking defensive ends every down, currently too slight for consistent pass protection duties.

  QB Rating When targeted: 98.3
  Hands: 72%
  Short Receiving: 75%
  Intermediate Routes: 74%
  Deep Threat: 85%
  Blocking: 59%

NFL Player Comparisons: TheoJohnson - Penn State (88%), LukeSchoonmaker - Michigan (85%), LukeMusgrave - Oregon State (82%)

His career-best performance came against NC State when he torched the Wolfpack secondary for seven catches and 109 yards, and he opened the season by leading Notre Dame with 97 receiving yards at Miami. Across 36 career games and 1,293 offensive snaps, Raridon has evolved from an injury rehab project into one of college football's most intriguing vertical tight end weapons. The 79-inch frame that basketball coaches loved has translated differently to football, creating both advantages and challenges. Raridon carries his 251 pounds across that long frame without the thickness scouts typically want at the position, ranking in just the 24th percentile for tight end weight. What matters more than the stopwatch is how that speed plays on Sundays, and Raridon's tape suggests the straight-line velocity works better than the testing number implies. He entered Notre Dame as the program's highest-rated tight end recruit since 2018, and despite the injury setbacks, he's delivered enough production as a senior to warrant significant draft consideration.
strengths: Tears apart zone coverages with his length and feel for finding space, settling into holes between levels and converting short gains into chunk yardage consistently. Forces safeties to respect him vertically, which opens up the entire middle of the field and gives the offense an extra dimension defenses have to account for. Timing at the route break point keeps improving, snapping his head around on schedule and giving quarterbacks clean throwing windows to hit him in rhythm. Brings more physicality as a blocker than that lean build suggests, delivering functional pop at the second level and showing he'll get after people in the trenches. Grip strength catches you off guard, locking onto defenders when blocking and securing passes through contact even when defenders are draped all over him. Works seam routes intelligently by using those long strides and reading coverage shells, knowing when to throttle down versus when to keep pushing vertical. Can handle three-down work with his blend of receiving chops and blocking commitment, the kind of tight end who doesn't come off the field based on situation. Route stems getting more advanced as he figures out how to manipulate defenders with tempo changes and leverage positioning, steering coverage before breaking into open grass.
weaknesses: Two torn ACLs on the same knee creates real medical red flags that team doctors need to dig into before anyone commits draft capital to this kid. Gets too tall in his stance when blocking, letting shorter defenders get underneath his pads and knock him backward when they come with power. Route tree remains pretty limited from lack of reps, looks uncomfortable when asked to run option routes or make adjustments on the fly mid-play. Doesn't make much happen after the catch with below-average change of direction and balance through contact, gets tracked down from behind too often for comfort. Needs to pack serious weight onto those legs and hips to hold up blocking defensive ends every down, currently too slight for consistent pass protection duties.
player_comparisons: TheoJohnson - Penn State (88%), LukeSchoonmaker - Michigan (85%), LukeMusgrave - Oregon State (82%)

name: Antonio Williams
scouting_report:
Background:
Antonio Williams came out of Dutch Fork High School in Columbia, South Carolina, where he was part of three state championship squads and lost just once across his entire prep career. A consensus four-star recruit ranked inside the top 85 nationally, Williams committed to Clemson in December 2021 and made an immediate splash as a true freshman in 2022, leading the Tigers with 56 catches for 604 yards and four touchdowns while earning Freshman All-America recognition. His sophomore year in 2023 was largely wiped out by a series of nagging injuries that held him to four regular-season games and a medical redshirt, though he returned for the Gator Bowl and delivered a clutch 16-yard catch on third-and-18 during Clemson's game-winning drive against Kentucky. Healthy again in 2024, Williams broke out with 75 catches, 904 yards, and 11 touchdowns while adding a rushing score, a passing touchdown, and a 9.65-yard punt return average. Williams played 10 games in 2025 before declaring for the draft, finishing with 55 catches for 604 yards and four scores. He left Clemson ranked fourth all-time in receptions (208) and tied fourth in touchdown catches (21), passing DeAndre Hopkins on the career receptions list in his final regular-season game.

Strengths:
Savvy route technician who uses tempo shifts and subtle body fakes to consistently win off the line. Tracks the deep ball well and adjusts his frame to make difficult grabs along the sideline. Absolutely zero hesitation working the middle of the field through heavy traffic. Natural feel for sitting in zone windows, giving his quarterback an easy landmark underneath. Explosive vertical jump translates to contested catch ability that belies his smaller frame. Reliable hands away from his body with strong concentration through contact at the catch point. Shifty and competitive after the catch, turns short throws into real yardage with vision and wiggle. Legitimate return game experience adds plug-and-play special teams value on day one.

Weaknesses:
Light frame with short arms will struggle against physical press coverage at the next level. Below-average play strength shows up as a liability when asked to block in the run game. Lost significant time to injuries in two of his four college seasons, raising durability flags. Good speed but lacks a true second gear to stack cornerbacks vertically on nine routes. Change of direction numbers suggest average short-area lateral agility in tight spaces. Occasional freelancing on his routes creates timing disruptions with the quarterback.

  QB Rating When targeted: 125.4
  Hands: 75%

Antonio Williams, 5-11/195 Wide Receiver Clemson

Antonio Williams Scouting Report By Charlie Campbell

Strengths: Dangerous speed receiver Fast, with sudden quickness Can generate separation with shear speed Easy acceleration Second gear speed Stretches the field Challenges defenses vertically Dangerous on posts and go routes Quick twitch Natural hands catcher Smooth route-runner Agressive at catch point Strong for his size Willing to go across the middle Natural hands Ready to compete immediately Creates mismatches with speed

Weaknesses: Small, undersized More of a No. 2 or 3 receiver Could have issues getting off jams versus pro corners Durability Missed games in multiple seasons

Prospect Summary: Clemson has produced some excellent wide receiver prospects for the NFL under head coach Dabo Swinney, and Williams will continue that tradition in 2026. As a freshman in 2022, Williams had an impressive debut with  56 catches for 604 yards and four touchdowns. The next season, his numbers dipped (21-208-2), but in 2024, Williams was superb for Clemson, with 75 catches for 904 yards and 11 touchdowns. In 2025, Williams had 55 receptions for 604 yards and four touchdowns while missing some time with injuries. For the NFL, Williams is a speed receiver with the ability to rip off a long reception on any given play. He is a fast receiver with easy acceleration to get downfield. Williams has a burst out of his breaks and a second gear that he uses to separate from defensive backs. With his speed, Williams is a threat to line up on the outside and take the top off of a defense. He is dangerous running deep posts and verticals along the sideline. With his quick twitch, Williams also could operate out of the slot in the NFL, along with being an X receiver on the outside. With his speed and athleticism, Williams is a smooth route-runner and is consistently able to create separation from man coverage. While Williams is not a big receiver, he is aggressive at the catch point and will out-fight defensive backs for the ball. For his size, he shows strength and toughness while also looking fearless in the middle of the field. After the catch, Williams shows nice ability to run away from the defense, and he has some toughness to fight off defensive backs. Williams has good hands and shows some natural hand-catching ability. With his skill set, Williams looks like a potential starter in the NFL. He may not become a high-volume No. 1 receiver, but he could be an excellent No. 2 or 3 wideout. In the 2026 NFL Draft, Williams could be a day-two pick. Check out my latest 2026 NFL Mock Draft to see where Williams is projected.

Prospect Comparison: Hollywood Brown. Like Brown, Williams is a smaller speed receiver with durability issues.

name: Drew Allar
scouting_report:
Background:
The record books at Medina High School in Ohio needed rewriting by the time Drew Allar finished his prep career under head coach Larry Laird. Standing 6-foot-5 and 235 pounds even as a high schooler, Allar was a three-year varsity starter who piled up 9,103 career passing yards and 98 touchdowns, both school and county records. His senior season was absurd: 305-of-511 for 4,444 yards and 48 touchdowns, numbers that earned him Ohio's Mr. Football award, Max Preps Ohio Player of the Year honors, and a first-team all-state nod. He was invited to the Adidas All-America Bowl and earned Elite 11 honors. The recruiting services couldn't agree on a star rating, but they all agreed he was special. 247Sports tabbed him a five-star and the top quarterback in the 2022 class; ESPN slotted him as a four-star and the No. 2 quarterback nationally; Rivals gave him four stars and ranked him No. 6 at the position. Allar arrived at Penn State and saw the field immediately as a true freshman in 2022, appearing in 10 games behind Sean Clifford and completing 35-of-60 passes for 344 yards with four touchdowns. When Clifford departed, Allar took the reins in 2023 and opened his tenure with a bang, setting an FBS record by completing 311 consecutive attempts without an interception to start his career. That sophomore campaign produced 2,631 yards, 25 touchdowns, and just two interceptions across 13 starts while leading Penn State to a 10-3 record and a Peach Bowl appearance against Ole Miss. The efficiency was staggering: his 12.5-to-1 touchdown-to-interception ratio ranked second nationally, and he earned All-Big Ten honorable mention honors from both the coaches and media. Year three brought a leap in production. Under new offensive coordinator Andy Kotelnicki, Allar completed 66.5% of his passes for 3,327 yards and 24 touchdowns with eight interceptions, adding 302 rushing yards and six scores on the ground. He was named a team captain, earned Davey O'Brien Award semifinalist recognition, and landed on the Johnny Unitas Golden Arm Award Top 10 list. Penn State went 13-3, captured a spot in the College Football Playoff, and advanced all the way to the national semifinal before falling 27-24 to Notre Dame in the Orange Bowl. Allar returned for 2025 with first-round aspirations, but the year unraveled quickly. After a 3-0 start against weak competition, three consecutive losses to Oregon, UCLA, and Northwestern dropped the bottom out. To make matters worse, Allar suffered a season-ending lower-body injury in the Northwestern game, his fifth appearance of the season, closing the door on his college eligibility. He finishes his Penn State career first all-time in completion percentage (62.9%) and interception percentage (1.19%), third in touchdown passes (53), and sixth in passing yardage (6,302).

Strengths:
Throws with effortless velocity and spin rate that make NFL-caliber windows accessible; balls jump off his hand on intermediate crossers and dig routes with the kind of zip that collapses throwing lanes in real time. At 6-foot-5 and 235 pounds, he has the frame and build that offensive coordinators draw up on whiteboards when they close their eyes and imagine their franchise quarterback. When he trusts his base and drives through his front foot, he can drop the ball into a bucket 40 yards downfield with a trajectory that takes away the safety's closing angle entirely. Competitive runner who will lower his shoulder into a linebacker on third-and-short without flinching; carried designed read-option looks and picked up 302 rushing yards and six touchdowns as a junior. Can manipulate his arm slot from a standard three-quarter delivery to sidearm or over-the-top depending on the rush lane, giving him the ability to muscle throws out around edge pressure when the pocket tightens. Put the tape on from West Virginia in 2024 and you see a quarterback capable of rhythm timing throws with real anticipation, firing to vacated spots before the coverage arrives on multiple reps. Takes care of the football at an elite rate across his career; a 1.19% interception rate over 843 career attempts speaks to a conservative decision tree that, at minimum, keeps the offense from bleeding out. His release is quick and compact for a quarterback his size, grading out well above average in release speed, which gives him the ability to survive tighter pockets than most 6-foot-5 passers can.

Weaknesses:
Short-area accuracy remains a legitimate concern; his ball placement on screens, quick slants, and check-downs grades out well below what NFL coordinators demand from their starting quarterbacks. Footwork is a constant battle, and when his base deteriorates under pressure he becomes a completely different thrower, reverting to flat-footed deliveries that sail on him or die at the receiver's ankles. Field processing and post-snap reads remain alarmingly raw for a three-year starter; he bird-dogs his primary target, and when the first look isn't there, the eyes drop and the play falls apart instead of working to the second or third option. Interior pressure wrecks him in a way that's unusual for a pocket passer this size; rather than climbing the pocket or stepping up into throwing lanes, he drifts backward and to the side, compounding the problem. The 2025 regression stripped away the benefit of the doubt: without Tyler Warren manufacturing easy completions underneath, Allar's big-time throw rate cratered to a career-worst 2.5% while his turnover-worthy play rate climbed, exposing a dependence on scheme and supporting cast rather than individual growth.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Defense Faced Rating: 76%
  Release Speed: 81%
  Short Passing: 68%
  Medium Passing: 80%
  Long Passing: 78%
  Rush/Scramble: 75%

NFL Player Comparisons: WillLevis - Kentucky (73%), JoeMilton III - Tennessee (72%), WillHoward - Ohio State (65%)

Drew Allar, 6-4/225 Quarterback Penn State

Drew Allar Scouting Report By Charlie Campbell

Strengths: Tall Thick build Strong arm Can fire fastballs into tight windows Easily can push the ball vertically Flashes accuracy Good ball adjustment Advanced body control Reliable hands Late hands to make receptions Natural hands catcher Makes contested catches Concentration Has some athleticism Experienced

Weaknesses: Inconsistent accuracy Lacks precision with his placement Field vision needs to improve Needs to work faster through progressions Will lock onto primary read too often Will force throws into coverage Gets tricked by coverage Footwork needs to improve Consistently fell short of leading his team to big wins in his college career Coming off serious leg injury

Prospect Summary: Allar flashed some ability as a freshman in 2022 while serving as the backup quarterback for Penn State. In 2023, Allar took over as the starter and had an impressive debut, completing 60 percent of his passes for 2,631 yards with 25 touchdowns and only two interceptions.  In 2024, Allar completed 67 percent of his passes for 3,327 yards with 24 touchdowns passing, six rushing scores, and eight interceptions while helping the Nittany Lions reach the college football playoff. In 2025, the season went off the rails for the Nittany Lions as Allar got hurt and head coach James Franklin was fired. Allar completed 65 percent of his passes for 1,100 yards with eight touchdowns and three interceptions before suffering a season-ending leg injury against Northwestern on October 11th. There is no doubt that Allar has NFL talent and the skill set to be a starter at the next level. Allar is tall with a thick build and a little bit of athleticism to move in the pocket. While he won’t be a running threat at the pro level, Allar could be a dangerous pocket passer. He has a good size and pocket presence to hang tough in the face of the rush. Allar also does a nice job of manipulating that pocket and keeping his eyes downfield while he climbs or rolls to the side. With his size and skill set, Allar has the look of a pro starter. Allar definitely has NFL arm talent. He has a powerful arm that can fire missiles into tight windows, and his quick release with ball velocity allows him to zip passes past coverage and into his receiver despite a lack of separation. Allar has plenty of arm to push the ball vertically downfield and can drill the deep-out routes without any issue. Allar flashes accuracy, but he is inconsistent. Occasionally, Allar will have some passes off the mark, and he needs to get better at throwing the ball with precision in the short to intermediate part of the field. For the NFL, there are areas that Allar needs to improve. The first point of emphasis will be field vision. Allar needs to improve on moving his eyes and working through his progressions. He locks onto his primary read too often, but that isn’t all his fault, as his offense runs a lot of plays with a quick pass after the ball is snapped. Because of this emphasis on throwing to the primary read, Allar will also force some throws into coverage. Allar gets confused and tricked by coverage at times, so that is something he will need to get better at identifying for the next level. Allar’s feet will need work, as he does not follow through on many throws. While he can move around some, Allar will never be a true running threat in the NFL. The physical skill is there for Allar to be a pro starter, but he does need a lot of coaching and development to get there. In the 2026 NFL Draft, Allar is probably a day three-pick and he could end up going in the middle portion of day three of the draft. Check my latest 2026 NFL Mock Draft to see where Allar is currently projected.

Prospect Comparison: Joe Flacco. In terms of skill set and style of play, Allar reminds me of Flacco.

name: Tyreak Sapp
scouting_report:
Background:
Tyreak Sapp has steadily climbed the Gators' depth chart since arriving as a four-star recruit from powerhouse St. Thomas Aquinas High School. After redshirting in 2021, Sapp gradually increased his production over three seasons, culminating in a breakout 2024 campaign where he led the Gators with seven sacks and 13 tackles-for-loss while forcing two fumbles. Standing 6'3" and weighing 272 pounds, Sapp possesses an NFL-ready frame that combines power and functional movement skills. His journey through Florida's program showcases steady development-starting with special teams contributions as a redshirt freshman in 2022 (20 tackles), becoming a rotational piece in 2023 (24 tackles, 2 sacks), before blossoming into a disruptive force in 2024. What's particularly impressive about Sapp's senior season was his consistency and timing of impact plays, with critical fourth-quarter sacks against ranked opponents like Ole Miss and clutch tackles for loss against LSU that changed game outcomes. The numbers only tell part of the story with Sapp, whose technical refinement caught the eye of analysts throughout the 2024 season. His 90.4 PFF grade ranked eighth among all FBS edge rushers and was the highest for any Florida defender since Jonathon Greenard in 2019. More telling was his dominant 94.8 run defense grade, which ranked among the elite in college football.

Strengths:
Exceptional hand technique with shocking power at the point of attack - consistently stacks and sheds blockers with violent initial punch that rocks offensive linemen backward. Plays with outstanding leverage against the run, maintaining a powerful base while anchoring against double teams - showed this repeatedly against Texas A&M's zone scheme. Natural block recognition skills help him diagnose plays quickly - rarely gets caught upfield and maintains disciplined gap integrity even against misdirection. Nasty edge setter who converts power to violence when engaging tackles - consistently forces runners to redirect into pursuit or bounce outside to less favorable angles. Developed an effective cross-chop as his go-to pass rush move - timed it beautifully for critical fourth quarter sack against Ole Miss that forced punt. Demonstrates surprising fluidity when working laterally - can scrape down the line against zone runs while maintaining position to finish plays. Scheme versatile frame allows him to reduce inside on passing downs - has enough quickness to exploit guards while maintaining power to bull rush. Reliable finisher who drives through ball carriers - missed tackle rate has decreased each season showing technical improvement and growing confidence.

Weaknesses:
First-step explosiveness is merely adequate by NFL standards - won't threaten elite tackles with pure speed around the corner at the next level. Rush plan can stall when initial move is countered - needs to develop more seamless transitions between primary and secondary moves against savvy tackles. Closing burst in space is middling - won't consistently run down backs or mobile QBs who break contain and get to the perimeter. Can get caught playing tall when navigating traffic - occasionally loses his leverage advantage when working through congestion between the tackles. Sack production (7 in 2024) suggests a complementary rusher rather than a dominant edge presence - may never develop into a double-digit sack artist.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Offense Rating: 81%
  Tackling: 66%
  Pass Rush: 72%
  Run Defense: 82%

NFL Player Comparisons: MarshawnKneeland - Western Michigan (90%), DavinVann - NC State (88%), DaelinHayes - Notre Dame (81%)

name: Jonah Coleman
scouting_report:
Background:
A four-year varsity career at Lincoln High School in Stockton, California, produced 3,319 rushing yards and 58 touchdowns on just 334 carries, plus 55 receptions for 799 yards and 10 more scores. Despite those numbers, Jonah Coleman was a consensus three-star recruit who landed at Arizona in the 2022 class. He appeared in all 25 games across two seasons with the Wildcats, accumulating 1,243 rushing yards and nine touchdowns, with his sophomore year featuring a breakout 179-yard, 11-carry performance against Colorado. When head coach Jedd Fisch left for Washington, Coleman followed. He became just the 15th Husky to eclipse 1,000 rushing yards in 2024, finishing with 1,053 yards and 10 touchdowns while earning third-team All-Big Ten honors. His 2025 senior season started strong, with 177 yards and two touchdowns against Colorado State and a five-touchdown game against UC Davis that tied a program record held since 1950 by Hugh McElhenny. A knee injury suffered against Wisconsin in November limited him down the stretch, but he still managed 758 rushing yards and 15 touchdowns along with 31 catches for 354 yards in 12 appearances. He closes his college career with 3,054 rushing yards and 34 touchdowns across 50 games.

Strengths:
Compact, low-center-of-gravity frame generates natural leverage and lets him churn through arm tackles consistently. Outstanding feel for zone concepts; patient enough to let blocks develop, then decisive when he commits to a lane. One fumble on 396 career touches is the kind of ball security that earns late-game trust immediately. Contact balance and leg drive keep him moving forward after initial contact; rarely goes down on first hit. Soft, natural hands out of the backfield; transitions from catch to run without losing momentum or rhythm. Played behind a patchwork offensive line at Washington and still produced, showing real mental toughness. Short-area burst gets him through creases quickly; reaches full speed in two steps once he picks a hole. Red zone production is proven with 34 career rushing touchdowns; pad level and determination shine in short yardage.

Weaknesses:
Top-end speed caps his big-play potential; faster defenders will track him down at the second level. Pass protection technique needs work; willing to engage but tends to lunge rather than anchor and sustain. Height limits his vision in traffic and ability to diagnose complex fronts over the pile at the line. Lateral explosiveness is average; not going to consistently create with sudden change-of-direction moves in tight quarters. Route tree is limited to underneath options and shallow crossers; not a separation threat as a receiver. Left knee injury derailed his 2025 stretch run; his YPC dropped from 4.9 to limited duty over the final month.

name: Mike Washington
scouting_report:
Background:
A 92-yard run at Bowling Green that still stands as Buffalo's longest in program history hinted at the kind of speed Mike Washington Jr. always had, but it took years and three schools before the rest of the football world caught on. He came out of Cicero-North Syracuse High School in New York as a two-way player who racked up 1,423 rushing yards and 15 touchdowns as a junior while contributing 31 tackles and three interceptions on defense, earning All-State honors and MVP of the Regional Championship Game. ESPN gave him three stars, but 247Sports had him as a two-star prospect, and he headed to Buffalo in 2021 without much fanfare outside of Central New York. Washington redshirted his first year in Buffalo, carrying the ball just 23 times across three games for 132 yards and a score. By 2022, though, he had taken over as the Bulls' lead rusher, piling up 625 yards and seven touchdowns while adding 23 catches out of the backfield. His 2023 campaign was less eventful with 362 yards as a rotational piece, and he entered the transfer portal after the season, landing at New Mexico State for 2024. With the Aggies, he picked up steam again: 713 yards, eight touchdowns, and a pair of 100-yard games, including a 152-yard outburst against Western Kentucky. The biggest leap came when Washington transferred to Arkansas for his fifth-year senior season in 2025. Starting all 12 games for the Razorbacks, he became just the 16th player in program history to crack 1,000 rushing yards, finishing with 1,070 yards and eight touchdowns on 167 carries while chipping in 28 receptions for 226 yards and another score. He strung together consecutive 100-yard games against Tennessee (131 yards) and Texas A&M (147 yards), the first Razorback to accomplish that against SEC opponents since Alex Collins did it in 2015. His performance at the Senior Bowl turned heads, and then his NFL Combine showing blew the doors off entirely: a 4.33-second 40-yard dash (the fastest by a running back since 2024), a 39-inch vertical, and a 10-foot-8 broad jump.

Strengths:
Rare size-speed combination at 223 pounds with verified elite straight-line speed that translates to consistent chunk plays at the second level and beyond. Runs downhill with real conviction and forward lean, consistently churning out yards after contact and punishing defensive backs who try to play hero in the hole. One-cut runner who trusts his reads, plants, and goes, turning designed inside zone and gap concepts into explosive gains because he refuses to waste time behind the line. Led the SEC with 16 plays of 20-plus yards in 2025, proving the big-play ability isn't just a combine trick; it shows up against quality competition on Saturdays. Loose hips and smoother lateral movement than you'd expect from a 223-pound frame, allowing him to glide through interior traffic and swerve past second-level defenders. Reliable hands on checkdowns and swing passes with 28 catches in his final season, giving coordinators a usable weapon on the edges and underneath coverage. Physical finisher who drives through arm tackles and falls forward; the Texas tape is a highlight reel in itself, including putting safety Michael Taaffe flat on his back in the run game. Combine and Senior Bowl performances validated what the tape already suggested: this kid competes with a chip on his shoulder and rises to the moment when the lights get brightest.

Weaknesses:
Ball security is a real red flag with ten career fumbles across his last three seasons of significant action, including three during his 2025 campaign at Arkansas. Pass protection is well below the standard needed for a three-down role right now; he lunges and bends at the waist rather than sitting down and striking with leverage. Can be a beat late to trigger through the hole on outside zone concepts, needing extra gathering steps to redirect that work against the timing NFL defensive fronts demand. Not a sudden or elusive runner in tight quarters, lacking the ankle-breaking wiggle to make defenders miss when the box gets crowded and space disappears. Route-running beyond simple checkdowns and screens remains raw, which will cap his involvement in passing situations until he expands his understanding of route concepts.

  Rushing: 86%
  Break Tackles: 83%
  Receiving/Hands: 63%
  Pass Blocking: 40%
  Run Blocking: 56%

name: Nate Boerkircher
scouting_report:
Background:
Nobody handed Nate Boerkircher anything. The Aurora, Nebraska product arrived in Lincoln as a walk-on out of a Class C-1 high school program, where he had earned all-state honors from the Omaha World-Herald and helped lead the Huskies to a perfect 13-0 season and state championship as a junior. He was also a second-team all-state basketball player, averaging nearly 17 points and over 8 rebounds per game as a senior. His only scholarship offer came from Chadron State. He chose to bet on himself at Nebraska instead. The early years in the Husker program tested that bet. After a redshirt in 2020, Boerkircher appeared in just three games in 2021 and caught a single pass. Even as he worked his way into four starts as a redshirt freshman in 2022, the stat line remained modest: six catches, 52 yards, one touchdown. The 2023 and 2024 seasons were more of the same from a volume standpoint, though his 2024 campaign showed a flash of what was coming, with six catches for 102 yards and a 17.0 yards-per-reception average. Throughout those four years, he earned Academic All-Big Ten honors and completed a finance degree in May 2024, the kind of detail that tells you about the person beyond the shoulder pads. The real chapter started when Boerkircher transferred to Texas A&M for his final season of eligibility. Competing against multiple tight ends the Aggies brought in, including Theo Melin Ohrstrom, he won the job and delivered the most productive year of his career: 19 catches for 198 yards and three receiving touchdowns, plus a rushing score. The signature moment came against Notre Dame, when Boerkircher hauled in a game-winning touchdown. His performance throughout the 2025 season earned him an invitation to the Senior Bowl, a fitting reward for a player who spent six college seasons grinding from the bottom of the depth chart to the top of it.

Strengths:
Punishing run blocker who plays with real nastiness on the move, consistently looking to put defenders on the ground and finishing through the whistle every single snap. Catches nearly everything thrown his way, posting an 86% catch rate in his final season on 22 targets, with soft hands that pluck the ball cleanly away from his frame. Understands zone coverage concepts at an advanced level, finding soft spots in the middle of the field with natural feel and giving his quarterback a trustworthy target in traffic. Versatile alignment profile, taking snaps inline, as an H-back, from the slot, and split wide, giving offensive coordinators multiple ways to deploy him on game day. Brings real pop to his initial punch when engaged on the move, using heavy hands to jolt linebackers and safeties as a lead blocker in the screen game and on outside runs. Competitive and physical at the catch point, winning contested situations and showing the body control to adjust to off-target throws without losing balance or the ball. Walk-on-to-starter trajectory speaks to a relentless work ethic and mental toughness that translates directly to grinding through NFL special teams and earning a bigger role over time. Showed encouraging route stem awareness in his final college season, manipulating defensive leverage and using subtle head fakes to create windows underneath and over the middle.

Weaknesses:
Will turn 25 during his rookie season after spending six years in college, leaving a shorter window for development compared to younger prospects at the position. Career receiving sample is alarmingly thin for evaluation purposes, with just 38 total catches across five active seasons, making it hard to project his ceiling as a pass catcher with confidence. Lacks the explosive burst and lateral agility to threaten defenses after the catch or consistently win against man coverage at the next level. Tends to bend at the waist and get narrow with his base when blocking inline, allowing defensive linemen to get into his chest and control the point of attack. Hands can arrive late in pass protection, causing his anchor to set a beat behind and leaving him vulnerable to power rushers who get an early push off the snap.

  QB Rating When targeted: 140.4
  Hands: 74%
  Short Receiving: 70%
  Intermediate Routes: 81%
  Deep Threat: 00%
  Blocking: 67%

name: Tanner Koziol
scouting_report:
Background:
Tanner Koziol arrived at Ball State out of Bloomingdale, Illinois as a two-star recruit who attended three different high schools before settling at Mt. Vernon, where he broke the school's single-season touchdown record as a senior wide receiver. He caught seven touchdowns as a true freshman in 2022, tying for the fifth-most among FBS tight ends that season. His sophomore year was quieter with 34 catches for 290 yards, but his junior campaign saw production explode to 94 receptions for 839 yards and eight scores, leading Ball State in catches and becoming the first Cardinal tight end to do so since 1989. That breakout junior season earned first-team Group of 5 All-American honors and drew transfer interest from Wisconsin, where Koziol initially committed before ultimately landing at Houston for his senior year. With the Cougars in the Big 12, he posted 74 receptions for 727 yards and six touchdowns, leading all FBS tight ends in catches and earning a Mackey Award semifinalist nod, Biletnikoff Watch List placement, and Pro Football Network All-America first-team recognition. He finished his career with 237 receptions, 2,236 yards, and 24 touchdowns across 49 games.

Strengths:
Outstanding length and catch radius at 6'6" that consistently wins above the rim in contested situations. Soft, natural hands that gather the ball cleanly away from his body on both short and deep targets. Receives with strong focus through contact, hauling in a high rate of contested throws over his career. Tested with excellent lower-body explosiveness at the Combine, and it shows on tape in his ability to high-point throws. Ran a full route tree at Houston, lining up in-line, in the slot, and split wide across formations. Dependable target underneath with good feel for finding soft spots against zone coverage on intermediate routes. Head fakes and subtle body movements at the top of routes create just enough hesitation from defenders. Durable over four seasons, logging nearly 2,500 offensive snaps and catching a pass in 27 straight games.

Weaknesses:
Lacks short-area quickness and burst to separate cleanly from athletic defenders at the break point. Route tempo is too uniform, making it easy for physical corners and safeties to pattern his releases. Run blocking is a clear weakness, with poor leverage and limited anchor strength at the point of attack. Too easy to reroute off the line with a forceful jam, disrupting his timing within the route. Weighed in well below the tight end average at the Combine, and the lack of mass shows in his anchor. Average play speed limits his ability to stack defenders vertically or create separation on deep routes.

  QB Rating When targeted: 110.1

name: Aamil Wagner
scouting_report:
Background:
Wagner grew up in Yellow Springs, Ohio and played his high school ball at Wayne under head coach Roosevelt Mukes. He was a consensus four-star recruit, ranked inside the top 120 nationally by both ESPN and 247Sports, and earned invitations to the Polynesian Bowl and All-American Bowl. He was also a standout in track and field, placing third in Ohio's Division I shot put at the state championships. He arrived at Notre Dame in 2022 and redshirted his first year. In 2023 he saw the field in seven games as the reserve right tackle on an offensive line group that earned Joe Moore Award semifinalist recognition. That limited action totaled just 52 snaps, but it set the table for what came next. Wagner stepped into the starting right tackle role in 2024 and never came off the field, starting all 16 games during Notre Dame's run to the national championship game. He was named a team captain heading into 2025, started all 12 games, and earned multiple character-based award nominations including the Jason Witten Collegiate Man of the Year and the Campbell Trophy.

Strengths:
Good arm length and active hands help him frame up and reset against rushers. Delivers a well-timed pass punch that maximizes his reach advantage off the snap. Allowed just three sacks across nearly 900 career pass-blocking snaps at Notre Dame. Excellent timed speed for a tackle gives him a quick first stride when jump-setting edge rushers. Showed clear improvement in pass protection technique over the course of his junior year. Comfortable identifying and picking up stunts and delayed blitz packages pre-snap. That straight-line speed helps him cover ground to the second level on zone combinations.

Weaknesses:
Light frame for the position and carries weight high through his torso. Run blocking took a noticeable step back during his 2025 junior season. Upright pad level limits his ability to generate movement at the point of attack. Footwork gets him in trouble; tends to up-kick and step under himself out of his stance. Body control and balance break down when redirecting against inside counter moves. Struggles adjusting to moving targets in space, particularly on reach blocks and pull assignments. Below-average lower body power reduces his capacity to displace defenders in the ground game.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Defense Faced Rating: 76%
  Pass Blocking: 81%
  Run Blocking: 71%

NFL Player Comparisons: TylanGrable - UCF (88%), CalebRogers - Texas Tech (87%), RyanHayes - Michigan (87%)

name: Beau Stephens
scouting_report:
Background:
Five-year letterman, two-way lineman, wrestler, basketball player, and team captain from sophomore year on at Blue Springs High School in Missouri. Beau Stephens did all of it before the recruiting services weighed in: 247Sports tagged him as a four-star prospect with a 0.90 grade, Rivals gave him four stars and a 5.8 rating, and though ESPN was less enthusiastic at three stars, the consensus was clear enough to land him in Iowa City as part of the 2021 class. As a senior he earned All-Conference honors on both sides of the ball, racking up 19 tackles with seven for loss and a sack while also anchoring the offensive front. Stephens redshirted in 2021 before earning the starting right guard job as a redshirt freshman in 2022, starting 10 games. That first year of real action was bumpy. He dealt with inconsistency, penalties, and some rough outings in pass protection that you'd expect from a young interior lineman learning on the fly. Then 2023 dealt him a tough hand. An injury limited him to just five games and 70 snaps, effectively wiping out an entire developmental season. He came back in 2024 and reclaimed the starting left guard spot for 12 games, earning the team's Comeback Player of the Year award and helping Iowa's ground game average nearly 200 yards per contest, the program's best mark since 2002. The 2025 season is where Stephens put it all together. He started all 13 games at left guard and was named a first-team AP All-American, first-team All-Big Ten by coaches, and helped Iowa's offensive line win the Joe Moore Award. He was the highest-graded guard in a power conference according to Pro Football Focus. The unit paved the way for five games with 200-plus rushing yards, including a 310-yard explosion against UAlbany and a 245-yard performance in a statement win over Penn State. Iowa was one of only two programs to place two offensive players on the AP first team, with Stephens joining center Logan Jones. In total, Stephens leaves Iowa City with 34 career starts, 1,764 offensive snaps, and a decorated final chapter that turned him from an under-the-radar interior lineman into a legitimate NFL draft prospect.

Strengths:
Fires off the snap with violent intent on combination blocks, generating real pop on his second and third steps to uproot defenders at the point of attack. Owns a nasty snatch-trap counter that flips the leverage battle when rushers try to walk him back with a bull rush or long-arm technique. Rarely beaten through his chest in pass protection thanks to a sturdy natural anchor that lets him absorb and re-set against power. Timing and assignment consistency in the run game grade out among the best in this guard class, with very few missed fits or blown calls on tape. Plays with a chippy, physical edge that shows up in his finishing effort; he wants to put people on the ground and plays through the whistle. Core and upper body strength allow him to latch onto three-techniques, torque them sideways, and steer them completely out of run lanes. Comfortable climbing to the second level with enough speed for his size, and he takes smart angles to arrive at linebackers with force. Remarkably clean penalty sheet over his final two seasons, showing the kind of discipline and body control that coaches trust in critical situations.

Weaknesses:
Below-average arm length for the position limits his ability to control defenders at range and forces him to win with body positioning instead. Bends at the waist rather than the knees in pass sets, which exposes him to swim moves and leaves his pads too high for leverage battles. Lateral agility and mirror ability are just okay; quicker interior rushers with counter moves can win the edge before he redirects his feet. Gets caught lunging and overextending into blocks in the run game, which lets defenders slip off his grasp at the worst possible moment. Injury history is a legitimate flag, with missed time in 2022, a lost 2023 season, two missed games in 2024, and dinged-up exits in 2025.

name: Javon Kilgore
scouting_report:
Javon Kilgore, 6-1/210 Safety South Carolina

Javon Kilgore Scouting Report By Charlie Campbell

Strengths: Good zone coverage strong safety Instinctive Closing speed Eats up ground in a hurry Good size, speed Quality ball skills Could play some man on slot receivers, tight ends Attacks ball carriers Happy to get physical Will dish out some violence and hit hard Looks ready to compete Good traits to contribute on special teams

Weaknesses: More of a strong safety Too many missed tackles Could have problems with fast slot receivers

Prospect Summary: The Gamecocks have produced some quality NFL defensive backs in recent years, and Kilgore will keep that tradition alive in 2026 or 2027. Kilgore was an impact player immediately for the Gamecocks and was a freshman All-American in 2023 when he totaled 76 tackles with one forced fumble, one interception, and six passes broken up. As a sophomore, Kilgore improved in pass coverage, totaling 5 interceptions, 5 pass breakups, and 48 tackles. In 2025, Kilgore had 54 tackles with 10 passes broken up and two interceptions. In pass coverage, Kilgore is a skilled zone corner who uses his speed to eat up a lot of ground. He has good instincts and vision to put him around the ball and disrupt passing lanes. His recognition and instincts put him around the ball and led to him being in position for his impressive interception total in 2024. Kilgore is dangerous in zone in the short to intermediate part of the field, and he has the athleticism to play some man coverage on slot receivers and tight ends. Kilgore is not a natural fit as a deep free safety. He is best as a nickel corner and strong safety. In the ground game, Kilgore attacks the ball carrier and does not show any hesitancy to come downhill to make a tackle. Kilgore’s tackling is at a good starting point, as he does look to wrap up the legs and not go for a bone-rattling hit without wrapping up the ball carrier. Kilgore has solid size and strength to contribute to run defense in the NFL, but he needs to fix problems with missed tackles. He also has issues taking on and getting off blocks. In the 2026 NFL Draft, Kilgore could be a day-two pick. Check my latest 2026 NFL Mock Draft to see where Kilgore is currently projected to go.

Prospect Comparison: Cam Bynum. In terms of skill set and style of play, Kilgore reminds me of Bynum.

name: Zane Durant
scouting_report:
Background:
The Florida native has steadily risen through the ranks at Happy Valley since arriving as a four-star recruit from Lake Nona High School. Durant wasted no time making his presence felt, appearing in all 13 games as a true freshman before earning 11 starts his sophomore campaign where he notched 17 tackles and 5.5 TFLs. That sophomore season offered a glimpse of his disruptive potential when he recorded a monster game against Northwestern with three tackles for loss - becoming the first Penn State defensive tackle to hit that mark against a Big Ten opponent since 2016. Durant's trajectory continued upward during his junior season, where he started all 16 games and registered career-highs across the board with 42 tackles, 11 tackles for loss, and 3 sacks. The interior defender turned heads with multi-TFL performances against Northwestern, Illinois, and Minnesota, showcasing the quick-twitch athleticism that has NFL scouts intrigued. His consistent production earned him All-Big Ten honorable mention recognition from both coaches and media, validating his development from rotational piece to full-time difference-maker. What truly separates Durant is the athletic profile packed into his somewhat undersized frame. His eye-popping testing numbers landed him on Bruce Feldman's Freaks List at #38 in 2023, where he turned heads with a blazing 4.66 forty-yard dash, 4.44 shuttle, and 9'10" broad jump - elite numbers that hint at the explosive first step that shows up consistently on game film. After flirting with the 2025 NFL Draft process, Durant opted to return for his senior season, setting the stage for what could be a draft-stock-boosting campaign as the anchor of Penn State's interior defensive line.

Strengths:
Athletic testing profile belongs to a first-round pick, not a Day Three prospect. First step off the snap is among the quickest you will find at the position. Lateral agility shows up on tape when working stunts and twist games. Low pad level is natural, not coached, giving him consistent leverage at the snap. Tracks screens and chases down perimeter plays with real closing burst. Versatile alignment history at Penn State across multiple interior techniques. Disruptive penetrator who collapses the pocket quickly when slanting into a gap.

Weaknesses:
Production never matched the athletic profile across four years of college tape. Short arms and compact frame make it hard to keep blockers off his chest. Gets driven off spots by double teams and coordinated combo blocks. Tackling has been unreliable, with too many missed attempts in 2023 and 2024. Pass rush plan is thin; stalls out when the initial burst gets countered. Senior year grading went backward, not forward, in run defense and overall play. Overpenetrates gaps at times, opening cutback lanes against zone runs.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Offense Rating: 80%
  Tackling: 60%
  Pass Rush: 69%
  Run Defense: 68%

NFL Player Comparisons: JordanJefferson - LSU (73%), ScottMatlock - Boise State (71%), OsaOdighizuwa - UCLA (68%)

name: Mason Reiger
scouting_report:
Background:
Mason Reiger (pronounced RYE-ger) was a walk-on at Louisville out of suburban Chicago, where he earned an honorable mention all-conference recognition in high school while captaining his team and leading them in tackles. He arrived in Louisville as a developmental body on the defensive line, redshirted his true freshman year after a single appearance against Virginia in 2020, and then slowly clawed his way into the rotation over the next three seasons. The production came in stages. As a redshirt freshman in 2021, Reiger logged nine tackles and two sacks across nine games, flashing enough juice off the edge to earn more snaps. His redshirt sophomore year in 2022 saw him play all 13 games with 18 tackles and 7.0 TFLs, though the sack numbers dipped to just one. The breakout arrived in 2023: 22 tackles, 5.0 sacks, and 6.0 TFLs in 10 games, including a monster performance against Notre Dame where he racked up four tackles, two sacks, and a forced fumble in a top-ten upset. He added another sack against Virginia Tech and was productive in road wins at Miami and in the ACC Championship against Florida State. Then came the setback. A leg injury requiring surgery wiped out his entire 2024 redshirt senior season at Louisville, and for a former walk-on with no safety net, the road could have ended right there. It didn't. Reiger entered the transfer portal and landed at Wisconsin as a graduate transfer, reuniting with a coaching staff that believed in his tape even without recent game film. He played all 12 games in 2025, starting 11 at outside linebacker, and delivered 33 tackles, 5.0 sacks, 6.0 TFLs, and 45 total pressures against a Big Ten schedule. He picked up All-Big Ten Honorable Mention from the media and then went to the East-West Shrine Bowl and absolutely torched the competition, winning Defensive MVP with three sacks. That performance turned heads across the league and put his name on draft boards that had previously overlooked him. Reiger graduated from Louisville with a degree in sports administration before his Wisconsin chapter, a detail that speaks to the kind of person evaluators will meet in interviews.

Strengths:
Carries elite-level speed for the position, and it shows up immediately off the snap with a first step that routinely beats tackles to the edge. Good length at 6-5 with long arms that he deploys effectively to create separation, swat away hands, and keep blockers from latching onto his frame. Really refined inside spin move that he times well at pocket depth, generating consistent pressures when his initial rush gets stalled on the arc. Feet and hands work in tandem on his pass rush, particularly on quick-win reps where he'll stab and slip inside before the tackle can anchor. Relentless chaser who refuses to quit on plays, consistently showing up in pursuit and tracking down ball carriers from the backside. Clear upward trajectory on film, from a developmental piece at Louisville to a legitimate disruptor by his Wisconsin season with much-improved technique. Generated 45 pressures in 2025 across 292 pass-rush snaps against Big Ten offensive lines, a rate that confirms his ability to win against quality competition. Shrine Bowl performance was not a fluke; it validated everything his film showed, and he looked like the best pass rusher on the field all week long in practice and the game.

Weaknesses:
Undersized at 250 pounds for the position, and it becomes a real issue when bigger tackles or tight ends get their hands on him and lock in. Tackling needs significant work; his miss rate spiked in 2025, and too often he arrives at the ball carrier without finishing with authority. Lacks the anchor and mass to hold up as a consistent edge setter against the run, getting washed out by down blocks and kick-out schemes. Rush tracks are somewhat predictable right now, taking similar angles at similar speeds, which gives experienced tackles a blueprint to sit on his moves. Doesn't yet have a reliable speed-to-power conversion, so when his initial burst doesn't win the rep, he can stall out and get controlled by bigger linemen.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Offense Rating: 80%
  Tackling: 64%
  Pass Rush: 87%
  Run Defense: 81%
  Coverage: 62%

NFL Player Comparisons: BrentonCox Jr. - Florida (90%), BarrynSorrell - Texas (87%), QueRobinson - Alabama (75%)

name: Max Llewellyn
scouting_report:
Background:
Max Llewellyn grew up in Urbandale, Iowa, about twenty minutes from Iowa City, and turned into one of the more productive defensive ends in Des Moines-area prep history. Standing 6'5" and weighing 263 pounds, Llewellyn was a three-year letterman and senior captain at Urbandale High School, where he doubled as a tight end and defensive end. His senior campaign featured 13 tackles for loss, 5.5 sacks, and 13 receptions for 278 yards on offense, earning him a spot on the Des Moines Register's Elite All-State team along with first-team all-state and all-district selections. Recruiting services were mixed on his upside: 247Sports graded him as a four-star (0.91 composite), while both Rivals and ESPN slotted him as a three-star with a 76 ESPN rating. He ranked as the No. 436 overall recruit on 247Sports' composite board. Llewellyn committed to Iowa and redshirted in 2021, earning the team's defensive Leader Award despite never seeing the field. He picked up scattered reps in 2022 across four games (44 snaps total), then worked as a rotational piece in 2023, logging 147 snaps across all 14 games and collecting his first two career sacks. The real breakout came in 2024 when, despite still playing behind the starters, Llewellyn torched opposing pass protectors in 290 snaps. He racked up 22 tackles, eight tackles for loss, 5.5 sacks, three passes defended, and a forced fumble in what amounted to a part-time role. His per-snap production was among the best on Iowa's defense that season. The 2025 season finally brought a full-time starting role, and Llewellyn responded with 26 tackles, 9.5 tackles for loss, 6.5 sacks, a team-leading eight quarterback pressures, five pass breakups, and two forced fumbles across 13 starts. He earned honorable mention All-Big Ten honors from both the coaches and media, along with Iowa's defensive Hustle Award. That production came within the framework of one of the nation's best defenses, as Iowa ranked fifth in first downs allowed, eighth in scoring defense (15.6 points per game), ninth in total defense, and tenth in passing defense. His brother Luke played baseball at Iowa, keeping the family firmly planted in Hawkeye territory.

Strengths:
Super quick first step that immediately threatens the offensive tackle's outside shoulder and forces blockers into panic mode on their initial kick-slide. Owns one of the better inside spin moves in this entire draft class, setting it up with long strides and body fakes that manipulate the tackle's weight distribution. Diverse pass-rush toolbox featuring chops, club-swims, long-arms, rips, swipe-rips, and two-handed swipes, giving him plenty of counters when Plan A stalls. Uses his long-arm strike to elevate blockers' pads and soften the edge, creating leverage advantages even against bigger tackles who outweigh him. Relentless pursuit of the quarterback with legitimate closing burst, particularly dangerous against mobile quarterbacks who try to escape the pocket laterally. Gets his hands up at the line when he loses the rush, batting down three passes in 2025 and disrupting throwing lanes even on non-pressure reps. Smooth gap-exchange ability on stunts and twists, transitioning into B-gap attacks without losing speed or balance through traffic.

Weaknesses:
In run defense he is limited; gets washed inside by down blocks and driven off his spot by tight ends in wide-zone concepts. Alarming missed-tackle rate that spiked badly in 2025 after a much cleaner 2024 season, calling into question his tackling reliability at the next level. Pad level stays elevated far too often, sapping his leg drive and preventing him from consistently bending the corner even from wide alignments. Over-reliance on the spin move makes him predictable for tackles who have done their homework; needs to weaponize his counter moves more effectively. Struggles to disengage once offensive linemen lock hands onto his frame, lacking the hand-fighting counters to keep his rush alive through contact.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Offense Rating: 81%
  Tackling: 47%
  Pass Rush: 92%
  Run Defense: 61%

NFL Player Comparisons: BralenTrice - Washington (77%), ColbyWooden - Auburn (75%), Sai'vionJones - LSU (75%)

name: Elijah Sarratt
scouting_report:
Background:
Elijah Sarratt played his prep ball at St. Frances Academy in Baltimore under head coach Messay Hailemariam, earning all-state honors as a defensive back during his junior year at Colonial Forge before transferring. Standing 6'2" and weighing 209 pounds, Sarratt began his collegiate career at FCS Saint Francis (Pa.) in 2022, where he wasted no time making an impact. As a true freshman, he hauled in 42 catches for 700 yards and tied the program's single-season record with 13 receiving touchdowns, earning FCS Freshman All-America and first-team All-Northeast Conference recognition. The production only grew from there. Sarratt transferred to James Madison for the 2023 season and flourished in the Sun Belt, posting 82 receptions for 1,191 yards and eight touchdowns while earning first-team all-conference honors. His 1,191 yards ranked 11th nationally that year. When Curt Cignetti left JMU for Indiana, Sarratt followed, and the decision paid dividends for both parties. During his first season in Bloomington in 2024, he caught 53 passes for 957 yards and eight scores, including a record-setting 165-yard performance against Purdue in the Old Oaken Bucket rivalry game. The Hoosiers finished 11-2 and reached the College Football Playoff. Sarratt earned third-team All-Big Ten honors from both the coaches and media panels, with the Associated Press tabbing him second-team. His 2025 senior campaign added another chapter to an already impressive resume. Despite missing two games due to injury, Sarratt caught 52 passes for 802 yards and 15 touchdowns across 13 appearances, tying for second in Indiana single-season history for receiving scores. He delivered in the biggest moments, catching game-winning touchdowns on the road against both Iowa and Oregon, then posting seven catches, 75 yards, and two scores against Oregon in the Peach Bowl semifinal. For his career across three programs and four seasons, Sarratt totaled 239 receptions for 3,649 yards and 44 touchdowns, leading all active FBS receivers in career touchdown catches. His brother Josh currently plays safety at James Madison.

Strengths:
Sarratt snatches the ball away from his body with natural confidence and rarely lets contested catches slip through his fingers in traffic. Body control belongs in the excellent category. The leaping grab he made against Michigan State in 2024, contorting mid-air while maintaining positioning on the defender, shows what he can do at the catch point. Intermediate route work is where he earns his paycheck. His ability to locate soft spots in zone coverage and settle at the precise depth needed to move the chains comes from serious film study. Owns remarkable feel for the sticks. Converting 43 of 53 catches into first downs or touchdowns during the 2024 campaign speaks to his understanding of spacing and leverage in critical situations. Sets up defensive backs with subtle salesmanship before his breaks. Watch how he uses head fakes and shoulder dips to create separation rather than relying on pure athleticism alone. Brings a physical mentality to perimeter blocking that you love to see from a receiver. He gets his hands on linebackers and safeties and sustains contact long enough to spring outside runs. Clutch gene runs through his DNA. Game-winning touchdown catches at Iowa and Oregon in back-to-back road tests during the 2025 season tell you everything about his competitive makeup. Production across three different programs at three different levels of competition shows adaptability. Learning new systems quickly and producing immediately is a skill that translates to the pro game. Red zone weapon with a nose for the end zone. His 44 career receiving touchdowns lead all active FBS receivers, and 15 scores in 2025 alone tied for second in Indiana single-season history.

Weaknesses:
He is not going to consistently threaten NFL secondaries vertically or run past cornerbacks down the sideline on nine routes. Release package needs refinement against physical press corners. Longer defenders who can match his play strength at the line will disrupt his timing and route stems early in reps. After-catch production is pedestrian at best. Do not expect him to turn short completions into explosive gains through broken tackles or open-field elusiveness. Run blocking technique requires attention despite his willingness. Hand placement and sustain against NFL edge defenders will be a work in progress during his first couple of seasons. Route tree could use expansion beyond his comfort zones. Leans heavily on slants, curls, comebacks, and go routes without much nuance on in-breaking patterns that require more athleticism.

name: Logan Fano
scouting_report:
Background:
NFL bloodlines run deep for Logan Fano, whose four uncles played professionally, including Gabe Reid (Bears) and Teni Palepoi (Chargers), and whose brother Spencer is a projected first-round pick in this same draft class. A four-star recruit out of Timpview High School in Spanish Fork, Utah, Fano was a two-time All-State selection and Region Defensive MVP who posted 20.5 sacks as a senior. He also earned Polynesian Bowl All-American honors. Fano originally enrolled at BYU in January 2022 but never played for the Cougars, instead serving a Mormon mission before transferring to Utah ahead of 2023. His debut season with the Utes was productive but brief: 14 tackles, 3.5 sacks, and two forced fumbles across five games before a torn ACL shut him down. He had also suffered an ACL tear in 2022. Healthy in 2024, Fano started nine of 12 games and finished with 35 tackles, 6.5 TFL, and 2.5 sacks. He took another step in 2025 as a full-time starter, totaling 44 tackles, 7.5 TFL, 4.5 sacks, and two pass breakups to earn All-Big 12 Second Team recognition. He declared for the draft following the regular season.

Strengths:
Good height and functional power at the point of attack against the run. Takes on blocks with a wide base and solid pad level consistently. Gets his hands up quickly and works to get extension on blockers. Steady improvement as a run defender, particularly in his final college season. Motor runs hot; second-effort plays show up regularly on tape. Effective long-arm move that sets up a useful counter to the outside. Pursues well laterally with good angles and closing burst. No trouble handling tight ends at the point of attack.

Weaknesses:
Short arms for the position limit his reach and leverage as a pass rusher. Lacks the bend to turn the corner and finish at the quarterback. Spin move is undeveloped; spins in place without gaining ground laterally. Gets reached on the backside of zone runs too often. Two torn ACLs before age 24 raise durability questions. Older prospect who turns 24 during his rookie year, capping developmental upside. Struggles to anchor and hold the edge against quality offensive tackles.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Offense Rating: 77%
  Tackling: 70%
  Pass Rush: 71%
  Run Defense: 74%

name: Zakee Wheatley
scouting_report:
Background:
Zakee Wheatley came out of Archbishop Spalding High School in Severn, Maryland as a consensus four-star recruit who played both sides of the ball, catching 56 passes for 810 yards and seven touchdowns while picking off five passes as a junior. Penn State won the recruiting battle, and Wheatley redshirted in 2021 before seeing early rotational work in 2022, originally at cornerback, collecting two interceptions across 12 appearances before moving to safety. His career turned in 2024 when starter Kevin Winston Jr. went down with an injury. Wheatley stepped into the lineup for all 16 games and posted 96 tackles, three interceptions, and a forced fumble, earning All-Big Ten honorable mention. His playoff run was the highlight, capped by 16 tackles, a sack, and an interception against Notre Dame in the Orange Bowl. Rather than declare for the 2025 draft, Wheatley returned as the lone safety with starting experience after both Winston and Jaylen Reed left for the NFL. He started 12 games, finishing with 74 tackles, an interception, and two pass breakups while logging the second-most defensive snaps on the roster. He earned All-Big Ten honorable mention again, opted out of the Pinstripe Bowl to begin draft prep, and was invited to the Senior Bowl.

Strengths:
Elite long speed that allows him to recover and erase mistakes from deep alignments. Improved steadily in coverage each year, posting strong marks as a senior. Run defense is a clear calling card, triggering downhill with good timing and intent. Ball production is real, with nine career takeaways including five interceptions. Alignment versatility across both safety spots, the box, and occasional slot reps. Good length at 6-3 that extends his tackle radius and disrupts catch points. Showed he can perform under pressure during Penn State's playoff run in 2024. Missed tackle rate dropped noticeably from his junior to senior season.

Weaknesses:
Weight is light for his frame, which limits his ability to stack and shed blocks. Tackling technique remains inconsistent, too often defaulting to arm tackles and ankle grabs. Play-action still freezes him, leaving him vulnerable to deep shots over the top. Pursuit angles get loose when he overflows, opening cutback lanes behind him. Physical press skills from the slot need work against bigger pass catchers. Pad level in run support runs hot and cold, affecting his ability to hold ground.

  Offense Rating: 80%
  QB Rating When targeted: 72.3
  Tackling: 77%
  Run Defense: 91%
  Coverage: 86%
  Zone: 87%
  Man/Press: 79%
