name: Joshua Josephs
scouting_report:
Background:
Joshua Kalebe Josephs grew up in Kennesaw, Georgia, where he turned heads at North Cobb High School with a frame that screamed future NFL pass rusher. Standing 6'3" with an absurd 82-inch wingspan that made offensive tackles nervous before he even moved, Josephs racked up 104 tackles and six sacks as a senior while leading his team to back-to-back Region 3 championships. Born on a date not widely publicized, the talented edge defender committed to Tennessee in November 2021, selecting the Volunteers over 26 other Division I programs including five SEC schools. His high school coach Shane Queen helped mold him into a four-star prospect ranked among the nation's top-15 edge rushers by 247Sports and On3. Tennessee's coaching staff knew they had something special when Josephs arrived in Knoxville in June 2022, but patience would be required. As a true freshman, he flashed playmaking ability with 12 tackles, 2.5 for loss, and his first collegiate sack at LSU. The next two years saw him develop behind NFL-caliber talents Byron Young and James Pearce Jr., the latter becoming Atlanta's first-round selection in 2025. During that apprenticeship period from 2023-24, Josephs accumulated 59 tackles, 13.5 tackles for loss, 4.5 sacks, and six forced fumbles while gradually earning more responsibility. His 2024 campaign proved particularly impressive when PFF graded him as one of the nation's top-10 edge rushers at 90.0 overall, ranking fifth on the team in tackles despite limited starts. The 2025 season finally gave Josephs the spotlight he had earned through years of patient development. Starting all 11 regular season games at the LEO position, he delivered dominant performances week after week. His 90.0 pass rush grade ranked third in the SEC and 19th nationally, while his game-changing plays mounted: a 41-yard fumble return touchdown at Mississippi State, a crucial strip-sack against Georgia that led to points, and three forced fumbles that tied him for 13th nationally. He crossed the 100-career tackle threshold at Kentucky and earned recognition on the 2025 Senior Bowl Top 300 list. Josephs graduated in December 2025 with a bachelor's degree in communication studies, capping a four-year journey from raw athletic specimen to one of the most disruptive defenders in the Southeastern Conference.

Strengths:
That 82-inch wingspan creates problems before the snap even happens, allowing him to strike offensive tackles at range while keeping his body clean from their punch attempts. First step explosiveness is legitimately special, with a 4.68 forty time that shows up on tape as elite get-off that leaves SEC linemen lunging at air. When setting the edge against the run, he plays with discipline and gap integrity that belies his age, anchoring hard and forcing ball carriers back inside. Pass rush repertoire has developed nicely with a devastating cross-chop, effective push-pull, and a bull-to-rip sequence that gave Alabama and Oklahoma fits. Shows legitimate football intelligence when diagnosing screen passes and misdirection, rarely biting on the eye candy that fools less patient rushers. Long arm technique converts his speed to power effectively, delivering shocking jolts that knock SEC tackles off their spot and reset the line of scrimmage. Closing speed on the quarterback is electric, and once he turns the corner his natural bend lets him flatten to the passer at angles tackles cannot mirror. Plays with a genuine mean streak when games hang in the balance, hunting down pressure opportunities with the killer instinct you cannot coach.

Weaknesses:
At 240 pounds, the lack of mass becomes apparent when power-profile tackles sit down and anchor against his rush, neutralizing his speed advantage. Stiff ankles limit his ability to maximize bend around the corner, preventing him from fully utilizing his flexibility through his torso and knees. Tight end chips and combo blocks collapse him inside far too easily, opening up outside run lanes that better anchored defenders would seal. Tackling inconsistency is concerning with a 23.5% missed tackle rate, suggesting issues with technique or effort when bringing down ball carriers. Sack totals have not matched his pressure rate throughout his career, indicating he struggles to finish plays when he wins his rep and reaches the quarterback.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Offense Rating: 81%
  Tackling: 62%
  Run Defense: 93%

NFL Player Comparisons: AshtonGillotte - Louisville (85%), JosaiahStewart - Michigan (84%), ArnoldEbiketie - Penn State (83%)

name: Josh Cuevas
scouting_report:
Background:
Josh Cuevas arrived at Alabama the old-fashioned way: he earned it. The North Hollywood native starred as a two-way player at Campbell Hall High School, racking up 55 catches for 700 yards and 11 touchdowns as a junior while also notching 62 tackles, including 10 for loss. Standing 6-3, 256 pounds with a December 29, 2001 birthday, Cuevas took the long road through college football. He began at Cal Poly, where he emerged as a legitimate pass-catching threat, hauling in 57 receptions for 622 yards and six touchdowns as a redshirt freshman to earn third-team All-Big Sky honors in 2022. The FCS production caught the attention of Washington, where Cuevas made the jump to Power Five football in 2023. In one season with the Huskies, playing alongside Michael Penix Jr. during their run to the national championship game, Cuevas caught four passes for 164 yards and a touchdown across all 15 games. When Kalen DeBoer packed up for Tuscaloosa, Cuevas followed his head coach. The familiarity with DeBoer's system gave him a head start, and after a productive 2024 campaign as a rotational piece (16 catches, 218 yards, one touchdown), he stepped into the starting role as a senior. The 2025 season showcased what Cuevas could do with a featured role. He started 11 games before a late-season injury shelved him for the final two regular season contests and the SEC Championship, but still finished with 37 catches for 411 yards and four touchdowns. He was named to the Mackey Award Watch List and the Hispanic College Football Player of the Year Watch List. Cuevas earned multiple player-of-the-week honors from the Alabama coaching staff, including a game against Oklahoma in the College Football Playoff where he posted a team-high 80 receiving yards on six catches with a 25-yard touchdown. He returned from injury to contribute 35 yards on three catches in the CFP rematch and grabbed four passes for 35 yards against Indiana before declaring for the draft.

Strengths:
Nasty route runner for a tight end. Snaps off his stems and eats up cushion, creating windows at the intermediate level that quarterbacks love. This kid can go get it vertically. Tracks the deep ball over his shoulder like a wideout and stretches the seam with legit speed for his size. Plucks the ball away from his body with strong hands. Watch the Oklahoma tape and you see him extending through contact to finish catches. North-south guy after the catch. No dancing, no wasted motion. Catches it and gets downhill immediately to pick up extra yardage. Knows how to work the boundary. Keeps his feet in, bodies up defenders, and understands leverage when the ball is thrown to the sideline. Four years at three programs and 54 games of experience. This is a grown man who has seen every coverage and knows how to prepare. Played for DeBoer at Washington and followed him to Tuscaloosa. Already speaks the language of a pro-style passing game. Gutted it out in the CFP after missing time with injury. Shows up when the lights are brightest and his team needs a play.

Weaknesses:
Ball adjustment is inconsistent. When throws drift or come in hot, he fights himself trying to recalibrate rather than trusting his hands. Blocking is serviceable at best. He will give you effort, but he gets walked back too easily and struggles to sustain at the point of attack. Undersized at 6-3 for an NFL tight end. That lack of length shows up in contested catch situations against longer, rangier defenders. Tested around the 50th percentile athletically. There is no second gear to pull away from pursuit or win with pure speed. Missed time late in his senior year with an injury that lingered into the postseason. Medical evaluation will need to clear that concern.

  Defense Faced Rating: 77%
  QB Rating When targeted: 119.5
  Hands: 71%
  Short Receiving: 79%
  Intermediate Routes: 80%
  Deep Threat: 78%
  Blocking: 64%

Watch the Oklahoma tape and you see him extending through contact to finish catches. North-south guy after the catch. No dancing, no wasted motion. Catches it and gets downhill immediately to pick up extra yardage. Knows how to work the boundary. Keeps his feet in, bodies up defenders, and understands leverage when the ball is thrown to the sideline. Four years at three programs and 54 games of experience. This is a grown man who has seen every coverage and knows how to prepare. Played for DeBoer at Washington and followed him to Tuscaloosa. Already speaks the language of a pro-style passing game. Gutted it out in the CFP after missing time with injury. Shows up when the lights are brightest and his team needs a play.
weaknesses: Ball adjustment is inconsistent. When throws drift or come in hot, he fights himself trying to recalibrate rather than trusting his hands. Blocking is serviceable at best. He will give you effort, but he gets walked back too easily and struggles to sustain at the point of attack. Undersized at 6-3 for an NFL tight end. That lack of length shows up in contested catch situations against longer, rangier defenders. Tested around the 50th percentile athletically. There is no second gear to pull away from pursuit or win with pure speed. Missed time late in his senior year with an injury that lingered into the postseason. Medical evaluation will need to clear that concern.
player_comparisons: BrevinJordan - Miami (100%), JamesMitchell - Virginia Tech (91%), BrentonStrange - Penn State (86%)

name: Austin Barber
scouting_report:
Background:
Austin Barber played his prep ball at Trinity Christian Academy in Jacksonville, Florida, where he helped the team capture a Class 3A state championship in 2020. He drew mid-major and SEC interest as a three-star recruit, ultimately choosing the Gators over Miami, Minnesota, Mississippi State, and Ole Miss. After a redshirt year in 2021, he earned early playing time as a redshirt freshman in 2022, starting five games at right tackle across 13 appearances and picking up Freshman All-American honors from College Football News. Barber flipped to left tackle in 2023 and started eight games, learning the blindside against SEC competition. He locked down the starting left tackle job full-time in 2024, playing all 13 games and scoring a touchdown on a tackle-eligible play against Mississippi State that earned him SEC Offensive Lineman of the Week. His final season in 2025 was his best work yet, particularly as a run blocker, earning All-SEC Third Team recognition and a spot on the Outland Trophy Watch List. Barber finished his Florida career with 47 games and over 2,700 snaps, the vast majority at left tackle. He was invited to the 2026 Senior Bowl and earned his bachelor's degree in Education Sciences along the way, making the SEC Academic Honor Roll four times across five seasons.

Strengths:
The basketball background shows up in his feet. Mirrors speed rushers with loose, balanced slides. Quick off the snap in the run game and consistently beats defenders to the spot. Sneaky snatch-and-trap move that takes away leverage and reroutes rushers out of the play. Good luck getting off this guy once his hands land clean on your chest plate. Uses jump sets on play-action to crowd the rusher's path and take away the outside track. Gets to the second level well for a big man. Finds linebackers and seals them on combos. Nearly 1,500 career pass blocking snaps in the SEC and rarely got beat clean. 47 games and five years at Florida. He has been through the wars in that conference.

Weaknesses:
Hands land high too often. Gives rushers a clear lane to work underneath his punch. When a bull rush gets into his pads, the anchor gives. Gets walked back into the pocket. That rigid two-hand punch limits his ability to recover against counters and spin moves. Doesn't bend his knees enough at contact, so his pad level rises and he loses leverage. Struggles to move people as a solo run blocker. Often needs a double team to generate push.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Defense Faced Rating: 78%
  Pass Blocking: 77%
  Run Blocking: 94%

NFL Player Comparisons: TalieseFuaga - Oregon State (84%), TylerSmith - Tulsa (83%), NicholasPetit-Frere - Ohio State (82%)

name: J.C. Davis
scouting_report:
Background:
J.C. Davis played his high school ball at McClymonds in Oakland, California, where he lined up as a defensive tackle and earned second-team all-conference honors as a senior. He made the switch to the offensive line at Contra Costa College and quickly took to it, landing All-CCCA First Team recognition before heading to New Mexico as a lightly recruited two-star prospect. At New Mexico, Davis stepped into the starting left tackle job immediately and never gave it up. He started all 24 games across 2022 and 2023, logging over 1,500 snaps and earning All-Mountain West First Team honors as a junior. PFF graded him as the No. 4 tackle nationally and the top-graded tackle in the Group of 5 that season, with particularly strong run blocking marks. His consistency made him the highest-graded returning tackle in college football heading into 2024. Davis transferred to Illinois and started all 25 games over two seasons in Champaign. He allowed zero pressures in the Cheez-It Citrus Bowl win over No. 14 South Carolina and finished 2025 as PFF's No. 3 graded left tackle nationally. He earned AP All-Big Ten First Team honors and closed his college career with 49 consecutive starts.

Strengths:
Thick frame with long arms and large hands gives him a real physical advantage at the point of attack. Reads gap exchanges and twists well; rarely caught guessing on combo blocks. Effective snatch-trap technique to counter rushers who lean into their moves. Good drive power when he gets his feet moving and locks on to a defender. Pin-and-climb combos are well-timed and show reliable coordination with interior linemen. Patient hands against spin moves; doesn't lunge or overcommit to the first threat. Enough lateral quickness to mirror inside counters and locate targets on screen blocks. 49 consecutive starts across three programs speaks to his durability and reliability.

Weaknesses:
Leads too often with his head and chest, compromising his base on contact. Hand placement is inconsistent; wide strikes expose his frame and limit sustain. Opens his outside hip prematurely against speed, giving up the corner too easily. Contact balance is shaky; gets pried open when defenders work his edges. Feet are late to fire after initial contact, leading to overextension and recovery issues. Punch lacks pop at the point of attack; rushers can work through it to set up counters.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Defense Faced Rating: 73%
  Pass Blocking: 78%
  Run Blocking: 81%

NFL Player Comparisons: DonovanJackson - Ohio State (93%), SeanRhyan - UCLA (88%), AjaniCornelius - Oregon (86%)

name: Eli Stowers
scouting_report:
Background:
Eli Stowers was a consensus four-star quarterback recruit out of Denton Guyer High School in Texas, rated by 247Sports, Rivals, and ESPN. He led Guyer to the Texas 6A state championship game as a junior and won the 2019 state high jump title at seven feet. He signed with Texas A&M and spent two seasons as a reserve quarterback before transferring to New Mexico State in 2023, where he switched to tight end full time. The position change clicked quickly. He posted 35 receptions for 366 yards and two touchdowns, contributed as a runner with 29 carries for 113 yards and two scores, and even threw a touchdown pass. He earned Honorable Mention All-Conference USA and graded inside PFF's top-20 tight ends despite limited snaps. He followed offensive coordinator Tim Beck to Vanderbilt the next offseason. At Vanderbilt, Stowers caught 50 passes for 644 yards and five touchdowns in 2024, earning first-team All-SEC honors. He returned for 2025 and won the John Mackey Award and the William V. Campbell Trophy while posting 62 catches for 769 yards and four touchdowns. He earned consensus first-team All-America recognition. Across three seasons at the position, Stowers totaled 148 receptions for 1,774 yards and 11 touchdowns.

Strengths:
Covers ground quickly in the seam with real speed, forcing safeties to respect his vertical ability. Soft, reliable hands that adjust well to off-target throws and contested windows. Quarterback background gives him advanced feel for sitting in zones and finding soft spots. Breaks out of route stems with quick feet that give linebackers real trouble mirroring him. Catch radius is outstanding thanks to long arms and an absurd vertical leap. Shows burst and awareness after the catch, turning short receptions into meaningful gains. Lines up everywhere: slot, wide, inline, backfield. Tough to game-plan around that versatility. Year-over-year improvement since converting to tight end has been steady and measurable.

Weaknesses:
Undersized at 239 pounds, which shows up quickly in physical matchups at the line. Run blocking is underdeveloped and he gets moved too easily by bigger defenders. Drop rate climbed in 2025 after a much cleaner 2024, worth monitoring going forward. Can get bumped off his route by physical coverage at the top of his stem. No special teams experience in college, which could limit early roster value.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Defense Faced Rating: 75%
  QB Rating When targeted: 108.9
  Hands: 84%
  Short Receiving: 93%
  Intermediate Routes: 94%
  Deep Threat: 94%
  Blocking: 66%

NFL Player Comparisons: TreyMcBride - Colorado State (91%), Ja'TavionSanders - Texas (79%), CadeStover - Ohio State (79%)

name: Davison Igbinosun
scouting_report:
Background:
Davison Igbinosun (IG-bih-no-son) came out of Union High School in New Jersey as a consensus four-star recruit, rated the top prospect in the state by On3. He enrolled at Ole Miss and started 10 games as a true freshman in 2022, finishing with 37 tackles and five pass breakups on his way to College Football News freshman All-American honors. That production earned him attention as a transfer portal target, and he made the move to Ohio State in January 2023. He started immediately for the Buckeyes and never stopped. Across three seasons in Columbus, Igbinosun started all 43 games and logged 22 pass breakups to tie for 11th in program history. His best year came in 2025: 53 tackles, eight PBUs, two interceptions, and a first-team All-Big Ten selection. Both picks came in meaningful spots, one off Bryce Underwood in the Michigan win and another against Indiana in the Big Ten Championship Game. For his career, Igbinosun totaled 194 tackles, 27 pass breakups, four interceptions, and three fumble recoveries across 53 starts between Ole Miss and Ohio State. He was a key member of the 2024 College Football Playoff national championship team, leading that group with nine PBUs over 16 games.

Strengths:
Uses his length at the line to jam and reroute, throws receivers off their stems early. Squeezes sideline routes and takes away the back shoulder, quarterbacks have to be perfect. Gets his hands on you at the catch point and makes receivers work to finish. Triggers downhill fast against screens and quick-game, reads it and goes. Shows real want-to in run support, fills his gap and sets the edge with force. Penn State end zone interception in 2024 showed improved ball-tracking and awareness. Looked like a different player in 2025, cleaned up his technique and cut the penalties.

Weaknesses:
Hips are tight, and it shows when he has to flip and run with quicker receivers. Thirty career penalties is a real number, the grabbing habit dies hard. When a receiver wins cleanly off the line, his first instinct is to reach and clutch. Opens his hips too soon in off-man, gives away which direction he is turning. Backpedal-to-break is mechanical, loses a step in the transition downhill. Run support tackling leans on shoulder strikes, not always finishing with his arms.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Offense Rating: 81%
  QB Rating When targeted: 52.0
  Tackling: 77%
  Run Defense: 89%
  Coverage: 83%
  Zone: 84%
  Man/Press: 78%

NFL Player Comparisons: MekhiGarner - LSU (84%), TysonCampbell - Georgia (81%), DenzelBurke - Ohio State (80%)

name: Ephesians Prysock
scouting_report:
Background:
Playing both wide receiver and cornerback under coach Casey Clausen at Bishop Alemany High School in Mission Hills, California, Ephesians Prysock helped the Warriors reach the CIF-Southern Section Division 2 championship game as a senior in 2021. He earned first-team All-CIF-SS, first-team All-Mission League, and a third-team all-state selection from CalHiSports. His versatility and raw physical tools drew serious recruiting attention, and he signed with Arizona as a consensus 4-star recruit rated by ESPN (82 overall, 4-star), 247Sports (0.90 grade, 4-star), and Rivals (5.8 grade, 4-star). Prysock saw limited action as a true freshman at Arizona in 2022, playing in 10 games with three starts and recording 16 tackles across 223 defensive snaps. His sophomore season in 2023 told a different story entirely. Starting all 13 games for a 10-3 Wildcats squad loaded with future NFL talent, he racked up 61 tackles, an interception, and seven pass breakups while earning honorable mention All-Pac-12 recognition. When head coach Jedd Fisch took the Washington job, Prysock followed through the transfer portal, arriving in Seattle ready for a larger role. The move paid off quickly. Prysock started all 13 games as Washington's top corner in 2024, finishing sixth on the team with 45 tackles and adding six pass breakups and a forced fumble. His 2025 season was his most complete, once again starting all 13 games and leading the secondary with 53 tackles, five pass breakups, and an interception. He capped the year by winning Washington's Defensive Skill Player of the Year award and earning honorable mention All-Big Ten from the media, along with a spot on the Big Ten All-Academic team. Across his four-year career, Prysock logged over 2,600 defensive snaps across 46 games, compiling 20 pass breakups and two interceptions while starting 42 of those contests. He closed things out with a strong performance in Washington's L.A. Bowl victory over Boise State.

Strengths:
Rare height at 6'4" with proportional length that lets him blanket throwing windows and crowd catch points in ways most corners simply cannot replicate. Showed real growth as a run defender throughout his career, frequently maintaining outside leverage and using his frame to funnel ball carriers back to pursuit help. Hip flexibility is startling for a man his size. He can flip, redirect, and sink into breaks with a smoothness that you just don't expect from someone built like a safety. Press technique features a powerful initial jam when he times it right, landing hands squarely into receivers' chests and disrupting their release patterns at the line. Zone awareness is one of his better traits, reading route combinations at the top of his drop and adjusting his positioning to take away windows between levels of a defense. Carries enough recovery speed to stay in the hip pocket of vertical threats after a misstep, closing ground with long strides that eat up space in a hurry. Durable and dependable. Starting 42 of 46 career games across two programs speaks to his reliability, and he consistently logged heavy snap counts north of 750 per season. Willing and active in the screen game, chasing plays sideline to sideline with urgency and using his length to disengage from blockers and make plays in the flat.

Weaknesses:
Ball production is disappointing for a player with his tools. Just two interceptions across 204 career targets suggests real limitations tracking and locating the football downfield. Gets grabby when he falls behind in coverage, and his penalty numbers reflect it. Seven flags in 2025 alone is a number that will make defensive coordinators nervous. Carries a high center of gravity that works against him at break points, leaving him upright in his pedal and a beat slow reacting to sharp cuts underneath. Tackling technique is more arm-and-swipe than wrap-and-finish, and his missed tackle rate has fluctuated too much to call it dependable in space. Average long speed for the position means faster vertical threats can gain separation on him over the top, and he lacks the gear to completely erase those kinds of mistakes.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Offense Rating: 81%
  QB Rating When targeted: 89.1
  Tackling: 79%
  Run Defense: 84%
  Coverage: 73%
  Zone: 69%
  Man/Press: 72%

NFL Player Comparisons: IsraelMukuamu - South Carolina (85%), RyanWatts - Texas (85%), AkaylebEvans - Missouri (81%)

name: Lander Barton
scouting_report:
Background:
The Barton family name is all over Utah's athletic history: father Paul played football and baseball for the Utes, mother Mikki is a two-time All-American basketball player in the school's Hall of Fame, and both brothers Cody and Jackson went on to NFL careers. Lander continued the tradition as a four-star recruit out of Brighton High School, rated the No. 2 prospect in Utah and invited to the All-American Bowl after posting 122 tackles and eight sacks as a senior. He made an immediate impression in 2022, collecting 46 tackles, 8.0 TFL, and 4.5 sacks across 14 games to earn Pac-12 Freshman Defensive Player of the Year. A season-ending injury limited his 2023 campaign to seven starts, though he still recorded 34 tackles, two interceptions, and a forced fumble before going down. Barton bounced back in 2024 with a team-leading 72 tackles, 5.0 TFL, and two interceptions across 12 starts. His 2025 senior season was less productive, totaling 55 tackles and 1.5 sacks in 13 games while also seeing occasional snaps at tight end. He accepted an invitation to the East-West Shrine Bowl and finished his career with 46 games and five interceptions, including multiple pick-sixes.

Strengths:
Exceptional length for the linebacker position creates disruption in passing lanes. Solid pre-snap recognition against the run; reads his keys and trusts them. Willing to attack move blocks with heavy, punishing hands at the point of contact. Adequate zone coverage range and uses his size to contest throws at the catch point. Five career interceptions show a natural feel for finding the football. Experience across multiple linebacker alignments adds scheme flexibility. Handful of offensive snaps at tight end creates interesting H-back roster versatility. Comes from a family with real NFL experience, and the preparation habits show.

Weaknesses:
Moves in straight lines; change of direction and reactive agility are below average. Tackling consistency has been a problem throughout his career, with too many misses. Struggles to disengage from second-level blocks once offensive linemen get their hands on him. Tends to overflow the junction point on run scrapes, opening cutback lanes. Coverage awareness regressed noticeably in 2025, including red zone busts. Light for the position, which limits his ability to anchor against the run inside. Overall production and grading took a clear step backward as a senior.

Lander Barton,                     LB, Utah.
bio: The Barton family name is all over Utah's athletic history: father Paul played football and baseball for the Utes, mother Mikki is a two-time All-American basketball player in the school's Hall of Fame, and both brothers Cody and Jackson went on to NFL careers. Lander continued the tradition as a four-star recruit out of Brighton High School, rated the No. 2 prospect in Utah and invited to the All-American Bowl after posting 122 tackles and eight sacks as a senior. He made an immediate impression in 2022, collecting 46 tackles, 8.0 TFL, and 4.5 sacks across 14 games to earn Pac-12 Freshman Defensive Player of the Year. A season-ending injury limited his 2023 campaign to seven starts, though he still recorded 34 tackles, two interceptions, and a forced fumble before going down. Barton bounced back in 2024 with a team-leading 72 tackles, 5.0 TFL, and two interceptions across 12 starts. His 2025 senior season was less productive, totaling 55 tackles and 1.5 sacks in 13 games while also seeing occasional snaps at tight end. He accepted an invitation to the East-West Shrine Bowl and finished his career with 46 games and five interceptions, including multiple pick-sixes.
strengths: Exceptional length for the linebacker position creates disruption in passing lanes. Solid pre-snap recognition against the run; reads his keys and trusts them. Willing to attack move blocks with heavy, punishing hands at the point of contact. Adequate zone coverage range and uses his size to contest throws at the catch point. Five career interceptions show a natural feel for finding the football. Experience across multiple linebacker alignments adds scheme flexibility. Handful of offensive snaps at tight end creates interesting H-back roster versatility. Comes from a family with real NFL experience, and the preparation habits show.
weaknesses: Moves in straight lines; change of direction and reactive agility are below average. Tackling consistency has been a problem throughout his career, with too many misses. Struggles to disengage from second-level blocks once offensive linemen get their hands on him. Tends to overflow the junction point on run scrapes, opening cutback lanes. Coverage awareness regressed noticeably in 2025, including red zone busts. Light for the position, which limits his ability to anchor against the run inside. Overall production and grading took a clear step backward as a senior.

name: Fa’alili Fa’amoe
scouting_report:
Background:
A former defensive lineman out of American Samoa, Fa'alili Fa'amoe committed to Washington State as a 3-star recruit in the 2020 class and flipped to the offensive side of the ball during spring practice in 2022. That transition paid off quickly. He started seven games as a redshirt freshman at right tackle and earned some of the highest offensive line marks on the team that season, particularly in pass protection at Stanford and Oregon State. Fa'amoe went on to start all 12 games in 2023 and earned Preseason All-Pac-12 recognition from multiple outlets heading into 2024. He was named team captain that year but was limited to nine appearances and seven starts. After the season, he transferred to Wake Forest for his redshirt senior year and started all 12 games for the Demon Deacons in 2025. At Wake Forest, he was part of an offensive line that helped produce the ACC's sixth-best rushing attack. He received All-ACC Honorable Mention and closed his college career with a strong showing in the Duke's Mayo Bowl victory over Mississippi State. He also participated in the Shrine Bowl. Across four seasons of action, Fa'amoe logged 38 career starts at right tackle.

Strengths:
True pro frame with thick arms and a wide base that fills out his lower half. Punch lands tight and inside; rarely gets his hands swatted away in pass sets. Uses a subtle hand feint to freeze the rusher's plan before connecting. Short, controlled kick-slides in pass pro keep him square to the rush lane. Held his ground against Virginia Tech's speed-to-power rush without giving up the pocket. When he locks on as the drive man in a combo, he walks defenders off the ball. Keeps his legs churning on down blocks; finished through contact in the bowl game consistently. Gave up only two sacks across his final 1,300 pass-blocking snaps at Wazzu and Wake.

Weaknesses:
Feet are slow to the edge; speed rushers turn the corner before he can get set. Consistently plays too tall at the point of attack and gets walked back in the run game. Hips are stiff on zone tracks; struggles to cover ground laterally on reach blocks. When a rusher sells speed and counters inside, he cannot redirect in time to recover. The NC State and SMU games showed stretches where he lost his assignment entirely in the run game. Drew seven penalties in only nine games during his 2024 season at Washington State.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Defense Faced Rating: 74%
  Pass Blocking: 71%
  Run Blocking: 61%

NFL Player Comparisons: JamesHudson - Cincinnati (88%), RichardGouraige - Florida (86%), CarterWarren - Pittsburgh (83%)

name: Seth McGowan
scouting_report:
Background:
Seth McGowan walked into Norman, Oklahoma as one of the more decorated running back recruits in the 2020 class. A consensus four-star prospect out of Poteet High School in Mesquite, Texas, he was rated the No. 13 running back nationally by Rivals and sat inside the top 30 recruits in the state. He had earned every bit of that billing over a high school career that produced more than 3,700 yards and 45 touchdowns on the ground. Oklahoma gave him the ball immediately as a true freshman, and McGowan delivered, ripping off 370 yards on 58 carries (6.4 YPC) with three touchdowns, highlighted by a 73-yard rush and three catches for 70 yards in the Cotton Bowl victory over No. 10 Florida. The future looked wide open. Then it all fell apart. McGowan was dismissed from Oklahoma's program in 2021 following an arrest connected to an alleged armed robbery. He ultimately pled guilty to felony larceny and spent three months in prison followed by a year of probation. His football career appeared finished. But McGowan refused to let that be the end of his story. He practiced at Texas College (NAIA) in 2022, then played six games at Butler Community College in Kansas during 2023, totaling 125 yards and four touchdowns on 39 carries. It was hardly a glamorous stat line, but it kept the door cracked. New Mexico State took a chance on him for the 2024 season, and McGowan rewarded the Aggies with 813 rushing yards on 153 carries (5.3 YPC) alongside 23 catches for 277 yards and three receiving touchdowns. He earned Second Team All-Conference USA honors and finished fourth among CUSA backs in rushing yards per game. That performance opened the door to the SEC. McGowan transferred to Kentucky for his final season in 2025 and stepped right into the lead back role, starting 10 of 11 games (missing the Tennessee contest with an injury). He led the Wildcats with 725 rushing yards and a team-best 12 touchdowns on 165 carries, ranking ninth in the SEC in rushing yards per game and fourth in rushing scores. He posted back-to-back 100-yard efforts against Eastern Michigan and at South Carolina, becoming the first UK player with 75-plus rushing yards and a touchdown in each of the first four games since George Adams in 1984. He also tallied 18 catches for 121 receiving yards. His play earned him an invitation to the Senior Bowl and multiple Earl Campbell Tyler Rose Award honorable mention nods. Across his full college career (31 games), McGowan totaled 1,918 rushing yards and 18 touchdowns on 375 carries, adding 55 receptions for 604 yards and four receiving scores.

Strengths:
Processes the front of the defense rapidly and identifies lanes before most backs would even pick a direction, playing with real decisiveness between the tackles. Runs with genuine patience behind developing blocks, letting his linemen work before accelerating through daylight with impressive burst for a 215-pound frame. Stacks cuts with loose hips and quick feet in tight quarters, stringing together multiple direction changes without losing forward momentum or balance through traffic. Converts speed to power effectively at the point of contact, lowering his pads and driving through arm tackles to churn out tough yards after the initial hit. Manipulates his torso and gets compact when absorbing contact from multiple defenders, squeezing out extra yardage that lesser backs would leave on the field. Thrives in gap and power concepts where his vision and downhill mentality are best utilized, averaging over six yards per carry on pull-lead runs across the last two seasons. Keeps his eyes downfield even while navigating jumpcuts, always angling toward the next first down marker rather than settling for what the blocking gives him. Willing and capable in pass protection when locked in, showing the frame and base strength to square up blitzers and anchor against inside pressure.

Weaknesses:
Alarming lack of explosive plays at the SEC level, with a long run of just 20 yards in 2025 and very few carries gaining 10-plus yards against quality defenses. Ball security is a legitimate red flag after five fumbles over the last two seasons, a number that will make NFL coaches nervous about trusting him in critical situations. Pass blocking grades fell off a cliff in his final season, with too many costly whiffs and missed assignments that would get exposed at the next level immediately. Limited receiving upside, running choppy routes out of the backfield with stiff mechanics that prevent him from threatening defenses as a true third-down weapon. Will turn 24 before his rookie season even begins, meaning his prime window is shorter than most backs entering the league and his tread count matters more.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Defense Faced Rating: 75%
  Rushing: 80%
  Break Tackles: 69%
  Receiving/Hands: 59%
  Pass Blocking: 36%
  Run Blocking: 57%

NFL Player Comparisons: CarsonSteele - UCLA (82%), KeaontayIngram - USC (82%), QuinshonJudkins - Ohio State (77%)

name: Jack Endries
scouting_report:
Background:
Modest recruiting interest as a three-star out of Monte Vista High School in Danville, California, meant Jack Endries initially walked on at Cal. He had been a three-sport athlete who posted 49 catches for 579 yards and nine touchdowns as a senior, earning MaxPreps third-team All-State honors and All-Metro recognition from the San Francisco Chronicle, but the offers never matched the production. He rewarded that bet quickly. Endries started all 26 games across two seasons in Berkeley, earning multiple Freshman All-American nods in 2023 after logging 35 catches for 407 yards and two scores. His sophomore year was a clear step forward: 56 receptions for 623 yards and two touchdowns made him Cal's leading receiver and earned him Honorable Mention All-ACC. A three-game stretch against Miami, Pittsburgh, and NC State featured a 57-yard touchdown and consecutive 100-yard outings. After two seasons and 91 catches for 1,030 yards at Cal, Endries entered the transfer portal and landed at Texas for his junior year. Playing in Steve Sarkisian's offense, he started all 11 games and finished with 33 catches for 346 yards and three touchdowns. His career totals across 39 starts sit at 124 receptions for 1,376 yards and seven touchdowns.

Strengths:
Catches everything thrown his way, with sure hands and clean ball tracking through traffic. Boxes out defenders like a rebounder and wins at the catch point even in tight coverage. Builds speed through his routes and gets on top of man coverage on intermediate stems. Plays through contact at the release and does not get rerouted by physical corners or linebackers. Quick hands to snatch high or off-target throws away from his frame without gathering. Lines up everywhere: inline, slot, H-back, and occasionally split wide without missing a beat. Keeps his feet moving on pass protection reps and rarely gives ground to interior rushers. Durable and available, starting 38 of 39 career games while logging heavy snap counts.

Weaknesses:
Gets walked back by defensive ends on the edge and lacks anchor strength in run blocking. Light frame at 245 limits his ability to hold the point of attack against bigger defenders. Breaks are rounded rather than sharp, and short-area separation is tough to find on tape. Not much happens after the catch; lacks the wiggle or burst to turn completions into chunk plays. Whiffs on reach blocks in space and misses his landmark on the move too often. Run blocking angles are hit or miss, with some good reps on pulls mixed with ugly losses.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Defense Faced Rating: 79%
  QB Rating When targeted: 118.6
  Hands: 98%
  Short Receiving: 83%
  Intermediate Routes: 91%
  Deep Threat: 88%
  Blocking: 71%

NFL Player Comparisons: MasonTaylor - LSU (88%), CadeStover - Ohio State (86%), JaredWiley - TCU (81%)

name: Kaelon Black
scouting_report:
Background:
Salem High School in Virginia Beach watched Kaelon Black pile up 3,360 career rushing yards and 55 touchdowns across three varsity seasons, earning three first-team All-Beach District selections, district Offensive Player of the Year, and all-region honors as both a sophomore and junior. A multi-sport athlete who also competed in basketball and track, Black committed to James Madison as a two-star recruit out of the 2020 class, arriving in Harrisonburg without much recruiting fanfare but with a proven track record of production on the ground. His time at JMU was a mixed bag of flashes and frustration. Black showed real promise as a true freshman in the spring 2021 season, ripping off a 141-yard debut performance against William & Mary, but a season-ending injury during his redshirt freshman year cut short what looked like a breakout campaign after just three starts. He returned in 2022 and landed on the Paul Hornung Award Honor Roll following a 131 all-purpose yard, two-touchdown effort at Appalachian State, earning VaSID Offensive Rookie of the Year honors as well. His junior season saw him serve as a steady contributor with 668 rushing yards and 214 receiving yards across 13 starts, though the per-carry average dipped. Over four years at JMU, he carried 266 times for 1,344 yards with 53 receptions for 440 yards and six receiving touchdowns. When Curt Cignetti packed his bags for Bloomington, Black followed. After a quiet 2024 as a rotational piece (251 yards on 46 carries), he seized his opportunity during Indiana's historic 2025 season. Black rumbled for 1,034 yards and 10 touchdowns on 186 carries (5.6 yards per attempt) as the Hoosiers reached the College Football Playoff and won a national championship. He saved some of his best work for the biggest stages: 99 yards against Alabama in the semifinals, a 110-yard outing against Maryland during the regular season, and a two-touchdown performance against Oregon in the title game. He finished his college career with 3,123 all-purpose yards, 23 total touchdowns, and his bachelor's degree in liberal studies.

Strengths:
Runs with a low pad level that makes him incredibly difficult to knock off balance, and he consistently wins the leverage game against linebackers and safeties in the hole. Patient enough to let blocks develop but decisive once he commits, attacking daylight with a quick burst that catches second-level defenders flatfooted at the mesh point. Generates serious yardage after contact thanks to a thick lower half and relentless leg churn; defenders rarely bring him down with arm tackles or first attempts. Compact build allows him to squeeze through tight creases along the offensive line, getting skinny between bodies where bigger backs would get swallowed up. Willing and aggressive in pass protection, stepping into oncoming blitzers without hesitation and showing no fear against linebackers with a full head of steam. Reliable ball security throughout his career, with only five fumbles across nearly 500 career carries and a clean sheet on lost fumbles during his breakout 2025 season. Strong lateral agility and quick feet let him redirect and jump-cut to exploit backside lanes when his primary gap gets walled off by the defense. Showed real competitive toughness in marquee games, delivering his best performances against top-tier competition like Ohio State, Alabama, and Oregon during the CFP run.

Weaknesses:
Lacks top-end long playing speed to pull away from defensive backs in the open field, and pursuit angles will catch him at the next level more often than in college. Receiving profile is alarmingly thin for the modern NFL, with just seven targets and five catches during his biggest season; his route tree barely exists beyond dump-offs. Undersized frame at 5'10" and 210 pounds raises durability concerns if asked to shoulder a featured workload over a 17-game NFL season. Run blocking grades were consistently mediocre throughout his college career, and his technique in pass protection does not always match his effort and willingness. Never carried the ball more than 186 times in a single season, leaving real questions about whether he can handle the volume that comes with an expanded NFL role.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Defense Faced Rating: 73%
  Rushing: 88%
  Break Tackles: 78%
  Receiving/Hands: 57%
  Pass Blocking: 45%
  Run Blocking: 62%

NFL Player Comparisons: TrevorEtienne - Georgia (83%), PierreStrong Jr. - South Dakota State (78%), JordanJames - Oregon (77%)

name: Caleb Douglas

name: Kaytron Allen
scouting_report:
Background:
The "Fatman"-a nickname Allen has embraced since his high school days-arrived at Penn State in 2022 as one part of a dynamic freshman duo alongside Nicholas Singleton. Despite sharing the spotlight and backfield responsibilities throughout his career, Allen has carved out his own identity as a punishing, downhill runner with exceptional vision. The Norfolk, Virginia native took a unique path to Happy Valley, transferring to IMG Academy after a standout freshman campaign at Norview High School where he rushed for 1,465 yards and 16 touchdowns. At IMG, Allen continued his dominance, accumulating over 3,000 yards and 42 touchdowns in three seasons for the national powerhouse program. He's ascended Penn State's record books, ranking ninth all-time in rushing attempts (559), 11th in rushing yardage (2,877) and tied for 15th in rushing touchdowns (24). His junior season marked his most productive, eclipsing the 1,000-yard mark with 1,108 yards while maintaining an efficient 5.1 yards per carry and finding the end zone 10 times (8 rushing, 2 receiving). Allen and Singleton became the first Penn State duo to each rush for 1,000 yards in a season, showcasing Allen's ability to produce even while sharing carries-something that should translate well to the NFL's committee-heavy approach. The 5'11", 229-pound back has maintained remarkable durability, appearing in all 41 games during his Penn State career while gradually increasing his production each season. His decision to return for his senior season alongside Singleton, Allar, and other key defenders signaled his commitment to bringing a championship to Happy Valley while also refining his game for the NFL level. With another productive season, Allen could cement himself as one of Penn State's all-time great running backs and position himself as one of the top power backs in the 2026 NFL Draft class.

Strengths:
Shows exceptional patience behind the line of scrimmage, allowing blocks to develop before hitting holes with decisive one-cut ability that maximizes yardage potential. Built with a thick, powerful lower half that generates substantial drive through contact, routinely pushing piles forward and falling forward for extra yardage when wrapped up. Runs with outstanding leverage and pad level, presenting minimal surface area to defenders while maintaining balance through glancing contact. Demonstrates elite vision when navigating traffic, instinctively finding cutback lanes and exploiting defensive over-pursuit with subtle shifts in running tracks. Possesses impressive burst through the line of scrimmage that belies his size, getting to the second level quickly before defenders can react. Shows surprising lateral agility in tight spaces, making compact jump cuts without losing momentum or sacrificing north-south orientation. Maintains exceptional ball security with high-and-tight carrying technique, cradling the football through traffic and contact situations. Thrives in short-yardage and goal-line situations, showing the vision to find microscopic creases and power to convert in congested areas.

Weaknesses:
Lacks the true breakaway speed to consistently threaten home run plays, getting caught from behind on longer runs when he reaches the third level. Needs significant development as a receiver, showing adequate hands but limited route sophistication and creativity in space after the catch. Pass protection technique remains inconsistent despite willing engagement, occasionally lowering his head and losing sight of blitzers. Could improve stamina for bell-cow workload, as effectiveness sometimes wanes when carries exceed 15-18 in a single game. Limited creativity in the open field to make defenders miss with advanced moves, relying primarily on power rather than elusiveness.

  Defense Faced Rating: 76%
  Rushing: 91%
  Break Tackles: 77%
  Receiving/Hands: 53%
  Pass Blocking: 64%
  Run Blocking: 57%

Kaytron Allen,                     RB, Penn State.
bio: The "Fatman"-a nickname Allen has embraced since his high school days-arrived at Penn State in 2022 as one part of a dynamic freshman duo alongside Nicholas Singleton. Despite sharing the spotlight and backfield responsibilities throughout his career, Allen has carved out his own identity as a punishing, downhill runner with exceptional vision. The Norfolk, Virginia native took a unique path to Happy Valley, transferring to IMG Academy after a standout freshman campaign at Norview High School where he rushed for 1,465 yards and 16 touchdowns. At IMG, Allen continued his dominance, accumulating over 3,000 yards and 42 touchdowns in three seasons for the national powerhouse program. He's ascended Penn State's record books, ranking ninth all-time in rushing attempts (559), 11th in rushing yardage (2,877) and tied for 15th in rushing touchdowns (24). His junior season marked his most productive, eclipsing the 1,000-yard mark with 1,108 yards while maintaining an efficient 5.1 yards per carry and finding the end zone 10 times (8 rushing, 2 receiving). Allen and Singleton became the first Penn State duo to each rush for 1,000 yards in a season, showcasing Allen's ability to produce even while sharing carries-something that should translate well to the NFL's committee-heavy approach. The 5'11", 229-pound back has maintained remarkable durability, appearing in all 41 games during his Penn State career while gradually increasing his production each season. His decision to return for his senior season alongside Singleton, Allar, and other key defenders signaled his commitment to bringing a championship to Happy Valley while also refining his game for the NFL level. With another productive season, Allen could cement himself as one of Penn State's all-time great running backs and position himself as one of the top power backs in the 2026 NFL Draft class.

name: Luke Altmeyer

name: Taylen Green
scouting_report:
Background:
Taylen Green grew up in Lewisville, Texas, where he was a three-star recruit at Lewisville High School and a standout track athlete who set the school record in the long jump. As a prep senior, he accounted for over 3,000 total yards and 29 touchdowns, earning recognition as a top-25 dual-threat quarterback nationally from both 247Sports and ESPN. Green signed with Boise State and redshirted in 2021 before taking over as starter during his redshirt freshman year. He was named Mountain West Freshman of the Year in 2022 after accounting for 24 total touchdowns, then earned MWC Championship Game Offensive MVP honors in 2023 by going 12-of-15 for 226 yards and four total scores in the title game. He transferred to Arkansas ahead of the 2024 season and posted 3,756 yards of total offense in the SEC, the second-highest single-season mark in program history, earning Liberty Bowl MVP after throwing for 341 yards against Texas Tech. In 2025, he threw for 2,714 yards and 19 touchdowns while adding 777 rushing yards. At the 2026 Combine, Green posted the fastest 40 among all quarterbacks, paired with a 43.5-inch vertical and 11'2" broad jump, finishing first in athletic testing among the entire quarterback group.

Strengths:
Combine workout confirmed historically rare athleticism for the position. Topped every quarterback at the combine in athletic testing, and it was not close. Capable of turning broken plays into first downs with his legs alone. Deep ball accuracy stands out, showing natural arm talent on vertical routes. Effective runner on designed quarterback concepts, not just scramble plays. Shows improved poise in the pocket compared to his Boise State tape. Elevated release point at his height creates throwing lanes over the interior. Strong in RPO situations, reading the conflict defender and delivering quickly. Competitive mindset shows up in big moments against ranked opponents.

Weaknesses:
Elongated throwing motion slows his release and gives defenders extra time. Short and intermediate accuracy is inconsistent, especially under duress. Turnover-worthy play rate spiked during his final college season. Tends to retreat from the pocket rather than climbing when pressure arrives. Post-snap processing against zone coverage remains a work in progress. Completion percentage never topped 61% in any full college season. Ball security needs attention, particularly on designed runs in traffic.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Defense Faced Rating: 75%
  Release Speed: 72%
  Short Passing: 69%
  Medium Passing: 83%
  Long Passing: 87%
  Rush/Scramble: 90%

name: Bryce Lance
scouting_report:
Background:
Growing up in Marshall, Minnesota, Bryce Lance spent his childhood chasing footballs thrown by his older brother Trey in the backyard, learning the game from a father, Carlton, who played professionally in the CFL. He was a multi-sport star at Marshall High School, setting school records in both receiving yards and interceptions on the football field while also making noise on the basketball court. Recruiting services pegged him as a three-star prospect with an 80/100 rating from 247 Sports, and he followed Trey's footsteps to Fargo, enrolling at North Dakota State in 2021. The last name carried weight in that building, but Bryce was determined to build something of his own. Patience is one way to describe his first three years. Anonymity might be more accurate. Lance redshirted in 2021, played strictly special teams in 2022, and caught a single pass as a sophomore in 2023. Three full seasons, one reception. Then his junior campaign in 2024 detonated like a bomb nobody saw coming. He grabbed 75 balls for 1,071 yards and 17 touchdowns, a scoring total that led every receiver in the FCS and tied the Missouri Valley Football Conference's single-season record. The playoffs only amplified his impact: 25 catches, 375 yards, and seven touchdowns across four postseason games. That semifinal against South Dakota State, where he went for six catches, 125 yards, and three scores, was the kind of performance that forces scouts to open a new file. He capped the year with nine receptions in the national championship win over Montana State and earned First-Team All-MVFC honors as NDSU claimed its tenth title. Lance returned for his senior season and became the first wide receiver in Bison history to string together consecutive 1,000-yard campaigns, finishing 2025 with 51 catches for 1,079 yards and eight touchdowns. A career-high 159-yard outing against Southeast Missouri State and an 81-yard scoring strike against St. Thomas (tied for ninth longest in program history) highlighted the year, along with a 75-yard rushing touchdown against Southern Illinois that showed his open-field ability. He turned down Big Ten transfer offers and more NIL money to stay in Fargo, a decision that says plenty about his makeup. Off the field, he completed his marketing degree in December 2024 and finished his MBA by December 2025, earning CSC Academic All-America first-team honors and finishing 12th in Walter Payton Award voting. He closed his collegiate career third on NDSU's all-time list with 25 touchdown receptions and eighth with 2,157 receiving yards, and he is widely regarded as the top FCS prospect in this draft class.

Strengths:
Combine-verified elite athlete who posted a 99 athleticism score and a 9.98 Relative Athletic Score, ranking seventh among all receivers tested at the combine since 1987. This is not a small-school mirage. Blazing long speed that showed up as a 4.34 forty with a 1.49-second 10-yard split, fifth and second among all receivers respectively. He can flat out run, and the tape backs it up on those deep balls over the top. Explosive lower half is absurd for a 6-3 frame. A 41.5-inch vertical (fourth among combine wideouts) and an 11-foot-1 broad jump (tied for third) mean he plays above the rim and covers ground in a hurry. Deep ball tracking ability is as natural as any receiver in this class. He locates the football over his shoulder with ease, adjusts his stride without losing a step, and finishes at the catch point with body control that belies his size. Contested catch rate of 62 percent is a real weapon. He attacks 50-50 balls with that length and vertical pop, turning jump balls into mismatches that corners simply cannot win consistently. Reliable hands throughout his career. Only two drops on 99 targets as a senior tells you he is locked in when the ball arrives, and there is nothing mechanical or forced about his catching motion. Intermediate route work is polished beyond what you typically see from the FCS level. He sinks his hips with balance, snaps off cuts cleanly, and finds the soft spots in zone with a feel that looks instinctive rather than coached. Scores touchdowns at a ridiculous rate. Twenty-five career scores on 127 receptions, roughly one every five catches, and his body control near the goal line is outstanding. Red zone weapon from day one.

Weaknesses:
Competition level is the elephant in the room. Back-to-back 1,000-yard seasons are impressive anywhere, but the corners he dominated at the FCS level are a different species from NFL defensive backs. Release package off the line needs serious work. He got away with raw athleticism against press at the FCS level, but NFL corners will eat him alive if he does not develop hand combatives and counter moves. Route tree felt limited on tape. He was at his best on verticals, posts, and intermediate crossers, but the nuance and deception required on option routes and double moves at the next level still needs refinement. Blocking effort and technique grade out as merely adequate. For a guy his size, there should be more willingness to sustain contact and finish in the run game if he wants to see early-down snaps. Only two truly productive seasons after three years of near-total invisibility. The breakout was dramatic, but the late development timeline raises fair questions about how much ceiling remains and whether the learning curve will steepen against better competition.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Defense Faced Rating: 64%
  QB Rating When targeted: 148.0
  Hands: 87%
  Short Receiving: 95%
  Intermediate Routes: 97%
  Blocking: 69%

NFL Player Comparisons: AndreiIosivas - Princeton (95%), XavierHutchinson - Iowa State (82%), MarvinHarrison Jr. - Ohio State (76%)

name: Jeremiah Wright
scouting_report:
Background:
Baseball, not football, was the first love for Jeremiah Wright growing up in Snow Hill and Selma, Alabama. It took weeks of persistent recruiting from his middle school coach, Eugene Foster, to convince Wright's mother to even let the kid try the sport. His first day of practice, he played a few snaps at tight end before getting moved to tackle. He put somebody on the ground and never looked back. By the time he was starring at Selma High School for Coach Willie Gandy, Wright had turned into a highly sought-after offensive and defensive tackle recruit with offers from Georgia, Tennessee, Ole Miss, Georgia Tech, and others. ESPN rated him a 4-star prospect and the No. 13 player nationally at his position, while 247Sports and Rivals had him as a consensus 3-star and a top-30 prospect in the state of Alabama. Wright signed with Auburn in the 2020 class under Gus Malzahn and immediately got thrown into the deep end of SEC football. The coaching staff converted him from offensive line to defensive tackle during his freshman preseason, and he saw his first action on the interior D-line at South Carolina that fall, picking up three tackles in the Citrus Bowl against Northwestern. Then the setback: a torn ACL wiped out his entire 2021 season. He bounced between the defensive and offensive lines again before finally settling on the O-line in 2022, working as a backup left guard with his first start coming at Ole Miss. Through it all, Wright earned his degree in liberal arts in August 2024, made the SEC Academic Honor Roll multiple times, and was named to the Wuerffel Trophy watch list for his community service. The last two seasons is where Wright made his mark. He locked down the starting right guard spot in 2024 and never let go, stringing together 24 consecutive starts across the 2024 and 2025 campaigns. In his final season, he led Auburn's offensive line with 33 knockdown blocks, earned a grade of 90 percent or higher in five individual games, and served as a game captain against Missouri and Arkansas. By the time he declared for the 2026 NFL Draft following his sixth year on the Plains, Wright had appeared in 53 games and survived three different coaching staffs. He became a father in July 2025 with the birth of Jeremiah Wright Jr.

Strengths:
Absolutely enormous frame at 348 pounds with thick lower half and long arms that allow him to swallow up defenders at the point of attack. Generates serious pop on first contact; when he gets his hands inside and fires his hips, bodies move backwards whether they want to or not. Physical, chippy temperament that never turns off. Loves finishing blocks into the turf and will stand over a defender after pancaking him. Effective on double-team feeds and down blocks where his raw mass and square power let him uproot nose tackles and create push at the line. Showed real improvement in his pass sets during 2024, keeping a patient base and working inside-out to wall off rushers and lengthen their path. Possesses the ankle flexibility to drop his hips and set a sudden anchor when bull rushers try to walk him into the quarterback's lap. Durable iron man who started every snap across two full seasons despite the physical toll of SEC trench warfare, including a comeback from a torn ACL. Mature, team-first character with a college degree, community service recognition, and the kind of locker room presence coaches trust to name as a game captain.

Weaknesses:
Below-average foot quickness and lateral agility create real problems when quicker interior rushers change direction or use counter moves on him. Hands are too wide and inconsistent with placement, which undercuts his ability to sustain blocks even when he wins the initial punch. Effectiveness drops off a cliff when asked to block in space on pulls or screens; he cannot redirect and recover once he is committed to a path. Tends to lean forward and play with his head and shoulders over his toes, which costs him leverage and leads to overextension on angle blocks. Struggled to process stunts and delayed blitzes quickly, allowing interior defenders to cross his face before he could slide and pick up the exchange.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Defense Faced Rating: 77%
  Pass Blocking: 76%
  Run Blocking: 73%

NFL Player Comparisons: TorricelliSimpkins III - South Carolina (84%), DillanGibbons - Florida State (84%), LukeKandra - Cincinnati (79%)

name: Tim Keenan
scouting_report: Tim Keenan,                     DT, Alabama.

name: Dametrious Crownover
scouting_report:
Background:
Dametrious Crownover grew up in Grandview, Texas, a small town south of the Dallas-Fort Worth metroplex, where he was a two-sport standout in football and basketball at Grandview High School. As a sophomore, he helped lead Grandview to the Texas Class 3A Division I state championship, and by the time his prep career wrapped up, he had earned first-team all-district honors at both tight end and defensive end. The recruiting services saw him differently: ESPN tagged him as a 4-star prospect while 247 Sports (0.88 grade) and Rivals both had him as a 3-star. He committed to Texas A&M as a tight end, arriving in College Station in 2021 as a raw, long-limbed athlete with a sky-high ceiling and very little certainty about where he'd line up long-term. That question got answered during his redshirt year when the coaching staff moved Crownover to the offensive line, a transition that began to pay dividends quickly. He appeared in eight games as a redshirt freshman in 2022, including his first career start at right tackle against Sam Houston, and took home the Aggie Offensive Unselfish Leadership Award for his willingness to embrace the position switch. By 2023, he was seeing action in all 13 games and earned his first significant start at right tackle in the Texas Bowl, where the offense put up 372 passing yards and allowed just two sacks. He also logged reserve snaps at right tackle against Alabama, Tennessee, South Carolina, and Ole Miss, steadily building a foundation for a larger role. The 2024 season is where Crownover locked down the starting right tackle spot for all 13 games and became a fixture on one of the SEC's best rushing attacks. Texas A&M ranked 26th nationally and second in the conference at 195.5 rushing yards per game, and Crownover was in the middle of all of it. He helped anchor a line that paved the way for 333 rushing yards against McNeese, 310 at Florida (the first back-to-back 300-yard rushing performances for the program since 2012), and 242 yards with five touchdowns in a statement win over No. 8 LSU. The Aggies recorded multiple rushing touchdowns in six consecutive games down the stretch. Crownover returned for a fifth year in 2025, starting every game at right tackle once again and logging 784 offensive snaps across the season. Over four years of action, he appeared in 45 games with nearly 1,900 blocking snaps at the position.

Strengths:
Rare combination of height, length, and mass at 6-7 and 336 pounds makes him an imposing presence who can wall off the edge and smother speed rushers with his reach. Runs fast which is genuinely impressive athleticism for a man that big and confirms his movement skills translate beyond the practice field. Background as a tight end recruit shows up in his ability to move in space, climb to the second level, and locate linebackers on designed runs. Excels as a double-team partner on combination blocks; he feeds defensive tackles to the guard and covers up targets with ease, which was a hallmark of that dominant 2024 rushing attack. Plays with real effort and tenacity as a finisher; he runs his feet through contact and does not quit on reps, which coaches will love in the meeting room. Can reset his hands after losing initial positioning, using his length to reassert control of a pass rusher who gained a brief advantage in the rep. Kept his quarterback clean at an impressive rate in 2024, allowing zero sacks and just 19 total pressures across the full season as the full-time starter. Durable and available, logging virtually every blocking snap in three of his four seasons and never missing time due to injury across 45 career games.

Weaknesses:
His pass protection got worse each year he started, not better, and the 2025 tape is a clear step backward from the solid work he put on film the year prior. Allowed 27 total pressures in 2025 including 23 hurries, a significant jump from the previous season that suggests pass rushers consistently found ways into his chest. Penalty issues are real: he racked up 11 flags in 2025 after just three the year before, a spike that signals technique breakdowns and a lack of discipline in his punch timing. Plays too tall and too wide with his hands, which exposes his chest plate and leads to a soft, unreliable anchor when bull rushers get underneath him. Lateral agility remains a limiting factor; he struggles to redirect and recover against speed-to-power conversions and inside counters that cross his face.

  Defense Faced Rating: 76%
  Pass Blocking: 65%
  Run Blocking: 79%

NFL Player Comparisons: TylerSteen - Alabama (80%), RasheedWalker - Penn State (80%), IsaiahAdams - Illinois (79%)

Dametrious Crownover,                     OT, Texas A&M.
bio: Dametrious Crownover grew up in Grandview, Texas, a small town south of the Dallas-Fort Worth metroplex, where he was a two-sport standout in football and basketball at Grandview High School. As a sophomore, he helped lead Grandview to the Texas Class 3A Division I state championship, and by the time his prep career wrapped up, he had earned first-team all-district honors at both tight end and defensive end. The recruiting services saw him differently: ESPN tagged him as a 4-star prospect while 247 Sports (0.88 grade) and Rivals both had him as a 3-star. He committed to Texas A&M as a tight end, arriving in College Station in 2021 as a raw, long-limbed athlete with a sky-high ceiling and very little certainty about where he'd line up long-term. That question got answered during his redshirt year when the coaching staff moved Crownover to the offensive line, a transition that began to pay dividends quickly. He appeared in eight games as a redshirt freshman in 2022, including his first career start at right tackle against Sam Houston, and took home the Aggie Offensive Unselfish Leadership Award for his willingness to embrace the position switch. By 2023, he was seeing action in all 13 games and earned his first significant start at right tackle in the Texas Bowl, where the offense put up 372 passing yards and allowed just two sacks. He also logged reserve snaps at right tackle against Alabama, Tennessee, South Carolina, and Ole Miss, steadily building a foundation for a larger role. The 2024 season is where Crownover locked down the starting right tackle spot for all 13 games and became a fixture on one of the SEC's best rushing attacks. Texas A&M ranked 26th nationally and second in the conference at 195.5 rushing yards per game, and Crownover was in the middle of all of it. He helped anchor a line that paved the way for 333 rushing yards against McNeese, 310 at Florida (the first back-to-back 300-yard rushing performances for the program since 2012), and 242 yards with five touchdowns in a statement win over No. 8 LSU. The Aggies recorded multiple rushing touchdowns in six consecutive games down the stretch. Crownover returned for a fifth year in 2025, starting every game at right tackle once again and logging 784 offensive snaps across the season. Over four years of action, he appeared in 45 games with nearly 1,900 blocking snaps at the position.
strengths: Rare combination of height, length, and mass at 6-7 and 336 pounds makes him an imposing presence who can wall off the edge and smother speed rushers with his reach. Runs fast which is genuinely impressive athleticism for a man that big and confirms his movement skills translate beyond the practice field. Background as a tight end recruit shows up in his ability to move in space, climb to the second level, and locate linebackers on designed runs. Excels as a double-team partner on combination blocks; he feeds defensive tackles to the guard and covers up targets with ease, which was a hallmark of that dominant 2024 rushing attack. Plays with real effort and tenacity as a finisher; he runs his feet through contact and does not quit on reps, which coaches will love in the meeting room. Can reset his hands after losing initial positioning, using his length to reassert control of a pass rusher who gained a brief advantage in the rep. Kept his quarterback clean at an impressive rate in 2024, allowing zero sacks and just 19 total pressures across the full season as the full-time starter. Durable and available, logging virtually every blocking snap in three of his four seasons and never missing time due to injury across 45 career games.
weaknesses: His pass protection got worse each year he started, not better, and the 2025 tape is a clear step backward from the solid work he put on film the year prior. Allowed 27 total pressures in 2025 including 23 hurries, a significant jump from the previous season that suggests pass rushers consistently found ways into his chest. Lateral agility remains a limiting factor; he struggles to redirect and recover against speed-to-power conversions and inside counters that cross his face.

name: Caden Curry
scouting_report:
Background:
Caden Curry arrived at Ohio State in January 2022 as one of the most decorated defensive linemen in Indiana high school history. The Greenwood native terrorized opponents at Center Grove, where he piled up nearly 300 career tackles, 83.5 tackles for loss, and 28 sacks while leading the Trojans to back-to-back Class 6A state championships and a remarkable 28-0 record over his final two prep seasons. As a senior in 2021, Curry recorded 65 tackles with 24 TFLs and seven sacks, earning Indiana's Mr. Football award for defensive linemen. He chose the Buckeyes over Alabama and Indiana, signing his National Letter of Intent in December 2021 as a consensus four-star recruit who earned an 82 rating from ESPN, a 0.94 grade from 247Sports, and a 5.9 grade from Rivals, ranking him among the top 100 prospects nationally and the No. 3 player in his home state. Curry contributed immediately upon arriving in Columbus, appearing in all 49 games across his four-year career while developing his craft under legendary defensive line coach Larry Johnson. He logged 14 tackles and 1.5 TFLs as a true freshman in 2022, followed by nine tackles and 3.5 TFLs in 2023. Beyond his defensive responsibilities, Curry distinguished himself as a rare defensive lineman willing to contribute on special teams, accumulating 287 special teams snaps over two seasons while handling punt, kickoff, and return unit duties. Standing 6-3 and 260 pounds, Curry's frame profiles as undersized for the position, but his junior season saw him post 22 tackles with 3.5 TFLs and 2.5 sacks, setting the stage for a breakout senior campaign. Everything clicked for Curry in 2025 under new defensive coordinator Matt Patricia. He exploded onto the national scene with 53 total tackles (32 solo), 13.5 tackles for loss, and nine sacks, earning First Team All-Big Ten honors from the media. His signature performance came against Washington when he recorded 11 tackles, a program record-tying five TFLs, and three sacks, earning Big Ten Defensive Player of the Week recognition. Curry also forced a fumble and recovered two more while accumulating over 1,000 snaps during his college career. His strip-sack and recovery against Rutgers on Senior Day, setting up a short touchdown, capped a dominant final season that helped Ohio State rank first nationally in total defense (203 yards per game) and scoring defense (7.8 points per game).

Strengths:
Gets off the ball like his hair is on fire, consistently winning the snap count battle with a first step that puts offensive linemen on their heels from the jump. Pass rush ability is legitimately elite, showing the combination of burst, timing, and counter moves to generate consistent pressure against quality tackles. Run defense matches his pass rushing prowess, demonstrating the discipline and physicality to set edges and collapse running lanes from multiple alignments. Hands are violent and active throughout his rush, showing advanced technique in hand-fighting that allows him to stack and shed blocks with purpose. Motor never quits on any snap, whether it's chasing down a screen pass 15 yards downfield or fighting through a double team on a run play away from his gap. Possesses legitimate versatility to line up at end in odd fronts or kick inside to tackle in even schemes, giving coordinators multiple alignment options. Production against top competition speaks for itself, with his five-TFL, three-sack game against Washington showcasing his ability to dominate quality tackles. Spin move is a legitimate counter weapon that he times well when his initial rush gets stonewalled, showing feel for when to deploy secondary moves.

Weaknesses:
Undersized frame at 6-3, 260 pounds for 4-3 DE, raising durability concerns against NFL power schemes. Needs to add functional strength in his lower half to consistently hold ground at the point of attack against NFL-caliber run blockers who outweigh him significantly. Does not possess the elite bend and ankle flexibility to consistently flatten around the corner as a pure speed rusher at the next level. Compact build limits his projection as an every-down player against power running schemes where he'll face 330-pound guards in the A-gap. Pass rush plan can become one-dimensional late in games when his initial burst and counter moves fail to generate pressure against tackles who adjust.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Offense Rating: 81%
  Tackling: 86%
  Pass Rush: 94%
  Run Defense: 93%

NFL Player Comparisons: JoshPaschal - Kentucky (89%), FelixAnudike-Uzomah - Kansas State (85%), DonovanEzeiruaku - Boston College (79%)
