name: Pat Coogan
scouting_report:
Background:
Blocking alongside Joe Alt and paving the way for Audric Estime's program-record 18 rushing touchdowns at Notre Dame is where most people first learned Pat Coogan's name, but the foundation was built at Marist on Chicago's South Side, where he helped the RedHawks reach the Illinois Class 8A state semifinals. A consensus three-star recruit ranked among the top 40 offensive guards nationally by both 247Sports and Rivals, he signed with Notre Dame in 2021 and redshirted his first year before making a brief debut in 2022. That 2023 season was his first as a starter, holding down left guard for all 13 games on an offense that finished second in program history in points per game (39.1). In 2024, he slid to center after an injury to Ashton Craig and started 13 games, helping anchor the line through Notre Dame's run to the College Football Playoff National Championship Game. Coogan transferred to Indiana for his redshirt senior season in 2025 and started all 15 games at center. The Hoosiers posted six 300-yard rushing games, the most in a single season in school history, and Coogan took home Rose Bowl Offensive MVP honors, becoming the first offensive lineman to win the award since 1944. He finished his college career with 45 appearances and 41 starts across both programs.

Strengths:
Punch timing in pass protection is advanced; stuns rushers before they build momentum. Reads and sorts stunts and twists quickly with natural processing speed. Reliable anchor against bull rushers, rarely gives ground into the pocket. Effective on combo blocks with well-timed feeds and clean releases to linebackers. Year-over-year improvement is notable, with 2025 being his best tape by a clear margin. Versatility at center and guard gives him added roster value at the next level. Keeps a wide, consistent base when mirroring, rarely narrows or crosses his feet.

Weaknesses:
Short arms limit his reach and let longer defenders work into his chest. Feet stall after the initial surge, leaving him stranded against slippery defenders. Recovery quickness is below average once he loses positioning. Forward lean in his pass sets opens the door for counter moves. Effectiveness fades outside the A-gaps; outside zone fits are a concern. Can be steered and shed by nose tackles with length and active hands.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Defense Faced Rating: 73%
  Pass Blocking: 92%
  Run Blocking: 80%

NFL Player Comparisons: JarrettPatterson - Notre Dame (90%), LukeFortner - Kentucky (84%), DelmarGlaze - Maryland (82%)

name: Micah Morris
scouting_report:
Background:
A three-year contributor at Georgia, Morris logged 792 snaps across 26 games, primarily at guard. In 2023, he appeared in 13 contests as part of Georgia's formidable offensive front that led the SEC in sacks allowed (0.93 per game) and powered an offense that averaged 496.5 yards and 40.1 points per game. After enrolling early at Georgia in January 2021, Morris saw initial action against Charleston Southern and Georgia Tech. His role expanded in 2022, appearing in seven games as a reserve offensive lineman during the Bulldogs' national championship campaign. The Camden County High School product arrived in Athens as the program's highest-rated recruit in history. Named to the All-American Bowl and Atlanta Journal-Constitution "Super 11," Morris earned first-team all-state honors in 2019. His senior season featured a dominant ground attack that accumulated 1,170 yards and 13 touchdowns in 11 games.

Strengths:
Heavy hands and solid anchor lead to consistent wins in phone booth matchups; establishes dominant initial position on contact Plus core strength and leg drive generate significant vertical push; overwhelms second-level defenders when he squares them up Operates with smooth kickslide and balanced base in pass pro; maintains proper knee bend through initial engagement Devastating down blocker who creates immediate displacement; excels at washing defenders completely out of gaps Snap-quick get-off when pulling around edge; arrives with bad intentions and looks to punish linebackers in space Reliable processor against stunts and blitzes; sound awareness to pick up late rushers and handle defensive line movement Heavy-handed punch stops defenders' momentum cold; resets anchor quickly when initially moved off spot Natural bender who plays with consistent pad level; generates power from ground up through his frame

Weaknesses:
Tends to overset against speed rushers, creating inside counter opportunities; needs to trust his kick-slide technique Late to recognize and react to defensive line games; can get caught leaning and lose leverage against quick penetrators Limited exposure to true pass sets against elite competition raises questions about immediate third down reliability Base narrows when redirecting in space; struggles to maintain leverage against shifty defenders in open field Forward lean leads to lunging and whiffing against second-level targets who can slip his initial punch

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Defense Faced Rating: 75%
  Pass Blocking: 77%
  Run Blocking: 77%

NFL Player Comparisons: GarrettDellinger - LSU (97%), MatthewJones - Ohio State (78%), DillanGibbons - Florida State (77%)

name: Jakobe Thomas
scouting_report:
Background:
Jakobe Thomas was a three-star recruit out of Tullahoma High School in Tennessee who played both ways as a prep senior. He rushed for 712 yards and 16 touchdowns, caught 20 passes for 361 yards and five more scores, and returned two punts for touchdowns. He earned Tennessee Region 4-A MVP and was a Mr. Football 4-A semifinalist. He signed with Middle Tennessee State and redshirted in 2021 before breaking through the following year with four interceptions, a pick-six against FIU, C-USA Defensive Player of the Week honors, and Second Team PFF All-Conference USA recognition. In 2023 he moved into a full-time starting role, recording 71 tackles, five pass breakups, and a blocked kick across 11 games. Thomas then transferred to Tennessee for 2024, where he played a rotational role and posted 22 tackles, three tackles for loss, a sack, and two pass breakups in 12 appearances. After 1,488 defensive snaps across three programs, Thomas entered the portal again and landed at Miami for 2025. His best game came against NC State, where he picked off two passes in the first quarter, including a 60-yard pick-six.

Strengths:
Good size and length to contest catches and disrupt throwing windows downfield. Reliable ball skills with soft hands; four career picks before arriving at Miami. Reads quarterback eyes well in zone and breaks on the ball with good timing. Comfortable operating in multiple coverages, from two-high to single-high to robber looks. NC State tape shows he can recognize route concepts early and jump throws underneath. Processes information quickly in coverage and finds throwing lanes to sit in. Has played in four different defensive systems and picked up each one without issue.

Weaknesses:
Tackling is a real problem. Whiffs too often in the open field and at the second level. In 2023 alone, he missed 21 tackles, which ranked among the worst for FBS safeties. Run defense is underwhelming; struggles to hold his ground and fill with physicality. Average speed for the position limits his range when asked to cover the deep middle alone. Allowed too many catches and yards when targeted in 2023 coverage assignments. Needs to add functional strength without losing his movement ability.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Offense Rating: 77%
  QB Rating When targeted: 59.1
  Tackling: 55%
  Run Defense: 69%
  Coverage: 88%
  Zone: 86%
  Man/Press: 81%

NFL Player Comparisons: TalanoaHufanga - USC (76%), BryanCook - Cincinnati (75%), JevonHolland - Oregon (74%)

name: Brenen Thompson
scouting_report:
Background:
Spearman, Texas, sits in the northern Panhandle with a population under 3,500, but it produced one of the fastest football players in the 2022 recruiting class. Brenen Thompson was a four-star recruit and decorated track star who won gold in the Texas Class 3A 200-meter dash (21.27) and silver in the 100 (10.40) as a junior, earned First-Team All-State honors on the football field, and was selected for the 2022 Under Armour Next All-America Game. Thompson signed with Texas but saw minimal action as a true freshman, then transferred to Oklahoma for 2023 and 2024. Ankle and hamstring problems limited him to six games as a sophomore, and his junior year produced just 19 catches across 11 games behind a crowded depth chart. He followed Jeff Lebby to Mississippi State for a final season and finally got his shot. In 2025, Thompson posted 57 receptions for 1,054 yards and six touchdowns, shattering Mississippi State's single-season receiving record. His 18.5 yards per catch led power conference receivers nationally. He earned Second-Team All-SEC honors, accepted a Senior Bowl invite, and ran a 4.26 at the NFL Combine, the third-fastest 40 by a wide receiver since 2003.

Strengths:
The 4.26 at the Combine confirmed what the tape showed: historically rare speed at the position. Hits top speed fast and holds it through the catch point without drifting off his line. Deep ball tracking looks natural, comfortable catching over his shoulder at full sprint. Cracked 1,000 yards in the SEC despite inconsistent quarterback play that left production on the field. The nine-route threat forces safety help every snap. Cannot be singled outside without risk. Quick feet and active hands at the line help him slip press before bigger corners lock on. Flashed real deceleration on double moves, freezing defensive backs who bail too early. Crossing routes become runways. Tough to corral once he catches the ball with a head of steam.

Weaknesses:
Measured 5-9 and 164 pounds at the Combine. Real durability flags for a 17-game season. Contested catch production is poor, converting roughly a third of contested opportunities. Route-running lacks polish beyond vertical concepts. Coasts into breaks instead of snapping them off. Drop rate ticked up during the breakout year. Hands get unreliable outside his frame. Run-after-catch on short and intermediate stuff is underwhelming on hitches and screens. At 164 pounds, the physicality question extends beyond durability to basic NFL survival.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Defense Faced Rating: 77%
  QB Rating When targeted: 111.5
  Hands: 72%
  Short Receiving: 83%
  Intermediate Routes: 88%
  Deep Threat: 90%
  Blocking: 66%

NFL Player Comparisons: RondaleMoore - Purdue (79%), JacobCowing - Arizona (78%), TutuAtwell - Louisville (76%)

weaknesses: Measured 5-9 and 164 pounds at the Combine. Real durability flags for a 17-game season. Contested catch production is poor, converting roughly a third of contested opportunities. Route-running lacks polish beyond vertical concepts. Coasts into breaks instead of snapping them off. Drop rate ticked up during the breakout year. Hands get unreliable outside his frame. Run-after-catch on short and intermediate stuff is underwhelming on hitches and screens. At 164 pounds, the physicality question extends beyond durability to basic NFL survival.
player_comparisons: RondaleMoore - Purdue (79%), JacobCowing - Arizona (78%), TutuAtwell - Louisville (76%)

bio: A.J. Haulcy was a three-star recruit out of Fort Bend Marshall High School in Houston who had 12 interceptions and three pick-sixes as a senior, earning First Team All-State honors. He signed with New Mexico, started nine games in 2022, and finished with 87 tackles, two picks, and two forced fumbles. A 24-tackle game against Fresno State was the most by any FBS player since Oklahoma's Kenneth Murray posted 28 against Army in 2018. Haulcy transferred to Houston in January 2023 and started immediately. He led the Cougars with 98 tackles as a sophomore, ranked sixth in the Big 12 at 8.2 per game, and posted program highs in solo stops since 2018. As a junior, he shifted into more of a coverage role and tied for the Big 12 lead with five interceptions while earning First Team All-Big 12 from the coaches, AP, and Phil Steele. He moved to LSU for his final season in 2025 and earned First Team All-SEC and Phil Steele All-American honors after posting 89 tackles, three interceptions, and four pass breakups. Across four years at three schools, Haulcy compiled 347 tackles, 10 interceptions, 19 passes defended, and four forced fumbles.
strengths: Outstanding zone coverage instincts; reads quarterback eyes from depth and triggers downhill with clean timing. Genuine ball skills at the catch point, attacking the football like a receiver on contested throws. Ten career interceptions and 19 passes defended validate the turnover production on tape. Thick 222-pound frame creates a real physicality advantage when driving through ball carriers at the point of contact. Patient in his zone drops, funneling quarterbacks into checkdowns by shrinking windows between routes. Willing and effective run defender who fills gaps with force and consistently grades well against the ground game. Reliable sideline-to-sideline pursuit; finishes plays well outside his initial alignment with effort and angles. Functional strength against second-level blockers, shedding pullers and working back to the ball on the perimeter.
weaknesses: Hip stiffness limits his ability to flip and run with vertical routes cleanly. Backpedal gets too upright, leaving him flat-footed against quick-breaking route concepts. Tackling technique is inconsistent; leads with the shoulder too often, creating missed tackle issues. Lacks the top-end speed to recover once displaced or out-leveraged on deep balls. Eyes occasionally drift too deep into the backfield, letting blockers wall him off from the play.
player_comparisons: XavierWatts - Notre Dame (82%), AndrewMukuba - Texas (81%), RonnieHickman - Ohio State (79%)

bio: Aaron Graves committed to Iowa as a high school freshman out of Dayton, Iowa, holding four-star ratings from ESPN, 247Sports, and Rivals. He skipped a redshirt year, logging snaps across 12 games as a true freshman in 2022 and recording his first career sack against Nevada. His sophomore season brought a full-time role, with 37 tackles, 4.5 tackles for loss, and 2.5 sacks across all 14 games. The 2024 campaign was his best all-around season. Starting all 13 games, Graves posted six sacks, eight tackles for loss, and a team-leading three forced fumbles. His two-sack, two-fumble performance against Washington earned him Co-Big Ten Defensive Player of the Week and Honorable Mention All-Big Ten recognition. As a senior in 2025, Graves earned second-team All-Big Ten honors while anchoring a defense that ranked eighth nationally in scoring and ninth in total defense. He finished with five sacks, 6.5 tackles for loss, and his first career interception, a 35-yard return that set up a touchdown in a shutout win at Wisconsin. Over four years in Iowa City, he totaled 122 tackles, 16.5 sacks, and four forced fumbles across 51 games.
strengths: Genuine pass-rush ability from the interior, with a clear feel for timing his get-off at the snap. Uses stemming and alignment tricks well to set up rush lanes before the ball is snapped. Flashes a swim move that creates separation against guards who sit heavy in pass sets. Good length for the position, able to get extension and control blockers at the point of attack. Pass-rush production improved every season, suggesting coachability and real development capacity. Decent athlete for his size with enough quickness to work both A-gap and B-gap assignments. Durable four-year starter who logged over 1,800 career defensive snaps without missing time.
weaknesses: Bends at the waist rather than the knees, costing him leverage and anchor against the run. Run defense tape is inconsistent; gets washed out of his gap on combo blocks too often. Hands are frequently late and wide when defending the run, exposing his chest to blockers. Slow to recognize and react to down blocks, leading to displacement inside. Hand usage as a pass rusher lacks precision and timing despite his production numbers. Tackling in the open field remains a concern, with too many missed attempts showing up on tape. Scouting Report: Summary Graves is a pass-rush specialist from the interior whose best path to an NFL role runs through his ability to pressure the quarterback. His production climbed each year at Iowa, from a raw freshman to a senior generating 22 total pressures. That trajectory suggests a player still developing rather than one who has already hit his ceiling. His stemming ability and swim move give him tools to work with, and his length lets him play with extension when he wants to. The question is whether those tools translate against NFL-caliber guards who will not let him win with effort alone. The run defense is the real obstacle. His waist-bending habit, late hands, and susceptibility to combo blocks all showed up consistently on tape, and those issues get magnified at the next level. He fits best in a scheme that lets him rush on obvious passing downs and limits his run responsibilities. A 4-3 defense that rotates its interior or a sub-package role in a 3-4 would give him the best chance to contribute early. Asking him to hold up as a three-down player right now would be asking too much. The ceiling is a useful rotational interior rusher who can push the pocket on third down. The floor is a camp body whose run defense limitations keep him off a roster. His combine snub will force him to prove himself at a pro day, but the pass-rush ability is real enough to warrant a look. Development time with an NFL defensive line coach could smooth out the hand timing and pad level issues holding back the rest of his game. bio: Adam Randall arrived at Clemson in January 2022 as one of the more decorated skill players in South Carolina prep history. A consensus four-star recruit out of Myrtle Beach High School, he was an Under Armour All-American and a five-star rated prospect by PrepStar who ranked him among the nation's top 80 players. His senior year at Myrtle Beach was absurd: 65 catches for 1,267 receiving yards and 16 touchdowns through the air, plus another 325 rushing yards and seven scores on the ground. He was also a standout track athlete, clocking a 10.94-second 100 meters as a junior, and 247Sports tagged him as one of its top national "Freak Athletes" in the 2022 cycle. Before he ever set foot on campus, he was widely expected to be a significant contributor at wide receiver for the Tigers. The college career didn't unfold the way anyone scripted it. Randall tore his ACL in spring practice before his freshman year, costing him the first two games of 2022, and when he finally got on the field, he was a rotational piece collecting 10 catches for 128 yards across 12 appearances. His sophomore year brought a modest bump to 22 receptions and 250 yards in 13 games, but the production never matched the recruiting pedigree. The 2024 season was more of the same at receiver, with just 16 catches for 155 yards across 12 games and a single start. He did flash in other ways, though. His 41-yard kick return in the ACC Championship Game against SMU set up the game-winning field goal, and he ripped off a 41-yard rush at Texas when the staff started experimenting with him in the backfield during the College Football Playoff. Those moments planted the seed for what came next. Clemson officially moved Randall to running back in the 2025 offseason, and the switch unlocked something. He was voted a permanent team captain by his teammates and became a 13-game starter, rushing 168 times for 813 yards and 10 touchdowns while adding 36 receptions for 254 yards and three more scores. His 1,281 all-purpose yards made him one of the most productive players on the roster. He joined Travis Etienne, C.J. Spiller, and Travis Zachery as only the fourth player in program history with 750 career rushing yards and 750 career receiving yards. Off the field, Randall earned his bachelor's degree in management in just three years and completed a master's in athletic leadership, was named to the AFCA Good Works Team, and was a finalist for the Pop Warner Award and a semifinalist for the Wuerffel Trophy.
strengths: Packs 230 pounds into a 6'2" frame and uses every bit of it, delivering punishment on contact and bowling through arm tackles at the second level. Elite straight-line speed for a back his size, and it shows up on film when he hits the hole clean and separates from pursuit angles. Runs with his pads low and shoulders squared, attacking the line of scrimmage north-south with no wasted motion or dancing behind the line. Showed surprising patience reading blocks for a first-year running back, particularly on gap and power concepts where he let pullers lead him to daylight. The receiver background is a legitimate weapon out of the backfield. He logged 84 career receptions and understands how to find space in zones as a checkdown target. Dangerous on kick returns with a career 23.2 yards-per-return average, adding a special teams dimension that helps his roster case significantly. Generated over 500 yards after contact in 2025, showing the ability to churn through traffic and drag tacklers for extra yardage on a consistent basis. Tremendous character and leadership profile. Voted a permanent captain, earned a master's degree, and his AFCA Good Works Team selection tells you who this kid is in the building.
weaknesses: Pass protection is a glaring hole right now. His technique is raw and he doesn't play with the anchor or aggression his frame should allow in blitz pickup. Drops remain a concern. They plagued him at receiver and followed him to the backfield, with six drops on 49 targets in 2025 suggesting unreliable hands under pressure. Lacks the lateral agility and quick-twitch burst to make defenders miss in tight quarters. He is not going to create something from nothing when the blocking breaks down. Essentially one full season of running back tape to evaluate. The small sample makes it difficult to know how much of his 2025 was real growth versus scheme and opponent context. Scheme-limited as a runner. His carries came almost exclusively on gap and power concepts at Clemson, and he did not show the vision or flexibility to thrive in outside zone.
player_comparisons: CarsonSteele - UCLA (78%), RaheimSanders - South Carolina (61%), PhilMafah - Clemson (56%)

bio: Ahmaad Moses from Arlington, Texas, earned District 4-5A Co-Defensive MVP honors at Mansfield Summit High School while helping the team reach the state semifinals. Three-star ratings from ESPN (77), 247 Sports (0.84), and Rivals (5.5) reflected solid recruitment interest. College offers trickled in slowly until Marshall broke the ice, followed by Memphis, Georgia Tech, Houston, Louisiana Tech, UTSA, and Tulane. SMU won out, landing the Arlington native who chose to stay close to home. Moses has appeared in 37 games over three seasons, accumulating over 1,000 defensive snaps while steadily increasing his production from 27 tackles as a freshman to 46 as a sophomore. His junior season delivered a breakout campaign with 73 tackles, six tackles for loss, three interceptions, six pass breakups, and one forced fumble. The performance earned him ACC Defensive Back of the Week honors after his two-interception game against TCU, including a 60-yard pick-six. He capped the year with All-ACC Honorable Mention recognition while starting four games and appearing in all 14 contests for the Mustangs.
strengths: Exceptional ball skills and hands – attacks the football with the aggression of a receiver making contested catches Reads quarterback's eyes like a seasoned veteran, consistently jumping routes before the ball leaves the passer's hand Willing and physical tackler who arrives with bad intentions and delivers solid contact in open-field situations Shows good range in coverage, tracking deep balls effectively and providing reliable help over the top Demonstrates elite football IQ, diagnosing plays pre-snap and adjusting coverage responsibilities based on offensive alignment and motion Plays with infectious energy and swagger that elevates teammates' performance and creates momentum-shifting moments for the defense Versatile coverage skills allow him to match slot receivers and provide help in multiple coverage schemes Natural playmaker who consistently finds ways to impact games beyond the stat sheet through deflections and quarterback pressures
weaknesses: Inconsistent tackling technique leads to missed opportunities, particularly when attempting to make plays in space against bigger ball carriers Speed limitations show up in coverage against elite burners, as he lacks true recovery speed Can be overly aggressive in pursuit, taking poor angles that allow ball carriers to slip tackles and gain extra yards Needs to improve consistency in zone coverage drops, occasionally getting caught in no-man's land between coverage responsibilities Struggles with leverage against bigger tight ends and receivers when asked to cover them in isolation situations

bio: Aiden Fisher walked on at James Madison in 2022, the program's first season as an FBS member, with no recruiting star next to his name out of Riverbend High School in Fredericksburg, Virginia. He spent that freshman year on special teams (10 appearances, 24 defensive snaps) before earning a starting role as a sophomore in 2023, when he led the Dukes with 108 tackles, added six tackles for loss, 1.5 sacks, an interception, and seven passes defended on his way to third-team All-Sun Belt honors. Fisher followed head coach Curt Cignetti to Indiana in 2024 and immediately became the anchor of a defense that set program records for fewest rushing yards and total yards allowed. He posted 118 tackles, 5.5 TFLs, and 1.5 sacks while earning first-team All-Big Ten and first-team All-America recognition from the FWAA and Phil Steele, a first for an Indiana linebacker. His senior year in 2025 brought another first-team All-Big Ten selection, 97 tackles, 10.5 TFLs, 4.5 sacks, two interceptions, a Butkus Award finalist nod, and a national championship. He finished his college career with 324 tackles across 50 games.
strengths: Vocal pre-snap communicator who gets the defense aligned and accounted for. Reads blocking schemes quickly and triggers downhill against the run with good timing. Consistently beats running backs to the gap on inside zone. Uses a subtle shoulder dip to avoid second-level blocks without losing ground. Reliable wrap-up tackler with good pad level between the tackles. Showed improved rush production as a senior, nearly tripling his sack total. Durable three-year starter who missed just one game in his career.
weaknesses: Undersized frame with limited room to add functional weight. Lacks the foot speed to handle backs on option routes in man coverage. Tight hips limit his ability to flip and run with crossers or play-action. Struggles to stack and shed when offensive linemen get hands on him at the second level. Open-field tackling drops off against shifty skill players in space. Allowed a high completion rate when targeted in zone coverage at Indiana.
player_comparisons: CamJones - Indiana (100%), TommyEichenberg - Ohio State (91%), EllisBrooks - Penn State (90%)

Background:
Aiden Fisher walked on at James Madison in 2022, the program's first season as an FBS member, with no recruiting star next to his name out of Riverbend High School in Fredericksburg, Virginia. He spent that freshman year on special teams (10 appearances, 24 defensive snaps) before earning a starting role as a sophomore in 2023, when he led the Dukes with 108 tackles, added six tackles for loss, 1.5 sacks, an interception, and seven passes defended on his way to third-team All-Sun Belt honors. Fisher followed head coach Curt Cignetti to Indiana in 2024 and immediately became the anchor of a defense that set program records for fewest rushing yards and total yards allowed. He posted 118 tackles, 5.5 TFLs, and 1.5 sacks while earning first-team All-Big Ten and first-team All-America recognition from the FWAA and Phil Steele, a first for an Indiana linebacker. His senior year in 2025 brought another first-team All-Big Ten selection, 97 tackles, 10.5 TFLs, 4.5 sacks, two interceptions, a Butkus Award finalist nod, and a national championship. He finished his college career with 324 tackles across 50 games.

Strengths:
Vocal pre-snap communicator who gets the defense aligned and accounted for. Reads blocking schemes quickly and triggers downhill against the run with good timing. Consistently beats running backs to the gap on inside zone. Uses a subtle shoulder dip to avoid second-level blocks without losing ground. Reliable wrap-up tackler with good pad level between the tackles. Showed improved rush production as a senior, nearly tripling his sack total. Durable three-year starter who missed just one game in his career.

Weaknesses:
Undersized frame with limited room to add functional weight. Lacks the foot speed to handle backs on option routes in man coverage. Tight hips limit his ability to flip and run with crossers or play-action. Struggles to stack and shed when offensive linemen get hands on him at the second level. Open-field tackling drops off against shifty skill players in space. Allowed a high completion rate when targeted in zone coverage at Indiana.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Offense Rating: 79%
  Tackling: 77%
  Pass Rush: 71%
  Run Defense: 83%
  Coverage: 68%

bio: The right tackle spot at Maryland needed a starter in 2024, and Alan Herron answered after two seasons at Division II Shorter University, where he started every game at left and right tackle across the 2022 and 2023 campaigns. Rated the No. 19 offensive tackle in the portal by On3, he drew more than 30 offers and picked the Terps over Penn State, Clemson, Miami, Auburn, and Syracuse. Herron started all 24 games during his two years in College Park, locking down right tackle from the day he arrived. In 2025, he anchored an offensive line that allowed just nine sacks all season, fifth fewest nationally and second in the Big Ten. He earned Honorable Mention All-Big Ten and Academic All-Big Ten honors, along with the George C. Cook Memorial Award as the senior with the highest GPA on the team. His final stretch of college football included the Panini Senior Bowl, the American Bowl All-Star Game, and an invite to the 2026 NFL Scouting Combine.
strengths: Fires out of his stance with surprising quickness for a man his size on run plays. His 2025 pass sets looked nothing like 2024; the improvement week to week was visible. Keeps his inside hand active and placed well, rarely giving up clean wins across his face. Uses a snatch-trap counter that catches leaning rushers off guard and redirects their momentum. Moves bodies on down blocks and angle drives; real pop as the drive man on doubles. Wide enough through the trunk to wall off linebackers on second-level climbs. Never missed a start in four college seasons, including every snap at RT for Maryland. Showed flashes at the Senior Bowl of widening the corner against high-side moves in 1-on-1s.
weaknesses: Speed rushers bend the corner on him too easily; his set points don't expand enough. Long-arm sequences expose his chest and collapse his anchor into the pocket. Rolls onto his toes late in the drive phase of run blocks, losing power and control. Punch lands late and soft too often; rushers work through it with secondary effort. Run blocking on tape stays choppy even when his pass pro looks solid that same game. Shorter arms and lighter frame leave thin margins against NFL-caliber edge rushers.
player_comparisons: JamesHudson - Cincinnati (93%), AustinDeculus - LSU (90%), JaelynDuncan - Maryland (89%)

bio: Alton McCaskill from Oak Ridge High School was rated a 4-star recruit by ESPN and handed a 3-star grade by 247 Sports. Following high school, McCaskill joined Houston. In 2021 as a freshman McCaskill saw action in 14 games for the Cougars as he ran for 961 yards on 189 attempts for an excellent 5.1 yards per carry, with 16 TDs, with one fumble. As a receiver he made 21 receptions for 113 yards. After the season McCaskill transferred to Colorado where as a sophomore in 2023 he played in 4 games. and rushed for just 59 yards on 14 carries for 4.2 ypc, with no TDs, with no fumbles. In the passing game he pulled in just 2 receptions for 19 yards.
strengths: McCaskill has the size and toughness to be developed as a pass protector. He has the athleticism, quick feet and functional strength to set the edge and open running lanes. Difficult to bring down one-on-one in space, regularly sidesteps would-be tacklers and uses offhand to use oncoming defenders' momentum against them without slowing down significantly. McCaskill shows decent burst and straight-line speed to turn in big plays. An excellent blocker on the outside, McCaskill also adds value in the running game. Does not possess top breakaway speed, though he's rarely caught from behind.
weaknesses: McCaskill can sometimes be impatient, not allowing his blocks to set up, a nuance required for backs to last a decade-plus. Offers little in pass protection and poor technique cut blocking, fails to do more than slow down blitzers by laying down in front of them. A tall, upright runner who lacks great power as a runner. Not a quick-twitch athlete and is forced to gear down too much. Not very loose throughout his frame; looks tight redirecting. Will dance and leave his feet instead of taking what's there.
player_comparisons: JaTarviousWhitlow - Western Illinois (80%), TrestanEbner - Baylor (77%), BryantKoback - Toledo (74%)

bio: Andre Fuller's football journey didn't start with a blue-chip recruiting pedigree or a Power Five scholarship offer. The son of Fran Edmond and Jackson Louis, Fuller grew up in Florida, where he played well enough to earn a selection to the FACA All-Star Game coming out of high school. His college path began at Arkansas Pine-Bluff in the SWAC, and it was there, against FCS competition, that he first flashed the kind of ball-hawking instincts that would eventually land him on NFL radars. In 2021, Fuller put together a breakout campaign for the Golden Lions with 29 tackles, 14 pass breakups, and three interceptions across 11 games. His SWAC Defensive Player of the Week performance against Grambling State was the stuff of highlight reels: a 95-yard interception return for a touchdown and eight pass breakups in a single game. Fuller transferred to Toledo ahead of the 2022 season, stepping into a much more competitive MAC conference. He played in all 14 games as a sophomore Rocket, starting two, and logged 18 tackles, three pass breakups, a tackle for loss, and a sack. His workload expanded and his responsibilities grew. In 2023, he appeared in 14 more games but contributed only 11 tackles in a limited role. Then came a cruel setback: Fuller missed the entire 2024 season due to injury, sitting out what should have been a pivotal developmental year. He returned to the field in 2025 and made the most of it. Fuller earned First-Team All-MAC honors after recording 49 tackles, one interception, and 10 pass breakups while defending 11 total passes to finish fourth in the conference. He posted a career-high nine tackles against Central Michigan and grabbed his first interception as a Rocket at Western Michigan. Fuller also turned heads at the East-West Shrine Bowl, where GPS tracking clocked him as the fastest cornerback on the field at 20.52 mph. He was one of three Toledo defensive backs invited to the 2026 NFL Combine in Indianapolis, tying the program's all-time record for combine invitations.
strengths: Outstanding frame for an outside corner at 6-2, 202 pounds, with arm length that lets him control the line of scrimmage at the snap. Uses a heavy, well-timed jam at the release point that reroutes receivers off their stems and disrupts timing concepts early. Disciplined footwork in press allows him to stay balanced and let fakes declare before committing, avoiding false steps on stutter releases. Recorded GPS speed of 20.52 mph at the Shrine Bowl, showing legitimate straight-line burst that translates to recovery ability downfield. Strong run support defender who plays the edge with physicality, filling his gap and setting the edge without shying from contact against bigger ball carriers. Showed real improvement in contested catch situations during his final college season, shrinking the window and attacking the hands at the catch point. Brings special teams value with kick and punt coverage reps in his background, giving him an early pathway to contributing on game day. Career arc shows a player who kept grinding through an FCS start, a conference jump, and a lost season to injury, delivering his best ball as a senior.
weaknesses: Late hands in press can delay his transition out of his pedal, costing him a half-step when he needs to flip his hips and run with vertical threats. Hip stiffness is apparent on tape when asked to mirror complex route combinations; double moves and sharp breaks below the route create separation. Ball tracking with his back to the quarterback remains a real concern, as he too often plays the receiver's body rather than finding the football in the air. Tackling technique needs an overhaul; he runs into ball carriers upright and fails to drive through contact, leading to missed tackles and broken arm attempts. Competition level at Arkansas Pine-Bluff and in the MAC leaves legitimate questions about how his physical style will translate against NFL route runners with elite separation quickness.
player_comparisons: JasonMarshall Jr. - Florida (93%), NohlWilliams - California (92%), CobyBryant - Cincinnati (87%)

bio: A consensus three-star recruit out of Alabama, Anez Cooper chose Miami over Auburn, Georgia, and South Carolina after earning first-team all-state honors and finishing as a finalist for Lineman of the Year. He played center, guard, and tackle in high school before the Hurricanes planted him at right guard, where he would start 47 of his 51 career games across four seasons. Cooper cracked the starting lineup as a true freshman in 2022, earning four starts over Miami's final five games. He locked down the right guard spot full-time as a sophomore, starting all 13 games and picking up All-ACC Honorable Mention while anchoring a line recognized on the Joe Moore Award midseason honor roll. His junior year followed the same script: 13 starts at right guard, another honorable mention nod, and a key role on the nation's top-ranked offense in both points per game (43.9) and total yards per game (537.2). Cooper's senior campaign represented his strongest individual work. He started all 13 games on a Miami line that again earned Joe Moore Award recognition, and the Hurricanes closed with a 308-yard rushing performance in the Pop-Tarts Bowl against Iowa State.
strengths: Wide, proportional frame with excellent arm length for the position. Heavy hands that land with real force and stall rushers on contact. Tough to disengage from once he gets into your chest with extension. Good feel for working phone-booth pass protection and shrinking the pocket. Effective on combo blocks, transferring weight to collapse interior gaps. Feet are nimble enough to climb to second-level targets on inside runs. Plays with a physical edge and finishes through the whistle consistently. Scouting Report: Weaknesses Below-average lateral quickness leaves him exposed against athletic rushers. Pad level stays high too often, costing him leverage in the run game. Slow to process and react to interior stunts and delayed blitz games. Limited effectiveness as a puller or blocker on the move in space. Run blocking lacks consistent drive, often walling off rather than displacing. Penalty-prone tendencies show up when his hands get sloppy late in reps. Scouting Report: Summary Four years of starting experience at a Power Four program, over 1,700 pass-blocking reps, and a pressure rate that held up against quality ACC competition. Cooper's resume in pass protection is hard to argue with. He uses every inch of that 6-6 frame to crowd rushers early, and once his hands land, most interior defenders spend the rest of the rep trying to get free rather than getting to the quarterback. The senior year tape showed real growth in his ability to sustain through contact and recover when beaten initially, which is the kind of development that translates. The run game keeps this from being a cleaner evaluation. Cooper walls off defenders more than he moves them, and his pad level works against him when he needs to generate push. Reach blocks and wide zone assignments consistently gave him trouble, and his below-average foot speed limits what you can ask him to do on the move. He is most effective working down blocks, climbing short distances to the second level, and finishing combo blocks where his size does the heavy lifting. A gap or inside zone offense that values vertical movement over lateral athleticism is the right landing spot. Cooper can step in as a starting right guard in that kind of system and hold up from day one in pass protection, even if the run-game contributions take time to develop at the NFL level. The athletic limitations narrow his scheme range, but within the right structure, there is enough here for a potential starter who keeps the pocket clean and competes physically every snap. The floor is a swing guard who contributes immediately on passing downs. bio: Ryan High School in Denton, Texas, produced one of the most decorated prep linebackers in state history when Anthony Hill Jr. anchored a defense that allowed just 15.8 points per game as the Raiders rolled to a perfect 15-0 record and the 5A Division I state championship in 2020. The recruiting services took notice immediately. By the time Hill finished his prep career, he had racked up 303 tackles, 13 sacks, and nine forced fumbles while earning consensus five-star status, ranking inside the top 25 nationally across every major recruiting service. His athleticism extended beyond football too. He ran a leg on Ryan's 4x400 relay that captured silver at the state track meet. Hill chose to stay home and play for Steve Sarkisian at Texas, and he wasted no time proving the hype was warranted. As a true freshman in 2023, he earned a starting role by midseason and finished with 66 tackles, 8.0 tackles for loss, and 5.0 sacks across 14 games. His signature moment came in a road upset at No. 3 Alabama, where he recorded six tackles, two TFLs, and two sacks, including one on the Crimson Tide's final offensive play to seal the victory. The performance earned him FWAA Freshman All-American honors and co-Big 12 Defensive Freshman of the Year recognition. The 2024 campaign elevated Hill into the national spotlight. Starting all 16 games during Texas' transition to the SEC and College Football Playoff run, he exploded for 107 tackles (team high), 16.0 TFLs (SEC leader), 7.5 sacks, four forced fumbles, and an interception. He earned Second-Team All-American nods from the AP, AFCA, Sporting News, and Walter Camp while collecting Walter Camp National Defensive Player of the Week honors after an 11-tackle, 2-sack demolition of Oklahoma. His junior year in 2025 saw continued production with 69 tackles, 7.0 TFLs, 4.0 sacks, three forced fumbles, and two interceptions across 10 starts before a late-season injury ended his college career. He was named a Second-Team All-American by the AP, AFCA, and FWAA, and finished as a Butkus Award finalist and Bednarik Award semifinalist.
strengths: Exceptional sideline-to-sideline range with the elite speed that allows him to chase down plays from the backside and close on the ball with elite burst. Runs downhill with violence and bad intentions. His tackle grades are elite (90th percentile), and he finishes through contact with explosive hip rotation that creates highlight-reel collisions. Quick-trigger processor who reads keys at the snap and fires into gaps before blockers can get set. Rarely takes false steps or gets manipulated by misdirection, which lets him play faster than his testing suggests. Ball production is off the charts with eight career forced fumbles against SEC competition. He has a nose for the football and weaponizes his hands at the point of attack to separate runners from the rock. Natural pass rush ability that most off-ball linebackers simply do not possess. He recorded 17 career sacks with legitimate dip-and-bend moves that give offensive linemen problems when he comes on delayed blitzes. Clutch performer who delivered in Texas' bli> Coverage responsibilities at Texas were extensive. He shows fluid hips in zone drops, gets good depth to his landmarks, and uses his length to feel routes developing when pattern-matching. Long arms create an impressive tackle radius and allow him to keep blockeiggest moments. From sealing the Alabama upset as a freshman to dominant performances against Oklahoma and Georgia, he elevates when the spotlight is brightest.
weaknesses: Overly aggressive with his reads and occasionally overruns plays, creating cutback lanes when he commits too hard to his initial key without maintaining gap integrity. Man coverage against quick receiving backs remains a work in progress. Shows some hip stiffness when asked to mirror choice routes, and athletic backs can create separation on breaks. Tends to tackle high at times, which allows physical runners to churn out extra yards through contact. Needs to be more consistent playing with low pad level when wrapping up in space. Stack-and-shed technique against longer offensive linemen who get into his frame needs refinement. Once engaged, he can struggle to disengage and prefers to slip around blocks rather than work through them. Play-action can draw his eyes into the backfield too quickly. Gets caught flat-footed in his zone drops when he bites on the fake, leaving windows behind him.

name: Anthony HillJr
scouting_report:
Background:
Ryan High School in Denton, Texas, produced one of the most decorated prep linebackers in state history when Anthony Hill Jr. anchored a defense that allowed just 15.8 points per game as the Raiders rolled to a perfect 15-0 record and the 5A Division I state championship in 2020. The recruiting services took notice immediately. By the time Hill finished his prep career, he had racked up 303 tackles, 13 sacks, and nine forced fumbles while earning consensus five-star status, ranking inside the top 25 nationally across every major recruiting service. His athleticism extended beyond football too. He ran a leg on Ryan's 4x400 relay that captured silver at the state track meet. Hill chose to stay home and play for Steve Sarkisian at Texas, and he wasted no time proving the hype was warranted. As a true freshman in 2023, he earned a starting role by midseason and finished with 66 tackles, 8.0 tackles for loss, and 5.0 sacks across 14 games. His signature moment came in a road upset at No. 3 Alabama, where he recorded six tackles, two TFLs, and two sacks, including one on the Crimson Tide's final offensive play to seal the victory. The performance earned him FWAA Freshman All-American honors and co-Big 12 Defensive Freshman of the Year recognition. The 2024 campaign elevated Hill into the national spotlight. Starting all 16 games during Texas' transition to the SEC and College Football Playoff run, he exploded for 107 tackles (team high), 16.0 TFLs (SEC leader), 7.5 sacks, four forced fumbles, and an interception. He earned Second-Team All-American nods from the AP, AFCA, Sporting News, and Walter Camp while collecting Walter Camp National Defensive Player of the Week honors after an 11-tackle, 2-sack demolition of Oklahoma. His junior year in 2025 saw continued production with 69 tackles, 7.0 TFLs, 4.0 sacks, three forced fumbles, and two interceptions across 10 starts before a late-season injury ended his college career. He was named a Second-Team All-American by the AP, AFCA, and FWAA, and finished as a Butkus Award finalist and Bednarik Award semifinalist.

Strengths:
Exceptional sideline-to-sideline range with the elite speed that allows him to chase down plays from the backside and close on the ball with elite burst. Runs downhill with violence and bad intentions. His tackle grades are elite (90th percentile), and he finishes through contact with explosive hip rotation that creates highlight-reel collisions. Quick-trigger processor who reads keys at the snap and fires into gaps before blockers can get set. Rarely takes false steps or gets manipulated by misdirection, which lets him play faster than his testing suggests. Ball production is off the charts with eight career forced fumbles against SEC competition. He has a nose for the football and weaponizes his hands at the point of attack to separate runners from the rock. Natural pass rush ability that most off-ball linebackers simply do not possess. He recorded 17 career sacks with legitimate dip-and-bend moves that give offensive linemen problems when he comes on delayed blitzes. Clutch performer who delivered in Texas' bli> Coverage responsibilities at Texas were extensive. He shows fluid hips in zone drops, gets good depth to his landmarks, and uses his length to feel routes developing when pattern-matching. Long arms create an impressive tackle radius and allow him to keep blockeiggest moments. From sealing the Alabama upset as a freshman to dominant performances against Oklahoma and Georgia, he elevates when the spotlight is brightest.

Weaknesses:
Overly aggressive with his reads and occasionally overruns plays, creating cutback lanes when he commits too hard to his initial key without maintaining gap integrity. Man coverage against quick receiving backs remains a work in progress. Shows some hip stiffness when asked to mirror choice routes, and athletic backs can create separation on breaks. Tends to tackle high at times, which allows physical runners to churn out extra yards through contact. Needs to be more consistent playing with low pad level when wrapping up in space. Stack-and-shed technique against longer offensive linemen who get into his frame needs refinement. Once engaged, he can struggle to disengage and prefers to slip around blocks rather than work through them. Play-action can draw his eyes into the backfield too quickly. Gets caught flat-footed in his zone drops when he bites on the fake, leaving windows behind him.

bio: Anthony Lucas was one of the top defensive line recruits in the 2022 class, earning Under Armour All-American honors and a five-star rating from 247 Sports after a senior season at Narbonne High School in Harbor City, California, that produced 64 tackles, 22 tackles for loss, and 9.5 sacks. Rivals gave him a 6.0 grade with four stars, and ESPN tagged him with an 83 rating. Lucas signed with Texas A&M and played in seven games as a true freshman in 2022, collecting 7 tackles with 1 for loss. He transferred to USC that spring and appeared in all 13 games in 2023, recording 10 tackles with 3.5 for loss off the bench. His 2024 junior season started with six starts at defensive end, but a lower leg injury against Penn State ended his year after 16 tackles and a pass breakup. Healthy for his senior year in 2025, Lucas posted career highs of 37 total tackles, 3.5 tackles for loss, and 3.0 sacks. He measured 6-6, 267 pounds at the NFL Combine, where he reported informal meetings with the Jets, Texans, Bears, and Chiefs.
strengths: Outstanding length with a seven-foot wingspan that smothers blocking surfaces. Improved steadily each season, with his best all-around tape coming in 2025. Good tackler who rarely misses; kept his whiff rate under nine percent as a senior. Hits with heavy pads and finishes through contact when he arrives on time. Quick enough to slip around tight ends working as second-level blockers. Lines up at multiple spots along the defensive line, mostly as a base end. Showed real growth as a pass rusher in 2025, generating pressure at a noticeably higher rate. Reliable edge setter whose run defense grades climbed each year at USC.
weaknesses: Below-average burst off the snap; lacks the twitch to threaten tackles early. Career sack total sits at just three across over 1,100 defensive snaps. Gathers himself before contact on blocks rather than striking with urgency. Durability is a real question after the 2024 season-ending lower leg injury. Limited counter-move development when his initial rush plan stalls out. Lateral quickness is a concern, especially as a looper on twist games. Light for a defensive end at 267 pounds; sits in the lower range for the position.

bio: Reed-Adams came out of DeSoto High School in the Dallas area as a two-to-three-star recruit, depending on the service, without much Power Five buzz. He signed with Kansas in 2020 and spent four seasons in Lawrence, slowly working his way from a raw freshman into a full-time starter by 2023, when he logged 605 snaps across 11 starts at left guard. The Jayhawks went 9-4 that year, and Reed-Adams held down multiple spots along the interior. He transferred to Texas A&M ahead of the 2024 season, reportedly drawn by a family connection to the school. The move paid off immediately. Reed-Adams locked in at right guard, started all 12 games, and was a key piece of a rushing attack that ranked second in the SEC at 195.5 yards per game. The Aggies reeled off stretches of 300-plus yard rushing games for the first time in over a decade with Reed-Adams clearing lanes up front. He returned for 2025 as a team captain and earned preseason All-SEC and AP preseason All-American recognition. He started 11 of 13 games and was named AP second-team All-American, though his on-field production took a noticeable step back from his strong 2024 campaign.
strengths: Good length at 6'6" with long arms that help him recover when beaten initially. Generates real power on drive blocks when he gets his hips involved at contact. Generally finds his anchor against bull rushes and holds the point of attack. Showed clear improvement in pass protection technique during his 2024 season at A&M. Plays with an edge and competes through the whistle on every rep. Adequate feel for stunts and games, rarely loses his assignment completely on designed movement. Durable starter with over 2,400 career snaps and 55 games of experience across two programs.
weaknesses: 2025 tape showed clear regression in hand usage, pad level, and overall consistency. Penalty-prone throughout his career, flagged 31 times in six college seasons. Lacks the lateral quickness to consistently reach second-level blocks on the move. Body composition and base narrow under fatigue, leading to balance issues late in drives. Pass protection remains a concern, especially against quicker interior rushers with counter moves. Gets handsy and grabs when he loses positioning, inviting flags at the next level. Was rotated off the field at times in 2025, raising conditioning questions.

bio: Armoni Goodwin from Hewitt-Trussville High School was rated a 4-star recruit by ESPN and handed a 4-star grade by 247 Sports. Following high school, Goodwin joined LSU. In 2021 as a freshman Goodwin saw action in 6 games for the Tigers as he ran for only 65 yards on 16 attempts for 4.1 yards per carry, with no TDs, with no fumbles. As a receiver he made only 2 receptions for 5 yards. As a sophomore in 2022 he played 7 games. and rushed for 267 yards on 45 carries for an impressive 5.9 ypc, with 5 TDs, with no fumbles. In the passing game he pulled in only 4 receptions for 19 yards.
strengths: He’s capable as a receiver, a weapon on swing passes and in the screen game. Goodwin accelerates 0-to-60 and hits top speed fast enough to outrun angles. He’s also flexible and agile, able to keep the pedal to the floor as Goodwin turns and weaves. He has very good vision and a knack for picking out cut-back lanes and then breaking offchunks of yardage. Has a smaller, compact build with adequate thickness throughout his frame. Confident and ambitious player who wants the ball. Has shifty footwork with the natural burst and lateral quickness to make defenders miss and turn upfield in a hurry - tough to tackle in space. He brings a nice blend of power and passing game skills. He has a compact build and a naturally low center of gravity, and he runs with excellent balance through traffic, allowing him to squeeze through for yardage. Goodwin shows suddenness off the line of scrimmage and accelerates quickly, and his top-end speed is among the best in this draft.
weaknesses: Short and lacks the ideal powerful frame to consistently run between the tackles. He’ll be relegated to outside runs and is little more than a potential cut blocker in blitz pickup Does not always read blocks behind pulling guards. May not be big enough to move piles at the next level and has a tendency to bury his head too soon.

bio: Billy Schrauth came to South Bend as part of Notre Dame's 2022 recruiting class, a consensus four-star guard prospect who earned high marks from every major recruiting service. The kind of recruit who doesn't need to redshirt, Schrauth saw meaningful action right away in 2023, logging 257 snaps across multiple games as a rotational interior lineman, primarily at right guard. His 2024 season marked the real arrival. Schrauth locked down the left guard spot and started all 12 games he appeared in, racking up 700 offensive snaps. He also saw 139 snaps at right guard that year, flashing the kind of positional flexibility that offensive line coaches covet. The performance wasn't always pretty on tape, but he allowed just one sack across 390 pass blocking reps and kept the Irish pocket clean enough to let the offense function at a high level. He missed four games with an ankle injury, a frustrating preview of what was to come. In 2025, Schrauth was named team captain and took on an even larger leadership role within Notre Dame's offensive line. Through seven starts at left guard, he was playing some of the best football of his career. Then a sprained MCL against USC ended his season with five games still to play. In total across three college seasons, Schrauth accumulated 1,393 offensive snaps over 28 games, allowed only three sacks and 16 total pressures, and drew just three penalties.
strengths: Exceptional pass protector who allowed zero sacks and just two hurries across 213 pass blocking snaps in 2025, keeping the pocket remarkably clean on the interior. Gets out of his stance with smooth lateral quickness and shows real fluidity in his feet for a 305-pound guard who ran a 5.25 forty at his size. Anchors with a vengeance against bull rushers by throwing his hips back and rooting into the ground, rarely giving up his chest or getting walked into the quarterback's lap. Hand placement is precise and violent at the point of attack, with the ability to jolt defenders backward on combo blocks and deliver real knock-back power on screens. Takes smart, efficient angles when climbing to the second level, arriving with enough force to make linebackers feel it and sustaining through the whistle. Keeps his elbows tight to his frame through contact, which allows him to convert leg drive into displacement rather than wasting energy with wide, sloppy hand fighting. Wore the captain's "C" for a reason. Pre-snap communication, stunt recognition, and assignment reliability were all on full display throughout his time as a starter in South Bend. Positional versatility adds real value. He logged over 1,000 snaps at left guard and 370 at right guard across his career, and scouting reports mention tackle experience as well.
weaknesses: Durability is the elephant in the room. A 2024 ankle injury cost him four games, then a 2025 MCL sprain shut him down for the final five, and NFL medical staffs will scrutinize both thoroughly. Can get caught chasing fakes and setup moves when isolated against pass rushers with a real plan, drifting off his landmark and opening up interior rush lanes. Run blocking grades lagged well behind his pass protection numbers, and he doesn't always generate the consistent push you want from a guard in gap-heavy rushing concepts. At 305 pounds, he's on the lighter end of the spectrum for guards in this class, and that frame could get stressed against NFL nose tackles who carry 320-plus. Pad level creeps up on him at times during extended blocks, and when he gets tall through contact, his leverage advantage disappears and defenders can shed more easily.

bio: Bishop Fitzgerald played quarterback at Gar-Field High School in Woodbridge, Virginia, leading the program to its first district title since 1994 as a senior in 2021. He threw a 97-yard touchdown and ran for a 39-yard score in the championship, earning offensive player of the year honors. But with the pandemic shifting Virginia's football schedule and most recruiting classes locked in, college offers at quarterback dried up. Fitzgerald landed at Coffeyville Community College in Kansas, switched to safety, and immediately took off. In 2022 he posted 50 tackles, 8 pass breakups, and 6 interceptions, finishing as the consensus No. 1 JUCO safety in the country. NC State signed him, and he produced two solid years in Raleigh: 97 tackles, 16 pass breakups, and 5 interceptions across 26 games, earning All-ACC honorable mention in 2024. His transfer to USC in January 2025 was supposed to add depth. Instead, Fitzgerald seized the starting job and led the Big Ten with 5 interceptions in just 10 games before a late-season knee injury cost him the final two contests. That season earned him Consensus All-American honors from AP, FWAA, and Walter Camp, and a Jim Thorpe Award semifinalist nod.
strengths: Hands are natural and soft. Catches the ball away from his body and secures it through contact. Coverage instincts are the best part of his game. Receivers look open and then he is just there. Sits in zone with patience, lets routes develop in front of him, then drives on the throw with conviction. Holds his own in man assignments too. Stays in phase through breaks and mirrors routes with clean footwork. Reads the quarterback's shoulders and gets his breaks started before the ball is out. The eyes never lie. The Purdue pick told you everything. Recognized the red zone concept pre-snap and jumped the route before it formed. Three schools, three systems, and the turnovers followed him to every stop. That is not scheme or luck. Picked up D'Anton Lynn's defense at USC in one offseason and started day one with no adjustment period. Combine positional drills showed smooth feet and fluid transitions that his testing numbers did not reflect.
weaknesses: Combine forty came in well below his pre-draft number, and the gap raises honest questions about his range. Vertical tested near the bottom of the safety group, which compounds the athletic concerns from the forty. Only ran two drills in Indianapolis. Teams are working with an incomplete picture of his movement profile. Takes poor angles in run support and gets washed by lead blockers more than you want to see. Gives up size to bigger tight ends and slot receivers, and it shows in contested situations near the boundary. Ten games at USC is a small window, and the late-season knee injury adds a medical question on top.

Teams are working with an incomplete picture of his movement profile. Takes poor angles in run support and gets washed by lead blockers more than you want to see. Gives up size to bigger tight ends and slot receivers, and it shows in contested situations near the boundary. Ten games at USC is a small window, and the late-season knee injury adds a medical question on top.

bio: Blake Shapen from Evangel Christian Academy High School was rated a 4-star recruit by ESPN and handed a 3-star grade by 247 Sports. After high school, Shapen opted to join Baylor. In 2021 as a freshman Shapen saw action in 5 games for the Bears as he threw for 596 yards on 87 attempts with a completion percent of 71.3 for an average of 6.9 yards per pass, with just 5 TDs, while taking 5 sacks, and an excellent QB rating of 109.2. He added 17 runs for a total of 104 yards at an average of 6.1 per run. In 2022 as a sophomore he played in 12 games as he passed for 2593 yards on 343 attempts with a completion percent of 64.1 for an average of 7.6 yards per pass, with 16 TDs, while also taking 20 sacks, and putting up an excellent QB rating of 90.4. He made 62 runs for a total of 206 yards at an average of 3.3 per rush. As a junior in 2023 Shapen played a total of 8 games. He passed for 2171 yards on 294 attempts and put up a completion percentage of 60.5 for an average of 7.4 yards per attempt, with 13 TDs, and allowing 23 sacks, with an excellent QB rating on the year of 93.8. As a runner he logged 71 runs for a total of 219 yards at an average of 4.7 per run. After season's end Shapen transferred to Mississippi State where as a senior Shapen saw action in 4 games as he threw for 975 yards on 109 attempts with a completion percent of 67.9 for an average of 8.9 yards per pass, with 8 TDs, while taking 11 sacks, and totaling a QB rating of 116.6.
strengths: He’s a pure athlete, loose-limbed and flexible. Shapen has the speed to pick up yardage in scramble situations. Ball placement is a strength. He has the natural instincts to make plays when forced to improvise. Picks up yards with his legs when needed. He’s a threat with his legs - his play-fakes are outstanding, and Shapen has the ability to manipulate safeties with his eyes. Sprays the ball anywhere on the field, especially when given a pocket from which to deliver. Will step up into pocket while looking downfield, reset his feet and deliver. Shapen plays with a confident demeanor and has the athleticism to move out of the pocket and create plays out of structure.
weaknesses: Undersized and short in stature - looks diminutive in the huddle. Shapen has to clean up his throwing motion when he’s on the move. Shapen was often too quick to pull the ball down and take off, and Shapen seemed to have trouble sifting through moving parts in the middle of the field. His arm is good but not great, and he loses accuracy and velocity when he is moved off his spot.

bio: Branson Robinson is a powerful running back from Georgia who brings a bruising style to the position. The 5'10", 220-pound back hails from Germantown, Mississippi, where he was a highly-touted four-star recruit. Robinson rushed for over 1,000 yards as a high school junior before joining the Bulldogs' loaded backfield. In his freshman season at Georgia, Robinson saw limited action but made the most of his opportunities. He appeared in 12 games, rushing for 341 yards on 68 carries for a solid 4.9 yards per attempt. His breakout performance came against Auburn, where he racked up 98 yards and a touchdown on 12 carries, earning SEC Freshman of the Week honors. Robinson capped off the season with two touchdowns in Georgia's national championship victory over TCU. Robinson's sophomore campaign was derailed before it began, as he suffered a ruptured patellar tendon in fall camp that forced him to miss the entire 2023 season. Despite the setback, he remained focused on his rehabilitation and was named to Bruce Feldman's College Football Freaks List for his remarkable strength in the weight room.
strengths: Super athletic - Made Bruce Feldman's 2023 Freak's List at #41. He Wrote: "the 5-10, 220-pound sophomore is one of the most powerful dudes in college football, pound for pound. His bench press this offseason went from 425 to 470 - insane numbers for a running back. He also squats 590 and power-cleaned 315. His broad jump is also elite at 10-7 and his vertical jump is 33 inches." Compact, muscular frame at 5'10" and 220 pounds provides natural leverage when running between the tackles Displays good vision and patience to set up blocks, particularly on inside zone runs Demonstrates solid ball security, rarely putting the ball on the ground Possesses the frame and strength to be an effective pass protector with proper coaching Shows good burst through the hole and ability to get to the second level quickly High character individual with strong work ethic - solid dedication to rehab
weaknesses: Lacks elite top-end speed to consistently break long runs at the NFL level Limited experience as a receiver out of the backfield, with only 2 receptions in his college career Coming off a significant knee injury that cost him his entire sophomore season Pass protection technique needs refinement, despite having the physical tools to excel May need to improve lateral agility to create more missed tackles in space against NFL defenders
player_comparisons: CharlesWilliams - UNLV (59%), MarcusWilliams Jr. - Louisiana Tech (57%), DenericPrince - Tulsa (55%)

bio: Brent Austin prepped at Cajon High School in San Bernardino, California, earning First Team All-League honors as both a junior and senior. He was also a sprinter on the track team, qualifying for the CIF Division I meet in the 100-meter dash and ranking fourth in the state in the 4x100 relay. Austin was a Dean's List student and helped Cajon win a CIF regional championship in 2019. Austin signed with James Madison under Curt Cignetti, playing in five games as a true freshman in 2022 before seeing action in 11 games as a sophomore in 2023, when he recorded an 81-yard pick-six against UConn. He transferred to USF for the 2024 season and started all 13 games, finishing with 48 tackles, two interceptions, and a team-best 10 pass breakups. He then moved to Cal for his senior year in 2025. At Cal, Austin started all 13 games and posted 42 tackles, 13 pass breakups (tied for second nationally), two forced fumbles, and a half sack. His signature play came against North Carolina, when he forced a fumble at the goal line on the Tar Heels' final offensive snap to preserve a 21-18 win. He earned Third Team All-ACC honors and was named to the East-West Shrine Bowl 1000.
strengths: His 2025 coverage tape at Cal is some of the best in this class for a zone corner. Receivers caught fewer than half the passes thrown his way in both 2024 and 2025. Feet are quiet and balanced in his backpedal. Flips his hips and goes without any gear change. Track background shows up when he has to carry a nine route. He stays in the hip pocket. Against Virginia, he was targeted 11 times and broke up six passes. Kept competing all game. The North Carolina forced fumble at the goal line was instinct, not luck. He read the play and attacked. Brought his missed tackles down noticeably from USF to Cal. Wraps up better now.
weaknesses: Under 180 pounds at the Shrine Bowl. Bigger receivers move him off his line in press. At Virginia Tech he gave up a TD and looked uncomfortable in man all night. Zone-to-man split is wide. Put him in press-man and the coverage thins out fast. Breaks up passes but does not finish. Only three interceptions across four college seasons. Does not want to take on blocks in run support. Gets washed down the line too easily. Three programs in four years. The talent kept him starting, but the pattern needs explaining.
player_comparisons: ChristianBraswell - Rutgers (91%), JaylinSimpson - Auburn (86%), JabbarMuhammad - Oregon (84%)

bio: Standing at 6'7" with a wiry 235-pound frame, Brett Norfleet carved out an impressive niche in Missouri's offensive attack during his first two collegiate seasons. The St. Charles, Missouri native burst onto the scene as a true freshman in 2023, earning SEC All-Freshman honors after working his way into the starting lineup midway through the season. His breakout performance came in the regular season finale against Arkansas, where he hauled in two touchdown passes to help cap off an outstanding rookie campaign that saw him record 18 catches for 197 yards and three scores. Norfleet's 2024 sophomore season was hampered by injury concerns that forced him to miss contests against Buffalo and Iowa, but he still managed to post solid production when healthy. Starting eight games throughout the year, he increased his reception total to 26 catches for 235 yards and two touchdowns while continuing to develop as both a receiver and in-line blocker. His most productive outing came against Arkansas once again, where he posted a season-high 50 receiving yards, demonstrating the kind of big-play ability that made him such a coveted recruit coming out of high school. The consensus four-star prospect was rated as high as the 242nd overall recruit nationally by 247Sports and ranked among the top tight end prospects in his class across all major recruiting services. What makes Norfleet's story particularly compelling is his two-sport background - he was heavily recruited as a Division I baseball pitcher before suffering a labrum injury that ultimately steered him toward focusing solely on football. His athleticism and competitive fire were evident early, as he earned all-state honors in both football and baseball at Francis Howell, helping lead his high school team to a Class 5 State Championship as a senior.
strengths: Imposing 6'7" frame creates massive catch radius and makes him a nightmare matchup for defensive backs in the red zone. Soft hands and reliable target who has shown consistent ability to secure passes in traffic throughout his collegiate career. Deceptive mobility for his size with surprising acceleration that allows him to create separation on intermediate routes and run after the catch. Physical presence as an in-line blocker who embraces contact and shows genuine nastiness when engaged with linebackers and safeties. High football IQ and route-running savvy that has allowed him to become a trusted target despite limited experience at the position. Clutch performer who rises to the occasion in big moments, evidenced by his multiple touchdown performances against quality SEC competition. Versatile skill set that allows him to align anywhere from in-line to slot to out wide, giving offensive coordinators flexibility in their formations. Strong work ethic and coachable mentality that has enabled rapid development since arriving at Missouri as a raw but talented prospect.
weaknesses: Lanky build - needs significant bulk to hold up against NFL-caliber pass rushers and linebackers in blocking assignments. Limited deep speed and acceleration means he's unlikely to threaten defenses vertically or create explosive plays down the seam consistently. Blocking technique remains inconsistent with footwork and hand placement issues that better athletes will exploit at the next level. Relatively small sample size of high-level production due to injuries and limited target share in Missouri's offensive system. Needs refinement in route running precision and timing, particularly on comeback routes and option concepts where quarterback chemistry is crucial.

bio: Recruited as the fifth-ranked center prospect nationally by Rivals, Brian Parker II came out of St. Xavier High School in Cincinnati as a consensus three-star who chose Duke over what were likely flashier offers. He helped St. Xavier win a state championship as a junior and earned back-to-back Greater Catholic League Offensive Lineman of the Year honors before heading to Durham. Athletics clearly run in the family: his cousin, Dereck Lively II, played one season of basketball at Duke before becoming a first-round NBA pick. Parker's college career tells the story of steady, deliberate growth. After logging just eight snaps across two games as a true freshman in 2022, he earned seven starts in 2023 and won the team's offensive rookie of the year award. That season, Duke's offensive line ranked third in the ACC in sacks allowed per game and surrendered the fewest rushing yards lost in the conference. By 2024, Parker had locked down the right tackle spot for all 13 games, earning second-team All-ACC recognition and producing one of the most dominant pass-blocking seasons in college football. He played over 500 pass-blocking snaps that year without allowing a single sack, the most in the country at that clip, and surrendered only one quarterback hit all season. His senior year was the crescendo. Parker earned All-America recognition from multiple outlets and was named first-team All-ACC by the Associated Press. He anchored a Duke offensive line that powered the program to its first outright ACC championship since 1962, a 27-20 overtime thriller against Virginia that capped a historic season. The Blue Devils set program records for points scored, total touchdowns, and passing efficiency while fielding the school's first-ever trio of a 3,000-yard passer, 1,000-yard receiver, and 1,000-yard rusher. Parker started all 13 regular-season games at right tackle before declaring for the draft ahead of the Sun Bowl. He graduated in December 2025 with a degree in economics, a two-time Academic All-ACC selection who logged over 2,400 career snaps across 40 games and 33 starts.
strengths: Outstanding pass protection efficiency throughout his career, rarely allowing clean shots to the quarterback and showing an uncanny feel for timing his sets. Works with tight, inside hand placement and real grip strength that lets him lock onto defenders and steer them where he wants them to go. Footwork is remarkably clean and controlled; he reaches his landmarks on zone combinations and second-level climbs without wasting motion or lunging. Flashes genuine nastiness at the point of attack with an underhook punch that carries surprising pop for a guy who does not win with raw mass. Mirror and redirect skills in pass protection are well above average; he keeps his feet underneath him and slides with rushers through their counter moves. Proven ability to recognize and pick up stunts, twists, and blitz games before they develop, consistently passing off and picking up new threats at the line. Shrine Bowl performance showed he can snap the ball, make protection calls, and handle interior responsibilities with a very short learning curve. Year-over-year improvement on tape is dramatic and consistent, jumping from a solid-but-raw redshirt freshman to one of the best offensive linemen in the ACC by his final season.
weaknesses: Below-average arm length and overall frame size limit his ability to keep power rushers at bay and create separation at the point of attack. Lacks the lower-body drive strength to consistently move defenders off the ball in gap-scheme run blocking; gets stuck at the point of contact too often. Anchor can get compromised against true bull rushers who can walk him back into the pocket, especially when he catches hands late. Tendency to dip his head and drop his eyes into contact, which leaves him susceptible to whiffing on moving targets and losing sight of his assignment. Recovery speed once a rusher has turned the corner or gotten into his edge is only average; needs to win early in the rep or he is in trouble.
