bio: Dan Lanning's open tryouts after taking over at Oregon in 2022 turned up a find nobody saw coming. Bryce Boettcher had walked on as a baseball player out of South Eugene High School, spending his freshman year patrolling center field. Rated a three-star recruit in high school where he starred in three sports, he earned a football roster spot through those tryouts and never looked back. He transitioned from defensive back to inside linebacker over two seasons, recording 37 tackles in 2023 while also hitting .276 with 12 home runs on the diamond. That spring performance earned him a collegiate Gold Glove and a 13th-round selection by the Houston Astros in the 2024 MLB Draft. Boettcher chose football over the minors and became Oregon's defensive leader. He posted 94 tackles, 8.0 tackles for loss, and 2.0 sacks in 2024, winning the Burlsworth Trophy as the nation's top walk-on and earning Second-Team All-Big Ten honors. He returned for a fifth year in 2025, tallying 104 tackles and another interception while again receiving second-team conference recognition. Across those two seasons, Oregon went 26-3 with Boettcher calling the defense.
strengths: Good coverage instincts for a linebacker; tightens throwing windows in zone and tracks the ball well downfield. Baseball background gives him natural ball-tracking skills and soft hands when contesting passes. Consistent tackler who wraps up and brings his feet; missed tackle rate stayed around 9-10% as a full-time starter. Communicates well pre-snap and owns the green dot role; gets teammates aligned and sorted before the ball is snapped. Moves fluidly laterally and can mirror running backs in the hole before committing. Willing to shoot gaps and play downhill when he reads run; not hesitant despite his size. Five-phase special teams contributor with the effort and awareness to fill multiple coverage and return units. Competitive makeup stands out on film; walk-on chip still shows in how hard he plays through the whistle.
weaknesses: Undersized at the position with short arms, which limits his ability to stack and shed blocks. Struggles to hold ground against offensive linemen who get their hands on him in the run game. Steep downhill angle against the run costs him containment on outside zone and stretch plays. Bites hard on play-action fakes and loses his landmark in coverage as a result. Average straight-line speed limits his chase ability when runners turn the corner. Older prospect who will be 24 as a rookie, which caps the developmental runway. Pass rush production is thin; lacks a reliable plan or the length to convert pressures into sacks.

bio: Bryce Foster, a 6'5", 330-pound center from Texas A&M, has been a cornerstone of the Aggies' offensive line since his freshman year. Born and raised in Katy, Texas, Foster's athletic prowess was evident early on, winning the Texas 6A shot put title as a senior with a throw of 71'1" and placing third in the discus. His high school football career at Katy Taylor High School was equally impressive, earning unanimous first-team all-district selections in his junior and senior years. Foster's transition to college football was seamless. As a freshman in 2021, he started all 12 games at center, helping A&M accumulate 595 yards of total offense in his collegiate debut against Kent State. His performance earned him spots on the SEC All-Freshman Team, FWAA Freshman All-America Team, and The Athletic Freshman All-America First Team. Foster's sophomore season in 2022 was cut short due to injury after four starts, but he bounced back in 2023, starting all 12 games he appeared in. Throughout his college career, Foster has demonstrated his ability to anchor the offensive line in both pass and run scenarios. In 2023, he helped the Aggies record over 300 yards passing in three games and over 200 yards rushing in three games. His performance in the Texas Bowl was particularly noteworthy, as he helped the offense pass for 372 yards while the offensive line allowed only two sacks in 35 pass attempts. Foster's consistency and durability are evident in his 28 career starts over three seasons.
strengths: Exceptional size and mass for the center position, measuring 6'5" and 330 pounds, providing a formidable presence in the interior offensive line Made Bruce Feldman's Freak's List - ranked #51. He wrote "Back in high school he could bench-press more than 400 pounds, and the 6-5, 325-pounder has only gotten more powerful. Foster, a sophomore, is an elite shot putter with a personal record of 64’ 8.75”, which ranks fifth best all-time for the Aggies." Impressive football IQ, demonstrated by his ability to identify defensive looks and communicate effectively with his linemates Extensive starting experience (28 games) against top-tier SEC competition, indicating readiness for NFL-level challenges Remarkable durability, having started every game in two of his three seasons, showcasing reliability and toughness Versatile blocking skills, showing proficiency in both pass protection and run blocking schemes Elite shot putter with a personal record of 64' 8.75", indicating explosive power and coordination that translates to football
weaknesses: Lacks ideal arm length Inconsistent in generating consistent push against zero-techs and shaded nose tackles in the run game Occasionally catches rather than punches, allowing defenders into his chest and compromising his base Needs refinement in hand usage - can be late to reset hands when initial punch is defeated Raw technique doesn't always match his physical tools - must improve consistency to maximize his athletic gifts
player_comparisons: JonahMonheim - USC (99%), CharlesTurner III - LSU (82%), DrewKendall - Boston College (82%)

bio: Byron Cardwell from Morse High School was rated a 4-star recruit by ESPN and handed a 4-star grade by 247 Sports. Following high school, Cardwell joined Oregon. In 2021 as a freshman Cardwell saw action in 11 games for the Ducks as he ran for 417 yards on 61 attempts for an elite 6.8 yards per carry, with 3 TDs, with no fumbles. As a receiver he made only 4 receptions for 3 yards. As a sophomore in 2022 he played just 2 games. and rushed for only 76 yards on 11 carries for an elite 6.9 ypc, with one TD, with no fumbles. In the passing game he pulled in only 4 receptions for 34 yards.
strengths: He’s patient, letting blocks develop then accelerating. Cardwell runs with a good forward lean and consistently falls forward. Flashes initial burst and good lateral quickness to sidestep defenders or bounce runs outside with very good foot quickness. Displays very good feel as an inside and outside runner with above average vision to make sharp cuts - good patience· and footwork to pick through defenses. Has the feet and feel running between the tackles and in the open field. Cardwell has very good vision and patience to pick through defenses. His stop-start ability in traffic, exceptional balance, lateral agility and body control allow him to squeeze out chunks of yardage when it appears that nothing is there. He’s not a burner, and Cardwell is more of a block-what’s-given runner than a guy who is going to create. He’s at his best working in space, showing the quickness and vision of a return specialist. His stop-start ability allows him to create initial separation.
weaknesses: Cardwell can sometimes be impatient, not allowing his blocks to set up, a nuance required for backs to last a decade-plus. His playing speed is less than elite. He won’t run past defenders like Cardwell did at the college level. Cardwell sometimes get too upright and fails to run behind his pads.
player_comparisons: JermarJefferson - Oregon State (80%), LewNichols III - Central Michigan (71%), VavaeMalepeai - USC (70%)

bio: CJ Daniels took one of the more winding roads to draft eligibility you'll find in this class. A two-star recruit out of Parkview High School in Georgia, where he hauled in 93 catches for 1,411 yards and 15 touchdowns as a senior, Daniels landed at Liberty in 2020 with almost no national recruiting buzz. He saw the field right away as a true freshman, catching 11 passes for 220 yards and 3 scores in 11 appearances, flashing a 20.0 yards-per-catch average that hinted at what was coming. An injury-wrecked 2022 limited him to just four games and 3 catches, but he bounced back in a major way the following year. The 2023 season is where Daniels put himself on the national map. He led Liberty in every receiving category with 55 catches for 1,067 yards and 10 touchdowns, earning Second-Team All-Conference USA honors while averaging a whopping 19.4 yards per reception. Five 100-yard games punctuated that campaign, including a pair of 157-yard outings against Sam Houston State and New Mexico State in the Conference USA Championship Game. He capped the year with 8 catches for 79 yards in the Fiesta Bowl against No. 6 Oregon, as Liberty finished 13-1 and made its first ever New Year's Six Bowl appearance. Across four seasons with the Flames, Daniels totaled 106 receptions for 1,954 yards and 21 touchdowns. Looking for a bigger stage, Daniels transferred to LSU for the 2024 season, where he started 10 of 11 games and finished fourth on the team with 42 catches for 480 yards. A nagging foot injury cost him the Ole Miss game and limited his effectiveness down the stretch. He entered the portal again that December and landed at Miami for 2025, where he posted 50 catches for 557 yards and 7 touchdowns through 10 games, including a playoff appearance against Ohio State in the Cotton Bowl. Across six collegiate seasons and three programs, Daniels has compiled 198 career receptions for 2,988 yards and 28 touchdowns.
strengths: Fluid hips and quick feet allow him to sink, snap off routes, and generate separation at the top of his breaks with consistent quickness that makes life tough on defensive backs. Natural ball tracker who locates throws over his shoulder with ease, adjusting his body mid-stride and extending to pluck passes away from his frame at the catch point. Put on an elite drop rate performance at LSU in 2024, recording zero drops on 42 catches and posting a 91.4 drop grade that season, showcasing dependable hands. Willing and capable run blocker who Miami trusted as a move blocker in their screen game and pre-snap motion packages, consistently sealing off defenders in the ground attack. Smart route runner who uses head fakes, tempo changes, and leverage manipulation to sell routes and create windows rather than relying purely on athletic traits. Versatile alignment profile, lining up predominantly outside (83% of snaps in 2025) while also showing comfort working from the slot, giving coordinators real flexibility. Competitive toughness shows up in contested catch situations, converting 11 of 17 contested targets at Miami for a 64.7% rate while also drawing 3 pass interference calls. Solid yards-after-catch producer who forced 31 missed tackles across his career and racked up 5.9 YAC per reception during his monster 2023 breakout at Liberty.
weaknesses: Lacks the extra gear to pull away from coverage on vertical routes, and while his forty is respectable, his play speed doesn't always match that number on tape. Production took a clear step back at the Power Four level, with his yards per route run dropping from 3.94 at Liberty in 2023 to 1.32 at LSU and 1.73 at Miami. Drop rate spiked to 9.8% during that big 2023 season at Liberty (6 drops on 61 catchable targets), and he added 2 more drops on 53 catchable balls at Miami in 2025. Does not always attack the football aggressively at the catch point, occasionally letting the ball travel to him rather than working back toward the throw, inviting disruption. Contested catch conversion of just 52.4% during his 2021 season and a career mark of 62.3% suggest he can get muscled off spots by physical corners when he doesn't initiate contact.
player_comparisons: LukeMcCaffrey - Rice (93%), KevinAustin Jr. - Notre Dame (88%), RomeoDoubs - Nevada (87%)

bio: Before he ever suited up in orange, Cade Klubnik was already building a resume that most Texas high school quarterbacks can only dream about. Growing up in Austin and playing at Westlake High School, the same program that produced Drew Brees and Nick Foles, Klubnik guided the Chaparrals to three consecutive state championships from 2019 to 2021. He never lost a game as a starter, going 27-0 over his final two seasons while compiling an absurd 86-to-7 touchdown-to-interception ratio through the air with another 31 scores on the ground. A track athlete who won the 400-meter state title as a sophomore, Klubnik was a consensus five-star prospect ranked as the top quarterback in the country by Rivals and Sports Illustrated. He earned Elite 11 MVP honors in the summer of 2021 and threw two touchdown passes in the All-American Bowl before enrolling early at Clemson in January 2022. Klubnik's college career has been defined by flashes of brilliance wrapped in stretches of inconsistency. He played sparingly as a true freshman in 2022 but turned heads when he came off the bench in the ACC Championship Game against North Carolina, completing 20 of 24 passes for 279 yards and earning MVP honors. He made his first career start in the Orange Bowl against No. 6 Tennessee, throwing for 320 yards. As the full-time starter beginning in 2023, he was steady if unspectacular, completing 290 of 454 passes for 19 touchdowns and nine picks while leading Clemson to a 9-4 finish and a comeback Gator Bowl win over Kentucky. Then came 2024, the season that put him on the national radar in a major way. Klubnik threw for 3,639 yards and 36 touchdowns against just six interceptions, added seven rushing scores, led Clemson to the ACC Championship, and earned a College Football Playoff berth. He torched Appalachian State with a 277.9 passer rating, hit a game-winning 50-yard rushing touchdown at Pitt, and threw four touchdowns in the ACC title game against SMU to become the third player in history to win multiple ACC Championship Game MVP awards. Expectations soared heading into 2025. Klubnik entered his senior year as a Heisman Trophy favorite with Clemson ranked in the top five nationally. The season that followed told a much different story. The Tigers stumbled to a 3-5 start before rallying to finish 7-5, and Klubnik's numbers reflected the turbulence: 2,943 yards, 16 touchdowns, and six interceptions across 12 starts. There were still moments of magic, like a near-perfect 22-of-24 performance at North Carolina with four touchdowns, and a career-high 385 yards against Duke, but the consistency that scouts needed to see simply was not there. He finished his Clemson career third in program history in passing yards with 10,123 and first all-time in completions with 916. Along the way, he became just the fifth Tiger quarterback to start 40 career games and the fourth to surpass 11,000 yards of total offense. Klubnik earned his marketing degree in December 2025 and declared for the draft shortly after a final game against Penn State in the Pinstripe Bowl.
strengths: Compact, quick-trigger release that gets the ball out in a hurry, with the ability to vary arm slots and still maintain accuracy on rhythm throws across the short and intermediate levels. Exceptional touch on the deep ball, particularly on posts and seam routes where he drops the football over defenders with a high arc that gives his receivers every chance to run under it. Operates at his best working off play action and RPO concepts, where he reads second-level defenders with patience and attacks the voids their false steps create with real conviction. Straight-line speed is legit for the position, evidenced by a 4.6/7 forty and a track background that shows up on designed quarterback runs and scramble situations where he can turn the corner. Pocket awareness stands out on tape, particularly his ability to sense interior pressure without seeing it, using subtle body movements and shoulder fakes to slide away from collapsing edges and keep his eyes downfield. Dominant throwing to the sideline against Cover 3, especially on back-shoulder fades and comeback routes where his ball placement consistently puts receivers in position to make the catch away from leverage. Showed real growth in his ability to attack man coverage with anticipation, throwing receivers open rather than waiting for separation, and ripping confident glance RPOs even when his targets were not helping him out. Toughness is not a question mark. He played behind a below-average offensive line for much of his career, took significant hits, and kept showing up. Teammates voted him a permanent captain for a reason.
weaknesses: Post-snap processing speed remains a real concern, as he too often locks onto his pre-snap read and gets caught flat-footed when the coverage rotates after the ball is snapped. Downfield arm strength has a ceiling that will be tested at the next level, and receivers have to slow down on nine routes, which closes windows that NFL corners will not leave open. Mechanical consistency with his lower half comes and goes; when his feet get sloppy, his platform suffers, velocity fades, and throws spray high, particularly when he is on the move. Tendency to trust his legs over his arm in clean pocket situations, checking the ball down or pulling it to run when there is room to push throws downfield and let receivers work. Protection calls and pre-snap command at the line of scrimmage were largely ineffective at Clemson, and his hot-route answers against pressure looked underdeveloped throughout his career.
player_comparisons: QuinnEwers - Texas (83%), MichaelPratt - Tulane (83%), ClaytonTune - Houston (82%)

Dominant throwing to the sideline against Cover 3, especially on back-shoulder fades and comeback routes where his ball placement consistently puts receivers in position to make the catch away from leverage. Showed real growth in his ability to attack man coverage with anticipation, throwing receivers open rather than waiting for separation, and ripping confident glance RPOs even when his targets were not helping him out. Toughness is not a question mark. He played behind a below-average offensive line for much of his career, took significant hits, and kept showing up. Teammates voted him a permanent captain for a reason.

Weaknesses:
Post-snap processing speed remains a real concern, as he too often locks onto his pre-snap read and gets caught flat-footed when the coverage rotates after the ball is snapped. Downfield arm strength has a ceiling that will be tested at the next level, and receivers have to slow down on nine routes, which closes windows that NFL corners will not leave open. Mechanical consistency with his lower half comes and goes; when his feet get sloppy, his platform suffers, velocity fades, and throws spray high, particularly when he is on the move. Tendency to trust his legs over his arm in clean pocket situations, checking the ball down or pulling it to run when there is room to push throws downfield and let receivers work. Protection calls and pre-snap command at the line of scrimmage were largely ineffective at Clemson, and his hot-route answers against pressure looked underdeveloped throughout his career.

bio: Caleb Downs was born into football royalty. His father Gary spent time with the Giants, Broncos, and Falcons as a running back, while his uncle Dre Bly carved out a two-time Pro Bowl career at cornerback and collected a Super Bowl ring with the Rams. His older brother Josh currently runs routes for the Colts. The Downs family tree reads like a NFL personnel directory, and Caleb appears destined to add his own chapter to that legacy. Standing 6'0" and weighing 205 pounds, he arrived at Alabama in 2023 as the consensus No. 1 safety in America, a five-star recruit with a 6.1 Rivals grade and 0.99 rating from 247Sports. That true freshman season in Tuscaloosa set the tone for everything that followed. Downs became the first freshman in Alabama history to lead the team in tackles, racking up 107 stops along with 3.5 tackles for loss, two picks, and a forced fumble. The SEC coaches named him Freshman of the Year, and Pro Football Focus gave him first-team All-American honors before he had even attended his first college spring practice as a sophomore. He also found time to take a punt 85 yards to the house. When Nick Saban retired that offseason, Downs entered the transfer portal as its most coveted prize and landed in Columbus. The move paid immediate dividends for Ryan Day's program. Downs earned unanimous All-American status in 2024 while helping the Buckeyes capture a national championship, finishing with 82 tackles, two interceptions (including one to seal the Cotton Bowl win over Texas), and six pass breakups. He also housed another punt return, this time a 79-yarder against Indiana that marked Ohio State's first punt return touchdown in a decade. His 2025 campaign brought the Jim Thorpe Award as the nation's best defensive back, a ninth-place finish in Heisman voting, and Big Ten Defensive Player of the Year honors. He departs Columbus with 257 career tackles, six interceptions, 12 pass deflections, and three forced fumbles across three seasons at college football's highest level.
strengths: Processes information at warp speed before the snap and after it, diagnosing route combinations and run fits faster than most safeties can identify the formation. Operates as a legitimate chess piece who can align in the box, split the field as a deep half defender, or match up with tight ends and slot receivers in man coverage without losing a step. Tackles with controlled aggression and textbook technique, breaking down in space with excellent balance and wrapping through contact rather than lunging or diving. Shows cornerback-level fluidity in his hips when transitioning from backpedal to drive, allowing him to mirror route breaks and stay in-phase against quicker receivers. Commands the secondary like a veteran defensive coordinator, relaying coverage calls and adjusting alignments with an authority that belies his age. Displays excellent ball-hawking instincts when playing zone, subtly manipulating quarterback reads with his eyes and body position before breaking hard on throwing windows. Brings physicality as a run defender, taking on blocks with sound pad level and working through contact to set edges or fill gaps without getting washed out of his assignment. Offers legitimate special teams value as a punt returner with vision, burst, and the ability to make the first man miss in space.
honors: 2025 Jim Thorpe Award 2025 Lott IMPACT Trophy
weaknesses: Occasionally sells out for the highlight reel hit instead of securing the tackle, leading to overaggressive angles that create missed opportunities in pursuit. Can get caught peeking too long in the backfield when playing zone, which opens throwing lanes to crossing routes that sneak behind his drop. Gambles for interceptions at times when the safer play is simply getting a hand on the ball and ensuring the incompletion. Struggles somewhat when climbing offensive linemen reach him at the second level, needing to improve his hand usage to disengage from bigger bodies. Most of his tape came in two-high shells, leaving some questions about his range and comfort operating as a true single-high free safety over the deep middle.
player_comparisons: JaquanBrisker - Penn State (75%), MalachiMoore - Alabama (66%), BrianBranch - Alabama (64%)

bio: Caleb Tiernan was a two-way starter and four-year varsity basketball letterman at Detroit Country Day School in Beverly Hills, Michigan. As a senior defensive end he posted 59 tackles, 22 for loss, and 8.5 sacks while also starting at left tackle. He earned all-state, all-region, and all-conference recognition, was rated a four-star prospect by 247Sports and Rivals, and committed to Northwestern over Michigan, Ohio State, Penn State, and Nebraska. Tiernan redshirted in 2021, then started five of 11 games at right tackle in 2022. He moved to left tackle in 2023 and started all 13 games, logging 846 snaps and allowing just one sack. He put together an eight-game stretch without surrendering a single quarterback hit from Week 4 through Week 11. He started every game in both 2024 and 2025, earning All-Big Ten Honorable Mention as a junior and Second-Team All-Big Ten as a graduate student. He was named to PFF's national Team of the Week twice in 2025, including after a strong outing against Oregon, and went without allowing a sack in eight of nine conference games. Across his career, Tiernan logged nearly 3,000 offensive snaps and started at both tackle spots.
strengths: Pass protection is his best trait, with hand timing at the point of contact that looks NFL-ready. Patient, measured footwork in his pass set that keeps rushers guessing and rarely overcommits. Absorbs bull rushes through his core, anchoring without sitting into his hips or losing ground. Takes clean angles on cutoff blocks, especially effective on play-side reach blocks and backside seals. Basketball background shows up in how he moves through traffic on screens at 323 pounds. Reads twist games and delayed pressures well, picking up late blitzers even when left on an island. Combine testing confirmed real lower-body explosiveness with a 99th-percentile vertical jump. Five years of starting experience at both tackle spots, plus a team captain role, add proven dependability.
weaknesses: Short arms for his frame let defenders work into his body and create separation at the arc. Run blocking trails his pass protection, with inconsistent leg drive and too many stalemates. Tight hips limit his ability to redirect on combo blocks and climb to the second level. Pad level runs high, and disciplined defenders with low leverage can walk him back on gap schemes. Lateral agility is a tick slow when countered inside after committing to the edge.
player_comparisons: OzzyTrapilo - Boston College (87%), PatrickPaul - Houston (86%), MatthewBergeron - Syracuse (77%)

bio: Carson Hinzman, a center from Ohio State, enters the NFL Draft after starting 12 games for the Buckeyes in 2023. The Spring Valley, Wisconsin native redshirted his first year before winning the starting job as a redshirt freshman. Hinzman was a highly-touted recruit coming out of high school, earning four-star ratings from major recruiting services and multiple all-state selections. At Ohio State, Hinzman quickly became a key contributor on the offensive line, logging 758 offensive snaps in his first season as a starter. Off the field, he excelled academically, earning Big Ten Distinguished Scholar honors and maintaining a GPA above 3.70 while majoring in sport industry. However, Hinzman's hold on the starting role has been tenuous, as he faced stiff competition in spring practice from Alabama transfer Seth McLaughlin. Despite losing his starting spot for Ohio State's Cotton Bowl due to snapping issues, Hinzman returned stronger in spring practice. Offensive line coach Justin Frye noted Hinzman's improved physicality and explosiveness, praising his offseason development. At 6'4" and 300 pounds, he has prototypical size for an NFL center and has shown the ability to compete against top-tier talent.
strengths: Above-average athleticism for the position, displaying good movement skills and ability to reach the second level Quick learner who earned starting role early in career at a powerhouse program Intelligent player with a strong grasp of offensive concepts - super smart guy Solid frame with room to add functional strength as he develops physically Demonstrates resilience and competitiveness, bouncing back from setbacks to compete for starting role Adapts well to stunts and blitzes, showing good awareness in pass protection High character individual with strong work ethic and leadership potential Experience in a pro-style offense against top competition in the Big Ten
weaknesses: Struggles with consistency in snapping, leading to benching for bowl game Lacks ideal power at point of attack and can get overwhelmed by stronger interior defenders Still raw technically, particularly in hand usage and leverage Pass protection needs refinement, often relies too much on athleticism over technique Limited starting experience with only one full season under his belt
player_comparisons: NickBrahms - Auburn (94%), DawsonDeaton - Texas Tech (92%), RyanWalk - Oregon (88%)

bio: A three-star recruit out of Durango High School in Colorado, Carver Willis spent five seasons at Kansas State before transferring to Washington for his final year of eligibility. He redshirted in 2021, saw limited action in 2020 and 2022, and then earned a starting role at right tackle in 2023, picking up All-Big 12 Honorable Mention from the league's coaches. That K-State offensive line earned semifinalist status for the Joe Moore Award. Willis started all 11 games at right tackle in 2024 and was voted a team captain. The Wildcats set school records in rushing yards per carry and offensive yards per play that season, while allowing just 13 sacks, their fewest since 2000. After entering the transfer portal, he landed at Washington and moved to left tackle, starting 10 games before an injury suffered against Ohio State cost him three weeks. He returned to start the final stretch, won UW's John P. Angel Offensive Lineman of the Year Award, and earned honorable mention All-Big Ten from both coaches and media. Willis accepted an invite to the 2026 Senior Bowl.
strengths: Quick out of his stance and consistently hits landmarks in the run game on time Good hand placement and grip strength create sustained leverage at the point of attack Fluid mover in space who tracks defenders well on perimeter and second-level blocks Plays with real effort and strain, finishing blocks through the whistle every rep Versatile enough to line up at tackle, guard, or center at the next level Run blocking improved notably in his final season after changing programs
weaknesses: Undersized frame and below-average length limit his anchor against power rushers Vulnerable to push-pull moves and long-arm bull rushes that exploit his lack of mass Tends to overset in pass protection, opening up inside rush lanes Elevated pad level getting into run blocks gives up leverage he has to fight to recover Penalty frequency ticked up during heavier workloads at Kansas State
player_comparisons: WanyaMorris - Oklahoma (91%), SpencerBurford - UTSA (88%), CadeMays - Tennessee (87%)

bio: Chad Lindberg from Clear Creek High School was rated a 4-star recruit by ESPN and handed a 4-star grade by 247 Sports. Following high school, Lindberg opted to join Georgia. In 2021 as a freshman Lindberg saw action in just 2 games and played a total of 16 snaps for the Bulldogs. He allowed no QB hurries, no QB hits, and no sacks while playing sparingly. As a sophomore in 2022 he played in 5 games and a total of 18 snaps. He conceded no QB hurries, no QB hits, and no sacks while playing sparingly. In 2023 as a junior Lindberg saw action in 10 games and totaled 83 snaps. He gave up no QB hurries, no QB hits, and no sacks while playing the majority of his snaps at tight tackle, and the rest at right guard.
strengths: Lindberg has the size teams look for at the tackle spots, and that is probably enough to get him a look as a swing tackle. Long enough to provide some position versatility, potentially capable of holding up at guard or even at right tackle. Shows a good feel for combo blocks, and Lindberg displays good quickness and flexibility when moving to the second level, capable of sealing the back side on reach blocks In the run game, he plays with a mean streak and does a nice job generating a push at the initial point of attack. He can get outside and does a decent job landing blocks in space. Awareness is above-average for his limited experience. Sells trap block and can turn to seal. Fluid getting to and adjust at the second level. Uses his hands well, with good hand placement to maximize his powerful punch. Lindberg shows impressive coordination between his upper and lower body when moving in pass protection.
weaknesses: Flashes the ability to move his man with his hands, but is not yet consistent in this area. Has a bad habit of panicking on reach blocks, lunging, and grabbing rather than trusting his athleticism to get him in position, which could lead to frequent flags for holding. As a pass protector, Lindberg gets caught reaching and lunging when matched up against quickness.

bio: Second in the state in the long jump, a 5A basketball state championship in 2021, and a three-star football recruit all out of Beaumont United in Texas. Chandler Rivers was that kind of athlete. He enrolled early at Duke in January 2022. Rivers contributed immediately as a true freshman, appearing in all 13 games with six starts and earning Freshman All-America honors. He was named Birmingham Bowl MVP after his sophomore season, then turned in a breakout 2024 junior year: 54 tackles, three interceptions in three consecutive games, 7.5 tackles for loss, First Team All-ACC, and multiple All-America selections. He returned as a senior and helped Duke win its first ACC Championship since 1962, posting eight tackles in the title game win over Virginia. Rivers finished the year with 59 tackles, two picks, and eight pass breakups before opting out of the Sun Bowl. Over four seasons he recorded 223 tackles, seven interceptions, and 29 pass breakups across 51 games. He was invited to the Senior Bowl and tested well at the Combine, posting the third-fastest forty among cornerbacks and finishing in the top ten in both the vertical and broad jumps.
strengths: Fluid hips and quick feet let him stay in phase through receiver stems and breaks Combine speed tested among the best at the position and shows up as recovery ability on tape Zone eyes are advanced; reads the quarterback while staying disciplined in his landmarks Plays bigger than his frame at the catch point thanks to real vertical pop and good timing Downhill trigger against screens is fast, and he takes clean angles to the ball Held receivers to roughly half their targets completed during his best coverage season in 2024 Smooth communicator in zone exchanges who passes off routes without confusion Effective as a slot blitzer with a knack for timing the snap and affecting the pocket Four years as a starter in the ACC, with his biggest games often coming in the biggest moments
weaknesses: Short arms and a narrow frame make press reps against bigger receivers a problem Tackling got worse as a senior; the missed tackle rate climbed to its highest mark Eyes drift into the backfield in man coverage, which opens up separation underneath Senior year coverage numbers took a noticeable step back from his excellent 2024 tape Overcommits to his initial read on occasion and gets burned by double moves Run defense has been average throughout his career and the size limits his impact there

bio: A multi-sport athlete out of Horn High School in Mesquite, Texas, Charles Demmings competed in both football and track before drawing interest from several smaller programs. He signed with Stephen F. Austin and saw just 15 snaps as a true freshman in 2021 before earning a full-time role the following year. Demmings started 11 games as a sophomore in 2022, picking up his first career interception and three pass breakups. His junior season brought a step forward: he logged 583 snaps, added two more interceptions and four pass breakups, and posted a career-high three breakups in a single game against Utah Tech. By 2024, he had earned Second-Team All-Southland honors with two interceptions and five pass breakups. His 2025 campaign was his best. Demmings earned First-Team All-Southland and First-Team AFCA FCS All-American recognition after recording four interceptions and six pass breakups. He finished his career with 63 tackles, nine interceptions, and 35 passes defended across 42 games, setting the Stephen F. Austin program record for career passes defended. He was invited to the 2026 Senior Bowl.
strengths: Finds the ball consistently; nine career picks because his eyes go to the right place. Hips are smooth and quiet out of his backpedal, stays square through the break. Has the speed to carry vertical routes and recover when he gives a step at the stem. Uses his length well to squeeze receivers toward the sideline and shrink throwing windows. Tackled much better as a senior; wrapped up cleanly and cut out the arm-reaching. Closes hard to the catch point and gets his hands into the receiver's pocket to disrupt. Good size for an outside corner with enough vertical pop to contest above the rim.
weaknesses: Southland receivers rarely tested him with NFL-caliber releases or separation ability. Sophomore tape was ugly; gave up too many easy completions underneath and looked lost at times. Slow to trigger downhill from off-coverage; sits flat-footed reading the QB's eyes. Gets grabby mid-route when he feels a receiver pulling away, and officials find it. Light for the position and bigger receivers can push him off his spot at the line. Shows some stiffness flipping his hips when quicker receivers change direction on him.

bio: Chase Bisontis came to Texas A&M from Don Bosco Prep in Ramsey, New Jersey as a consensus 4-star recruit and the top-ranked player in his state. He earned MaxPreps All-America honors as a high school sophomore and helped lead Don Bosco to a state championship appearance. Bisontis started all 13 games as a true freshman in 2023 after injuries reshuffled the offensive line, lining up primarily at right tackle with one start at right guard in the Texas Bowl. He earned ESPN Freshman All-America and SEC All-Freshman Team honors. As a sophomore, he moved to left guard and started 10 games before a lower leg injury cost him three contests. He helped anchor a rushing attack that ranked second in the SEC at 195.5 yards per game. He returned for his junior year in 2025 and started every game at left guard, logging 795 offensive snaps. Across 36 career games, Bisontis has been one of the steadiest linemen in the conference. His Combine workout further boosted his stock, with a 40-yard dash among the best at the guard position.
strengths: Outstanding short-area quickness and foot speed for an interior lineman. Gets underneath defenders despite his height to win the leverage battle early. Balanced pass protector who mirrors well once hands are engaged. Heavy, accurate hands when he strikes first at the point of attack. Core strength and low center of gravity let him sustain and steer blocks. Effective puller and move blocker who reaches targets cleanly in space. Stays active and attentive as the unoccupied blocker, picking up late work. Showed clear improvement in pass protection from freshman to junior year.
weaknesses: Hands arrive late against patient, technically refined interior rushers. Below-average arm length creates occasional lunging and overextension. Hips open early in pass sets, exposing a soft inside shoulder. Pad level at first contact is inconsistent and sometimes too high. Can get walked back by power rushers who attack the middle of his frame. Penalty frequency is a concern, with 19 flags across three seasons.

bio: Football pedigree runs in the family for Chris Brazzell II, whose father Chris Sr. was a sixth-round pick of the New York Jets in 1998 and spent nine years playing professionally between the NFL and CFL. The younger Brazzell grew up in Fort Worth, Texas before his family relocated to Midland, where he developed into a lanky, coordinated pass-catcher at Midland Legacy High School. He finished his prep career with 52 receptions for 859 yards and 12 touchdowns as a senior, and the major recruiting services tagged him as a consensus three-star prospect, ranking him among the top 200 players in talent-rich Texas. Brazzell originally committed to Tulane, where a shoulder injury limited him to just two games as a true freshman in 2022. He bounced back in a big way during his redshirt freshman campaign in 2023, leading the Green Wave with 44 catches for 711 yards and five touchdowns while averaging 16.2 yards per reception. That production earned him Freshman All-American recognition and All-AAC Third Team honors. After two seasons in New Orleans totaling 45 receptions for 722 yards, the allure of SEC competition and Tennessee's explosive offense proved too strong to resist. His transition to Knoxville started slowly in 2024, playing behind established receivers and contributing 29 catches for 333 yards and two scores in 13 games. One of those touchdowns came against seventh-ranked Alabama, a diving 16-yard grab in the corner of the end zone with under six minutes remaining that gave the Vols the lead in one of the program's biggest wins of the season. With the receiver room largely turned over heading into 2025, Brazzell seized the opportunity and became the unquestioned alpha. He finished the year with 62 receptions for 1,017 yards and nine touchdowns, leading the SEC in receiving yards and receiving yards per game while tying for the conference lead in touchdown catches. His six-catch, 177-yard, three-touchdown destruction of Georgia in the SEC opener stands as perhaps the signature performance of the entire college football season. The Biletnikoff Award semifinalist earned First Team All-SEC honors and Third Team All-America recognition, capping a career that saw him total 136 receptions for 2,072 yards and 16 touchdowns across stints at Tulane and Tennessee.
strengths: Watch the Georgia game and you will see a receiver who understands how to manipulate coverage with subtle body movements and stem variations before exploding out of breaks. Tracks the deep ball with rare patience and concentration, adjusting his frame mid-flight while absorbing contact from trailing defenders without losing focus on the catch point. His 6-4 frame creates a massive catch radius that corners simply cannot contest, and he consistently wins at the high point with strong hands and outstanding body control. Surprisingly fluid hip movement for a receiver his size, allowing him to sink into his breaks on curls, hitches, and comebacks without the false steps that plague most tall targets. Gets into his routes quickly off the line with varied release packages, using well-timed hand swipes and jab steps to clear press attempts before defensive backs can establish contact. Plays with excellent spatial awareness in zone coverage, throttling down to find soft spots and presenting himself as an available target rather than running through windows. Toe-drag sideline catches are already polished at the college level, showing the footwork discipline and boundary awareness that coaches expect from a pro-ready perimeter weapon. The combine confirmed what the tape suggested all along: this is a legitimate deep threat with elite long speed, not just a big body who wins on length alone. Willing to get his nose dirty in the run game and shows effort sustaining blocks when he engages, using his length to wall off defenders on the backside of plays.
weaknesses: The 198-pound frame is lean for a receiver his height, and questions about durability linger given the shoulder injury that cost him most of his true freshman season. Concentration drops show up on film more often than you want to see, particularly on routine catches underneath where he takes his eyes to the second move too early. Lacks the lateral agility and sudden change of direction ability to threaten defenders after the catch, limiting his yards-after-reception ceiling on short and intermediate targets. Can get handsy and grabby at the stem when press corners match his physicality, occasionally drawing offensive pass interference flags when he does not trust his footwork. Did not see much tight man coverage this season, and the question remains whether he can consistently separate against NFL corners who will challenge him at the line every snap.
player_comparisons: BrianThomas Jr. - LSU (83%), MattLanders - Arkansas (81%), GeorgePickens - Georgia (79%)

bio: Eleanor Roosevelt High School in Eastvale, California, is where Chris Johnson first showed the kind of two-way versatility that makes evaluators pay attention. His senior tape showed a defensive back with 62 tackles, three interceptions, and 18 pass deflections, but he also caught 16 passes for 342 yards and six touchdowns as a receiver. Despite that kind of production, the recruiting world slotted him as a consensus three-star prospect. He chose San Diego State over Boston College, Northwestern, and Boise State, a decision that looks better with every passing year. Johnson didn't redshirt. He stepped onto campus in 2022 and earned the team's Outstanding Freshman Award after contributing across 12 games, mostly on special teams. His sophomore year in 2023 brought a bigger role and the SDSU Special Teams Player of the Year nod, and he started flashing real coverage instincts with 30 tackles, two pass breakups, and a 44-yard interception return against San Jose State. By 2024, he had locked down a starting spot at corner, playing 757 defensive snaps and finishing with 67 tackles, four pass breakups, and another interception. All-Mountain West Honorable Mention followed, and the league was starting to take notice. Everything came together in 2025. Johnson was named Mountain West Co-Defensive Player of the Year, earned AP Second Team All-American honors, and reached the semifinal round for the Jim Thorpe Award. The numbers were absurd: 44 solo tackles, four interceptions, nine pass breakups, and a forced fumble in 634 defensive snaps. Quarterbacks who tested him completed just 18 of 43 attempts for 185 yards, zero touchdowns, and four picks, good for a 16.1 passer rating allowed. That is historically elite production, regardless of conference. Then he showed up in Indianapolis and ran a 4.40 forty, posted a 38-inch vertical and a 10-foot-6 broad jump, and earned a Relative Athletic Score of 9.67, ranking him in the 93rd percentile among cornerbacks tested over the last four decades. The tape was already loud. The combine just turned the volume all the way up.
strengths: Processes information at the line of scrimmage like a veteran, reading route concepts and quarterback eyes before committing, which lets him jump throws with real conviction. Fluid hip transitions allow him to flip, run, and mirror receivers through their breaks without grabbing or losing ground, a trait that translates to any coverage scheme. Zone coverage is his bread and butter and it shows on tape: all four 2025 interceptions came in zone, and quarterbacks posted a miserable 20.6 rating throwing into his areas. True ball hawk who attacks the football at the catch point with active, well-timed hands, converting opportunities into turnovers when most corners are content to just play the swat. Combine verified the athleticism in a major way, running 4.40 with a 38-inch vertical and a 10-6 broad jump, silencing long-speed concerns and confirming top-shelf explosiveness. Dependable, textbook tackler who wrapped up and finished all year, missing just three of 54 attempts, the kind of reliability that keeps coordinators from losing sleep. Lined up at boundary corner, slid inside to the slot, and even took some box reps without looking uncomfortable, giving future defensive coordinators options in how they deploy him. Disciplined in press at the line, refusing to bite on stutter steps or false releases, using the sideline as a second defender and funneling receivers into traffic.
weaknesses: Still on the lighter side at 194 pounds and can get washed out when asked to stack and shed blocks on the perimeter or wrap up bigger ball carriers at full speed. Contested catch situations against physical, taller receivers remain a problem. His timing arriving at the catch point is inconsistent and he does not high-point the ball well enough. Needs to be more aggressive getting hands on receivers at the release point in press looks, because when he lets guys run free off the line, he ends up trailing instead of dictating. Can get his feet stuck momentarily when receivers sell vertical and snap back on comeback or curl routes, creating brief windows that NFL quarterbacks will not hesitate to attack. Mountain West competition level is a fair question. He dominated his conference, but the jump to facing NFL-caliber route runners and timing concepts every Sunday is significant.
player_comparisons: BenjaminMorrison - Notre Dame (92%), ChristianGonzalez - Oregon (86%), DeonteBanks - Maryland (86%)

bio: Chris McClellan came out of Oklahoma as one of the more coveted interior prospects in the 2022 class -- all three major recruiting services had him as a four-star, and both Rivals and 247 Sports ranked him inside the top 120 players nationally. He was the top or second-best prospect in Oklahoma depending on which service you checked, and the top-ten defensive tackle ranking from Rivals was no accident. At Edison and then Owasso, he put up the kind of production that gets a kid to a program like Florida: 46 tackles, five sacks and 12 quarterback hurries in his senior season alone, three consecutive state playoff appearances, and a trip to the All-American Bowl. He was a legitimate blue-chip recruit. The Florida years were a mixed bag. McClellan played in 25 games over two seasons with the Gators, collecting 46 tackles, 3.5 tackles for loss and two sacks in a rotational role. He earned one start, against Florida State in the 2023 finale, but never cracked the regular lineup in Gainesville. After two seasons in the rotation, he made the decision to transfer to Missouri, where the opportunity to start was real and immediate. It paid off. He stepped into the starting lineup in 2024 and delivered 39 tackles, 5.5 tackles for loss, 2.5 sacks, two passes defended and a forced fumble across 13 games on 504 snaps. The Tigers trusted him with the job, and he held it. His senior season in 2025 was the best of his career by every measure. McClellan started all 13 regular season games and finished with 48 tackles, 8.0 tackles for loss, 6.0 sacks, six quarterback hurries and two pass breakups. He was a key piece of a Missouri defensive line that produced 21.5 combined sacks alongside ends Damon Wilson II and Zion Young, one of the more disruptive trios in the SEC. The performance against Texas A&M -- seven tackles and a pass breakup against the third-ranked team in the country -- stood out. So did the Alabama game, where McClellan and the Tigers held Ty Simpson to a season-low 200 passing yards and limited one of the country's better offenses to 325 total yards. The SEC coaches took notice, naming him second-team All-SEC at the end of the year. He has accepted a Senior Bowl invitation and will participate in the NFL Scouting Combine starting February 23.
strengths: 323 pounds and he carries it well -- proportional build with enough length that he checks the NFL body box at nose tackle without needing to add weight before training camp. Tackling grade is excellent and the film shows why -- once he locates the ball carrier, his pursuit angles are sharp and he finishes plays, which not every big interior guy can say. One-on-one against single blockers he's a problem; wide base, hard to move, and he takes on blocks with the kind of anchor that makes running at him a losing proposition. Block recognition is better than average -- he doesn't get caught sleeping on down blocks or fold blocks, stays in his gap more often than not and keeps himself clean enough to make plays. The bull rush has genuine pop when his pad level is right -- he's collapsed pockets on tape, and his hump move has flashed as something worth developing at the next level. His hands are legitimately big, among the best in this class, and on reps where he punches on time he controls the block and sheds clean -- that's the version of him you're betting on. Two full seasons of starting-caliber production in the SEC is not nothing -- 8.5 sacks, 13.5 tackles for loss, and he held up on a real snap count against real competition. Showed enough hip fluidity to work a swim move when given the chance, so there's at least a second gear in there as a pass rusher, even if it hasn't shown up consistently yet.
weaknesses: Pad level is the problem that won't go away -- he plays tall, and that single issue is why double-teams move him, why his anchor breaks down, and why offensive linemen can get into his chest and control him. Pass-rush grade is average and honestly some reps are worse than that -- the get-off is slow, the first step doesn't threaten anybody, and when the initial move stalls there is nothing behind it. No counters. That's not an exaggeration. When his bull rush gets picked up or his swim gets swatted, he stops generating pressure and blockers just ride him past the quarterback. Scoop blocks and reach blocks are where defenses can scheme against him -- he won't sink his hips moving laterally and gets washed out of the gap more than you can live with on a weekly basis. Hand timing in pass rush is genuinely inconsistent -- he arrives late and wide, gives blockers his chest, and gets locked up on reps where a better-timed punch would have won the rep outright.
player_comparisons: TyHamilton - Ohio State (87%), JadenCrumedy - Mississippi State (87%), JordanPhillips - Maryland (84%)

So did the Alabama game, where McClellan and the Tigers held Ty Simpson to a season-low 200 passing yards and limited one of the country's better offenses to 325 total yards. The SEC coaches took notice, naming him second-team All-SEC at the end of the year. He has accepted a Senior Bowl invitation and will participate in the NFL Scouting Combine starting February 23.

Strengths:
323 pounds and he carries it well -- proportional build with enough length that he checks the NFL body box at nose tackle without needing to add weight before training camp. Tackling grade is excellent and the film shows why -- once he locates the ball carrier, his pursuit angles are sharp and he finishes plays, which not every big interior guy can say. One-on-one against single blockers he's a problem; wide base, hard to move, and he takes on blocks with the kind of anchor that makes running at him a losing proposition. Block recognition is better than average -- he doesn't get caught sleeping on down blocks or fold blocks, stays in his gap more often than not and keeps himself clean enough to make plays. The bull rush has genuine pop when his pad level is right -- he's collapsed pockets on tape, and his hump move has flashed as something worth developing at the next level. His hands are legitimately big, among the best in this class, and on reps where he punches on time he controls the block and sheds clean -- that's the version of him you're betting on. Two full seasons of starting-caliber production in the SEC is not nothing -- 8.5 sacks, 13.5 tackles for loss, and he held up on a real snap count against real competition. Showed enough hip fluidity to work a swim move when given the chance, so there's at least a second gear in there as a pass rusher, even if it hasn't shown up consistently yet.

Weaknesses:
Pad level is the problem that won't go away -- he plays tall, and that single issue is why double-teams move him, why his anchor breaks down, and why offensive linemen can get into his chest and control him. Pass-rush grade is average and honestly some reps are worse than that -- the get-off is slow, the first step doesn't threaten anybody, and when the initial move stalls there is nothing behind it. No counters. That's not an exaggeration. When his bull rush gets picked up or his swim gets swatted, he stops generating pressure and blockers just ride him past the quarterback. Scoop blocks and reach blocks are where defenses can scheme against him -- he won't sink his hips moving laterally and gets washed out of the gap more than you can live with on a weekly basis. Hand timing in pass rush is genuinely inconsistent -- he arrives late and wide, gives blockers his chest, and gets locked up on reps where a better-timed punch would have won the rep outright.

bio: An undefeated 12-0 senior season capped by a Class A state title at Omaha Westside High School should have drawn more recruiting attention than Cole Payton received. Over three seasons as the starter, he went 31-5 and piled up 5,338 passing yards with 65 touchdowns through the air, plus another 2,122 yards and 37 scores on the ground. That senior year was the crown jewel: 2,742 total yards and 39 touchdowns. Nebraska named him its Gatorade Player of the Year for the 2020-21 cycle, and he earned two all-state first-team nods. He was also a baseball player, a multi-sport kid with competitive DNA. Despite all of that production, Payton was only a two-star recruit with a 0.77 composite grade from 247Sports, which tells you plenty about how the recruiting world viewed an FCS-bound quarterback from Omaha. Payton landed at North Dakota State knowing full well he was walking into a quarterback factory. Brock Jensen, Carson Wentz, Easton Stick, Trey Lance, Cam Miller. That lineage drew him to Fargo, and he has said publicly that he believed one year of starting at NDSU would carry more weight than multiple seasons elsewhere. He redshirted in 2021, then spent three full years (2022-2024) as the backup behind Miller, mostly deployed as a designed runner in short-yardage and goal-line packages. In that backup role he still managed to rack up impressive rushing totals: 84 carries for 615 yards and 13 touchdowns in 2023 alone, including back-to-back 100-yard games to open the season against Eastern Washington and Maine. An injury in October 2024 cut his junior year short after eight games, but by that point the Bison coaching staff already knew what they had in him. When Miller graduated and Payton finally took over in 2025, the results were immediate and historic. He led NDSU to a 12-1 record and an 8-0 conference mark, completing 161 of 224 passes (71.9%) for 2,719 yards and 16 touchdowns against just four interceptions. He also rushed 136 times for 777 yards and 13 scores, averaging 5.7 yards per carry as the team's second-leading rusher. Payton set NDSU single-season records for pass efficiency (193.8), yards per attempt (12.1), and total offense per game (268.9). Three of the top six single-game offensive performances in school history belong to him, including a 380-yard explosion at South Dakota State where he beat the No. 2-ranked Jackrabbits with four rushing touchdowns. He finished third in Walter Payton Award voting, earned multiple All-America honors from Stats Perform, FCS Football Central, and the Associated Press, and graduated as NDSU's career record holder in completion percentage (.702), pass efficiency (185.5), and yards per attempt (11.3). He capped it off with invitations to both the Senior Bowl and the NFL Scouting Combine.
strengths: Built like a prototypical NFL quarterback at 232 pounds with broad shoulders and a thick lower half that absorbs contact and keeps him upright in the pocket and on designed runs. Freakish combine athlete who posted a 40-inch vertical (third all-time for QBs), 10'10" broad jump, 4.56 forty, and 7.12 three-cone, confirming rare explosive power for the position. Dangerous rushing threat with 31 career touchdowns on the ground; runs with real anger between the tackles and has the build-up speed to rip off chunk plays once he hits the second level. Deep ball placement is outstanding, particularly outside the numbers, where he can lay it over the shoulder in stride and give receivers room to run after the catch. Comfortable rolling to his left as a natural southpaw and delivers accurate strikes on the move without losing velocity or ball placement on bootlegs and designed rollouts. Poise under pressure stands out for a guy with limited starting reps; keeps his eyes downfield when the pocket collapses and looks to create rather than just bail and scramble. Scored well across all three passing levels on film study, flashing genuine touch on intermediate crossers and the ability to drop the ball into tight windows over the middle of the field. Extremely low turnover-worthy play rate for a first-year starter, showing good decision-making discipline and an understanding of when to throw the ball away or tuck it and run.
weaknesses: Only one season of starting experience at the FCS level, meaning his processing speed and anticipation against complex NFL coverages remain largely untested and underdeveloped. Mechanical overhaul is needed; he throws with a wide base and an arm-dominant delivery that leaves his lower half disconnected, leading to inconsistent accuracy on short and intermediate throws. Lacks the drive velocity to consistently fit the ball into tight NFL windows, especially on in-breaking routes where late throws get jumped by linebackers and safeties. Fumble issues cropped up with increased volume in 2025 (four fumbles on 120 rushing attempts), and ball security will be tested further against NFL-caliber pass rushers collapsing the pocket. Zone coverage reading remains a genuine concern; he thrived against man-heavy FCS schemes but has not proven he can manipulate safeties or work through full-field progressions against disguised shells.
player_comparisons: JustinFields - Ohio State (57%), JaydenDaniels - LSU (57%), CamMiller - North Dakota State (57%)
name: Cole Payton

bio: Colton Hood's uncle Roderick spent eight seasons as a defensive back in the NFL after playing at Auburn, and the younger Hood followed that same path to the football field at Eagles Landing Christian Academy in McDonough, Georgia. He was the definition of a do-everything athlete, lining up at cornerback, wide receiver, quarterback, and running back while also earning all-state honors in baseball and running on a state-qualifying 4x100 relay team. He racked up over 1,100 yards of total offense and 13 touchdowns on one side of the ball, then flipped around and posted 40 tackles and three interceptions on the other. Two punt return touchdowns and a kickoff return score rounded out a senior year that earned him Region 5-2A Athlete of the Year. The recruiting services pegged him as a three-star prospect, with On3 ranking him the No. 20 cornerback nationally and 247Sports slotting him at No. 48 at the position. Hood followed his uncle's footsteps to Auburn for his true freshman year in 2023, appearing in four games before taking a redshirt. The following offseason, he made the first of two transfers that would define his college trajectory, landing at Colorado to join Deion Sanders' secondary. Playing behind Heisman Trophy winner Travis Hunter, Hood carved out meaningful reps in a 13-game rotation and finished 2024 with 24 tackles, six pass breakups, and two interceptions. The highlight came against Arizona, when Hood drew the assignment on future top draft pick Tetairoa McMillan and held him to just five catches for 38 yards while snagging an interception and returning it 46 yards. His other pick, a 59-yard return against Kansas State after Hunter left with an injury, showed the kind of opportunistic playmaking that would become his calling card. The final stop was Rocky Top. Hood committed to Tennessee in May 2025 and immediately stepped into the starting lineup opposite Jermod McCoy's spot, with McCoy sidelined by an ACL injury. What followed was the best season of his career by a wide margin: 50 tackles, 4.5 tackles for loss, eight pass breakups, a pick-six at Mississippi State, and a fumble recovery returned for a touchdown against Syracuse in the opener that earned him SEC Co-Defensive Player of the Week. He became the first SEC player to return both an interception and a fumble for touchdowns in the same season since 2019. By season's end, Hood had collected All-SEC Second Team honors from the Associated Press and Third Team from the coaches, landed on the Jim Thorpe Award semifinalist list, and established himself as one of the most talked-about cornerback prospects at the Senior Bowl, where receivers reportedly named him the toughest matchup they faced all year.
strengths: Fluid hips and quick feet in his backpedal allow him to mirror receivers through their breaks without losing a half-step, especially effective on comebacks and curls where his deceleration is seamless. Press-man technique at the line is where he lives. Gets his hands on you early with a forceful two-hand jam that disrupts timing and keeps wideouts from running clean stems off the line. Ball production speaks for itself: three career interceptions, 14 pass breakups, and two forced fumbles across just 29 games, with a knack for showing up at the catch point when it matters most. 4.4 speed gives him a legitimate recovery gear that allows him to close windows even when he's initially beaten off the snap, and quarterbacks who test him deep pay for it. Diagnostic ability at the snap is advanced for a one-year starter. Reads receiver releases and formation cues quickly, rarely biting on double moves or hesitation fakes at the stem. Competitive fire burns hot in contested catch situations. Fights through the whistle and refuses to concede positioning, which showed up repeatedly during his Senior Bowl reps against top wideouts. Versatility in coverage schemes translates well. Looked comfortable sitting in Cover 2 flats, carrying vertical routes in quarters, and playing isolated man on the boundary throughout his Tennessee tape. Closing burst downhill on underneath routes is a real weapon. Drives on the throw with conviction and arrives with enough force to deter receivers from extending over the middle.
weaknesses: Run support effort is inconsistent and position-dependent. Willingness to set the edge and take on blocks varies from game to game, and he can disappear against the run when not directly targeted. Tackling mechanics break down in space far too often. Tends to lunge at legs and lead with his shoulder rather than wrapping through contact, resulting in four missed tackles on the stat sheet and more on film. Physicality in routes invites laundry. Four penalties in 2025 with the Vols, and his aggressive hand usage past five yards will draw even more flags at the next level where officials have a shorter leash. Tracking the ball and receivers downfield beyond the sticks is a clear developmental area. His play at the line is night-and-day better than his ability to locate and play the ball on deep routes when trailing. Only one full year as a starter across three college programs leaves real questions about his experience level and ability to handle a full 17-game NFL workload against elite competition week after week.
player_comparisons: KamariLassiter - Georgia (93%), TreyAmos - Ole Miss (89%), QuinyonMitchell - Toledo (88%)

bio: Tollison grew up in Jackson, Missouri, where he was a four-year letterman at Jackson High School under coach Brent Eckley. As a senior, he anchored an offense that went 14-0 and won the MSHSAA Class 5 State Championship, averaging 424.7 yards per game with 62 rushing touchdowns across those 14 contests. He earned a four-star rating from 247Sports (No. 140 nationally, No. 19 offensive tackle) and three-star marks from both Rivals and ESPN. Jackson went 47-5 during his four years on varsity. At Missouri, Tollison saw limited action as a true freshman in 2021 before stepping into the starting lineup at center for all 13 games in 2022. He started every game again in 2023, when the Tigers' offensive line was named a semifinalist for the Joe Moore Award. That unit helped Missouri surpass 500 total yards five times and rush for 200-plus yards on six occasions. Tollison started the first nine games of 2024 before a knee injury ended his season. He returned for 2025 as a fifth-year senior and third-year captain of the line, taking every snap at center while the Tigers ran the nation's eighth-best rushing attack at 228.3 yards per game.
strengths: Feet pop on film when he pulls or gets out on screens; covers ground like a tight end. Handles reach blocks with easy lateral slides, stays connected through the whistle in zone runs. Releases off combo blocks with good timing and finds linebackers at the second level naturally. Head stays on a swivel in pass pro; picks up late blitzers and redirects to cut them off. Flashes real power in his arm extensions when he gets his hands inside, as he showed against Georgia in 2023. Kept his quarterback remarkably clean across four years as a starter, rarely giving up sacks. Iron man availability with nearly 3,200 career snaps; came back from a 2024 knee injury and missed nothing in 2025. Consistent shotgun snapper who puts the ball on target; only one notable misfire on tape.
weaknesses: Very lean through his lower half; gets walked back when bigger nose tackles lock onto his chest. Hands arrive wide and late too often, giving rushers a clear lane to his pads. Got rocked by a push-pull-swim in 2023 that put him on the ground; vulnerable to counter moves. Struggles to create any push on base blocks against physical fronts, especially on inside runs. Takes poor angles climbing to the second level at times and arrives without finishing. Drops his head into run blocks, loses sight of the target, and gets stacked and shed. Seventeen penalties across four starting seasons; the flags come in bunches on tape.
player_comparisons: CameronJurgens - Nebraska (85%), DylanMcMahon - NC State (82%), TanorBortolini - Wisconsin (80%)

bio: Cyrus Allen came out of Louisiana as a three-star recruit in the 2021 class, ranked the 17th-best player in the state by ESPN. He caught 25 passes for 458 yards and nine touchdowns as a high school sophomore, flashing the kind of big-play ability that would define his college career. He landed at Louisiana Tech, where he wasted no time making an impact as a true freshman in 2022, posting 23 catches for 502 yards and four touchdowns while averaging a staggering 21.8 yards per reception. That number ranked second nationally, and he led all FBS freshmen with 10 plays of 30 or more yards that season, opening with a 121-yard, two-touchdown performance in his first collegiate game. His sophomore year at Louisiana Tech brought increased volume and continued production. Allen hauled in 46 catches for 782 yards and four more touchdowns, averaging 70.7 receiving yards per game (fifth in Conference USA) and topping the century mark three times, including a career-best 170-yard outing against Sam Houston. He closed that season with a catch in 13 consecutive games, showing the kind of consistency that caught the attention of bigger programs. That attention brought him to Texas A&M for the 2024 season, where things didn't quite click. He started five games for the Aggies and managed just 18 catches for 269 yards and a single touchdown, with his best moment being a 73-yard scoring play at Florida. The limited role and production made it clear he needed a fresh start. That fresh start came at Cincinnati in 2025, and Allen made the most of it. He exploded for 49 catches, 661 yards, and 12 touchdowns in the Big 12, tying a program record with 13 total scores (third nationally). His route-running drew praise throughout the season, and he earned a PFF Big 12 Team of the Week nod after the Kansas game. The Senior Bowl in Mobile capped his college career on a high note, as Allen was widely considered one of the best wide receivers in attendance, with multiple draft analysts singling him out for his quickness, separation ability, and polished route technique against top competition.
strengths: Exceptional route technician who sinks his hips fluidly out of breaks and uses tempo changes to create clean separation windows against man coverage. Diverse release package at the line gives him real answers against press corners, using head fakes, foot fire, and lateral quickness to win early in the route. Legitimate vertical threat with 4.4 speed who can stretch the field from the slot, averaging 16.3 yards per catch across his four-year career. Fearless worker over the middle of the field who finds soft spots in zone coverage and positions his body to absorb contact through the catch point. Reliable hands that improved dramatically as his career progressed, posting just a 2.0% drop rate on 69 targets during his breakout 2025 season at Cincinnati. Versatile route tree that includes slot fades, whip routes, posts, and option routes, giving offensive coordinators flexibility in how they deploy him. Strong after-the-catch instincts with 269 yards after contact in 2025 and eight missed tackles forced, showing the ability to create with the ball in his hands despite his frame. Proven production across three different programs and conferences, adapting to new systems and quarterback relationships each time while maintaining his identity as a playmaker.
weaknesses: Undersized frame at just 180 pounds raises real durability questions and limits his ability to win physical reps against bigger NFL corners. Contested catch situations remain a concern, winning just 20% of contested targets in 2025 and lacking the catch radius to compensate for tight coverage. Blocking effort and ability grade out as a significant liability, which will limit his value on early downs and in the run game at the next level. Career catch rate of 59.9% is below what you want to see, even accounting for his deep target profile and the inconsistent quarterback play at his earlier stops. The Texas A&M season raises questions about his ability to produce in a featured role against elite SEC defensive backs, as his numbers and grades dipped noticeably in that environment.

Cyrus Allen came out of Louisiana as a three-star recruit in the 2021 class, ranked the 17th-best player in the state by ESPN. He caught 25 passes for 458 yards and nine touchdowns as a high school sophomore, flashing the kind of big-play ability that would define his college career. He landed at Louisiana Tech, where he wasted no time making an impact as a true freshman in 2022, posting 23 catches for 502 yards and four touchdowns while averaging a staggering 21.8 yards per reception. That number ranked second nationally, and he led all FBS freshmen with 10 plays of 30 or more yards that season, opening with a 121-yard, two-touchdown performance in his first collegiate game. His sophomore year at Louisiana Tech brought increased volume and continued production. Allen hauled in 46 catches for 782 yards and four more touchdowns, averaging 70.7 receiving yards per game (fifth in Conference USA) and topping the century mark three times, including a career-best 170-yard outing against Sam Houston. He closed that season with a catch in 13 consecutive games, showing the kind of consistency that caught the attention of bigger programs. That attention brought him to Texas A&M for the 2024 season, where things didn't quite click. He started five games for the Aggies and managed just 18 catches for 269 yards and a single touchdown, with his best moment being a 73-yard scoring play at Florida. The limited role and production made it clear he needed a fresh start. That fresh start came at Cincinnati in 2025, and Allen made the most of it. He exploded for 49 catches, 661 yards, and 12 touchdowns in the Big 12, tying a program record with 13 total scores (third nationally). His route-running drew praise throughout the season, and he earned a PFF Big 12 Team of the Week nod after the Kansas game. The Senior Bowl in Mobile capped his college career on a high note, as Allen was widely considered one of the best wide receivers in attendance, with multiple draft analysts singling him out for his quickness, separation ability, and polished route technique against top competition.

Strengths:
Exceptional route technician who sinks his hips fluidly out of breaks and uses tempo changes to create clean separation windows against man coverage. Diverse release package at the line gives him real answers against press corners, using head fakes, foot fire, and lateral quickness to win early in the route. Legitimate vertical threat with 4.4 speed who can stretch the field from the slot, averaging 16.3 yards per catch across his four-year career. Fearless worker over the middle of the field who finds soft spots in zone coverage and positions his body to absorb contact through the catch point. Reliable hands that improved dramatically as his career progressed, posting just a 2.0% drop rate on 69 targets during his breakout 2025 season at Cincinnati. Versatile route tree that includes slot fades, whip routes, posts, and option routes, giving offensive coordinators flexibility in how they deploy him. Strong after-the-catch instincts with 269 yards after contact in 2025 and eight missed tackles forced, showing the ability to create with the ball in his hands despite his frame. Proven production across three different programs and conferences, adapting to new systems and quarterback relationships each time while maintaining his identity as a playmaker.

Weaknesses:
Undersized frame at just 180 pounds raises real durability questions and limits his ability to win physical reps against bigger NFL corners. Contested catch situations remain a concern, winning just 20% of contested targets in 2025 and lacking the catch radius to compensate for tight coverage. Blocking effort and ability grade out as a significant liability, which will limit his value on early downs and in the run game at the next level. Career catch rate of 59.9% is below what you want to see, even accounting for his deep target profile and the inconsistent quarterback play at his earlier stops. The Texas A&M season raises questions about his ability to produce in a featured role against elite SEC defensive backs, as his numbers and grades dipped noticeably in that environment.
