bio: DJ Campbell came out of Arlington Bowie High School in Arlington, Texas as a consensus five-star recruit and the top-ranked interior offensive lineman in the 2022 class. He earned Under Armour All-American honors and was a MaxPreps All-America First Team selection, drawing offers from programs across the country before committing to Texas. Campbell saw spot duty as a true freshman in seven games during 2022, then locked in as the starting right guard for the final three years of his career. He started all 14 games in 2023 as part of a Joe Moore Award semifinalist unit that blocked for Doak Walker semifinalist Jonathon Brooks and a pair of backs who combined for over 1,800 rushing yards. That line helped Texas reach the Big 12 Championship, where Quinn Ewers threw for 452 yards and four touchdowns against Oklahoma State. In 2024, Campbell started all 16 games on a Joe Moore Award finalist offensive line as Texas made a College Football Playoff Semifinal run. He returned for his senior season in 2025 and started all 13 games, finishing his career with 43 consecutive starts at right guard and over 2,600 offensive snaps. He was invited to the 2026 East-West Shrine Bowl.
strengths: You can see the growth on tape each year; his senior film looks like a different player than 2023. When a nose tackle tries to walk him back, Campbell sinks his hips and the rush just dies. Those 34-inch arms are sneaky long for a 6-3 guard; he gets inside chest before defenders can counter. Wins the leverage game consistently, bends at the knees and works his hips to seal run lanes. Eyes never panic in pass pro; picks up late-twisting linebackers and looping ends without hesitation. Started 43 straight games at right guard, many of them against quality SEC defensive fronts. Shows real pop as a down blocker; watch the Georgia and Arizona State tape for examples. Kept his quarterback remarkably clean as a senior despite facing every opponent's best interior rush.
weaknesses: Hips are stiff on film; when a 3-tech crosses his face, he struggles to flip and recover. Pull reps and screen assignments look labored; feet get heavy away from the line of scrimmage. Hands wander wide too often, especially on initial punch, and he loses inside positioning. Penalties became a real issue as a senior; too many false starts and holding calls on tape. Leads with his helmet into contact, gets his weight out over his toes and loses balance. Combo-to-climb timing is off; tends to stay on the double team a beat too long before releasing.

bio: Twelve interceptions and 45 pass breakups across three varsity seasons at Sierra Canyon in Chatsworth made DJ Harvey one of the most productive prep defensive backs in California. The Palmdale native earned MaxPreps All-American Second Team honors as a junior and made the MaxPreps All-Defense Team as a senior, drawing four-star ratings from 247Sports and Rivals before signing with Virginia Tech. Harvey saw limited action with the Hokies, collecting just eight tackles in 14 games over two seasons before transferring to San Jose State ahead of the 2023 campaign. He worked into a starting role with the Spartans and broke out as a redshirt junior in 2024, posting 58 tackles, four interceptions (including a pick-six), 10 pass breakups, and two sacks across 12 games. That production earned him a transfer to USC for his redshirt senior year, where he reunited with childhood friend Kamari Ramsey in the Trojans' secondary. Harvey started the 2025 opener but lost his job within weeks as redshirt freshmen Marcelles Williams and Braylon Conley pushed past him in the rotation. He played just five defensive snaps in Week 5 against Illinois, then missed time with an injury and finished the season with only 141 total defensive snaps.
strengths: Reads quarterback eyes and route combinations well in zone coverage. Productive ball skills with four career interceptions and 14 pass breakups. Willing and aggressive in run support; posted strong run defense marks at SJSU. Has played outside corner, slot, and box, offering defensive coordinators flexibility. Competitive temperament shows up on tape, especially in contested catch situations. Comfortable sitting in off-coverage and driving downhill on short and intermediate throws. Four years of college experience across three programs sharpened his route recognition.
weaknesses: Below-average speed limits his ability to recover once beaten vertically. Man coverage technique breaks down against quick receivers on sharp-breaking routes. Undersized frame with short arms creates a small catch radius for contested balls. Lost his starting job at USC to younger corners and was benched by mid-season. Press technique is raw; gets grabby when receivers threaten his leverage at the line. Missed tackle rate climbed at USC, and bigger backs can run through him.
player_comparisons: AlijahHuzzie - North Carolina (83%), JabbarMuhammad - Oregon (82%), MacMcWilliams - UCF (82%)

bio: Sixty catches for 1,205 yards and 14 touchdowns as a senior only told half the story for Dae'Quan Wright at Perry High School in Georgia, where he also carried the ball 32 times for 103 yards and 10 scores under head coach Kevin Smith. That dual-threat production followed a strong junior campaign of 52 receptions for 524 yards and six touchdowns, plus another 26 carries for 197 yards and three scores on the ground. He drew a mix of three- and four-star recruiting attention, with 247Sports grading him as a four-star prospect, and he ultimately signed with Virginia Tech. Wright contributed immediately in Blacksburg, catching 19 passes for 208 yards as a true freshman in 2022 before sliding into the starting lineup as a sophomore and posting 28 receptions for 366 yards across 12 games in 2023. After two productive seasons with the Hokies, he entered the transfer portal and landed at Ole Miss in January 2024, participating in spring drills before working his way into the rotation. His first year in Oxford saw him play all 13 games with six starts split between tight end and wide receiver, hauling in 27 catches for 394 yards and four touchdowns. The breakout performance came against Arkansas, where he set career highs with nine receptions for 99 yards and two touchdowns, earning a spot on PFF's SEC Team of the Week. He capped that 2024 season with a three-catch, 73-yard, one-touchdown effort in the Gator Bowl victory over Duke. The 2025 campaign brought the full arrival. Wright was named to the Mackey Award preseason watch list and earned preseason All-SEC recognition from multiple outlets. Through a full senior season in Lane Kiffin's spread offense, he finished with 39 catches for 635 yards and five touchdowns, leading all FBS tight ends in yards per reception for a stretch of the year. He played through a significant injury during the Oklahoma game, refusing to sit out, and his production climbed in spite of an offense that by design doesn't funnel a ton of targets to the tight end position. Across four college seasons and two programs, Wright totaled 113 receptions for 1,603 yards and nine touchdowns on 169 career targets.
strengths: Carries legit speed for a 255-pound tight end; his long speed creates vertical problems that most players at the position simply cannot replicate. Smooth, fluid mover who eats up ground with deceptive acceleration and transitions between gears without losing balance through his route stems. Catches the ball well away from his body and adjusts naturally to off-target throws, plucking passes with soft hands rather than body-catching. Showed real growth as a route runner at Ole Miss, sinking his hips into breaks with enough snap to create separation on curls, hitches, and crossing patterns underneath. Dangerous after the catch with a no-nonsense running style; attacks upfield quickly and picks up chunk yards before defenders can rally to the football. Versatile alignment profile, lining up inline, in the slot, and occasionally split wide, giving offensive coordinators multiple ways to deploy him on game day. Run blocking technique shows promising flashes when he is locked in, particularly on reach blocks and down blocks where he can use his lower-body power to seal the edge. Tough kid who played through a serious undisclosed injury in 2025 and never asked out of the lineup; that mentality translates to the pro level.
weaknesses: Blocking effort and intensity are wildly inconsistent; too many snaps where he lunges with a shoulder instead of engaging with his hands and driving his feet. Contested-catch production is genuinely concerning for a tight end with his frame; he has won very few reps when a defender is draped on him at the catch point. Twelve career drops on 169 targets is a number that will give evaluators pause, even though his drop rate improved significantly during his final college season. Route tree was limited at Ole Miss due to scheme; he ran a steady diet of flats, hitches, and crossers, leaving questions about his ability to consistently threaten the seam at the next level. Gets knocked off his route stem by physical defenders at the second level, with balance issues popping up when linebackers or safeties get hands on him early in his release.
player_comparisons: WillMallory - Miami (83%), LukeSchoonmaker - Michigan (81%), BrevinJordan - Miami (80%)

bio: Dallen Bentley a product of Taylorsville High School in Utah, Bentley logged roughly five total snaps during his prep career. He earned a contributing role as a senior following a major growth spurt, caught a 40-yard reception in the season opener, and then snapped his tibia and fibula on that same play. That was it for high school football. He served an LDS mission in Colorado afterward, and college football was nowhere on his radar until his cousin talked him into walking on at Snow College. At Snow College, Bentley took to the position quickly despite having never played tight end before arriving in Ephraim. Over one season with the Badgers in 2022, he hauled in eight catches for 114 yards and two touchdowns, bulked up to 250 pounds, and earned NJCAA All-America Second Team and NJCAA All-Academic honors. Programs from across the country came calling, with offers from Washington, Colorado, Louisville, San Diego State, and New Mexico. But Utah's tight-end pedigree, fresh off turning Dalton Kincaid from an FCS transfer into a first-round pick, sealed the deal for a kid who grew up cheering for the Utes. Bentley's first two years in Salt Lake City tested his patience. In 2023 he battled injuries and appeared in just six games, catching one pass for five yards in the Las Vegas Bowl. The 2024 season was a different kind of frustration: he played 376 offensive snaps across all 12 games and earned seven starts, but the vast majority of his work was blocking. He finished with just two catches for 15 yards while Brant Kuithe absorbed most of the tight end targets. Everything changed in 2025. With a clear path to the TE1 role and new quarterback Devon Dampier, Bentley broke out in a big way. He started 12 of 13 games, caught 48 passes for 620 yards and six touchdowns, ranked seventh among all tight ends nationally in receiving yards, and was named a John Mackey Award semifinalist and All-Big 12 Third Team. His six-catch, 106-yard, one-touchdown performance in the Las Vegas Bowl capped what had been a long time coming for a player who spent years waiting for his shot.
strengths: Thick, well-built frame at 264 pounds with above-average weight for the position, giving him a natural advantage absorbing contact through his routes and after the catch. Outstanding ball security and concentration at the catch point; recorded zero drops on 76 targets in 2025, which is borderline ridiculous reliability for any pass catcher. Flashes real body control and flexibility adjusting to off-target throws, going up to high-point the football and playing bigger than his 6'4" frame would suggest. Genuine versatility in his alignment, logging meaningful snaps inline, from the slot, and out wide, with experience in pre-snap motion and H-back looks that give coordinators options. Solid receiving production across all three levels of the field, with his best work coming on intermediate routes where he finds soft spots in zone coverage and sits down with good timing. Lower-body strength and contact balance stand out on film; he absorbs hits through his routes without losing his feet and picks up extra yardage with physicality after the catch, forcing ten missed tackles in 2025. Shows craft in his route running with an effort to sell stems, work in head fakes, and get low at his breaks, giving him more nuance than his build would suggest. Near-flawless pass protection efficiency in 2025, allowing just one total pressure on 47 pass blocking snaps while also keeping his man clean on the vast majority of his reps.
weaknesses: Below-average height for an inline tight end limits his ability to box out defenders at the catch point, and he won just four of 15 contested targets in 2025. Run blocking remains the biggest concern on tape; his technique is inconsistent with hand placement and leverage, and he struggles to sustain blocks through the whistle. Lacks top-end speed and explosive athleticism, giving him an average athletic profile that limits his ability to threaten defenses vertically or separate down the seams. Heavy production against zone coverage raises questions about how well his route running translates against NFL man-to-man, where his lack of suddenness at the top of stems will be more exposed. One-year production sample at the FBS level makes it harder to trust the 2025 breakout, especially considering he totaled just three catches in two prior seasons at Utah.

bio: Dalton Johnson came out of Katy, Texas as a three-star safety who helped lead his high school to a 6A Division II state championship in 2020, earning Defensive Player of the Year honors from the Padilla Poll along the way. Standing 5-11, 198 pounds, he was a two-time team captain who drew steady but unspectacular recruiting attention, landing at Arizona as a player who would need to earn every snap. He redshirted in 2021 after appearing in just three games, then saw limited special teams duty across 11 games in 2022, where he blocked a kick against San Diego State. Nothing about those early years screamed future All-Conference defender. That changed in 2023, when Johnson became a full-time starter and racked up 86 tackles, four forced fumbles (tied for first in the Pac-12), six tackles for loss, two sacks, and his first career interception. His performance in the Alamo Bowl win over No. 12 Oklahoma was the signature moment of that season: 13 tackles and a forced fumble that swung the game during Arizona's comeback. He followed that up in 2024 with a team-leading 94 tackles, finishing seventh in the Big 12, and the production kept climbing. By the end of that year, he had established himself as the most dependable tackler on the roster, even if the ball production had yet to catch up to his run-and-hit consistency. The 2025 season is where Johnson truly separated himself. He led Arizona again with 97 tackles, but this time he added four interceptions, seven pass breakups, 11 total passes defended, a sack, and a forced fumble. He was named AP First-Team All-Big 12, earned Big 12 Defensive Player of the Week twice (including an 18-tackle outburst against Baylor, the most by a Wildcat since 2014), and received honorable mention for Big 12 Defensive Player of the Year. He became the first Arizona defensive back to lead the team in tackles since Darrell Brooks in 2005. Johnson capped his college career with an invite to the East-West Shrine Bowl, where he logged five tackles for the West squad and drew praise from scouts for his instincts and range, and he received a formal invitation to the NFL Scouting Combine.
strengths: Reads quarterbacks with patience and discipline, staying square in his backpedal before breaking on the ball with real burst and conviction to the catch point. His 2025 coverage tape is outstanding. Allowed receptions on barely half of targets thrown his way, a massive jump from earlier seasons that reflects real development, not just a hot streak. Versatile alignment profile is a genuine asset. Arizona lined him up in the box, at free safety, and in the slot, and he looked comfortable in all three spots without a significant drop in production. Quick trigger against the run when he can see blockers coming. Arrives with intent and fills gaps before running backs can find cutback lanes, even if he gives up size at the point of contact. Ball production spiked at the right time. Four picks and seven pass breakups as a senior suggest genuine growth in anticipation and playmaking, not just volume. Consistent tackler across three full seasons as a starter. Over 270 tackles and a missed tackle rate that stayed around 12 percent, which is solid for a player who operates in space as much as he does. Smooth transitions out of his pedal. bio: Dani Dennis-Sutton came to Penn State from McDonogh School in Maryland as one of the top edge rushers in the 2022 recruiting class, earning five-star ratings from both Rivals and 247Sports. He was a two-time captain and All-American Bowl selection who also lettered in basketball and track, though COVID-19 wiped out his junior season and a dislocated elbow limited his senior year. Dennis-Sutton made an immediate mark as a true freshman, recording three sacks and an interception across 13 games to earn ESPN True Freshman All-American honors. A rotational sophomore year produced 26 tackles and 3.5 sacks before his breakout in 2024, when he started all 16 games opposite Abdul Carter and tallied 42 tackles, 8.5 sacks, and 13 tackles for loss during Penn State's playoff run. His senior season matched that output with 42 tackles, 8.5 sacks, and 12 tackles for loss while leading the Big Ten in forced fumbles. Dennis-Sutton finished his career with 127 tackles, 23.5 sacks, 34.5 tackles for loss, and seven forced fumbles across 54 games. He earned Academic All-Big Ten honors three straight years and was invited to the Senior Bowl.
strengths: Heavy hands create immediate disruption at the point of attack against both run and pass. Long arms let him control blockers and keep them from reaching his chest plate. Sets a firm edge against the run and holds ground versus double teams without giving ground easily. Bull rush has real force when he drops his pads and drives through contact. Combine workout was historic for his size, posting top-tier explosion and agility marks among all DEs tested. Lower-body power shows up in testing and on tape when he gets downhill or closes on scrambling quarterbacks. Agility numbers suggest untapped pass rush potential that has not consistently translated to game film yet. Competitive effort shows up late in games and in postseason moments, with four sacks across two playoff games. Versatile enough to handle multiple alignments and occasional shallow zone drops without confusion.
weaknesses: First step lacks the suddenness to threaten tackles before they can get set. Tight hips limit his ability to bend and flatten around the arc on speed rushes. Pad level stays elevated too often, giving blockers easy access to his chest. Rush plan gets predictable; needs a deeper counter repertoire beyond the bull rush. Missed tackle numbers are a concern, particularly against shifty runners in space. Penalty totals need cleaning up, with roughing calls popping up too frequently.

bio: Duce Chestnut established himself as a foundational piece of Syracuse's secondary, starting all 12 games as a true freshman in 2021. The Camden, New Jersey native earned Freshman All-American honors and was runner-up for ACC Defensive Rookie of the Year, showcasing his immediate impact at the Power 5 level. Through his collegiate career, Chestnut accumulated 89 tackles (63 solo), five interceptions, and nine pass breakups across 28 games. At Syracuse, Chestnut demonstrated his versatility and football IQ by anchoring a defensive unit that finished 14th nationally in passing defense (184.8 yards per game) during the 2022 season. His sophomore campaign highlighted his coverage skills, allowing just four touchdowns on 356 coverage snaps while drawing only one penalty. After a brief stint at LSU in 2023 where he appeared in four games, recording six tackles and an interception, he returned to Syracuse. The former Camden High School standout arrived in college as a highly-regarded prospect, earning four stars from Rivals and recognition as one of the top-25 cornerbacks in his class. His high school career showcased his playmaking ability with three interceptions and an impressive 13 special teams touchdowns, demonstrating his natural athletic gifts and instincts for finding the end zone.
strengths: Natural feel in zone drops with plus click-and-close ability, drives downhill on underneath routes with conviction Fluid hip flip and mirror technique in man coverage, maintains leverage through stem phase of routes Plus jam technique at LOS, consistently re-routes receivers and disrupts timing with heavy hands Above-average ball skills when playing through receiver's hands, tracks deep ball effectively over either shoulder Shows position flexibility between boundary corner and box safety, demonstrating smooth backpedal and plant-and-drive Brings pop as a tackler, wraps up consistently and drives through contact with proper pad level Snap count machine who answered the bell for 24 straight starts at Syracuse, showing plus durability Quick processor who IDs route combinations early, maintains disciplined eyes against play action
weaknesses: Lacks ideal long speed to carry vertical routes, will need safety help over top against burners Gets choppy in transition, plays too high in pedal and struggles to maintain balance through breaks Undersized frame leads to issues when matching up against bigger bodies in contested catch situations Takes questionable angles to the football in run support, gets washed out by bigger blockers Limited playing time post-transfer raises red flags about development curve

bio: Darrell Jackson Jr. took a winding path to get where he is. A three-star defensive end out of Gadsden County High School in Florida, he signed with Maryland in 2021 and appeared in all 13 games as a true freshman, collecting 22 tackles in a rotational role. He transferred to Miami the following year and stepped into a starting job immediately, recording 27 tackles, 4.5 tackles for loss and 3.0 sacks across 12 starts for the Hurricanes. Jackson moved again after that season, landing at Florida State, but NCAA transfer rules forced him to sit out the entire 2023 regular season. His only action came in the Orange Bowl against Georgia, where he logged three tackles including a half-TFL. Once eligible in 2024, he started all 12 games for the Seminoles and put together 32 tackles, 4.0 TFLs, 3.5 sacks and a forced fumble, earning All-ACC honorable mention and the Bobby Bowden Leadership Award on a team that went 2-10. Jackson returned for 2025 rather than entering the draft, serving as team captain and winning FSU's Defensive MVP award. He started all 12 games and posted a career-high 45 tackles with 3.0 TFLs and 1.0 sack, closing his college career with 25 consecutive starts and 129 total tackles across three programs and 50 games played.
strengths: Rare size and length at the position with a wingspan that creates problems at the snap. Best trait is anchoring against the run; holds ground well versus single blocks. Uses long arms to stack blockers and locate the ball carrier before shedding. Improved tackler as a senior; cut his missed tackle rate significantly in 2025. Can consume two gaps when he gets his hands on a blocker early. Showed a reliable bull rush in 2024, generating steady interior pressure on passing downs. Reaches out and slows ball carriers even while engaged, using his length as a disruptor. Willing chaser who pursues down the line and factors into gang tackles.
weaknesses: Pass rush production fell sharply in 2025 after a solid 2024 campaign. Limited rush moves beyond the bull rush; no reliable counter when stalled. Slow off the snap and often stands up out of his stance too quickly. Tight hips and stiff lower body limit his ability to redirect and work laterally. Gets uprooted by double teams when his pad level creeps up on him. Hands are late to fire on passing downs, giving blockers time to establish position.

bio: Dasan McCullough, a 6'5" linebacker out of Oklahoma, brings a rare blend of size and athleticism to the position. Born on March 7, 2003, McCullough's football pedigree runs deep, with his father Deland McCullough and grandfather Sherman Smith both having played and coached in the NFL. McCullough's journey to the draft is marked by versatility and adaptability. A highly touted four-star recruit out of high school, he initially committed to Ohio State before flipping to Indiana. As a true freshman for the Hoosiers, McCullough made an immediate impact, earning honorable mention All-Big Ten honors with 49 tackles, 6.5 tackles for loss, and four sacks in 12 games. Following a transfer to Oklahoma, McCullough showcased his versatility by playing the "Cheetah" position - a hybrid linebacker/safety role. In 10 games for the Sooners, he recorded 30 tackles, 3.5 tackles for loss, and three pass breakups, demonstrating his ability to impact the game in multiple facets.
strengths: Exceptional height and length for the position, allowing for impressive range in coverage and run defense Displays good straight-line speed for his size - has run in the high 4.5's - elite for a linebacker Versatility to play multiple positions, including traditional linebacker and hybrid safety roles Strong football IQ and instincts, likely honed through his family's deep football background Demonstrates good coverage skills, particularly in zone, with the ability to disrupt passing lanes Shows potential as a pass rusher, having recorded 4 sacks as a freshman at Indiana Tackling ability is above average, with a knack for wrapping up and limiting yards after contact High-ceiling prospect with the physical tools and pedigree to develop into a three-down linebacker
weaknesses: Lean frame for an NFL linebacker at 230 pounds, may struggle to hold up against more physical offensive linemen Pass rush skills need refinement - currently he gets by athleticism alone Can improve consistency in shedding blocks, particularly against power run schemes May need time to adjust to a full-time linebacker role after playing hybrid positions in college Occasionally takes poor angles in pursuit, leading to missed tackles in space

bio: David Daniel-Sisavanh from Woodstock High School was rated a 4-star recruit by ESPN and handed a 4-star grade by 247 Sports. Following high school, Daniel-Sisavanh opted to join Georgia after being heavily recruited. In 2021 as a freshman Daniel-Sisavanh saw action in 9 games and played a total of 113 snaps at safety for the Bulldogs. He recorded only 4 tackles, 2 assists, while adding one stop. In coverage Daniel-Sisavanh notched up no pass breakups, no interceptions, and an unimpressive QB rating when targetted of 147.2. As a sophomore in 2022 he played in 10 games and logged 142 snaps for the Bulldogs. He made only 9 tackles, 3 assists, and had 6 stops. In coverage he totaled one pass breakup, no interceptions, and an unimpressive QB rating when targetted of 122.6. In 2023 as a junior Daniel-Sisavanh played in 6 games and contributed on 150 snaps. He chalked up 12 tackles, 2 assists, and added no stops. In the secondary Daniel-Sisavanh forced one pass breakup, no interceptions, and gave up an unimpressive QB rating when targetted of 116.7.
strengths: Tracks the ball well and has the natural ball skills for forcing turnovers. His ball skills are very good. Daniel-Sisavanh tracks the ball well downfield and has the hands and coordination to come away with interceptions even when Daniel-Sisavanh isn’t targeted often. He’s tall-framed with long arms to match. With his powerful hands, straight-line speed, and fluidity in his hips, turning and chasing downfield shouldn’t be a problem. He’s also very good in zone coverage, showing a great understanding of routes and the ability to close quickly. Good instincts and anticipation reads and reacts with discipline. Dangerous with the ball in his hands. Does a good job getting his head around in time when playing with his back to the line of scrimmage. Has the ability to cover quickness underneath.
weaknesses: Needs to get stronger. Too easily lost in the trash. Not a sudden athlete and shows body stiffness when attempting to quickly redirect. Plays out of control too often, laying a lot of big hits but also missing a lot of tackles along the way. Daniel-Sisavanh also needs to do a better job reading routes when sitting back as a single-high safety. Has a lean, narrow frame and needs to get stronger.

bio: Three years at Grand Terrace High before a senior transfer to Cajon High in San Bernardino gave David Gusta a winding prep path through the Inland Empire. As a junior, he racked up 62 tackles and 14 sacks playing along the defensive line, earning First Team All-Sunkist League honors. That kind of production drew attention from recruiting services, and Gusta landed as a consensus three-star prospect, with 247Sports slotting him as the No. 45 defensive tackle and No. 97 overall player in the state of California. Washington State brought him in for the 2021 class, and Gusta spent his first year as a redshirt before working his way into the rotation as a redshirt freshman in 2022. He appeared in nine games that year, including a start against Oregon, and flashed early when he notched a tackle for loss and a batted pass in the season opener against Idaho. His role expanded considerably in 2023, when he started 11 games and posted 30 tackles with three QB hurries. That sophomore tape showed a player growing into his body and learning how to use his hands at the point of attack. His junior season in 2024 is where things really clicked: Gusta started all 12 games and tallied 21 tackles, three TFLs, one sack, and three passes defended while drawing the attention of programs across the country. That breakout campaign put Gusta squarely on the transfer portal radar. Michigan, Alabama, and Oklahoma all came calling, but he chose Kentucky, betting on himself in the SEC. In his lone season with the Wildcats, Gusta started all 12 games and recorded 12 tackles, 3.0 TFLs, a sack, and two quarterback hurries. His biggest play of the year came against Ole Miss, where a QB hurry forced an errant throw that resulted in a Ty Bryant interception. For his career across 44 games and 36 starts, Gusta finished with 74 tackles, 9.0 TFLs, and 2.5 sacks. He was invited to the East-West Shrine Bowl following his senior year, joining Kentucky teammates Jager Burton and Kendrick Law in Frisco.
strengths: Thick, powerful lower half with dense hips and tree-trunk thighs that give him a strong anchor at the point of attack against single blocks. Hands are genuinely violent on contact. His club-rip combination off the snap can catch guards off balance and create immediate displacement in the pocket. Effort never wavers from snap one to snap sixty. You will not catch this guy coasting on a play, even deep into the fourth quarter of a lopsided game. Stays disciplined against zone-read concepts and does a solid job fighting through cut-off blocks to maintain his gap responsibility on wide zone runs. Sneaky first step for a man his size. He gets off the ball quicker than the tape speed suggests, and that initial burst is what creates so many of his pocket disruptions. Creates consistent interior pressure without relying on one go-to move. He can win with a bull rush, swim, or two-hand swipe depending on what the blocker gives him. Good awareness against misdirection and play-action. He does not bite on the fake and keeps his eyes in the backfield, trusting his keys instead of guessing. Carried his physicality and competitiveness into the SEC without flinching. The Ole Miss game showed he can affect passing lanes and create turnovers against top-tier competition.
weaknesses: Sack numbers simply are not there despite all the pressure. 2.5 sacks in 44 career games tells you he creates disruption but cannot finish at the quarterback. Shorter arms make it tough for him to keep offensive linemen from latching onto his chest, and once they get into his frame, he struggles to disengage. Tackling is a legitimate concern. His missed tackle rate spiked in 2025, and too many of his attempts end with arm grabs instead of wrapping through the ball carrier. Double teams swallow him up. He will fight and compete, but he lacks the length and anchor to consistently split combo blocks or hold his ground when two linemen get to his body. Base tends to narrow when he gets into his pass-rush moves as a penetrator, which costs him balance and leads to him ending up on the ground more than you would like to see.
player_comparisons: CJWest - Indiana (78%), DeWayneCarter - Duke (77%), HaskellGarrett - Ohio State (77%)

bio: Daylen Everette grew up in Norfolk, Virginia, picking off 11 passes as a sophomore at Norview High School before transferring to IMG Academy in Bradenton, Florida for his final two prep seasons. A consensus top-10 cornerback recruit nationally and 247Sports five-star, he played in the 2022 Under Armour All-American Game and committed to Georgia after flipping from Clemson. After a redshirt-style freshman year on special teams in 2022, Everette stepped into the starting lineup as a sophomore in 2023, finishing with 39 tackles and an interception in the Orange Bowl. His 2024 junior season was the breakout: 58 tackles, three interceptions, two forced fumbles, a sack, and SEC Championship Game MVP honors after two picks against Texas. He earned Third Team All-SEC that year. Rather than entering the draft, Everette came back for 2025 and posted 50 tackles, a career-high 10 passes defensed, and a 47-yard fumble return touchdown in the Sugar Bowl. He added another interception in the SEC Championship win over Alabama and earned Third Team All-SEC for the second straight season. Career totals: 55 games, 41 starts, 150 tackles, five interceptions, and 19 passes defensed.
strengths: Verified top-end straight-line speed that shows up tracking receivers vertically down the boundary. Good size at 6-1 with enough frame to absorb contact and compete physically at the catch point. Strong run defender for the position; fills his gap, sets edges, and tackles with intent. Patient hands at the line of scrimmage in press, forcing receivers to declare before committing. Reads the quarterback's eyes well in zone coverage and triggers downhill on underneath throws. Productive in big moments: interceptions in back-to-back SEC Championship Games, including an MVP performance. Massive experience base of 55 games and 41 starts against SEC-caliber competition over four seasons. Combine performance confirmed the athletic profile; second-fastest top speed among cornerbacks tested.
weaknesses: Transitions and change of direction do not match his straight-line burst; stays beaten when he loses phase. Grabby at the break point when he feels receivers separating; NFL officials will not let that slide. Tackles too high and relies on arm tackles instead of driving through with proper form. Man coverage technique needs work; tends to shoot the wrong hand and give free releases off the line. Coverage grading was inconsistent across four years rather than showing a steady upward curve. Concedes soft completions underneath in zone, allowing easy chain-moving receptions in front of him.
player_comparisons: AaronRobinson - UCF (98%), ShaunWade - Ohio State (91%), NohlWilliams - California (86%)

bio: A three-star recruit out of Hawaii in the 2020 class, De'Zhaun Stribling made an immediate mark at Washington State as a true freshman in 2021, catching 44 passes for 471 yards and five touchdowns to lead all Pac-12 freshmen in every major receiving category. That earned him All-Pac-12 honorable mention as an 18-year-old. His sophomore year brought continued production with 51 receptions for 602 yards and five more scores as the Cougars' top pass catcher. Stribling transferred to Oklahoma State ahead of 2023 but saw his season cut short after four starts due to injury, finishing with just 14 catches and 198 yards. He returned healthy in 2024 and posted career-best numbers: 52 receptions for 882 yards and six touchdowns, averaging 17.0 yards per catch. His seven-catch, 174-yard, two-touchdown performance at Tulsa stood out as his best individual game as a Cowboy. Another portal move brought him to Ole Miss for 2025, where he landed on the Biletnikoff Award preseason watch list and was named to the Senior Bowl Top-300. He finished as the Rebels' second-leading receiver with 55 catches for 811 yards and six touchdowns across 15 games, including two College Football Playoff appearances that produced a combined 199 receiving yards.
strengths: Eats cushion in a hurry off the line; corners playing off cannot recover once he gets vertical. Plucks the ball away from his frame with natural hand extension on downfield throws. Sold a beautiful double move against Tulsa in 2024 that set up a 63-yard score on pure route craft. Tracks deep balls over his shoulder well and adjusts his angle without losing speed. Spins away from contact after the catch with good spatial feel for where the next defender is. Pacing on curls, hitches, and comebacks is controlled; settles into soft spots against zone with timing. Willing to get his nose in there as a blocker and fit up on safeties in the run game. Noticeably better player on 2024-2025 tape than the 2021-2022 version; the growth is real and visible.
weaknesses: Leggy in his breaks; hips stay high on in-cuts and digs, giving DBs time to close. Patient corners who mirror his release can ride him off his stem at the line of scrimmage. Gets displaced at the catch point; physical DBs knock him off balance through contact on 50/50 balls. Build-up speed profile means he takes a beat longer to threaten the top of a route. Release repertoire is narrow; relies mainly on feet and a swipe, needs more counters against press. Age and three school changes will raise questions in draft rooms about long-term trajectory.
player_comparisons: TyquanThornton - Baylor (83%), VelusJones Jr. - Tennessee (79%), ErikEzukanma - Texas Tech (79%)

bio: By the time Deontae Lawson finished his junior season at Mobile Christian School with 101 solo tackles and 33 assists, every major recruiting service in the country knew his name. He led his team to the Class 3A title game and earned Alabama Sports Writers Association Class 3A Lineman of the Year honors in 2019. That kind of production, combined with a frame that measured 6'2" and 228 pounds heading into college, made him a consensus top-tier recruit. 247Sports rated him a five-star prospect and the No. 45 player in the country, while ESPN slotted him as a four-star at No. 118 on their ESPN300 list. Rivals gave him four stars and a 5.9 grade. He chose Alabama over Georgia Tech and Texas A&M, and was selected to the All-American Bowl roster before ever stepping foot on campus in Tuscaloosa. Lawson redshirted in 2021 before earning rotational reps and four starts as a sophomore in 2022, finishing that year with 51 tackles, 2.5 tackles for loss, and four pass breakups across 439 defensive snaps. He took full ownership of the middle linebacker spot in 2023, starting 11 games and posting 67 tackles, 5.5 tackles for loss, and three sacks despite missing three contests with injury. His 2024 campaign was his most complete: 76 tackles, 6.5 tackles for loss, two sacks, an interception, and four pass breakups through 11 games before a torn ACL at Oklahoma ended his season. He earned third-team All-SEC honors from the conference coaches that year and was named a semifinalist for the Butkus Award for the second consecutive season. Lawson returned for a fifth year in 2025 and captained the defense once again, becoming just one of 16 two-time team captains in Alabama history. He led the Tide with 89 tackles, 4.5 tackles for loss, and 1.5 sacks across 15 games, adding four pass breakups, two forced fumbles, and two fumble recoveries. His late-game forced fumble at South Carolina set up Alabama's game-winning touchdown, and his 10-tackle, one-sack performance against Oklahoma in the postseason underscored his ability to show up when the lights are brightest. He finished his career with 283 tackles, good for 10th on Alabama's all-time list, and collected semifinalist nods for the Butkus Award, the Bednarik Award, and the Nagurski Trophy along the way.
strengths: His best work shows up against the run, where he consistently reads his keys, stays square, and uses his length to keep blockers off his frame. His tape in 2024 and 2025 is full of clean, decisive fits. Covers serious ground sideline to sideline with a 4.60 forty. When he has a clear lane to the ball carrier, he closes in a hurry and rarely gets outrun to the edge. Long arms and a 6'2" frame give him a wider tackling radius than most linebackers. He can reach out and corral runners who try to slip past his initial angle of pursuit. Displays a natural feel for zone coverage that improved markedly in 2025. Gets depth on his drops, tracks the quarterback's eyes well, and has 13 career pass breakups plus an interception to show for it. Legitimate blitz threat who disguises his rush timing and finds creative paths to the quarterback. Generated 52 career pressures, including 21 in 2023 when he had four sacks and three quarterback hits. Battle-tested leadership credentials are hard to ignore. Two-time team captain at Alabama, one of just 16 players in program history to earn that distinction twice. That carries weight in every locker room. Proven durability in terms of workload, logging over 2,400 career defensive snaps and 49 games. Came back from a torn ACL and performed at a high level in 2025 without any visible drop in athleticism. Fluid mover who slips past blockers at the second level with quickness and body control. More of a finesse block-avoider than a block-destroyer, but it works often enough to create disruptive plays behind the line.
weaknesses: At 228 pounds, he is undersized for the position and lacks the functional mass to consistently anchor against pulling guards and climbing centers in the run game. Tackling reliability is a genuine concern. He has missed too many tackles throughout his career, and the problem actually got worse in 2025, suggesting the issue has not corrected with more experience. Coverage numbers tell a troubling story when you dig deeper. Receivers completed a high percentage of passes thrown his direction in both 2024 and 2025, and the yards allowed in those windows add up quickly. Recognition against zone and misdirection schemes remains inconsistent. He can freeze momentarily against motion, losing a half-step that puts him out of position when gap assignments shift. The injury history is real and cannot be waved away. A torn ACL in late 2024, a quad issue that cost him three games in 2023, and several other in-game exits add up to a pattern that will concern medical staffs.
player_comparisons: ChadMuma - Wyoming (89%), OluwafemiOladejo - UCLA (79%), HenryTo'oTo'o - Alabama (79%)

bio: Desmond Reid, coming out of Miramar High School where he rushed for 1,536 yards and 22 touchdowns as a senior, Reid wasn't on the radar of major college programs, earning just a three-star rating from recruiting services. His journey began at Western Carolina, where he quietly built a foundation that would eventually catch the attention of Power Five programs. Reid's breakthrough came during his sophomore season at Western Carolina in 2023, when he exploded for 897 rushing yards and 13 touchdowns in just eight games before injury cut his campaign short. The American Football Coaches Association took notice, naming him a second-team All-American despite the limited games played. His 112.1 rushing yards per game average and the ability to pile up 1,180 all-purpose yards in a truncated season demonstrated the versatility that would define his game. When Western Carolina offensive coordinator Kade Bell made the jump to Pittsburgh, Reid followed, setting the stage for his breakout performance in the ACC. The 2024 season became Reid's coming-out party on the national stage, where he shattered expectations and rewrote the record books at Pittsburgh. His 962 rushing yards on 183 carries and 579 receiving yards on 52 catches made him one of college football's most complete offensive weapons. Reid became the first player since Christian McCaffrey in 2015 to record three games with at least 100 receiving yards and 50 rushing yards, while his 154.91 all-purpose yards per game ranked fifth nationally. Multiple All-America selections from the Associated Press, CBS Sports, ESPN, and The Athletic validated what film watchers had been seeing all season - a dynamic playmaker who could impact the game in multiple phases.
strengths: Lightning-quick lateral movement that turns defenders into spectators, consistently making multiple defenders miss in tight quarters with ankle-breaking cuts. Devastating breakaway speed that shows up repeatedly on film, with the ability to outrun entire secondaries once he hits the open field. Exceptional receiving skills that extend far beyond checkdowns, running precise routes and creating separation like a natural slot receiver. Rare vision and patience for his size, pressing holes to manipulate linebackers before exploding through creases with perfect timing. Electric return ability that adds another dimension to his value, proven by his 78-yard punt return touchdown against Kent State. Outstanding acceleration and burst through the hole, reaching top speed in just a few steps to maximize every opportunity. Exceptional hands and ball security, showing natural instincts as a pass-catcher with the ability to adjust to poorly thrown balls. Competitive fire that shows up in clutch moments, like his historic 100-yard rushing and receiving performance in Pittsburgh's comeback victory at Cincinnati.
weaknesses: Concerning frame at 175 pounds raises legitimate questions about his ability to withstand the physical punishment of every-down NFL carries. Pass protection remains a significant weakness, showing hesitancy and poor technique when asked to pick up blitzing linebackers and defensive backs. Limited power between the tackles makes him vulnerable in short-yardage situations where teams need guaranteed yards rather than explosive plays. Injury history at Western Carolina and missed games at Pittsburgh create durability concerns that NFL teams will scrutinize heavily during medical evaluations. Inconsistent ball security in traffic, with a tendency to carry the ball loosely when navigating through heavy contact in the secondary.
player_comparisons: DeuceVaughn - Kansas State (52%), EvanHull - Northwestern (45%), BlakeWatson - Memphis (42%)
name: Desmond Reid

Background:
Desmond Reid, coming out of Miramar High School where he rushed for 1,536 yards and 22 touchdowns as a senior, Reid wasn't on the radar of major college programs, earning just a three-star rating from recruiting services. His journey began at Western Carolina, where he quietly built a foundation that would eventually catch the attention of Power Five programs. Reid's breakthrough came during his sophomore season at Western Carolina in 2023, when he exploded for 897 rushing yards and 13 touchdowns in just eight games before injury cut his campaign short. The American Football Coaches Association took notice, naming him a second-team All-American despite the limited games played. His 112.1 rushing yards per game average and the ability to pile up 1,180 all-purpose yards in a truncated season demonstrated the versatility that would define his game. When Western Carolina offensive coordinator Kade Bell made the jump to Pittsburgh, Reid followed, setting the stage for his breakout performance in the ACC. The 2024 season became Reid's coming-out party on the national stage, where he shattered expectations and rewrote the record books at Pittsburgh. His 962 rushing yards on 183 carries and 579 receiving yards on 52 catches made him one of college football's most complete offensive weapons. Reid became the first player since Christian McCaffrey in 2015 to record three games with at least 100 receiving yards and 50 rushing yards, while his 154.91 all-purpose yards per game ranked fifth nationally. Multiple All-America selections from the Associated Press, CBS Sports, ESPN, and The Athletic validated what film watchers had been seeing all season - a dynamic playmaker who could impact the game in multiple phases.

Strengths:
Lightning-quick lateral movement that turns defenders into spectators, consistently making multiple defenders miss in tight quarters with ankle-breaking cuts. Devastating breakaway speed that shows up repeatedly on film, with the ability to outrun entire secondaries once he hits the open field. Exceptional receiving skills that extend far beyond checkdowns, running precise routes and creating separation like a natural slot receiver. Rare vision and patience for his size, pressing holes to manipulate linebackers before exploding through creases with perfect timing. Electric return ability that adds another dimension to his value, proven by his 78-yard punt return touchdown against Kent State. Outstanding acceleration and burst through the hole, reaching top speed in just a few steps to maximize every opportunity. Exceptional hands and ball security, showing natural instincts as a pass-catcher with the ability to adjust to poorly thrown balls. Competitive fire that shows up in clutch moments, like his historic 100-yard rushing and receiving performance in Pittsburgh's comeback victory at Cincinnati.

Weaknesses:
Concerning frame at 175 pounds raises legitimate questions about his ability to withstand the physical punishment of every-down NFL carries. Pass protection remains a significant weakness, showing hesitancy and poor technique when asked to pick up blitzing linebackers and defensive backs. Limited power between the tackles makes him vulnerable in short-yardage situations where teams need guaranteed yards rather than explosive plays. Injury history at Western Carolina and missed games at Pittsburgh create durability concerns that NFL teams will scrutinize heavily during medical evaluations. Inconsistent ball security in traffic, with a tendency to carry the ball loosely when navigating through heavy contact in the secondary.

bio: Devin Moore was a four-star recruit out of Naples, Florida, where he posted five interceptions as a junior and earned an invitation to the 2022 All-American Bowl. He originally committed to Notre Dame before flipping to Florida in December 2021, choosing the Gators over Alabama, LSU and Auburn. His first three seasons in Gainesville were defined by injuries. Moore played five games as a true freshman in 2022, saw seven appearances and grabbed his first career interception against Tennessee in 2023, then started six games in 2024 before a shoulder issue ended his year early. Across those three seasons he logged just 639 defensive snaps. When available, the production was there: two interceptions and five pass breakups in limited action, plus a seven-tackle, two-PBU, pick game against Kentucky in 2024. Everything came together in 2025. Moore started all 11 games, logged 556 defensive snaps, and posted career bests across the board: 35 tackles, two interceptions, four pass breakups, a sack and a forced fumble. He picked off Arch Manning in a win over ninth-ranked Texas and finished the fall with his bachelor's degree in General Business and a four-time SEC Academic Honor Roll distinction.
strengths: Outstanding size and length at 6'3", 198 pounds for an outside corner. Uses his frame to jam receivers at the line and disrupt release timing in press. Tracks deep balls well at full speed and wins contested catch-point battles. Five career interceptions against SEC quarterbacks, not inflated by weak competition. Comfortable in both man and zone assignments with strong grading in each. Willing and physical run defender who fills alleys and tackles with authority. Career missed tackle rate sits around 8%, which is strong for a boundary corner. Showed clear year-over-year improvement in coverage grading and snap durability.
weaknesses: Only one full season of consistent starting experience across four college years. Injury history is extensive: shoulder surgeries, back problems and concussions throughout. Average speed for the position, and closing burst is not going to bail him out. Gets upright in his backpedal and can be late flipping hips on sharp breaking routes. Allowed receptions on over half his targets in 2025, including two touchdowns. Tendency to grab past five yards could draw flags at the NFL level consistently.

bio: Diego Pavia from Albuquerque, New Mexico, was a three-sport standout at Volcano Vista High School where he wrestled, played baseball, and ran the football team's offense. He was good enough to lead Volcano Vista to an 11-0 regular season as a senior before falling in the state semifinals, and tough enough to reach the state wrestling finals three times, winning a title along the way. None of that was enough to earn an FBS scholarship. Without a single offer from the top level of college football, Pavia packed his bags for the New Mexico Military Institute in Roswell, where he won the starting job as a true freshman during the COVID-altered 2020 season. By 2021, he was throwing for 1,728 yards and 21 touchdowns with 658 rushing yards and seven scores on the ground, helping NMMI capture the NJCAA National Championship. New Mexico State gave Pavia his first crack at FBS football in 2022, where he appeared in 12 games with eight starts. He threw for 1,450 yards with 13 touchdowns and six interceptions, flashing enough dual-threat ability to earn Quick Lane Bowl MVP honors after torching Bowling Green for 167 passing yards, two touchdowns, and 65 rushing yards. The 2023 season was the full breakout. Starting all 15 games, Pavia racked up 2,973 passing yards with 26 touchdowns against nine picks while adding 923 rushing yards and seven scores. He led the Aggies to their first 10-win season since 1960, claimed Conference USA Offensive Player of the Year, and authored one of the program's most iconic moments when he carved up Auburn for three touchdowns and zero interceptions on 67.9% passing in NMSU's first-ever win over an SEC opponent. Pavia followed head coach Jerry Kill to Nashville as a graduate transfer in 2024 and immediately transformed the Vanderbilt program. In his first season with the Commodores, he completed 177 of 298 passes for 2,293 yards with 20 touchdowns and just four interceptions while gashing defenses for 801 rushing yards and eight scores on the ground. The signature win came against top-ranked Alabama, where he went 16-of-20 for 252 yards and two touchdowns with 56 rushing yards, making him just the 13th starting quarterback since 2004 to beat both Auburn and Alabama in the same season. He earned SEC Newcomer of the Year and capped the year with a five-touchdown Birmingham Bowl MVP performance. In 2025, everything went to another level. Pavia completed 70.6% of his passes for 3,539 yards with 29 touchdowns and eight interceptions, rushed for 862 yards and 10 scores, and led Vanderbilt to its first 10-win season in program history. He finished as the Heisman Trophy runner-up behind Fernando Mendoza, was named SEC Offensive Player of the Year, AP Newcomer of the Year, a CBS Sports All-America First Teamer, and became Vanderbilt's first All-America quarterback since Bill Wade in 1951. Over 52 career games across three programs, Pavia accounted for more than 70% of Vanderbilt's total offense in his final season, a mark no other Heisman finalist came close to touching.
strengths: Put the tape on against Alabama. 16-of-20. Two touchdowns through the air, another 56 yards on the ground. That is not a fluke. This kid shows up when the lights come on. Runs a 4.6 and it shows on every designed carry. He hits the hole with real burst and runs angry through contact, not dancing around looking for the sideline. The ball jumps out of his hand. Three-quarters release with genuine velocity and a tight spiral, and he can really spin it on deep shots when he plants and lets it rip. Light feet in the pocket. No heel click, clean base, and he senses pressure before it arrives. Watch how he slides off the rush and keeps his eyes up. That part is natural. Went from a 59% completion rate to 70.6% in one offseason. That kind of jump tells you the kid is coachable and puts in the work. The rhythm game got noticeably better in 2025. 862 rushing yards and 10 scores is not some read-option gimmick. He takes hits, breaks tackles, and punishes linebackers who take bad angles. There is some dog in him as a runner. 52 games across three programs, SEC road environments, a Heisman stage, bowl games. Nothing rattles this guy. He has been through the wringer and keeps coming back tougher. Deep ball accuracy is the real surprise. When he trusts his mechanics and drives through his release, he can drop it in the bucket from 40-plus yards. That part of his game does not get talked about enough.
weaknesses: Here is the deal: he measured 5-9 7/8 at the Senior Bowl. That is not 6-foot, which is what Vanderbilt had him listed at. At that height, throwing lanes disappear behind NFL offensive lines and you are playing the position blindfolded at times. 198 pounds soaking wet. He takes a beating as a runner and does not protect himself. You watch him lower his shoulder into linebackers and wonder how long that body holds up on Sundays. One read and go. The pocket can be perfectly clean and he is already looking to bail. NFL defensive coordinators will sit in coverage, let him scramble into trouble, and make him pay for it. Vanderbilt's offense was built on quick throws, screens, and predetermined reads. He has not been asked to work through full-field progressions consistently, and when defenses rotated coverages post-snap, he got fooled. Stares down his first read something fierce. Safeties at the next level will jump routes in their sleep against that kind of eye discipline. It is the one habit that could sink him fast.
player_comparisons: JordanTravis - Florida State (71%), StetsonBennett - Georgia (68%), CamMiller - North Dakota State (68%)

bio: The Thieneman name was already part of Purdue football lore before Dillon ever stepped on campus. His brothers walked on with the Boilermakers, but Dillon took a different path to West Lafayette. Coming out of Westfield High School in Indiana, he was a three-star prospect ranked No. 12 in the state and No. 61 nationally at his position by 247Sports. He earned Indiana's Mr. Football honor at defensive back in 2022 and put up eye-popping prep numbers: 246 tackles, 11 tackles for loss, 11 passes defended, and five interceptions across his high school career. Recruiting services had him pegged as a solid but unspectacular get, with ratings of 5.6 from Rivals and an 0.87 composite from 247. That unspectacular label lasted about three weeks into his freshman season. In his collegiate debut against Fresno State, Thieneman posted 10 tackles and an interception, and he never slowed down. By season's end, he had racked up 106 total tackles (74 solo), six interceptions, and two forced fumbles while earning FWAA Defensive Freshman of the Year, Big Ten Freshman of the Year, and third-team All-American recognition. He became the first defender in Big Ten history to win a conference weekly award five times in a single season. His sophomore campaign brought more of the same production, leading all Big Ten defensive backs with 104 tackles while adding seven pass breakups and a sack across 12 starts, though the Boilermakers cratered to a 1-11 record around him. That dismal team record pushed Thieneman into the transfer portal, where Oregon's Dan Lanning pounced. The move to Eugene was transformative in every sense. Thieneman earned first-team All-Big Ten and first-team All-American honors in 2025, picking off two more passes while continuing to pile up tackles (99 total, 72 solo) and adding four pass breakups and 30 run stops. His 5:30 a.m. arrival time at the facility became a running joke among teammates who quickly realized the Indiana kid was wired differently. After three seasons of elite production totaling over 300 career tackles and eight interceptions, Thieneman declared for the 2026 NFL Draft with feedback projecting him as a top-50 selection.
strengths: Coverage instincts are top-shelf across this entire safety class. His eyes stay anchored on the quarterback, reads route concepts early, and positions himself to take away throwing windows before the ball leaves the passer's hand. Fluid hips and a silky backpedal at all depths allow him to transition smoothly from zone drops to aggressive breaks on underneath routes without any wasted motion or false steps. Elite speed at 205 pounds gives him legitimate range to play single-high and close sideline to sideline. That closing burst shows up constantly on tape when he's crashing on short throws. True alignment chameleon who lined up at free safety, strong safety, and in the slot across his career. Logged over 1,100 snaps at free safety, 700-plus in the box, and handled nickel responsibilities without looking out of place. Tackles with real intent and follows through on contact. Wraps up with textbook technique and drives through ball carriers with hip rotation that generates power well beyond what his frame suggests. Ball skills are legitimate, not just opportunistic. Eight career interceptions on limited targets, and he tracks the football like a receiver when it's in the air, adjusting his body and high-pointing at the catch point. Triggers downhill against the run game with urgency and violence. He sees run blocking schemes developing and fires into the action, posting 30 run stops in 2025 alone while splitting time between deep and box alignments. Competitive fire burns hot and never flickers. Teammates and coaches at both Purdue and Oregon consistently point to his obsessive preparation and relentless effort as the standard for the entire defense.
weaknesses: Play-action remains a trap for him at times. His eagerness to trigger downhill against the run gets him caught leaning forward on play fakes, opening up space behind him. Man coverage starts to crack on longer-developing routes. His change-of-direction quickness is average in tight spaces, and savvy route runners can create separation past the break. Pursuit angles need cleaning up, particularly on perimeter runs. His aggression gets him over-running plays, turning what should be clean tackles into ankle grabs or missed attempts entirely. Block-shedding is a work in progress. At 205 pounds, he can get stuck on bigger bodies when he fails to use active hands and proper leverage to disengage from blockers in the run game. Processing speed against complex route combinations shows occasional hesitation. Typically a strength, but there are moments on tape where he's a beat late passing off responsibilities or recognizing what's developing in front of him.
player_comparisons: MalachiMoore - Alabama (86%), RonnieHickman - Ohio State (80%), JevonHolland - Oregon (80%)

bio: Domani Jackson came out of Mater Dei High School in Santa Ana, California, as one of the most decorated defensive back recruits in recent memory. He earned consensus five-star ratings across all major recruiting services and was a two-sport athlete who tied the California state record in the 100 meters during his senior track season. On the football field, Jackson tallied 48 tackles and nine pass breakups as a sophomore in 2019 while helping Mater Dei capture multiple championships during his prep career. His college career took a winding route. Two seasons at USC yielded just 18 games, 28 tackles, and four pass breakups, and Jackson never locked down a full-time starting job with the Trojans. He transferred to Alabama before the 2024 season and immediately stepped into the lineup, starting all 13 games. That first year in Tuscaloosa produced 52 tackles, seven pass breakups, two interceptions, and a forced fumble. The coaching staff recognized his efforts with five weekly defensive awards across the season. Jackson came back for 2025 and started 10 of 15 games as a senior, finishing with 39 tackles and a pass breakup. He accepted a Shrine Bowl invitation following the season.
strengths: Good length and frame for an outside corner who can press and reroute bigger pass catchers. Fluid hips and a clean backpedal let him turn and run with vertical stems. Not afraid to fill against the run and shows urgency getting downhill on outside runs. Has enough long speed to recover when he gets stacked or loses a step off the line. Improved steadily across his two Alabama seasons after a quiet stretch at USC. Effective early in the route using his length to disrupt release timing at the catch point. Handles zone responsibilities well, passing off routes and staying aware of spacing around him.
weaknesses: Two career interceptions across nearly 2,000 snaps is a poor return on his athletic ability. Relies on grabbing when he loses leverage downfield, leading to avoidable flags. Susceptible to fakes and double moves when playing off, biting early and giving up the top. Tackling effort fluctuates, sometimes opting to arm-tackle rather than drive through contact. Late finding the ball in man coverage, which limits his chances at contested catches. Allowed a high completion rate in his final season despite reduced target volume.
player_comparisons: NohlWilliams - California (93%), JasonMarshall Jr. - Florida (92%), AaronRobinson - UCF (92%)
name: Domani Jackson DB USC

Susceptible to fakes and double moves when playing off, biting early and giving up the top. Tackling effort fluctuates, sometimes opting to arm-tackle rather than drive through contact. Late finding the ball in man coverage, which limits his chances at contested catches. Allowed a high completion rate in his final season despite reduced target volume.

bio: Domonique Orange is a Kansas City, Missouri native who was a two-time all-state and three-time all-district selection in high school. Rivals rated him a four-star recruit and the 15th-best defensive tackle nationally, while 247Sports and ESPN had him as a three-star. He chose Iowa State over Arkansas, Florida State, and Texas A&M. Orange played all 12 games as a true freshman in 2022 before taking on a bigger role in 2023, recording 16 tackles and 2.0 TFLs across 13 games. His junior season was his most productive: 24 tackles, 4.5 TFLs, and his first career sack in the Big 12 Championship game against Arizona State. He earned Honorable Mention All-Big 12 from coaches in both 2023 and 2024. As a senior, Orange started all 12 games and earned Bednarik National Player of the Week honors after a six-tackle performance against Rimington Trophy winner Logan Jones and Iowa. He finished 2025 with All-Big 12 Third Team and Phil Steele Fourth Team All-American recognition. Across 50 career games, he totaled 66 tackles, 7.0 TFLs, 1.0 sack, and 13 quarterback hurries. He was invited to the Senior Bowl and the NFL Combine, opting to skip combine drills in favor of Iowa State's Pro Day.
strengths: Good first-step quickness for his size; wins the initial timing battle at the snap consistently. Strong anchor against double teams; holds his ground in the A-gaps and forces the combo to stay on him. Long arms and big hands allow him to control blockers and create separation at the point of attack. Impressive upper-body pop; generates real knockback on contact and resets the line of scrimmage. Active feet against zone schemes; runs his legs to prevent reach blocks from cutting off his gap. Excellent recovery ability when initially displaced; fights back to his landmark after absorbing contact. Shows ability to make tackles with blockers still attached, using length to corral runners. Stepped up in big moments, including his performance against Iowa's All-American center. Made the 2025 Feldman freaks list - he wrote:"Orange had a solid 2024 season, making 24 tackles, including 4.5 for a loss - 3.5 of those TFLs came in the last five games of the season. The 6-4, 325-pound Orange, who spent much of his offseason refining his diet, has benched 450 pounds, squatted 650 and cleaned 365. He also has vertical jumped 34 inches. "
weaknesses: Tackling consistency is a real concern; missed tackle rate climbed sharply as a senior. Very limited pass rush production; just one career sack across four college seasons. Struggles to disengage from blocks once locked up; shed quickness needs significant work. Tends to play with a narrow base, which leaves him vulnerable when combo blocks sustain contact. Overshoots the backfield on penetration; needs to learn to break down in the hole. Below-average lateral agility and body control when asked to redirect or slide with moving targets. Slow testing numbers raise questions about range and pursuit ability at the next level.

bio: A prep career at Colerain High School that produced 137 tackles and 19.5 sacks earned Dontay Corleone Division I First-Team All-Ohio honors and kept him close to home in Cincinnati. A three-star recruit with 247Sports and ESPN, he committed to his hometown Bearcats and redshirted in 2021. His 2022 redshirt freshman season was a breakout: 44 tackles, 5.5 tackles for loss, three sacks, two forced fumbles, and PFF's top defensive player grade in the country. He earned AP Third-Team All-American recognition alongside several freshman All-American honors. The 2023 season brought 39 tackles, 6.5 TFLs, three sacks, and Second-Team All-Big 12 from both the AP and conference coaches. A blood clot scare in June 2024 sidelined him for three months, but he returned to start 11 games and collect First-Team All-Big 12 with 26 tackles, five TFLs, and a career-high 3.5 sacks. His 2025 season saw production dip considerably to just 14 tackles and no sacks across 10 games due to an ankle injury suffered early in the year. Nicknamed "The Godfather," Corleone earned his bachelor's degree in December 2024 and finishes his career with 109 tackles, 17 TFLs, and 9.5 sacks across 44 games, with four All-Conference selections.
strengths: Natural leverage at 6-0 is a genuine advantage; fires out low and stays beneath blockers' pads every snap. Refuses to get moved off his spot against double teams; widens his base, bends his knees, and anchors down. Quicker than 340 pounds suggests; short-area burst shows up when shooting gaps or sliding against zone runs. Hand placement and timing are sharp; shoots hands into blockers' chests quickly and controls at the point of attack. Recognizes down blocks early and gets hands on pulling guards or climbing linemen before they reach their spots. Contact balance keeps him upright through chaos; absorbs contact and stays engaged to finish tackles in traffic. Competes on every snap with genuine pursuit effort; redirects quickly on screens and cutbacks without coasting.
weaknesses: Carries too much bad weight at times; reports had him past 350 pounds, and conditioning will need monitoring. Pass rush arsenal is thin beyond first-step quickness and a bull rush; stalls near the line when those fail. Short arms at 31 7/8 inches hurt his ability to extend and shed blocks once blockers get into his chest. Stands up out of his stance too early, killing bull rush power and giving double teams angles to uproot him. Medical history raises real flags: blood clots in his lungs, a 2022 knee issue, and a 2025 ankle injury.
player_comparisons: JJPegues - Ole Miss (71%), JordanPhillips - Maryland (55%), CameronYoung - Mississippi State (54%)
name: Dontay Corleone

bio: Drew Pyne from New Canaan High School was rated a 4-star recruit by ESPN and handed a 3-star grade by 247 Sports. After high school, Pyne opted to join Notre Dame. In 2020 as a freshman Pyne saw action in just 2 games for the Fighting Irish as he threw for just 12 yards on 3 attempts with a completion percent of 66.7 for an average of 4.0 yards per pass, with no TDs, while taking no sacks, and QB rating of 74.3. In 2021 as a sophomore he played in just 2 games as he passed for just 224 yards on 30 attempts with a completion percent of 50 for an average of 7.5 yards per pass, with just 2 TDs, while also taking 3 sacks, and putting up an excellent QB rating of 97.1. As a junior in 2022 Pyne played a total of 11 games. He passed for 2021 yards on 254 attempts and put up a completion percentage of 64.6 for an average of 8.0 yards per attempt, with 22 TDs, and allowing 15 sacks, with an excellent QB rating on the year of 108.1. As a runner he logged 43 runs for a total of 167 yards at an average of 3.9 per run. After season's end Pyne transferred to Arizona State where as a senior Pyne saw action in just 2 games as he threw for just 273 yards on 49 attempts with a completion percent of 53.1 for an average of 5.6 yards per pass, with just 2 TDs, while taking 11 sacks, and totaling a QB rating of 57.6.
strengths: Pyne is enough of a threat as a runner to earn respect from back-side defenders at the next level, opening opportunities for running backs. Physically, Pyne is a natural athlete who plays with the balance and light feet to float in the pocket. Picks up yards with his legs when needed. Ball placement is a strength. Pyne has the natural instincts to make plays when forced to improvise. Generally accurate on intermediate and short throws; flashes anticipation and placement on intermediate outs and the ability to lead receivers on deeper throws. Possesses an NFL arm. Gets the ball from one hash to the opposite sideline in a hurry. Has the zip to hit tight windows on short and intermediate throws.
weaknesses: Sails throws to either sideline; receivers make him look good with acrobatic catches. Back-foot throws are not accurate. Sometimes trusts his arm too much, trying to stick passes late in the play or when off-balance. Pyne has to clean up his throwing motion when he’s on the move. He was often too quick to pull the ball down and take off, and Pyne seemed to have trouble sifting through moving parts in the middle of the field. Pyne will have to prove he can handle an expanded, multi-progression NFL offense.
player_comparisons: ZacThomas - Appalachian State (71%), DrewPlitt - Ball State (63%), SpencerSanders - Ole Miss (62%)

bio: Dylan Brooks from Handley High School was rated a 4-star recruit by ESPN and handed a 4-star grade by 247 Sports. After high school, Brooks joined Auburn after being heavily recruited. In 2022 as a freshman Brooks saw action in 10 games and played a total of 140 snaps for the Tigers. He recorded just 3 tackles, 3 assists, while adding 2 stops. After season's end Brooks transferred to Kansas where as a sophomore for the Jayhawks in 2023 Brooks played 7 games and took part in 80 snaps for the Jayhawks. He recorded just 3 tackles, 2 assists, while adding 2 stops. As a pass rusher, he chalked up 4 total pressures, and 4 QB hurries, no QB hits, and no sacks during the year.
strengths: Quick to read run, showing good agility and speed to get past blockers and finish. Experienced in coverage and gets a strong jam on outside receivers. Combines very good instincts with above-average reaction skills. Almost always finds the ball in traffic. Brooks will always try to plow into the largest piles and has a good knack of ending up glued to the ballcarrier. He has quickness in his hands and uses his length effectively. Tougher between the tackles than many expect. Fluid and quick drop into zone, covers enough ground to be Tampa-2 "MIKE" and knows where the markers are. Brooks has an excellent feel for working off blockers and knowing how and when to counter as the play goes on.
weaknesses: He’s perfectly capable of converting speed into power when Brooks gets to the ball, but too often he slips off tackles or turns into a grab-and-drag tackler rather than a hitter, failing to keep his legs driving through the hit. Brooks is on the small side, and his frame is pretty much maxed out. Despite his effort and intensity, he’ll simply get swallowed up at times. Possesses below-average instincts for the linebacker position. Leaves more plays on the field than he makes.

bio: Emmanuel McNeil-Warren was a three-star recruit who posted 55 tackles and three interceptions as a high school senior but never drew Power Five interest. Toledo took a chance on him with his ESPN rating at 75, Rivals grade at 5.4, and 247 composite at 0.84. He spent 2022 mostly on special teams with seven tackles and a forced fumble across 13 games. He stepped into a starting role in 2023 and finished fourth on the team with 69 tackles, two interceptions, and three tackles for loss. A 13-tackle, one-interception outing against Western Michigan earned him MAC West Defensive Player of the Week. His 2024 season produced 61 tackles through seven games before an injury ended his year. McNeil-Warren put together his best season in 2025: 77 tackles, a 37-yard pick-six against Western Kentucky, three forced fumbles, and two fumble recoveries. He earned Second-Team All-America from the Sporting News and the FWAA along with a Third-Team AP All-America nod, becoming just the second Toledo player on the AP All-America team in 18 years. He was a Jim Thorpe Award semifinalist, PFF's G-6 Defensive Player of the Year, and accepted Senior Bowl and East-West Shrine Bowl invitations. Over four seasons he totaled 214 tackles, five interceptions, and nine forced fumbles.
strengths: Measured 6-4 at the combine with 32-plus-inch arms, a rare physical profile at safety. Nine career forced fumbles reflect a trained habit of attacking the ball at every contact point. Covers ground in zone with fluid transitions, reading quarterback eyes and closing on underneath throws. Fills gaps against the run with physicality and decisiveness, anchoring through contact downhill. Hip fluidity is impressive for his size, redirecting out of his backpedal without the expected stiffness. Best reps come in robber and bracketed zone concepts where his length walls off throwing lanes. Competitive temperament is obvious on film; plays with an edge that disrupts timing across the middle. Production against Western Michigan and Western Kentucky showed he raises his level in key games.
weaknesses: Man coverage against quick-twitch receivers is a concern; feet get heavy on sudden breaks. Combine testing confirmed average explosiveness with a bottom-tier vertical and broad jump. Pre-snap movement is too bouncy on run reads, leading to false steps and delayed triggers. Open-field tackling breaks down in space; lunges and dives instead of breaking down at 201 pounds. Struggles to disengage from offensive linemen climbing to the second level on run plays.
player_comparisons: JLSkinner - Boise State (72%), JadenHicks - Washington State (72%), AntonioJohnson - Texas A&M (70%)
