bio: Emmett Johnson, born and raised in Minneapolis, Minnesota, didn't arrive at Nebraska with the recruiting fanfare that typically accompanies future All-Americans. Standing 5'11" and weighing 200 pounds, the Academy of Holy Angels product earned three-star status from Rivals, ESPN, and 247Sports coming out of high school, where he was named Minnesota's Mr. Football after rushing for over 2,500 yards and 42 touchdowns as a senior. He was also a two-way force, logging 85 tackles and 13 tackles for loss at safety that same year. Johnson appeared in 12 games with six starts as a redshirt freshman in 2023, totaling 411 rushing yards on 90 carries with two touchdowns while helping Nebraska finish second in the Big Ten in rushing. The following season, he served primarily as a change-of-pace back, ranking second on the team with 598 rushing yards on 117 carries while leading the Cornhuskers with 908 all-purpose yards. His 39 receptions that year were the most by a Nebraska running back since 2007, signaling the dual-threat capabilities that would define his breakout campaign. Everything clicked in 2025. Johnson started all 12 regular-season games and exploded for 1,451 rushing yards on 251 carries with 12 touchdowns, posting Nebraska's first 1,000-yard rushing season since 2018 and the program's highest rushing total since Ameer Abdullah's 1,611 yards in 2014. He added 46 receptions for 370 yards and three more scores, becoming just the seventh Big Ten player since 1956 to reach 1,400 rushing yards and 350 receiving yards in a single season. Only Saquon Barkley had accomplished that feat in the previous 20 years. Johnson led the nation in yards from scrimmage per game (151.8), was named Big Ten Running Back of the Year, earned First-Team All-American honors from multiple outlets, and became a semifinalist for both the Maxwell and Doak Walker Awards before declaring for the draft following an historic November in which he averaged 200.5 scrimmage yards per contest.
strengths: Exceptional vision allows him to set up blocks and identify running lanes before they fully materialize, pressing the line of scrimmage with the patience of a veteran. Electric lower-body agility produces string-together cuts that leave defenders grasping at air; his jump-cut ability to bounce outside the tackle box consistently creates positive yardage. Versatile scheme fit with proven production in inside zone, outside zone, and duo concepts; reads his keys cleanly and makes decisive decisions on whether to bounce or bang it up inside. Legitimate weapon in the passing game with 46 receptions in 2025; his route tree and soft hands make him more than a checkdown option on third downs. Slippery ball carrier who gets skinny through tight creases and minimizes the strike zone for incoming defenders, preserving his body and avoiding the big clean shots. Contact balance exceeds what his 200-pound frame suggests; he pinballs off tacklers, runs through arm tackles, and keeps his legs churning after initial contact. Explosive burst through the hole once a crease appears; his acceleration at the second level turns what should be modest gains into chunk plays. Dependable volume back who shouldered a heavy workload down the stretch, demonstrating durability with eight 100-yard rushing games and the ability to get stronger as games wore on.
weaknesses: Pass protection remains a developmental area; his chips lack the stopping power to stall bull rushers, and his blitz pickup technique needs refinement against pressure packages. Home-run hunting tendencies occasionally hurt him; he'll chase cutbacks one too many times instead of planting his foot and getting vertical for available yards. Timed speed is adequate but not special; he won't consistently outrun high-end pursuing defenders to the edge on the NFL level. Slight frame at 200 pounds raises questions about his ability to handle a true bell-cow role and hold up in short-yardage and goal-line situations against NFL front sevens. Power at the point of attack is average at best; he's not going to consistently push piles or drag defenders for extra yardage the way bigger backs can.
player_comparisons: RayDavis - Kentucky (79%), MarShawnLloyd - USC (72%), KennyMcIntosh - Georgia (71%)
name: Emmett Johnson
scouting_report:
Background:
Emmett Johnson, born and raised in Minneapolis, Minnesota, didn't arrive at Nebraska with the recruiting fanfare that typically accompanies future All-Americans. Standing 5'11" and weighing 200 pounds, the Academy of Holy Angels product earned three-star status from Rivals, ESPN, and 247Sports coming out of high school, where he was named Minnesota's Mr. Football after rushing for over 2,500 yards and 42 touchdowns as a senior. He was also a two-way force, logging 85 tackles and 13 tackles for loss at safety that same year. Johnson appeared in 12 games with six starts as a redshirt freshman in 2023, totaling 411 rushing yards on 90 carries with two touchdowns while helping Nebraska finish second in the Big Ten in rushing. The following season, he served primarily as a change-of-pace back, ranking second on the team with 598 rushing yards on 117 carries while leading the Cornhuskers with 908 all-purpose yards. His 39 receptions that year were the most by a Nebraska running back since 2007, signaling the dual-threat capabilities that would define his breakout campaign. Everything clicked in 2025. Johnson started all 12 regular-season games and exploded for 1,451 rushing yards on 251 carries with 12 touchdowns, posting Nebraska's first 1,000-yard rushing season since 2018 and the program's highest rushing total since Ameer Abdullah's 1,611 yards in 2014. He added 46 receptions for 370 yards and three more scores, becoming just the seventh Big Ten player since 1956 to reach 1,400 rushing yards and 350 receiving yards in a single season. Only Saquon Barkley had accomplished that feat in the previous 20 years. Johnson led the nation in yards from scrimmage per game (151.8), was named Big Ten Running Back of the Year, earned First-Team All-American honors from multiple outlets, and became a semifinalist for both the Maxwell and Doak Walker Awards before declaring for the draft following an historic November in which he averaged 200.5 scrimmage yards per contest.

Strengths:
Exceptional vision allows him to set up blocks and identify running lanes before they fully materialize, pressing the line of scrimmage with the patience of a veteran. Electric lower-body agility produces string-together cuts that leave defenders grasping at air; his jump-cut ability to bounce outside the tackle box consistently creates positive yardage. Versatile scheme fit with proven production in inside zone, outside zone, and duo concepts; reads his keys cleanly and makes decisive decisions on whether to bounce or bang it up inside. Legitimate weapon in the passing game with 46 receptions in 2025; his route tree and soft hands make him more than a checkdown option on third downs. Slippery ball carrier who gets skinny through tight creases and minimizes the strike zone for incoming defenders, preserving his body and avoiding the big clean shots. Contact balance exceeds what his 200-pound frame suggests; he pinballs off tacklers, runs through arm tackles, and keeps his legs churning after initial contact. Explosive burst through the hole once a crease appears; his acceleration at the second level turns what should be modest gains into chunk plays. Dependable volume back who shouldered a heavy workload down the stretch, demonstrating durability with eight 100-yard rushing games and the ability to get stronger as games wore on.

Weaknesses:
Pass protection remains a developmental area; his chips lack the stopping power to stall bull rushers, and his blitz pickup technique needs refinement against pressure packages. Home-run hunting tendencies occasionally hurt him; he'll chase cutbacks one too many times instead of planting his foot and getting vertical for available yards. Timed speed is adequate but not special; he won't consistently outrun high-end pursuing defenders to the edge on the NFL level. Slight frame at 200 pounds raises questions about his ability to handle a true bell-cow role and hold up in short-yardage and goal-line situations against NFL front sevens. Power at the point of attack is average at best; he's not going to consistently push piles or drag defenders for extra yardage the way bigger backs can.

bio: Eric McAlister grew up in Azle, Texas, about 20 miles northwest of Fort Worth, and established himself as one of the state's premier high school receivers. During his senior year at Azle High, he hauled in 70 catches for 1,498 yards and 20 touchdowns while leading his team to back-to-back district titles. Those numbers earned him three-star status across the major recruiting services and recognition as one of the top 100 receivers nationally according to 247Sports. He signed with Boise State and redshirted in 2021 before beginning his collegiate career in earnest. McAlister's time in Boise showed steady growth. After limited action as a redshirt freshman in 2022 where he averaged an eye-popping 23.55 yards per catch on 11 receptions and four scores, he broke out as a sophomore with 47 catches for 873 yards and five touchdowns across nine games. The production earned attention, but McAlister wanted to come home. He transferred to TCU ahead of the 2024 season, joining a loaded receiver room that included Savion Williams, Jack Bech, and JP Richardson. Despite the competition for targets, he carved out a significant role with 39 receptions for 762 yards and five touchdowns while leading the nation in first-down catch percentage at 89.7 percent on receptions. Everything clicked during his senior year in Fort Worth. With Williams and Bech gone to the NFL, McAlister became quarterback Josh Hoover's clear number one option and responded with one of the best receiving seasons in TCU history. He finished with 1,190 receiving yards, second-most ever by a Horned Frog wideout, and 10 touchdowns. His 254-yard, three-touchdown performance against SMU stands as the second-best single-game receiving total in program history. The season earned him third-team All-American honors from the Associated Press, first-team All-Big 12 recognition, and a spot as a Biletnikoff Award semifinalist. He capped his career by choosing to play in the Alamo Bowl despite losing his starting quarterback to the transfer portal, catching eight passes for 69 yards in TCU's win over USC.
strengths: Gets vertical in a hurry and stacks corners with ease. Once he's on top of you, good luck. That long speed is real. Ball tracking is a thing of beauty. Looks it in over his shoulder, adjusts to underthrows, plucks it away from his frame. Natural hands. This kid runs angry after the catch. Broke five tackles on one play against SMU. Defenders bounce off him and he keeps his legs churning. At 6'3" and 205, he wins the physical battles at the catch point. Back-shoulder fades are automatic. Just throw it up and let him go get it. Sinks his hips and snaps out of breaks cleanly on intermediate stuff. The decel is smooth for a bigger receiver. Shows some dog in the run game. Laid out a DB to spring a 75-yard touchdown against North Carolina. Takes pride in that dirty work. Works the middle of the field without flinching. Takes shots from linebackers, hangs onto the ball, pops right back up. Finds the soft spots in zone coverage like a veteran. Good feel for settling in windows and giving his quarterback an easy target.
weaknesses: Drifts at the top of his stems. That extra half-second he takes to gather himself will get him blanketed by NFL corners. The 4.5 forty is what it is. Play speed looks faster, but he's not blowing by anybody at the next level on pure jets alone. Blocking technique is sloppy. The effort is there, the want-to is there, but he whiffs on assignments too often to trust in the run game. Concentration drops pop up on the easy ones. Makes the circus catches, then lets a simple dig bounce off his hands. Maddening. Route tree was pretty limited in college. Lots of slants, posts, and go balls. Need to see more variety before trusting him on the full tree.
player_comparisons: A.T.Perry - Wake Forest (84%), MattLanders - Arkansas (83%), NickNash - San Jose State (79%)

bio: Eric O'Neill grew up on Staten Island, where he starred at Tottenville High School as a two-sport athlete in football and basketball. Born on the island with a chip on his shoulder and barely a blip on the recruiting radar as a two-star prospect, O'Neill began his college career at Long Island University in 2021. He saw just three games as a true freshman before things clicked in a major way. By his sophomore year, the 6'3", 261-pound defensive end had become the most disruptive player in the Northeast Conference, piling up 51 tackles, 21 tackles-for-loss, and 10 sacks to earn FCS Freshman All-America honors and the NEC Defensive Rookie of the Year award. He was also a finalist for the Jerry Rice Award, given to the top freshman in FCS football. His junior year at LIU cemented him as one of the best defenders at the FCS level. O'Neill racked up 80 tackles and a staggering 23.5 tackles-for-loss while finishing as a finalist for the Buck Buchanan Award, which goes to the top defensive player in the FCS. He earned NEC Defensive Player of the Year and multiple All-America selections, and was the type of player opponents had to scheme around every single week. That kind of dominance at a smaller school naturally raised questions about what he could do at higher levels of competition, and O'Neill answered by transferring to James Madison for the 2024 season. In Harrisonburg, he was nothing short of spectacular: 52 tackles, 18.5 tackles-for-loss, and 13 sacks in 13 games. He collected First Team All-Sun Belt honors, was named a Group of Five All-American by HERO Sports, and ranked fifth nationally in sacks per game. A 27-yard pick-six against Ball State and a career-high 2.5-sack performance against Marshall were just a few of the highlights from a breakout FBS campaign. For his final college season, O'Neill transferred once more, this time to Rutgers, bringing his act to the Big Ten. The homecoming put him just miles from where he played high school ball. The step up in competition was apparent in the box score, as his numbers dipped to 37 tackles, 9.5 tackles-for-loss, and 2.5 sacks across 12 starts. But he still flashed at times, recording a career-high 10 tackles in a win over Maryland and notching a sack against Iowa. Over four full seasons of production, O'Neill compiled 222 career tackles, 72.5 tackles-for-loss, and 31 sacks across 50 games, adding nine pass breakups, three forced fumbles, and two interceptions. He was a three-time First Team All-Conference selection, earned invitations to the Senior Bowl Top 300 and Shrine Bowl 1000, and was named to the Lott IMPACT Trophy watch list heading into his final campaign.
strengths: First-step quickness off the snap is his bread and butter, firing out of his stance with a violence that forces tackles into panic mode immediately. Owns a legitimate counter game that he pulls out at the right moments, particularly a cross-chop that works well after he establishes speed upfield early in drives. Run defense grading consistently strong throughout his career, showing he sets edges with discipline and does not get washed out of his gap despite undersized frame. Accumulated over 90 career quarterback hurries, suggesting his pressure rate stays high even when sack totals fluctuate from season to season. His transfer history from LIU to JMU to Rutgers demonstrates unusual self-awareness and drive; he kept chasing better competition rather than staying comfortable. Alignment versatility is real, having taken meaningful snaps everywhere from a wide-nine look to kicked inside as a 4i-technique on passing downs. Shows genuine feel for timing the snap count, getting off the ball a half-beat before blockers set their hands, which compensates for physical limitations at the point of attack. Backside pursuit is tireless and he takes honest angles to the ball carrier; the Maryland game in 2025 showed ten tackles worth of that effort against Big Ten competition.
weaknesses: Tackling has been a persistent problem throughout college, with missed tackle rates that stayed stubbornly high across every level of competition he played at. The Rutgers season told a sobering story about what happens against premier offensive lines: his pass rush production fell off a cliff in the Big Ten. Undersized at both height and weight for the position, sitting in the bottom third of the EDGE class in both measurements, which limits his ability to stack and shed NFL blockers. Relies too heavily on effort and quickness, because when his initial rush gets stonewalled, he lacks the anchor strength to win late in the rep at the point of attack. At 13 sacks during his JMU year but just 2.5 at Rutgers, there is a legitimate question about whether his production was inflated by weaker Sun Belt and FCS competition.
player_comparisons: DaelinHayes - Notre Dame (92%), DayonHayes - Colorado (90%), AdetokunboOgundeji - Notre Dame (85%)

bio: All-state honors in football, basketball, and track at McCallie School in Chattanooga made Eric Rivers one of the more versatile prep athletes in Tennessee. A three-star recruit according to 247Sports, he was a do-it-all playmaker for the Blue Tornado, lining up at wide receiver, running back, cornerback, and kick returner while helping McCallie capture back-to-back state championships in 2019 and 2020. Despite that high school pedigree, his college path was anything but straightforward. Rivers walked on at Memphis as a defensive back in 2021, seeing action in six games and recording six tackles, mostly on special teams. A shoulder injury wiped out his 2022 season entirely. From there, he made the decision to transfer to Florida International, arriving as a walk-on again and making the full-time switch to wide receiver. He earned a scholarship after his first semester and caught 32 passes for 370 yards and two touchdowns in 2023. The 2024 season is where things took off: Rivers hauled in 62 receptions for 1,166 yards and 12 touchdowns, highlighted by an 11-catch, 295-yard, three-touchdown performance against New Mexico State that set a school record. That season made him the first Associated Press All-American in FIU history, earning third-team honors along with first-team all-Conference USA recognition. For his final college season, Rivers transferred to Georgia Tech and stepped into the ACC. The production naturally dropped in a more competitive conference with a different offensive system, but he still finished with 45 receptions for 649 yards, two touchdowns, and a 68-yard long reception. He earned his bachelor's degree from FIU and is currently studying business administration at Georgia Tech. Rivers also participated in the 2026 Shrine Bowl, where he stood out as a reliable target and flashed his ability as a kick returner. A two-time academic all-district honoree, he will be 24 years old on draft day.
strengths: Legit speed that showed up on tape at FIU, where he averaged 18.8 yards per catch and posted a 3.57 yards per route run in 2024. Fluid release off the line against both press and off-coverage, using quick feet and tempo changes to win early in the route without relying on pure power. Clean footwork at the top of his routes, particularly on comebacks and deep outs, where he sinks his hips and snaps off breaks with real precision. Chews up cushion in a hurry against off-coverage and forces corners to respect the vertical, which opens up his intermediate game underneath on curls and crossers. Deep ball tracking is a real weapon, adjusting to throws in stride without losing speed, and his deep receiving grade backs up what the tape shows. Competitive blocker who takes his responsibilities seriously and plays through the whistle, showing more fight in that area than most receivers his size. Versatile alignment history with the majority of snaps outside (72.7% wide at Georgia Tech) but functional enough in the slot (27.3%) to move around a formation. Brings legitimate special teams value as a punt and kick returner, which adds to his roster-spot case and gives him a clear path to contributing on Sundays from day one.
weaknesses: At 180 pounds, durability is a fair concern, and bigger corners at the NFL level will be able to push him around at the line of scrimmage. Twelve drops on 138 career catches (8.0% drop rate) is a number that jumps off the page, and too many of them are focus drops on catchable balls rather than difficult situations. Contested catch numbers are rough, converting just 10 of 27 career contested targets (37%), and he lacks the frame and catch radius to win 50/50 balls consistently. Production dipped noticeably at Georgia Tech, going from 3.57 Y/RR at FIU down to 2.07 in the ACC, raising real questions about whether his 2024 breakout was competition-driven. Turns 24 before draft day, which limits his developmental ceiling compared to younger receivers and puts pressure on him to contribute immediately in a defined role.
player_comparisons: TahjWashington - USC (90%), MarvinMims Jr. - Oklahoma (84%), RondaleMoore - Purdue (83%)

bio: Ezra Dotson-Oyetade from Lakeview Centennial High School was rated a 4-star recruit by ESPN and handed a 4-star grade by 247 Sports. Following high school, Dotson-Oyetade opted to join TCU. In 2022 as a freshman Dotson-Oyetade saw action in just 2 games and played a total of 13 snaps for the Horned Frogs. He allowed no QB hurries, no QB hits, and no sacks while playing sparingly. As a sophomore in 2023 he played in just one game and a total of 8 snaps. He conceded no QB hurries, no QB hits, and no sacks while playing sparingly.
strengths: Does a nice job on the second level, moving upfield quickly and showing the flexibility and balance to land blocks in space. Quick-footed with smooth movement skills to cover a large area. Rangy with above average lateral ability. Bursts off the snap and sets up quickly with little wasted motion. A smooth athlete with terrific mobility and footwork, staying light on his feet. Has the lateral range, natural shuffle movements, and lower body quickness to mirror speed rushers. Has the agility, technique, and size to contribute early in his professional career. Quick, active hands to latch on to defenders. Dips shoulder and moves his feet to get outside leverage when needed. Strong and low coming out of his stance, effective in short-yardage situations. An agile mover who puts his length to good use, using his hands aggressively and landing solid initial punches. Dotson-Oyetade can move to the second level as a run blocker, with the coordination and balance to hit moving targets.
weaknesses: A short-area blocker with limited range and doesn't consistently get to the second level. Will play undisciplined at times, especially in space. Lacks a finishing attitude. Is a little too much of a waist bender, and Dotson-Oyetade doesn’t play with ideal power and functional strength. Most concerning - he often gets caught dropping his head, a bad habit that will have to be corrected. Raw as a pass protector, looking choppy and unnatural when moving back into his set, often playing too upright.
player_comparisons: JakeAndrews - Troy (89%), SamGerak - Northwestern (88%), ColinNewell - Iowa State (86%)
name: Ezra Dotson Oyetade
scouting_report:
Background:
Ezra Dotson-Oyetade from Lakeview Centennial High School was rated a 4-star recruit by ESPN and handed a 4-star grade by 247 Sports. Following high school, Dotson-Oyetade opted to join TCU. In 2022 as a freshman Dotson-Oyetade saw action in just 2 games and played a total of 13 snaps for the Horned Frogs. He allowed no QB hurries, no QB hits, and no sacks while playing sparingly. As a sophomore in 2023 he played in just one game and a total of 8 snaps. He conceded no QB hurries, no QB hits, and no sacks while playing sparingly.

Strengths:
Does a nice job on the second level, moving upfield quickly and showing the flexibility and balance to land blocks in space. Quick-footed with smooth movement skills to cover a large area. Rangy with above average lateral ability. Bursts off the snap and sets up quickly with little wasted motion. A smooth athlete with terrific mobility and footwork, staying light on his feet. Has the lateral range, natural shuffle movements, and lower body quickness to mirror speed rushers. Has the agility, technique, and size to contribute early in his professional career. Quick, active hands to latch on to defenders. Dips shoulder and moves his feet to get outside leverage when needed. Strong and low coming out of his stance, effective in short-yardage situations. An agile mover who puts his length to good use, using his hands aggressively and landing solid initial punches. Dotson-Oyetade can move to the second level as a run blocker, with the coordination and balance to hit moving targets.

Weaknesses:
A short-area blocker with limited range and doesn't consistently get to the second level. Will play undisciplined at times, especially in space. Lacks a finishing attitude. Is a little too much of a waist bender, and Dotson-Oyetade doesn’t play with ideal power and functional strength. Most concerning - he often gets caught dropping his head, a bad habit that will have to be corrected. Raw as a pass protector, looking choppy and unnatural when moving back into his set, often playing too upright.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Defense Faced Rating: 69%
  Pass Blocking: 63%
  Run Blocking: 63%

NFL Player Comparisons: JakeAndrews - Troy (89%), SamGerak - Northwestern (88%), ColinNewell - Iowa State (86%)

bio: Febechi Nwaiwu walked on at North Texas in 2021 without a single star next to his name from Rivals, ESPN, or 247Sports. No scholarship offers, no recruiting hype, no blue-chip pedigree. He redshirted that first year, appeared in just two games, and quietly went to work. By his true freshman season in 2022, Nwaiwu had forced his way into the starting lineup, logging 12 starts across 14 games at right tackle and right guard. The Mean Green offense ranked 25th nationally in rushing that year, and Nwaiwu was a significant reason why. He earned second-team Freshman All-American honors from The Athletic and third-team All-C-USA recognition, blocking for an attack that averaged nearly 200 rushing yards per game and hung a school-record 475 yards on the ground against Louisiana Tech. His 2023 campaign at North Texas was cut short by an injury that cost him two games, but he still managed seven starts before the setback. The bigger move came after that season, when Nwaiwu transferred to Oklahoma for the 2024 season and immediately stepped into the starting right guard spot. That first year in Norman was a grind. The Sooners' offensive line was banged up all year, and Nwaiwu was the only lineman to start all 13 games. He dealt with a revolving door of linemates and took his lumps in a transition year, but the toughness and dependability were obvious even then. His standout performance came in a win over No. 7 Alabama, where the Sooners gashed the Crimson Tide for 257 rushing yards, the most Alabama had given up in 26 games. Nwaiwu earned SEC Offensive Lineman of the Week for that one. The 2025 season was where it all came together. Nwaiwu again started every game, this time earning second-team All-SEC honors from the league's coaches. He dropped his weight from 339 to 326 pounds and, by all accounts from teammates and staff, played the best football of his career. The Sooners converted all 31 of their red zone opportunities into points with Nwaiwu anchoring the interior. He allowed just two pressures on 374 pass blocking snaps during a dominant midseason stretch, and he even slid to center for the final two games of the regular season, picking up that new position on the fly. Oklahoma honored him with the Don Key Award, the program's highest annual distinction, and he was named a finalist for the Burlsworth Trophy, given to the nation's top player who began his career as a walk-on. He also received the Pat Tillman Award at the Shrine Bowl for his character and service.
strengths: Hands fire on contact at the snap, and his grip strength is vice-like once he latches onto a defender's breastplate or chest. Flat-out walls off bull rushers with a wide, rooted base that absorbs power and gives almost nothing back to the pass rusher. Even when he initially yields ground, his ability to reset his feet and re-anchor keeps the pocket intact and the quarterback clean. Deploys a quick one-arm stab that keeps rushers out of his chest and maximizes his 33-and-three-quarter-inch arms to full effect. Reads line games and twists well from the guard position, quickly passing off stunts and picking up loopers with disciplined eyes. His combo-block work is genuinely disruptive; he generates real movement at the line of scrimmage on duo and double-team concepts before climbing to the second level. Excellent size-to-position value for a potential center convert, with the frame, wingspan, and mass to hold up against NFL nose tackles in pass protection. The character and work ethic reviews are as strong as you will find in this draft class, and his body transformation from 339 to 326 pounds speaks to his commitment.
weaknesses: Below-average lateral quickness and short-area burst limit his ability to recover when a defender wins the initial move or crosses his face. Run blocking lacks a finishing element; too many reps where he gets position on a defender but fails to put him on the ground or sustain the block. Reach blocks and long pulls are unreliable, as his foot speed simply does not allow him to consistently get where he needs to be in space. Pad level runs hot and cold, and when his chest gets out over his toes he loses leverage and falls off blocks at the point of attack. Center experience is extremely limited, and his blitz identification and stunt recognition from the pivot still need significant development before he can be trusted there full-time.
player_comparisons: LoganParr - SMU (77%), MarquisHayes - Oklahoma (76%), TylerBooker - Alabama (76%)

bio: Fernando Carmona Jr. began his collegiate career at San Jose State, where he transitioned from tight end to offensive tackle. After redshirting in 2021, Carmona started all 24 games over the next two seasons at left tackle for the Spartans. In 2023, he earned Honorable Mention All-Mountain West honors, anchoring an offensive line that paved the way for a potent rushing attack. The Las Vegas native's journey to becoming an NFL prospect is noteworthy. At Las Vegas High School, Carmona played tight end, earning second-team All-Sunrise League honors in 2019. His versatility was evident as he also competed in basketball and tennis. Carmona's athletic bloodline includes his father, Fernando Sr., who was an All-Western Athletic Conference first baseman for Utah in 1985. Carmona's standout 2023 season at San Jose State caught the attention of larger programs. He logged 765 snaps with an impressive 81.5 overall grade per PFF, including an 81.0 pass block grade. His performance was instrumental in San Jose State's co-regular season Mountain West Championship. Following this success, Carmona transferred to Arkansas, setting the stage for a crucial 2024 season against SEC competition.
strengths: Ideal size and length for a tackle, with a dense, well-distributed frame that provides a strong foundation for both pass and run blocking. Displays elite explosiveness off the snap, allowing him to quickly engage defenders and establish positioning in both pass protection and run blocking scenarios. Demonstrates impressive foot speed and agility for his size, enabling effective recovery against speed rushers and efficient movement in space. Possesses outstanding power at the point of attack, consistently driving defenders off the line of scrimmage and creating lanes in the run game. Shows good hand placement and punch timing, effectively neutralizing pass rushers' initial moves and maintaining control throughout engagements. Exhibits excellent spatial awareness and blocking range, effectively picking up stunts and blitzes while also excelling as a puller in the run game. Demonstrates a nasty finishing mentality, playing through the whistle and consistently looking to pancake defenders when the opportunity arises. Versatile skill set allows him to excel in both zone and power run schemes.
weaknesses: Occasionally plays with high pad level, particularly in pass protection, which can compromise his leverage and make him susceptible to bull rushes. Needs to improve consistency in his kick-slide technique, as he sometimes fails to gain sufficient depth, leaving him vulnerable to speed rushes off the edge. Can be overly aggressive at times, lunging at defenders and compromising his balance, which savvy pass rushers may exploit at the next level. While athletic, lacks elite flexibility in his lower half, which can hinder his ability to mirror quicker edge rushers through their full arc. Despite his impressive physical tools, is still refining his technique after transitioning from tight end, and may face a learning curve against elite SEC competition.
player_comparisons: CadeMays - Tennessee (98%), JamesHudson - Cincinnati (94%), CaedanWallace - Penn State (91%)

bio: Sixteen interceptions across his prep career at Hamilton High School in Chandler, Arizona, made Genesis Smith one of the more productive defensive backs in the state regardless of recruiting ranking. He averaged over four tackles per game and earned a three-star rating from 247Sports and Rivals, with ESPN bumping him to a four-star. He held offers from Washington, Oregon State, Iowa State, and Colorado State, among others, before staying home to play for Arizona. Smith appeared in all 13 games as a true freshman in 2023, contributing 24 tackles and picking off his first career pass against Oklahoma in the Alamo Bowl. That game capped a 10-win season for the Wildcats. His sophomore year brought a much larger role: 63 tackles, three interceptions, two forced fumbles, and a Big 12 Co-Defensive Player of the Week nod after a strong performance against Houston. As a junior in 2025, Smith posted 77 tackles, nine pass breakups, one interception, and two forced fumbles. He earned Third Team All-Big 12 recognition and was named a semifinalist for the Wuerffel Trophy, which honors community service alongside on-field performance.
strengths: Outstanding zone coverage instincts with the patience to read route development before committing. Uses his length to contest catch points without drawing flags. Long strides and smooth transitions let him close on throws in a hurry. Comfortable sitting in a deep half or single-high and quarterbacking the back end. Fluid backpedal with a level base that lets him break on the ball in any direction. Ball production is real: five career picks, 10 pass breakups, four forced fumbles. Versatile alignment history, logging snaps at free safety, in the box, and in the slot.
weaknesses: Tackling technique is genuinely poor, with a career missed tackle rate near 20 percent. Takes questionable pursuit angles that let ball carriers reach the second level. Run defense effort fluctuates and his impact against the run is minimal. Recovery speed after biting on fakes or overcommitting can be a problem. Stop-start quickness is limited for a player who may see slot reps at the next level. Light frame at 202 pounds, and the tackling issues make the weight a bigger concern.
player_comparisons: CalenBullock - USC (79%), JevonHolland - Oregon (78%), JadenHicks - Washington State (77%)

bio: Few college defenders in this class have taken a more unconventional route to the edge. Gumbs enrolled at Northern Illinois as a walk-on wide receiver, caught four passes as a tight end in 2022, then moved to defensive end the following year and immediately posted 3.5 sacks and 6.5 tackles for loss in seven starts. That kind of raw athletic translation earned him a scholarship, a spot in the transfer portal, and ultimately a landing place at Florida after flipping from Cincinnati. In two seasons with the Gators, Gumbs started 12 of 22 games and compiled 7.5 sacks, 14.5 tackles for loss, and 37 quarterback pressures across 311 pass-rushing snaps. His 2025 campaign was his best work on tape, particularly a five-tackle, forced-fumble performance against third-ranked LSU and a nine-tackle, one-sack outing versus fifth-ranked Georgia. A meniscus injury in his right knee cut the season short by two weeks and required surgery. Gumbs earned an invitation to the East-West Shrine Bowl and participated in the NFL Combine, where his vertical jump tied for sixth-best among edge rushers tested since 1999. He earned his bachelor's degree in Education Sciences at Florida in the fall of 2025.
strengths: Outstanding vertical explosiveness shows up in his get-off and closing burst. Uses a cross-chop and leaping entry to get past tackles' set points consistently. Converts speed to power well on his Euro-step counter back inside. Bends naturally at the top of his rush arc without losing speed. Willing run defender who works to spill blocks and hold contain. Fluid mover in space who can drop into short zones without looking uncomfortable. Competitive edge and work ethic have fueled three position changes in five years.
weaknesses: Leads with his helmet too often rather than getting hands on blockers first. Lacks a consistent bull rush to complement his speed-based moves. Offensive linemen who get into his chest can walk him off his spot. Edge-setting technique against the run needs fundamental refinement. Owns a deep catalog of rush moves but sequences them without a real plan. Overruns plays in pursuit and gives up leverage on cutback runs.
player_comparisons: JeffreyGunter - Coastal Carolina (92%), IkennaEnechukwu - Rice (91%), EthanDowns - Oklahoma (90%)

bio: Giovanni El-Hadi from Adlai Stevenson High School was rated a 4-star recruit by ESPN and handed a 4-star grade by 247 Sports. Following high school, El-Hadi opted to join Michigan. In 2021 as a freshman El-Hadi saw action in just one game and played a total of 11 snaps for the Wolverines. He allowed no QB hurries, no QB hits, and no sacks while playing sparingly. As a sophomore in 2022 he played in 10 games and a total of 366 snaps. He conceded just 2 QB hurries, 3 QB hits, and no sacks while playing at left guard. In 2023 as a junior El-Hadi saw action in 9 games and totaled 103 snaps. He gave up just one QB hurry, no QB hits, and one sack while playing at left guard.
strengths: Has good footwork and technique. Balanced and agile pass protection set with excellent hand placement. Quick feet to get inside of defensive tackles to wall off inside and reach linebackers at the second level. He’s a coordinated athlete who can win at the point of attack in the run game, and El-Hadi can also move to consistently land blocks at the second level. Shows good quickness and flexibility on reach blocks, and he has enough range to get out as a puller. Shows excellent awareness against twists and stunts, and he has the strong, flexible core and wide base to catch and handle moving defenders. In pass protection, he flashes a strong initial punch and typically has good hand placement.
weaknesses: Tends to slip off blocks when going against bigger linemen, and El-Hadi too often finishes plays on the ground. Had issues against quality counter moves, struggling to readjust. That’s something he’ll see frequently against NFL technicians in the pass rush. Could be a liability against speed-to-power pass rushers early in his career.

bio: Hurricane Katrina relocated Harold Perkins Jr.'s family from New Orleans to the Houston area, and he eventually landed at Cypress Park High School in Cypress, Texas, where he became a consensus five-star recruit. ESPN ranked him No. 4 overall nationally in the 2022 class, 247Sports had him at No. 8, and he participated in the Under Armour All-America Game. Perkins was one of the best true freshmen in recent SEC history at LSU in 2022, recording 72 tackles, 13 tackles for loss, and 7.5 sacks across 14 games. The tackle-for-loss total led all Power 5 freshmen. He earned First-Team All-SEC from the AP, Freshman All-America honors, and back-to-back SEC Defensive Player of the Week awards against Alabama and Arkansas. His sophomore year brought a position shift to off-ball linebacker, where he still managed 75 tackles, 13 tackles for loss, and 5.5 sacks en route to Second-Team All-SEC. A torn ACL against UCLA in 2024 limited him to four games as a junior. Perkins returned for 2025 and posted 56 tackles, 8 tackles for loss, 4.5 sacks, and 3 interceptions across 12 games. He finished his LSU career with 35.5 tackles for loss and 17 sacks in 43 games.
strengths: Elite straight-line speed for a linebacker; closes ground in pursuit faster than most at the position. Natural feel for timing blitzes and working through stunts with sharp stop-start footwork. Generates real power on contact when he commits to driving through ball carriers downhill. Good shoulder strength as a pass rusher; can rip through initial contact to reach the pocket. Works the sideline well, using leverage and body control to finish plays near the boundary. Showed discipline in zone coverage passing off routes and sitting in landmarks in 2023. Ball production is notable: 5 career interceptions, 8 forced fumbles, 17 sacks across four years.
weaknesses: Significantly undersized at his listed weight; gets washed out by bigger blockers too often. Tackling reliability has trended the wrong direction since his freshman year, with too many whiffs. Coverage tape is inconsistent; gets caught flat-footed on option routes and loses backs out of the backfield. Spends too much time on his heels, which robs him of the explosive first step his athleticism should provide. Lacks anchor against downhill blocks and struggles to re-establish his base once knocked off balance. Awareness lapses show up in traffic post-catch, drifting from assignments and opening lanes. Post-ACL athleticism has not fully matched his 2022 tape; closing burst appears diminished on film. v

name: Harold PerkinsJr
scouting_report:
Background:
Hurricane Katrina relocated Harold Perkins Jr.'s family from New Orleans to the Houston area, and he eventually landed at Cypress Park High School in Cypress, Texas, where he became a consensus five-star recruit. ESPN ranked him No. 4 overall nationally in the 2022 class, 247Sports had him at No. 8, and he participated in the Under Armour All-America Game. Perkins was one of the best true freshmen in recent SEC history at LSU in 2022, recording 72 tackles, 13 tackles for loss, and 7.5 sacks across 14 games. The tackle-for-loss total led all Power 5 freshmen. He earned First-Team All-SEC from the AP, Freshman All-America honors, and back-to-back SEC Defensive Player of the Week awards against Alabama and Arkansas. His sophomore year brought a position shift to off-ball linebacker, where he still managed 75 tackles, 13 tackles for loss, and 5.5 sacks en route to Second-Team All-SEC. A torn ACL against UCLA in 2024 limited him to four games as a junior. Perkins returned for 2025 and posted 56 tackles, 8 tackles for loss, 4.5 sacks, and 3 interceptions across 12 games. He finished his LSU career with 35.5 tackles for loss and 17 sacks in 43 games.

bio: Harrison Wallace III was a two-sport standout at Pike Road High School in Montgomery, Alabama, where he caught 27 passes for 696 yards and seven touchdowns as a senior and led his team to a 10-1 record. He earned First-Team All-Metro and All-State honors in Class 3A and drew a mixed bag of recruiting attention: a four-star rating from ESPN and Rivals, three stars from 247Sports. Penn State signed him in 2021, and he redshirted his first year before working into the rotation as a redshirt freshman in 2022 with 19 catches for 273 yards and a touchdown across 13 games. His role expanded significantly in 2024, when he started all 15 games and finished with 46 receptions for 723 yards and four scores during the Nittany Lions' run to the College Football Playoff semifinal. Wallace transferred to Ole Miss in January 2025 and posted career highs in Lane Kiffin's up-tempo offense: 61 catches for 934 yards and four touchdowns. He was named to the Biletnikoff Award preseason watch list and earned a Senior Bowl Top-300 selection. Across five college seasons, Wallace totaled 145 receptions for 2,155 yards and 10 touchdowns in 52 games.
strengths: Tracks deep balls with natural instincts, adjusting his path to off-target throws without losing body control. Head fakes and subtle shoulder leans create just enough hesitation from corners to win off the line. Hands have cleaned up considerably on film; early-career body catches have given way to reliable grabs. Takes care of the football. You almost never see him put the ball on the ground after the catch. Shows no hesitation working into traffic, climbs the ladder well, and competes hard on 50/50 balls. Operated in a brand-new offense at Ole Miss after a coaching shakeup and never looked lost or tentative. Finds soft spots in zone coverage quickly and works back toward his quarterback's throwing lane.
weaknesses: Thin frame gets bullied in press-man; bigger corners re-route him off his stem with ease. Route tree looks limited on tape, mostly vertical stems with rounded, drifty breaks underneath. Loses too many contested reps for a receiver who will be asked to win on the boundary. Speed is only average for the position, which undercuts his value as a pure vertical threat. Never lined up inside in college, leaving real questions about whether he can work from the slot. Disappears as a run blocker, rarely initiating contact and giving up on reps early. Struggles to create separation on short and intermediate routes without a clean, free release.

bio: After struggling to find his footing in College Station, Haynes King, the Longview, Texas native, discovered his calling in Atlanta, where he's carved out a legacy as one of the ACC's most dynamic dual-threat quarterbacks. King's 2023 breakout campaign put him in rarified air - joining LSU's Jayden Daniels as the only Power Five signal-caller to eclipse 2,800 passing yards, 25 touchdown passes, 700 rushing yards, and 10 rushing touchdowns. That production earned him honorable mention All-ACC honors and the unwavering respect of teammates who voted him permanent captain. King's athletic pedigree runs deeper than football, having competed in track and field during his high school days at Longview, where he ran the 400-meter dash, anchored the 4x400 relay, and threw discus. His Under Armour All-American credentials as a prep star translated into a four-star recruiting ranking across all major services, with his dual-sport background foreshadowing the versatility that would eventually make him a college star. The quarterback's journey through College Station included flashes of brilliance - notably a 364-yard passing performance against Sam Houston State in 2022 - but injuries and inconsistency prevented him from seizing the starting role long-term. His Georgia Tech tenure has been defined by clutch performances and record-breaking production across two seasons. King's game-winning touchdown pass to Christian Leary with one second remaining against No. 17 Miami in 2023 stands as one of the most memorable moments in recent Yellow Jackets history, while his four-touchdown passing display in a comeback victory over No. 17 North Carolina showcased his ability to rise to the occasion against ranked competition. The quarterback's 2023 season rewrote multiple school records, including most touchdowns responsible for in a single season (37) and second-most total offense (3,729 yards). His 2024 campaign showcased improved efficiency despite battling injuries, throwing for 2,114 yards with an impressive 14-2 touchdown-to-interception ratio while adding 11 rushing touchdowns. King's leadership was on full display during Georgia Tech's stunning 28-23 upset of undefeated No. 4 Miami, where he completed all six pass attempts and rushed for 93 yards in his return from a shoulder injury, helping the Yellow Jackets claim their first victory over a top-five opponent since 2009.
strengths: Electric runner with legitimate sub-4.5 speed who can turn broken plays into explosive gains and stress defensive coordinators with designed runs. Possesses a rocket launcher for an arm, consistently delivering frozen ropes on deep throws with impressive velocity and outstanding ball placement. Quick-twitch athlete who gets the football out of his hands in a hurry, showing rapid processing and trigger finger in rhythm passing situations. Throws absolute dimes on the move, displaying rare accuracy while rolling out or scrambling with defenders breathing down his neck. Natural-born leader who commands the huddle and locker room, earning captain status from teammates who would run through walls for him. Clutch gene runs through his veins, engineering multiple game-winning drives against ranked opponents when the lights shine brightest and pressure mounts. Extends plays with his escapability and pocket awareness, buying time for receivers to break open while keeping his eyes downfield like a seasoned veteran. Shows impressive arm talent from multiple release points and platforms, able to sidearm bullets through tight windows or throw off his back foot under pressure.
weaknesses: Lean build raises red flags about durability, as his 215-pound frame may struggle to absorb punishment from NFL pass rushers week after week. Accuracy becomes spotty on short and intermediate throws, missing too many gimme completions that should be automatic for NFL-caliber quarterbacks. Gunslinger mentality leads to questionable decisions, forcing throws into tight coverage when checkdowns or throwaways would be the smarter play. Still learning to read complex defensive schemes, occasionally getting fooled by disguised coverages and late rotations that NFL defenses excel at executing. Mechanics break down under pressure, leading to off-balance throws and errant passes when pocket collapses or defenders close in on him.
player_comparisons: MaxDuggan - TCU (84%), BradyCook - Missouri (79%), EJPerry - Brown (78%)

bio: Coming out of Deerfield Beach High School in South Florida, Hezekiah Masses didn't exactly have a trail of scholarship offers behind him. A two-star recruit ranked No. 2,198 in his class by 247Sports, he landed at FIU as an afterthought on most recruiting boards. He wasn't an afterthought for long. Masses cracked the rotation as a true freshman in 2022, earning six starts and collecting 25 tackles, 3.5 tackles for loss, and four pass breakups across 12 appearances. College Football News named him a Freshman All-American, and Conference USA put him on the All-Freshman Team. For a kid nobody recruited, the early returns were hard to ignore. He settled into a full-time starting role in 2023, starting 11 games and posting 42 tackles with an interception he took back 40 yards for a touchdown against North Texas. The production was steady if unspectacular, and by 2024 he was drawing preseason All-Conference USA recognition from both Phil Steele and Athlon. But the real inflection point came when Masses made the decision to transfer to Cal for his final season. Jumping from Conference USA to the ACC was a massive leap in competition, and there were fair questions about whether his game would translate. It translated. Masses led the entire nation in 2025 with 18 passes defended in 2025, piling up five interceptions and 13 pass breakups while starting all 13 games for the Golden Bears. He returned those five picks for 91 yards, added 47 tackles, and earned Second Team All-America honors from the Associated Press, Walter Camp, and the AFCA. The ACC named him First Team All-Conference and Defensive Back of the Week on November 8th.
strengths: Arm length gives him a legitimate reach advantage at the catch point, and he knows how to weaponize it to contest throws and knock balls loose from receivers' hands. In zone coverage, his eyes stay glued to the quarterback and he reads throwing windows before they open, allowing him to cheat toward routes and jump passing lanes with anticipation that belies his experience level. Comfortable living in a receiver's back pocket through the stem of a route, then decelerating and changing direction in sync with breaks on digs, curls, comebacks, and out routes. Ball production is legit and not inflated by garbage time. Five picks and 13 breakups against ACC competition, with a 48.4% completion rate allowed on 62 targets in 2025, tell a real story. Uses the boundary as an extra defender on outside releases, funneling receivers into the sideline and compressing their route trees with physicality and positioning. Quick trigger in zone lets him click and close on underneath routes with burst, and his willingness to drive downhill to contest throws in the flat is a genuine weapon in Cover 3 shells. Tracks vertical routes with surprising comfort for a corner whose timed speed is only average. Rarely looked panicked carrying fades or posts down the field, staying in phase and contesting at the top. Special teams background across kick coverage, punt return, punt coverage, and field goal block units gives him a built-in role on Sundays while he develops as a defensive contributor.
weaknesses: At 180 pounds, the frame is a genuine concern. He gets knocked around in the run game, struggles to stack and shed blocks, and tacklers at the next level will be bigger and meaner. Penalty flags follow him. Fourteen combined penalties over 2024 and 2025 point to a habit of grabbing and tugging at receivers when he loses positioning, especially at the top of routes where he panics instead of playing the ball. Run defense is a clear weakness. He takes poor angles to the ball carrier, shows limited enthusiasm as a run defender, and lacks the play strength to hold his ground against stalk blocks from physical receivers or tight ends. Press technique is inconsistent. His punch lacks pop and his timing is readable, making it too easy for savvy receivers to swipe his hands and win off the line. He also opens his hips too early against inside-out releases, giving up free access to the field. Missed tackle rate of 15.3% in 2025 is a red flag, and the tape confirms it. He drops his head, lunges at ankles, and doesn't wrap up consistently, which will get exposed against NFL skill players in space.

bio: Isaac Smith grew up watching his father Reggie suit up for Ole Miss in the 1990s, but the younger Smith would forge his own legacy across state lines. At Itawamba Agricultural High School, Smith developed into one of Mississippi's most decorated prep players under coach Clint Hoots, earning the state's Mr. Football award in 2022 after rushing for 823 yards, catching for 652 more, and scoring eighteen total touchdowns while adding 65 tackles and nine interceptions on defense. His dominance continued from his junior season when he compiled an absurd stat line of 1,196 rushing yards with 23 touchdowns, 92 tackles, eight picks, and three defensive scores that earned him Northeast Mississippi Daily Journal's Two-Way Player of the Year honors. The 6-foot, 205-pound safety arrived in Starkville as a consensus four-star recruit, ranked as high as the ninth-best safety nationally by ESPN and earning Under Armour All-American recognition. Smith made an immediate impact as a true freshman in 2023, appearing in all twelve games and recording fifteen tackles while learning the complexities of SEC football. His versatility showed early as he took snaps at safety, linebacker, and even edge defender, setting the stage for what would become a historic sophomore campaign. That 2024 season saw Smith lead the entire SEC with 127 tackles despite missing one game, including a remarkable twenty-tackle effort against seventh-ranked Tennessee where he recorded ten stops in the opening quarter alone - the only Power Four player to reach that single-game mark all season. Through two college seasons, Smith has logged 142 career tackles, 1.5 tackles for loss, three pass breakups, and one forced fumble across 793 defensive snaps. His sophomore breakthrough earned him Second Team All-SEC recognition from conference coaches, making him the third straight Mississippi State defender to lead the conference in tackles following Nathaniel Watson and Jett Johnson.
strengths: Violent striker who brings legitimate bad intentions when he fills downhill - defenders bounce off him regularly Reads offensive flow like he's got the playbook memorized, rarely gets caught peeking in the wrong gap Possesses linebacker mentality trapped in a safety's body - absolutely lives for contact between the tackles Versatility to align at five different spots without looking lost - true defensive Swiss Army knife Tackles with the reliability of a heat-seeking missile once he locks onto a ball carrier Shows genuine love for the violent aspects of football that you simply can't teach or fake Sheds blocks from tight ends and pulling guards with surprising effectiveness for his size Natural feel for shallow zone coverage where he can keep everything in front of him
weaknesses: Deep coverage skills remain largely theoretical - looks uncomfortable when asked to play centerfield safety Lacks the elite recovery speed to erase mistakes when beaten vertically by faster receivers Frame maxes out around 205 pounds which limits his ceiling as a true box defender Hip fluidity in man coverage needs serious refinement against NFL-caliber route runners Sometimes his aggression gets weaponized against him on play-action and misdirection concepts

bio: Isaiah Jatta the 6-foot-6, 315-pound San Diego native first made his mark at Helix High School in La Mesa, California, where he anchored an offensive line that bulldozed opponents for 272 rushing yards per game during his senior campaign, with the ground attack averaging a remarkable 7.9 yards per carry while punching in 45 rushing touchdowns. After redshirting in 2020, Jatta traded the Southern California sunshine for the small-town atmosphere of Ephraim, Utah, spending two seasons at Snow College under former BYU receiver Zac Erekson. The culture shock from San Diego to a town with just a Walmart, McDonald's, and gas station didn't slow Jatta's development - he thrived in the junior college ranks, earning NJCAA First Team All-American honors in 2022 while helping Snow College compile a 15-4 record over his two seasons. His dominance at the JUCO level made him the 17th-ranked junior college prospect nationally in the 2023 class, drawing attention from Power Five programs including Deion Sanders' Colorado rebuild. bio: Isaiah Nwokobia was a three-star safety prospect out of Skyline High School in Dallas, where he helped his team reach the second round of the 6A Division 1 UIL State playoffs. He held offers from Texas, Oregon, Oklahoma State, and a dozen other Power Five programs but committed to SMU and stayed home. After a rough true freshman season in 2021 (22 tackles, two interceptions in nine games) and a redshirt year cut short by injury in 2022, Nwokobia put things together as a redshirt sophomore in 2023. He won AAC Championship MVP honors, the first defensive player to receive the award, and led the conference with two defensive touchdowns while adding four interceptions. His 2024 junior campaign was his best: 100-plus tackles, three interceptions, and All-ACC First Team recognition as SMU made its first College Football Playoff appearance. As a redshirt senior in 2025, Nwokobia finished second on the team in tackles (80) and pass breakups (seven) while adding two more interceptions. He was named to the Jim Thorpe, Nagurski, and Bednarik Award watch lists and was selected to play in the American Bowl. He was not invited to the NFL Combine.
strengths: Eyes stay locked on the quarterback in zone, reads the throwing motion early, and drives on the ball. Triggers downhill fast against the run and takes on blockers without waiting for traffic to clear. Catches the ball cleanly away from his body, not just deflections that fall into his lap. SMU moved him around pre-snap constantly and he handled box, deep half, and slot without confusion. Closes ground in a hurry when he sees the ball out, particularly in zone windows underneath. Wraps up and finishes tackles in the run game, rarely bounces off at the point of contact. Vocal and organized in the secondary, gets teammates lined up before the snap consistently on film.
weaknesses: Got beat deep too often in 2025, and the tape showed more hesitation in his backpedal than the year before. Misses more tackles than you want to see, particularly when he has to change direction in space. Hips are stiff when he has to flip and run, which shows up on crossing routes and double moves. Slot receivers and athletic tight ends create easy separation on him in man assignments. Does not have the range to play true center field, loses ground sideline to sideline in two-deep looks. Offers almost nothing as a blitzer, rarely wins when sent off the edge or through a gap.

bio: J'Mari Taylor, the 204-pound back didn't crack 1,000 yards rushing until his redshirt senior season at FCS North Carolina Central in 2024, posting 1,146 yards and 15 touchdowns while becoming the Eagles' first four-digit rusher in nearly two decades. Taylor built his game in relative obscurity at NCCU, steadily climbing from special teams contributor as a redshirt freshman in 2021 to third-team All-MEAC honors in 2022 before his breakout 2024 campaign, where he graded out as PFF's 20th-ranked rusher in FCS. That performance caught Virginia's attention during the transfer portal cycle, and Tony Elliott brought him to Charlottesville to inject some physicality into the Cavaliers' backfield. The transition from FCS to Power Four competition didn't faze Taylor one bit. He took over as Virginia's feature back in 2025 and proceeded to lead the ACC in rushing yards (1,062), rushing attempts (187), and yards after contact (653) while forcing 48 missed tackles and scoring 14 touchdowns on the ground. His 150-yard, three-touchdown performance at NC State announced his arrival on the big stage, and he followed that up with multiple 100-yard efforts, including a 133-yard explosion against Duke featuring a 78-yard touchdown run that tied for 11th-longest in program history. Taylor earned first-team All-ACC honors and became UVA's first 1,000-yard rusher since Jordan Ellis in 2018, doing it all while battling through various bumps and bruises that would have sidelined lesser competitors. His senior season at Virginia showcased both his production and his versatility, as Taylor contributed 253 receiving yards and added a passing touchdown on a trick play against Virginia Tech. He earned Senior Bowl and Burlsworth Trophy recognition while demonstrating the consistency coaches crave, never missing a game despite playing through injury. Taylor's career numbers tell the story of steady development: 2,944 rushing yards, 37 rushing touchdowns, 97 receptions for 868 receiving yards, and 10 receiving scores across five collegiate seasons. That workload, combined with his late-bloomer profile and smaller stature, creates an interesting evaluation puzzle for NFL decision-makers looking for value in the middle rounds.
strengths: Contact balance stands out immediately on tape as Taylor absorbs hits from linebackers and safeties without going down, consistently churning out an extra 3-5 yards after defenders think they have him wrapped up. Vision between the tackles allows him to find cutback lanes in zone schemes with excellent patience, letting blocks develop before hitting the crease with conviction and decisive footwork. Footwork in tight spaces creates separation through jump cuts and lateral agility that keeps defenders grasping at air, as evidenced by his 54 forced missed tackles in 2025. Runs with legitimate nastiness and finishes runs by lowering his shoulder into contact rather than sliding out of bounds, displaying the kind of toughness that earns respect in NFL locker rooms. Pass protection willingness stands out for a smaller back, as he squares up blitzers and delivers pop on contact despite giving up 40-50 pounds to most edge rushers. Receiving skills translate beyond checkdowns with soft hands and enough route diversity to motion into the slot, catching 97 balls in his college career while averaging over 8 yards per reception. Durability proves itself through his availability despite heavy workload, playing through minor ailments while handling 20-plus carries in multiple games without breaking down over the season. Burst through the hole at the second level generates explosive runs when he gets to the perimeter, showing 4.39 speed (93rd percentile) that can stress defenses horizontally and create home-run potential.
weaknesses: Size limitations at 5'9" and 204 pounds raise durability questions against NFL linebackers and safeties who will bring significantly more force than ACC defenders brought to the tackle point. Pass protection technique needs refinement as he sometimes lunges at rushers rather than staying square, and while he competes hard his 51% pass-blocking grade reflects inconsistent fundamentals against speed. Long speed doesn't match his initial burst, as he gets run down from behind on breakaway attempts more often than his 40-time suggests he should based on game tape analysis. Limited sample size against elite competition raises questions about how his game translates, considering he spent four years at FCS level before one productive season in a Power Four conference. Route running lacks polish beyond basic option routes and swings, limiting his ability to create mismatches in the passing game against linebackers who can match his athleticism at the next level.
player_comparisons: BlakeWatson - Memphis (82%), TrevorEtienne - Georgia (82%), JeromeFord - Cincinnati (81%)

bio: Ja'Kobi Lane was a two-sport athlete at Red Mountain High School in Mesa, Arizona, where he totaled 141 catches for 1,813 yards and 25 touchdowns across his junior and senior seasons. He took home the NFF Arizona Chapter J.D. Hill Wide Receiver of the Year and the Elite 11 All-Tourney Wide Receiver MVP, among other honors, and helped Red Mountain reach the 6A semifinals. A four-star recruit by 247Sports, Lane signed with USC's 2023 class. Lane's freshman year was limited to six games and seven catches, but both touchdowns came in the Holiday Bowl against Louisville. He broke out in 2024 with 43 catches for 525 yards and 12 touchdowns across 13 games, finishing fifth nationally in receiving scores. The Las Vegas Bowl was his signature game: seven catches, 127 yards, a bowl-record three touchdowns, and MVP honors. A shoulder injury cost him one game in 2025, but he finished with 49 receptions for 750 yards and four touchdowns. His career totals at USC: 99 catches, 1,368 yards, 18 touchdowns. Lane trained with T.J. Houshmandzadeh before the draft process, stood out at the Senior Bowl, then turned heads at the combine with a 40-inch vertical, a 10-foot-9 broad jump, and a diving sideline catch during drills that got the crowd going.
strengths: Wins consistently at the catch point with strong body control and reliable ball tracking overhead. Long frame and catch radius turn contested targets into completions, especially on fades and back-shoulders. Red zone scoring was a calling card at USC, with 16 touchdowns over his last two seasons. Adjusts well to off-target throws downfield, keeping plays alive when the ball isn't perfectly placed. Uses head fakes and tempo changes at the top of routes to freeze corners on double moves. Press release has more variety than expected for a size-based receiver, including a solid swipe technique. Explosive lower half, confirmed by combine testing, translates to his ability to high-point the football. Competes through contact after the catch and doesn't go down easy on first contact. Finds soft spots in zone coverage quickly and understands how to sit in windows for the quarterback.
weaknesses: Needs to add functional weight to hold up against physical NFL press corners at the line. Route tree was narrow at USC, leaning heavily on fades, hitches, and clearing concepts. Contested catch rate is strong, but his overall drop numbers remain a concern across both seasons. Speed is adequate but not a separator; he will not consistently stack corners on vertical routes. Run blocking effort and technique are both below standard for a starting outside receiver. Production dipped in 2025, with touchdowns falling from 12 to four despite more targets.

bio: Weber State gave Jack Kelly his start after a modest recruiting profile out of Kearns High School in Utah. He recorded 6.0 sacks as a redshirt freshman and then led the Big Sky with 10.5 sacks and four forced fumbles in 2023, posting 56 total tackles and 12.0 tackles for loss. That production earned him a four-star transfer rating from 247Sports and a move to BYU. Kelly fit right away in Jay Hill's defense, collecting 5.0 sacks and 10.0 tackles for loss during his first Big 12 season in 2024. The College Football Network named him Defensive Newcomer of the Year. His 2025 campaign raised the bar: 10 sacks in 12 games, 13.5 tackles for loss, two forced fumbles, and a touchdown off a blocked field goal. That sack total was BYU's highest since Bronson Kaufusi posted 11 in 2015, and Kelly set the program's post-2000 record with 14 quarterback hurries. A two-time captain at BYU, Kelly earned midseason All-American honors in 2025 and finished with 49 career games. He was named linebacker practice player of the week at the Senior Bowl, and his combine produced a 9.83 RAS score, ranking 57th among linebackers tested over the last 40 years.
strengths: Wins with a sharp inside dip off the edge and keeps his feet moving through contact. Reads the snap count well on designed blitzes and times his A-gap entry cleanly. Jolts climbing guards with his initial strike and knocks them off their landmark. BYU used him as a QB spy often and he closed on scramblers in a hurry. Combine verified the burst you see on his rush tape, 37-inch vertical and 10'5" broad. Plays with some dog in him, finishes through the whistle and competes every snap. Comfortable standing up or with his hand in the dirt, lined up everywhere for BYU.
weaknesses: Lunges and whiffs in space too often, especially when breaking down on backs in the open field. Gets walled off by linemen who sink their hands into his chest because of his short arms. Eyes drift to the backfield against zone runs and he loses his gap assignment laterally. Gave up too many easy catches underneath in zone drops, late to feel routes behind him. Turns his shoulders and overruns the play against outside zone, saw it repeatedly on the Texas Tech tape. Same run-fit mistakes show up on his 2025 tape that were there in 2024, no real growth.

bio: Jack Velling arrived at Oregon State in 2022 as one of the nation's most coveted tight end recruits, earning recognition from major recruiting services as a top-30 prospect at his position and a top-15 player in Washington state. His father Jerry's basketball background at Santa Clara and grandfather Roy's baseball legacy at Gonzaga and Washington established athletic bloodlines that would manifest in Velling's own versatility on the gridiron. Velling's freshman campaign with the Beavers showcased immediate impact, hauling in 16 passes for 281 yards and three touchdowns across 11 games. His 60-yard touchdown reception against Colorado marked the longest score by an Oregon State tight end in over two decades, earning him College Football News Freshman All-American honors. The following season delivered his breakout performance - 29 catches for 438 yards and a program-record eight touchdowns for a tight end, garnering second-team All-Pac-12 recognition and establishing himself as one of the conference's premier receiving threats at the position. When coach Jonathan Smith departed Corvallis for Michigan State, Velling followed his mentor to East Lansing, bringing 35 career collegiate games of experience and 81 receptions for 1,130 yards. His 2024 Spartan debut season produced 36 catches for 411 yards across 12 games, though the statistical output didn't match the explosive scoring pace that defined his Oregon State tenure.
strengths: Exceptional seam-stretching ability with smooth acceleration that consistently creates separation against zone coverage in the vertical passing game Refined route-running technique featuring nuanced releases and tempo variations that manipulate defensive back leverage across all three levels Reliable hands with natural catching radius, demonstrating consistent ball skills when working outside his frame on difficult throws Impressive run-after-catch instincts with long striding ability that maximizes yardage after securing possession in open field situations Formation flexibility allows deployment from traditional Y-receiver, H-back, and detached slot alignments without compromising route precision or timing Proven red zone threat with eight touchdown receptions in 2023, showcasing ability to win contested catches in compressed field situations Special teams coverage experience adds immediate roster value with demonstrated competence on field goal protection and coverage units Advanced route concepts understanding enables effective execution of option routes and hot reads within complex passing schemes

name: Jack Velling
scouting_report:
Background:
Jack Velling arrived at Oregon State in 2022 as one of the nation's most coveted tight end recruits, earning recognition from major recruiting services as a top-30 prospect at his position and a top-15 player in Washington state. His father Jerry's basketball background at Santa Clara and grandfather Roy's baseball legacy at Gonzaga and Washington established athletic bloodlines that would manifest in Velling's own versatility on the gridiron. Velling's freshman campaign with the Beavers showcased immediate impact, hauling in 16 passes for 281 yards and three touchdowns across 11 games. His 60-yard touchdown reception against Colorado marked the longest score by an Oregon State tight end in over two decades, earning him College Football News Freshman All-American honors. The following season delivered his breakout performance - 29 catches for 438 yards and a program-record eight touchdowns for a tight end, garnering second-team All-Pac-12 recognition and establishing himself as one of the conference's premier receiving threats at the position. When coach Jonathan Smith departed Corvallis for Michigan State, Velling followed his mentor to East Lansing, bringing 35 career collegiate games of experience and 81 receptions for 1,130 yards. His 2024 Spartan debut season produced 36 catches for 411 yards across 12 games, though the statistical output didn't match the explosive scoring pace that defined his Oregon State tenure.

Exceptional seam-stretching ability with smooth acceleration that consistently creates separation against zone coverage in the vertical passing game Refined route-running technique featuring nuanced releases and tempo variations that manipulate defensive back leverage across all three levels Reliable hands with natural catching radius, demonstrating consistent ball skills when working outside his frame on difficult throws Impressive run-after-catch instincts with long striding ability that maximizes yardage after securing possession in open field situations Formation flexibility allows deployment from traditional Y-receiver, H-back, and detached slot alignments without compromising route precision or timing Proven red zone threat with eight touchdown receptions in 2023, showcasing ability to win contested catches in compressed field situations Special teams coverage experience adds immediate roster value with demonstrated competence on field goal protection and coverage units Advanced route concepts understanding enables effective execution of option routes and hot reads within complex passing schemes
