bio: Jadon Canady was a three-star recruit who earned all-state track honors and picked off nine passes as a high school senior, Canady signed with Tulane and made an immediate splash as a true freshman in 2021, racking up 55 tackles and two interceptions while appearing in all 12 games. That included a pick in his very first collegiate snap count against Oklahoma, which set the tone for what would become a five-year career across three different programs. After a sophomore season at Tulane where he started eight games and recovered two fumbles (returning one for a touchdown against Houston), Canady transferred to Ole Miss ahead of the 2023 campaign. A lower-body injury limited him to just two appearances that year, but he bounced back in 2024 to start 11 of 12 games for the Rebels, tallying 38 tackles and a career-best 12 pass breakups. His performance against LSU stood out: four passes defensed in a hostile road environment against a top-10 opponent. Still, something about Oxford did not quite fit, and Canady entered the portal again that December. His final stop was Oregon for the 2025 season, and it turned out to be the perfect landing spot. Playing the STAR nickel role in Tosh Lupoi's defense, Canady posted 39 tackles, two interceptions (in consecutive games against USC and Washington), six passes defensed, and a forced fumble in the College Football Playoff semifinal against Indiana. PFF graded him as the tenth-best cornerback in the country that year. He capped his college career with a strong showing at the East-West Shrine Bowl, winning the majority of his one-on-one reps against top receivers. Across 50 career games and 2,451 defensive snaps, Canady leaves college with 130 tackles, four interceptions, and 17 pass breakups.
strengths: Outstanding feel for route breaks from off-man and zone looks, frequently sliding his feet and changing direction before the receiver even declares his stem. This anticipation separates him from most corners in this class. Closing burst is twitchy and sudden rather than long-striding, which allows him to eat up cushion in a hurry and arrive at the catch point right as the ball does. Receivers rarely get clean separation windows against him. Transitions out of his pedal and shuffle are butter-smooth, with little wasted motion when flipping hips or redirecting. He looked especially comfortable working out of a backpedal in Oregon's zone concepts. Coverage versatility across the secondary is legitimate and battle-tested. He has meaningful reps at boundary corner, slot, and safety across three different programs with three different defensive schemes, and he looked comfortable in all of them. Contact timing at the top of routes disrupts rhythm without drawing flags. He knows exactly when and where to get physical, jamming receivers just enough to stay in phase without getting handsy. Only two accepted penalties across 603 snaps at Oregon tells you he is disciplined. Ball tracking downfield is natural and instinctive. He uses whatever length he has to swipe or chop at the football through the catch point rather than playing the body, which explains why his pass breakup numbers have been consistently strong. Competitive energy is palpable on film. Dan Lanning called him the most competitive player on the practice field, and you see it translate to games in the way he chases plays, hustles to contain on spill action, and competes through the whistle on every single rep. Triggers downhill with conviction in off-coverage, reading the quarterback's eyes and driving on throws with real confidence. His two picks against USC and Washington in consecutive weeks were both products of aggressive, decisive breaks on the ball.
weaknesses: Tackling inconsistency has followed him throughout his career. He aims too high or too low on contact and whiffs at a rate that will concern NFL defensive coordinators, especially when asked to be a force defender in the run game. Slender frame with short arms limits his catch radius on potential interceptions and lets bigger receivers push him around at the top of routes. Physical wideouts will bully him at the break point when they can get their hands on him. Aggressiveness is a double-edged sword in man coverage. He bites hard on fakes and manipulation moves, which leaves him vulnerable to double moves and layered release packages from savvy route runners at the next level. Run defense is a real concern. He struggles to disengage from blocks, particularly from offensive linemen on the perimeter, and too often resorts to diving at ankles rather than setting a firm edge. Teams running 11-personnel heavy will target his side. Speed profile is decent but not elite, and that shows up when he gets turned around by vertical stems or needs to recover after biting on an underneath fake. There is not a second gear to bail him out when he guesses wrong.
player_comparisons: AsanteSamuel Jr. - Florida State (97%), ChristianBraswell - Rutgers (96%), JabbarMuhammad - Oregon (87%)
name: Jadon Canady DB Tulane

bio: Jaeden Roberts came out of North Shore High School in Houston, Texas, as a four-star ESPN recruit who helped lead his team to back-to-back Texas 6A Division I state championships. He was rated among the top offensive guard prospects nationally, earning selection to the Under Armour All-America Game before signing with Alabama over Auburn in the 2021 class. Roberts redshirted his first year and saw only 18 snaps across three games in 2022, including a brief appearance in the Sugar Bowl against Kansas State. His breakout came in 2023 when he strung together eight consecutive starts at right guard, earning offensive player of the week honors for his performance against Tennessee and sharing recognition with the entire offensive line after Alabama's 303-yard rushing day against LSU. He posted a career-high six knockdown blocks against Georgia's top-ranked defense that season. In 2024, Roberts locked down the starting right guard spot for 12 of 13 games and landed on both the Lombardi and Outland Trophy watch lists. He earned Outland Player of the Week for his outing at Wisconsin. His 2025 season was disrupted by injury, limiting him to four starts in 10 appearances. He was invited to the Shrine Bowl following the season.
strengths: Anchor is sturdy in pass protection; absorbs bull rushes and rarely gets walked back into the pocket. Pass sets showed real improvement in 2025, with better patience and posture in his kick-slide. Wingspan allows him to smother basic rush moves and keep defenders from turning the corner. Hands jar rushers on arrival and can reset the whole engagement when he locks on cleanly. Feldman freaks-list athletic profile: 805-pound squat, 525 bench, 415 power clean at 327 pounds. Initial punch carries real force; you see defenders jolt backward on first contact consistently. Wash-down blocks on angle and combo assignments are where he looks best in the run game. Finishes through the whistle and looks to put people on the ground after winning the rep.
weaknesses: Run game tape got worse from 2023 onward; not the progression you want to see from a senior. Second-level work is poor; takes bad angles to linebackers and arrives late to combo targets. Footwork is sloppy fitting up run blocks; misses his aiming points too often on tape. Plays stiff through the hips and lacks the bend to stay balanced through contact and in space. Ends up on the ground more than any starting guard at this level should. Quick-twitch interior rushers who counter off his initial set can catch him lunging and off-balance. Injuries over the last two years have visibly restricted his movement and lateral quickness.
player_comparisons: ChandlerZavala - NC State (80%), JacksonSlater - Sacramento State (77%), LukeKandra - Cincinnati (77%)

bio: Jager Burton from Frederick Douglass High School was rated a 4-star recruit by ESPN and handed a 4-star grade by 247 Sports. Following high school, Burton opted to join Kentucky. In 2021 as a freshman Burton saw action in just 3 games and played a total of 34 snaps for the Wildcats. He allowed no QB hurries, no QB hits, and one sack while playing at right guard. As a sophomore in 2022 he played in 12 games and a total of 729 snaps. He conceded 4 QB hurries, 4 QB hits, and 5 sacks while playing at left guard. In 2023 as a junior Burton saw action in 11 games and totaled 590 snaps. He gave up 9 QB hurries, 3 QB hits, and one sack while playing the majority of his snaps at right guard, and the rest at center.
strengths: Coordinated when moving to the second level as a run blocker, capable of landing blocks on the move. Adequate foot quickness to remain outside. Can get to the second level with surprising agility when adjusting to moving defenders. Underrated athleticism, agility, balance, and even straight-line speed to contribute. Shows a good feel for combo blocks, and Burton displays good quickness and flexibility when moving to the second level, capable of sealing the back side on reach blocks Dips shoulder and moves his feet to get outside leverage when needed. Strong and low coming out of his stance, effective in short-yardage situations. Showed impressive range and the ability to get out and land blocks on the move Burton does a nice job working with teammates to build the pocket, anchoring against bull rushers and displaying good awareness against stunts and twists.
weaknesses: Raw as a pass protector, looking choppy and unnatural when moving back into his set, often playing too upright. As a pass protector, Burton gets caught reaching and lunging when matched up against quickness. Burton doesn’t have ideal coordination between his upper and lower body and is often overeager to land an initial punch, he is prone to playing off-balance and struggling against counter moves.

bio: Barham grew up in District Heights, Maryland, and played his prep ball at DeMatha before finishing at St. Frances Academy in Baltimore. He was a consensus four-star recruit, ranked inside the top 150 nationally, and earned Defensive MVP honors at the 2022 Polynesian Bowl. He signed with Maryland and started immediately as a true freshman in 2022. At Maryland, Barham was a two-year starter at linebacker who produced from day one. As a freshman he led all Big Ten freshmen in tackles with 58, added 6.5 tackles for loss and four sacks, and earned Freshman All-American honors from College Football News. His sophomore year brought 37 tackles, three sacks, and his first career interception. He was named All-Big Ten honorable mention in both seasons before entering the transfer portal and heading to Ann Arbor. Barham started all 13 games at linebacker for Michigan in 2024, finishing second on the team with 66 tackles while adding 3.5 TFLs and 20 quarterback pressures. In 2025, the coaching staff moved him to edge for the majority of his snaps, and he responded with 32 tackles, 10.0 TFLs, 4.0 sacks, and 21 pressures across 12 games. He earned All-Big Ten honorable mention and was a two-time Academic All-Big Ten selection at Michigan.
strengths: Elite run defender who ranked second among Power Four edge rushers in run defense grading. Violent hands at the point of attack; stacks and sheds blocks with authority. Quick first three steps off the snap create immediate pressure as a rusher. Good bend and ankle flexibility to flatten around the arc against slower tackles. Versatility to align at inside linebacker, stand-up OLB, or hand-down edge. Processes screens and misdirection well; recognizes blocking schemes pre-snap. Tested with above-average speed for his size at the Combine. Plays with intensity and aggression on every rep; effort is never a question.
weaknesses: Coverage remains a clear liability, particularly on deep drops and in space. Missed tackle rate spiked in 2025 after a very clean 2024 season. Pass rush wins come mostly from athleticism; hand counters are underdeveloped. Aggression leads to penalties, including a head-butt on an official against Ohio State. Can lose contain by crashing inside too aggressively on the edge. Only a half-season of true edge work; technique at the position is still raw.

bio: Jake Golday arrived at Central Arkansas in 2021 without a single recruiting star, out of Arlington High School in Tennessee. Both parents and a brother played college basketball, but the football recruiting world overlooked him entirely. He redshirted his first year as a defensive end, then rotated into the lineup in 2022 with 37 tackles and 5.5 tackles for loss. His 2023 season changed the conversation: 84 tackles, 7.0 TFLs, 4.5 sacks, and Second Team All-UAC honors, capped by a 14-tackle game at Austin Peay in the finale. Golday transferred to Cincinnati and jumped from FCS to the Big 12 in 2024. He started eight games, finished second on the Bearcats with 58 tackles and seven tackles for loss, and turned heads with a six-tackle, 2.5-TFL, 1.5-sack performance at UCF that included a forced fumble and recovery. That earned Big 12 Newcomer of the Week and honorable mention All-Big 12 recognition. His 2025 campaign produced 105 tackles, 6.0 TFLs, and 3.5 sacks across eleven games, earning another All-Big 12 nod and a spot on Bruce Feldman's "Freaks" list, where his short shuttle of 4.25 seconds would have topped every linebacker at the 2025 combine.
strengths: Rare size-speed combination at 6-4 and 239 pounds with a 39-inch vertical and 10-foot-5 broad jump. Covers ground sideline to sideline with rangy strides; backside pursuit is a real weapon at his size. Gets hands on blockers early and keeps his chest clean; run defense tape against climbing linemen is impressive. Consistent wrap-up tackler with a 10.5% missed tackle rate across five college seasons at two programs. UCF game showed his full toolkit: forced fumble, recovery, 1.5 sacks, and relentless effort on every snap. Defensive end background provides a rush toolkit with gap-shooting ability and timing off the snap count. Drops into zone coverage with fluid hips and carries routes deeper than most 240-pound linebackers can manage. Motor runs the same in the fourth quarter as the first; that consistency translates directly to special teams value.
weaknesses: Zone awareness lags behind the physical tools; gets manipulated by route combinations and drifts off landmarks too often. Half-step slow diagnosing run action despite the speed, leaving him late to the fit point against misdirection. Pulling guards on gap schemes displace him too easily; play strength at the point of attack needs real improvement. Only one full season of traditional off-ball linebacker work at a competitive level raises processing concerns. Pass rush production is thin for someone with 585 career edge snaps; lacks counters when his bull rush stalls.
player_comparisons: NoahTaylor - Virginia (57%), CarsonWells - Colorado (55%), AdamAnderson - Georgia (55%)

Drops into zone coverage with fluid hips and carries routes deeper than most 240-pound linebackers can manage. Motor runs the same in the fourth quarter as the first; that consistency translates directly to special teams value.

Weaknesses:
Zone awareness lags behind the physical tools; gets manipulated by route combinations and drifts off landmarks too often. Half-step slow diagnosing run action despite the speed, leaving him late to the fit point against misdirection. Pulling guards on gap schemes displace him too easily; play strength at the point of attack needs real improvement. Only one full season of traditional off-ball linebacker work at a competitive level raises processing concerns. Pass rush production is thin for someone with 585 career edge snaps; lacks counters when his bull rush stalls.

bio: Jalen Berger from Don Bosco Prep High School was rated a 4-star recruit by ESPN and handed a 4-star grade by 247 Sports. Following high school, Berger joined Wisconsin. In 2020 as a freshman Berger saw action in 4 games for the Badgers as he ran for 301 yards on 60 attempts for 5.0 yards per carry, with 2 TDs, with no fumbles. As a receiver he made just 2 receptions for 13 yards. As a sophomore in 2021 he played just 3 games. and rushed for just 88 yards on 24 carries for just 3.7 ypc, with one TD, with no fumbles. In the passing game he pulled in just 2 receptions for 17 yards. After the end of the year Berger transferred to Michigan State where as a junior Berger saw action in 12 games for the Spartans. He ran for 678 yards on 148 attempts for 4.6 ypc, with 6 TDs, with 2 fumbles. As a receiver he made 19 receptions for 128 yards. As a senior in 2023 he played in 5 games and ran for just 93 yards on 25 carries for just average of 3.7 ypc, with one TD, with no fumbles. In the passing game he had 5 receptions for just 40 yards.
strengths: He’s patient, letting blocks develop then accelerating. Berger runs with a good forward lean and consistently falls forward. He’s a quality receiver with soft hands, and Berger shows a good feel for catching and transitioning upfield in the screen game. Not truly explosive, but possesses enough speed to beat linebacker to the edge. Does not possess top breakaway speed, though he's rarely caught from behind. He shows good vision and good explosion upfield once he plants his foot. Berger has lateral movement skills and enough speed to threaten big plays. He’s excellent in the passing game, capable of flexing out as a receiver and creating mismatches against linebackers, and he’s a solid back in the screen game. Possesses quick feet and straight-line speed but doesn't have the elite burst to be a constant big-play threat. Improving patience as a runner, following pulling guards and makes good decisions on his cut.
weaknesses: Offers little in pass protection and poor technique cut blocking, fails to do more than slow down blitzers by laying down in front of them. Berger really isn’t a guy who will create a lot of separation. Berger isn’t slow, but Berger lacks the third gear to be a home run threat. His hands are improving but still inconsistent in the passing game.
player_comparisons: RickyPerson Jr. - NC State (84%), GaryBrightwell - Arizona (73%), JirehlBrock - Iowa State (69%)

bio: Jalen Catalon, a 5'11", 205-pound safety from UNLV, has had a tumultuous college career marked by flashes of brilliance and setbacks due to injuries. Born on March 15, 2001, in Mansfield, Texas, Catalon was a standout at Mansfield Legacy High School, earning All-Area Defensive Player of the Year honors as a junior. His senior season was cut short by a knee injury, but he still garnered a four-star recruit rating. Catalon began his college career at Arkansas, where he redshirted his freshman year before breaking out in 2020. He recorded 99 tackles, 3 interceptions, and 2 forced fumbles, earning first-team All-SEC and Freshman All-American honors. However, injuries limited him to just six games in 2021 and one game in 2022. He transferred to Texas for the 2023 season, appearing in eight games with 17 tackles and one forced fumble. After entering the transfer portal again, Catalon landed at UNLV for his final season of eligibility in 2024. Throughout his college career, he has totalled176 total tackles, 5 interceptions, and 4 forced fumbles in 29 games played across four schools.
strengths: Aggressive downhill player who excels in run support, showing a quick trigger and willingness to fly into the box Possesses good instincts and play recognition, often diagnosing plays quickly and putting himself in position to make stops Exhibits solid ball skills when healthy, with the ability to high-point passes and create turnovers in the secondary Demonstrates leadership qualities and solid football IQ, having played in multiple defensive systems across his college career Shows versatility to line up at various spots in the defensive backfield, including deep safety and in the box Tackles with authority and brings a physical presence to the secondary, intimidating receivers coming across the middle Has experience in big-time college football environments, having played in the SEC and Big 12 conferences Displays good short-area quickness and change of direction skills when healthy, aiding in coverage and pursuit angles
weaknesses: Extensive injury history raises red flags about long-term durability and ability to withstand the physical demands of the pro game Lacks prototypical length for the safety position, potentially struggling to match up with jumbo-sized tight ends and contested catch specialists Long speed is a concern, potentially limiting his ability to cover ground as a single-high safety or recover when beaten deep Inconsistent tackling technique shows up on tape, occasionally leading to missed tackles in space or allowing extra yardage after contact May be scheme-limited at the next level, lacking the athleticism to consistently hold up in man coverage against NFL-caliber athletes
player_comparisons: EvanWilliams - Oregon (92%), PatrickMcMorris - California (92%), YusufCorker - Kentucky (89%)

bio: Two-way production at Eastside High School in Covington, Georgia, defined Jalen Farmer's prep career. He played left guard and defensive tackle as a senior, racking up 50 tackles, two sacks, and a touchdown while anchoring the offensive front under head coaches Troy Hoff and Jay Cawthon. That workload helped him earn the 2021 Georgia AAAAA Region 8 Lineman of the Year award, and 247Sports slotted him as a top-35 offensive lineman nationally in the composite rankings. He signed with Florida as a consensus three-star recruit across ESPN, 247, and Rivals. Farmer redshirted his true freshman year at Florida in 2022, appearing briefly against Eastern Washington and South Carolina. The following season he saw reserve duty in two more games, logging snaps against McNeese State and Vanderbilt. With limited opportunity to crack the Gators' lineup, he entered the transfer portal and landed at Kentucky for the 2024 season, returning to a program that had been among his finalists out of high school. The move paid off immediately. Farmer earned the starting right guard job from Week 1 against Southern Miss and never relinquished it, starting all 12 games that year. He allowed just one sack across the entire season, posted a 99 percent assignment grade, and tallied 95 blocks at the point of attack with 18 knockdown blocks. His junior year in 2025 brought another step forward. Farmer logged a team-high 831 snaps, graded out at 84 percent by the coaching staff with only two missed assignments, and earned SEC Co-Offensive Lineman of the Week honors for his dominant showing against Auburn, where he played all 64 offensive snaps without allowing a single pressure or committing a penalty. He was named a game captain four times and accepted an invitation to the 2026 Panini Senior Bowl. After the season, with Kentucky parting ways with Mark Stoops and offensive line coach Eric Wolford departing for LSU, Farmer declared for the 2026 NFL Draft with a year of eligibility still remaining.
strengths: Plays with a violent temperament and real finishing mentality; he wants to bury defenders and rarely lets up once he's latched onto a body. Impressive length at 34.5-inch arms allows him to control the point of attack and create a wide sphere of influence in both phases. Heavy, punishing hands that land with authority on contact; his initial punch staggers interior rushers and resets their momentum instantly. Anchors down quickly and decisively in pass protection, particularly effective on short sets where he clamps and sits into his base to hold pocket depth. Devastating puller who can kick out defensive ends or wrap to the second level; his combination of mass and effort makes him a wrecking ball on the move. Strong vertical displacement in the run game, especially on double-teams where his core strength and heavy legs consistently move defenders off the ball. Improved dramatically from his first to second year as a starter, cutting his penalties from six down to one and raising his overall blocking grades across the board. Reliable snap-to-snap competitor who logged over 800 snaps in consecutive seasons while maintaining a pressure efficiency rate above 97 percent both years.
weaknesses: Lateral agility is a real limitation when asked to redirect against second-level defenders; his feet can get heavy and trail behind his upper body. Inconsistent timing off the snap hurts him on backside cut-offs and reaching wide-aligned defenders, leaving him late to landmarks. Susceptible to twists, stunts, and games up front; can be a beat slow to recognize and react when rushers exchange gaps post-snap. Overaggressiveness causes occasional issues on drive blocks where he lunges and fails to connect cleanly, whiffing on his target entirely. Has played exclusively right guard throughout his college career, with all 1,637 snaps at one spot, raising questions about positional versatility at the next level.
player_comparisons: TrevorKeegan - Michigan (79%), TylerCooper - Minnesota (78%), LukeKandra - Cincinnati (78%)

bio: Jalen Huskey played both cornerback and wide receiver at Quince Orchard High School in Gaithersburg, Maryland, where he helped the team finish 14-0 and win a state title in 2021. He drew modest recruiting attention, landing as a two-to-three star prospect depending on the service, and signed with Bowling Green. After a redshirt-level role as a true freshman in 2022, he locked down the starting cornerback spot in 2023 and posted 52 tackles, four interceptions, and six pass breakups across 12 starts, earning First Team All-MAC honors. Huskey transferred to Maryland ahead of the 2024 season and moved to safety under head coach Mike Locksley, who had recruited him out of high school. The position switch came with growing pains, but he still started 11 of 12 games and picked off three passes. As a senior in 2025, everything clicked. He finished with 72 tackles, four interceptions, and a Second Team All-Big Ten selection from both media and coaches. Maryland named him its Defensive Player of the Year, and he received invitations to the Shrine Bowl and the NFL Scouting Combine.
strengths: Eleven interceptions over his last three seasons speak to genuine ball skills and route recognition, not just volume opportunity. Reads the quarterback's eyes in zone and triggers downhill with clean timing on breaking routes. Corner background shows up when asked to match slot receivers or tight ends in man coverage. Willing run defender who uses active hands to stack and shed blocks near the line of scrimmage. Arrives under control in the alley, wraps up consistently, and limits yards after contact. Vocal leader who was voted permanent team captain and drew praise from scouts at the Shrine Bowl. Steady year-over-year improvement at Maryland, particularly from his junior to senior season in coverage. Good length at 6-1 helps him contest catch points and disrupt throwing windows over the middle.
weaknesses: Below-average straight-line speed shows up when asked to carry vertical routes deep. Hip transitions are stiff, and quicker receivers can eat up his cushion on breaks. Gave up too many completions as a junior before tightening up his coverage as a senior. Pursuit angles widen against outside runs, and he lacks the recovery burst to correct them. Light frame raises concerns about holding up against bigger ball carriers in the box.
player_comparisons: BradHawkins - Michigan (91%), KitanOladapo - Oregon State (84%), JoshProctor - Ohio State (82%)

bio: Jalen Stroman arrived at Notre Dame in January 2025 as a graduate transfer from Virginia Tech, seeking both a fresh start and a chance to compete for a national championship after a season-ending injury against Vanderbilt cut short his 2024 campaign. The Nokesville, Virginia native had posted 55 tackles in 11 games during 2023 for the Hokies, showcasing the physical, downhill style that made him a three-star recruit out of Patriot High School. He's the younger brother of NFL defensive back Greg Stroman. His lone season in South Bend was a redemption story. Stroman appeared in all 12 games for the Fighting Irish, starting five times while accumulating 37 tackles with three tackles for loss and two pass breakups. The defining moment came against Syracuse when he picked off his first career interception on the second play from scrimmage and took it 44 yards to the house, then added a blocked punt that resulted in another Irish touchdown. That performance earned him Senior Bowl Defensive Player of the Week honors. Over four collegiate seasons split between Blacksburg and South Bend, Stroman logged 148 career tackles across 49 games. Stroman earned three-star ratings across the board from ESPN (75 grade), 247Sports (0.880 composite grade), and Rivals (5.6 grade) coming out of Patriot High, where he earned first-team all-region honors at defensive back while also catching 18 passes for 470 yards and five touchdowns as a receiver during his junior year. The late-blooming production and injury setback mean he enters the draft as an older prospect with fewer splash plays than his physical tools might suggest, but the tape shows plenty to work with at the next level.
strengths: Fires downhill with bad intentions when he reads run, attacking gaps with force and delivering contact through ball carriers instead of just wrapping up at the point of attack. Processes information quickly pre-snap and shows excellent anticipation, allowing him to trigger and close on plays faster than his timed speed suggests he should move. Zone coverage instincts are legitimately impressive, as he reads quarterback eyes well and understands route concepts quickly enough to be in position when the ball arrives downfield. Length and wide shoulders give him the frame to handle bigger slot receivers and tight ends in coverage, plus he can add weight without losing mobility if needed. Maintains competitive edge on every snap with the kind of urgency and intensity that shows up on special teams film and makes him a tone-setter in practice. Ball skills are better than the stat sheet indicates, as he drives well on throws in front of him and shows natural hands when given opportunities to make plays. Takes on blocks aggressively when setting edges in run support, refusing to be easily washed out of lanes and fighting through contact to make plays near the line of scrimmage. Body control in space allows him to flip hips and stay in phase with receivers working intermediate routes, demonstrating the fluidity to handle coverage responsibilities in two-high structures.
weaknesses: Man coverage technique needs refinement, as he can get too handsy at the top of routes and struggles to mirror sudden breaks when playing tight coverage on athletic receivers. Takes false steps occasionally when diagnosing plays, leading to him being out of position when his initial read proves incorrect and the offense attacks elsewhere. Tackling consistency leaves something to be desired given his physical tools, as he sometimes aims too high or tries to deliver the knockout blow instead of securing the tackle. Range working backward in single-high looks isn't his strength, as he's more comfortable working downhill than covering ground deep down the field against vertical threats. Production profile shows just one career interception despite playing 49 college games, suggesting either limited ball production skills or schemes that didn't create opportunities for him to capitalize on chances.
player_comparisons: TreyTaylor - Air Force (86%), SebastianCastro - Iowa (84%), BeauBrade - Maryland (82%)

bio: Jalon Daniels came to Lawrence out of Lawndale High School in Lawndale, California as a three-star recruit and wound up playing six seasons for the Jayhawks. He started as a true freshman during the shortened 2020 campaign, completing his very first pass attempt for a 48-yard touchdown against TCU. Kansas went 0-9 that year, but Daniels showed enough flashes to keep eyes on him going forward. The Lance Leipold era changed things. Daniels won the starting job for good in late 2021, and by 2022 he was the engine behind Kansas's first bowl appearance since 2008. He threw for 2,014 yards and 18 touchdowns that season, then capped it with a 544-yard, six-touchdown Liberty Bowl performance against Arkansas that set a school record. A back injury wiped out most of 2023 after he had been named Big 12 Preseason Offensive Player of the Year, limiting him to three games. He returned in 2024 and threw for 2,454 yards with 14 touchdowns and 12 interceptions before closing his career in 2025 with 2,531 yards and a career-high 22 passing touchdowns. He finished second in Kansas history in career passing yards (9,282) and passing touchdowns (67), and his 23 career rushing touchdowns are tied for the most ever by a Jayhawk quarterback.
strengths: Very good athlete for the position with legit speed as a runner. Quick release gets the ball out in a hurry on short concepts. Capable of making throws from off-platform and awkward body positions. Willing scrambler who picks up first downs with his legs consistently. Can uncork the deep ball when his feet are set and mechanics are right. Good enough touch on screens and check-downs to let receivers run after the catch. Competitive runner between the tackles who does not shy from contact.
weaknesses: Intermediate accuracy is a real problem, especially on timing routes. Does not climb the pocket well and tends to bail too early. Coverage recognition is inconsistent, particularly reading deep safeties. Six college seasons and the processing still lags under pressure. Significant injury history including shoulder, back, and knee concerns. Ball security is poor; fumble issues showed up again in 2025. Undersized frame raises durability questions given his running style.

name: Jalon Daniels
scouting_report:
Background:
Jalon Daniels came to Lawrence out of Lawndale High School in Lawndale, California as a three-star recruit and wound up playing six seasons for the Jayhawks. He started as a true freshman during the shortened 2020 campaign, completing his very first pass attempt for a 48-yard touchdown against TCU. Kansas went 0-9 that year, but Daniels showed enough flashes to keep eyes on him going forward. The Lance Leipold era changed things. Daniels won the starting job for good in late 2021, and by 2022 he was the engine behind Kansas's first bowl appearance since 2008. He threw for 2,014 yards and 18 touchdowns that season, then capped it with a 544-yard, six-touchdown Liberty Bowl performance against Arkansas that set a school record. A back injury wiped out most of 2023 after he had been named Big 12 Preseason Offensive Player of the Year, limiting him to three games. He returned in 2024 and threw for 2,454 yards with 14 touchdowns and 12 interceptions before closing his career in 2025 with 2,531 yards and a career-high 22 passing touchdowns. He finished second in Kansas history in career passing yards (9,282) and passing touchdowns (67), and his 23 career rushing touchdowns are tied for the most ever by a Jayhawk quarterback.

Strengths:
Very good athlete for the position with legit speed as a runner. Quick release gets the ball out in a hurry on short concepts. Capable of making throws from off-platform and awkward body positions. Willing scrambler who picks up first downs with his legs consistently. Can uncork the deep ball when his feet are set and mechanics are right. Good enough touch on screens and check-downs to let receivers run after the catch. Competitive runner between the tackles who does not shy from contact.

Weaknesses:
Intermediate accuracy is a real problem, especially on timing routes. Does not climb the pocket well and tends to bail too early. Coverage recognition is inconsistent, particularly reading deep safeties. Six college seasons and the processing still lags under pressure. Significant injury history including shoulder, back, and knee concerns. Ball security is poor; fumble issues showed up again in 2025. Undersized frame raises durability questions given his running style.

bio: Two-way production at Putnam County High School in Eatonton, Georgia, told the full story of Jalon Kilgore's athleticism: 34 catches for 921 yards and 11 touchdowns on offense, 88 tackles and four forced fumbles at safety, and a Georgia 2A long jump state title as a sophomore. He chose South Carolina over Clemson, Oklahoma, Michigan, and Florida State. Kilgore was thrown into action as a true freshman in 2023 when Nick Emmanwori went down in the opener against North Carolina. He responded with 12 tackles and never gave the job back, finishing with 76 tackles and First-Team Freshman All-America honors from ESPN and 247Sports. As a sophomore, he tied for the SEC lead with five interceptions, including game-sealing picks against Old Dominion and Missouri. His peers elected him a permanent team captain ahead of the 2025 season, and he shared the Joe Morrison Defensive MVP award. He finished with 54 tackles, 10 pass breakups, and two interceptions across 11 starts before declaring for the draft. Over three years, Kilgore compiled 178 tackles, eight interceptions, and 21 pass breakups in 36 games
strengths: Outstanding run defender who takes on blocks, fills gaps, and arrives with force near the box. Watch the Missouri tape: 10 tackles, consistently shedding blocks and arriving at the ball carrier first. Long arms and full wrap when he closes on receivers after the catch. Finishes tackles with conviction. Sealed wins over Old Dominion and Missouri with late interceptions. Shows up in clutch moments. Eyes stay disciplined in zone, reads the quarterback's shoulders, and triggers downhill before the ball arrives. Jams bigger slot receivers and tight ends off the line with real play strength at the contact point. South Carolina moved him all over the secondary and he held up at nickel, box safety, and deep half. Explosive lower half shows up in his vertical, broad jump, and how quickly he closes space in the short area.
weaknesses: When shifty receivers win off the line, he lacks the recovery burst to get back into phase. Hands get active and grabby in press when he loses his feet. NFL officials will punish that. Rounds his breaks instead of snapping through them, giving receivers extra separation on sharp cuts. Shuts the motor down on plays away from him. You'll see him jogging in pursuit too often. Telegraphs his blitzes from the nickel spot and lacks a plan when a blocker picks him up. Gets rotational as a tackler at times, spinning off ball carriers instead of driving through them. Stays squared up too long against vertical stems, which lets receivers stack him before he can flip and run.

bio: The transformation from wide receiver to running back doesn't happen overnight, but Jamal Haynes made it look seamless when he switched positions during Georgia Tech's 2023 fall camp. What followed was a breakout campaign that saw him become the Yellow Jackets' first 1,000-yard rusher since 2017, accumulating 1,059 yards on the ground while earning All-ACC third-team honors. His journey from Grayson High School's state championship squad to Atlanta's premier offensive weapon tells the story of an athlete who maximizes every opportunity handed to him. Haynes arrived in Atlanta as a three-star recruit from the same Grayson program that produced numerous Power Five talents, where he helped capture an undefeated 14-0 state title as a senior. After spending his first two collegiate seasons buried on the depth chart as a receiver without recording statistics, the position switch unlocked his true potential. His 2023 breakout included four 100-yard rushing performances, highlighted by a career-long 67-yard scamper against South Carolina State and a Gasparilla Bowl MVP performance where he gashed UCF for 128 yards on 18 carries. Despite battling injuries throughout 2024, he still managed 944 rushing yards and maintained his status as one of the ACC's most dangerous threats. The dual-threat capability that made him attractive as a prep athlete has translated beautifully to his current role, where he's accumulated over 2,000 rushing yards across his two seasons as a starter. His receiving background provides an extra dimension that keeps defensive coordinators awake at night, as evidenced by his 48 career receptions for 317 yards. When healthy, Haynes represents everything modern offenses covet in a backfield weapon - speed, versatility, and the vision to turn routine handoffs into explosive plays. His 5.8 yards per carry over two seasons demonstrates consistency that goes beyond just breakaway speed.
strengths: Lightning-quick acceleration through gaps with the burst to separate from pursuing defenders at the second level of coverage. Natural receiving skills from his wide receiver background create mismatches against linebackers in coverage situations and checkdown scenarios. Exceptional vision between the tackles allows him to identify and exploit cutback lanes that other backs might miss entirely. Deceptive power for his frame demonstrated by his ability to break arm tackles and drag defenders into the end zone. Home run speed that transforms routine carries into explosive plays, evidenced by multiple career runs exceeding 65 yards. Outstanding yards after contact production shows his ability to maximize every touch despite lacking prototypical size for the position. Versatility to line up in multiple alignments keeps defenses guessing and creates favorable matchups in space. Competitive toughness displayed through his willingness to lower his shoulder in short-yardage situations despite his smaller stature.
weaknesses: Undersized frame raises durability concerns about withstanding the punishment of a full NFL season. Pass protection remains underdeveloped as his blocking technique often relies more on effort than proper fundamentals. Limited experience at the position compared to lifelong running backs may show up in complex NFL schemes. Injury history includes concussion issues that could become problematic with increased contact at the professional level. Ball security occasionally becomes sloppy when attempting to maximize yardage through contact situations.

bio: Jam Miller arrived at Alabama as a consensus four-star running back out of Tyler Legacy High School in Tyler, Texas, where he rewrote the school record books with nearly 5,000 career rushing yards across four varsity seasons. His senior year alone produced 1,355 rushing yards with 13 touchdowns on the ground plus another 380 yards and six scores through the air. Ranked as high as the No. 78 overall prospect and No. 4 running back nationally by 247Sports, Miller was coveted by programs across the country and ultimately chose the Crimson Tide over the in-state Texas Longhorns. His first two years in Tuscaloosa were spent learning behind established backs Roydell Williams and Jase McClellan, though Miller still flashed in limited opportunities. As a true freshman in 2022, he appeared in all 13 games and ripped off 223 yards and two touchdowns on just 33 carries, highlighted by a 38-yard burst in the Sugar Bowl against Kansas State. The following year he expanded his role to 41 carries for 201 yards while contributing on special teams, where his coaches recognized him for standout coverage efforts against Tennessee and Kentucky. His 77-yard, six-carry explosion against Chattanooga that season hinted at what was coming. Miller finally took hold of the lead back role under first-year head coach Kalen DeBoer in 2024, finishing with 676 yards and seven rushing touchdowns on 146 attempts while also catching 16 passes for 155 yards and a score. He returned as the starter in 2025 and was named to the Doak Walker Award Watch List, but an upper-body injury in fall camp cost him the first three games. He fought his way back to tally 504 yards and three touchdowns on 130 carries, plus 19 receptions for 109 yards, before another injury in the Iron Bowl against Auburn limited him down the stretch. Over four seasons at Alabama, Miller compiled 1,604 rushing yards and 13 touchdowns on 350 career carries alongside 40 receptions for 336 yards.
strengths: Built like a fire hydrant at 221 pounds with a naturally low center of gravity that makes him difficult to square up and knock backward at the point of attack. Decisive one-cut runner who trusts his reads and hits the hole with conviction instead of dancing around in the backfield waiting for something to develop. Genuinely physical ball carrier who seeks contact and delivers punishment; you can hear pads pop when he collides with linebackers in the hole. Outstanding contact balance allows him to absorb shots from bigger defenders, stay on his feet, and consistently fall forward for an extra two or three yards after initial contact. Impressive leg drive and lower-body power to churn through arm tackles and push the pile, especially in short-yardage and goal-line situations where he thrives. Clean vision between the tackles with the ability to read frontside blocks, anticipate defensive movement, and redirect to backside lanes with quick jump cuts. Reliable ball security throughout his college career with only two fumbles on 350 rushing attempts, a trait that will keep coaches trusting him in critical game situations. Tested with elite straight-line speed at the combine level, which flashes on tape when he finds a crease and gets downhill, even if he does not always separate from pursuit angles.
weaknesses: Production regressed noticeably in 2025 with his yards per carry dropping nearly a full yard and his overall rushing effectiveness taking a clear step backward from his 2024 campaign. Pass protection is a real concern; his anticipation reading blitzes and positioning against rushers graded out poorly, and he lacks meaningful reps in that phase of the game. Limited receiving upside with a troubling drop rate across his career, and he does not create much after the catch, which restricts his value on passing downs. Open-field elusiveness is below average for the position; he lacks the hip flexibility and lateral quickness to consistently make defenders miss one-on-one in space. Never truly shouldered a bellcow workload at Alabama, topping out at 146 carries in a season, which raises questions about durability and his ability to handle a full NFL snap count.

bio: James Brockermeyer from All Saints Episcopal High School was rated a 4-star recruit by ESPN and handed a 4-star grade by 247 Sports. Following high school, Brockermeyer opted to join Alabama. In 2022 as a freshman Brockermeyer saw action in just one game and played a total of 9 snaps for the Crimson Tide. He allowed no QB hurries, no QB hits, and no sacks while playing sparingly. As a sophomore in 2023 he played in just 3 games and a total of 28 snaps. He conceded no QB hurries, no QB hits, and no sacks while playing at center.
strengths: Fundamentally sound and understands blocking angles, using body positioning to his advantage. Disciplined awareness and good intelligence. Brockermeyer will have a chance to develop as a right tackle or possibly inside at guard, where Brockermeyer could be an interesting prospect for a zone-blocking team. If nothing else, that versatility is enough to make him at least a quality reserve. Brockermeyer gets off the snap quickly, and he is consistently able to generate movement at the point of attack, typically finishing those blocks strong. Shows good awareness after the snap, reacting and positioning himself against twists and stunts Willing to attack and punches multiple times to finish the play. Quick to pick up edge blitz and shows no panic, easily adjusting angles and resetting his feet. Can recover and reset his anchor. Rarely late off the snap. Above-average football smarts and general intelligence. Plays with a nasty streak. Solid character and work ethic.
weaknesses: Stronger pass rushers can get under his pads and steer him aside. A short-area blocker with limited range and doesn't consistently get to the second level. Will play undisciplined at times, especially in space. Lacks a finishing attitude. Often simply tries to push pass rushers past the pocket. Brockermeyer can also look a bit hesitant when it comes to recognizing and reacting to twists, stunts and blitzes up front.

bio: Jaren Kanak was born in Hays, Kansas, where he became a school legend at Hays High School. Standing 6'2" and weighing 233 pounds, Kanak was the consensus top recruit in Kansas and ranked among the nation's elite athletes in the 2022 class. He earned four-star ratings across all major recruiting services, including an 83 from ESPN and a 0.94 grade from 247Sports. What made his recruitment fascinating was the position ambiguity. College coaches couldn't decide if he belonged on offense or defense because he dominated both sides of the ball. As a senior quarterback/linebacker, he rushed for a school-record 1,615 yards at 10.2 yards per carry with 23 touchdowns while also throwing for 910 yards and 12 scores. Defensively, he was just as disruptive. He ultimately chose Oklahoma over Alabama, Clemson, and Michigan. The athletic testing numbers from his high school track career tell you everything about his physical gifts. At just over 200 pounds, Kanak ran a 10.37 in the 100 meters, which ranked as the sixth-fastest time in Kansas state history. He also posted a 22.06 in the 200 meters and 51.90 in the 400. This wasn't some skinny track kid either. Kanak arrived at Oklahoma as a linebacker and spent three seasons learning the position from scratch under Brent Venables. He played in all 39 games from 2022 through 2024, starting nine times as a sophomore in 2023 when he tallied 62 tackles, 6.0 tackles for loss, and 2.0 sacks. But with Danny Stutsman, Kip Lewis, and Kobie McKinzie ahead of him on the depth chart, meaningful snaps became harder to find. Rather than enter the transfer portal like so many players in similar situations, Kanak approached the coaching staff with an unconventional request before his senior season: let me play tight end. The gamble paid off in spectacular fashion. In 2025, Kanak started all 13 games at tight end and finished as Oklahoma's third-leading receiver with 44 catches for 533 yards. Early in the season, he led all FBS tight ends in receiving yards despite the position switch happening just months earlier. His production helped Oklahoma make a surprise run to the College Football Playoff. The John Mackey Award voters took notice, placing him among the nation's best at the position. Named to the SEC Fall Academic Honor Roll, Kanak finished his career with 106 tackles on defense and 44 receptions on offense.
strengths: That 4.4 speed is real, and it shows up on tape. Linebackers have no prayer staying with him down the seam, and safeties have to cheat their coverage. Zero drops on 44 catches tells you all you need to know about his hands. Ball sticks to him in traffic and he wins contested situations consistently. Three years reading offenses as a linebacker gave him an innate feel for zone coverage. He finds the soft spots like a veteran, not a one-year tight end. Tracks the deep ball beautifully. Adjusts on the move, high-points throws, and pulls in passes over his shoulder without losing speed. Easy mover for his size. You can line him up anywhere. Inline, slot, wing, H-back. Defensive coordinators hate game-planning for guys who move around this much pre-snap. The kid flat-out works. Coaches say he watches more film than anyone on the roster, and you see it in how fast he picked up the position. Runs angry after the catch. That linebacker mentality didn't disappear when he switched sides. He'll lower his shoulder and punish tacklers in the open field. Gets off the line quick when defenders don't jam him. That track explosion translates to easy separation on vertical stems against off coverage.
weaknesses: At 6'2", he's not really a tight end. He's a big slot receiver they're calling a tight end. That height kills him at the catch point against NFL safeties and limits his red zone ceiling. The blocking is rough. He gets washed out of plays, can't sustain on the edge, and needs significant work before you can trust him in the run game. One year at tight end means a limited route tree. He wins with speed and instincts right now, not with polished technique on intermediate concepts. Zero touchdowns on 44 catches jumps off the page. Whether that's usage or an inability to finish in traffic near the goal line, it's worth watching. His frame probably maxes out around 240 pounds before he starts losing the speed that makes him special. There's no path to a traditional tight end body here.
player_comparisons: ChigoziemOkonkwo - Maryland (78%), JaheimBell - Florida State (70%), BrantKuithe - Utah (68%)

At just over 200 pounds, Kanak ran a 10.37 in the 100 meters, which ranked as the sixth-fastest time in Kansas state history. He also posted a 22.06 in the 200 meters and 51.90 in the 400. This wasn't some skinny track kid either. Kanak arrived at Oklahoma as a linebacker and spent three seasons learning the position from scratch under Brent Venables. He played in all 39 games from 2022 through 2024, starting nine times as a sophomore in 2023 when he tallied 62 tackles, 6.0 tackles for loss, and 2.0 sacks. But with Danny Stutsman, Kip Lewis, and Kobie McKinzie ahead of him on the depth chart, meaningful snaps became harder to find. Rather than enter the transfer portal like so many players in similar situations, Kanak approached the coaching staff with an unconventional request before his senior season: let me play tight end. The gamble paid off in spectacular fashion. In 2025, Kanak started all 13 games at tight end and finished as Oklahoma's third-leading receiver with 44 catches for 533 yards. Early in the season, he led all FBS tight ends in receiving yards despite the position switch happening just months earlier. His production helped Oklahoma make a surprise run to the College Football Playoff. The John Mackey Award voters took notice, placing him among the nation's best at the position. Named to the SEC Fall Academic Honor Roll, Kanak finished his career with 106 tackles on defense and 44 receptions on offense.

Strengths:
That 4.4 speed is real, and it shows up on tape. Linebackers have no prayer staying with him down the seam, and safeties have to cheat their coverage. Zero drops on 44 catches tells you all you need to know about his hands. Ball sticks to him in traffic and he wins contested situations consistently. Three years reading offenses as a linebacker gave him an innate feel for zone coverage. He finds the soft spots like a veteran, not a one-year tight end. Tracks the deep ball beautifully. Adjusts on the move, high-points throws, and pulls in passes over his shoulder without losing speed. Easy mover for his size. You can line him up anywhere. Inline, slot, wing, H-back. Defensive coordinators hate game-planning for guys who move around this much pre-snap. The kid flat-out works. Coaches say he watches more film than anyone on the roster, and you see it in how fast he picked up the position. Runs angry after the catch. That linebacker mentality didn't disappear when he switched sides. He'll lower his shoulder and punish tacklers in the open field. Gets off the line quick when defenders don't jam him. That track explosion translates to easy separation on vertical stems against off coverage.

Weaknesses:
At 6'2", he's not really a tight end. He's a big slot receiver they're calling a tight end. That height kills him at the catch point against NFL safeties and limits his red zone ceiling. The blocking is rough. He gets washed out of plays, can't sustain on the edge, and needs significant work before you can trust him in the run game. One year at tight end means a limited route tree. He wins with speed and instincts right now, not with polished technique on intermediate concepts. Zero touchdowns on 44 catches jumps off the page. Whether that's usage or an inability to finish in traffic near the goal line, it's worth watching. His frame probably maxes out around 240 pounds before he starts losing the speed that makes him special. There's no path to a traditional tight end body here.

bio: Two high school stints, one at Bishop Gorman in Las Vegas and the other back home in Norco, California, shaped Jaydn Ott's recruiting profile before he ever touched a college football. He was a consensus four-star recruit who rushed for over 2,200 yards and 30 touchdowns as a senior, earning MaxPreps Big XIII League Back of the Year. He signed with Cal and made an immediate impact as a true freshman in 2022, rushing for 897 yards and eight touchdowns on 170 carries while adding 321 receiving yards and three scores through the air. That earned him freshman All-America honors from The Athletic and honorable mention All-Pac-12 recognition. His sophomore year was the real statement. Ott ran for 1,315 yards and 12 touchdowns in 2023, leading the Pac-12 in rushing yards per game at 109.6. He was named first-team All-Pac-12 by both coaches and media and finished as a finalist for the Polynesian College Football Player of the Year Award. His 274-yard game against Arizona stands as the most ever by a Pac-12 freshman. Injuries limited his 2024 season to 385 rushing yards across 10 games. He transferred to Oklahoma for 2025 but saw just 31 offensive snaps, totaling 68 rushing yards on 21 carries with no touchdowns.
strengths: Good initial burst and acceleration to hit creases before they close. Patient runner who lets blocks develop before committing downhill. Contact balance keeps him on his feet through arm tackles and glancing hits. Willing pass catcher with soft hands; 98 career receptions across four years. Testing numbers confirm real explosiveness: vertical and broad jump both well above average. Runs with physicality and forward lean despite a frame under 210 pounds. Shows good vision on zone runs, finding cutback lanes at full speed.
weaknesses: Production fell off a cliff after his sophomore year and never recovered. Poor agility testing raises concerns about lateral quickness and change of direction. Pass protection is a clear liability; lunges at blitzers instead of anchoring. Bench press numbers suggest he lacks the functional strength to hold up inside. Pad level gets high in traffic, which invites bigger collisions and stalls his legs. Turns 23 before his rookie season, limiting the window on his prime years. Only 31 snaps at Oklahoma make it nearly impossible to evaluate his current form.
player_comparisons: RasheenAli - Marshall (72%), JeromeFord - Cincinnati (66%), TylerGoodson - Iowa (65%)

bio: Jeff Caldwell's path to the NFL Draft started on a soccer field in Louisville, Kentucky, where football wasn't even on the radar. That changed when a late growth spurt before his senior year of high school took him from 5-foot-8 to nearly 6-foot-5, and suddenly the game his older brother Frank played at Butler Traditional looked a lot more interesting. Caldwell followed Frank to Lindenwood University in St. Charles, Missouri, where he appeared in seven games as a true freshman in 2022, hauling in eight catches for 141 yards and three touchdowns. A modest debut, but the touchdown-per-game ratio hinted at what was coming. By his sophomore year, Caldwell had become a legitimate weapon for the Lions, posting 31 receptions for 590 yards and eight touchdowns in 2023, earning first-team All-OVC honors. He racked up two 100-yard outings that season and torched Western Illinois for 185 yards and four scores on just five catches. His junior campaign was the real breakout: 53 catches, 1,032 yards, and 11 touchdowns made him a finalist for the Walter Payton Award as the top offensive player in FCS. Five games over the century mark and a four-touchdown explosion against Eastern Illinois tied for the most by any FCS receiver that year. He earned Stats Perform Second-Team All-American and First-Team All-OVC recognition for his efforts. That kind of production at the FCS level, combined with freakish testing numbers, made Caldwell a hot commodity in the transfer portal. He landed at Cincinnati for the 2025 season and stepped into a much tougher Big 12 environment. The numbers came back to earth: 32 catches for 478 yards and six touchdowns across 13 games. But the Bearcats staff saw immediate flashes of his physical ceiling, including a jaw-dropping one-handed grab against Bowling Green that set up a score and a five-catch, 109-yard performance in that same game. He also earned a spot on Bruce Feldman's annual Freaks List at No. 29, thanks to an 11-foot-9 broad jump that would have won the NFL Combine and a top speed clocked around 22 mph on Cincinnati's Catapult system.
strengths: His frame is something you cannot coach or replicate: 6-5 with a 78-inch wingspan and the kind of catch radius that lets quarterbacks throw to a spot and trust the ball will get hauled in. Tested as one of the most explosive athletes at the entire Combine regardless of position, and that burst translates on film where he gets vertical in a hurry and stacks corners before they can recover. Long, fluid stride covers ground deceptively fast, and corners who give him a cushion at the line find themselves in a foot race they were never going to win. Scored touchdowns at an absurd clip throughout college, finding the end zone 28 times on just 124 career catches, which tells you he understands how to use his body in tight spaces near the goal line. Put on significant mass during his year at Cincinnati without losing any of his speed or explosion, suggesting his body is still filling out and he has not yet hit his physical ceiling. Tracks the deep ball with natural ease and can adjust to underthrown passes on the move, giving his quarterback a generous margin of error on vertical shots down the sideline. Solid body control for a receiver his size, showing the coordination to get both feet down along the sideline on boundary catches that most players his height would stumble through. Runs with real effort as a blocker in the run game and Cincinnati trusted him enough to leave him on the field in their heavy personnel packages, which says something about his willingness even if the technique is behind.
weaknesses: Route tree is still in its infancy beyond vertical concepts and basic stems; his breaks are soft and rounded, and he tips off where he is headed with his hips and eyes far too early. For a man his size, the contested catch numbers are a real head-scratcher: he won fewer than half of his 50/50 balls across four college seasons, often playing passive when he should be going up and attacking. Hands remain his most concerning trait, with drops consistently showing up on film at a rate of roughly one in every ten catchable targets throughout his career. Production took a clear step backward once he faced FBS-level corners at Cincinnati, and the jump from OVC competition to Big 12 secondaries exposed how much of his Lindenwood dominance was context-dependent. Blocking technique at the point of attack is unrefined; he has the desire but not the leverage, hand placement, or sustain to hold up against NFL-caliber defenders in the run game.
player_comparisons: IsaiahNeyor - Nebraska (69%), JacobHarris - UCF (67%), Dont'eThornton Jr. - Tennessee (65%)

bio: Jeff Sims from Sandalwood High School was rated a 4-star recruit by ESPN and handed a 3-star grade by 247 Sports. After high school, Sims opted to join Georgia Tech. In 2020 as a freshman Sims saw action in 10 games for the Yellow Jackets as he threw for 1879 yards on 257 attempts with a completion percent of 54.5 for an average of 7.3 yards per pass, with 13 TDs, while taking 24 sacks, and QB rating of 73.7. He added 101 runs for a total of 642 yards at an average of 6.4 per run. In 2021 as a sophomore he played in 7 games as he passed for 1466 yards on 187 attempts with a completion percent of 59.9 for an average of 7.8 yards per pass, with 12 TDs, while also taking 12 sacks, and putting up an excellent QB rating of 90.5. He made 61 runs for a total of 449 yards at an average of 7.4 per rush. As a junior in 2022 Sims played a total of 7 games. He passed for 1114 yards on 188 attempts and put up a completion percentage of 58.5 for an average of 5.9 yards per attempt, with just 5 TDs, and allowing 18 sacks, with QB rating on the year of 77.7. As a runner he logged 71 runs for a total of 405 yards at an average of 5.7 per run. After season's end Sims transferred to Nebraska where as a senior Sims saw action in just 3 games as he threw for just 282 yards on 47 attempts with a completion percent of 59.6 for an average of 6.0 yards per pass, with just one TD, while taking 6 sacks, and totaling a QB rating of 44.2. He added 37 runs for a total of 230 yards at an average of 6.2 per run.
strengths: Ball placement is a strength. Sims has the natural instincts to make plays when forced to improvise. He’s an athlete who runs well and must be accounted for in the read-option game. Excellent height to see over the defense. A tall, lean pocket passer with room to add weight, Sims is an aggressive, confident downfield thrower. He’s a pure athlete, loose-limbed and flexible. Sims has the speed to pick up yardage in scramble situations. Sees the field very well when the play breaks down and occasionally changes plays at the line.
weaknesses: His arm strength is decent but less than ideal; Sims lacks a little zip, and accuracy becomes an issue when he’s unable or unwilling to set his feet. Inconsistent downfield passer and puts too much air under his throws. Has struggled with his decision-making and needs to improve his pre-snap recognition skills to read defenses and see blitzes. Doesn't decipher information as quickly as you would like, but does see the entire field and understands coverage.

bio: The journey from Kissimmee to Tallahassee represents a full-circle moment for Wilson, whose nomadic college career tells the story of talent searching for the right system. After earning three-star recognition from all major recruiting services as one of the nation's top-100 cornerbacks in 2022, Wilson began his collegiate path at Syracuse under defensive coordinator Tony White. His freshman campaign showcased remarkable maturity for a true first-year player, appearing in all 13 games while contributing 21 tackles and earning a spot on the ACC Honor Roll. Syracuse became Wilson's proving ground during his sophomore season, where he transitioned from special teams contributor to legitimate starter. His breakout moment arrived against Colgate when he snatched an interception and housed it for his first collegiate touchdown, announcing his arrival as a legitimate playmaker. That 66-yard pick-six highlighted a campaign that saw him start five games while recording 16 tackles and four pass breakups. However, Wilson's most impressive performance came at Purdue, where he dominated with 10 tackles, a tackle for loss, and two pass breakups in a single afternoon that demonstrated his complete skill set. Houston represented Wilson's transfer portal destination following the 2023 season, reuniting him with opportunities in the Big 12. His junior campaign with the Cougars proved transformative, as he started the final eight games and established himself as one of the conference's premier ball hawks. Wilson's four interceptions ranked third in the Big 12, including a spectacular pick-six against Baylor that served as Houston's only defensive touchdown all season. Additional interceptions against Oklahoma and Kansas State proved his ability to perform against elite competition, while his 86.9 PFF grade ranked fourth among all Power Four cornerbacks.
strengths: Ball skills border on elite territory with natural hands and exceptional timing to high-point passes at their apex Route recognition shows advanced development as he consistently jumps underneath patterns before receivers complete their breaks Physical press coverage technique disrupts timing routes while maintaining legal contact throughout the stem Zone coverage instincts allow him to play robber effectively, breaking on throws with conviction and proper angles Competitive fire burns hot in contested situations, refusing to back down from bigger receivers in jump ball scenarios Tackling fundamentals remain sound despite his smaller frame, wrapping up properly and driving through contact Football intelligence translates across multiple defensive schemes, evidenced by seamless transitions between Syracuse and Houston systems Playmaking consistency shows up when games matter most, with interceptions against quality Big 12 quarterbacks proving clutch gene
weaknesses: Size limitations become apparent against physical receivers who can body him off routes and win contested catches Deep speed gets tested by burners, forcing him to rely on technique and anticipation rather than pure acceleration Run support lacks the pop needed to consistently shed blocks from slot receivers and tight ends Press coverage can become grabby when beaten, leading to potential penalty flags in crucial situations Consistency wavers between spectacular plays and mental lapses that leave receivers wide open in coverage

bio: Jermod McCoy burst onto the national scene after transferring from Oregon State to Tennessee, where his ball-hawking skills transformed the Volunteers' secondary. McCoy wasn't exactly a blue-chip recruit coming out of high school - a three-star prospect who excelled as a two-way player and track star in the Lone Star State. His athleticism was never in question (state titles in both long jump and triple jump), but his football instincts took time to develop as he balanced multiple positions and sports. After cutting his teeth as a freshman at Oregon State (31 tackles, 2 INTs in 12 games), McCoy found his true home in Knoxville where he blossomed into one of college football's premier corners. In 2024, his first season with the Vols, he racked up 44 tackles while defending 13 passes and snagging 4 interceptions - three of which came inside the opponent's 3-yard line, showing an uncanny knack for making momentum-shifting plays. The former multi-sport athlete turned heads in the SEC, earning Second-Team All-America honors and finding himself on the Jim Thorpe Award semifinalist list, cementing his status as an elite defensive back. The path was set for McCoy to build on his breakthrough campaign in 2025, but a torn ACL suffered during an offseason training session in January has temporarily derailed his ascent. The injury comes at a critical juncture for the Volunteers' defense, as coaches have already noted his development as a vocal leader during rehabilitation. Before the setback, McCoy had established himself as a versatile corner with exceptional ball skills and coverage instincts that translated to an elite 97.2% overall coverage grade - the kind of production that has NFL scouts salivating despite the injury concern.
strengths: Elite ball skills and instincts that translate to turnovers - shows rare spatial awareness to track and high-point passes while maintaining body control through contact. Exceptional timing in zone coverage, baits quarterbacks into dangerous throws by appearing to give cushion before closing with explosive burst at perfect moment. Shows advanced route recognition beyond his years, rarely fooled by double moves or misdirection - processes route combinations quickly in real time. Natural fluidity in his transitions that allows him to mirror receivers through breaks without losing stride or position leverage. Brings real dog to the field in contested catch situations - fights through the catch point with violence and doesn't concede anything even against bigger receivers. Displays clinical technique in press coverage, using well-timed jams that disrupt timing without overextending and compromising his position. Excels at disguising coverage intentions pre-snap, manipulates quarterback reads by selling false leverage before flipping his hips at the last second. Brings added value in return game with natural vision and acceleration - averaged 18.8 yards per punt return in high school and has shown flashes at Tennessee.
weaknesses: January 2025 ACL tear creates medical flag and potential timeline questions for his rookie season - recovery progress will be heavily scrutinized. Can occasionally get grabby downfield when beaten, showing inconsistent trust in his recovery speed against vertical routes. Needs to improve tackling form in space - sometimes leads with shoulder instead of wrapping up, resulting in missed opportunities against elusive ball carriers. Run support engagement can be inconsistent - occasionally takes conservative angles rather than attacking downhill with the same aggression he shows in coverage. Despite multi-sport background, doesn't consistently translate his track explosiveness to football movements, showing good but not elite combine-style measurables.
player_comparisons: Kool-AidMcKinstry - Alabama (87%), TreyAmos - Ole Miss (79%), RogerMcCreary - Auburn (79%)

Jermod McCoy burst onto the national scene after transferring from Oregon State to Tennessee, where his ball-hawking skills transformed the Volunteers' secondary. McCoy wasn't exactly a blue-chip recruit coming out of high school - a three-star prospect who excelled as a two-way player and track star in the Lone Star State. His athleticism was never in question (state titles in both long jump and triple jump), but his football instincts took time to develop as he balanced multiple positions and sports. After cutting his teeth as a freshman at Oregon State (31 tackles, 2 INTs in 12 games), McCoy found his true home in Knoxville where he blossomed into one of college football's premier corners. In 2024, his first season with the Vols, he racked up 44 tackles while defending 13 passes and snagging 4 interceptions - three of which came inside the opponent's 3-yard line, showing an uncanny knack for making momentum-shifting plays. The former multi-sport athlete turned heads in the SEC, earning Second-Team All-America honors and finding himself on the Jim Thorpe Award semifinalist list, cementing his status as an elite defensive back. The path was set for McCoy to build on his breakthrough campaign in 2025, but a torn ACL suffered during an offseason training session in January has temporarily derailed his ascent. The injury comes at a critical juncture for the Volunteers' defense, as coaches have already noted his development as a vocal leader during rehabilitation. Before the setback, McCoy had established himself as a versatile corner with exceptional ball skills and coverage instincts that translated to an elite 97.2% overall coverage grade - the kind of production that has NFL scouts salivating despite the injury concern.

Strengths:
Elite ball skills and instincts that translate to turnovers - shows rare spatial awareness to track and high-point passes while maintaining body control through contact. Exceptional timing in zone coverage, baits quarterbacks into dangerous throws by appearing to give cushion before closing with explosive burst at perfect moment. Shows advanced route recognition beyond his years, rarely fooled by double moves or misdirection - processes route combinations quickly in real time. Natural fluidity in his transitions that allows him to mirror receivers through breaks without losing stride or position leverage. Brings real dog to the field in contested catch situations - fights through the catch point with violence and doesn't concede anything even against bigger receivers. Displays clinical technique in press coverage, using well-timed jams that disrupt timing without overextending and compromising his position. Excels at disguising coverage intentions pre-snap, manipulates quarterback reads by selling false leverage before flipping his hips at the last second. Brings added value in return game with natural vision and acceleration - averaged 18.8 yards per punt return in high school and has shown flashes at Tennessee.

Weaknesses:
January 2025 ACL tear creates medical flag and potential timeline questions for his rookie season - recovery progress will be heavily scrutinized. Can occasionally get grabby downfield when beaten, showing inconsistent trust in his recovery speed against vertical routes. Needs to improve tackling form in space - sometimes leads with shoulder instead of wrapping up, resulting in missed opportunities against elusive ball carriers. Run support engagement can be inconsistent - occasionally takes conservative angles rather than attacking downhill with the same aggression he shows in coverage. Despite multi-sport background, doesn't consistently translate his track explosiveness to football movements, showing good but not elite combine-style measurables.
