bio: Jimmy Rolder was a standout third baseman at Marist High School with Division I baseball offers on the table and a verbal commitment to play ball at Illinois. But football kept pulling at him. His stepfather, Scott Kehoe, had played at the University of Illinois and spent time with the Miami Dolphins, so the game was in the bloodline. When Illinois came calling with a football offer late in the recruiting process, the dominoes started falling fast. Within weeks, programs like Ohio State, Michigan, and Minnesota were on the phone. Rolder chose Ann Arbor, and Marist lost a pretty good third baseman. The recruiting services saw something in Rolder before he ever put on the winged helmet. He earned four-star ratings from 247Sports, Rivals, and ESPN, slotted as the No. 279 overall prospect nationally and a top-30 linebacker by the composite rankings. Rivals pegged him even higher at No. 183 overall and the seventh-best inside linebacker in the country. Back at Marist under coach Ron Dawczak, Rolder had racked up 115 tackles, 13 tackles for loss, four sacks, three interceptions, and two forced fumbles as a senior while helping the RedHawks to a 9-4 record and a Class 8A state semifinal berth. He was a Dick Butkus Award semifinalist and an IHSFCA All-State selection. The production and the pedigree were both there. Rolder's Michigan career required patience. He contributed on special teams as a true freshman in 2022, playing in all 13 games and earning shared Special Teams Freshman of the Year honors. An injury limited him to just six games during the Wolverines' 2023 national championship run, and while he appeared in 12 games as a junior in 2024, he was still working behind established starters and logged only 26 tackles. The breakout finally came in 2025 when he locked down a starting job and led Michigan with 73 tackles, 7.0 for loss, 2.0 sacks, an interception, and a fumble recovery across 12 games and 11 starts. That performance earned him second-team All-Big Ten honors from both the coaches and media, plus a Big Ten Defensive Player of the Week nod after a dominant showing at Michigan State where he tallied 10 tackles, two TFLs, a sack, a fumble recovery, and a pass breakup. He was a three-time Academic All-Big Ten honoree and a four-time letterman before declaring for the draft in January 2026.
strengths: Plays with a low center of gravity and anchors against blocks with a stout base that belies average measurables for the position. His tackling technique is borderline textbook; wraps through contact every single time and almost never lets a ball carrier wriggle free on film. Reads his keys quickly in the run game and triggers downhill with conviction, rarely caught flat-footed or hesitating at the second level. Violent hands at the point of attack; jolts offensive linemen on contact and creates separation to disengage and make plays in the hole. Navigates trash and traffic in the box like a veteran, finding creases through congestion to tighten the alley on ball carriers. Against the run, he consistently fits his gap, stacks blocks at the point of attack, and forces runners to bounce into less favorable lanes. His Michigan State game was a masterclass in linebacker play: 10 tackles, two TFLs, a sack, a fumble recovery, and he looked like the best player on the field. Penalty-free career at Michigan speaks to discipline and body control in tight spaces where less composed linebackers pick up flags.
weaknesses: Only 11 career starts across four seasons raises real questions about why coaches didn't trust him with more playing time sooner. When runs bounce to the perimeter, his lateral agility and pursuit speed strain to keep contain, and he loses angles too often. Coverage is a clear limitation; receivers and tight ends found soft spots around him at a high clip when targeted in his zone. Pass rush is more effort than impact, lacking a go-to move or the burst off the edge to threaten quarterbacks with any consistency. Against climbing offensive linemen who get into his frame at the second level, he can get washed out of the play when he fails to shed early.
player_comparisons: TommyEichenberg - Ohio State (89%), OluwafemiOladejo - UCLA (83%), JackKiser - Notre Dame (81%)

bio: Born in Ohio and raised in the Cincinnati area, Joe Royer has come full circle in his collegiate journey. The 6'5", 255-pound tight end from Elder High School began his career at Ohio State where he appeared in 14 games over three seasons before transferring back to his hometown Cincinnati Bearcats. His return home proved transformational as Royer exploded onto the national scene in 2024, breaking the Cincinnati single-season reception record for tight ends with 50 catches - surpassing the previous mark set by future Hall of Famer Travis Kelce (45). Royer's journey has been marked by perseverance through personal struggles, including the devastating loss of his mother during his time at Ohio State. His return to Cincinnati represented more than just a football decision - it was a homecoming that rekindled his passion for the game. Under the guidance of tight ends coach Jordan Stepp, Royer found his footing again, crediting the coach with "helping save his football career" after he "lost the love of the game for a while." The former consensus four-star recruit and top-10 player in Ohio has flourished in Cincinnati's tight-end heavy offense, earning All-Big 12 First Team honors in 2024. Despite having opportunities to enter the 2025 NFL Draft with invitations to the Shrine Bowl and Senior Bowl, Royer elected to return for his final season at Nippert Stadium with the goal of being the first tight end selected in the 2026 NFL Draft. His decision speaks to his commitment to development and his belief that his best football is still ahead of him.
strengths: Shows exceptional body control and spatial awareness, adjusting to poorly thrown balls with natural fluidity rarely seen in bigger tight ends. Displays soft, reliable hands with a catch radius that makes him a quarterback's best friend in tight windows and contested situations. Creates separation on intermediate routes with deceptive acceleration and subtle head fakes that consistently move linebackers off their spots. Runs angry after the catch, showing a nasty competitive streak when fighting through arm tackles and working for extra yardage. Exhibits excellent football intelligence, finding soft spots in zone coverage and presenting a clean target with perfect timing. Possesses impressive athletic testing numbers that translate to game speed, with a 4.65 forty time that shows up on film. Demonstrates patient route discipline, allowing plays to develop before hitting his breaks with precise timing and footwork. Shows tremendous growth trajectory with significant year-over-year improvement, suggesting his ceiling remains untapped.
weaknesses: Still developing as a blocker with inconsistent hand placement and leverage that limits his effectiveness at the point of attack. Shows occasional lapses in concentration, as evidenced by his fumble in the endzone against Miami (Ohio) when fighting for extra yardage. Could improve functional strength, particularly in his lower body, to better anchor against edge defenders in the run game. Sometimes rounds off his cuts on breaking routes, telegraphing his intentions to savvy defensive backs. Must prove durability over a full NFL season given his limited playing time during three seasons at Ohio State.
player_comparisons: JeremyRuckert - Ohio State (95%), TannerMcLachlan - Arizona (85%), LukeSchoonmaker - Michigan (84%)

bio: Gyllenborg didn't pick up a football until his senior year at Rockhurst High School in Kansas City, spending his earlier prep years focused on basketball, where he earned All-District honors as a junior. That late start made his recruitment a modest affair: a two-star rating from Rivals, a three-star from 247 Sports, and a ranking outside the top 2,000 nationally. Wyoming and Columbia were his primary options, and he chose the Cowboys. After a redshirt season in 2021 and a limited role as a sophomore in 2022, Gyllenborg began making noise in 2023. Playing all 13 games with four starts, he hauled in 23 catches for 360 yards and three touchdowns, including an 89-yard scoring strike against Hawaii that marked Wyoming's longest passing play of the season. A high ankle sprain suffered in 2024 fall camp cost him three games and slowed his return, but he still led the Cowboys in receiving yards per game at 47.2 and earned Second Team All-Mountain West honors as the conference's highest-graded tight end. Gyllenborg finished his Wyoming career with 80 receptions for 1,023 yards and seven touchdowns across 33 games. He competed at the Senior Bowl and turned heads at the Combine, landing on the NFL's All-Combine Team at tight end.
strengths: Rare burst off the line for a tight end; acceleration eats up cushion in a hurry. Stacks linebackers on seam routes before they can recover their leverage. Creates easy separation on over routes where his speed advantage is most obvious. Smooth hip sink and quick feet at the top of stems allow sharp vertical cuts. Natural hands catcher who completes grabs through contact at the second level. Alignment flexibility: takes snaps in-line, in the slot, and at H-back. Combine performance confirmed the athletic testing pops you see on tape. Special teams experience on four units gives him an early NFL roster path.
weaknesses: Run blocking fell off noticeably from 2023 into his final two seasons. Rounds his in-breaking and out-breaking routes, giving defenders time to recover. Absorbs unnecessary contact early in routes by drifting into coverage. Ball tracking and focus waver near the catch point, leading to avoidable drops. Goes down on first contact too often; limited elusiveness after the catch. Pass protection technique is unreliable, with inconsistent hand placement and anchor.
player_comparisons: LucasKrull - Pittsburgh (82%), LeonardTaylor - Cincinnati (80%), JoshWhyle - Cincinnati (76%)

bio: Jonathan Jefferson from Douglas County High School was rated a 4-star recruit by ESPN and handed a 4-star grade by 247 Sports. After high school, Jefferson joined Georgia after being heavily recruited. In 2021 as a freshman Jefferson saw action in just one game and played a total of 20 snaps for the Bulldogs. He recorded just 3 tackles, one assist, while adding 2 stops. As a sophomore for the Bulldogs in 2022 Jefferson played in 4 games and took part in 24 snaps for the Bulldogs. He recorded just 2 tackles, no assists, while adding one stop. In 2023 as a junior Jefferson played in 7 games and contributed on 67 snaps. He recorded just one tackle, no assists while making one stop. As a pass rusher, he produced 3 total pressures, which included 3 QB hurries, no QB hits, and no sacks on the year.
strengths: He’s a high-effort pass rusher who shows good initial quickness and the ability to beat blockers to the inside. He’s quick to react and locate the ball, and Jefferson shows enough speed to succeed in backside pursuit. Jefferson plays with excellent balance and a strong, flexible core, rarely ending up on the ground. Rarely goes to the ground, showing good balance from his high school wrestling days. Agile run-stopper at end, extends to grab backs from the backside and possesses foot quickness and hustle to chase plays downfield. or to the sideline. His spin move is already NFL-caliber, and he displays a very good motor. Jefferson has an excellent motor and shows the stamina to last deep into the fourth quarter.
weaknesses: Jefferson doesn’t have a lot of suddenness to his game, and Jefferson won’t wreck plays on a regular basis. He’s very much a work in progress with his hand in the dirt. Jefferson doesn’t play with ideal physicality, often failing to set the edge as a run defender. While he moves well laterally, he doesn’t always get there in pursuit.

bio: Jordan White, a center from Liberty, has steadily climbed the ranks of college football. Born and raised in Maryland, White made his mark at DeMatha Catholic High School in Hyattsville, earning All-WCAC Capital Division First Team honors and participating in the prestigious Maryland Crab Bowl. His high school performance caught the attention of several top programs, ultimately leading to his commitment to West Virginia. At West Virginia, White saw limited action, appearing in 11 games over three seasons with 328 offensive snaps. His breakthrough came after transferring to Liberty for the 2023 season. As a redshirt sophomore, White started all 14 games at center, anchoring an offensive line that powered Liberty to the nation's top rushing offense at 293.3 yards per game. White's junior year at Liberty has been marked by preseason accolades, including spots on the Outland Trophy and Rimington Trophy watch lists. His sophomore campaign earned him Conference USA All-Conference second team honors and a place on the Phil Steele Postseason Conference USA All-Conference first team. White's durability and consistency were evident as he played 945 of 1,008 offensive snaps, ranking third on the team in total offensive snaps.
strengths: Iron man durability, rarely misses snaps and maintains performance deep into the fourth quarter. Road grader in the run game, consistently moves the pile and creates lanes for ball carriers. Plus mobility when pulling, shows good hip flip and foot speed to reach the second level. Stout anchor in pass pro, sets a wide base and absorbs bull rushes effectively. Executes combo blocks with precision, works well in tandem to control the point of attack. Gamer who elevates play in the red zone and on crucial downs. Key cog in a dominant ground attack, consistently opens holes for chunk plays. Position flexible, can moonlight at guard in a pinch.
weaknesses: Undersized for the position, may struggle against NFL nose tackles with superior length and mass. Hands tend to land outside the frame, leading to potential holding calls at the next level. Limited counters in his toolbox, can be overwhelmed by savvy interior rushers with multiple moves. Lacks ideal lateral agility, vulnerable to quick swim moves and cross-face techniques. Frame maxed out, unlikely to add significant functional strength without sacrificing mobility.
player_comparisons: DrakeNugent - Michigan (82%), DylanMcMahon - NC State (79%), JuiceScruggs - Penn State (78%)

bio: Josh Cameron walked into Baylor as a zero-star recruit out of Cedar Park High School in Texas, where he had racked up back-to-back seasons of 80-plus catches and 14 touchdowns while leading his team to a state championship appearance in 2020. Nobody in the recruiting world was paying attention. He redshirted in 2021, saw the field only in the final three games, and didn't record a single stat. Nothing about his freshman year suggested what was coming. By 2022, Cameron had carved out a real role, starting four games and finishing with 28 catches for 386 yards as a redshirt freshman. His production tailed off in 2023 with just 21 catches and 222 yards across 12 appearances, though he made noise as a punt returner, ripping off a 70-yard return that ranked among the Big 12's longest on the year. The receiving numbers were modest, the touchdowns nonexistent, but Cameron was building something. He was learning how to use his body, developing the physicality that would later define his game. Everything came together in 2024. Cameron started all 13 games, led the Bears with 52 catches for 750 yards and 10 touchdowns, and earned second-team All-America honors as a punt returner from CBS Sports and the FWAA. His 20.7-yard punt return average was so dangerous that the last four opponents on the schedule refused to kick to him. He followed that up in 2025 with 69 catches for 872 yards and nine scores, landing on the Biletnikoff Award Watch List and earning preseason All-America recognition. Over five seasons in Waco, Cameron finished with 170 career receptions for 2,236 yards and 19 touchdowns, a remarkable arc for a kid nobody recruited.
strengths: Built like a bowling ball at 224 pounds, and he plays like one too. Defenders bounce off him at the catch point and after it. Hands are among the most trustworthy in this class. Seven drops on 261 career targets tells the whole story right there. Wins contested catches with body positioning and late hands, attacking the ball at its highest point on fades and back-shoulders like a power forward boxing out. After the catch, he turns into a running back. Thick legs churn through arm tackles and he pinballs off contact to extend plays. Reads zone coverage well, settling into soft spots and presenting himself as a target. Quarterbacks always know where to find him. Red zone weapon who owns the fade route. Nine touchdowns in 2025 and ten in 2024 weren't an accident; he physically dominates smaller corners in tight spaces. Alignment flexibility lets him operate outside at the X or slide into the slot, where his density creates mismatches against nickel corners and linebackers. Proven punt return ability adds special teams value from day one. His 20.7-yard average in 2024 was so electric that opponents stopped punting to him entirely.
weaknesses: Bottom-of-the-barrel speed for the position. His long speed simply won't scare NFL corners into playing off, and that limits his route tree. Release at the line is predictable and lacks violence. Patient press corners with length can wall him off and throw his timing into chaos. Gets upright too early coming out of breaks, which telegraphs his direction and lets defensive backs close the gap before the ball arrives. Footwork at the top of routes is sloppy. Extra steps and wasted motion kill whatever small separation window he's created on the stem. Baylor's offensive scheme asked relatively simple things of him. The jump to a full NFL route tree with more complex concepts will be a steeper climb than most.
player_comparisons: PatBryant - Illinois (78%), TheoWease Jr. - Missouri (74%), SageSurratt - Wake Forest (73%)

bio: Josh Moten came out of National Christian Academy in Waldorf, Maryland as a four-star recruit with offers from Michigan, Oregon, Oklahoma, and Penn State, among others. He signed with Texas A&M in the Class of 2020 but saw only 15 defensive snaps across three seasons before entering the transfer portal after the 2022 campaign. At Marshall, Moten started all 13 games as a sophomore in 2023, recording 30 tackles, a pick-six against NC State, and six pass breakups. His junior year was a clear step forward. He logged five interceptions across 12 starts, tying for third nationally, and made a diving pick in Marshall's Sun Belt Championship win over Louisiana. He also posted five tackles and an interception against eventual national champion Ohio State and All-American receiver Jeremiah Smith. That season earned him HERO G5 All-American and All-SBC honorable mention recognition. Moten followed head coach Charles Huff to Southern Miss for his senior year in 2025, where he again produced five interceptions across 13 games, including a three-pick performance against Arkansas State that earned him Sun Belt Defensive Player of the Week. He also returned an interception 99 yards for a touchdown. Moten was named to the Bednarik Award watch list and led a Southern Miss defense that finished first nationally in interceptions with 23. The Golden Eagles went 7-6, falling to Western Kentucky in the New Orleans Bowl. Moten was not among the prospects invited to the NFL Combine.
strengths: Eyes stay glued to the quarterback in zone, reading windows and jumping routes before the ball arrives. Tracks the ball over his shoulder naturally on deep throws without drifting or losing stride. Fluid hips let him mirror receivers through breaks without false stepping or opening up early. Showed he belongs against top talent, matching up with Jeremiah Smith and coming away with a pick. Reliable tackler who wraps up in space; the whiff issues from 2023 are mostly cleaned up on recent film. Plays both sides of the field and can handle slot reps, giving coordinators flexibility in alignment. Good foot speed to carry vertical stems and close on underneath stuff when he reads it.
weaknesses: Thin frame gets moved around in run support; struggles to stack and shed when blockers find him. Bigger receivers bully him at the catch point on contested throws, especially on back-shoulder fades. Gets grabby when he feels a receiver gaining separation, and it showed in the penalty numbers in 2025. Can get caught peeking into the backfield on play-action and lose his man on crossers behind him. Gave up more completions in 2025, including a rough outing against Louisiana-Monroe where he looked off. Sun Belt tape only tells you so much; the Ohio State game was encouraging, but it was one game.

bio: Josiah Trotter is the son of Jeremiah Trotter Sr., a four-time Pro Bowler and two-time All-Pro linebacker for the Philadelphia Eagles. His older brother, Jeremiah Jr., won a Super Bowl with those same Eagles after back-to-back All-American honors at Clemson. Josiah starred at St. Joseph's Prep in Philadelphia, helping the program win two PIAA Class 6A state titles and earning 2021 All-Eastern PA Co-Player of the Year. He was rated a four-star prospect by ESPN and Rivals. Trotter signed with West Virginia but a lower leg injury cost him the entire 2023 season. He posted 92 tackles, 4.0 tackles for loss, and an interception as a redshirt freshman in 2024, earning Big 12 Defensive Freshman of the Year. He transferred to Missouri and took over as the starting middle linebacker. He led the Tigers with 84 tackles in 2025, added 13.0 tackles for loss and two sacks, and anchored a defense that ranked 10th nationally. He earned First-Team All-SEC from the coaches and Second-Team from the AP. His 11-tackle game against South Carolina came when Missouri held the Gamecocks to minus-9 rushing yards, and he closed with 10 tackles and two sacks against Arkansas.
strengths: Run defense is his calling card; erases rushing lanes before they develop on a consistent basis. Processes run schemes quickly, diagnosing zone and gap concepts before the ball is snapped. Heavy hands at the point of attack that generate separation and knock linemen off their tracks. Lower body strength lets him hold ground against double teams and stay square through contact. Quick first step to shoot gaps and arrive in the backfield before backs can redirect. Flexible hips help him squeeze through traffic and redirect when plays bounce outside. Reliable wrap-up tackler who finishes with authority and rarely gives up yards after contact. Shows real pass rush ability on delayed blitzes and zone dogs with his hand work.
weaknesses: Arm length measures short for the position, which could limit block control at the next level. Lacks sideline-to-sideline range; more explosive in short areas than fast across the field. Coverage is a clear weakness; slow to recognize routes and gets stacked by athletic tight ends. Lateral movement can look labored when asked to mirror receivers or backs in space. The 2023 lower leg injury that cost a full season will draw medical attention before boards finalize.
player_comparisons: ChrisPaul Jr. - Ole Miss (79%), DemetriusKnight Jr. - South Carolina (76%), JackKiser - Notre Dame (75%)

bio: Jude Bowry came to Boston College from St. Frances Academy in Maryland, a program that had claimed five consecutive state No. 1 rankings by the time he graduated. A three-star recruit rated as the top offensive tackle in the state by Rivals, Bowry saw the field early, appearing in seven games as a true freshman in 2022 with two starts at right tackle. He preserved his redshirt the following year while logging 89 snaps across three appearances, including 71 at left tackle against Florida State when the Eagles needed a fill-in up front. The 2024 season is where Bowry carved out his role. He started all 12 games at left tackle, allowed just two sacks in pass protection, and helped anchor a line that produced four games of 200-plus rushing yards. That performance earned him a captaincy heading into 2025, though his redshirt junior year was interrupted by multiple injuries that held him to nine starts. When healthy, he remained the starter at left tackle, blocking for an offense that topped 500 total yards twice and consistently moved the football on the ground.
strengths: Natural kick-slide explosiveness creates a wide pocket arc that speed rushers struggle to bend around Stays square through his kick-slide and maintains good base width. Combine testing confirmed rare lower-body power for the position. Firm anchor when hands land inside, able to stall power moves. Shows range and fluidity to widen speed rushers off the edge. Good burst on the move for screens, pulls, and second-level climbs. Generates force on down blocks and can displace defenders at the point of attack. Made the 2025 Feldman freaks list - he wrote:"In 2024, Bowry allowed only two sacks in 321 dropback snaps. Bowry’s athleticism has really impressed the staff; he achieved a peak velocity of 1.18 meters per second and a peak power of 2,610 watts while performing multiple sets of five reps on back squats with 435 pounds. "
weaknesses: Below-average arm length creates tight margins in pass protection. Punch timing is erratic and hand placement often drifts wide. Gets pushed into the pocket by speed-to-power conversions. Passive on angle-drive blocks, settling for position rather than displacing. Inconsistent pad level leaves him upright and vulnerable to counter moves. Durability question after missing multiple games in 2025 due to injury.
player_comparisons: CaedanWallace - Penn State (99%), TylerSteen - Alabama (90%), RasheedWalker - Penn State (90%)

bio: San Francisco's Mission District doesn't produce many Division I football prospects, but Neal was a multi-sport standout at Mission High School who earned All-City honors in both football and basketball while playing receiver, defensive end, and defensive back. The recruiting attention never matched the production: he held a three-star rating from ESPN and 247Sports, two stars from Rivals, and his best offers came from Eastern Washington and San Jose State. He signed with Fresno State and redshirted as a freshman in 2021. Neal barely played in 2022 and logged just 116 defensive snaps across 11 games as a redshirt sophomore in 2023, recording nine tackles. The 2024 season changed his trajectory. He appeared in all 12 games with four starts, finishing with 35 tackles, five tackles for loss, a sack, two interceptions, and six pass breakups. After briefly committing to Stanford through the transfer portal, he wound up at Arkansas instead. The move to Fayetteville brought career-best numbers. Neal started all 12 games as a redshirt senior, posting 55 tackles, two interceptions, and ten pass breakups. His 11-tackle, one-interception, two-PBU outing against Arkansas State made him just the third Razorback defensive back since 1997 to hit that statistical combination in a single game.
strengths: Ideal boundary corner frame with long arms that contest throws at the high point. Run defense is the calling card; fills gaps and takes on blocks willingly. Tackling improved sharply in 2025, finishing through contact with few misses. Reads the quarterback's eyes well in zone and jumps routes with good timing. Length squeezes throwing windows and forces contested catches underneath. Tested as an above-average athlete vertically, and the explosiveness shows on tape. Lined up at corner, in the slot, and in the box at Arkansas, giving coordinators options.
weaknesses: Long speed is average, and it shows when isolated on vertical routes. Hips get tight in transitions, opening windows on double moves and comebacks. Three-cone time confirms the change-of-direction stiffness visible on film. Press technique is inconsistent; gives up clean releases too often at the line. Bites on play-action and loses his receiver when his eyes drift to the backfield. Allowed a higher completion rate in SEC play despite solid overall coverage marks.
player_comparisons: JuliusBrents - Kansas State (91%), KhyreeJackson - Oregon (86%), MartinEmerson - Mississippi State (85%)

bio: Before he ever set foot in Tuscaloosa, Justin Jefferson was already turning heads on the junior college circuit. A Memphis product out of Bartlett High School, Jefferson racked up 116 tackles and two interceptions as a prep senior before heading to Pearl River Community College in Mississippi. He wasted no time making noise there either, posting 57 stops with 13 tackles for loss and three sacks as a freshman, then following that up with 86 tackles, two sacks, and a pick as a sophomore. By the time the 2023 recruiting cycle rolled around, Jefferson was the consensus No. 1 JUCO linebacker in the country and the No. 2 overall junior college prospect, drawing offers from Auburn, Florida, Oregon, and Texas A&M before ultimately choosing Alabama. Jefferson's first year in Tuscaloosa was a redshirt-style campaign in every way but the official designation. He appeared in all 14 games during the 2023 season, but his defensive snaps were almost nonexistent. His real contributions came on special teams, where he flashed the kind of explosive athleticism that made him such a coveted recruit, including a massive forced fumble against Ole Miss on coverage. The 2024 season brought a much bigger role. Jefferson worked his way into a rotational spot and earned three starts across 12 games, piling up 60 tackles with 6.5 for loss, two sacks, a forced fumble, and a pass breakup. The production was solid, but it was clear he was still learning the nuances of playing linebacker at the SEC level. Everything came together in 2025. Jefferson locked down the starting middle linebacker job and became one of the anchors of Alabama's defense. He finished second on the team with 85 tackles, added 6.5 tackles for loss, three sacks, five pass breakups, an interception, and a forced fumble across 15 games. His dominant performance against Vanderbilt, where he posted 14 tackles, a sack, and a forced fumble, earned him SEC Defensive Player of the Week honors, along with Bednarik Award Player of the Week and Senior Bowl Defensive Player of the Week recognition. He also landed on the watch list for the Butkus Award, given annually to the nation's best linebacker.
strengths: Rare closing speed for a linebacker, consistently turning free rushes into sacks and chasing down ball carriers from the backside with legitimate 4.4 wheels. Smooth mover in zone coverage who gets to his landmarks quickly and showed real improvement contesting throws, including five pass breakups and a pick in 2025. Wrap-up tackler who rarely whiffs. His missed tackle rate dropped significantly from his first year as a rotational piece to his starting campaign, and it shows on tape. Lateral agility and change-of-direction burst allow him to mirror tight ends in man coverage, which is increasingly valuable in today's pass-heavy NFL offenses. Reads and reacts to run schemes quickly, beating offensive linemen to the point of attack on inside zone and showing the instincts to sniff out misdirection plays before they develop. Proven special teams contributor who has been a mainstay on kick and punt coverage units since day one at Alabama, with the hustle and fearlessness that coaches crave on those units. Shows pop as a hitter despite his frame, delivering contact at the catch point to jar balls loose and force incompletions when he arrives on time in coverage. Capable of spying mobile quarterbacks thanks to his combination of burst, acceleration, and the discipline to stay home before triggering downhill when the QB takes off.
weaknesses: Light frame at 227 pounds sits well below the threshold for NFL linebackers, and it shows when fullbacks and pulling guards get hands on him at the second level. Struggles to anchor against blocks from tight ends and offensive linemen, frequently getting pushed off his spot and losing his gap responsibility on power runs. Pursuit angles need refinement, particularly when plays bounce outside. He tends to overrun the play or take too deep a path to the sideline on stretch concepts. Zone spacing can be inconsistent, drifting too far from his landmark or sitting too shallow to squeeze crossers, occasionally leaving soft spots for opposing quarterbacks to exploit. No discernible pass-rush plan as a blitzer beyond winning as a free runner. When he has to actually defeat a block to get home, the toolbox is empty right now.
player_comparisons: BrianAsamoah - Oklahoma (84%), SteeleChambers - Ohio State (84%), SirVoceaDennis - Pittsburgh (83%)

Before he ever set foot in Tuscaloosa, Justin Jefferson was already turning heads on the junior college circuit. A Memphis product out of Bartlett High School, Jefferson racked up 116 tackles and two interceptions as a prep senior before heading to Pearl River Community College in Mississippi. He wasted no time making noise there either, posting 57 stops with 13 tackles for loss and three sacks as a freshman, then following that up with 86 tackles, two sacks, and a pick as a sophomore. By the time the 2023 recruiting cycle rolled around, Jefferson was the consensus No. 1 JUCO linebacker in the country and the No. 2 overall junior college prospect, drawing offers from Auburn, Florida, Oregon, and Texas A&M before ultimately choosing Alabama. Jefferson's first year in Tuscaloosa was a redshirt-style campaign in every way but the official designation. He appeared in all 14 games during the 2023 season, but his defensive snaps were almost nonexistent. His real contributions came on special teams, where he flashed the kind of explosive athleticism that made him such a coveted recruit, including a massive forced fumble against Ole Miss on coverage. The 2024 season brought a much bigger role. Jefferson worked his way into a rotational spot and earned three starts across 12 games, piling up 60 tackles with 6.5 for loss, two sacks, a forced fumble, and a pass breakup. The production was solid, but it was clear he was still learning the nuances of playing linebacker at the SEC level. Everything came together in 2025. Jefferson locked down the starting middle linebacker job and became one of the anchors of Alabama's defense. He finished second on the team with 85 tackles, added 6.5 tackles for loss, three sacks, five pass breakups, an interception, and a forced fumble across 15 games. His dominant performance against Vanderbilt, where he posted 14 tackles, a sack, and a forced fumble, earned him SEC Defensive Player of the Week honors, along with Bednarik Award Player of the Week and Senior Bowl Defensive Player of the Week recognition. He also landed on the watch list for the Butkus Award, given annually to the nation's best linebacker.

Strengths:
Rare closing speed for a linebacker, consistently turning free rushes into sacks and chasing down ball carriers from the backside with legitimate 4.4 wheels. Smooth mover in zone coverage who gets to his landmarks quickly and showed real improvement contesting throws, including five pass breakups and a pick in 2025. Wrap-up tackler who rarely whiffs. His missed tackle rate dropped significantly from his first year as a rotational piece to his starting campaign, and it shows on tape. Lateral agility and change-of-direction burst allow him to mirror tight ends in man coverage, which is increasingly valuable in today's pass-heavy NFL offenses. Reads and reacts to run schemes quickly, beating offensive linemen to the point of attack on inside zone and showing the instincts to sniff out misdirection plays before they develop. Proven special teams contributor who has been a mainstay on kick and punt coverage units since day one at Alabama, with the hustle and fearlessness that coaches crave on those units. Shows pop as a hitter despite his frame, delivering contact at the catch point to jar balls loose and force incompletions when he arrives on time in coverage. Capable of spying mobile quarterbacks thanks to his combination of burst, acceleration, and the discipline to stay home before triggering downhill when the QB takes off.

Weaknesses:
Light frame at 227 pounds sits well below the threshold for NFL linebackers, and it shows when fullbacks and pulling guards get hands on him at the second level. Struggles to anchor against blocks from tight ends and offensive linemen, frequently getting pushed off his spot and losing his gap responsibility on power runs. Pursuit angles need refinement, particularly when plays bounce outside. He tends to overrun the play or take too deep a path to the sideline on stretch concepts. Zone spacing can be inconsistent, drifting too far from his landmark or sitting too shallow to squeeze crossers, occasionally leaving soft spots for opposing quarterbacks to exploit. No discernible pass-rush plan as a blitzer beyond winning as a free runner. When he has to actually defeat a block to get home, the toolbox is empty right now.

bio: Justin Joly played wide receiver, running back, linebacker and defensive end at Iona Prep in New Rochelle, N.Y., where he piled up 123 catches for 1,827 yards and 22 touchdowns across three varsity seasons while helping his team post an 11-1 record as a senior. Lightly recruited as a two-star prospect, he signed with UConn and converted to tight end for the first time as a true freshman in 2022. Joly caught 18 passes for 250 yards and two touchdowns in his first college season before breaking out as a sophomore with 56 receptions for 586 yards. He transferred to NC State in January 2024 and made an immediate impact, finishing with 43 catches for 661 yards and four touchdowns while averaging 15.4 yards per reception, fourth among all FBS tight ends. He was named to the Mackey Award preseason watch list ahead of that first season in Raleigh. As a senior in 2025, Joly posted career highs in receptions (49) and touchdowns (seven, tying the NC State program record) and earned first-team All-ACC honors. He capped his college career by winning Tight End MVP at the Senior Bowl.
strengths: Outstanding hands and catch radius; drops fell steadily each year in college. Wins contested catches at a high rate with strong body control through contact. Deep and intermediate receiving grades are among the best in this TE class. Smooth athlete who can run seams and force safeties to respect his vertical ability. Lines up everywhere: inline, slot, wide, backfield, giving coordinators flexibility. Subtle route craft at stems, using stutter-steps and tempo changes to uncover. Surprisingly clean pass protection; allowed almost nothing across four seasons. Red zone producer with eleven touchdowns over his final two college seasons.
weaknesses: Undersized frame for the position; light and short by NFL tight end standards. Run blocking lacks push and consistent effort; more wall-off than drive blocker. Pad level runs high and base gets wide in blocking engagements. Yards after catch and explosiveness declined noticeably from 2024 to 2025. Drifts on route breaks at times, giving defenders a window to undercut. Doesn't always extend hands away from frame to meet the football cleanly.
player_comparisons: IsaiahLikely - Coastal Carolina (95%), ElijahArroyo - Miami (94%), CadeStover - Ohio State (82%)

bio: Ranked as the top offensive tackle in the 2023 class, Kadyn Proctor was a consensus five-star prospect out of Southeast Polk High School in Iowa, where he led the team to a Class 5A state title and placed fifth at the state track meet in shot put. He committed to Iowa before flipping to Alabama on signing day and started all 13 games at left tackle as a true freshman, earning All-SEC Freshman Team honors. After Nick Saban retired, Proctor briefly transferred back to Iowa but returned to Tuscaloosa within three months. His 2025 junior season was his best work. Proctor started 12 games while playing through a torn labrum, earning First-Team All-SEC honors and co-winning the Jacobs Blocking Trophy. He was a consensus All-American and finished second on Bruce Feldman's Freaks List: 815-pound squat, 535-pound bench, 405-pound power clean. Alabama handed him the ball five times during the year as a nod to his athleticism. At the NFL Combine, Proctor weighed in at 352 pounds after spending most of his college career above 360. His 32.5-inch vertical set a combine record for players over 350 pounds, clearing the previous best by two inches. Those numbers pushed him further up boards heading into the spring.
strengths: Combine confirmed the athletic profile: 32.5-inch vertical and 109-inch broad jump at 352 pounds are historic numbers. Anchor is the best trait on his tape; once he sinks and plants, bull rushers go nowhere. Generates a strong initial punch that can knock defenders off their rush plan before the rep develops. Gap and power run blocking is where he shines; fires downhill and displaces defenders off the ball consistently. Walls off edge defenders effectively when he cannot get push, using positioning and mass to seal the lane. Moves well enough in space to reach linebackers and get out in front when pulling. Plays with a finisher mentality; the tape shows a player who wants to bury defenders through the whistle. Three years starting at left tackle in the SEC, with a zero-pressure game against Georgia as his best showing.
weaknesses: Pass blocking technique is too inconsistent for a three-year starter; beaten inside far too often. Lateral redirect is his most exploitable issue; once committed to a direction, his mass works against him on counters. Feet get too narrow or cross over under pressure, which kills his base and balance. Pad level rises as games wear on, and his frame naturally plays high, giving shorter rushers leverage. Arms measured short for a tackle at the combine, especially for his height, shrinking his margin on hand placement. Zone blocking looks shaky on tape; takes poor angles to second-level targets and loses track of linebackers. Weight management is a real concern; touched 370 during the season and needed coaching staff motivation to slim down. Allowed five pressures in Week 1 before the weight dropped, raising questions about conditioning at his size.
player_comparisons: EvanNeal - Alabama (78%), AmariusMims - Georgia (75%), JCLatham - Alabama (72%)

bio: Kaidon Salter emerged as a dynamic playmaker for Liberty in 2023, leading the Flames to a perfect 13-0 regular season and Conference USA championship. The dual-threat quarterback set school records with 32 passing touchdowns and 1,089 rushing yards by a QB. Salter's breakout campaign earned him CUSA MVP honors and put him on the national radar as a Maxwell Award semifinalist. Originally a four-star recruit out of Cedar Hill High School in Texas, Salter's college career took a detour after a brief stint at Tennessee ended with his dismissal following two arrests. He found redemption at Liberty, working his way from a redshirt year in 2021 to part-time starter in 2022 before seizing the reins in 2023. Salter's electric play and leadership were instrumental in Liberty's successful transition to Conference USA.
strengths: Plus-plus arm talent with the juice to zip throws into tight windows and stretch defenses vertically Twitchy athlete with sudden burst and electric wheels, making him a constant home run threat in the open field Savvy field general who can influence second-level defenders with his eyes and subtle pump fakes Possesses a live arm capable of making all the NFL throws, particularly on outside breaking routes Quick trigger and compact delivery help mitigate his lack of prototypical size in the pocket Tough as nails with the grit to stand tall against the rush and deliver strikes under duress Shows flashes of being able to get to his second and third read when the primary is bottled up Natural alpha dog mentality evident in his ability to rally troops during clutch situations
weaknesses: Undersized signal-caller with a slender frame that may struggle to withstand NFL punishment Deep ball accuracy is hit-or-miss, especially when trying to drop dimes over the top of coverage Can get happy feet in the pocket, bailing too quickly instead of climbing and resetting Gunslinger mentality leads to forced throws and risky decisions when plays break down Needs to sharpen his anticipation and timing on bang-bang plays over the middle Red flags from early college career raise character concerns, though likely chalked up to rookie mistakes and immaturity
player_comparisons: GarrettGreene - West Virginia (74%), MalikCunningham - Louisville (72%), KedonSlovis - BYU (69%)

bio: Fifteen sacks across his sophomore and junior seasons at Moreau Catholic High School in Hayward, California, were enough to earn Kaleb Elarms-Orr four-star ratings from both ESPN and 247Sports despite COVID wiping out his senior year. He played both linebacker and tight end for the Mariners, and his tape landed him at Cal with offers from programs across the Pac-12. A redshirt year in 2021 due to injury meant his college career got a slow start, and a bench role on special teams in 2022 kept him waiting even longer. When he finally got his shot as a full-time starter in 2023, Elarms-Orr wasted no time establishing himself. He ripped off 92 tackles in 11 games for the Golden Bears, adding an interception, five pass breakups, and six quarterback hurries. That kind of production in the old Pac-12, going up against offenses led by Caleb Williams, Bo Nix, and Michael Penix, was enough to land him Second Team All-Conference honors from the AP and Phil Steele. After the Pac-12 dissolved, Elarms-Orr transferred to TCU for the 2024 season, but found himself stuck behind Namdi Obiazor and Johnny Hodges. He played in all 13 games without starting one, contributing 54 tackles and a couple of sacks while biding his time again. The 2025 season was the full arrival. Starting every game for the Horned Frogs, Elarms-Orr led the Big 12 in tackles with 130, tacked on 11 tackles for loss, four sacks, and earned First Team All-Big 12 honors. He posted double-digit tackle games six times, including consecutive 15-plus tackle performances against Baylor and West Virginia that hadn't been done by a TCU player since at least 1995. He capped the year by winning Alamo Bowl Defensive MVP with 11 tackles and 1.5 TFLs against USC. A Big 12 Defensive Player of the Week recipient and Senior Bowl invitee, Elarms-Orr declared for the 2026 NFL Draft in January, looking to become the first TCU linebacker drafted since Dee Winters in 2023.
strengths: Legitimate sideline-to-sideline range thanks to above-average long speed and quick-twitch burst out of his stance. Closes on the ball in a hurry. Flies downhill against the run with bad intentions. His run defense grading jumped dramatically from 2023 to 2025 as his reads and fits improved within TCU's scheme. Tackled with real authority and discipline this past season. Cut his missed tackle rate nearly in half from his Cal days, finishing and wrapping through contact consistently. Comfortable and effective as a green dog blitzer. Shows feel for timing his rush, and his 25 total pressures in 2025 came on a relatively modest number of rush attempts. Contact balance stands out when he's filling the gap. Uses leverage and a solid base to anchor against lead blockers better than his 235-pound frame suggests. Covered a ton of ground in Andy Avalos's defense, logging the most snaps of any Power Four linebacker in 2025. Durability and conditioning are not concerns. Tremendous year-over-year growth trajectory. Went from middling overall grades at Cal to strong marks across the board at TCU, suggesting a player still ascending. Competes with an edge. His willingness to wait behind established starters at both Cal and TCU without checking out says something about his makeup and competitive drive.
weaknesses: Processing speed against the run remains inconsistent. Gets caught reading the wrong key on misdirection and play-action, losing his gap responsibility too often for a starter. Zone coverage is serviceable but nothing more. Allowed a completion rate north of 70% on targets in 2025, and receivers routinely found soft spots in his area. Undersized at 235 pounds with a below-average weight profile for the position. Gets swallowed by fullbacks and pulling guards when he can't avoid the block entirely. Pursuit angles to the perimeter run hot and cold. He'll take too steep a path and overrun the play, turning what should be a tackle for loss into a cutback lane. One interception across nearly 1,900 career defensive snaps is a red flag for ball production in coverage, even accounting for his zone responsibilities.
player_comparisons: MarteMapu - Sacramento State (84%), JackKiser - Notre Dame (84%), JaminDavis - Kentucky (80%)

bio: Kamari Ramsey came out of Sierra Canyon High School in Chatsworth, California as a consensus four-star defensive back in the 2022 class. His senior year produced 58 tackles, two interceptions, and seven passes defensed as Sierra Canyon won the Gold Coast League title. Originally committed to Stanford, Ramsey flipped to UCLA and redshirted as a true freshman before starting all 11 games in 2023, posting 40 tackles, 2.5 tackles for loss, and his first college interception against Cal. USC defensive coordinator D'Anton Lynn brought Ramsey across town through the transfer portal ahead of 2024. He responded with 60 tackles, 5.5 tackles for loss, two sacks, and five pass breakups in 11 starts. A nine-tackle showing against LSU earned LOTT Impact Player of the Week honors, and he added 10 tackles against Penn State on his way to All-Big Ten Honorable Mention. Ramsey returned for 2025 but a knee injury against Iowa in mid-November ended his season at nine games and 27 tackles. He finishes his college career with 100 tackles, eight tackles for loss, nine pass breakups, and three interceptions across 33 games.
strengths: Reads quarterback eyes and triggers on short routes before the ball is out. Covers tight ends with physicality and fluid hips through the stem of routes. Diagnoses screens and flats quickly, firing downhill to blow up plays behind the line. Smooth backpedal-to-drive transition keeps him balanced against underneath and vertical concepts. Processes route combinations at a high level, closing windows in traffic. Lines up at deep safety, in the box, or over the slot without losing effectiveness. Arrives at the catch point with force and rakes through the receiver's hands consistently. Brings special teams experience across multiple units, adding immediate roster depth.
weaknesses: Only three career interceptions despite good positioning and coverage awareness. Run defense is a clear weak spot with hesitation filling alleys and shedding blocks. Tackling in space breaks down, defaulting to arm tackles and grab attempts. Aggressive pursuit angles leave him exposed to cutbacks by shifty runners. Lacks the straight-line speed and range to play center-field safety consistently. Has missed time in three straight seasons, including the knee injury that ended 2025 early.

bio: Kamran James from Olympia High School was rated a 4-star recruit by ESPN and handed a 4-star grade by 247 Sports. After high school, James joined Florida after being heavily recruited. In 2023 as a freshman James saw action in 12 games and played a total of 77 snaps for the Gators. He recorded just 2 tackles, one assist, while adding 2 stops. As a sophomore for the Gators in 2024 James played in 4 games and took part in 117 snaps for the Gators. He recorded just 7 tackles, 3 assists, while adding 3 stops. As a pass rusher, he chalked up 5 total pressures, and 5 QB hurries, no QB hits, and no sacks during the year.
strengths: Good not great quickness and change of direction agility. Chases, using a long spin move to clear blocks. Low, strong off the line. James explodes off the line with a quick first step, with agile feet to counter inside and closing burst to finish plays. His core flexibility and balance are excellent, and James has prototypical length. His motor is excellent, and he’s quick to locate the ball and make plays against the run. James has the speed to be effective in pursuit, and he shows enough fluid athleticism that he might be able to transition to outside linebacker and hold up in coverage. While he has the flexibility to dip and bend under tackles, what makes him truly dangerous is his ability to counter.
weaknesses: Rarely the first player off the snap, even when lined up over the ball, but his length, effort and short-area quickness still pressures double teams and helps penetrate gaps. He’s often slow to react and locate the ball against the run. Lean lower body - must improve core and leg strength. Suspect body flexibility and tight hips.

bio: Kayden McDonald arrived at Ohio State in June 2023 as one of Georgia's most decorated high school defensive linemen, a four-star recruit out of North Gwinnett High School in Suwanee who had left a trail of destruction through opposing offenses. His prep career was nothing short of absurd: 234 tackles, a school-record 46 sacks, and 131 tackles for loss across 40 games. During his junior season alone, he racked up 19 sacks and 58 tackles for loss, then followed it up with 13 sacks and 40 TFLs as a senior. At one point, he registered seven sacks in a single playoff game. He was also productive enough on offense to tote the rock 77 times for 409 yards and 11 touchdowns his final year at North Gwinnett, a testament to the athletic versatility that made him the nation's No. 12 ranked defensive tackle and earned him Gwinnett Daily Post Defensive Player of the Year honors along with Georgia High School Association all-state recognition. The transition to Columbus required patience. McDonald saw limited action as a true freshman in 2023, appearing in seven games and notching one tackle for loss while also moonlighting as a short-yardage fullback in the Goodyear Cotton Bowl. His sophomore campaign in 2024 kept him behind Tyleik Williams, who would go on to become a first-round pick, but McDonald contributed as a rotational piece across all 16 games of Ohio State's national championship run, finishing with 16 tackles and 1.5 tackles for loss. Born February 8, 2005, the 6-foot-3, 326-pound nose tackle spent those first two years learning the system and developing his technique while maintaining his commitment to academics as a two-time OSU Scholar-Athlete and Academic All-Big Ten honoree majoring in communications. Everything clicked for McDonald in 2025. Thrust into a starting role following the departure of last year's draft class, he anchored a Buckeyes defense that finished No. 1 nationally in total defense, scoring defense, and rushing defense, allowing just 9.3 points per game. His stat line told the story: 65 tackles, 8 tackles for loss, 3 sacks, and 2 forced fumbles across another dominant Ohio State campaign. He was named a unanimous All-American and the Big Ten's Defensive Lineman of the Year while earning semifinalist recognition for the Outland Trophy. His best performances came on the biggest stages, including a career-high eight tackles against top-ranked Texas in the season opener and another eight-tackle effort against Penn State. Off the field, McDonald has served as an ambassador for Ronald McDonald House Charities of Central Ohio, demonstrating the character and maturity that NFL organizations covet in their locker rooms.
strengths: Human-sized roadblock. Centers try to move him and end up going backward instead. That kind of play strength you just cannot teach. Watch the Texas game and count how many times their guards get thrown around like tackling dummies. He ragdolls people at the point of attack. His anchor is ridiculous. Power runs die at the line because he plants those tree trunks in the ground and refuses to budge no matter what they throw at him. Gets tremendous pop in his hands on contact and knows how to lock out blockers, then times his shed perfectly when the ball gets close. Way more athletic than the stopwatch says. He can slant, stunt, and even hit blockers with a little shimmy to keep them honest through the game. Even when he gets caught in a bad spot, his combination of strength and quick feet lets him recover and still blow up plays near the line. Finds the football. He has a nose for the ball through all that trash at the line and does not just eat blocks for the sake of eating blocks. Won a natty and showed up in the biggest games. Eight tackles against Texas, eight more against Penn State. The moment is not too big for him.
weaknesses: Those arms are shorter than you would like. Once a back gets to the edge of his reach, he is grabbing at air and hoping for the best. His pass rush right now is basically bull rush or bust. When that does not work, he kind of stands there looking for something else to do. Gets caught guessing against combo blocks. By the time he figures out what is happening, the guard is already climbing to the second level and he has lost his gap. Has a bad habit of popping up out of his stance and getting narrow with his base. Good offensive lines will see that and wash him out of the play. The motor runs a little cold on passing downs. If his first move gets stuffed, you will see him take a play off instead of fighting through the whistle.
player_comparisons: KennethGrant - Michigan (72%), JordanPhillips - Maryland (69%), JJPegues - Ole Miss (69%)

bio: Keagen Trost played high school ball at Kankakee in Illinois, earning two-time all-conference honors and serving as team captain his final two years. A three-star recruit per 247Sports in the 2019 cycle, he started his college career at Morgan State, picking up two starts as a true freshman before transferring to Indiana State. He spent four seasons with the Sycamores, appearing in 22 games with 13 starts at tackle, but never stood out beyond a competent FCS starter. A move to Wake Forest for 2024 brought a step up in competition. Trost started 11 games at right tackle, helping the offense produce nine games with 100-plus rushing yards. Solid, but nothing that turned NFL heads. Then he landed at Missouri for his seventh college season, and the production changed completely. Playing right tackle in the SEC, Trost earned first-team All-American recognition from PFF and second-team honors from Walter Camp. He played all 891 offensive snaps across 13 games, anchored the nation's eighth-ranked rushing attack, and allowed almost nothing in pass protection. Missouri's offensive line landed on the Joe Moore Award Watch List, and Trost took home SEC Offensive Lineman of the Week after the South Carolina win. Fifty career games across four programs, and the best version showed up last.
strengths: Fires off the ball on drive blocks and walks defenders backward with square pads and a grinding base. Gets to the second level with pads under control and walls off linebackers before they scrape to the ball. Hands arrive early in pass protection; catches rushers in the chest before they build momentum on the bull. Gave SEC pass rushers almost nothing to work with all season, staying clean snap after snap at right tackle. Never came off the field for Missouri in 2025; the coaching staff clearly never considered spelling him. Stays patient in his vertical sets, keeps his chest over his toes, and lets speed rushers run into his power. Has seen every stunt and twist imaginable after 50 games at four schools across two levels of football. The Missouri run tape is where he sells himself, especially the South Carolina and Mississippi State games.
weaknesses: Arms at 32 3/8 inches are guard length, and you see it when speed rushers work to his edge. Feet die on him against inside-out counters; when a rusher stutters and redirects, he lunges instead of sliding. Lack of recovery speed shows on tape; once a rusher gets his outside shoulder, the rep is over. Four years of average tape before Missouri makes you wonder how much of the 2025 breakout was scheme. Bites on high-side fakes, stops his feet to punch down the middle, and surrenders the corner.
player_comparisons: LiamEichenberg - Notre Dame (94%), JavonFoster - Missouri (90%), MaxMitchell - Louisiana (84%)

bio: Keaten Wade, a versatile EDGE defender from Colorado, brings an intriguing blend of size and athleticism to the NFL Draft. Standing at 6'5" and weighing 250 pounds, Wade's frame falls in the 96th percentile for height and 85th percentile for weight among edge rushers. Born on October 22 in Nashville, Tennessee, Wade's football journey began at Summit High School in Spring Hill, where he was a decorated two-way player. Wade's high school accolades include helping lead Summit to a 40-5 record over three years, including a Division I, Class 5A state championship as a junior. His senior year performance earned him Region 7-6A defensive MVP honors, as he amassed 84 tackles, 8 sacks, 26 tackles for loss, 2 interceptions, and 5 forced fumbles. These accomplishments garnered significant attention from college recruiters, with Wade earning four-star ratings from major recruiting services. After two seasons at Kentucky, where he played in 26 games and recorded 51 tackles (5.5 for loss), 2.5 sacks, and five quarterback hurries, Wade transferred to Colorado for his final two years of eligibility. His move to Boulder under the tutelage of Deion Sanders presents an opportunity to refine his skills and boost his draft stock in a high-profile program.
strengths: Freakish athleticism for an EDGE, with a jaw-dropping burst off the line that will have offensive tackles backpedaling from the snap Prototypical NFL frame with plus length and bulk, giving him the tools to set a strong edge and bull rush when needed Shows flashes of dominance against the run, consistently maintaining gap integrity and shedding blocks to make plays in the backfield Positional flexibility to play as a stand-up linebacker or with his hand in the dirt, fitting multiple defensive schemes at the next level High-motor player who never quits on a play, often making hustle tackles downfield and wearing down opposing linemen Reliable wrap-up tackler who rarely whiffs when he gets his hands on ball carriers, limiting yards after contact Brings added value on special teams with his ability to get skinny through gaps and block kicks, a coveted trait for rookies Quick processor who diagnoses plays rapidly, allowing him to react and disrupt before offenses can fully execute their designs
weaknesses: Raw as a pass rusher, lacking a diverse repertoire of moves and often relying solely on his first-step quickness to beat tackles Struggles to convert speed to power consistently, getting stonewalled by anchor-heavy offensive linemen when he can't win with pure athleticism Production doesn't match his physical gifts, raising questions about his ability to be a difference-maker at the next level Needs significant technique refinement in hand usage and leverage, often playing too high and negating his natural strength advantage Can get washed out against the run when facing double teams, needing to improve his anchor and play strength to hold his ground
player_comparisons: AmareBarno - Virginia Tech (78%), StevenParker - Texas State (72%), JeffreyGunter - Coastal Carolina (71%)

bio: No recruiting interest came out of a senior season at Helix High School in La Mesa, California, where Keionte Scott played both ways in 2019, catching 36 passes for 563 yards and 10 touchdowns while grabbing four interceptions at cornerback and helping the Highlanders win the CIF San Diego Section Open Division title. He had bounced between Las Vegas and San Diego before landing there, and when no offers materialized, the JUCO route was the only door open. Scott ended up at Snow College in Utah, where he earned NJCAA All-American honors twice and was named conference co-Defensive Player of the Year. Ranked the No. 4 JUCO prospect nationally, he transferred to Auburn in 2022 and started immediately. He recorded 54 tackles as the nickel in 2022, earned All-SEC Second Team honors in 2023, and led the conference in punt return average. After an injury-limited 2024, he transferred through Houston and landed at Miami for 2025. That final season was his best work. Scott posted 64 tackles, 13 tackles for loss, five sacks, and two pick-sixes while earning Thorpe Award semifinalist recognition. He returned from a November foot injury to record 10 tackles, two sacks, and a forced fumble against Texas A&M in the CFP first round, then added a 72-yard interception return for a touchdown against Ohio State in the Cotton Bowl.
strengths: Physical, downhill run defender who flies to the ball and fits gaps with real aggression. Effective blitzer with good timing off the edge; five sacks in 2025 were not a scheme product. Reads quarterback eyes well in zone, shown by jumping a screen for a 72-yard pick-six against Ohio State. Loose hips and quick feet let him mirror receivers through breaks without grabbing. Willing to take on blockers bigger than him and hold his ground at the point of attack. Extensive experience at multiple positions: outside corner, nickel, and some safety reps. Track background shows up in his play speed closing on the ball in space. Tough competitor who returned from a November foot injury and was the best player on the field in the CFP.
weaknesses: Missed tackle rate around 20 percent is a real problem, too often lunging instead of wrapping. Undersized for the outside and got exposed there at Auburn in 2024; not a boundary corner. Will peek into the backfield and bite on fakes, leaving space behind him on double moves. Turns 25 in August, with six college seasons worth of mileage on the tires. Career completion rate against in coverage sits near 65 percent, which raises questions about consistency. Aggressiveness can tip into recklessness, taking himself out of plays when he guesses wrong.
player_comparisons: AsanteSamuel Jr. - Florida State (83%), MaxMelton - Rutgers (82%), JalenPitre - Baylor (80%)

bio: Keith Abney II grew up in Waxahachie, Texas, where he was a two-way standout at Waxahachie High School. Over a 25-game varsity career, he racked up over 1,000 all-purpose yards and 20 tackles, finishing his senior season with 19 catches for 396 yards and six touchdowns on offense while chipping in two interceptions and a fumble recovery on defense. That production earned him a consensus three-star rating across the major recruiting services, with 247 Sports slotting him as the No. 103 athlete nationally and No. 203 overall in the state of Texas. He was named to the 2021 Texas District 11-6A first team as a junior. Abney arrived at Arizona State and wasted little time getting on the field. As a true freshman in 2023, he appeared in eight games as a reserve defensive back and on special teams, recording his first career interception against Oregon on November 18. His 56 defensive snaps were limited, but he allowed just one catch for seven yards on two targets and earned the third-highest grade among Pac-12 true freshman corners who played at least 50 snaps. That small sample was enough to hint at what was coming. By 2024, he stepped into the starting lineup at corner for all 14 games, tying for the team lead with three interceptions and pacing the squad with nine pass breakups and 52 tackles. After giving up two touchdowns in the season's first four games, Abney surrendered just two more over the final ten, tightening up a coverage profile that only got better as the year wore on. The junior year was the one that turned heads. In 2025, Abney earned All-Big 12 honors and cemented himself as Arizona State's best cover man, posting a pair of interceptions, nine more pass breakups, 39 solo tackles, and just two missed tackles across the season. His dominant late-November performance against BYU, where he was targeted only once on 35 passing plays and allowed zero completions, became one of the signature moments of the Sun Devils' campaign. A former speed skating competitor with a track background, Abney's athleticism has always been quietly present, but it was his work between the ears and his relentless competitive fire that accelerated his rise from three-star afterthought to legitimate NFL prospect. He declared for the draft ahead of the Sun Devils' season finale and spoke at the Combine in Indianapolis, making it clear he believes he can change games at the next level.
strengths: Incredibly competitive temperament that jumps off the film; this kid wants to fight you for every single catch and does not back down from anyone regardless of matchup. Outstanding catch-point disruption with receiver-like ball skills; he attacks the football at its highest point and has legitimate hands to finish interceptions. Coverage processing improved dramatically from his sophomore to junior year, reading quarterback eyes and triggering on routes with impressive timing and anticipation. Versatile alignment history spanning boundary corner, field corner, and slot reps, giving coordinators flexibility to deploy him across multiple coverage shells. Background:

Over a 25-game varsity career, he racked up over 1,000 all-purpose yards and 20 tackles, finishing his senior season with 19 catches for 396 yards and six touchdowns on offense while chipping in two interceptions and a fumble recovery on defense. That production earned him a consensus three-star rating across the major recruiting services, with 247 Sports slotting him as the No. 103 athlete nationally and No. 203 overall in the state of Texas. He was named to the 2021 Texas District 11-6A first team as a junior. Abney arrived at Arizona State and wasted little time getting on the field. As a true freshman in 2023, he appeared in eight games as a reserve defensive back and on special teams, recording his first career interception against Oregon on November 18. His 56 defensive snaps were limited, but he allowed just one catch for seven yards on two targets and earned the third-highest grade among Pac-12 true freshman corners who played at least 50 snaps. That small sample was enough to hint at what was coming. By 2024, he stepped into the starting lineup at corner for all 14 games, tying for the team lead with three interceptions and pacing the squad with nine pass breakups and 52 tackles. After giving up two touchdowns in the season's first four games, Abney surrendered just two more over the final ten, tightening up a coverage profile that only got better as the year wore on. The junior year was the one that turned heads. In 2025, Abney earned All-Big 12 honors and cemented himself as Arizona State's best cover man, posting a pair of interceptions, nine more pass breakups, 39 solo tackles, and just two missed tackles across the season. His dominant late-November performance against BYU, where he was targeted only once on 35 passing plays and allowed zero completions, became one of the signature moments of the Sun Devils' campaign. A former speed skating competitor with a track background, Abney's athleticism has always been quietly present, but it was his work between the ears and his relentless competitive fire that accelerated his rise from three-star afterthought to legitimate NFL prospect. He declared for the draft ahead of the Sun Devils' season finale and spoke at the Combine in Indianapolis, making it clear he believes he can change games at the next level.

Strengths:
Incredibly competitive temperament that jumps off the film; this kid wants to fight you for every single catch and does not back down from anyone regardless of matchup. Outstanding catch-point disruption with receiver-like ball skills; he attacks the football at its highest point and has legitimate hands to finish interceptions. Coverage processing improved dramatically from his sophomore to junior year, reading quarterback eyes and triggering on routes with impressive timing and anticipation. Former speed skater with a track background that gives him a sneaky athletic baseline; he can turn and run better than initial film impressions suggest.

Weaknesses:
Undersized frame at 5-10 and 187 pounds sits in the bottom quartile for the position, raising durability questions against bigger NFL receivers in contested situations. Penalty frequency is a real concern; 13 flags over his last two seasons suggest his physicality crosses the line too often, and NFL officials will not be more lenient. Lacks the explosive top-end speed to recover when beaten cleanly off the line; his closing quickness from depth is functional but not special against vertical threats. bio: Kendal Daniels from Beggs High School was rated a 4-star recruit by ESPN and handed a 4-star grade by 247 Sports. Following high school, Daniels opted to join Oklahoma State after being heavily recruited. In 2022 as a freshman Daniels saw action in 12 games and played a total of 607 snaps at safety for the Cowboys. He recorded solid 51 tackles, 15 assists, while adding 30 stops. In coverage Daniels notched up 2 pass breakups, 3 interceptions, and an elite QB rating when targetted of 44.8. He added 13 total pressures, and no sacks on the season. As a sophomore in 2023 he played in 13 games and logged 876 snaps for the Cowboys. He made solid 81 tackles, 29 assists, and had 37 stops. In coverage he totaled no pass breakups, one interception, and a disapointing QB rating when targetted of 121.4.
strengths: Has a solid frame with a strong lower body and much improved bulk. Fluid and rangy with the coordination and loose hips to quickly change directions. Has good ball skills to locate and attack the ball in the air. Daniels tracks the ball well and does a nice job of getting his head around in plenty of time to avoid flags. Has smooth body control with the fluid ability to plant and redirect in any direction. Plays fast and decisive, arriving to the play with fierce intentions. Tracks the ball well and has the natural ball skills for forcing turnovers. Has the violent hands to discard receivers and smaller tight ends, and Daniels is a sure tackler. In coverage, he shows good instincts when sitting back in zone, with click-and-close ability and good ball tracking.
weaknesses: Needs to tighten his angles in pursuit. Needs to show better tackling technique to consistently finish. Needs to develop a better understanding of route combinations and better overall vision. Daniels plays high and tends to struggle with change of direction, and Daniels also shows some stiffness when he has to flip his hips.

bio: A consensus four-star recruit and top-100 national prospect out of Warren, Arkansas, Kendrick Law signed with Alabama and saw immediate action as a true freshman in 2022, catching eight passes for 103 yards across 11 games. He spent three seasons in Tuscaloosa but never climbed past a rotational role, totaling just 33 receptions for 343 yards and one touchdown while earning his primary reputation as a kick returner, averaging 23.7 yards on 30 career returns. Law transferred to Kentucky for the 2025 season and found the consistent opportunity that had eluded him. Starting all 12 games, he led the Wildcats in receptions (53) and receiving yards (540), including an 11-catch, 124-yard game against Tennessee Tech and a 71-yard touchdown against Tennessee that marked his first career 100-yard receiving performance. He was named a game captain twice. Law posted strong testing numbers at the NFL Combine, highlighted by a 42-inch vertical jump that ranked third among all receivers. He was invited to the East-West Shrine Bowl following his senior season.
strengths: Elite lower-body explosiveness confirmed by combine testing; 42-inch vertical and 10'8" broad jump. Thick, compact frame that absorbs contact and stays balanced through the catch. Reliable hands with strong grip strength; almost never puts the ball on the ground. Dangerous after the catch on short throws, breaks arm tackles and finds creases. Good sideline awareness; keeps his feet in bounds on contested receptions near the boundary. Extensive special teams background with real kick-return value from his Alabama years. Athletic upside suggests his college usage barely scratched what his body can do.
weaknesses: Limited route tree in college; most targets came at or behind the line of scrimmage. Tight hips round off his breaks and reduce separation on intermediate and deep routes. Lacks burst out of cuts; doesn't accelerate sharply enough to stack corners downfield. Slow to track and adjust to deep balls, limiting his reliability on vertical concepts. Four career touchdowns across 46 games points to trouble winning contested situations.
