bio: Before Kevin Coleman Jr. ever became one of the more well-traveled receivers in recent college football memory, he was lighting up the state of Missouri at St. Mary's High School in St. Louis. A consensus four-star recruit rated as the top prospect in the state by every major recruiting service, Coleman put together a prep career that reads like fiction: 179 catches, nearly 4,000 receiving yards, 56 touchdowns, 14 interceptions on defense, and a MSHSAA Class 3 State Championship in 2021. He earned MaxPreps Missouri Player of the Year honors as a senior after racking up 985 receiving yards and 17 scores, and his recruitment heated up quickly. Standing 5'11" and weighing 174 pounds with a February 2004 birthday, Coleman made the surprise decision to follow Deion Sanders to Jackson State, joining Shedeur Sanders and Travis Hunter at the FCS program. Coleman's freshman season at Jackson State in 2022 was a statement. He posted 32 catches for 475 yards and three touchdowns, winning SWAC Freshman of the Year over the more heralded Hunter. That success didn't anchor him in place, though. Coleman transferred to Louisville for 2023, where he slotted in as a starter alongside Chris Bell and Jamari Thrash but managed a quieter stat line of 26 receptions for 362 yards and two touchdowns in Jeff Brohm's offense. One year later he was on the move again, this time to Mississippi State under Jeff Lebby. That 2024 season in Starkville was his breakout: Coleman hauled in 74 catches for 932 yards and six touchdowns, more than doubling the production of any other Bulldog wideout and earning All-SEC Third Team recognition. He connected with quarterback Blake Shapen for an 80-yard touchdown against Arizona State, the longest reception by a Mississippi State player in five years. Coleman's fourth and final stop came at Missouri for the 2025 season, where he returned home to play in front of family and friends. Once again he established himself immediately, leading the Tigers with 66 receptions for 732 yards despite inconsistent quarterback play, and his 78.8 percent catch rate ranked sixth nationally. He delivered his biggest moments when they mattered most: 10 catches for 126 yards against Kansas, 12 grabs for 108 yards versus UMass, and a game-sealing 67-yard punt return touchdown at Arkansas. Coleman closed out his college career with 200 catches, over 2,500 receiving yards, and 12 touchdowns across 51 games at four different programs, then turned heads all week at the Senior Bowl with a dominant performance in practices and interviews alike.
strengths: Quick-twitch feet and sudden hip snap at the top of routes create instant separation against linebackers and nickel defenders working the slot. Catches the ball away from his body with soft, reliable hands. Only seven drops on 271 career targets is a remarkable rate for a receiver with his volume. Gets to top speed in a hurry off the line and through his stem, clocking a 4.4 forty. Cornerbacks who give him a free release are in serious trouble. Fearless working over the middle of the field against traffic. Converted nine of 11 contested catches at Mizzou, including multiple grabs through heavy contact. Translates the catch into yards after the catch with urgency, averaging 5.7 YAC per reception at Missouri while forcing 18 missed tackles on the season. Four different playbooks at four different schools, and he earned a starting role every single time. That adaptability is not something you can teach. Willing and competitive as a stalk blocker despite his smaller frame, giving consistent effort through the whistle and showing no hesitation engaging in the run game. Proven punt return ability with a 67-yard house call at Arkansas and a 12.6 yard average on returns in 2025, adding legitimate special teams value on Day One.
weaknesses: At just174 pounds in the third percentile for weight, NFL press corners with length are going to challenge his release timing and ability to stay on schedule. Overwhelming majority of his snaps came from the slot, with less than seven percent of his career reps on the boundary. He is a one-alignment player right now. Route tree leaned heavily on underneath concepts like screens, hitches, and flat releases from motion. His average depth of target sat at just 7.8 yards at Mizzou. Tends to go down on first contact against bigger defenders in the open field, and lacks the power base to consistently break arm tackles at the second level. Career contested catch rate of 53.7 percent across all four schools tells a different story than his strong 2025 finish, signaling inconsistency against physical coverage.
player_comparisons: TahjWashington - USC (93%), ParkerWashington - Penn State (88%), CharlieJones - Purdue (85%)

bio: Kenneth Charles Concepcion grew up in Charlotte, North Carolina, where he starred at Julius L. Chambers High School under head coach Brandon Wiggins. During his final two prep seasons, he tallied 65 receptions for 977 yards and 12 touchdowns while also flashing his return ability with three punt return scores as a junior. Recruiting services were split on his projection, with Rivals rating him a 4-star prospect and the No. 8 overall player in North Carolina, while ESPN and 247Sports each tagged him as a 3-star recruit. He committed to NC State in the spring of his senior year. Concepcion wasted no time making his presence felt in Raleigh. As a true freshman in 2023, he started 11 of 12 games and rewrote the Wolfpack record books, posting 71 catches for 839 yards and 10 touchdowns while adding a surprising wrinkle as a ball carrier with 41 rushes for 320 yards. That performance earned him ACC Rookie of the Year, ACC Offensive Rookie of the Year, and Freshman All-America recognition from the FWAA. His sophomore campaign in 2024 saw a statistical dip with 53 receptions for 460 yards and six scores, though he remained a consistent contributor in NC State's offense. Following the 2024 season, Concepcion entered the transfer portal and landed at Texas A&M, where he formed a dynamic tandem with fellow wideout Mario Craver. His 2025 campaign in College Station was his best yet: 61 catches for 919 yards and nine touchdowns while averaging a career-best 15.1 yards per reception. The 2025 season also showcased his return ability with two punt return touchdowns, and he capped the year by winning the Paul Hornung Award as college football's most versatile player. Following the Aggies' loss to Miami in the postseason, Concepcion declared for the 2026 NFL Draft with a career total of 185 receptions for 2,218 yards and 25 receiving touchdowns across 38 games at two Power Five programs.
strengths: Possesses twitch and burst that jumps off the screen immediately, with outstanding acceleration through the first 15 yards of his route stem that creates natural separation. Sinks his hips beautifully into breaks and explodes out of cuts without needing to gear down, making him a nightmare for cornerbacks trying to mirror in tight spaces. Converted 10 of 15 contested catch opportunities during the 2025 season, showing strong hands and body control when attacking the football despite his smaller frame. Dynamic after the catch with over half his 2025 receiving yards coming following the reception, displaying vision and wiggle to make tacklers miss in confined areas. Flashes legitimate deep speed and can stack defenders vertically when given a clean release, punishing missed jam attempts with chunk plays down the field. Locates soft spots in zone coverage with natural feel and opens his hips to the quarterback quickly, making him a reliable option on rhythm throws. Brings a physical edge that belies his size, showing willingness to scrap with press corners and finish runs with contact rather than dancing out of bounds. Proven return specialist who changes games with the ball in his hands, evidenced by two punt return touchdowns during the 2025 season alone and three more during his high school career.
weaknesses: Compact build, which limits his catch radius and allows physical corners to reroute him at the line of scrimmage. Drop rate is a legitimate concern with 19 drops against 186 career catches, particularly when working over the middle into traffic and absorbing contact. Routes lack variance in tempo and setup, often running at one speed and relying purely on athleticism rather than manipulating defenders with pacing or false steps. Struggles to adjust his body and hands to throws with unexpected velocity or trajectory, leading to concentration lapses on balls that require tracking adjustments. Blocking effort and technique from the slot leaves something on the table, with inconsistent positioning and patience when asked to sustain on run plays.

bio: Key Lawrence from Ensworth High School was rated a 4-star recruit by ESPN and handed a 4-star grade by 247 Sports. Following high school, Lawrence opted to join Tennessee after being heavily recruited. In 2020 as a freshman Lawrence saw action in 6 games and played a total of 105 snaps at cornerback for the Volunteers. He recorded only 4 tackles, 4 assists, while adding one stop. In coverage Lawrence notched up one pass breakup, no interceptions, and QB rating when targetted of 57.6. After the end of the season he transferred to Oklahoma where as a sophomore for the Sooners in 2021 Lawrence played in 13 games and logged 461 snaps for the Sooners. He made 31 tackles, 13 assists, and had 10 stops. In coverage he totaled 3 pass breakups, no interceptions, and QB rating when targetted of 97.9. In 2022 as a junior Lawrence played in 10 games and contributed on 399 snaps. He chalked up 41 tackles, 13 assists, and added 15 stops. In the secondary Lawrence forced 4 pass breakups, one interception, and gave up QB rating when targetted of 102.1. In 2023 as a senior Lawrence saw action in 12 games while playing 398 snaps. He logged 36 tackles, 7 assists, and made 12 stops. In coverage Lawrence had 2 pass breakups, 2 interceptions, and surrendered QB rating when targetted of 60.9.
strengths: Tough in run support and works hard to get off blocks. Hard-nosed player who attacks the run and doesn't back down. He’s tall-framed with long arms to match. With his powerful hands, straight-line speed, and fluidity in his hips, turning and chasing downfield shouldn’t be a problem. Can get up on the line of scrimmage and deliver a solid punch early in routes, then use that quickness to shadow, or back off and close quickly when the ball comes out Very good in run support, an aggressive tackler, and capable of breaking down and securing tackles in space. He’s also very good in zone coverage, showing a great understanding of routes and the ability to close quickly. Willing in run support, coming downhill quickly when Lawrence has a chance to make a play.
weaknesses: Needs to develop a better understanding of route combinations and better overall vision. Will slip off tackles and needs to finish more consistently. He’s a little bit tight and straight-line-ish as an athlete, and Lawrence struggles when he has to mirror quickness.

scouting_report:
Background:
Key Lawrence from Ensworth High School was rated a 4-star recruit by ESPN and handed a 4-star grade by 247 Sports. Following high school, Lawrence opted to join Tennessee after being heavily recruited. In 2020 as a freshman Lawrence saw action in 6 games and played a total of 105 snaps at cornerback for the Volunteers. He recorded only 4 tackles, 4 assists, while adding one stop. In coverage Lawrence notched up one pass breakup, no interceptions, and QB rating when targetted of 57.6. After the end of the season he transferred to Oklahoma where as a sophomore for the Sooners in 2021 Lawrence played in 13 games and logged 461 snaps for the Sooners. He made 31 tackles, 13 assists, and had 10 stops. In coverage he totaled 3 pass breakups, no interceptions, and QB rating when targetted of 97.9. In 2022 as a junior Lawrence played in 10 games and contributed on 399 snaps. He chalked up 41 tackles, 13 assists, and added 15 stops. In the secondary Lawrence forced 4 pass breakups, one interception, and gave up QB rating when targetted of 102.1. In 2023 as a senior Lawrence saw action in 12 games while playing 398 snaps. He logged 36 tackles, 7 assists, and made 12 stops. In coverage Lawrence had 2 pass breakups, 2 interceptions, and surrendered QB rating when targetted of 60.9.

Strengths:
Tough in run support and works hard to get off blocks. Hard-nosed player who attacks the run and doesn't back down. He’s tall-framed with long arms to match. With his powerful hands, straight-line speed, and fluidity in his hips, turning and chasing downfield shouldn’t be a problem. Can get up on the line of scrimmage and deliver a solid punch early in routes, then use that quickness to shadow, or back off and close quickly when the ball comes out Very good in run support, an aggressive tackler, and capable of breaking down and securing tackles in space. He’s also very good in zone coverage, showing a great understanding of routes and the ability to close quickly. Willing in run support, coming downhill quickly when Lawrence has a chance to make a play.

Weaknesses:
Needs to develop a better understanding of route combinations and better overall vision. Will slip off tackles and needs to finish more consistently. He’s a little bit tight and straight-line-ish as an athlete, and Lawrence struggles when he has to mirror quickness.

bio: Keyon Ware-Hudson from Mater Dei High School was rated a 4-star recruit by ESPN and handed a 3-star grade by 247 Sports. After high school, Ware-Hudson joined Oregon after being heavily recruited. In 2019 as a freshman Ware-Hudson saw action in just 3 games and played a total of 30 snaps for the Ducks. He recorded only 2 tackles, no assists, while adding 3 stops. As a sophomore for the Ducks in 2020 Ware-Hudson played in 7 games and took part in 83 snaps for the Ducks. He recorded only 7 tackles, 2 assists, while adding 3 stops. In 2021 as a junior Ware-Hudson played in 10 games and contributed on 343 snaps. He recorded 11 tackles, 2 assists while making 7 stops. As a pass rusher, he produced 7 total pressures, which included 4 QB hurries, 2 QB hits, and one sack on the year. In 2022 as a senior Ware-Hudson logged playing time in 12 games and logged a total of 327 snaps for the Ducks. He also added only 9 tackles, 7 assists, and 8 stops. As a pass rusher, he tallied 10 total pressures, which included 9 QB hurries, no QB hits, and one sack on the season. In 2023 as a fifth year senior Ware-Hudson logged playing time in 13 games and logged a total of 213 snaps for the Ducks. He also added 14 tackles, 4 assists, and 8 stops. As a pass rusher, he tallied 6 total pressures, which included 4 QB hurries, one QB hit, and one sack on the season.
strengths: His hand use is excellent; he’s strong in the upper body and consistently swats away blockers. He’s a high-effort pass rusher who shows good initial quickness and the ability to beat blockers to the inside. Ware-Hudson has a wide base and a low center of gravity to hold his ground, as well as the upper-body strength to shed blockers. Delivers a strong punch and uses his hands to shed blocks to make plays in the hole or outside the box. Uses lower-body strength to anchor inside. Strong tackler. Has good power in hands and can anchor, shed, and chase. Legitimate burst to close and an effective wrap tackler.
weaknesses: Stands up well against the run but is very pursuit-conscious and will take unneeded wide angles. Lacks the ability to be a true edge burner. While Ware-Hudson was able to physically overpower opponents at the college, he will have to develop a wider repertoire of moves in the pros. Ware-Hudson remains a bit raw, as he is inconsistent with his hand use and lacks violence in his hands

bio: Richmond, Missouri, is the kind of small town that builds legends around its best athletes, and Keyshaun Elliott gave them plenty to talk about. A three-sport star at Richmond High School, he dominated on Friday nights as both the starting quarterback and linebacker, racking up 128 offensive touchdowns and 180 tackles with 20 sacks on the defensive side. He punted, too. Won a state title in the 4x200m relay for the track team and graduated with honors. Football runs in the family: his cousin Linval Elliott played in the NFL for the Bengals and 49ers, earning a Super Bowl ring with San Francisco in Super Bowl XVI. Despite all that production, the recruiting services pegged Elliott as a two-to-three-star prospect, and the Power Five offers never materialized. Elliott landed at New Mexico State, where he saw the field as a true freshman in 2022, logging 12 appearances and making 27 tackles. His sophomore year in Las Cruces was the breakout. Playing nearly every snap on defense, Elliott piled up 111 tackles to lead the Aggies and finish 30th nationally, adding 10 tackles for loss, 2.5 sacks, and six pass breakups. That production helped carry NMSU to its first 10-win season since 1960, a Conference USA Championship Game appearance, and just the sixth bowl game in program history. He earned C-USA Second-Team honors for the effort. With nothing left to prove at that level, Elliott entered the transfer portal and headed west to Arizona State. The transition to the Big 12 took some adjusting. In 2024, his first year with the Sun Devils, Elliott played in all 13 games but started only eight, finishing with 65 tackles, four TFLs, two sacks, and an interception. He made his presence felt down the stretch, posting a 12-tackle game in a road win at Texas State and turning in six defensive stops in the Peach Bowl against Texas, one of the best individual postseason performances in the country that year. Then came 2025, and Elliott took over. Starting all 12 regular season games, he led Arizona State with 98 tackles, 14 tackles for loss, and seven sacks. Only one other FBS player matched those numbers across all three categories. He earned All-Big 12 Second Team honors, landed on the Butkus Award Preseason Watch List, wore the green dot for a second straight season, surpassed 300 career tackles, and accepted invitations to both the East-West Shrine Bowl and the Senior Bowl.
strengths: Plays with a downhill trigger that is almost impossible to fake out; reads his run keys, trusts them, and fires into gaps before blockers can climb to the second level. Hands are violent and purposeful at the point of attack. He keeps his chest clean against climbing linemen and wins the leverage battle with a wide, anchored base that belies his size. Generates serious pop on contact as a tackler. Ball carriers do not fall forward against this kid. When he arrives, he arrives with bad intentions and wraps through the whistle. Excellent feel for working through trash in the box. He scrapes over the top of blocks, navigates combo schemes, and finds the football with a natural instinct for traffic patterns at the line of scrimmage. Legitimate pass rush weapon from the linebacker spot. Seven sacks as a senior did not happen by accident. His timing on the blitz, combined with a compact frame and good closing burst, makes him a credible A-gap and B-gap threat. Rarely freelances or guesses wrong in his run fits. He is disciplined, assignment-sound, and fills his gap with the kind of conviction that coaches trust on early downs. Wore the green dot for two straight years at Arizona State, which tells you what the coaching staff thought of his processing speed. He relayed calls, set the defense, and adjusted on the fly with a veteran presence. Career production speaks for itself: 300-plus tackles across four seasons at two programs, with a clear upward trajectory in his ability to generate negative plays after moving to a Power conference.
weaknesses: Athletic limitations are real and they show up in coverage. When tight ends or backs run vertical stems or break routes outside the numbers, Elliott simply cannot stay in phase. Missed tackle rate stayed stubbornly consistent across his college career. There is some stiffness in his movement when he has to redirect in space, and the wrap-up is not always clean in the open field. Coverage grade over four seasons was the weakest part of his game by a wide margin. He is functional sitting in zone windows, but asking him to match up man-to-man on NFL-caliber athletes is asking for trouble. Lateral agility and change-of-direction quickness do not scream three-down linebacker. Offensive coordinators will test him on option routes and wheel concepts to expose his hip stiffness. His frame, at 235 pounds with below-average weight for the position, raises questions about whether he can hold up taking on NFL-level interior linemen on a snap-to-snap basis over a full season.
player_comparisons: Ty'RonHopper - Missouri (90%), JordanMagee - Temple (86%), D'MarcoJackson - Appalachian State (84%)

bio: Red Murdock arrived at Buffalo from Fork Union Military Academy after earning 3A Region Defensive Player of the Year at Hopewell High School in Virginia. The 6'3", 240-pound linebacker barely saw the field as a freshman in 2022, logging just six tackles across six appearances. His sophomore year in 2023 showed real promise when he cracked the starting lineup midway through the season and finished with 60 tackles, 9.5 for loss, and four forced fumbles in 12 games. Nothing extraordinary yet, but the foundation was clearly being built. His junior and senior seasons are where the production exploded. Started all 13 games in 2024 and finished second in the country with 156 tackles. Those seven forced fumbles led the nation and broke Buffalo's school record, establishing what would become his signature trait. Add in 16.5 tackles for loss, two sacks, and an interception while earning first-team All-MAC honors. His Bahamas Bowl performance against Liberty captured everything - 13 tackles, 2.5 for loss, two forced fumbles, and a pick-six for Defensive Player of the Game. Senior year in 2025 delivered another 142 tackles (second nationally again), 13.5 tackles for loss, five sacks, and six more forced fumbles. Second-team All-American recognition from Walter Camp, AP, and FWAA followed, along with a Campbell Trophy finalist nod. The capstone achievement was breaking the NCAA record with 17 career forced fumbles, more than anyone in college football history. The consistency matched the production - ten games with double-digit tackles as a junior, Butkus Award semifinalist as the only Group of Five linebacker in consideration. Academic All-MAC honors multiple times while majoring in education studies. From the 19-tackle eruption against Ball State to the record-breaking strip at Bowling Green, Murdock built his reputation through steady, violent work. He entered college as a three-star recruit who spent a year at prep school, nothing suggesting he'd rewrite record books
strengths: Attacks downhill with conviction and plays through contact at the point of attack - gap discipline stays consistent and he finishes tackles with force rather than arm-tackling. The forced fumble record represents legitimate skill, not statistical luck - actively works to strip the ball on every tackle attempt with proper technique and persistent hands. Reads his keys quickly and processes blocking schemes before they develop, positioning himself where the ball is going rather than chasing plays from behind. Sheds blockers with violent hands when offensive linemen try to wall him off - doesn't get stuck on blocks and can disengage to make plays at the second level. Tackles reliably in space using proper form instead of launching recklessly, wraps up ball carriers consistently even when they attempt to make him miss in open field. Functions effectively as a blitzer between the tackles when schemed properly, showing enough burst and power to pressure quarterbacks through manufactured gaps in protection. Motor runs hot throughout four quarters - pursues sideline to sideline even on plays flowing away from his alignment and shows up in tackle radius more than expected. Production over two years speaks clearly with consecutive 140-tackle seasons - not padding numbers but making impactful plays in critical situations that affect game outcomes.
weaknesses: Coverage skills fall off a cliff against receiving backs on wheel routes or tight ends working vertically, just doesn't have the fluid hips or change-of-direction to stay connected. Sideline-to-sideline speed leaves you wanting more - gets there eventually but lacks that extra gear to run down ball carriers once they get outside the tackle box. Man coverage is asking him to do something he's not built for, struggles to mirror backs or match route-running tight ends when isolated without help over the top. Timed speed confirms what the tape shows at 4.7 in the forty - won't chase many people down from behind at the next level when they get a step. Pass rush technique needs refinement beyond just effort and power, doesn't have the counters or hand moves to consistently beat NFL-caliber linemen when he's coming on designed pressures.
player_comparisons: JaylanFord - Texas (84%), JayHiggins - Iowa (82%), MaristLiufau - Notre Dame (82%)

bio: Both offensive guard and defensive tackle were on Kobe Baynes' resume at Sandalwood High School in Jacksonville, Florida, where he earned first-team all-conference honors under coach Adam Geis. A three-star recruit across the board from Rivals, ESPN, and 247Sports, Baynes drew interest from Power Five programs including Florida State, Arkansas, Mississippi, and South Carolina. He ranked as the 62nd offensive guard nationally and the 124th overall prospect in the state of Florida. Baynes enrolled early at Louisville in 2020 but did not see game action before eventually transferring to Kansas. His first two seasons in Lawrence were spent learning the system and waiting his turn. Baynes appeared in just three games during 2022, getting his feet wet against Tennessee Tech, at Texas, and versus Kansas State. The 2023 campaign brought more opportunity as he played in 11 games and earned three starts, including action in the Jayhawks' bowl win over UNLV. That experience proved invaluable for what was to come, as he began developing the physical, scrappy identity that would define his play in the trenches. Baynes took ownership of the right guard spot in 2024, starting all 12 games and helping Kansas average 211.4 rushing yards per game while allowing just 10 sacks as a unit. His senior year in 2025 brought individual recognition to match the on-field production. He started all 12 games again at right guard, earning All-Big 12 Honorable Mention from coaches and Second Team All-Big 12 from the AP. Over 41 career games and nearly 1,900 offensive snaps, Baynes established himself as a dependable, physical presence along the interior offensive line.
strengths: Exceptional 81.5-inch wingspan gives him natural advantages at the point of attack and allows him to redirect defenders with extended reach in pass protection. Plays with a nasty demeanor in the run game, finishing blocks through the whistle and looking for work on the second level when his initial assignment is handled. Two full seasons as a starter have refined his understanding of blocking schemes, line calls, and identifying defensive fronts pre-snap with consistency. Moves well laterally for a 315-pound interior lineman, showing quicker feet than his 5.30 forty time suggests when sliding to pick up stunts and twists. Pass blocking grades out at an excellent level, rarely allowing pressure up the middle and anchoring effectively against interior rushers who try to bull rush. Reliable hand placement in both phases keeps defenders from getting into his frame, using that length to steer defenders away from his quarterback. Run blocking production matches his aggressive mentality, consistently creating movement at the line of scrimmage and opening lanes for backs. Durability and availability stand out across his career with no notable injury history despite logging heavy snap counts over multiple seasons.
weaknesses: Average build for the position without much room to add mass. NFL defensive tackles will test that frame early and often. Testing numbers do not jump off the page. Quick-twitch interior rushers with legit get-off could give him fits at the next level. Late bloomer who needed time to develop. The upside feels capped compared to guys who dominated from day one in college. Big 12 competition has improved but he has not consistently faced the caliber of nose tackles and three-techs waiting for him on Sundays. Can get walked back when defenders win with counter moves. Needs to do a better job resetting his anchor after initial contact.
player_comparisons: MarquisHayes - Oklahoma (98%), MatthewJones - Ohio State (85%), WalterRouse - Oklahoma (84%)
name: Kobe Baynes

Moves well laterally for a 315-pound interior lineman, showing quicker feet than his 5.30 forty time suggests when sliding to pick up stunts and twists. Pass blocking grades out at an excellent level, rarely allowing pressure up the middle and anchoring effectively against interior rushers who try to bull rush. Reliable hand placement in both phases keeps defenders from getting into his frame, using that length to steer defenders away from his quarterback. Run blocking production matches his aggressive mentality, consistently creating movement at the line of scrimmage and opening lanes for backs. Durability and availability stand out across his career with no notable injury history despite logging heavy snap counts over multiple seasons.

Weaknesses:
Average build for the position without much room to add mass. NFL defensive tackles will test that frame early and often. Testing numbers do not jump off the page. Quick-twitch interior rushers with legit get-off could give him fits at the next level. Late bloomer who needed time to develop. The upside feels capped compared to guys who dominated from day one in college. Big 12 competition has improved but he has not consistently faced the caliber of nose tackles and three-techs waiting for him on Sundays. Can get walked back when defenders win with counter moves. Needs to do a better job resetting his anchor after initial contact.

bio: Kyron Drones from Shadow Creek High School was rated a 4-star recruit by ESPN and handed a 4-star grade by 247 Sports. After high school, Drones opted to join Baylor. In 2022 as a freshman Drones saw action in just 3 games for the Bears as he threw for just 219 yards on 22 attempts with a completion percent of 63.6 for an average of 10.0 yards per pass, with just one TD, while taking no sacks, and an excellent QB rating of 92.8. After the season Drones transferred to Virginia Tech where as a sophomore he played in 12 games as he passed for 1989 yards on 265 attempts with a completion percent of 57.4 for an average of 7.5 yards per pass, with 15 TDs, while also taking 21 sacks, and putting up an excellent QB rating of 95.3. He made 128 runs for a total of 781 yards at an average of 6.1 per rush.
strengths: Good zip and ball placement on the quick slant. Good touch down the seam and on post-corner, flag routes to attack the defense vertically and horizontally. Drones has a compact release and keeps his eyes up in the pocket. Drones has enough speed and athleticism to threaten with his legs when plays break down. Sprays the ball anywhere on the field, especially when given a pocket from which to deliver. Will step up into pocket while looking downfield, reset his feet and deliver. Athletic quarterback who is a threat to leave the pocket and gain positive yardage at any time. Possesses very good vision balance, elusiveness and deceptive speed and power as a runner. Generally accurate on intermediate and short throws; flashes anticipation and placement on intermediate outs and the ability to lead receivers on deeper throws. Sticks throws into tight windows over the middle, throwing to spot on slant or between zone defenders before the receiver is open.
weaknesses: Drones often holds the ball too long. While Drones keeps his eyes up while moving, his mechanics get increasingly sloppy once he’s pushed off his spot. Drones has a bad habit of fading and throwing off his back foot when the pocket gets muddled, and he is too often all arm when throwing on the move, sacrificing accuracy. Too often fails to give receivers a chance to make a play after the catch. High completion percentage padded by many quick screens.
player_comparisons: KedonSlovis - BYU (88%), KJJefferson - UCF (84%), DevinLeary - Kentucky (78%)

bio: LJ Johnson Jr. from Cy-Fair High School was rated a 4-star recruit by ESPN and handed a 4-star grade by 247 Sports. Following high school, Johnson Jr. joined Texas A&M. In 2021 as a freshman Johnson Jr. saw action in 4 games for the Aggies as he ran for just 76 yards on 21 attempts for just 3.6 yards per carry, with no TDs, with no fumbles. As a receiver he made just one reception for 14 yards. As a sophomore in 2022 he played 6 games. and rushed for just 39 yards on 10 carries for 3.9 ypc, with 2 TDs, with no fumbles. In the passing game he pulled in no receptions for no yards. After the end of the year Johnson Jr. transferred to SMU where as a junior Johnson Jr. saw action in 10 games for the Mustangs. He ran for 543 yards on 101 attempts for an excellent 5.4 ypc, with 3 TDs, with no fumbles. As a receiver he made just 5 receptions for 43 yards.
strengths: He’s equally dangerous in catch-and-run situations as Johnson is taking the top off a defense He’s solid in blitz pickup, and he consistently showed the ability to anchor against bigger players. Possesses agile feet with the patience, balance and vision to sidestep and elude defenders - nice job following his blocks and working well in tight areas. Physical at the point of attack and keeps his feet moving Johnson finishes runs well and flashes the ability to get small and squeeze through traffic. He shows suddenness off the line of scrimmage and accelerates quickly, and his top-end speed is among the best in this draft. He’s patient in the backfield, willing to wait out his blocking and not panicking, and he consistently spots cutback lanes and takes advantage. Johnson has very good vision and a knack for picking out cut-back lanes and then breaking offchunks of yardage.
weaknesses: Johnson isn’t going to overwhelm opposing defensive backs with speed and athleticism. He’s not going to threaten to take the top off of a defense. Johnson can sometimes be impatient, not allowing his blocks to set up, a nuance required for backs to last a decade-plus.
player_comparisons: MulbahCar - Houston (80%), Cam'RonHarris - Miami (75%), IsaihPacheco - Rutgers (70%)

bio: A four-star recruit out of Lake Travis High School in Texas, McRee enrolled early at USC in the spring of 2021 and played in four late-season games as a true freshman. He caught seven passes for 91 yards that year, flashing enough to earn a start against UCLA. Over the next two seasons he settled into a steady role, appearing in 25 games with six starts and posting 36 receptions for 368 yards and three touchdowns between 2022 and 2023. A torn ACL during bowl preparation at the end of 2023 threatened to derail his trajectory. McRee returned for the 2024 season opener but took a low hit in a week-four loss at Michigan that cost him roughly a month. He finished that year with 24 catches for 238 yards across ten games, earning All-Big Ten Honorable Mention despite the setbacks. His five-year commitment to USC stood out in an era of constant roster churn. Everything came together in 2025 under new tight ends coach Chad Savage. McRee set career highs with 30 receptions for 445 yards and four touchdowns, including a 62-yard score against Missouri State. His average depth of target jumped noticeably, and he looked like a different player working the seam and intermediate areas of the field.
strengths: Reliable hands throughout his career with a very low drop rate on catchable targets Wins at the intermediate level with sharp breaks and subtle route leverage Attacks the seam with confidence and can stack linebackers vertically Tough over the middle and will absorb contact to finish catches in traffic Good feel for zone coverage, sits in soft spots and gives the quarterback easy windows Pass protection numbers are outstanding, rarely charged with a pressure allowed Showed improved yards after catch in 2025, running with more purpose post-reception
weaknesses: Undersized frame raises real questions about holding up against NFL defensive ends Run blocking lacks power and his hand placement drifts under contact Ball security is a concern; fumble issues showed up in both 2024 and 2025 Can be late sitting down on underneath routes, giving defenders time to close Two ACL tears create durability risk that evaluators cannot ignore Wasted movement fitting up blocks leads to missed assignments in the run game
player_comparisons: DallinHolker - Colorado State (90%), CameronLatu - Alabama (84%), JamesMitchell - Virginia Tech (83%)

bio: Le'Veon Moss came out of Istrouma College Prep in Baton Rouge as a consensus four-star recruit, also carrying serious track credentials with a 10.84-second 100-meter time. He saw limited action as a true freshman in 2022, touching the ball 27 times for 114 yards across seven games, but earned the team's Top Offensive Newcomer Award and multiple character honors at the year-end banquet. His sophomore year brought a larger workload and better results. Moss led the Aggies in rushing yards per game at 53.8 and posted his first career 100-yard outing against Arkansas, finishing the year with 484 yards and five touchdowns in nine games before a hamstring injury cost him time. The real jump came as a junior in 2024, when he ran for 765 yards and 10 touchdowns on just 121 carries, earning All-SEC Second Team recognition. A torn MCL/ACL against South Carolina ended that season early. Back for his senior year in 2025, Moss tallied 397 yards and six scores in seven games before an ankle injury shut him down again. He opted out of combine drills, citing his recovery.
strengths: One-cut decisiveness on film; sees it, plants, and goes. No dancing behind the line. Stays on his feet through arm tackles and glancing hits at the second level. Quick first two steps get him to the hole before linebackers can trigger downhill. The Florida game showed it best: 84 of his 110 yards came after initial contact. Ran zone and power looks at A&M without a drop-off; no scheme dependency on tape. Cleaned up his ball security as a senior after some loose-ball moments in 2024. Shows real leg churn on third-and-short; falls forward and moves piles consistently. Steps up to pick up blitzers without flinching, even if his technique needs work.
weaknesses: Torn ACL/MCL in 2024, then an ankle in 2025. Back-to-back years missing real time. Gets walked back into the quarterback's lap in pass pro; technique is not there yet. Almost never targeted as a receiver, and when he was, the hands were unreliable. Downhill linebackers eat him up in the hole; he absorbs contact more than avoids it. Runs he housed at the college level often came on missed tackles, not pure speed separation. The Notre Dame tape was telling: 20 carries, 70 yards, couldn't find creases against a real front.
player_comparisons: ZachEvans - Ole Miss (68%), ZamirWhite - Georgia (65%), IsraelAbanikanda - Pittsburgh (65%)

bio: Boston College's offensive line featured a captain in 2025 who had already logged four years of starting experience across two programs. Logan Taylor arrived at Virginia as a four-star recruit in 2021 and worked his way into the starting lineup at offensive tackle by his redshirt freshman season, starting all 11 games for the Cavaliers in 2022. He transferred to Boston College ahead of 2023 and stepped in at left tackle, starting all 13 games and earning Honorable Mention All-ACC as the Eagles averaged nearly 199 rushing yards per game. Taylor moved to left guard in 2024 and earned Honorable Mention All-ACC again while starting 10 games at the position, plus one at left tackle in the Pinstripe Bowl. He did not allow a sack in 364 pass blocking snaps that season. Named captain for the first time in 2025, he started all 12 games across three positions: eight at right guard, three at left tackle, and one at right tackle. That versatility earned him Second Team All-ACC honors and a Senior Bowl invitation.
strengths: Good feel for angles on kick-out and reach blocks in the run game. Resets hands quickly and stays attached through the whistle on sustained blocks. Identifies blitzes and line games well, rarely fooled by pre-snap movement. Better than expected adjusting in space when asked to pull or climb to the second level. Strong grip strength lets him latch onto defenders and control the rep. Four-year starter at four positions, proven ability to handle multiple assignments. Kept a clean sack sheet in 2024 across 364 pass blocking snaps.
weaknesses: Plays too tall and lacks the pad level to generate consistent push at the point of attack. Limited lateral quickness makes it difficult to cut off defenders working outside. Anchor widens under bull rush, leaving him vulnerable to push-pull counters. Struggles against two-gap defenders who can stack and shed his blocks. Athletic testing confirms average movement skills for the position.
player_comparisons: TylerCooper - Minnesota (95%), WalterRouse - Oklahoma (80%), ConnorColby - Iowa (79%)

bio: Lorenzo Styles Jr. from Pickerington Central High School was rated a 4-star recruit by ESPN and handed a 4-star grade by 247 Sports. Following high school, Styles Jr. opted to join Ohio State after being heavily recruited. In 2023 as a freshman Styles Jr. saw action in just one game and played a total of one snap at cornerback for the Buckeyes. He recorded no tackles, no assists, while adding no stops. In coverage Styles Jr. notched up no pass breakups, no interceptions, and an outstanding QB rating when targetted of .0.
strengths: He’s a fluid athlete with excellent short-area quickness; Styles should be able to create separation, and he has the straight-line speed to threaten down the seam. He’s extremely dangerous in the open field, showing the vision and creativity to run through entire defenses. Ohio native, the son of a former OSU Buckeye who became a third-round pick and was on the Rams team that won Super Bowl XXXIV Made Bruce Feldman's Freak's List - ranked #79. He wrote "This offseason, the 6-1, 195-pounder squats 510 pounds, broad-jumped 10-10 1/2 and hit 22 mph on the GPS." When used downfield, he shows the ball-tracking skills and hand-eye coordination to make the adjustment on underthrown balls and back-shoulder throws. Styles has reliable hands and shows the willingness to make catches in traffic over the middle. He also has value as a catch-and-run threat, running hard and consistently picking up yards after the catch. Styles accelerates 0-to-60 and hits top speed fast enough to outrun angles. He’s also flexible and agile, able to keep the pedal to the floor as he turns and weaves. He’s extremely coordinated as a pass catcher, a natural in the screen game. He’s deadly when he’s in space, with true breakaway speed.
weaknesses: Styles really isn’t a guy who will create a lot of separation. Styles isn’t going to overwhelm opposing defensive backs with speed and athleticism. Physically, Styles profiles as a guy who should have success facing the quarterback, but he struggles to gear down and make the necessary sharp cuts to create separation underneath.

bio: Louis Moore a product of Corsicana, Texas, Moore spent three years at Navarro Community College, where he split time between wide receiver and safety. He hauled in 48 catches for 713 yards and five touchdowns across 2020 and 2021, but his defensive reps planted the seed for what was to come. He arrived at Indiana in 2022 as part of a large transfer class, saw action in 12 games as a reserve, and worked his way into a starting role by the following fall. The 2023 season was Moore's first real showcase. He started 10 games for the Hoosiers, racked up 90 tackles and three interceptions, forced three fumbles, and flashed the kind of ball-hawking instincts that would define his game. His two-pick performance against Akron, which included a 22-yard return for his first career touchdown, hinted at something special. But after the season, Moore made a surprising move, transferring to Ole Miss for the 2024 campaign. He appeared in 11 games for the Rebels with just two starts, and the numbers told the story of a player who never quite found his footing in Oxford. A 10-tackle game at Florida was the lone statistical highlight. Then came the boomerang. Moore returned to Bloomington for 2025 and immediately became the heartbeat of a defense that powered Indiana to the College Football Playoff National Championship Game. He started all 15 games, posted 87 tackles, hauled in six interceptions, and broke up five more passes. The accolades piled up: first-team All-American from CBS Sports, On3, and Sports Illustrated, second-team AP All-American, and first-team All-Big Ten from the coaches. His nine career interceptions placed him tied for 10th among active FBS players. The fact that he played the entire season under a temporary court injunction after the NCAA ruled he had exhausted his eligibility only added another layer to an already remarkable story.
strengths: Owns elite instincts reading route combinations from a deep half or single-high look, consistently jumping throws before the ball is released to the intended target. Terrific ball tracker who locates the deep ball over his shoulder without losing stride, turning potential completions into contested plays or turnovers. Smooth rotator in zone concepts who disguises his post-snap movement well enough to bait quarterbacks into throwing to covered receivers. Nine career interceptions and strong hands at the catch point tell you everything about his playmaking ability when the ball is in the air around him. Willing and physical run defender who fills gaps aggressively and does not shy away from contact near the line of scrimmage despite his smaller frame. Processes offensive formations and backfield action quickly, allowing him to trigger downhill on screens, draws, and RPOs before blockers can set up. Functions effectively as a robber over the middle, sitting on crossing routes and reading quarterback eyes to jump underneath throws with burst and anticipation. Versatile alignment history with significant snaps from the box, free safety, slot, and even along the defensive line in subpackage looks, giving coordinators lineup flexibility.
weaknesses: Below-average long speed limits his recovery ability when beaten vertically, and NFL receivers will stress that consistently at the next level. Man coverage technique remains a work in progress, particularly against shifty slot receivers and athletic tight ends who can win with nuance at the break point. Undersized frame at 5'11", 200 pounds with shorter arms creates leverage issues in space, where bigger ball carriers can run through arm tackles. Shows a visible hitch when flipping his hips out of a backpedal, costing him a half-step in transition that better route runners will exploit at the pro level. Missed tackle numbers have trended in the right direction but remain a concern, particularly when he shoots low and lunges at ball carriers instead of wrapping up.
player_comparisons: ElijahMolden - Washington (89%), MalachiMoore - Alabama (82%), JammieRobinson - Florida State (77%)
name: Louis Moore DB Indiana
scouting_report:
Background:
Louis Moore a product of Corsicana, Texas, Moore spent three years at Navarro Community College, where he split time between wide receiver and safety. He hauled in 48 catches for 713 yards and five touchdowns across 2020 and 2021, but his defensive reps planted the seed for what was to come. He arrived at Indiana in 2022 as part of a large transfer class, saw action in 12 games as a reserve, and worked his way into a starting role by the following fall. The 2023 season was Moore's first real showcase. He started 10 games for the Hoosiers, racked up 90 tackles and three interceptions, forced three fumbles, and flashed the kind of ball-hawking instincts that would define his game. His two-pick performance against Akron, which included a 22-yard return for his first career touchdown, hinted at something special. But after the season, Moore made a surprising move, transferring to Ole Miss for the 2024 campaign. He appeared in 11 games for the Rebels with just two starts, and the numbers told the story of a player who never quite found his footing in Oxford. A 10-tackle game at Florida was the lone statistical highlight. Then came the boomerang. Moore returned to Bloomington for 2025 and immediately became the heartbeat of a defense that powered Indiana to the College Football Playoff National Championship Game. He started all 15 games, posted 87 tackles, hauled in six interceptions, and broke up five more passes. The accolades piled up: first-team All-American from CBS Sports, On3, and Sports Illustrated, second-team AP All-American, and first-team All-Big Ten from the coaches. His nine career interceptions placed him tied for 10th among active FBS players. The fact that he played the entire season under a temporary court injunction after the NCAA ruled he had exhausted his eligibility only added another layer to an already remarkable story.

Strengths:
Owns elite instincts reading route combinations from a deep half or single-high look, consistently jumping throws before the ball is released to the intended target. Terrific ball tracker who locates the deep ball over his shoulder without losing stride, turning potential completions into contested plays or turnovers. Smooth rotator in zone concepts who disguises his post-snap movement well enough to bait quarterbacks into throwing to covered receivers. Nine career interceptions and strong hands at the catch point tell you everything about his playmaking ability when the ball is in the air around him. Willing and physical run defender who fills gaps aggressively and does not shy away from contact near the line of scrimmage despite his smaller frame. Processes offensive formations and backfield action quickly, allowing him to trigger downhill on screens, draws, and RPOs before blockers can set up. Functions effectively as a robber over the middle, sitting on crossing routes and reading quarterback eyes to jump underneath throws with burst and anticipation. Versatile alignment history with significant snaps from the box, free safety, slot, and even along the defensive line in subpackage looks, giving coordinators lineup flexibility.

Weaknesses:
Below-average long speed limits his recovery ability when beaten vertically, and NFL receivers will stress that consistently at the next level. Man coverage technique remains a work in progress, particularly against shifty slot receivers and athletic tight ends who can win with nuance at the break point. Undersized frame at 5'11", 200 pounds with shorter arms creates leverage issues in space, where bigger ball carriers can run through arm tackles. Shows a visible hitch when flipping his hips out of a backpedal, costing him a half-step in transition that better route runners will exploit at the pro level. Missed tackle numbers have trended in the right direction but remain a concern, particularly when he shoots low and lunges at ball carriers instead of wrapping up.

bio: Luke Altmyer grew up around football - his father Chad served as Mississippi State's team orthopedic surgeon - and that early immersion in the game shows in his cerebral approach. At Starkville High School under coach Chris Jones, Altmyer torched defenses for over 3,000 yards as a junior with a remarkable 37-to-5 touchdown ratio while completing 67 percent of his passes. His 426-yard performance against Germantown still stands as the school's single-game passing record, and those numbers earned him Elite 11 finalist honors, Under Armour All-American recognition, and unanimous four-star recruit status across all major services. The Mississippi native initially chose to stay close to home, enrolling at Ole Miss where he spent two seasons learning behind future pros Matt Corral and Jaxson Dart. After appearing in nine games across his freshman and redshirt sophomore campaigns, completing 20 of 37 passes for 317 yards and three touchdowns, Altmyer recognized the opportunity wasn't coming in Oxford. His transfer to Illinois ahead of the 2023 spring semester proved transformational for both player and program. After a respectable first season as the starter - throwing for 1,883 yards and 13 touchdowns in nine starts - Altmyer caught fire as a junior, orchestrating a magical 10-3 campaign that tied the program's single-season wins record. The 2024 season saw Altmyer evolve from game manager to game-breaker, completing 60.8 percent of his passes for 2,717 yards with 22 touchdowns against just six interceptions while adding four rushing scores. His three game-winning touchdown passes in the final two minutes or overtime - including dramatic victories at Nebraska and Rutgers - led the nation and earned him spots on the Davey O'Brien Award's Class of 2024 list and All-Big Ten Honorable Mention honors. Through five games this fall, the communication degree holder has continued building on that momentum, earning preseason All-Big Ten Second Team recognition from Phil Steele while cementing his status as one of the conference's most reliable quarterbacks. His 35 career touchdown passes rank ninth in Illinois history, and with seven more games remaining, he's positioned to climb even higher before declaring for the 2026 draft.
strengths: Elite clutch gene with five career game-winning drives in final minute/OT, doesn't shrink when stadium gets loud Releases the ball with serious velocity on deep shots, hitting receivers in stride on vertical routes consistently Shows tremendous touch dropping passes into buckets on intermediate routes between linebackers and safeties effectively Mental processor runs at warp speed, rarely holds ball past third read in progression Pocket navigation resembles a shortstop turning two, subtle movements buy crucial extra seconds consistently Tough as a two-dollar steak, bounces up from crushing hits without flinching or losing confidence Film junkie who diagnoses defensive concepts pre-snap and audibles into favorable matchups with veteran savvy Accuracy on timing routes hits different, consistently puts balls where only his receiver can make plays
weaknesses: Frame lacks ideal bulk at 205 pounds, durability concerns persist after taking three sacks against FCS competition Throwing motion dips under pressure, leading to sailed passes over the middle of field Arm talent won't blow anyone away, struggles driving ball into tight windows against zone coverage Athletic ceiling limited compared to modern dual-threat quarterbacks, won't create explosive plays with legs Pocket awareness occasionally lapses when protection holds up, taking unnecessary coverage sacks rather than throwing away

bio: Luke Hasz the consensus four-star recruit chose Arkansas over powerhouse programs like Alabama, Ohio State, and Texas, ranking as the nation's third-best tight end prospect. His twin brother Dylan made the same commitment, creating a unique family dynamic in Fayetteville that would eventually see Luke chart his own course through the transfer portal. The 2023 season showcased Hasz's immediate impact potential before a devastating collarbone injury against Texas A&M cut short what was shaping up as a spectacular freshman campaign. Through just five games, he accumulated 16 catches for 253 yards and three touchdowns, earning recognition on The Athletic's Midseason Freshman All-America team. His breakout performance came against LSU, where six catches for 116 yards and two scores earned him John Mackey Tight End of the Week honors - a rare achievement for any freshman, let alone one playing his fourth collegiate game. Following a 2024 season that saw him post 26 receptions for 324 yards and four touchdowns across 11 games at Arkansas, Hasz made the pivotal decision to transfer to Ole Miss. The move reunited him with former Razorback teammates Jaylon Braxton and Patrick Kutas while positioning him in Lane Kiffin's offense alongside established tight end Dae'Quan Wright. Pro Football Focus recognized his potential by naming him one of ten tight ends to watch for the 2026 NFL Draft, noting his 82.3 receiving grade ranks fourth among returning players and his perfect career catch rate.
strengths: Reliable hands with zero career drops create immediate trust from quarterbacks in crucial situations. Explosive straight-line speed creates significant matchup problems for linebackers unprepared for his vertical burst. Basketball background translates to exceptional body control and spatial awareness when adjusting to errant throws. Runs routes with precision timing, particularly excelling in the intermediate passing game between 10-20 yard range. Physical blocking mentality shows genuine effort to engage defenders despite needing to add functional strength. High football intelligence allows him to find soft spots in zone coverage consistently. Competitive fire burns brightest in contested catch situations where his basketball instincts take over. Versatile route tree demonstrates comfort running everything from quick slants to deep posts effectively.
weaknesses: Below-average frame measurements limit his ceiling as an in-line blocking tight end at the professional level. Lateral agility restrictions become apparent when asked to create separation on quick-breaking horizontal routes. Lacks the raw power to consistently win at the point of attack against Power Five defensive ends. Hip flexibility issues prevent him from sinking into routes as fluidly as elite pass-catching specialists. Needs significant strength gains to handle the physical demands of NFL defensive fronts consistently.

bio: Madden Sanker from South Paulding High School was rated a 4-star recruit by ESPN and handed a 4-star grade by 247 Sports. Following high school, Sanker opted to join Louisville. In 2023 as a freshman Sanker saw action in just one game and played a total of 8 snaps for the Cardinals. He allowed no QB hurries, no QB hits, and no sacks while playing sparingly. As a sophomore in 2024 he played in just 2 games and a total of 22 snaps. He conceded no QB hurries, no QB hits, and no sacks while playing most of his snaps at left guard.
strengths: Does a nice job on the second level, moving upfield quickly and showing the flexibility and balance to land blocks in space. Coordinated when moving to the second level as a run blocker, capable of landing blocks on the move. Sanker will have a chance to develop as a right tackle or possibly inside at guard, where Sanker could be an interesting prospect for a zone-blocking team. If nothing else, that versatility is enough to make him at least a quality reserve. He’ll consistently finish blocks, dropping on defenders when he has them on the ground. Sanker shows good awareness reacting to twists and stunts as a pass protector, and he has the strength to anchor against bigger defensive linemen. Outstanding on reach blocks, showing good initial quickness and flexibility to seal the backside. He’s a heady player who is quick to recognize and react to twists and stunts up front, and he shows a good sense of timing on combo blocks. He's infinitely coachable and athletically gifted with the ideal frame, physique, weight distribution, knee bend and hand strength to be molded into a special blocker.
weaknesses: Sanker often sets too tall, which will be problematic against NFL speed-to-power rushers. He’s a bit underpowered in the run game, often coming off the ball too high and failing to play with proper leverage. Has fairly heavy feet, struggling to mirror against quicker defenders. Sanker lacks the range to get out as an effective puller, and Sanker can often look stiff when moving out into space and zeroing in on bodies. He’s a bit underpowered the more he has to move in the run game, better in a phone booth than out in space.

bio: Malachi Lawrence played outside linebacker and tight end at duPont Manual High School in Louisville, Kentucky, earning first-team all-district recognition as a senior. A three-star recruit who also medaled in the state shot put, he signed with UCF in 2021 and spent two years developing before seeing limited action in three games during his 2022 redshirt freshman campaign. The 2023 season changed things. Lawrence started all 13 games as a redshirt sophomore, collecting 27 tackles, 10.5 tackles for loss, and 7.5 sacks to earn Honorable Mention All-Big 12 honors. He followed that with 11 appearances and 10 starts in 2024, adding five sacks and a strip-sack forced fumble at West Virginia. Lawrence saved his best for 2025. He posted career highs of 11 tackles for loss, seven sacks, two forced fumbles, and three pass breakups to earn All-Big 12 First Team recognition. His best game came at Baylor, where he recorded seven tackles, three tackles for loss, and two sacks. He finished his UCF career with 20 sacks, 10th on the program's all-time list, and impressed throughout the East-West Shrine Bowl week.
strengths: Deep rush plan with chops, swipes, rips, and inside counters that keeps tackles guessing. Hands work quickly to disengage and create secondary rush opportunities when initial moves stall. Natural hip flexibility lets him flatten his angle around the edge without losing speed. Explosive first step and closing burst create immediate pressure off the snap. Relentless effort in pursuit; chases quarterbacks out of the pocket and finishes on scrambles. Good length creates problems for tackles trying to get hands on him early. Combine testing confirmed rare explosiveness for the position across every measurable.
weaknesses: Run defense is a real concern; gets washed out of his gap against power schemes. Struggles to anchor when offensive linemen get into his chest on double teams. Missed tackle rate is too high; needs to be more reliable as a finisher. Light frame raises questions about adding functional mass without losing his speed advantage. Assignment discipline lapses show up on tape, particularly against misdirection and play-action.

bio: Malik Benson was a multi-sport standout in high school, posting 1,119 receiving yards and 11 touchdowns over three varsity seasons while also qualifying for the state track championships in the 100-meter, 200-meter, 4x100-relay, and long jump. He ran a 10.4-second 100 meters in high school, the kind of speed that makes scouts perk up before they even see a route tree. But the classroom wasn't his priority back then, and Benson landed at Hutchinson Community College with a promise to his mother that he'd get serious about school while betting on his talent to carry him forward. That bet paid off in a hurry. Over two seasons at Hutchinson, Benson shattered the program's all-time receiving yardage record with 2,152 yards and added 21 touchdowns on 97 receptions. His 2021 campaign alone (43 catches, 1,229 yards, 11 touchdowns) broke a single-season record that had stood since 1970, and he took home Salt City Bowl Offensive MVP honors after a seven-catch, 198-yard, two-touchdown performance. He led Hutchinson to the NJCAA National Championship Game as a sophomore and earned first-team NJCAA DI All-America recognition. The consensus No. 1 JUCO recruit in the 2023 class, Benson committed to Alabama and played in all 14 games under Nick Saban, though he was mostly used in a rotational role, finishing with 13 catches for 162 yards and a touchdown. Saban's retirement after the 2023 Rose Bowl sent Benson searching for a new home, and he landed at Florida State, where he started all 12 games and was named the team's offensive newcomer of the year. But the Seminoles cratered to 2-10, and Benson's numbers (25 catches, 311 yards, one touchdown) didn't tell the full story of a receiver stuck in a broken offense. His best game came against Memphis: five grabs for 99 yards, including a 67-yard catch that was FSU's second-longest play all season. When the NCAA's December 2024 blanket waiver granted former JUCO players an extra year of eligibility, Benson made one more move, this time to Oregon, where he would finally get the chance to play in an offense that could showcase what he does best. Standing 6-1, 195 pounds, he stepped into Eugene and became one of Dante Moore's most trusted targets, leading the Ducks in receiving yards (719 on 43 catches with six touchdowns) as they marched to the College Football Playoff semifinals. He also returned a punt 85 yards for a score against USC, caught five passes for 119 yards and two touchdowns in a first-round playoff win over James Madison, and was named The Daily Emerald's Athlete of the Season.
strengths: Blazing top-end speed that forces safeties to honor his vertical presence on every snap, warping the coverage shell before he even runs a route. Exceptional ball tracking on deep throws, adjusting his stride and body angle to locate and secure the football at full speed without breaking pace. His intermediate and deep route production took a real leap at Oregon, suggesting he needed the right system to unlock what was always there. Catch rate climbed significantly in his final season while drops nearly disappeared, showing refined concentration and improved trust from his quarterback. When targeted, quarterbacks posted strong passer ratings, confirming he creates genuine separation rather than winning on 50/50 balls alone. Played four different programs and produced at every stop, which says something about his adaptability and willingness to compete in unfamiliar surroundings. Showed clutch instincts at Oregon with a habit of delivering in tight moments, like the late-game conversion against Iowa and the seal-the-deal score in Seattle. Versatile enough to handle punt return duties and thrive there, housing an 85-yarder against USC despite being the third-string option at the position.
weaknesses: Run blocking is essentially nonexistent on tape, and at 195 pounds, he's going to struggle to hold up on the perimeter against physical press corners in the run game. Production on short and underneath routes has been limited throughout his career, raising questions about whether he can win consistently in the short-area game at the next level. Not a YAC creator in traffic; he's a linear runner after the catch who rarely breaks tackles, which limits his value on screens and quick-hitters. Three FBS programs in three years is a lot of movement, and his total target volume at the Power Four level remains relatively modest for a draft-eligible receiver. Contested catch numbers have been uneven across his career, and while he improved at Oregon, the sample size against true press coverage remains thin.

Exceptional ball tracking on deep throws, adjusting his stride and body angle to locate and secure the football at full speed without breaking pace. His intermediate and deep route production took a real leap at Oregon, suggesting he needed the right system to unlock what was always there. Catch rate climbed significantly in his final season while drops nearly disappeared, showing refined concentration and improved trust from his quarterback. When targeted, quarterbacks posted strong passer ratings, confirming he creates genuine separation rather than winning on 50/50 balls alone. Played four different programs and produced at every stop, which says something about his adaptability and willingness to compete in unfamiliar surroundings. Showed clutch instincts at Oregon with a habit of delivering in tight moments, like the late-game conversion against Iowa and the seal-the-deal score in Seattle. Versatile enough to handle punt return duties and thrive there, housing an 85-yarder against USC despite being the third-string option at the position.

Weaknesses:
Run blocking is essentially nonexistent on tape, and at 195 pounds, he's going to struggle to hold up on the perimeter against physical press corners in the run game. Production on short and underneath routes has been limited throughout his career, raising questions about whether he can win consistently in the short-area game at the next level. Not a YAC creator in traffic; he's a linear runner after the catch who rarely breaks tackles, which limits his value on screens and quick-hitters. Three FBS programs in three years is a lot of movement, and his total target volume at the Power Four level remains relatively modest for a draft-eligible receiver. Contested catch numbers have been uneven across his career, and while he improved at Oregon, the sample size against true press coverage remains thin.

bio: Malik Muhammad was a two-way standout at South Oak Cliff High School in Dallas, helping the program capture back-to-back state championships. As a senior he put up 60 tackles and 14 passes defended, returning an interception for a score in the title game. He also competed in track, posting marks of 43-6.5 in the triple jump and 18-8.5 in the long jump. A four-star recruit, Muhammad picked Texas and contributed right away as a true freshman in 2023, playing all 14 games with 31 tackles, one interception, and four pass breakups. He scooped a blocked punt for a touchdown against Oklahoma in the Red River Rivalry. As a sophomore he started all 16 games, recording 36 tackles and eight pass breakups against College Football Playoff competition including Michigan and Georgia's NFL-caliber receivers in the SEC Championship. At the Combine, Muhammad ran a 4.42 forty with the best 10-yard split among cornerbacks. He measured in with 32 3/8-inch arms and posted a 39-inch vertical and 130-inch broad jump. His change-of-direction work and ball skills during on-field drills confirmed what Texas fans already knew about his athletic profile.
strengths: Fluid hips and smooth transitions let him stay in phase through the route stem and break. Zone eyes are advanced for his age; reads quarterback intentions and triggers downhill with conviction. Combine-verified speed and burst give him the recovery ability to survive occasional missteps. Comfortable in press, off, and bail techniques within Texas' multiple coverage schemes. Willing and competitive run support defender who sets edges and plays with physicality. Arm length (32 3/8") and catch radius show up at the catch point when he is in position. Proven against top competition in the SEC and College Football Playoff without shrinking from the moment. Special teams contributor with the speed and effort to be an asset in the kicking game early on.
weaknesses: Tackling technique is inconsistent; tends to shoulder-charge rather than wrap and finish. Ball production has not matched his coverage ability; deflections have not turned into picks. Light frame at 182 pounds raises durability questions against physical receivers at the next level. Can open hips prematurely on vertical routes, giving free releases to savvy wideouts. Play-action can pull his eyes into the backfield and create windows behind him.

bio: Malik Spencer solidified his role in Michigan State's secondary during the 2023 season. The 6'1", 195-pound defensive back started 8 games at safety, recording 72 tackles and 6 pass breakups. Spencer's path to East Lansing began at Buford High School in Georgia, where he showcased his versatility, contributing at linebacker and wide receiver while helping lead his team to three consecutive state championships. A three-star recruit out of high school, Spencer was rated among the top safeties in the country by major recruiting services. He saw limited action as a freshman in 2022, appearing in just five games due to injury. However, Spencer's sophomore campaign in 2023 marked a significant leap forward, as he ranked second on the team in tackles and pass breakups while logging over 600 defensive snaps. Spencer's athletic background and positional flexibility have been evident throughout his career. At Buford High, he excelled as both a linebacker and receiver, demonstrating his ability to impact the game on both sides of the ball.
strengths: Versatile chess piece in the secondary, capable of lining up at multiple spots and contributing in various defensive packages and schemes Boasts above-average size and length for the cornerback position, providing matchup advantages against bigger receivers and tight ends Displays natural instincts as a run supporter, consistently flowing to the action and wrapping up ball carriers with textbook form Shows innate feel for zone coverage, demonstrating the ability to read quarterback progressions and break on the ball effectively Quick play recognition skills, likely developed from his experience playing multiple positions at the high school level Frame and skill set suggest potential to transition to a hybrid safety/linebacker role, offering scheme flexibility at the next level Brings valuable special teams experience and willingness to contribute in that phase, enhancing his day-one value to NFL rosters Demonstrates impressive motor and endurance, logging significant snaps on both defense and special teams throughout the 2023 season
weaknesses: Lacks ideal long speed for the cornerback position, potentially limiting his ability to recover against vertical threats at the next level Limited experience as a full-time starter, with only one season of significant playing time against high-level competition under his belt May struggle to mirror and match quicker receivers in man-to-man coverage due to average change of direction skills and hip fluidity Needs to refine his technique and situational awareness in deep coverage assignments to avoid getting caught out of position Could benefit from adding more functional strength to his frame to better shed blocks and maintain leverage against NFL-caliber opponents
player_comparisons: DellPettus - Troy (89%), MarkPerry - TCU (87%), AkeemDent - Florida State (86%)

bio: Mansoor Delane spent his freshman year at the Landon School, a small college prep academy in Bethesda better known for lacrosse than Friday night lights. Games were played on Thursday afternoons. Film sessions were lonely affairs. The fire burned, but there was no oxygen to feed it. He transferred to Archbishop Spalding High School as a sophomore seeking competition, and the move delivered exactly what he wanted. Surrounded by future Division I talent like Zakee Wheatley and Lavain Scruggs, Delane was pushed daily in ways Landon never could. He was benched early that first year, a humbling experience that shaped his approach to preparation. The lesson stuck. Three years at Virginia Tech established Delane as one of the ACC's most reliable corners. He earned True Freshman All-America honors from 247Sports in 2022 after recording eight passes defended and an interception in just eight games. His sophomore campaign saw him start all 13 contests, adding 54 tackles and another pick. The junior season brought third-team All-ACC recognition after he finished third in the league with four interceptions and added 11 passes defended and a pair of forced fumbles. By the time his Blacksburg tenure ended, he had logged 29 consecutive starts and accumulated 146 tackles, 22 passes defended, and six interceptions across 33 games. Rather than entering the 2025 draft as a projected mid-round selection, Delane made the calculated choice to transfer to LSU and test himself against SEC competition under defensive backs coach Corey Raymond. The gamble paid dividends immediately. In his Tigers debut against Clemson under the lights, he surrendered just one reception on eight targets with two pass breakups and a pick. Against Florida, six targets yielded only 11 receiving yards. By midseason, opposing quarterbacks had received the message loud and clear. Over his final four games, he was targeted just five times total. For the season, Delane posted 45 tackles, 11 passes defended, and two interceptions while allowing a completion percentage of just 26.1% on 200-plus coverage snaps. His younger brother Faheem signed with Ohio State in December 2024 as the nation's top-ranked safety prospect.
strengths: Elite coverage instincts that border on clairvoyant; he reads route combinations before they develop and positions himself in throwing lanes with uncanny consistency. Generated an absurd 27.5 passer rating when targeted in 2025, essentially turning his side of the field into a no-fly zone for SEC quarterbacks. Fluid hip turns and quick-twitch athleticism allow him to mirror receivers through their breaks without losing ground or grabbing cloth. Ball production is outstanding with eight career interceptions and 27 passes defended, proving he finishes plays when the ball is in the air. Did not allow a single touchdown in 2025 and surrendered just two receptions of 20-plus yards across the entire season. Posted zero penalties during his LSU campaign, showcasing the body control and discipline to compete physically without drawing flags. Genuine scheme versatility having thrived in Virginia Tech's zone-heavy system and LSU's more aggressive man concepts without missing a beat. Competitive toughness shows up against the run where he triggers downhill with bad intentions and wraps up through contact despite his frame.
weaknesses: Long speed is adequate but not special; true vertical burners who can threaten his cushion may create challenges that technique alone cannot solve. Below-average arm length at 30 inches could limit his ability to disrupt passing windows against taller boundary receivers in contested situations. Lean frame at 190 pounds may draw questions about durability when covering bigger slot receivers and tight ends week after week. Aggressive nature occasionally gets exploited by savvy quarterbacks who can manipulate his eyes with pump fakes on double moves. Will need to continue adding functional strength to handle the physical pounding of NFL competition over a 17-game season.
player_comparisons: Kool-AidMcKinstry - Alabama (90%), TrentMcDuffie - Washington (87%), DevonWitherspoon - Illinois (87%)
