bio: Markel Bell walked out of Cleveland Central High School in Cleveland, Mississippi, in 2022 without a single star next to his name. Zero recruiting services ranked him. No Power Five programs came calling for a kid who was already bigger than most college tackles. The combination of a small-town high school, limited camp exposure, and raw technique left Bell invisible to the recruiting world despite the kind of frame that scouts dream about. He had offers from a handful of HBCUs, but Bell bet on himself and took the junior college route, enrolling at Holmes Community College with the intention of building a resume from scratch. Two years at Holmes changed everything. Bell reshaped his body, refined his technique, and by the time the 2024 recruiting cycle rolled around, he was a completely different prospect. 247Sports rated him a four-star recruit, the No. 4 overall junior college player in the country, and the No. 1 offensive lineman in the JUCO ranks. On3 slotted him as the No. 12 JUCO prospect regardless of position and the No. 2 offensive tackle. He earned NJCAA All-Region honors after a dominant 2023 season. Programs like Arkansas, Auburn, Ole Miss, and Mississippi State all extended offers, but Bell chose Miami, where the Hurricanes were building one of the most explosive offenses in college football. His first season in Coral Gables started as a rotational role before opportunity knocked. Bell appeared in 12 games and made five starts at left tackle during Miami's 2024 campaign, filling in when Jalen Rivers dealt with injury and eventually rotating in as the coaching staff looked to develop him for the future. That offense finished No. 1 nationally in both points per game (43.9) and yards per game (537.2), and the offensive line earned a spot on the Joe Moore Award Honor Roll. Bell contributed to multiple 500-plus-yard performances, including a 750-yard explosion against Ball State. Entering 2025 as the full-time starting left tackle, Bell took a significant leap. Over 1,034 offensive snaps as the unquestioned starter, he allowed zero sacks and just one quarterback hit while posting an 83.5 pass blocking grade, a massive jump from the 63.6 he recorded as a part-time player in 2024. His season culminated in a national championship game appearance, a Senior Bowl invitation, and recognition as one of the fastest-rising offensive line prospects in the 2026 draft class.
strengths: At 6'9" with 36-plus-inch arms, Bell's dimensions are genuinely freakish; he can establish contact and steer rushers wide before they ever threaten his frame. Grip strength is a real weapon. Once Bell latches on, defenders look like they're trying to escape a bear trap, and their rush is effectively dead. The year-over-year improvement is striking. His pass blocking grade jumped from below average in 2024 to solidly good territory in 2025, showing a player whose ceiling keeps rising. Zero sacks allowed on 558 pass blocking snaps in 2025 is the headline number, and his elite pass blocking efficiency rate backs up what you see on film. Bell has developed a club move that can violently redirect a rusher's path and force them completely out of their planned attack angle. His ability to recover after losing initial contact is underrated. When he gets walked back, he resets his feet, sinks his hips, and regains ground rather than panicking. Independent hand usage has gotten noticeably sharper since arriving at Miami. He times his strikes well and can punch with accuracy to keep speed rushers at bay. On frontside kick-outs and double-team feeds, Bell brings his feet and generates enough torque to create running lanes when he stays low enough to compete at the point of attack.
weaknesses: Run blocking remains a clear liability. Below-average grades in both 2024 and 2025 tell you he consistently struggles to displace defenders and sustain blocks at the second level. His 6'9" frame creates a permanent leverage disadvantage; defenders who get underneath his pads can pry him open and neutralize his size advantage entirely. Inside counters expose him badly. He bites on outside fakes, over-sets, and then lacks the lateral quickness to redirect when rushers cross his face toward the B-gap. Limited foot speed, and it shows up on tape when he is asked to climb, pull, or reach block in space. Bell has a tendency to lean forward and punch with unset feet, which throws his balance off and creates openings for rushers to convert with counter moves.
player_comparisons: AnthonyBelton - NC State (71%), TylerGuyton - Oklahoma (71%), PatrickPaul - Houston (67%)

bio: Marlin Klein's path to Ann Arbor is unlike just about any other prospect you will find in this draft class. Born and raised in Cologne, Germany, Klein spent his first two years of high school playing American football for the Cologne Crocodiles before making the decision to uproot his entire life and move to the United States. He landed at Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School in Georgia ahead of his junior year, where he put together back-to-back productive seasons as a prep tight end, totaling 39 receptions, 601 yards, and nine touchdowns across his final two years. That was enough to earn him three-star ratings across the major recruiting services, ranking as a consensus top-30 tight end nationally and drawing Power 5 offers from Florida State, Ole Miss, West Virginia, and others before ultimately choosing Michigan. Klein's first two years in Ann Arbor were about patience and development. He appeared in just two games as a true freshman in 2022, and his sophomore season in 2023 was spent almost entirely on special teams, with only one catch for eight yards across ten appearances. The turning point came in 2024. When starter Colston Loveland missed significant time with injuries, Klein stepped into a larger role and delivered 13 catches for 108 yards across all 13 games, earning the team's Most Improved Player on Offense award. It was his junior season that signaled to coaches and scouts alike that Klein's physical tools were starting to catch up with his football understanding. By 2025, Klein was Michigan's starting tight end and a team captain. He opened the year with a career day against New Mexico: six catches, 93 yards, and his first collegiate touchdown. Although his production tailed off as the season progressed and Michigan's offense struggled to find consistency under freshman quarterback Bryce Underwood, Klein still finished with 24 receptions for 248 yards and one score in 11 games. He earned All-Big Ten honorable mention recognition from both coaches and media, and was a three-time Academic All-Big Ten selection throughout his career, a four-year letterman who appeared in 36 games overall with 12 starts at tight end.
strengths: Outstanding top-end speed for his size, the kind that forces safeties to respect the vertical threat and opens up intermediate windows underneath. Long strider who eats up ground once he gets rolling, particularly dangerous on crossers and seam routes where he can build into his speed. Strong hands at the catch point with real grip strength; he attacks the ball aggressively and plucks it away from defenders rather than body-catching. Versatile alignment profile, lining up inline, in the slot, at H-back, and even split wide, giving offensive coordinators schematic flexibility to create mismatches. Competitive and physical run blocker who brings effort and a wide base; he drove defenders down the line effectively on angle blocks throughout 2024 and 2025. Legitimate developmental upside given he only started playing organized American football in high school; there is still a lot of untapped ceiling here for the right coaching staff. Tough over the middle of the field with zero fumbles in his college career; he willingly works the seam and absorbs contact without shying away from traffic. Quick initial release off the line of scrimmage that puts defenders on their heels before they can jam him, creating early separation even when the route itself is still developing.
weaknesses: Route tree remains limited and his cuts lack the fluidity you want from a tight end who can threaten all three levels consistently at the next level. Blocking technique is a real concern despite the willingness; hand placement is erratic and he gets stacked and shed by bigger edge defenders too often on film. Production at Michigan was never overwhelming, with just 38 career receptions across four years, leaving a thin resume of work to project from confidently. Not a tackle-breaker after the catch; his yards-after-contact numbers are modest and he lacks the power or elusiveness to consistently turn short throws into chunk plays. Contested catch rate was poor in 2025, winning on just two of five truly contested opportunities, which raises questions about his ability to win at the point of attack in tight coverage.
player_comparisons: JoshWhyle - Cincinnati (87%), AJBarner - Michigan (87%), LukeMusgrave - Oregon State (82%)

Born and raised in Cologne, Germany, Klein spent his first two years of high school playing American football for the Cologne Crocodiles before making the decision to uproot his entire life and move to the United States. He landed at Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School in Georgia ahead of his junior year, where he put together back-to-back productive seasons as a prep tight end, totaling 39 receptions, 601 yards, and nine touchdowns across his final two years. That was enough to earn him three-star ratings across the major recruiting services, ranking as a consensus top-30 tight end nationally and drawing Power 5 offers from Florida State, Ole Miss, West Virginia, and others before ultimately choosing Michigan. Klein's first two years in Ann Arbor were about patience and development. He appeared in just two games as a true freshman in 2022, and his sophomore season in 2023 was spent almost entirely on special teams, with only one catch for eight yards across ten appearances. The turning point came in 2024. When starter Colston Loveland missed significant time with injuries, Klein stepped into a larger role and delivered 13 catches for 108 yards across all 13 games, earning the team's Most Improved Player on Offense award. It was his junior season that signaled to coaches and scouts alike that Klein's physical tools were starting to catch up with his football understanding. By 2025, Klein was Michigan's starting tight end and a team captain. He opened the year with a career day against New Mexico: six catches, 93 yards, and his first collegiate touchdown. Although his production tailed off as the season progressed and Michigan's offense struggled to find consistency under freshman quarterback Bryce Underwood, Klein still finished with 24 receptions for 248 yards and one score in 11 games. He earned All-Big Ten honorable mention recognition from both coaches and media, and was a three-time Academic All-Big Ten selection throughout his career, a four-year letterman who appeared in 36 games overall with 12 starts at tight end.

Strengths:
Outstanding top-end speed for his size, the kind that forces safeties to respect the vertical threat and opens up intermediate windows underneath. Long strider who eats up ground once he gets rolling, particularly dangerous on crossers and seam routes where he can build into his speed. Strong hands at the catch point with real grip strength; he attacks the ball aggressively and plucks it away from defenders rather than body-catching. Versatile alignment profile, lining up inline, in the slot, at H-back, and even split wide, giving offensive coordinators schematic flexibility to create mismatches. Competitive and physical run blocker who brings effort and a wide base; he drove defenders down the line effectively on angle blocks throughout 2024 and 2025. Legitimate developmental upside given he only started playing organized American football in high school; there is still a lot of untapped ceiling here for the right coaching staff. Tough over the middle of the field with zero fumbles in his college career; he willingly works the seam and absorbs contact without shying away from traffic. Quick initial release off the line of scrimmage that puts defenders on their heels before they can jam him, creating early separation even when the route itself is still developing.

Weaknesses:
Route tree remains limited and his cuts lack the fluidity you want from a tight end who can threaten all three levels consistently at the next level. Blocking technique is a real concern despite the willingness; hand placement is erratic and he gets stacked and shed by bigger edge defenders too often on film. Production at Michigan was never overwhelming, with just 38 career receptions across four years, leaving a thin resume of work to project from confidently. Not a tackle-breaker after the catch; his yards-after-contact numbers are modest and he lacks the power or elusiveness to consistently turn short throws into chunk plays. Contested catch rate was poor in 2025, winning on just two of five truly contested opportunities, which raises questions about his ability to win at the point of attack in tight coverage.

bio: Two-time First Team All-FCIAC and a 2019 first-team all-state selection by both the New Haven Register and Walter Camp, Matt Gulbin was a two-way lineman at Wilton High School in Connecticut under head coach E.J. DiNunzio. He racked up 47 tackles, four sacks, and 10 tackles for loss in two defensive seasons while also scoring twice on offense and serving as team captain. Despite those accolades, his recruiting profile was modest: a three-star prospect ranked No. 1,031 nationally by 247Sports, No. 5 in Connecticut's Class of 2021, and unranked by ESPN with a 76 grade. His 2020 season was limited to 7-on-7 action due to COVID-19. Gulbin signed with Wake Forest and began a steady climb through the Demon Deacons' offensive line rotation. He appeared in four games as a true freshman in 2021, playing just 14 snaps to preserve his redshirt. As a redshirt freshman in 2022, he saw action in all 13 games and contributed to Wake Forest's 8-5 season and Gasparilla Bowl victory over Missouri. His role expanded significantly in 2023, when he started 11 games at right guard and logged 706 snaps, earning a reputation as a dependable interior blocker. By 2024, he was a full-time starter at left guard for all 12 games, tallying a career-high 806 snaps and earning honorable mention All-ACC recognition. He graduated from Wake Forest with a bachelor's degree in communication in December 2024. Looking for a new challenge, Gulbin enrolled at Michigan State in January 2025 as a graduate transfer. The coaching staff moved him to center, a position he had only played sparingly at Wake Forest. The transition was seamless. Gulbin was voted team captain, named to the Rimington Trophy Watch List, and started all 11 games he played before an undisclosed injury kept him out of the regular-season finale against Maryland. Over those 11 starts, he played every single offensive snap, totaling 727 snaps at center. He earned honorable mention All-Big Ten honors from the media and was graded by PFF as the No. 2 center in the FBS, with the top run-blocking grade at the position nationally.
strengths: Absolute wall in pass protection who stalls rushers on contact; allowed only two sacks and five total pressures across 405 pass-blocking snaps in his final season at Michigan State. Re-anchors with startling quickness after absorbing a bull rush, resetting his base without losing ground or getting pulled off balance. Serious upper-body pop and grip strength that threatens pancakes on every rep; when he latches onto your chest plate, the play is over. Punishing run blocker with relentless leg drive who finishes well past the whistle and generates real displacement at the point of attack. Comfortable working combo blocks up to the second level and shows good feel for when to release and climb to a linebacker. Versatility across the interior is legitimate: 11 starts at left guard, 11 at right guard, and 12 at center across his career, plus some right tackle experience. Natural leader who was named team captain at both Wilton High School and Michigan State; teammates gravitate toward him and coaches trust him to set protections.
weaknesses: Below-average athletic testing and foot speed will be a limiting factor when trying to mirror twitchy interior rushers or redirect against stunts at the NFL level. Center experience is still relatively thin; his first full-time season at the position was 2025, and he misread blitzes and stunts multiple times, most notably against Minnesota. Strike timing and hand placement need refinement; he gets away with wide, late punches in college because of his raw power, but NFL defenders will exploit the exposed chest. Pad level creeps too high in pass protection, particularly on longer sets, which invites leverage issues against more powerful NFL nose tackles. Grip consistency is a concern: he flashes impressive latch-and-steer ability but also falls off blocks with alarming frequency, especially when his leg drive runs him past ideal positioning.

bio: Sluka, a graduate transfer from Holy Cross to UNLV, is an interesting dual-threat quarterback prospect. The 6'3", 215-pounder from Locust Valley, New York, carved out an impressive FCS career before making the jump to FBS competition. At Holy Cross, Sluka racked up 5,916 passing yards with 59 touchdowns and 3,583 rushing yards with 38 scores on the ground over four seasons. A two-time state champion in high school, Sluka originally garnered interest as a lacrosse recruit before opting to pursue football. He quickly made his mark at Holy Cross, earning Patriot League Rookie of the Year honors as a freshman. Sluka's production steadily increased, culminating in a senior campaign where he threw for 1,728 yards and 20 touchdowns while rushing for 1,247 yards and 9 scores. His rushing total led all Division I quarterbacks in 2023. Sluka's dual-threat ability reached new heights when he set the Division I single-game record for rushing yards by a quarterback with 330 against Lafayette. He capped his Holy Cross career as the Patriot League Offensive Player of the Year and finished fourth in Walter Payton Award voting for FCS Player of the Year. Now at UNLV, Sluka aims to prove he can replicate his success at the FBS level.
strengths: Elite dual-threat ability, can stress defenses with both his arm and legs Excellent rushing production, led all D-I QBs with 1,247 yards in 2023 Shows good vision and instincts as a runner, knows when to tuck and go Flashes playmaking ability, can extend plays and create outside structure Improved passing numbers each season, demonstrating development as a passer Good size at 6'3" 215 lbs, sturdy frame to absorb hits as a runner Experienced four-year starter with 24 games played, battle-tested leader Productive college career with nearly 6,000 passing yards and 3,500 rushing yards
weaknesses: Lacks experience against top-level competition, played mostly FCS opponents Passing accuracy and ball placement can be inconsistent, especially on deep throws Release speed and mechanics need refinement for the pro level Decision-making can be questionable at times, needs to improve reading defenses Will need to adjust to pro-style offense after running spread system in college

bio: Max Iheanachor (pronounced ee-hay-nah-chore) didn't touch a football until he was already old enough to vote. Born in Nigeria, he immigrated to Los Angeles at age 13 and gravitated toward basketball and soccer, the sports he knew. He was good enough on the hardwood to attract college interest, but his AAU coach saw something different in the way the kid moved at his size and pushed him toward football. Iheanachor enrolled at East Los Angeles College with virtually zero football experience and spent two seasons there learning the game from scratch, developing quickly enough to become one of the most coveted JUCO offensive tackle prospects in the 2023 class. 247Sports ranked him a Top-10 junior college prospect regardless of position, and schools like Cal, BYU, Iowa State, and Missouri came calling. The plan was Fresno State. Iheanachor had built a relationship with offensive line coach Saga Tuitele during his time at ELAC, and the two had mapped out a future together. Then Kenny Dillingham landed the Arizona State head coaching job after the 2022 season and brought Tuitele with him to Tempe. Iheanachor followed his coach. He enrolled in January 2023 to get a head start with spring practices, and when injuries decimated the Sun Devils' offensive line that fall, the big man from JUCO was thrust into the fire. He started five of six appearances as a true newcomer, logging 324 offensive snaps at right tackle. The growing pains were real, but so was the potential. From there, Iheanachor's trajectory bent sharply upward. He locked down the right tackle spot full-time in 2024, starting all 14 games as Arizona State made its run into the College Football Playoff. He logged 923 snaps that season and did not miss a single game despite undergoing offseason labrum surgery on his shoulder that had cost him all of spring ball. His 2025 campaign brought further improvement: 860 snaps, zero sacks allowed on 484 pass blocking reps, and a Second-team All-Big 12 selection. He capped his college career with a dominant showing at the Senior Bowl, going toe-to-toe with some of the best edge rushers in the country. Over three seasons in Tempe, Iheanachor accumulated 2,107 offensive snaps across 32 games, an impressive workload for someone who picked up the sport barely five years ago.
strengths: His feet are absurdly quick for a 325-pound man; the basketball and soccer background shows up in every lateral slide and redirect he makes at the line of scrimmage. Carries legitimate length at 34.5-inch arms, giving him a wide catch radius that forces rushers into uncomfortable angles before they can threaten his chest plate. Natural hip fluidity allows him to mirror speed off the edge and stay connected through the arc of the rush without lunging or losing his base. Tailor-made for zone blocking concepts where his ability to seal front side, climb to linebackers, and win cutoff angles on the backside all show up consistently on tape. Showed real power at the point of attack when he stays square; his initial punch can visibly knock defenders off their spot and reset the line of scrimmage. Allowed zero sacks and just three quarterback hits across nearly 500 pass blocking snaps in 2025, posting a 98.5% pressure efficiency rate that quietly ranked among the best in the Big 12. The Texas Tech tape is the best calling card in his portfolio: he blanketed Romello Height and David Bailey, two of the more dynamic edge rushers in college football, surrendering just two pressures all game. Competitive temperament runs hot; he finishes blocks with a meanness that coaches love and carries an edge that never lets him get pushed around, even against more technically refined defenders.
weaknesses: His hand timing is still catching up to the rest of his tools; the initial punch lands late or wide too often, which lets quicker defenders into his chest and draws holding flags (16 penalties in three seasons). Recognition against stunts and twists remains a real liability, as he can get caught flat-footed when defensive fronts start gaming and shifting responsibilities mid-play. Run blocking grade lagged well behind his pass protection marks; pad level stays too high on drive blocks, sapping the leg drive needed to move bodies at the point of attack. Tends to overset against speed rushers to protect the outside, which opens a lane for the inside counter move that NFL edge defenders will exploit relentlessly. Second-level sustain is inconsistent: he can climb to linebackers well enough, but too often he shoves and disconnects rather than locking on and finishing through the whistle.
player_comparisons: AbrahamLucas - Washington State (88%), RogerRosengarten - Washington (87%), MattGoncalves - Pittsburgh (84%)

bio: Michael Harris from Lake Brantley High School was rated a 4-star recruit by ESPN and handed a 4-star grade by 247 Sports. After high school, Harris joined Maryland after being heavily recruited. In 2023 as a freshman Harris saw action in 8 games and played a total of 70 snaps at linebacker for the Terrapins. He recorded just 7 tackles, 2 assists, while adding 4 stops. In coverage Harris notched up one pass breakup, no interceptions, and QB rating when targetted of 60.4. As a sophomore for the Terrapins in 2024 Harris played in 4 games and took part in 74 snaps for the Terrapins. He recorded just 7 tackles, 2 assists, while adding 4 stops. As a pass rusher, he chalked up 4 total pressures, and 2 QB hurries, one QB hit, and one sack during the year.
strengths: He’s rangy and instinctive when he’s looking into the backfield as a zone defender. Harris has a compact build, but Harris is a crafty edge player who is very good against the run. He sets the edge and effectively wards off blocks with a strong core, powerful limbs, and natural leverage. He has good quickness and an excellent understanding of angles, often beating blockers to the spot. An undersized but explosive athlete, Is a sideline-to-sideline playmaker. He’s a fluid mover who reads, reacts and accelerates quickly. Keeps feet moving after initial contact. Jumps inside as a counter to typical rush, but needs to show a larger variety of moves. Harris has ability as a blitzer, with the length and flexibility to get around the edge.
weaknesses: He’ll need to be covered up to be effective. He’s not the kind of guy you can line up in the pass rush and have him bend the edge; he’s limited to shooting through gaps. Harris is on the small side, and his frame is pretty much maxed out. Despite his effort and intensity, he’ll simply get swallowed up at times. Harris is on the small side and struggles to slip blocks; he’ll need to be covered up in order to make plays.

bio: Michael Taaffe walked on at Texas as a zero-star recruit from Westlake High School in Austin, turning down offers from Rice, Brown, and Colgate because he wanted to play for the program his family had attended for five generations. At Westlake, he won back-to-back Texas 6A state championships in 2019 and 2020, taking defensive MVP in both title games. His most notable prep moment was picking off future Texas quarterback Quinn Ewers twice in the 2020 state championship. Taaffe redshirted in 2021 and played 13 games as a redshirt freshman in 2022, earning a scholarship before the Alamo Bowl. His 2023 season changed things: nine starts, 48 tackles, and interceptions in three straight games against Houston, BYU, and Kansas State. He earned honorable mention All-Big 12 and a Burlsworth Trophy nomination. He started all 16 games in 2024 during Texas's College Football Playoff run, posting 78 tackles, 5.5 tackles for loss, two sacks, two interceptions, and 10 pass breakups en route to AP All-America Second Team honors. He returned for a fifth year in 2025, collected 70 tackles and two interceptions in 10 starts despite missing two games with a broken thumb, and won the Wuerffel Trophy before declaring for the draft.
strengths: Coverage instincts are the headliner; reads quarterback eyes from depth and jumps routes with rare anticipation. Runs the pre-snap operation, communicating checks, passing off motions, and aligning teammates with real authority. Fluid in his backpedal with clean hip transitions; mirrors routes from two-high shells without false steps. Seven career interceptions with natural ball tracking over his shoulder and clean hands at the catch point. Cut his missed tackle rate from 21% in 2024 down to 9.3% in 2025, showing genuine coachability. Meaningful snaps at free safety, in the box, and in the slot across multiple coverage schemes over four years. Over 500 career special teams snaps including a blocked punt; immediate NFL value as a gunner and coverage unit piece. Shows up in big games: nine tackles against Georgia in 2024, an interception and forced fumble at Vanderbilt.
weaknesses: At 190 pounds, he gets swallowed by tight ends and pulling guards near the line of scrimmage. Recovery speed is limited when beaten vertically; functional quickness but no margin for error in man coverage. Drops his eyes on contact and can overrun pursuit angles against outside zone, opening cutback lanes. The 2024 missed tackle rate of 21% on 89 attempts clouds the improved 2025 numbers and raises consistency questions. When Texas lost him for two games with a broken thumb in 2025, the secondary's coverage busts spiked noticeably.

name: Michael Taaffe DB Texas
scouting_report:
Background:
Michael Taaffe walked on at Texas as a zero-star recruit from Westlake High School in Austin, turning down offers from Rice, Brown, and Colgate because he wanted to play for the program his family had attended for five generations. At Westlake, he won back-to-back Texas 6A state championships in 2019 and 2020, taking defensive MVP in both title games. His most notable prep moment was picking off future Texas quarterback Quinn Ewers twice in the 2020 state championship. Taaffe redshirted in 2021 and played 13 games as a redshirt freshman in 2022, earning a scholarship before the Alamo Bowl. His 2023 season changed things: nine starts, 48 tackles, and interceptions in three straight games against Houston, BYU, and Kansas State. He earned honorable mention All-Big 12 and a Burlsworth Trophy nomination. He started all 16 games in 2024 during Texas's College Football Playoff run, posting 78 tackles, 5.5 tackles for loss, two sacks, two interceptions, and 10 pass breakups en route to AP All-America Second Team honors. He returned for a fifth year in 2025, collected 70 tackles and two interceptions in 10 starts despite missing two games with a broken thumb, and won the Wuerffel Trophy before declaring for the draft.

Strengths:
Coverage instincts are the headliner; reads quarterback eyes from depth and jumps routes with rare anticipation. Runs the pre-snap operation, communicating checks, passing off motions, and aligning teammates with real authority. Fluid in his backpedal with clean hip transitions; mirrors routes from two-high shells without false steps. Seven career interceptions with natural ball tracking over his shoulder and clean hands at the catch point. Cut his missed tackle rate from 21% in 2024 down to 9.3% in 2025, showing genuine coachability. bio: Michael Trigg was a consensus four-star recruit out of Seffner Christian Academy in Tampa, Florida, where he starred as both a pass-catching tight end and a defensive end, piling up nearly 1,900 all-purpose yards and 29 touchdowns as a senior. He was also a standout basketball player, averaging over 20 points per game as an underclassman. Trigg committed to USC and saw limited action as a true freshman in 2021, catching seven passes for 109 yards and a touchdown across four games. He transferred to Ole Miss as the top-rated tight end in the portal and scored three receiving touchdowns in his first game against Central Arkansas in 2022, setting a school record. Injuries hampered his time in Oxford, and after a redshirt season in 2023 with just four catches, Trigg moved on again to Baylor. His 2024 redshirt junior year produced 30 catches for 395 yards and three touchdowns, earning Second-Team All-Big 12 recognition. The 2025 season was comfortably his best. Through 11 games, Trigg posted 50 receptions for 694 yards and six touchdowns, including a 155-yard outing against Kansas State that set a Baylor single-game record for tight ends. He also turned heads at the NFL Combine by recording the largest wingspan ever measured at the position.v
strengths: Record-setting wingspan and catch radius create an enormous target window for quarterbacks. Moves with real fluidity for his size, sinking his hips and snapping out of breaks cleanly. Wins vertically against linebackers who cannot match his speed up the seam. Dangerous at every level of the route tree, with his best work coming on intermediate concepts. High-points the ball well and extends to make catches outside his frame. Good contact balance after the catch, grinding through arm tackles for extra yards. Has real speed for the position, enough to stress safeties down the seam on vertical stems. Alignment flexibility to line up inline, in the slot, or split wide.
weaknesses: Run blocking is a real problem, with poor pad level and inconsistent hand placement. Light frame at 240 pounds limits his ability to anchor against NFL edge setters. Too many concentration drops in 2025 after cleaning that up the year before. Does not win enough 50/50 balls given his length and catch radius advantage. Can get redirected off his route stem by physical press coverage. Three schools in five years raises fair questions about consistency and buy-in.
player_comparisons: SamLaPorta - Iowa (87%), GregDulcich - UCLA (86%), ErickAll - Iowa (80%)

bio: A senior season at Stone Bridge High School in Ashburn, Virginia, that produced 34 tackles for loss and 15 sacks earned Mikail Kamara Region 5A Defensive Player of the Year honors and helped the Bulldogs reach back-to-back state championship games. A three-star recruit, he signed with James Madison and started six games as a true freshman during the 2020 spring season before a 2021 injury cost him an entire year. Kamara returned in 2022 but was limited to five games due to another injury, recording four sacks in that short stretch. Healthy in 2023, he started 12 games and posted 17.5 tackles for loss and 6.5 sacks, earning Second Team All-Sun Belt honors. That production caught the attention of bigger programs. He followed head coach Curt Cignetti to Indiana in 2024 and delivered immediately: 10 sacks, 15 tackles for loss, and First Team All-Big Ten recognition. He returned for the 2025 season and helped lead the Hoosiers to a national championship, earning CFP Championship Game Defensive MVP. Despite that resume, he was notably left off the Scouting Combine invite list.
strengths: Explosive first step off the snap consistently beats offensive tackles to the edge. Natural leverage at 6'1" allows him to get underneath taller blockers' pads. Refined pass rush plan with counters, including a violent swim and speed-to-power conversion. Ankle flexibility and bend around the arc let him flatten his rush path to the quarterback. Career pressure totals climbed each healthy season, peaking at 68 in 2024 against Big Ten tackles. Effective as a run defender when asked to penetrate gaps and disrupt in the backfield. Relentless effort on every snap with consistent backside pursuit and hustle to the ball. Proven ability to force fumbles in critical moments, with seven across his college career.
weaknesses: Undersized frame with below-average length raises real concerns about holding the edge against NFL tackles. Coverage snaps were a disaster; tight hips and stiff backpedal limit any drop responsibilities. Sack production fell sharply in 2025, converting just two of his 59 pressures into sacks. Gets washed down the line against combo blocks and struggles to hold his ground at the point of attack. Two separate injuries have cost him significant time, and a 2025 shoulder issue needs medical clearance. Base tends to narrow against power, limiting anchor strength on early downs.
player_comparisons: KaimonRucker - North Carolina (93%), JoshPaschal - Kentucky (82%), JosaiahStewart - Michigan (77%)

bio: Miles Kitselman, the 6-foot-5, 255-pound pass catcher spent his high school days playing offensive tackle and defensive end at Lyndon High School before his father and uncle convinced him to try his hand at tight end. That position change happened at Hutchinson Community College, where he played just three games during the 2021 season before Alabama came calling with a late scholarship offer for the 2022 recruiting class. Kitselman's time in Tuscaloosa was defined by patience rather than production. He caught two passes for 18 yards in his debut against Utah State and then essentially vanished from the offensive game plan, appearing in six games as a sophomore and all 13 as a junior without recording a single statistic during that 2023 campaign. When the opportunity came to transfer to Tennessee for the 2024 season, Kitselman seized it with both hands. Despite being ranked just the 50th-best tight end in the transfer portal by 247Sports, he outperformed four-star addition Holden Staes and locked down the starting job immediately. The 2024 campaign validated every bit of that decision. Kitselman started all 13 games for Tennessee's College Football Playoff squad, catching 22 passes for 301 yards and four touchdowns while adding a rushing score against Georgia from a fullback alignment. He was named to the 2025 Senior Bowl Top 300 List and earned a spot on the 2023 Fall SEC Academic Honor Roll. An NCAA waiver granted him a fifth year of eligibility, allowing him to return to Knoxville for 2025, where he added 26 receptions for 253 yards and two more scores before a lower-body injury ended his regular season against New Mexico State in November.
strengths: Brings prototypical NFL size at 6-foot-5 and 255 pounds with a frame that can hold additional weight at the next level without sacrificing mobility. Releases off the line of scrimmage with urgency and builds to a legitimate top-end speed that challenges second-level defenders down the seam. Decelerates exceptionally well for a player his size, allowing him to sit down in zones and convert on intermediate comebacks and stop routes. Converts at an absurd rate on possession downs, with 17 of 22 catches going for first downs in 2024 and 15 of 26 moving the chains in 2025. Functions as a sledgehammer in the run game when asked to get downhill and seal linebackers or defensive backs on perimeter blocks. Shows the competitive fire and willingness to do the dirty work that offensive line coaches will fall in love with during the interview process. Demonstrates a sturdy anchor in pass protection that prevents rushers from collapsing the pocket even when he gets beat initially off the snap. Proved he could be a leader when Tennessee's roster churned following Nico Iamaleava's departure, stepping up as a team captain and mouthpiece for the locker room.
weaknesses: Lacks the loose hips and lateral agility to generate consistent separation at the top of his routes, limiting his ceiling as a receiving threat. Ball security took a concerning dip in 2025, with five drops on 42 targets after recording zero drops on 27 targets the previous season. Had three Joey Aguilar interceptions bounce off his hands during the 2025 campaign, and while not all were easy catches, the frequency was alarming. Plays with pad level that runs high in the run game, which limits his ability to generate push against physical SEC linebackers like Georgia's CJ Allen. Contact balance after the catch is below average, and he rarely makes defenders miss in the open field to generate chunk plays on his own.
player_comparisons: DerrickDeese Jr. - San Jose State (82%), CadeOtton - Washington (75%), CameronLatu - Alabama (74%)

bio: Bishop Alemany in Mission Hills produced a consensus four-star quarterback in Miller Moss, who earned PrepStar Dream Team honors and a Southern California News Group Fab 15 first-team selection while growing up in Pacific Palisades, California. As a junior, he threw for over 3,100 yards and 28 touchdowns, earning Cal-Hi Sports All-State recognition and the Los Angeles Daily News Offensive Player of the Year award. Moss enrolled early at USC in 2021 and spent three seasons backing up Caleb Williams. His breakout came in the 2023 Holiday Bowl, where he started in place of Williams and threw for 372 yards and six touchdowns in a win over Louisville, setting multiple USC and Pac-12 bowl records. He won the starting job for 2024, opened with a strong showing in a victory over LSU, but struggled through a five-loss stretch and was eventually benched after nine starts. He finished the year with 2,555 yards, 18 touchdowns, and nine interceptions. Moss transferred to Louisville for his final season, playing under Jeff Brohm. He threw for 2,679 yards and 16 touchdowns against seven interceptions across 28 games played in his career at both schools. He earned Johnny Unitas Golden Arm Award Top 10 and Davey O'Brien Great 8 honors.
strengths: One of the quickest releases in this class, with a consistent sub-2.65 second time to throw. Deep ball accuracy is a genuine strength, placing the ball where only his receiver can get it. Comfortable working through progressions and getting to his second or third read without hesitation. Sells play-action convincingly and keeps his eyes downfield through the fake. Willing pocket passer who hangs in against pressure and delivers on time. Threw well on the move more than expected at Louisville, especially on designed bootlegs. Handles full audible and protection responsibilities, commanding the line before the snap.
weaknesses: Arm is functional but not a weapon. Deep shots need perfect timing or receivers adjust. Carries the ball loose in the pocket. Got caught cradling it low too often at Louisville. Forced balls into coverage more frequently as the 2025 season wore on. Trust issues with reads. Narrow frame that absorbs contact poorly. Gets knocked off his spot by interior pressure. Went down too easy at Louisville. Did not sense or slide away from edge pressure well. The 2025 tape is a clear step back from his best work at USC in terms of overall throw quality.
player_comparisons: EJPerry - Brown (75%), DustinCrum - Kent State (65%), MichaelPratt - Tulane (65%)

bio: Nadame Tucker a basketball kid out of Teaneck High School in New Jersey, didn't even put on a football helmet until his senior year. He averaged nearly 14 points a game on the hardwood across two varsity seasons and looked headed toward a basketball future before something pulled him toward the gridiron. That raw, late-arriving athleticism would become the foundation of everything that followed, though it took years and a winding road through the JUCO ranks to figure that out. Tucker landed at Independence Community College in 2020, where he recorded 12 tackles and two sacks in seven games before moving on to Hutchinson Community College the following year. It was at Hutchinson where things started clicking. He racked up 50 tackles, 19.5 tackles for loss, and 12 sacks across 11 games, earning Defensive MVP honors at the Salt City Bowl after a seven-tackle, 1.5-sack performance. That production attracted Power Five attention, and Tucker signed with the University of Houston as a mid-year enrollee. But the move to the Big 12 didn't go as planned. Over three seasons with the Cougars from 2022 to 2024, he appeared in just 13 games, totaled only 10 tackles, and never recorded a single sack at the FBS level. Then came Kalamazoo. Tucker transferred to Western Michigan for his sixth college season and absolutely detonated. In 2025, he tied for the FBS lead with 14.5 sacks, added 21 tackles for loss, earned MAC Defensive Player of the Year and the Vern Smith Leadership Award as the conference's MVP, and picked up second-team All-American recognition. He anchored a Western Michigan defense that propelled the Broncos to a MAC championship. Tucker then carried that momentum to the Senior Bowl, where he was the only MAC representative invited and proceeded to dominate the week of practices before closing with two sacks, three tackles for loss, a forced fumble, and five total tackles in the game itself. The strip-sack he ripped off Boston College tackle Judy Bowry in the fourth quarter was the play of the afternoon.
strengths: First-step explosion off the snap is genuine and repeatable, giving tackles fits before they can settle into their sets. Hand timing is advanced for a player with so little experience; his swipes land on time and knock away blockers' punches with precision. Bends and flattens around the corner like a player 15 pounds lighter, keeping his pad level low through the arc of his rush. Hands and feet work in concert in a way you rarely see from one-year producers; the coordination between upper and lower body feels natural. Forced four fumbles in 2025 and added the strip-sack highlight at the Senior Bowl, showing a knack for finishing with ball disruption. Closes on the quarterback with real burst once he turns the corner, converting pressures into sacks at a strong rate throughout the season. Lateral agility and short-area quickness allow him to redirect and finish on plays where the pocket slides away from his initial rush lane. Showed glimpses of coverage ability when asked to drop into space on limited reps, moving his feet well enough to stay connected to routes underneath.
weaknesses: One full season of FBS production is an extremely thin sample, and three dormant years at Houston raise questions about what kept him off the field. Undersized frame at 250 pounds with below-average length gets swallowed up by jump-sets from longer tackles who can smother his rush arc. Run defense is a clear liability right now; he plays too far under blocks, loses leverage, and gets washed out of his contain lane consistently. Missed tackles piled up at a concerning rate in 2025, and his tackling technique lacks the consistency needed to hold up on every down. Lacks a reliable counter move once his initial rush gets stalled at the apex; when the speed rush is taken away, his plan B is undeveloped.
player_comparisons: JonahElliss - Utah (90%), KaimonRucker - North Carolina (90%), RonniePerkins - Oklahoma (84%)

Lacks a reliable counter move once his initial rush gets stalled at the apex; when the speed rush is taken away, his plan B is undeveloped.

bio: Nicholas Singleton arrived at Penn State as the consensus No. 1 running back in the 2022 recruiting class, a five-star product of Governor Mifflin High School in Reading, Pennsylvania. He earned Gatorade National Player of the Year honors after rushing for 2,043 yards and 41 touchdowns as a high school senior, and every major program wanted him. He chose the Nittany Lions and shared the backfield with fellow freshman Kaytron Allen from day one. Singleton rushed for 1,057 yards and 12 touchdowns as a true freshman to earn Big Ten Freshman of the Year. A banged-up sophomore season saw his efficiency dip (753 yards, 4.4 YPA), but he expanded his receiving role with 26 catches. His junior year was his best: 1,106 rushing yards at 6.4 per carry, 41 receptions for 375 yards, and 17 total touchdowns. He returned for 2025 rather than entering the Draft, but Allen seized the lead role and Singleton's production fell to 547 yards on 124 carries. He still scored 13 times and finished as Penn State's all-time leader in touchdowns (55) and all-purpose yards (5,586). A fifth metatarsal fracture at the Senior Bowl required surgery and kept him from testing at the Combine.
strengths: Gets to top speed in a hurry and erases pursuit angles once he hits the second level. Runs angry through contact, falls forward, and is a load to bring down near the goal line. Took care of the football better each year, going from three fumbles as a freshman to zero as a senior. Soft hands out of the backfield and his drop rate improved noticeably over four seasons. Squares up blitzers with real intent in pass protection, no hesitation picking up the free runner. Press the hole, one cut, go. His decisiveness in zone concepts is the best thing on his tape. Verified squat north of 650 pounds, a Penn State program record. The strength shows up on contact.
weaknesses: Does not make people miss. His elusiveness fell off a cliff from freshman year to senior year on tape. Straight-line athlete who gets stiff when asked to redirect or chain cuts together in traffic. Misses cutback lanes on gap runs more than you would like, leaving yards on the table. Yards after contact nearly halved from freshman to senior season, a troubling downward trend. Longest run as a senior was 59 yards, down from 87 as a freshman. The home run threat has faded. Broken fifth metatarsal at the Senior Bowl adds to a pattern of nagging injuries dulling his burst.

bio: Noah Whittington grew up in Fort Valley, Georgia, where he put together a standout prep career at Peach County High School, finishing with 1,554 rushing yards, 30 touchdowns, and 8.0 yards per carry. He was also a capable pass catcher out of the backfield with 60 receptions for 776 yards and seven scores. Despite that production, the recruiting services weren't exactly beating down his door. ESPN gave him three stars and a 73 rating, while 247Sports and Rivals both pegged him as a two-star prospect. He signed with Western Kentucky in the 2020 class and wasted no time getting on the field, appearing in all 12 games as a true freshman, though his role was largely limited to special teams and spot duty (16 carries, 39 yards). Things changed quickly in his sophomore year at WKU. Whittington took over as the Hilltoppers' lead back, rushing for 617 yards on 101 carries with two touchdowns across 12 games and seven starts. He averaged 6.1 yards per carry, good for third in Conference USA, and ripped off 18 runs of 10-plus yards, including an 86-yard touchdown in the Boca Raton Bowl against Appalachian State where he piled up 150 yards on just seven carries. That performance, combined with his steady production, earned him a transfer ticket to Oregon, where he landed ahead of the 2022 season. His first year in Eugene was his most complete. Playing in all 13 games with one start, he finished second on the team with 779 rushing yards and five touchdowns on 139 carries, adding 22 receptions for 169 yards and another score. He recorded a strong 79.9 overall grade from PFF that year with an 82.7 rushing mark. Whittington's 2023 junior campaign was cut short after just four games due to injury, though in limited action he looked sharp (148 yards and two touchdowns on 20 carries, plus 10 catches for 78 yards). He took a redshirt and returned in 2024, playing all 14 games and producing 540 rushing yards with six touchdowns and 24 receptions for 136 yards. His signature play that year came against Boise State, when he housed a 100-yard kickoff return in the fourth quarter to help spark Oregon's comeback, tying for the second-longest return in program history. In 2025, as a sixth-year senior, he put together his best statistical season: 829 rushing yards and six touchdowns on 129 carries (6.4 YPC) with 19 receptions for 98 yards. He earned an 83.6 overall offensive grade and an 88.5 rushing grade from PFF, both career highs. Over his full career, Whittington accumulated 2,986 rushing yards and 21 touchdowns on 527 carries with 88 receptions for 508 yards.
strengths: Runs with excellent pad level and leverage, using his compact frame to duck under contact and churn through arm tackles at the second level. Vision between the tackles is above average; he reads blocks well, lets lanes develop, and trusts his offensive line before committing to a hole. Ball security is outstanding across his career, fumbling only three times on 527 carries, and his 2025 film showed zero fumbles on 129 attempts. Quick lateral cut ability allows him to sidestep traffic at the line of scrimmage; his jump-cut is sudden and keeps defenders off balance in tight quarters. Tough, physical runner who falls forward consistently and converts marginal gains into positive yardage through sheer effort and lower-body drive. Former high school wide receiver background shows up in his route running out of the backfield, particularly on option routes where his footwork freezes linebackers. Experienced kick returner who has shown big-play ability on special teams, including that electric 100-yard return against Boise State in 2024. Improved significantly year over year at Oregon, with his best rushing performance coming in his final season despite sharing a backfield and facing Big Ten competition.
weaknesses: Undersized at 5'8" and 203 pounds, which puts him in the bottom tier of the position physically and limits his utility in short-yardage and goal-line packages. Despite testing well in the forty, his on-field acceleration and top-end burst rarely match that number; he wins with craft, not explosiveness, and breakaway runs are scarce. Pass protection technique is raw and inconsistent; he tends to lunge forward and bend at the waist, giving up leverage to oncoming rushers instead of sitting into contact. Receiving production flatlined over his Oregon career, with declining yards per route run and a concerning number of drops and double-catches on routine targets. Turns 25 before training camp, which is a significant red flag for a running back trying to earn a roster spot at a position where teams covet youth and upside.

bio: Nolan Rucci was born into football royalty. The Lititz, Pennsylvania native is the son of Todd Rucci, a second-round pick out of Penn State who went on to start for the New England Patriots across eight NFL seasons. His mother Stacy was a first-team All-American field hockey player for the Nittany Lions, and his grandfather Thomas Gilburg suited up for four years with the Baltimore Colts after starring at Syracuse. Brother Hayden played tight end at Wisconsin. Football was always going to be the path for Nolan, and he emerged from Warwick High School as one of the most coveted offensive line prospects in the 2021 recruiting cycle. Standing 6-foot-8 and carrying the frame to eventually fill out north of 300 pounds, Rucci earned five-star status from 247Sports and Rivals while pulling a four-star grade from ESPN. He was the No. 17 overall prospect nationally, the fifth-ranked offensive tackle, and the top recruit in the state of Pennsylvania. The All-American Bowl selection had his pick of programs and initially chose Wisconsin over his family's alma mater. The Madison years did not unfold as expected. Rucci appeared in just six games across three seasons with the Badgers, logging a mere 70 snaps in reserve duty. His lone memorable moment came during a 2023 road win at Illinois when he caught a three-yard touchdown pass as a tackle-eligible receiver. Despite the elite recruiting pedigree, the playing time never materialized under the Wisconsin staff. Following the 2023 season, Rucci entered the transfer portal and made the decision his parents had hoped for all along. He committed to Penn State in January 2024, joining the program where his father had built his reputation and where his family legacy remained strong. The return to Happy Valley reignited Rucci's career. In 2024, he appeared in all 16 games for a Penn State squad that reached the College Football Playoff National Championship Game. He made six starts at right tackle during the playoff run after Anthony Donkoh went down with an injury, earning some of Pro Football Focus's highest grades among offensive linemen during that postseason stretch. The Nittany Lions dominated on the ground throughout the year, rushing for 222 yards against West Virginia, 292 against Oregon in the Big Ten Championship Game, and 189 yards against SMU's fourth-ranked rush defense. Rucci picked up Academic All-Big Ten honors and returned for his fifth-year senior season in 2025, appearing in all 16 games again with six starts while competing alongside the returning Donkoh and rising redshirt sophomore J'ven Williams. Head coach James Franklin praised his preparation habits and leadership, with left tackle Drew Shelton calling him "that big brother, always looking out for everybody." After 28 games across two productive seasons in Happy Valley, Rucci declared for the 2026 NFL Draft.
strengths: Elite length and frame give him natural advantages at the point of attack; his 6-foot-8 build with 80-inch reach creates problems for shorter edge rushers trying to corner him. Sets a wide, low base in pass protection that makes him difficult to bull rush; defenders struggle to generate push against his anchor when his feet are set. Initial punch arrives with accuracy and pop; when he lands his hands inside, he can jolt defenders and control the engagement from snap to whistle. Executes combo blocks effectively and can climb to the second level on zone concepts; showed ability to scrape linebackers and lead on screen plays at Penn State. Patient in pass sets and trusts his length; does not lunge or guess against speed rushers, letting them come to him before redirecting. Absolutely mauled overmatched competition when he could get position; turns defenders completely out of running lanes with sustained drive blocking. Football bloodlines show in his preparation habits; consistently praised as one of the first players in the facility and last to leave the meeting room. Tough and physical demeanor at the point of attack; plays with an edge and shows willingness to finish blocks through the whistle in the run game.
weaknesses: Feet appear heavy and somewhat labored when required to mirror quick-twitch rushers who work from inside to outside; lateral agility is a legitimate concern. Pad level can get elevated against physical defenders, especially when working in space; loses leverage advantages that his frame should provide. Limited explosiveness off the snap restricts his effectiveness on outside zone and reach concepts where first-step quickness determines outcomes. Experienced just one season of consistent starting reps after limited action across three years at Wisconsin; development timeline remains a question mark. Weight distribution at 307 pounds sits light for his frame; needs to continue adding functional mass without sacrificing whatever movement skills he possesses.

bio: A consensus four-star prospect out of Dillard High School in Fort Lauderdale, Florida, Nyjalik Kelly piled up 25 sacks across his junior and senior seasons and earned All-Broward first-team recognition from the Miami Herald while helping Dillard to a 12-1 record and a district championship. Standing 6'5" and weighing 250 pounds, he had his pick of blue-blood programs, choosing Miami over scholarship offers from Auburn, Clemson, Georgia, LSU, Michigan, Oregon, and Texas. ESPN slotted him as the No. 119 overall prospect nationally, while 247Sports had him at No. 84 in their composite rankings. Kelly's time in Coral Gables was a mixed bag. As a true freshman in 2022, he saw the field in all 12 games and delivered four sacks and 4.5 tackles for loss, tying for third on the Hurricanes in sacks. It was a promising debut, but his sophomore year was cut short by injury. He appeared in just five games in 2023, managing only eight tackles and one tackle for loss before missing the second half of the season. That lost development time loomed large, and Kelly eventually made the decision to transfer to UCF. The move to Orlando breathed new life into his career. Under Scott Frost, Kelly immediately became a fixture on the Knights' defensive line, starting 11 of 12 games as a junior in 2024 and racking up 53 tackles, 5.5 sacks, and three forced fumbles. His senior season in 2025 brought another full slate of starts across all 12 games, 46 tackles, 7.5 tackles for loss, three sacks, two forced fumbles (both returned for touchdowns), his first career interception, and All-Big 12 Honorable Mention honors. The West Virginia game stood out: eight tackles, 1.5 sacks, and a forced fumble that helped the defensive line earn Big 12 Defensive Line of the Week recognition. Over two years at UCF, Kelly compiled 99 tackles, 8.5 sacks, 17 tackles for loss, and five forced fumbles in 24 games. He accepted an invite to the 2026 NFL Scouting Combine in Indianapolis, running his forty at 4.68 seconds.
strengths: Carries legitimate NFL length at 6'5" with arms that allow him to keep blockers at bay and control the point of attack before they can latch on. Excellent long-arm move that he times off the snap to jolt tackles backward and create early separation on the edge. Good speed for his size, and he flashes the ability to flatten and turn the corner when his rush plan is clean and decisive. Attacks the outside hand of tackles with a sharp chop technique, prying open the edge and forcing quarterbacks to feel pressure from the blind side. Reads quarterback pocket movement well and adjusts his rush angle, working back underneath when the passer climbs to keep plays alive. Generated consistent pressure throughout his UCF career, finishing his senior year with 26 total pressures including hurries, hits, and sacks. Ball awareness is better than you'd expect from a defensive end; his interception against North Carolina and three pass breakups in 2025 show active hands at the line. Reliable fumble producer who creates turnovers at key moments, forcing five fumbles across his two seasons at UCF with multiple resulting in defensive scores.
weaknesses: Considerably undersized at 250 pounds for an every-down defensive end, and his frame does not suggest he can carry much more weight without losing agility. First step off the ball is just average; he rarely wins with pure explosion and instead relies on length and timing that can be neutralized by athletic tackles. Inside counter moves lack any real bite, making him too predictable for offensive tackles who can sit on his outside rush and wall him off. Tackling regressed noticeably in 2025, with missed tackle numbers climbing and too many whiffs in space that allowed extra yardage after contact. Sack production dropped significantly in his final college season despite steady pressure numbers, which raises real questions about his ability to finish at the NFL level.

bio: Omar Cooper Jr. came out of Lawrence North High School in Indianapolis as a four-star Rivals recruit ranked No. 155 nationally. He finished his prep career with 132 catches for 2,856 yards and 22 touchdowns, earned two-time IFCA Top 50 all-state honors, and helped Lawrence North win its first sectional title since 1990. After a redshirt year on kickoff return duty in 2022, he caught 18 passes for 235 yards and three touchdowns across nine games in 2023. His 2024 sophomore season was a breakout: 28 receptions for 594 yards and seven touchdowns, with a conference-best 21.2 yards per reception. Twenty-four of those 28 catches went for a first down or score. But inconsistency and availability issues kept him out of the starting lineup most weeks, and head coach Curt Cignetti publicly questioned his dependability. Cooper moved to the slot in 2025 after Indiana lost its primary inside receivers to the NFL and injuries. Through eight games he caught 37 passes for 581 yards and seven touchdowns with a catch rate above 82 percent, just one drop, and strong yards-after-catch production. Background:
Omar Cooper Jr. came out of Lawrence North High School in Indianapolis as a four-star Rivals recruit ranked No. 155 nationally. He finished his prep career with 132 catches for 2,856 yards and 22 touchdowns, earned two-time IFCA Top 50 all-state honors, and helped Lawrence North win its first sectional title since 1990. After a redshirt year on kickoff return duty in 2022, he caught 18 passes for 235 yards and three touchdowns across nine games in 2023. His 2024 sophomore season was a breakout: 28 receptions for 594 yards and seven touchdowns, with a conference-best 21.2 yards per reception. Twenty-four of those 28 catches went for a first down or score. But inconsistency and availability issues kept him out of the starting lineup most weeks, and head coach Curt Cignetti publicly questioned his dependability. Cooper moved to the slot in 2025 after Indiana lost its primary inside receivers to the NFL and injuries. Through eight games he caught 37 passes for 581 yards and seven touchdowns with a catch rate above 82 percent, just one drop, and strong yards-after-catch production. His four-touchdown game against Indiana State tied a program record. Against Michigan State, he posted eight catches for 113 yards and a score in his most complete Big Ten performance.

bio: A zero-star recruit out of Bishop Kelley High School in Tulsa, Heinecke played running back and safety and earned District 5A-III Player of the Year in 2020. Also a top lacrosse recruit in the state, he chose Ohio State for that sport over football offers from Army and Navy. After three games as a walk-on he transferred to Oklahoma in 2022 to play football. His uncle Cory was a defensive end on OU's 2000 national championship team. Heinecke spent 2023 and 2024 on special teams and reserve linebacker duty across 26 games before earning a starting role over the final five of 2025. Once on the field, production came fast: 74 tackles, 12.0 TFLs, 3.0 sacks, four pass breakups, and a fumble forced. At Tennessee he posted 13 tackles and a sack-fumble returned 71 yards for a score, earning SEC Defensive Player of the Week. The AP named him All-SEC Second Team, and Oklahoma gave him the Don Key Award, the program's highest honor. His eligibility remains unresolved. The NCAA counted his three lacrosse games at Ohio State against his football clock and denied his appeal for a fourth season. He filed an injunction in Cleveland County court in March 2026, with a hearing set one week before the draft.
strengths: Spools up fast in pursuit and tops 20 MPH tracking ball carriers sideline to sideline. Sack-fumble at Tennessee showed his ability to time the snap and win clean off the edge. Avoids blockers with lateral quickness rather than trying to power through them. Converts tackles for loss at a high rate when pointed downhill in gap-shoot assignments. Lacrosse footwork translates to fluid movement in short-area changes of direction. Explodes through contact with hip pop and wrap technique on his best reps. Four-phase special teams value is obvious on tape; runs through blockers on coverage units. Plays with a nasty streak that forces opponents to account for him every snap.
weaknesses: Short arms create a small tackling radius; whiffs too often on the periphery. Blockers absorb him easily on direct engagement and he ends up on the ground. Ole Miss misdirection exposed slow processing; bit hard on fakes and lost contain. When asked to scrape laterally, his reads lag behind his feet and he overruns gaps. Alabama tape showed poor recovery when re-routed by pass protectors early in the rep. Coverage usage stayed narrow: flat follows, hook zones, and the occasional mugged-up drop. Frame looks maxed out; adding weight would likely cost him the quickness that makes him useful.

bio: PJ Williams has taken an unconventional path to becoming an NFL Draft prospect. The Dickinson, Texas product began his football career on the defensive line before transitioning to offensive tackle as a sophomore in high school, where he earned three consecutive first-team all-district selections. His athletic versatility shone through as a dual-sport athlete, averaging 12.8 points and 6.9 rebounds per game as a senior basketball player while dominating the gridiron. Originally a highly-touted four-star recruit who signed with Texas A&M, Williams saw action in just one game before transferring to SMU. His 2023 campaign with the Mustangs showcased his adaptability, as he appeared in 12 games with four starts at left tackle while also filling in at guard when needed. The 6'5", 318-pound lineman logged 862 offensive snaps in his redshirt freshman season. Williams' prep career was decorated with accolades, including Under Armour All-American honors and a Class 6A second-team all-state selection by the Texas Sportswriters Association. He helped lead Dickinson High School to an undefeated district record his senior year and multiple playoff appearances, establishing himself as the ninth-ranked overall recruit in Texas.
strengths: Exceptional arm length and wingspan provide natural advantages in pass protection, allowing him to control defenders before they can engage his frame Demonstrates impressive lateral mobility for his size, consistently reaching the second level with fluid movement and connecting with moving targets in space Basketball background translates to superior foot quickness and balance, particularly evident in his ability to mirror defenders in pass protection Shows natural power at the point of attack, generating consistent movement in the run game when he maintains proper leverage Versatility to play multiple positions along the offensive line, having worked at both tackle spots and guard during his collegiate career Quick first step off the snap allows him to establish initial positioning advantage against both speed and power rushers Frame carries significant room for additional muscle mass without compromising his athletic traits, suggesting substantial physical development potential Demonstrates competitive toughness and adaptability, stepping into multiple positions with minimal practice time when called upon
weaknesses: Technical refinement needs significant development, particularly in hand placement and timing when engaging defenders at the point of attack Tendency to play with high pad level diminishes his natural power advantage and leaves him vulnerable to bull rushes in pass protection Limited experience with only one full season as a starter raises questions about his readiness to handle NFL-caliber defenders consistently Must improve anchor strength against power rushers, as he can get walked back into the pocket when caught with suboptimal leverage Pass protection technique occasionally relies too heavily on athletic ability rather than sound fundamentals, leading to recovery-based protection
player_comparisons: CadeMays - Tennessee (99%), AjaniCornelius - Oregon (94%), KarsenBarnhart - Michigan (92%)

bio: Parker Brailsford prepped at Saguaro High School in Mesa, Arizona, where he helped lead the Sabercats to a 12-1 record and a state championship in 2021. He earned All-State First Team honors and drew attention as a consensus four-star interior line recruit, ranking among the top guard prospects nationally across ESPN, 247Sports, and Rivals. Brailsford signed with Washington and redshirted during the 2022 season. As a redshirt freshman in 2023, he started all 15 games, splitting time between center and right guard, and anchored the offensive line that won the Joe Moore Award. That unit paved the way for a Husky offense that reached the College Football Playoff National Championship Game. He picked up Freshman All-America honors from the AFCA, FWAA, and Pro Football Focus, along with second-team All-Pac-12 recognition. When Kalen DeBoer left for Alabama, Brailsford followed. He started all 13 games at center in 2024 and earned second-team All-America nods from several outlets. In 2025, he was named a permanent team captain, started 14 of 15 games, and landed on watch lists for the Rimington Trophy, Outland Trophy, and Lombardi Award. Over three seasons as a starter, he logged 42 games and more than 2,600 snaps.
strengths: Gets off the ball fast and reaches linebackers at the second level before they can fill their gap. Feet never stop moving; watch his Georgia tape and you see a guy who finishes every single rep. Pass sets are clean and controlled, with quick hands that consistently beat rushers to the punch. Head is always on a swivel after his initial assignment; finds work and stays engaged throughout the play. Handles twist pickups and delayed blitzes smoothly, rarely caught guessing at the point of attack. Low pad level gives him natural leverage that bigger defenders struggle to get underneath. Pulls well and looks comfortable working in space, covering ground you don't expect from a center. Shows some dog in him; competes through the whistle and scraps to sustain blocks even when outmatched physically.
weaknesses: Gives up size to almost every nose tackle he faces, and it shows when they get into his chest. Short arms mean he works inside defenders' reach, and when he loses hand position he grabs cloth to survive. Does not move people in the run game; you rarely see him create a new line of scrimmage. Bull rushers who get square on him can walk him into the quarterback's lap over the course of a rep. Gets his head down and lunges when he's overaggressive, which puts him off-balance and leads to holding. The 17 flags in three seasons tell a story: a smaller center compensating for length he doesn't have.
player_comparisons: TanorBortolini - Wisconsin (85%), CameronJurgens - Nebraska (72%), CharlesTurner III - LSU (69%)

bio: Preston Stone, SMU's redshirt sophomore signal-caller, emerged as a dual-threat playmaker in 2023. The Dallas native was a blue-chip recruit out of Parish Episcopal, where he amassed over 13,100 passing yards and 145 touchdowns. Stone's high school pedigree attracted offers from several Power Five programs. At SMU, Stone climbed the depth chart methodically. After redshirting in 2021 and limited snaps in 2022, he seized the starting role in 2023. Stone lit up the stat sheet, throwing for 3,197 yards and 28 touchdowns in 12 starts. He topped the AAC with a 161.3 passer rating and ranked fourth in passing yards. Stone's breakout campaign earned him three AAC Offensive Player of the Week nods and a spot on the Davey O'Brien Midseason Watch List. His performance was instrumental in SMU's 10-2 regular season record, showcasing his ability to elevate in crucial situations.
strengths: Plus arm talent with the ability to drive the ball vertically and fit throws into tight windows Exhibits a quick trigger and compact release, allowing for rapid progression through reads Displays functional mobility to extend plays and pick up chunk yardage on designed runs Demonstrates advanced football IQ, adept at diagnosing coverages and making pre-snap adjustments Natural leader who commands the huddle and elevates teammates' performance in critical moments Shows touch and anticipation on intermediate routes, particularly on seam throws and crossing patterns Executes play-action with conviction, creating separation for receivers downfield Proven performer in pressure situations
weaknesses: Lacks prototypical NFL quarterback height, potentially leading to obstructed throwing lanes Mechanics break down under duress, resulting in erratic ball placement and reduced velocity Tendency to lock onto primary targets, telegraphing intentions to defensive backs Overcompensates on deep balls, creating too much arc and allowing defenders to recover Displays gunslinger mentality at times, forcing throws into coverage and risking turnovers Lost his starting job to Kevin Jennings early in 2024

bio: Nineteen sacks and 21 tackles for loss as a senior at Minneapolis North High School earned Jamison-Travis all-state and all-metro recognition while he led the Polar Bears to the Class 2A state title game. He had been a three-sport athlete in Minneapolis, but football took hold early and the production made the path forward clear. The college route ran through Iowa Western Community College in Council Bluffs, Iowa. Over two JUCO seasons, Jamison-Travis racked up 90 tackles, 20 TFL, and 10.5 sacks, earning NJCAA first-team All-America honors in 2022. That year he helped the Reivers win the national championship, contributing 2.5 sacks and two fumble recoveries in the title game. He finished as the No. 1 JUCO prospect in Iowa and the No. 3 JUCO defensive lineman nationally. Jamison-Travis signed with Auburn and played sparingly as a sophomore in 2023, appearing in three games. He fought his way from the bottom of the depth chart to a starting role by 2024, recording 14 tackles and a sack across the season. As a senior in 2025, he started all 12 games at nose tackle, leading Auburn's interior linemen with 36 tackles and two TFL while anchoring a defense that ranked 10th nationally in rushing yards allowed per game.
strengths: Carries 328 pounds with plus arm length, giving him the frame to eat space at the point of attack. Quick first step off the snap consistently beats centers into the neutral zone before they can set. Run defense is the anchor of his game and his best tape is against SEC rushing attacks. Gets hands on chest plates early with accurate, repeatable placement on contact. Transfers hip power into his punch effectively when squared up and engaged. Tackling reliability improved sharply from his junior to senior year, cutting missed tackles significantly. Closing burst in short areas is better than his straight-line speed suggests.
weaknesses: Straight-line speed is well below average and limits his range on lateral pursuit. Pass-rush plan is thin, mostly bull rush with few counters or hand moves. Gets locked onto blocks too often and struggles to disengage against combo schemes. Anchor can slip against double teams, especially when caught moving laterally. Zero career coverage snaps makes him a two-down player until he adds passing-down value. Loses track of play design when entangled with his blocker for extended reps.
player_comparisons: JordanPhillips - Maryland (78%), JerrodClark - Coastal Carolina (77%), JJPegues - Ole Miss (71%)
