bio: Quintayvious Hutchins was a three-star recruit out of Bessemer City High School, who ranked in the 40s among Alabama prospects by most services, he chose Boston College not just for the ACC competition but because he wanted an education he felt he could not get at home. He has spoken openly about growing up without much, and that hunger to seize every opportunity has defined his college career from the jump. The early years at BC were anything but glamorous. Hutchins redshirted as a true freshman in 2021, then spent his redshirt freshman season bouncing between defensive end and tight end before settling back on the defensive side. Through 2022 and 2023, his contributions came almost entirely on special teams, logging just 18 defensive snaps across two years with only a handful of tackles to show for it. The turning point arrived in 2024 under head coach Bill O'Brien, when Hutchins finally cracked the rotation at defensive end, playing in all 13 games with seven starts. He finished that season with 31 tackles, 3.5 sacks, a forced fumble, two fumble recoveries, and a 21-yard interception return against Louisville. Working alongside Donovan Ezeiruaku, who went on to become a second-round pick of the Dallas Cowboys, Hutchins credited his teammate's relentless standard for pushing him to another level. The 2025 season brought a captain's "C" and a full-time starting role. Playing in 10 games with nine starts before a couple of injury absences, Hutchins posted 35 tackles, two sacks, and seven quarterback hits. He notched sacks against Michigan State's Aidan Chiles and Clemson's Cade Klubnik, showing he could deliver in marquee matchups. His performance earned him a Senior Bowl invitation, bringing his career full circle back to the Deep South, where family members who had not seen him play since high school could finally watch in person.
strengths: Plays with a nasty motor and physical edge that belies his 240-pound frame, consistently winning leverage battles against bigger offensive tackles at the point of attack. Smooth hip flexibility and natural bend around the corner allow him to flatten his rush arc and threaten the quarterback's back shoulder without losing speed. Owns a filthy spin counter that he times well off initial contact, freeing himself from blocks when his primary rush lane gets walled off. Reliable tackler whose technique improved dramatically over his final two seasons, cutting his missed tackle rate by more than half and wrapping up with authority in the open field. Tested as one of the faster edge rushers in this class and uses that speed to close on ball carriers in pursuit from the backside with real urgency. Showed legitimate coverage ability when asked to drop into zones, looking comfortable reading quarterback eyes and breaking on throws underneath. Displays a well-sequenced rush plan that includes a Euro-step setup into a chop-rip finish, keeping tackles guessing rather than relying on one move every snap. Special teams warrior with years of experience as a core contributor, which gives him immediate NFL roster value on game day beyond his defensive reps.
weaknesses: Significantly undersized for the position at 240 pounds, sitting at the very bottom among edge rushers, and lacks the length to keep blockers off his frame consistently. First-step explosiveness is merely adequate, which means offensive tackles with good feet can mirror him and prevent clean outside wins on the snap. Taller, longer tackles can wall him off and neutralize his rush entirely, erasing his bend advantage before he can ever get to the corner. Penalty issues surfaced in 2025 with eight flags, suggesting some discipline concerns that need cleaning up before he can be trusted in situational roles. Run defense can be swallowed up when bigger bodies collapse on him inside the box, leaving him unable to hold his gap or shed blocks against double teams.
player_comparisons: ZionTupuola-Fetui - Washington (79%), XavierThomas - Clemson (78%), KhalidDuke - Kansas State (77%)
name: Quintayvious Hutchins
scouting_report:
Background:
Quintayvious Hutchins was a three-star recruit out of Bessemer City High School, who ranked in the 40s among Alabama prospects by most services, he chose Boston College not just for the ACC competition but because he wanted an education he felt he could not get at home. He has spoken openly about growing up without much, and that hunger to seize every opportunity has defined his college career from the jump. The early years at BC were anything but glamorous. Hutchins redshirted as a true freshman in 2021, then spent his redshirt freshman season bouncing between defensive end and tight end before settling back on the defensive side. Through 2022 and 2023, his contributions came almost entirely on special teams, logging just 18 defensive snaps across two years with only a handful of tackles to show for it. The turning point arrived in 2024 under head coach Bill O'Brien, when Hutchins finally cracked the rotation at defensive end, playing in all 13 games with seven starts. He finished that season with 31 tackles, 3.5 sacks, a forced fumble, two fumble recoveries, and a 21-yard interception return against Louisville. Working alongside Donovan Ezeiruaku, who went on to become a second-round pick of the Dallas Cowboys, Hutchins credited his teammate's relentless standard for pushing him to another level. The 2025 season brought a captain's "C" and a full-time starting role. Playing in 10 games with nine starts before a couple of injury absences, Hutchins posted 35 tackles, two sacks, and seven quarterback hits. He notched sacks against Michigan State's Aidan Chiles and Clemson's Cade Klubnik, showing he could deliver in marquee matchups. His performance earned him a Senior Bowl invitation, bringing his career full circle back to the Deep South, where family members who had not seen him play since high school could finally watch in person.

Strengths:
Plays with a nasty motor and physical edge that belies his 240-pound frame, consistently winning leverage battles against bigger offensive tackles at the point of attack. Smooth hip flexibility and natural bend around the corner allow him to flatten his rush arc and threaten the quarterback's back shoulder without losing speed. Owns a filthy spin counter that he times well off initial contact, freeing himself from blocks when his primary rush lane gets walled off. Reliable tackler whose technique improved dramatically over his final two seasons, cutting his missed tackle rate by more than half and wrapping up with authority in the open field. Tested as one of the faster edge rushers in this class and uses that speed to close on ball carriers in pursuit from the backside with real urgency. Showed legitimate coverage ability when asked to drop into zones, looking comfortable reading quarterback eyes and breaking on throws underneath. Displays a well-sequenced rush plan that includes a Euro-step setup into a chop-rip finish, keeping tackles guessing rather than relying on one move every snap. Special teams warrior with years of experience as a core contributor, which gives him immediate NFL roster value on game day beyond his defensive reps.

Weaknesses:
Significantly undersized for the position at 240 pounds, sitting at the very bottom among edge rushers, and lacks the length to keep blockers off his frame consistently. First-step explosiveness is merely adequate, which means offensive tackles with good feet can mirror him and prevent clean outside wins on the snap. Taller, longer tackles can wall him off and neutralize his rush entirely, erasing his bend advantage before he can ever get to the corner. Penalty issues surfaced in 2025 with eight flags, suggesting some discipline concerns that need cleaning up before he can be trusted in situational roles. Run defense can be swallowed up when bigger bodies collapse on him inside the box, leaving him unable to hold his gap or shed blocks against double teams.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Offense Rating: 78%
  Tackling: 78%
  Pass Rush: 75%
  Run Defense: 75%

bio: Harvey Thomas played defensive end at Florida in the early 1990s, and his son R Mason Thomas took that same pass-rushing DNA to Cardinal Gibbons High School in Fort Lauderdale, where he racked up 109 tackles, 36 tackles for loss, and 19 sacks across his junior and senior seasons. He anchored a defense that won consecutive Florida 4A state championships in 2020 and 2021 while also competing in the 100, 200, and 400 meters on the track team. Rated a four-star prospect by 247Sports and On3, and a three-star by ESPN and Rivals, Thomas originally committed to Iowa State before flipping to Oklahoma. His first two years in Norman were defined more by the training room than the field. A high-ankle sprain cost him three games as a true freshman in 2022, limiting him to 10 appearances, seven tackles, and half a sack. A second high-ankle sprain, this time to the other ankle, kept him out of four games in 2023, where he managed nine tackles, 2.5 tackles for loss, and a single sack in a rotational role. Two ankles, two surgeries, two frustrating seasons spent watching from the sideline. The breakout came in 2024. Thomas started 11 of 13 games and delivered 23 tackles, 12.5 tackles for loss, and 9.0 sacks, earning All-SEC Second Team honors from the coaches and Bronko Nagurski National Defensive Player of the Week recognition after a dominant performance at Auburn. He forced two fumbles, recovered two more, and took one back nine yards for a touchdown against LSU. His senior year in 2025 brought continued production: 6.5 sacks and a 14.6% pressure rate across 10 games, along with AP Second Team All-American, First Team All-SEC, and Bednarik Award semifinalist nods. A quad and hamstring injury suffered during a 71-yard fumble return touchdown cut his season short, but he returned for the CFP opener against Alabama to close out his college career.
strengths: Explosive first step off the snap creates instant stress for tackles before their hands are even set. Converts speed to power with violent technique, catching blockers off guard when they sit back for the speed rush. Remarkable bend and ankle flexion allow him to flatten around the arc while staying balanced through contact. Natural pad level keeps him low without thinking about it, winning the leverage war against taller, longer opponents consistently. Relentless pursuit from the backside. Closing speed on running backs is unusual for an edge player and creates unexpected tackle opportunities. Fourth-quarter animal. Six of his nine sacks in 2024 came protecting leads, which tells you something about his competitive wiring. Carved a shallow path inside against slower tackles in 2024, showing the ability to collapse pockets with different rush angles. Plays with a nasty competitive streak that belies his frame. Does not concede ground willingly and scraps through double teams with effort and quick feet.
weaknesses: Undersized frame at 249 pounds with below-average arm length invites bigger blockers to lock onto his chest and smother him. Anchor dissolves against down blocks and combination blocks from the run game, getting washed inside and abandoning his gap. Hands arrive late at the point of attack. Tackles with quick sets consistently win first contact and control the engagement. Durability is a legitimate red flag: high-ankle sprains to both ankles early in his career, then soft-tissue issues as a senior. Taller tackles with active hands can widen his rush arc and ride him past the pocket when he loses leverage and stands up.
player_comparisons: JosaiahStewart - Michigan (93%), ArnoldEbiketie - Penn State (92%), JonahElliss - Utah (89%)
name: RMason Thomas

bio: Raheem Anderson, a center from Michigan, entered college as a highly-touted four-star recruit. The Detroit native excelled academically at Cass Tech High School, maintaining a 4.1 GPA while captaining the football team for three years. Anderson's versatility shone through as he played center, guard, and defensive tackle, earning All-City and All-League honors as a senior. At Michigan, Anderson's on-field impact was limited. He appeared in just six games over three seasons, primarily in a reserve role. Despite the lack of playing time, Anderson's work ethic earned him recognition as a four-time Scout Team Offensive Player of the Week. He also maintained his academic excellence, twice being named to the Academic All-Big Ten team. Anderson's college career statistics are sparse, with only 69 offensive snaps recorded. His limited game action makes it challenging to assess his in-game development at the collegiate level. However, his high school accolades and initial recruiting rankings suggest untapped potential that NFL teams may find intriguing.
strengths: Technically sound anchor man with plus hand placement and knee bend, consistently getting under defenders' pads in pass pro Cerebral pivot with sharp pre-snap recognition, adept at diagnosing fronts and making line calls Stout frame with good core strength and natural leverage, able to absorb bull rushes and create vertical push Quick trigger off the snap, allowing him to beat defenders to the punch and establish position Plays with a wide base and active feet, maintaining balance through contact in both run and pass sets High-motor grinder with a lunch pail mentality, bringing intensity to practice and scout team reps Position versatility from high school experience, offering potential swing value across the interior line Above-average arm length for the position, creating separation and controlling defenders in tight quarters
weaknesses: Severely limited game tape against quality competition, raising questions about ability to handle NFL-caliber defenders Lacks ideal sand in the pants as a drive blocker, struggling to consistently move defenders off the ball Undersized frame for an NFL interior lineman, potentially leading to issues anchoring against massive nose tackles Average straight-line speed limits effectiveness as a puller and on screens, may struggle to hit moving targets Raw technique in space, showing hesitation and inconsistent angles when climbing to the second level
player_comparisons: MoseVavao - Fresno State (92%), ZekeCorrell - NC State (89%), WillPutnam - Clemson (87%)

bio: Reggie Virgil grew up in Apopka, Florida, and played both ways at Mount Dora Christian Academy. As a senior, he put together a monster season with 44 catches for 873 yards and 12 receiving touchdowns while also contributing five rushing scores and a pick-six on defense. He added 22 tackles and four interceptions as a junior. The two-time team captain was a three-star recruit ranked as an "athlete" by most services, and he landed at Miami (OH) in the 2022 class. Virgil's first two years in Oxford were quiet. He caught one pass each season, spending most of his time on special teams and learning the position. Then in 2024, everything clicked. He led the Redhawks with 816 receiving yards and nine touchdowns on just 41 catches, averaging a gaudy 19.9 yards per reception and earning second-team All-MAC honors. His seven-game touchdown streak included scores of 75, 69, 58, and 34 yards. That junior tape was the calling card that drew serious Power 4 interest when he entered the portal, and he picked Texas Tech over a dozen-plus programs including Michigan, Penn State, Oklahoma, and Miami (FL). ESPN ranked him the 47th overall transfer and 10th among wide receivers. Moving from the MAC to the Big 12 was a real litmus test, and Virgil held up. He led the Red Raiders with 55 receptions while adding 676 yards and six receiving touchdowns plus two rushing scores during a season that included a Big 12 Championship victory over BYU and a College Football Playoff berth. He eclipsed 1,000 and then 1,500 career receiving yards during the 2025 campaign, earned All-Big 12 Honorable Mention, and picked up an invite to the Senior Bowl. He finished his college career with 100 catches, 1,583 yards, and 16 touchdowns across 42 games played at both schools.
strengths: Uses quick feet and subtle body fakes at the line to slip past press coverage, rarely getting stuck or rerouted at the snap. Excellent deep-ball tracking ability lets him adjust his angle and timing on throws over his shoulder without breaking stride. Catch radius is enormous thanks to 6'3" length; he plucks the ball confidently outside his frame and high-points throws above traffic. Savvy route seller who disguises his intentions well, making verticals and intermediate breaks look identical through the stem. Legitimate speed forces corners to respect the deep ball, which opens up shorter routes and gives him free access underneath. Finds the soft spots in zone coverage with real feel for spacing, settling in windows rather than drifting through them. Works back to the quarterback aggressively on comebacks and curls, closing the window for defenders to jump the throw. Showed he can perform against better competition by maintaining production after jumping from the MAC to the Big 12 in one offseason.
weaknesses: Built like a beanpole at 190 pounds; his thin frame limits his ability to fight through physical corners and survive contact as a runner. Contested catch production is underwhelming for his size, with defenders getting hands on too many of his targets in traffic. Focus drops pop up more than you want to see; nine on 100 career catches is a number that will concern evaluators. Route breaks can get rounded and loose, with too much drift upfield that gives defenders time to recover to the ball. Run blocking is more effort than execution; he is willing to get in the way but lacks the functional strength to sustain or drive blocks.
player_comparisons: NickNash - San Jose State (98%), SageSurratt - Wake Forest (87%), ToryHorton - Colorado State (86%)

bio: Riley Mahlman, a native of Lakeville, Minnesota, has steadily risen to prominence as an offensive tackle for the Wisconsin Badgers. Standing at an imposing 6'8" and weighing 320 pounds, Mahlman was a highly sought-after four-star recruit coming out of Lakeville South High School. His prep career was decorated with accolades, including first-team All-Metro honors from the Minneapolis Star Tribune as a senior and first-team all-state recognition as a junior. Mahlman's excellence extended beyond the gridiron, as he earned Academic All-State honors in both his junior and senior years. Mahlman's collegiate journey began with a redshirt season in 2021, where he saw limited action, making his Badger debut in a victory against Rutgers. His sophomore campaign in 2022 was hampered by an early-season injury, restricting him to seven appearances with six starts at right tackle. Despite the setback, Mahlman showed promise in pass protection, yielding minimal pressure when on the field. The 2023 season marked Mahlman's emergence as a cornerstone of the Badgers' offensive line. He started all 13 games at right tackle, playing a pivotal role in Wisconsin's offensive success. Mahlman's contributions were instrumental in the Badgers' ground game, highlighted by a 314-yard rushing performance in the season opener against Buffalo.
strengths: Massive frame with ideal length for the tackle position, creating a wide arc for pass rushers to navigate around Powerful base allows him to anchor effectively against bull rushes, rarely giving ground in pass protection Displays good initial quickness off the snap, often beating edge rushers to the spot in pass sets Strong hands enable him to latch onto defenders and control the point of attack in the run game Shows the ability to drive defenders off the ball with leg drive and core strength on down blocks Demonstrates improved footwork and balance in pass protection, particularly against speed rushers Exhibits a nasty finishing attitude, looking to pancake defenders and play through the whistle High football IQ, consistently picks up stunts and blitzes, rarely caught off guard by defensive schemes
weaknesses: Tends to play with high pad level, allowing defenders to get under his pads and compromise his leverage Occasionally struggles with hand placement, leaving his chest exposed to power rushers Lateral agility is average, can be beaten by quick inside moves from elite edge rushers Sometimes oversets against speed, creating vulnerabilities to counter moves back to the inside Needs to improve consistency in sustaining blocks through the entire play, especially in space
player_comparisons: EthanDriskell - Marshall (95%), ReubenFatheree II - Texas A&M (88%), NickSaldiveri - Old Dominion (86%)

bio: A walk-on linebacker at Wisconsin, Nowakowski converted to fullback in 2022 and then made the full-time move to tight end in 2023 after a coaching change eliminated the fullback role. He caught his first career touchdown at Minnesota that junior season and gradually earned more playing time in Madison, appearing in 40 games across five years with limited receiving production: 18 catches for 131 yards entering 2025. The transfer to Indiana changed everything. Nowakowski started all 15 games for the undefeated Hoosiers in 2025, catching 32 passes for 387 yards and two touchdowns while adding a rushing score. He turned into a reliable intermediate target and a dependable blocker at the Y-tight end spot. His 43-yard touchdown on a tight end screen against Illinois and a four-catch, 65-yard performance against his former team Wisconsin stood out as signature moments in a breakout year. Nowakowski earned second-team All-Big Ten honors from the conference media and third-team recognition from the coaches panel. He was named a game captain in seven of Indiana's final nine contests and scored the first touchdown of the College Football Playoff Championship Game against Oregon. He holds a bachelor's degree in industrial engineering from Wisconsin.
strengths: Excellent natural hands that grade among the best in this tight end class. Did not drop a single pass on 36 targets during the 2025 season at Indiana. Plucks the ball cleanly away from his body and secures through contact at the catch point. Outstanding run-after-catch ability, averaging 7.7 YAC per reception in 2025. Forced 10 missed tackles as a receiver, showing vision from his running back days. Understands leverage and timing as a run blocker with consistent effort to the whistle. Allowed just two total pressures across 154 pass-blocking snaps for Indiana. Alignment versatility across inline, slot, wide, and fullback looks on film.
weaknesses: Undersized frame at the position limits ceiling as an every-down inline tight end. Struggles to separate against man coverage, particularly on vertical routes. Lacks burst out of route breaks, giving defenders time to recover. Run blocking power can stall at the point of attack against bigger defenders. Limited college production before 2025 leaves a thin sample of receiving tape.
player_comparisons: BrentonStrange - Penn State (65%), MasonTaylor - LSU (65%), MarshonFord - Louisville (63%)

bio: Henry is a product of Lumberton, Mississippi, where he rushed for over 7,000 yards and 99 touchdowns across four varsity seasons. He won two Class 1A Mr. Football awards, earned four all-state selections, and was named MVP of the 2020 state championship game. Despite Division I interest, he took the JUCO route to Jones College. That detour paid off quickly. After a modest freshman year (495 yards, six touchdowns), Henry led the entire NJCAA in rushing as a sophomore with 1,302 yards and 18 scores, earning the Walter Jones Trophy and NJCAA Offensive Player of the Year. UTSA came calling, and his first FBS season brought 588 yards and a team-high 11 touchdowns on 127 carries, including a two-score day in the Frisco Bowl to seal the program's first bowl win. Henry took over the starting role in 2024 and posted 706 yards and seven touchdowns with 21 receptions. His 2025 season was a clear step forward: 1,045 rushing yards and nine scores on just 151 carries (6.9 per attempt), a Doak Walker Award semifinal nod, and a first-team All-American Athletic Conference selection. He was invited to the East-West Shrine Bowl, participated in the NFL Combine, and ran a full workout at UTSA's pro day.
strengths: Quick-twitch burst gets him to top speed in a hurry and shows up as a consistent threat on chunk plays. Wiggly in tight spaces with ankle flexibility and hip bend that let him redirect without losing speed. Lower-body explosiveness tested well at the combine, with strong vertical and broad jump numbers confirming the pop on tape. Yards after contact climbed every season, finishing 2025 at over four yards per carry after first hit. 99 missed tackles forced over three UTSA seasons; he makes first defenders pay regularly. Runs well in zone concepts, with patient eyes to find the cutback and the burst to hit it. Reliable hands out of the backfield; caught 55 of 70 targets with only three drops in three years. Showed up against quality opponents, including his 177-yard opener versus Texas A&M.
weaknesses: Undersized for the position, which limits his ability to absorb a full professional workload. Combine shuttle and 10-yard split both tested below average, raising questions about his short-area quickness off tape. Pass protection is a real problem on film; he gets walked back by blitzing linebackers too easily. Only managed 17 reps on the bench at pro day, reflecting limited upper-body strength at his size. Six fumbles over three college seasons, including two in 2025, will scare coordinators. Tends to pull runs off their designed track and bounce outside when patience would gain more. Receiving role was limited to swing passes and checkdowns; yards per route run dropped in 2025.

bio: A dynamic defensive back out of Broken Arrow, Oklahoma, Robert Spears-Jennings made his mark as one of the state's premier two-way talents. His senior campaign showcased game-changing ability on both sides of the ball - recording 56 tackles, four interceptions and two forced fumbles while adding 36 receptions for 626 yards and eight touchdowns. That dual-threat dominance earned him Oklahoma All-District 6A-I Safety of the Year honors while leading his team to the state semifinals. After choosing Oklahoma over Arkansas and Ole Miss, Spears-Jennings steadily worked his way into the defensive rotation. His role expanded significantly in 2023, where the 6'1", 219-pound safety recorded 38 tackles and showed particular impact down the stretch with 24 stops in his final five games. His development curve became evident with back-to-back seven-tackle performances against Oklahoma State and West Virginia. Rated as a consensus four-star prospect, Spears-Jennings garnered national attention as one of the top safety recruits in the country. ESPN ranked him as the No. 10 safety nationally, with Rivals (No. 11) and 247Sports (No. 12) similarly high on his potential.
strengths: Instinctive run defender who diagnoses quickly and attacks downhill with purpose, consistently beating blockers to their spot Physical tackler who breaks down well in space and drives through contact, particularly effective when coming downhill Shows natural feel in zone coverage, maintaining proper depth and leveraging routes while keeping eyes in the backfield Versatile defender capable of aligning at multiple levels - effective both in the box and playing deep Brings excellent size at 219 pounds while maintaining the athleticism to range sideline to sideline Strong closing burst to the football, evident in his late-season surge of production as a junior Natural ball skills carried over from his receiver background, tracks the ball well and attacks at the highest point Ascending player who showed consistent improvement, peaking late in his junior season
weaknesses: Man coverage technique needs refinement, particularly against skilled route runners who can manipulate leverage Limited starting experience with only two starts in 2023 raises questions about immediate three-down readiness Still developing consistency in his defensive keys and recognition Can be overaggressive pursuing runs, occasionally creating cutback lanes Will need to prove he can maintain production with increased snap counts at the next level
player_comparisons: TylerOwens - Texas Tech (73%), JaylenReed - Penn State (67%), TycenAnderson - Toledo (67%)

bio: Roderick Kearney came to Florida from Orange Park High School as a four-star recruit in the 2023 class, rated as the third-best interior offensive lineman nationally. During his prep career, he earned All-First Coast honors and was named OL MVP at the Under Armour All-America Camp in Miami. He chose Florida over offers from Clemson, Georgia, Michigan, and several other programs, opting to stay close to home in Gainesville. After redshirting his first season in 2023, Kearney saw his first collegiate action in 2024, appearing in seven games with 74 offensive snaps and 22 special teams snaps. He worked primarily as a reserve offensive lineman while contributing on the field goal block unit throughout the season. His most significant playing time came in the Gasparilla Bowl against Tulane, where he logged 42 offensive snaps. Kearney maintained strong academics during his time at Florida, earning SEC Fall Academic Honor Roll recognition in both 2023 and 2024. The limited snaps Kearney received in 2024 showed flashes of his potential, particularly in pass protection where he allowed zero pressures in 30 blocking attempts. He demonstrated versatility by taking snaps at multiple interior positions, serving as depth at left guard, center, and right guard. While his sample size remains small, the consistency he showed when on the field caught the attention of coaches and earned him increased opportunities as the season progressed.
strengths: Exceptional lateral quickness for his size, showcasing surprising agility that allows him to mirror pass rushers effectively in tight quarters. Devastating run blocker who generates tremendous push with his lower body, consistently driving defenders backward and creating running lanes with authority. Outstanding hand placement and timing, consistently landing strikes in the sweet spot and maintaining control throughout the engagement process. Remarkable football intelligence for a young player, quickly identifying stunts, blitzes, and defensive adjustments while maintaining composure under pressure. Impressive versatility across all three interior positions, demonstrating the technical skills and mental acuity needed to excel anywhere along the line. Relentless competitor who finishes blocks with conviction, often driving opponents well past the whistle and creating additional yardage opportunities. Exceptional pass protection instincts, showing an innate understanding of rush lanes and consistently keeping his quarterback pocket clean and secure. Strong work ethic and film study habits that translate to advanced preparation and anticipation of defensive schemes and tendencies.
weaknesses: Limited game experience creates concerns about consistency and performance when facing extended snaps against quality competition over a full season. Needs to add functional strength to his frame, occasionally getting overwhelmed by powerful interior rushers who can bull-rush through his punch. Hip flexibility issues can limit his ability to recover when beaten initially, making it difficult to regain proper positioning mid-rep. Tendency to play too tall at times, losing leverage advantage and allowing smaller, quicker rushers to get underneath his pads. Inconsistent anchor strength against pure power moves, sometimes getting pushed backward into the pocket despite proper hand placement and technique.
player_comparisons: JoshuaGray - Oregon State (73%), JonGaines II - UCLA (70%), JustinShaffer - Georgia (62%)

bio: Roman Hemby emerged as a dynamic playmaker for Maryland over the past two seasons. The Baltimore native redshirted in 2021 before breaking out in 2022 with nearly 1,300 yards from scrimmage. Hemby followed that up with a solid 2023 campaign, starting all 13 games for the Terrapins. At John Carroll High School, Hemby racked up over 1,500 rushing yards and 18 touchdowns in two seasons. He earned First Team All-MIAA honors in 2019 and was named John Carroll's Offensive MVP. A three-star recruit, Hemby chose Maryland over several other programs. In 2022, Hemby earned Honorable Mention All-Big Ten honors after rushing for 989 yards and 10 touchdowns while adding 298 receiving yards. He was named Big Ten Freshman of the Week twice and finished third among FBS freshmen in yards from scrimmage. In 2023, Hemby tallied 680 rushing yards, 349 receiving yards, and 7 total touchdowns despite battling some injuries.
strengths: Explosive acceleration and top-end speed, consistently outrunning defenders' angles in the open field Soft hands and natural receiving ability out of the backfield, excelling on screens and checkdowns Demonstrates good vision and patience, allowing blocks to develop before hitting the hole decisively Versatile three-down back with experience in both zone and gap running schemes Elusive in space, using quick cuts and jump-cuts to make defenders miss in the open field Productive despite limited career touches, averaging over 5 yards per carry in college Flashes big-play ability, with multiple runs of 70+ yards on his college resume Young for the draft class at just 21 years old, with potential for further physical development
weaknesses: Lacks ideal size and power for an every-down NFL back, may struggle in short-yardage situations Pass protection needs significant improvement, often struggling with technique and recognition Regression in production from 2022 to 2023 raises questions about consistency and durability Limited experience as a true workhorse back, never exceeding 200 carries in a college season Can improve ball security, with occasional fumbles due to running style and grip

bio: Twenty-seven tackles for loss, 16 sacks, a pick-six, and a Class 2A state championship as a senior at Dublin High School in Georgia put Romello Height on every major program's radar. ESPN and 247Sports stamped him a four-star prospect and the No. 24 outside linebacker nationally, though Rivals came in a bit lower at three stars. Auburn beat out Florida, Tennessee, Miami, and a dozen other programs for his signature. What followed was one of the more wandering college careers you'll find in this draft class. Height enrolled at Auburn in spring 2020, barely saw the field as a true freshman during the COVID year, then contributed sparingly as a redshirt freshman with 19 tackles and three for loss across nine games. He transferred to USC in 2022, won the starting job out of camp, and promptly tore up his shoulder in week two against Stanford. Season over. He bounced back in 2023 with 20 tackles, six tackles for loss, and four sacks as a rotational piece, then moved again to Georgia Tech for 2024. That year in Atlanta produced his first real sustained success: 34 tackles, a team-high 6.5 tackles for loss, 2.5 sacks, and an interception while starting all 12 games. Pro Football Focus credited him with 32 pressures and the second-best coverage grade among FBS edge rushers at 92.5. The final stop came in Lubbock, where Height joined Texas Tech's loaded defensive front for his sixth collegiate season in 2025. Flanking David Bailey on the opposite edge, Height finally put together the production his athleticism always promised. He finished with 38 tackles, 10.5 tackles for loss, and 10 sacks while earning First Team All-Big 12 honors, a spot on the Sports Illustrated Midseason All-America Team, and an invitation to the Senior Bowl. By the end of his college career, Height had accumulated 69 total pressures across 1,645 defensive snaps and 50 games played at four different programs.
strengths: First step absolutely explodes off the snap, immediately threatening the outside shoulder of tackles before they can establish any sort of kick-slide rhythm or base. Hand repertoire goes well beyond what you typically see from college edge rushers, deploying cross-chops, rip moves, and spin counters that keep linemen guessing throughout games. His ghost move is legitimate, dipping his shoulder and flattening around the corner while protecting himself from getting knocked off his rush lane by grabby tackles. Pursuit motor stays hot from whistle to whistle, showing the kind of backside effort that turns what should be modest gains into no gains or losses. Coverage ability stands out as a genuine weapon, dropping into zones with comfort and even carrying tight ends vertically without looking like a fish out of water. Change-of-direction skills allow him to glide across the formation, beating tackles across their faces with lateral agility that bigger edge players simply cannot replicate. Sets up his pass rush moves intelligently by stemming inside or outside before winning in the opposite direction, creating two-way-go situations that stress offensive tackles. The 96.1% pass rush grade from PFF reflects the consistency here: 69 career pressures including 16.5 sacks across 1,645 snaps represents sustained production, not just splash plays.
weaknesses: At 234 pounds measured at the Senior Bowl, he's giving away 30-40 pounds to most NFL tackles, and when they get hands on him cleanly, he gets stonewalled at the point of attack. Run defense remains a concern that shows up on tape consistently, getting washed down the line or caught inside when he tries to knife gaps too aggressively. Tackling technique breaks down in the open field where he arrives too high and misses opportunities that should be routine conversions against shifty backs. Lacks the functional length and anchor strength to hold the point against double teams, getting displaced off his spot when offenses scheme extra bodies his direction. Pass rush plan can become predictable when speed around the edge fails, lacking the power conversion moves that would keep tackles honest and provide counter options.
player_comparisons: WillMcDonald IV - Iowa State (96%), JonahElliss - Utah (84%), JalyxHunt - Houston Christian (82%)

bio: A former Hueytown High School standout, Roydell Williams (5'10", 214) racked up 5,929 career rushing yards and 84 touchdowns before joining Alabama's loaded backfield. The Golden Gopher product earned first-team all-state honors in 2018 after a 2,757-yard junior campaign that included 32 touchdowns. Through four seasons at Alabama, Williams accumulated 1,165 yards on 234 carries (5.0 YPC) with 11 touchdowns. His most productive season came in 2023, recording 560 yards and five touchdowns on 111 carries while adding 11 receptions for 58 yards. After transferring to Florida State for the 2024 season, Williams struggled early with 54 yards on 20 carries and a critical fumble against Memphis before being sidelined with an extended injury. Williams' collegiate career includes notable performances like his 129-yard outburst against USF and a 110-yard showing versus Southern Miss. He demonstrated versatility with 16 career receptions for 115 yards and two touchdowns while consistently contributing on special teams with multiple tackles in kick coverage.
strengths: Showcases plus instincts in the A and B gaps, displaying decisive plant-and-go ability when hitting the hole Anchors effectively in pass pro, squares up blitzers, and maintains leverage against bigger defenders Natural hands catcher away from frame, tracks ball effectively on swing routes and checkdowns Demonstrates quick-twitch lateral agility at the line with efficient jump-cut mechanics Runs behind pads with proper lean, consistently falls forward through contact Plus blitz recognition skills, identifies and mirrors second-level defenders in protection Downhill runner who attacks the line with conviction, shows chain-moving ability in short yardage Maintains contact balance through initial hits, keeps legs churning post-contact
weaknesses: Lacks second gear to threaten the edge or pull away from pursuit angles Limited wiggle in space, doesn't consistently make unblocked defenders miss Medical red flags after significant time missed in 2024 season Lacks elite burst to consistently create when blocking breaks down Minimal home run ability, limited explosive plays despite volume at Power 5 level

Showcases plus instincts in the A and B gaps, displaying decisive plant-and-go ability when hitting the hole Anchors effectively in pass pro, squares up blitzers, and maintains leverage against bigger defenders Natural hands catcher away from frame, tracks ball effectively on swing routes and checkdowns Demonstrates quick-twitch lateral agility at the line with efficient jump-cut mechanics Runs behind pads with proper lean, consistently falls forward through contact Plus blitz recognition skills, identifies and mirrors second-level defenders in protection Downhill runner who attacks the line with conviction, shows chain-moving ability in short yardage Maintains contact balance through initial hits, keeps legs churning post-contact

Weaknesses:
Lacks second gear to threaten the edge or pull away from pursuit angles Limited wiggle in space, doesn't consistently make unblocked defenders miss Medical red flags after significant time missed in 2024 season Lacks elite burst to consistently create when blocking breaks down Minimal home run ability, limited explosive plays despite volume at Power 5 level

bio: Rueben Bain Jr. comes from a South Florida football family with deep ties to Miami. His grandfather Herman starred at Northwestern High in the 1960s, his father played offensive line at Carol City, and cousin Tolbert was part of Miami's 1987 title team. Tolbert used to bring young Rueben to campus for youth camps, and when Cristobal offered, there was nothing to decide. Bain attended Miami Central, where he won four state championships and racked up 77 career sacks. As a junior he posted 29.5 sacks on a defense that allowed 15.4 points per game. He earned the Nat Moore Trophy as South Florida's top player and was selected for the Under Armour All-America Game before choosing Miami over Alabama, Georgia, and LSU. He started by Week 3 as a true freshman in 2023 and led all FBS defensive linemen with 7.5 sacks, earning ACC Defensive Rookie of the Year honors. A calf injury limited him to nine games in 2024. In 2025 he started all 16 games as team captain, posting 54 tackles, 9.5 sacks, and 15.5 tackles for loss while winning the Ted Hendricks Award, ACC Defensive Player of the Year, and consensus All-American honors. He helped lead Miami to the national championship game against Indiana.
strengths: First-step quickness is a real separator; he is moving before tackles can get their hands set. Initial punch lands with force and consistently knocks blockers off their spot at the point of attack. Converts speed to power naturally, winning outside before walking tackles back into the pocket. Takes tight angles around the edge and gets his shoulder down without drifting wide or running past the quarterback. Plays the run with real aggression, crashing gaps and holding up against double teams from tackles and tight ends. Effort is consistent from first snap to last; cleanup sacks and backside pursuit show up regularly on tape. Versatile alignment options give coordinators flexibility to move him from a wide nine to interior passing-down looks. Performs in big moments: five sacks during the College Football Playoff, including three against Texas A&M.
weaknesses: Arm length measured under 31 inches at the combine; NFL tackles with reach will challenge him differently. Gap discipline is inconsistent; he gambles and vacates his lane, giving quarterbacks easy escape routes. Secondary hand moves need development; blockers who absorb the initial punch can recover and neutralize him. Missed tackle rate above 20 percent across his career suggests issues finishing in space against shifty backs. Counter rush repertoire is limited when plan A stalls; needs a deeper set of moves against NFL-level tackles.

bio: Sage Ryan from Lafayette Christian Academy High School was rated a 4-star recruit by ESPN and handed a 4-star grade by 247 Sports. Following high school, Ryan opted to join LSU after being heavily recruited. In 2021 as a freshman Ryan saw action in just 3 games and played a total of 121 snaps at cornerback for the Tigers. He recorded just 4 tackles, 2 assists, while adding no stops. In coverage Ryan notched up one pass breakup, no interceptions, and QB rating when targetted of 90.8. As a sophomore in 2022 he played in 13 games and logged 277 snaps for the Tigers. He made 15 tackles, 4 assists, and had 4 stops. In coverage he totaled one pass breakup, no interceptions, and a disapointing QB rating when targetted of 143.3. In 2023 as a junior Ryan played in 12 games and contributed on 620 snaps. He chalked up 25 tackles, 7 assists, and added 11 stops. In the secondary Ryan forced 2 pass breakups, no interceptions, and gave up QB rating when targetted of 86.5.
strengths: Eager to come downhill as a tackler and plays with a physical edge, and he’s effective as a blitzer. Good speed in pursuit with the chase skills to track down the ballcarrier. Has a stocky build and sets a physical tone in the middle of the field. Fills hard against the run, showing speed and taking good paths to the ball. Ryan tracks the ball well and does a nice job of getting his head around in plenty of time to avoid flags. Shows good instincts when facing the action in zone coverage, and Ryan has solid ball skills. With his length, athleticism, and natural ball skills, he will be highly valued by press cover teams and could earn a top 100 pick. Willing in run support, coming downhill quickly when he has a chance to make a play.
weaknesses: Physicality comes and goes when facing the run. Ryan doesn’t show much in the way of take-on skills once Ryan meets a blocker Plays out of control too often, laying a lot of big hits but also missing a lot of tackles along the way. He also needs to do a better job reading routes when sitting back as a single-high safety. Despite his size, he’s not an overly physical tackler. Ryan might have to add some weight at the next level in order to become more physical.

bio: Among the all-time leaders in Texas high school history with 11,302 passing yards and 135 touchdowns at Coronado High School in Lubbock, Sawyer Robertson was named Gatorade Texas Player of the Year as a senior after leading the Mustangs to a 12-1 record and the Class 5A regional semifinals. He was a consensus four-star recruit ranked as a top-10 pocket passer nationally. Robertson signed with Mississippi State and spent two seasons in Starkville, seeing limited action behind Will Rogers. He transferred to Baylor ahead of the 2023 season and started four games that year as an injury replacement, finishing with a sharp 17-of-19 showing against West Virginia. When Dequan Finn went down early in 2024, Robertson took over and threw for 3,071 yards and 28 touchdowns against eight interceptions while playing through a lower-body injury that put him in a walking boot between games for the final two months. His 2025 redshirt senior campaign produced 3,681 yards and 31 touchdowns with 12 interceptions across 12 starts. He was named Big 12 Scholar-Athlete of the Year, earned second-team All-Big 12 honors, and participated in the Senior Bowl.
strengths: Quick release gets the ball out in rhythm and beats tight coverage windows. Good arm strength to challenge all three levels, especially on rope throws over the middle. Stands tall in the pocket and delivers under pressure without panicking. Lower-body explosion shows up on scrambles and escapes; combine confirmed it with a 37.5-inch vertical and 10'3" broad. Mobility translates on tape with scramble ability and designed runs that move the chains. Willing to push the ball downfield, averaging nearly 10 air yards per attempt in 2025. Showed clear growth from 2023 to 2024, with significantly improved efficiency and decision-making. Played through a nagging lower-body injury for half of 2024 and never missed a start.
weaknesses: Accuracy is inconsistent, particularly on throws outside the numbers and downfield. Turnover-worthy play rate stayed flat from 2024 to 2025 despite more experience. Tends to lock onto his first read and force throws into coverage against zone looks. Completion percentage dropped in 2025 while volume increased, suggesting mechanical regression. Deep ball often comes out flat and forces receivers to adjust or slow down. Game-to-game grading was wildly inconsistent in 2025, with several poor outings mixed in.

bio: The state of Arkansas hasn't produced many elite tight end prospects in recent years, but Shamar Easter broke that mold when he burst onto the scene at Ashdown High School. Standing 6'5" and weighing 228 pounds with a December 30th, 2004 birth date, Easter dominated small-town competition while building a reputation as the kind of multi-sport athlete that makes scouts take notice. His basketball background shows in his body control and spatial awareness, while his track experience translates to the kind of long speed that makes defensive coordinators lose sleep. Easter's recruitment reflected his status as Arkansas's crown jewel in the 2023 class, earning four-star ratings across the board from major recruiting services. Rivals ranked him 157th nationally and seventh among tight ends, while 247Sports placed him at 158th overall and tenth at his position. ESPN was slightly more conservative at 201st nationally but still rated him as the sixth-best tight end prospect. The Razorbacks beat out a who's who of college football programs including Auburn, Florida State, Michigan State, and Tennessee to secure his commitment. His junior season numbers told the story of his dominance - 30 catches for 519 yards and five touchdowns while averaging over 17 yards per reception. After redshirting his freshman season in Fayetteville, Easter made his collegiate debut in Arkansas's 2024 opener against Arkansas-Pine Bluff, hauling in a 16-yard reception during a crucial fourth-quarter scoring drive. His limited usage throughout the season culminated in Arkansas coaches experimenting with moving him from tight end to wide receiver during Liberty Bowl preparations, signaling their belief that his skill set might be better utilized in space rather than attached to the formation. This position change experiment, combined with limited opportunities, led to his entry into the transfer portal. Easter found a new home at North Carolina, where he'll have three years of eligibility remaining and the chance to showcase his talents in an offense that has historically maximized tight end production under head coach Mack Brown.
strengths: Blazing forty time creates vertical separation and forces safeties to respect his deep speed Exceptional catch radius allows him to snag passes outside his frame and make contested grabs in traffic Route-running precision in the intermediate zones shows advanced footwork and understanding of leverage concepts Multi-alignment versatility gives offensive coordinators flexibility to deploy him anywhere from the backfield to the boundary Natural ball-tracking ability combined with soft hands makes him a reliable target in crucial situations Second-level speed creates explosive play potential once he clears the initial coverage defender Basketball background translates to excellent body positioning and box-out techniques in contested catch scenarios High football intelligence evident in his route adjustments and ability to find soft spots in zone coverage
weaknesses: Lightweight frame struggles against physical press coverage and needs significant bulk to handle NFL-caliber edge rushers Limited college production makes it difficult to project consistency at the next level against elite competition Blocking technique remains underdeveloped with poor hand placement and inconsistent pad level in run support Relies too heavily on athleticism rather than technical soundness when engaged with physical defenders Pass protection experience is minimal, which could limit his three-down potential early in his career Position uncertainty after Arkansas moved him to wide receiver raises questions about his true NFL fit
player_comparisons: LeonardTaylor - Cincinnati (47%), DanielBellinger - San Diego State (43%), BenSims - Baylor (38%)

bio: COVID-19 wiped out his senior season at The Taft School in Watertown, Connecticut, but Skyler Bell had already shown enough as a junior with 34 catches for 534 yards and seven touchdowns to sign with Wisconsin as a three-star recruit. The Bronx native redshirted in 2021, then started 12 of 13 games in 2022, finishing with 30 catches for 444 yards and five scores, earning honorable mention Freshman All-American honors from CFB News. His 2023 season at Wisconsin was quieter, with 38 receptions for 296 yards and one touchdown. Bell entered the transfer portal and landed at UConn, where his career shifted. In 2024 he led the Huskies with 860 receiving yards and five touchdowns on 50 catches, highlighted by a 153-yard, three-score game against Buffalo. The 2025 season put Bell on the national map. He finished with 101 receptions for 1,278 yards and 13 touchdowns, becoming UConn's first consensus All-American and a Biletnikoff Award finalist. He earned invitations to both the Senior Bowl and East-West Shrine Bowl, then posted a 41-inch vertical and 11-foot-1 broad jump at the NFL Combine.
strengths: Explosive lower half confirmed at the Combine; vertical and broad jump both tested near the top of the class. Plays to his timed speed on tape, stacking corners vertically and pulling away in the open field. Sharp hip sink at route stems opens consistent throwing windows underneath and on digs. Outstanding after-the-catch production, averaging over eight YAC per reception in 2025. Throttle control and tempo changes within routes make him a tough cover in man. Good hand size for his frame helps him secure the ball in traffic and on contested grabs. Versatile alignment, working from the slot and outside with nearly equal comfort. Cut his drop rate drastically in 2025 after battling concentration issues in prior seasons.
weaknesses: Undersized frame with below-average weight and arm length for the position at the NFL level. Physical press corners can disrupt his release and knock him off his route timing. Career drop history is a concern despite the 2025 improvement being real. Gets bumped off crossing routes when linebackers or safeties deliver contact at the catch point. Contested catch ability is limited by his frame and average catch radius. Blocking grade is poor and will need significant development at the next level. Will be 24 as a rookie, which limits his developmental runway compared to younger peers.

bio: Skyler Gill-Howard started his college career as a walk-on linebacker at Division II Upper Iowa in 2021, playing in a town of 800 people after being barely recruited out of Greenfield High School despite All-State honors. After one season, he transferred to Northern Illinois as a walk-on, packed on over 50 pounds, and switched to defensive tackle. That redshirt year in 2022 earned him NIU's Defensive Futures Player of the Year award, and the transformation was underway. The 2024 season at Northern Illinois put Gill-Howard on the map. He led all Huskies defensive linemen with 51 tackles while adding 8.5 tackles for loss and 5.0 sacks, earning Third Team All-MAC honors and a team captain designation. Pro Football Focus graded him at 84.0 overall, ranking 10th among FBS interior linemen, with his run defense grade of 88.7 placing sixth nationally. His seven-tackle, three-tackle-for-loss performance against Bowling Green earned MAC Co-Defensive Player of the Week honors and caught the attention of major programs. Texas Tech won his services, and he became the emotional heartbeat of one of college football's best defenses in 2025. Gill-Howard's senior season at Texas Tech lasted just six games before an ankle injury against Kansas ended his year, but he made every snap count. He posted nine tackles with 2.5 tackles for loss, six quarterback hits, and a 55-yard pick-six off a tipped pass against Kent State that earned him Big 12 Defensive Player of the Week. Pro Football Focus rated him the fourth-best defensive tackle in the country before the injury. A two-time Wisconsin state wrestling finalist in high school, Gill-Howard credits that background for teaching him leverage, hand placement, and how to keep blockers off balance.
strengths: Explosive first step out of his stance is legitimately NFL-caliber, consistently beating guards to the punch and creating immediate disruption in both A and B gaps that forces offensive lines into panic mode. Plays with outstanding leverage for his size, keeping his pad level low and using textbook hip and hand technique learned from wrestling to maintain gap integrity and shed blocks at the point of attack. Shows excellent gap discipline and football intelligence, reading plays quickly and flowing to the ball with purpose rather than just crashing recklessly, which led his team in tackles among defensive linemen. Interior pass rush ability is legitimate and translatable, generating 29 total pressures in 2024 with quick hands, active feet, and the kind of get-off that creates havoc up the middle for quarterbacks. Runs to the football with relentless effort and motor that never quits, making tackles downfield and pursuing ball carriers sideline to sideline despite his role in the interior trenches. Displays rare versatility for a defensive tackle, capable of playing multiple techniques along the line and even showing enough range to tip passes at the line and return one 55 yards for a touchdown. Competitive toughness and mental resilience shine through his entire journey, from walk-on linebacker to FBS captain, proving he can handle adversity and find ways to contribute regardless of circumstances. Wrestling background provides exceptional hand fighting ability and body control, allowing him to disengage from blocks more efficiently than most defensive tackles and maintain balance through contact.
weaknesses: Size limitations are real at 6'1" and 290 pounds, as he'll face significantly taller and heavier guards in the NFL who can use length to keep him at bay and wear him down physically. Bull rush power is inconsistent when matched against bigger, stronger blockers who can anchor and stalemate him at the point of attack, limiting his effectiveness on obvious rushing downs against physical offensive lines. Limited sample size at the highest level of competition, with just six games against Big 12 competition before injury and most of his production coming against MAC-level competition that doesn't replicate NFL blocking schemes. Durability concerns emerge with the ankle injury that cost him his senior season, raising questions about whether his body can hold up to the weekly grind of professional football over a 17-game schedule. Tackling technique can get sloppy in space, particularly when pursuing ball carriers laterally, and his wrap-up fundamentals need refinement to avoid missed tackles at the next level where everyone is faster.

After one season, he transferred to Northern Illinois as a walk-on, packed on over 50 pounds, and switched to defensive tackle. That redshirt year in 2022 earned him NIU's Defensive Futures Player of the Year award, and the transformation was underway. The 2024 season at Northern Illinois put Gill-Howard on the map. He led all Huskies defensive linemen with 51 tackles while adding 8.5 tackles for loss and 5.0 sacks, earning Third Team All-MAC honors and a team captain designation. Pro Football Focus graded him at 84.0 overall, ranking 10th among FBS interior linemen, with his run defense grade of 88.7 placing sixth nationally. His seven-tackle, three-tackle-for-loss performance against Bowling Green earned MAC Co-Defensive Player of the Week honors and caught the attention of major programs. Texas Tech won his services, and he became the emotional heartbeat of one of college football's best defenses in 2025. Gill-Howard's senior season at Texas Tech lasted just six games before an ankle injury against Kansas ended his year, but he made every snap count. He posted nine tackles with 2.5 tackles for loss, six quarterback hits, and a 55-yard pick-six off a tipped pass against Kent State that earned him Big 12 Defensive Player of the Week. Pro Football Focus rated him the fourth-best defensive tackle in the country before the injury. A two-time Wisconsin state wrestling finalist in high school, Gill-Howard credits that background for teaching him leverage, hand placement, and how to keep blockers off balance.

Strengths:
Explosive first step out of his stance is legitimately NFL-caliber, consistently beating guards to the punch and creating immediate disruption in both A and B gaps that forces offensive lines into panic mode. Plays with outstanding leverage for his size, keeping his pad level low and using textbook hip and hand technique learned from wrestling to maintain gap integrity and shed blocks at the point of attack. Shows excellent gap discipline and football intelligence, reading plays quickly and flowing to the ball with purpose rather than just crashing recklessly, which led his team in tackles among defensive linemen. Interior pass rush ability is legitimate and translatable, generating 29 total pressures in 2024 with quick hands, active feet, and the kind of get-off that creates havoc up the middle for quarterbacks. Runs to the football with relentless effort and motor that never quits, making tackles downfield and pursuing ball carriers sideline to sideline despite his role in the interior trenches. Displays rare versatility for a defensive tackle, capable of playing multiple techniques along the line and even showing enough range to tip passes at the line and return one 55 yards for a touchdown. Competitive toughness and mental resilience shine through his entire journey, from walk-on linebacker to FBS captain, proving he can handle adversity and find ways to contribute regardless of circumstances. Wrestling background provides exceptional hand fighting ability and body control, allowing him to disengage from blocks more efficiently than most defensive tackles and maintain balance through contact.

Weaknesses:
Size limitations are real at 6'1" and 290 pounds, as he'll face significantly taller and heavier guards in the NFL who can use length to keep him at bay and wear him down physically. Bull rush power is inconsistent when matched against bigger, stronger blockers who can anchor and stalemate him at the point of attack, limiting his effectiveness on obvious rushing downs against physical offensive lines. Limited sample size at the highest level of competition, with just six games against Big 12 competition before injury and most of his production coming against MAC-level competition that doesn't replicate NFL blocking schemes. Durability concerns emerge with the ankle injury that cost him his senior season, raising questions about whether his body can hold up to the weekly grind of professional football over a 17-game schedule. Tackling technique can get sloppy in space, particularly when pursuing ball carriers laterally, and his wrap-up fundamentals need refinement to avoid missed tackles at the next level where everyone is faster.

bio: Terrence Javaughn Hall grew up in Fresno, California, the oldest son of Monique and Terrence Hall Sr., a former standout wide receiver at Western Illinois. TJ was a four-year letterman at San Joaquin Memorial High School, where he played both sides of the ball as a defensive back, wide receiver, and even quarterback while also returning kicks and punts. His teams went 41-5 over four years and won three straight league championships. As a senior, he posted 26 tackles and three interceptions on defense while hauling in 48 catches for 805 yards and 10 touchdowns on offense, earning all-conference and all-state honors along with team MVP and conference Specialist of the Year. He was also team captain. Born April 26, 2004, Hall checks in at 6-0, 190 pounds. The recruiting path was anything but straightforward. Rated a three-star prospect across ESPN, 247Sports, and Rivals, Hall originally committed to Arizona in the summer of 2021 before flipping to Washington. A personal connection through his father's former college coach, Don Patterson, who had become an Iowa assistant, eventually opened an unexpected door. Hall took an official visit to Iowa City in October 2021 and watched the Hawkeyes knock off No. 4 Penn State, arriving just in time to witness the famous Kinnick Stadium wave and the field-storming celebration that followed. He committed shortly after and enrolled early, skipping his final high school semester to begin spring practice. It was a bold move for a kid from the Central Valley who, by his own admission, didn't know much about Iowa football growing up. Hall's first two seasons in Iowa City were a trial by fire. He appeared in all 13 games as a true freshman in 2022, getting thrown into extended action against Nebraska when starter Cooper DeJean went down with an injury. The Cornhuskers attacked him relentlessly, and Trey Palmer burned him for an 87-yard touchdown that left a mark long after the final whistle. Hall was so upset he apologized to teammates and coaches afterward. A season-ending injury in 2023 limited him to just six games and five tackles as a sophomore. But he kept grinding. Hall won the starting job ahead of the 2024 campaign and appeared in 12 games with seven starts, posting 19 tackles, two pass deflections, an interception, and a fumble recovery. Then came the real payoff: as a senior in 2025, Hall racked up 47 tackles (30 solo), one interception, and 10 passes defended across 602 defensive snaps, earning Third-Team All-Big Ten recognition and a Senior Bowl invitation. His run defense grading was among the best at any position in the conference that year, and he finished the season without a single missed tackle.
strengths: Run defense ability is flat-out special for a cornerback. His grading against the run over the course of his career sits in rare territory for a corner, and the film backs it up with rep after rep of him firing downhill and setting edges against the run and screen game. Thrives working the boundary in zone concepts, reading route stems and triggering on break points with terrific anticipation and spacing discipline that belies his relatively limited time as a full-time starter. Press technique improved dramatically in his final season. Delivers well-timed jabs at the line that disrupt receiver timing and then flips his hips smoothly to carry routes when needed. The physicality is genuine and he wants contact. Catch-point competitor who attacks the ball at its highest point. He tallied nine pass breakups as a senior by getting his hands into the passing window and punching through the receiver's catch radius at the precise moment it mattered. Tackling reliability took a massive leap as a senior. After struggling with missed tackles earlier in his career, he cleaned up his angles and wrap-up mechanics and did not miss a single tackle in his final college season. Football intelligence shows up in how quickly he processes route combinations and adjusts his responsibility post-snap. Recognizes bunch formations and pick plays faster than most corners in this class, and his zone coverage grading reflects that processing speed. Versatile enough to line up in different spots. Iowa used him on the boundary, in the slot, and even in box alignments at various points, and he handled all three without looking lost or uncomfortable. Toughness and competitive character define his journey. Getting embarrassed as a freshman would break a lot of players, but he came back from that Nebraska game and a lost sophomore season to become one of the better defenders in the Big Ten by the time he left.
weaknesses: Recovery speed is a legitimate concern at the next level. His below-average timed speed shows up on film when receivers stack him vertically, as there is a noticeable hitch in his transition that creates separation he cannot close. Man coverage grading trails his zone numbers noticeably, and the gap between those two tells you where his comfort zone ends. NFL offensive coordinators will scheme to isolate him in man situations if given the chance. Carries his hands too low in press alignment, giving receivers a cleaner release and more time to counter his jams. At the next level, wideouts will exploit that habit immediately and consistently. Ball skills remain a work in progress despite the pass breakup numbers. He gets his hands on plenty of passes but is a poor tracker and catcher of the football, which caps his turnover ceiling. Only two career interceptions in 1,127 snaps tells the story. Iowa's defensive scheme kept him in a relatively confined role, so there is a limited sample of him working in true man-to-man against speed over extended field. How much of his coverage production was scheme-aided versus his own ability remains a fair question heading into the evaluation process.
player_comparisons: ShaunWade - Ohio State (84%), TommiHill - Nebraska (81%), NohlWilliams - California (80%)

bio: Wide receiver production of 26 catches for 463 yards and six touchdowns at nearly 18 yards per reception wasn't even the main selling point for Tacario Davis at Millikan High School in Long Beach, California. On defense, he racked up 74 total tackles across two varsity seasons and picked off passes with the kind of ball skills that would define his college career. He also ran track, and that combination of size and speed made scouts take notice, but the recruiting world was slow to catch on. Davis left high school as a three-star prospect with offers from Arizona State and Kansas before landing at Arizona in the 2022 class. His first year in Tucson was a quiet one, limited to five appearances and seven tackles as a true freshman. Everything changed in 2023. Davis locked down one of Arizona's starting corner spots for 11 of 13 games and led the entire Pac-12 with 15 pass breakups and 16 total passes defended. He added 25 tackles, snagged an interception at Washington State, and earned second-team All-Pac-12 honors from the AP along with honorable mention from the league's coaches. It was the kind of sophomore leap that gets scouts pulling up your film on a Tuesday night. Arizona's move to the Big 12 in 2024 didn't slow Davis down. He started 11 of 12 games, collected 44 tackles and six pass deflections, and was named second-team All-Big 12. A five-pass-breakup masterclass against then-No. 10 Utah earned him Big 12 Defensive Player of the Week and put him on the Jim Thorpe Award semifinalist list. After the season, Davis entered the transfer portal and followed former Arizona head coach Jedd Fisch to Washington, reuniting with the coaching staff that had originally recruited him. His 2025 season with the Huskies was disrupted by injuries, limiting him to seven of 13 games, but he still managed honorable mention All-Big Ten recognition while recording two interceptions, four pass breakups, and 19 tackles in his limited action.
strengths: His 6'4" frame is a genuine outlier at cornerback, and the length creates passing windows that feel claustrophobic for quarterbacks trying to fit balls into tight spots. Ran a 4.41 at the Combine at 194 pounds, which is absurd for a corner this tall and validated the long speed you see on tape when he uncoils downfield. Forceful and deliberate in press, using those long arms to jam receivers at the line and throw off timing before the route even develops. Sticky in man coverage once he gets into a receiver's hip pocket, and his stride length lets him eat up ground without looking like he's working hard. Legitimate run defender who embraces contact on the boundary, using his frame to stack and shed blocks on the edge like a bigger player. Reliable wrap-up tackler who drives through ballcarriers instead of lunging or swiping, and his career tackle grades back up the eye test. Ball skills are real, not theoretical. Twenty career pass breakups and three interceptions show a player who consistently locates and attacks the football at its highest point. Reads the quarterback's eyes from zone coverage with natural feel, jumping underneath routes and squeezing throwing lanes with his wingspan.
weaknesses: Transition quickness is the biggest concern on film. He pops upright during his backpedal and gets caught flat when receivers snap off sharp breaks underneath. Twitchier route runners can beat him off the line in press because his long stride takes a beat to reach full speed, and that first step can be exploited. Penalty count is a red flag; he resorts to grabbing when his feet aren't in position, and NFL officials will eat him alive for that habit. Multi-breaking routes and pick concepts give him real trouble because his frame makes it hard to redirect and navigate traffic at the second level. Coverage numbers dipped noticeably during his final two seasons, and his 2025 injury history at Washington raises durability questions heading into the draft process.
player_comparisons: IsraelMukuamu - South Carolina (87%), AaronRobinson - UCF (67%), JaylonJones - Texas A&M (65%)

bio: York arrived at Texas A&M as a three-star recruit out of Temple High School in Texas, where he was a four-year varsity starter and three-time district Defensive MVP. He racked up 435 career tackles, 47 tackles for loss, and 15 sacks at the prep level, though the recruiting services never bumped him above a three-star consensus rating. He stepped into the starting lineup from day one in College Station and never gave the job back. As a true freshman in 2023, York started all 13 games and finished second on the team with 74 tackles while adding 8.5 tackles for loss and five hurries. That production earned him ESPN Freshman All-America honors and a spot on the SEC All-Freshman Team. He was also named a semifinalist for the Shaun Alexander Freshman of the Year Award. His sophomore season brought a captain's title before the year even started. York led the Aggies with 82 tackles and 9.5 tackles for loss, picked off his first career pass against LSU, and strung together back-to-back double-digit tackle games against Auburn and Texas to close the regular season. He was nominated for the team's Defensive MVP award at the end of the year.
strengths: Processes blocking schemes and run fits quickly, rarely caught guessing on misdirection or play-action. Natural leverage advantage at the point of attack thanks to low pad level and dense lower half. Sideline-to-sideline range is legitimate; closes ground fast and arrives with intent. Showed major growth as a coverage defender, particularly in zone drops and route recognition. Scrapes to the football with urgency and maintains disciplined gap assignments on the move. Willing and capable run defender who stacks blockers despite his frame and frees himself to make plays. Reliable wrap tackler who finishes through contact and brings ball carriers down in the open field. Competitive temperament shows up every snap; effort never wavers regardless of game situation. v
weaknesses: Undersized frame limits his ability to hold up when bigger blockers lock onto him at the second level. Missed tackle rate trended the wrong direction in his junior year after improving as a sophomore. Short arms reduce his catch-denial radius and make contesting throws over the middle difficult. Man coverage against athletic tight ends and bigger slot receivers will be a consistent mismatch. Pass rush plan is mostly effort-based; lacks developed counter moves when his initial rush stalls. Can commit too aggressively downhill and lose contain on cutback runs when he overrides his read.
player_comparisons: BrianAsamoah - Oklahoma (85%), DorianWilliams - Tulane (80%), JeremiahTrotter Jr. - Clemson (75%)

bio: Ted Hurst grew up in Savannah, Georgia, where he earned First Team All-Region honors at Sol C. Johnson High School after posting 41 catches for 591 yards and six touchdowns as a senior. He was also a standout basketball player, averaging 16.6 points per game. With no major recruiting attention, Hurst landed at Division II Valdosta State, where he spent two seasons catching 60 passes for over 1,000 yards and 10 touchdowns across the 2022 and 2023 campaigns. He transferred to Georgia State ahead of the 2024 season and immediately became the Panthers' top target. That first year produced 56 receptions for 961 yards and a program single-season record nine touchdowns, highlighted by a game-winning score against Vanderbilt with 15 seconds remaining and a 76-yard touchdown against Georgia Southern. He earned Second Team All-Sun Belt honors. As a senior in 2025, Hurst crossed 1,000 receiving yards for the first time with 71 catches for 1,004 yards and six touchdowns. He collected First Team All-Sun Belt recognition and became the first Georgia State player named to The Athletic's College Freaks List. By the end of his career, Hurst sat fourth in program history in receiving yards (1,965), touchdowns (15), and 100-yard games (8). He received an invitation to the Senior Bowl.
strengths: Gets off the line fast and forces corners to honor vertical immediately. Long strider who builds speed through routes and closes gaps downfield. Tracks deep throws with consistent focus and adjusts well in stride. Clean hip sink on comebacks and out-breaking routes for a receiver his size. Deep ball production backed by strong grades; tracks and adjusts at all levels. After-catch production jumped significantly as a senior; more than doubled his missed tackles forced. Reads zone leverage well, sits in windows, and led the team in first-down receptions. Red zone target whose length and body control show up on fades and back-shoulders. Lower-body explosiveness tested among the best in the class and shows up on tape.
weaknesses: Drop rate stayed stubbornly high across both seasons at Georgia State. Contested catch rate is only average despite his length and frame. Release package is too reliant on footwork; lacks counter moves against press. Lean build invites physical corners to reroute him off his stems. Run blocking is an afterthought on tape and his frame limits sustain. Shows a slight hitch before firing out of his stance at times. Can lose steam on extended drives and late-game snaps.
player_comparisons: CorneliusJohnson - Michigan (94%), NicoCollins - Michigan (93%), BryceFord-Wheaton - West Virginia (92%)
