bio: Two-time first-team All-Ohio at Sandusky High School, Terion Stewart rushed for 2,758 yards and 39 touchdowns as a junior and followed that with 2,150 yards and 29 scores as a senior. Despite that production, he signed with Bowling Green as a three-star recruit. His early years with the Falcons were quiet. He ran for 295 yards as a freshman in the shortened 2020 season, then added 412 yards and five scores in 2021. Stewart did not play in 2022 due to personal and mental health reasons. He returned in 2023 looking like a different player, rushing for 762 yards and eight touchdowns in nine games to earn second-team All-MAC. His yards per rush after contact that season ranked second nationally. Stewart built on that in 2024, totaling 898 yards and six touchdowns while earning first-team All-MAC honors. He transferred to Virginia Tech for his final season and settled into a complementary role behind Marcellous Hawkins, carrying 82 times for 469 yards in 10 games. His best performance came at NC State: 175 yards including an 85-yard run.
strengths: Career missed-tackles-forced rate of 0.47 per attempt is among the best in this class. Compact frame and low center of gravity give him real contact balance through traffic. Stiff arm is a go-to weapon, and he deploys it with good timing and force. Quick first two steps let him hit the hole before linebackers can close. Change-of-direction ability and lateral agility are well above average for his size. Runs with natural pad level and leverage, making him hard to square up in the hole. Showed willingness in pass protection, and his low base gives him a starting point there.
weaknesses: Ball security is a real problem: five fumbles and three drops across his last two seasons. Virtually nonexistent as a receiver, with just nine catches in five college seasons. Vision comes and goes; he sometimes misses cutback lanes that are clearly there. Below-average long speed limits his ability to finish runs once he reaches the second level. Shorter stride length caps his gear on breakaway attempts against pursuit angles. Off-field concerns and a lost 2022 season will draw questions from teams during the process.
player_comparisons: DeWayneMcBride - UAB (55%), CamerunPeoples - Appalachian State (52%), KimaniVidal - Troy (51%)

bio: Terrence Ferguson II, a former Alabama offensive lineman, made waves in the college football landscape by transferring to Florida State for the 2024 season. The 6-foot-4, 318-pound interior lineman was a highly touted recruit coming out of Peach County High School in Fort Valley, Georgia, where he was ranked as a five-star prospect by PrepStar and a four-star by other major recruiting services. Ferguson's journey at Alabama saw him gradually work his way into the rotation. In 2023, he earned his first career start against USF and saw action in six games throughout the season. His versatility was on display as he took snaps at both guard and center positions. Despite limited playing time, Ferguson showed flashes of the potential that made him a coveted recruit, helping Alabama's offense accumulate impressive yardage, including a season-high 315 rushing yards against Chattanooga. Coming out of high school, Ferguson was highly regarded, ranking as the No. 54 recruit nationally and the No. 2 guard on the 247Composite. His accolades included selection to the Under Armour All-America Game roster and recognition as a 2018 MaxPreps High School Sophomore All-American at tight end. This background, combined with his experience in Alabama's elite program, sets the stage for Ferguson's potential impact at Florida State.
strengths: Versatile interior lineman with experience at both guard and center, providing valuable flexibility for offensive schemes and depth charts Imposing physical presence at 318 pounds with good height (76 inches), offering an ideal build to anchor against NFL-caliber defensive linemen High-ceiling prospect with a strong pedigree - consensus four-star prospesct Quick learner who rapidly ascended Alabama's depth chart, suggesting strong football IQ and ability to absorb complex offensive schemes Experience in a pro-style offense at Alabama, which should ease his transition to NFL offensive systems and concepts Demonstrates good mobility for his size, as indicated by his involvement in plays that required pulling or reaching the second level High-character individual
weaknesses: Limited collegiate experience with only one career start, raising questions about his readiness for immediate NFL contribution Pass protection skills need refinement - has struggled facing sophisticated pass rush moves Run blocking efficiency requires improvement, with inconsistent performance in creating lanes and sustaining blocks through the whistle Average athleticism for the position, potentially limiting his effectiveness in zone-blocking schemes and when pulling in space May need time to adjust to the speed of the NFL game due to his limited snap count over his college career
player_comparisons: CedricePaillant - Marshall (93%), ColeSchneider - UCF (89%), PrincePines - Tulane (87%)

bio: Thaddeus Dixon bounced between Paramount High and La Mirada High during his prep years, and when the recruiting process wrapped up, he didn't have a single Division I offer waiting for him. Academic concerns and maturity questions scared programs off. So Dixon landed at Long Beach City College, where he finally got his shot to prove himself on the field. Two productive seasons with the Vikings changed everything. Dixon racked up 76 tackles, five interceptions, and a touchdown while earning recognition as a top-50 junior college prospect nationally. ESPN had him as their fifth-ranked JUCO corner, and 247Sports slotted him at eighth. Washington came calling in December 2022, and Dixon jumped at the chance. His first season in Seattle saw him contribute in 14 of 15 games during the Huskies' national championship run, recording 26 tackles, six pass breakups, and a pick. He made two tackles in the title game loss to Michigan. The 2024 campaign brought a starting role and legitimate recognition. Dixon locked down 12 starts, finished with 69 tackles across his Washington tenure, posted 16 pass deflections, two interceptions, three tackles for loss, and a forced fumble. The coaches rewarded him with honorable mention All-Big Ten honors, and his teammates voted him Washington's defensive skill player of the year. When Bill Belichick took the North Carolina job and brought defensive coordinator Steve Belichick with him, Dixon followed his DC to Chapel Hill in January 2025. Ranked as the third-best cornerback transfer in the nation by 247Sports, Dixon entered his final college season as one of four Tar Heels nominated for preseason All-ACC honors. The 2025 campaign at UNC proved challenging. Dixon started the first five games and looked like the lockdown presence Chapel Hill needed, recording 14 tackles and three pass deflections while allowing just nine catches on 16 targets. Then came the Clemson game on October 4th, where he snagged an interception but suffered an upper-body injury that sidelined him for over a month. He returned for the Duke game in late November and finished the season, but the injury-shortened year limited his production to 20 total tackles and six passes defended across seven games. The Tar Heels finished 4-8, going 2-6 in ACC play during Belichick's first season at the helm.
strengths: Built like a prototype outside corner with legit length at 6'1" and arms that disrupt timing at the catch point consistently. Plays with a physicality that belies his 185-pound frame; you'd swear he weighs 195 based on how he competes at the line of scrimmage. Washington trusted him to shadow opponents' top receivers regardless of alignment, sliding between boundary and slot responsibilities seamlessly. Downhill trigger when playing off coverage jumps off the tape; his breaks on curls and comebacks arrive with real force and disruption. Ball-hawking mentality shows up in his five JUCO interceptions carrying over to the Power Five level with two more picks at Washington. Competitive fire burns hot on every snap; teammates and coaches describe him as relentless, and his effort against Riverside CC while down two scores exemplifies that. National championship game experience provides invaluable reps; he's faced top competition in the biggest moments and kept his composure. Shows real versatility working under Steve Belichick's scheme, handling multiple coverage responsibilities and adjusting to offensive formations pre-snap.
weaknesses: Gets handsy with quicker slot receivers when his feet fall behind; the grabbing draws flags and better officials will punish it. Needs to sharpen his recognition of release variations; too often guessing instead of reading, which leads to false steps at the line. Tackling grade sits below what you'd want from a starting corner; his angles can get sloppy and he misses in space more than acceptable. Run support could use more consistency; doesn't always fit with authority when asked to come up and set the edge. The JUCO-to-Power-Five path means fewer reps against elite competition than corners who started at major programs from day one. Durability question surfaced in 2025 when an upper-body injury cost him six weeks and limited his final college season to just seven games.
player_comparisons: JoshJobe - Alabama (96%), JasonMarshall Jr. - Florida (94%), GregNewsome II - Northwestern (89%)

bio: Tim Keenan III is a Birmingham, Alabama native who played at Ramsay High School, helping lead his team to a 10-3 record and a 5A state quarterfinal berth. A four-star recruit by ESPN and Rivals, he was ranked the No. 18 defensive tackle nationally by Rivals. A knee injury his senior year contributed to him arriving at Alabama at roughly 380 pounds. Keenan redshirted in 2021 and saw only two games in 2022. After a significant body transformation that brought him down closer to 330 pounds, he earned the starting nose tackle job in 2023 and recorded 38 tackles, 1.5 for loss, and one sack across 14 games. His 2024 season was his best: 40 tackles, 7.5 for loss, and 2.5 sacks in 13 games, highlighted by a nine-tackle, 2.5-TFL effort against South Florida that earned SEC Defensive Lineman of the Week honors. Keenan returned for 2025 as a permanent team captain. He missed the first three games with an ankle injury but started 12 contests, finishing with 16 stops, three tackles for loss, two sacks, two fumble recoveries, and a blocked punt. Off the field, he was recognized on watch lists for the AFCA Good Works Team, the Jason Witten Collegiate Man of the Year, and the Wuerffel Trophy.
strengths: Excellent run defender who stacks blocks, holds ground, and controls his gaps consistently. Fires hands with good timing at the snap, often winning the initial strike against centers. Wide base and low pad level give him a natural leverage advantage at the point of attack. Absorbs double teams effectively and keeps linebackers clean behind him. Reliable gap discipline; rarely chases and stays responsible within the defensive structure. Shows functional hand variety with club, swim, and two-handed swipe techniques. Physical transformation from 380 to 327 pounds speaks to serious commitment and discipline. Useful as a stunt picker; generates enough push to create openings for looping defenders.
weaknesses: Short arms let blockers get into his chest before he can establish control. Limited pass rush upside; wins come through effort and push, not burst or bend. Below-average lateral range limits him chasing plays outside his immediate gap. Testing numbers across the board confirm a narrow athletic profile for the position. Gets displaced when sustained double teams walk him off the ball in power schemes. Weight management will need ongoing attention given his frame and history.
player_comparisons: DJDale - Alabama (79%), KeondreCoburn - Texas (78%), JamareeCaldwell - Oregon (78%)

bio: Three high schools in St. Louis before landing at East St. Louis for his senior year tells you the path wasn't straightforward for Toriano Pride Jr. That final prep season in 2021 put him on the map: 84 tackles, three interceptions, and an 82-yard pick-six against Belleville West while helping the Flyers reach the IHSA Class 6A state championship. He earned All-American Bowl honors and was a consensus four-star recruit rated as high as the No. 6 cornerback nationally by 247Sports. Pride signed with Clemson in January 2022. Two years at Clemson yielded modest production in a rotational role: 31 tackles, five pass breakups, and an interception across 481 snaps. The playing time never materialized into a starting job, and Pride entered the portal after 2023. He found a home at Missouri. In 2024, Pride played all 13 games with six starts, posting 22 tackles, two interceptions including a pick-six against Murray State, and four pass breakups. That earned him a full-time starting role in 2025, and starting 12 of 13 games he recorded 24 tackles, two more interceptions, a 62-yard fumble recovery return, and four pass breakups. The signature moment came on Senior Night against Mississippi State: a pick-six and a fumble scoop-and-score in the same game.
strengths: Closing burst from off-coverage is a real weapon, and the 4.32 at the Combine confirmed what the tape shows. Competitive and physical in press coverage, jamming receivers and disrupting timing at the line despite his size. Fluid hips allow him to carry vertical routes and close cushion before receivers can stack him. Excellent ball-tracking ability on deep throws, consistently turning his head around to play the ball. Zone awareness stands out on film; reads the quarterback's eyes and jumps passing lanes with real confidence. Active hands at the catch point create contested situations, snaking a hand in to rake the ball loose. Willing run-support defender who triggers downhill without hesitation and fills the alley against perimeter runs. Cleaned up his tackling dramatically in 2025, cutting his missed tackle rate and wrapping with better technique.
weaknesses: Undersized frame with short arms limits his ability to contest throws against bigger perimeter receivers. Change of direction is a concern; struggles to redirect when receivers snap off hard-breaking routes. Gets caught flat-footed against sharp release moves and savvy route runners can win early off the line. Lacks functional strength as a tackler; ball carriers can drag him and pick up yards after first contact. Speed is legitimate in a straight line, but the lateral agility does not match, leaving him exposed on cuts.
player_comparisons: KaryVincent Jr. - LSU (87%), A.J.Woods - Pittsburgh (83%), JabbarMuhammad - Oregon (82%)

bio: Trey Cooley from Knightdale High School was rated a 4-star recruit by ESPN and handed a 4-star grade by 247 Sports. Following high school, Cooley joined Louisville. In 2021 as a freshman Cooley saw action in 12 games for the Cardinals as he ran for 420 yards on 86 attempts for 4.9 yards per carry, with one TD, with one fumble. As a receiver he made only 12 receptions for 173 yards. As a sophomore in 2022 he played 6 games. and rushed for 278 yards on 59 carries for 4.7 ypc, with 2 TDs, with no fumbles. In the passing game he pulled in only 9 receptions for 66 yards. After the end of the year Cooley transferred to Georgia Tech where as a junior Cooley saw action in 9 games for the Yellow Jackets. He ran for 270 yards on 64 attempts for 4.2 ypc, with 3 TDs, with 3 fumbles. As a receiver he made only 11 receptions for 112 yards.
strengths: Quick feet with good vision to pick through defenses. Cooley has solid hands and body control and is a reliable receiver. Does not possess top breakaway speed, though he's rarely caught from behind. Flashes initial burst and good lateral quickness to sidestep defenders or bounce runs outside with very good foot quickness. Displays very good feel as an inside and outside runner with above average vision to make sharp cuts - good patience· and footwork to pick through defenses. Possesses quick feet and straight-line speed but doesn't have the elite burst to be a constant big-play threat. Improving patience as a runner, following pulling guards and makes good decisions on his cut. He’s patient in the backfield, willing to wait out his blocking and not panicking, and Cooley consistently spots cutback lanes and takes advantage. Cooley has the straight-line speed to outrun angles, and his flexibility allows him to turn the corner on the perimeter and stay near top speed as he does; he’s especially effective on fly sweeps.
weaknesses: Offers little in pass protection and poor technique cut blocking, fails to do more than slow down blitzers by laying down in front of them. He’s not going to threaten to take the top off of a defense. His speed is decent, but he’s not a home run hitter at the next level.
player_comparisons: MulbahCar - Houston (79%), LDBrown - Oklahoma State (74%), BrendenKnox - Marshall (66%)

Offers little in pass protection and poor technique cut blocking, fails to do more than slow down blitzers by laying down in front of them. He’s not going to threaten to take the top off of a defense. His speed is decent, but he’s not a home run hitter at the next level.

bio: Trey Holly from Union Parish High School was rated a 4-star recruit by ESPN and handed a 3-star grade by 247 Sports. Following high school, Holly joined LSU. In 2023 as a freshman Holly saw action in just 3 games for the Tigers as he ran for just 110 yards on 11 attempts for an outstanding 10.0 yards per carry, with one TD, with no fumbles. As a receiver he made just one reception for 5 yards.
strengths: A balanced runner with good combination of wiggle and lower body strength. Possesses above average initial burst with natural explosion and start-and- stop ability to make guys miss - can turn on the jets in a flash. Holly shows good vision and good explosion upfield once Holly plants his foot. He has lateral movement skills and enough speed to threaten big plays. He’s excellent in the passing game, capable of flexing out as a receiver and creating mismatches against linebackers, and he’s a solid back in the screen game. He’s outstanding as a receiver, with good hands and the coordination to snatch away from his body and fluidly transition to a runner. An instinctive runner, he has a knack for setting up defenders and making people miss in space. He’s at his best in space and on the perimeter, where he shows the balance and acceleration, plus pure speed, to make a move and then take off. He has outstanding lateral agility; his jump cuts combined with stop-start ability make him difficult to get a hold of. He’s capable as a receiver, a weapon on swing passes and in the screen game. Holly has very good vision and a knack for picking out cut-back lanes and then breaking offchunks of yardage.
weaknesses: Has a lean frame and lacks ideal bulk or muscle for the position. Doesn't have great strength, lacking a power element to his game to break many tackles. Holly does most of his work on the perimeter - and ran a lot of fly sweeps Holly doesn’t have the ability as a run blocker or the violent run-after-catch ability.

bio: Marcus Davenport's school record for single-season tackles for loss at UTSA stood until Trey Moore broke it as a redshirt freshman in 2022 with 18, adding eight sacks and Freshman All-American recognition across 14 starts. Moore had earned District 27-6A Defensive Player of the Year honors at Smithson Valley High School in Spring Branch, Texas, signed with UTSA as a four-star recruit, and redshirted in 2021 before that breakout debut. Moore's 2023 campaign at UTSA was even more productive. He racked up a school-record 14 sacks, 17.5 tackles for loss, and an interception across 12 starts, taking home AAC Defensive Player of the Year. He then transferred to Texas ahead of the 2024 season and started all 16 games for the Longhorns, finishing with 6.5 sacks and 10.5 tackles for loss while contributing nine tackles and three sacks across three College Football Playoff games. In 2025, Moore's role shifted. He played 12 games with fewer starts and increased coverage responsibility while still logging three sacks and 33 tackles. He declared for the NFL Draft following the regular season and impressed at the combine, finishing with the seventh-best athleticism score among edge defenders. Moore was also named Texas' Spring 2025 Team Academic MVP.
strengths: Sets up tackles with a plan on every rush; sequences hand moves into counters naturally. Spin move is his money card, times it off the tackle's punch and stays balanced through contact. Does not quit on the rush; circles back and collapses the pocket when the initial move stalls. Moves around the formation pre-snap and forces protections to account for him. Flattens his rush arc well and takes a tight angle to the quarterback at the top of the loop. Looked capable in short zone drops during 2024, reading the quarterback's eyes and sitting in windows. Racked up 33 career sacks and 140 pressures across five seasons of significant snaps. Combine confirmed the athletic tools; showed excellent speed and short-area burst for his size.
weaknesses: Gets swallowed up by bigger tackles at the point of attack; frame limits his anchor. Does not consistently threaten the edge with pure speed; tackles can sit and wait for him. Effort against the run is uneven; you see him washed out of his gap too often on tape. Looked lost in coverage during 2025 when Texas asked him to drop on nearly every third down. Lunges and overruns ball carriers in the open field; missed tackles spiked in 2024. Pass-rush wins came easier at UTSA; SEC tackles gave him more problems with length and power. Hands buzz but do not jolt; needs to generate more pop to control the point of attack.
player_comparisons: NickHampton - Appalachian State (97%), CollinOliver - Oklahoma State (87%), BaronBrowning - Ohio State (85%)

bio: Trey Zuhn III, a 6'7", 315-pound offensive tackle from Texas A&M, has become a cornerstone of the Aggies' offensive line. Born and raised in Colorado, Zuhn was a standout two-sport athlete at Fossil Ridge High School, excelling in both football and track and field (shot put, discus). His high school performance earned him a 4-star rating from major recruiting services, with ESPN giving him an 80 rating, 247 Sports a 91.0 grade, and Rivals a 5.8 grade. Zuhn chose Texas A&M over other Power Five offers, redshirting in 2021 while appearing in four games. He earned the Freshman Academic Award that year, showcasing his commitment both on and off the field. In 2022, Zuhn secured the starting left tackle position, starting all 11 games he played. His performance earned him the offense's Attitude and Most Improved awards at the team's annual banquet. The 2023 season saw Zuhn start all 13 games at left tackle, solidifying his role as a key protector for the Aggies' quarterbacks. He was part of an offensive line that allowed only one sack in 53 pass attempts against Miami and helped the offense achieve a season-high 399 yards passing against ULM. Zuhn's contributions were crucial in games where the Aggies rushed for over 200 yards, including wins against Auburn (209), Arkansas (204), and Mississippi State (246). His pass protection skills were evident in nine games where the offensive line allowed two sacks or fewer. The season culminated with Zuhn receiving the Offensive Trenches Award.
strengths: Prototypical NFL frame with room to add good weight without sacrificing his athleticism or mobility. Impressive straight-line speed for a big man, clocking a 40 time that puts him in the upper echelon for his position. Possesses a wide kick-slide in pass pro, using his length effectively to stone edge rushers at the point of attack. Demonstrates good knee bend and hip flexibility, allowing him to redirect against counter moves and maintain leverage. Exhibits a mauler mentality in the run game, consistently looking to finish blocks and play through the whistle. Versatile prospect with experience at left tackle, potentially offering swing tackle value at the next level. High football IQ and strong work ethic- quickly made a starter and received multiple team awards. Dependable performer who answered the bell every game as a sophomore, a trait highly valued by NFL front offices.
weaknesses: Still developing consistency in his punch timing and hand placement, occasionally leaving his chest exposed to bull rushers. Can struggle to match up with speed-to-power converters, needing to improve his anchor and core strength. Sometimes gets overaggressive in pass pro, leaving himself susceptible to inside counter moves from savvy edge defenders. Lower body power needs refinement to generate more consistent movement in the run game, especially against stout defensive fronts. While showing good straight-line speed, his change of direction skills and lateral agility require improvement for elite zone blocking schemes.
player_comparisons: GarretGreenfield - South Dakota State (98%), ObinnaEze - TCU (84%), NickSaldiveri - Old Dominion (83%)

bio: Treydan Stukes walked on at Arizona out of Millennium Park High School in Litchfield Park, Arizona with zero recruiting stars to his name. Nobody was knocking down the door for a kid from a small program in Goodyear, despite the fact that he intercepted 11 passes in his high school career and won a state championship in the triple jump. His father Ray played college ball at Pacific University and suited up in the Arena Football League, so competitiveness ran in the family, but the recruiting services missed entirely on Stukes. He arrived in Tucson in 2020 with something to prove and never stopped proving it. His trajectory at Arizona reads like a steady climb with one painful detour. After logging 66 snaps as a true freshman in the COVID-shortened 2020 season, Stukes worked his way into five starts by 2021, tallying 31 tackles and a team-best seven pass breakups while helping the Wildcats hold opponents under 200 passing yards per game for the first time in over a decade. By 2022, he was a regular starter across 11 games with 42 tackles and an interception, playing primarily on the boundary. His breakout arrived in 2023: named a team captain as a redshirt junior, he started 12 of 13 games, made 55 tackles with nine pass breakups, earned Honorable Mention All-Pac-12, and was nominated for the Burlsworth Trophy, which honors college football's top player who began as a walk-on. He also helped anchor a defense that won seven straight to close the regular season, capping it with a win over No. 12 Oklahoma in the Alamo Bowl. Then came the setback. Four games into 2024, Stukes tore his ACL against Utah and missed the rest of the year. He took a medical redshirt and returned for a sixth season in 2025, missing the first two games while finishing his recovery. What he did in the 10 games that followed was remarkable. Operating primarily from the slot and occasionally rotating into safety responsibilities, Stukes posted 52 tackles, four interceptions, six pass breakups, and a sack while serving as the heartbeat of a Wildcats secondary that finished first nationally in pass efficiency defense and second in total interceptions. He picked off passes against ranked Iowa State, ranked BYU, Baylor, and Arizona State in the Territorial Cup. The honors piled up: AP Third Team All-American, Sporting News Second Team All-American, and First Team All-Big 12. Not bad for a kid nobody recruited.
strengths: Terrific size for a nickel at 6'2", 195 pounds, giving him a physical advantage over most slot receivers at the catch point and in contested situations. Moves with surprising fluidity for his height; his hips don't betray him the way they do with most tall corners, and he can flip and run without losing ground. Outstanding zone coverage processor who reads the quarterback's eyes, picks up route combinations early, and triggers downhill before the ball arrives. Delivers a forceful two-handed jam at the line that knocks receivers off their stems and disrupts timing in the passing game from the first snap. Ball production speaks for itself: seven career interceptions, 21 pass breakups, and 207 targets faced with a career completion rate against him well under the national average. Blows up screens with frightening recognition speed; he sees the blocking pattern develop and attacks through stalk blockers before the catch is even made. Long-striding top-end speed allows him to recover on vertical routes and contest fades even when he gets a late jump off the line or bites on underneath fakes. Versatile special teams contributor with reps across kick return, kick coverage, punt return, punt coverage, and field goal block units, giving him immediate NFL roster value on day one.
weaknesses: Will be 24 before his rookie season, and the torn ACL in 2024 means teams are buying a player under two years removed from significant knee surgery. Gets caught flat-footed and stacked vertically far too often; receivers with good stems can get on top of him because he commits his weight forward hunting the break. Lacks the short-area quickness and mirror ability to survive extended reps in press-man coverage against twitchy slot receivers who win with sharp lateral cuts. Takes poor pursuit angles in the run game, overcommitting to one gap and opening up wide lanes; his aggressiveness becomes a liability when he can't redirect. Struggles to consistently stack and shed blocks at the point of attack, lacking the play strength to anchor against bigger bodies in the run game.
player_comparisons: QuinyonMitchell - Toledo (90%), TreyAmos - Ole Miss (85%), T.J.Tampa - Iowa State (82%)

bio: Tristan Leigh, born April 28, 2003, is an offensive tackle from Clemson. Standing at 6'6" and weighing 315 pounds, Leigh enters the NFL Draft after three seasons with the Tigers. A highly decorated recruit out of Robinson High School in Virginia, Leigh was a five-star prospect across major recruiting services. He earned Virginia High School Player of the Year honors from the DC Touchdown Club and was named first-team All-DMV by NBC Sports Washington. Leigh's accolades extended to the national stage, as he was selected for both the All-American Bowl and Polynesian Bowl. His high school performance garnered attention from top programs, including Alabama, Oklahoma, LSU, and Ohio State. At Clemson, Leigh's career began in 2021 with a redshirt year, followed by limited action in 2022. His breakout came in 2023, starting 11 games and logging 756 offensive snaps. Throughout the season, Leigh displayed versatility by playing both tackle and guard positions. During his time at Clemson, Leigh contributed to several notable offensive performances. He played 91 snaps in a 466-yard effort against North Carolina, 84 snaps in the Gator Bowl win over Kentucky, and was part of the offensive line that helped running back Phil Mafah achieve a career-best 186 rushing yards against Notre Dame. Leigh enters the draft having played a total of 826 offensive snaps over 20 career games, with 11 starts to his name.le.
strengths: Prototypical NFL tackle size with a 6'6" frame and room for additional muscle mass, providing an excellent foundation for development at the next level. Demonstrates impressive athleticism for his size, allowing for effective pulling and second-level blocking in various offensive schemes. Exhibits a powerful initial punch in pass protection, disrupting defenders' rush plans and setting a solid anchor. Shows promising run-blocking potential with the ability to drive defenders off the line of scrimmage and create running lanes. Versatility to play both tackle and guard positions, as demonstrated during his time at Clemson, increasing his value to NFL teams. Displays a nasty streak and competitive fire, finishing blocks with aggression and playing through the whistle consistently. Strong pedigree as a former five-star recruit, indicating untapped potential and room for continued growth at the pro level.
weaknesses: Pass protection technique needs refinement, as he sometimes relies too heavily on his athleticism rather than sound fundamentals. Tendency to lean into blocks and play with high pad level, affecting his balance and leverage against more technically sound defenders. Hand placement can be inconsistent, particularly in pass sets, leading to vulnerability against counter moves from skilled edge rushers. Lower body strength and anchor need improvement to better withstand bull rushes and maintain pocket integrity at the NFL level. Limited experience as a full-time starter (11 games) may lead to a steeper learning curve against pro-level competition and schemes.

bio: Tyler Van Dyke emerged as a promising quarterback prospect at Miami, showcasing his potential with an impressive redshirt freshman season in 2021. The Connecticut native earned ACC Rookie of the Year and ACC Offensive Rookie of the Year honors, throwing for 2,931 yards, 25 touchdowns, and only six interceptions. Van Dyke's performance put him on the NFL Draft radar early, but his development stalled in subsequent seasons. In 2022, injuries limited Van Dyke to nine games, where he threw for 1,835 yards, 10 touchdowns, and five interceptions. His 2023 campaign showed improved efficiency but still fell short of his breakout year, with 2,703 yards, 19 touchdowns, and 12 interceptions in 11 games. Despite the statistical regression, Van Dyke maintained his status as a notable quarterback prospect. After three seasons at Miami, Van Dyke transferred to Wisconsin for his final year of eligibility. As he enters his age-24 season, Van Dyke aims to showcase the potential that once had him projected as an early-round selection.

bio: Tywone Malone from Bergen Catholic High School was rated a 4-star recruit by ESPN and handed a 4-star grade by 247 Sports. After high school, Malone joined Mississippi after being heavily recruited. In 2021 as a freshman Malone saw action in just one game and played a total of 9 snaps for the Rebels. He recorded only 2 tackles, no assists, while adding 3 stops. As a sophomore for the Rebels in 2022 Malone played in 9 games and took part in 78 snaps for the Rebels. He recorded only 6 tackles, 4 assists, while adding 4 stops. After season's end Malone transferred to Ohio State where as a junior for the Buckeyes in 2023 Malone played in 8 games and played 47 snaps for the Buckeyes. He recorded only 2 tackles, no assists while making 2 stops.
strengths: Malone can also win going speed-to-power, with the leg drive to push blockers back. He has violent hands and the long arms to ward off blockers. He plays with a good pad level and can win with power as a bull rusher. Plays with leverage at the point of attack whether inside or outside. Keeps eyes in the backfield when inside; moves linemen to either side with strong hands. He shows impressive burst and enough quickness to occasionally threaten the edge as a pass rusher. He has an excellent feel for working off blockers and knowing how and when to counter as the play goes on. He’s quick to react and locate the ball, and Malone shows enough speed to succeed in backside pursuit. Malone plays with excellent balance and a strong, flexible core, rarely ending up on the ground.
weaknesses: His pass-rush productivity might not translate, and he’s a bit of a tweener. He’s squat and short, and Malone will have a tough time warding off blockers as a pass rusher. Malone is on the short side, and he too often relies on his ability to overpower blockers rather than developing a repertoire of pass-rush moves.

bio: Buford, Georgia, has cranked out Division I talent for years, and VJ Payne added to that pipeline by helping lead the program to three state championships during his prep career under head coach Bryant Appling. He earned first-team all-region honors as a senior on a 14-1 squad, though the recruiting services saw him as a linebacker rather than a safety, with 247Sports ranking him No. 80 at the position in the 2022 class as a three-star. Payne picked Kansas State over what were likely bigger-name offers, betting on himself in the Big 12. That bet paid off almost immediately. Payne cracked the starting lineup as a true freshman in 2022, becoming the first Wildcat true freshman to start a season opener on defense in over three decades. He logged four starts that year, including the Big 12 Championship game against TCU and the Sugar Bowl against Alabama, where he posted 10 tackles against the Crimson Tide. By 2023, he had locked down a full-time starting role and recorded 57 tackles, his first career interception (a one-handed snag at Texas Tech that set up a K-State touchdown), and a fumble recovery across 694 defensive snaps. His junior season in 2024 brought 64 tackles, two interceptions, and a pair of game-defining plays: a pick in the end zone with five seconds left to seal a win at Tulane and another end zone interception at Colorado. As a senior captain in 2025, Payne put together 59 tackles, an interception, four passes defended, and two forced fumbles, earning his second consecutive All-Big 12 Honorable Mention nod. Over 52 career games (42 starts, including 41 consecutive to close his career), he amassed 207 tackles, 11.5 tackles for loss, four interceptions, and 13 pass deflections. Payne capped his college career with a strong Senior Bowl showing in January, drawing praise for his coverage skills, length, and football intelligence in Mobile.
strengths: At 6'3" with long arms and elite-level speed, his physical profile is exactly what NFL defensive coordinators are hunting for at the safety position right now. Alignment versatility is real, not just a talking point. He lined up in the box, over the slot, and at free safety throughout his career and looked comfortable in all three spots. Reads the quarterback's eyes from depth and breaks on the ball with impressive timing, covering ground quickly to close throwing windows before they open. When matched on tight ends in man coverage, his length becomes a serious problem. He blanketed Baylor's Michael Trigg in their matchups and held his own against the Big 12's best pass-catching tight ends. Willingness to mix it up at the catch point is notable. Uses well-timed hand swipes and his long wingspan to contest 50/50 balls and tilt them in the defense's favor. Four career interceptions undersell his ball-hawking instincts. The one-handed pick at Texas Tech and back-to-back end zone takedowns at Tulane and Colorado were not luck; he tracks the ball naturally. Showed steady improvement tackling over his career, bringing his missed tackle rate down from his rough junior year and finishing 2025 as a more reliable wrap-up defender. Proven leader and captain who interviewed with all 32 teams at the Senior Bowl and, by all accounts, crushed the whiteboard sessions with his schematic knowledge.
weaknesses: Transition quickness is a concern when receivers snap off sharp in-breaking routes. He can get his hips caught and gives up separation at the break point too often. Lacks the physicality and aggression you want from a run-support safety. He plays too polite against the run and does not attack downhill with the kind of violence that scares ball carriers. Gets stuck on blocks for too long, riding linemen and tight ends laterally instead of working to disengage and get to the football with urgency. Open-field tackling remains a work in progress. He is slow to gather his feet and takes poor angles that allow runners to slip past him or gain extra yards after contact. The 2024 tape was genuinely concerning across the board, with noticeable dips in his coverage and run defense grades that raised questions about consistency from year to year.
player_comparisons: MarkqueseBell - Florida A&M (80%), CharlieThomas - Georgia Tech (79%), CraigWoodson - California (78%)

bio: Olaivavega "Vega" Ioane never touched a football until his sophomore year at Graham-Kapowsin High School, and even that came with a catch: his parents, who prioritized academics over athletics, had no idea their son was suiting up on Friday nights until they noticed he kept missing dinner. Once that secret was out, football became a permanent fixture in the Ioane household. What followed was a meteoric rise through the Washington prep scene. Ioane anchored an offensive line at Graham-Kapowsin that helped the Eagles run roughshod over Class 4A competition, culminating in a perfect 15-0 senior campaign and a state championship in 2021. The team averaged a staggering 456.3 yards and 44.1 points per game that season, with Ioane earning MaxPreps Second Team All-American recognition. Despite the accolades, major recruiting services remained unconvinced, pegging him as a consensus three-star prospect across ESPN (79 rating), 247Sports (0.89 grade), and Rivals (5.6 grade). He initially committed to Washington, but when Jimmy Lake's staff was dismissed, Ioane pivoted to Penn State just sixty minutes before signing day in February 2022. The Nittany Lions proved to be the perfect landing spot. After redshirting his true freshman year in 2022, Ioane earned five starts as a redshirt freshman in 2023 and won the coaching staff's Offensive Player of the Week award against Iowa. He seized the starting left guard job full-time in 2024, starting all 16 games for a Penn State squad that reached the College Football Playoff semifinals, earning Second-Team All-Big Ten honors from both coaches and media. His 2025 campaign was nothing short of dominant: he surrendered just four pressures across 613 pass blocking snaps, allowing zero sacks and zero hits while earning First-Team All-Big Ten and First-Team All-American recognition. Across 43 career games and 2,304 offensive snaps, Ioane has been the heartbeat of an offensive line that routinely produced 100-yard rushers and explosive plays, including a Big Ten Championship Game performance against Oregon where Penn State gashed the nation's stingiest run defense for 292 yards at 8.3 yards per carry.
strengths: Pass protection is his calling card and it translates immediately to Sundays; zero sacks, zero hits, and only four hurries across 613 snaps in 2025 tells you everything about his ability to keep quarterbacks clean. Sets anchors like he's got roots growing into the turf; bull rushers who think they can walk this man back into the pocket are wasting their time because he simply does not give ground. His grip strength borders on unfair; once those hands latch onto a defender's chest plate, the rep is over because nobody is ripping free from that vice no matter how many counter moves they try. Reads stunts and games at an advanced level, passing off twists with the kind of poise you rarely see from college guards; watch the USC and Ohio State tape to see him handle complex pressure packages. Uses shoe-and-short sets effectively to establish inside-out leverage, hardening his inside shoulder and forcing rushers to work predictable paths that play into his power. Built like a fortress at 330 pounds with dense mass through his limbs and torso; his frame swallows up interior defenders and his contact balance keeps him upright through violent exchanges. Creates genuine displacement on base blocks and double teams, rolling his hips under his hands to generate the torque needed to move defensive tackles off their landmarks. Remarkably clean with the flags despite his physical style, drawing just three penalties across 16 starts in 2024 while maintaining that discipline through his All-American 2025 campaign.
weaknesses: Run blocking lacks the consistency of his pass protection; while he wins with power at the point of attack, his work on the move shows real limitations that cap his ceiling in outside zone schemes. Lateral quickness is below average and it shows up against twitchy three-techniques who can cross his face; when defenders win with speed and change of direction, Ioane looks heavy-footed chasing. Pad level pops up too quickly after initial contact, surrendering the leverage he needs to sustain drive blocks and giving technicians a window to work underneath his hands. Struggles as a move blocker when asked to reach the perimeter or climb to the second level with any urgency; his 5.35 forty time (42nd percentile) shows up on tape when linebackers beat him to the spot. Hand timing remains inconsistent, particularly in the run game where he's frequently a beat late getting his strike off and allows defenders to control initial contact and set the edge.
player_comparisons: TylerBooker - Alabama (87%), MarquisHayes - Oklahoma (82%), ChristianMahogany - Boston College (78%)

bio: Football heritage shaped Vincent Anthony Jr. long before he suited up at Jordan High School in Durham, North Carolina, where his father Vincent Sr. had played at nearby North Carolina Central in the late 1980s. A three-year letterman, Anthony compiled 127 tackles, 39 TFL, and 19 sacks over his final two prep seasons and participated in the 2022 All-American Bowl. He carried four-star ratings from 247Sports and Rivals while ESPN pegged him as a three-star and the state's 24th-ranked prospect. Anthony enrolled early at Duke in January 2022 and started 12 of 13 games as a true freshman, earning the team's rookie of the year award and Academic All-ACC honors. His sophomore year was limited to 11 appearances and four starts, but he still logged two sacks in a reserve role. The junior season brought a significant step forward: 25 tackles, 9.5 TFL, five sacks, and 13 quarterback pressures, which earned him the program's most improved player award. As a senior in 2025, Anthony posted 33 tackles, 13 TFL, and 7.5 sacks while earning Honorable Mention All-ACC. He was part of a Duke program that won 35 games over his four years, captured the school's first ACC title since 1962, and made four consecutive bowl games.
strengths: Long arms let him stab into tackles' chests before they can get set at the top of the set. Fluid bend around the corner, dips his inside shoulder and flattens to the quarterback naturally. Ghost move and euro step are legitimate weapons; watch the Illinois game for the full toolbox. Hands stay busy throughout the rush, swatting and ripping to keep blockers from locking on. First step wins him the edge early; tackles are already in recovery before contact arrives. Looked comfortable dropping into flat zones as a senior, carrying backs out of the backfield cleanly. Chases from the backside with effort you can't coach, finishing plays away from his gap. Three-sack game against ranked Illinois showed his rush plan working at its sharpest.
weaknesses: Gets walked back into the B-gap on run plays; tight ends and guards move him off his spot. Too many arm tackles and lunges in the open field, especially on outside zone reads. Light frame shows up on film when guards pull or fullbacks kick out on his edge. Inside counter moves look labored; hips are tight when he tries to redirect back inside. Jumped offsides and drew holding flags too often as a senior, undoing good rush reps. Offensive coordinators started running at his side late in games, and he couldn't hold up. Lacks the closing burst to finish on quarterbacks who move in the pocket.

bio: Walker Parks from Frederick Douglass High School was rated a 4-star recruit by ESPN and handed a 4-star grade by 247 Sports. Following high school, Parks opted to join Clemson. In 2020 as a freshman Parks saw action in 11 games and played a total of 201 snaps for the Tigers. He allowed just 3 QB hurries, no QB hits, and no sacks while playing the majority of his snaps at left tackle, and the rest at tight tackle. As a sophomore in 2021 he played in 13 games and a total of 862 snaps. He conceded a disappointing 21 QB hurries, one QB hit, and 4 sacks while playing at right tackle. In 2022 as a junior Parks saw action in 12 games and totaled 813 snaps. He gave up a disappointing 17 QB hurries, 4 QB hits, and one sack while playing at right guard. In 2023 as a senior Parks saw action in just 2 games and played a total of 122 snaps for the Tigers. He allowed 5 QB hurries, no QB hits, and no sacks while playing at right guard.
strengths: Shows good quickness and flexibility on reach blocks, and Parks has enough range to get out as a puller. Fires off the snap quickly, and he shows the flexibility to get out and seal on reach blocks. He’s at his best, however, in the phone booth. He’s a coordinated athlete who can win at the point of attack in the run game, and he can also move to consistently land blocks at the second level. Has the natural power to move defenders at the point of attack. Parks also has the agility and speed to get out in front and land blocks at the second level. He also shows a good understanding of angles when climbing to the second level. As a pass protector, he plays with good balance and holds up against the bull rush. Parks has strong hands and does a nice job gripping and latching on once he has his hands on the pass rusher. Dips shoulder and moves his feet to get outside leverage when needed. Strong and low coming out of his stance, effective in short-yardage situations.
weaknesses: A short-area blocker with limited range and doesn't consistently get to the second level. Will play undisciplined at times, especially in space. Lacks a finishing attitude. Often gets too high in his sets, sacrificing some of his power, and Parks doesn’t show great coordination between his upper and lower body when sliding back in pass protection. While he probably fits best as a right tackle, he has an outside chance at surviving on the left side for a team with a power-running attack.

bio: Bissainthe was a consensus four-star recruit out of Miami Central, where he helped the program win back-to-back Florida state championships in 2019 and 2020. He earned first-team All-Dade honors twice from the Miami Herald, posting 21 tackles for loss and seven sacks as a junior. He chose Miami over offers from Alabama, Oregon, Penn State and USC, among others. He saw action as a true freshman in 2022, working his way into a starting role by mid-season and finishing with 30 tackles across 11 games. His sophomore year brought 10 starts and 44 tackles with 6.5 tackles for loss and two sacks, highlighted by a three-TFL outing at Florida State. In April 2022, Bissainthe was involved in a serious car crash that left him with an ankle laceration requiring stitches, though he recovered in time for the season. His breakout came as a junior in 2024, when he started all 13 games and finished second on the team with 59 tackles, five tackles for loss, two sacks and an interception. As a senior on Miami's National Championship contending roster, he recorded 71 tackles, three TFL and one sack, tying a season high with eight stops in the title game. He earned Third-Team All-ACC honors and an East-West Shrine Bowl invite.
strengths: Reads blocking schemes quickly and gets aligned to the correct gap pre-snap. Active communicator who gets the defense lined up before the snap. Good hand placement when absorbing blocks at the point of attack. Reliable tackler between the tackles when he has a clean path. Carries good straight-line speed to chase plays toward the boundary. Tracks the quarterback's eyes in zone and covers ground in curl/flat areas. Uses his length to disrupt throwing lanes underneath.
weaknesses: Slow to trigger downhill, giving blockers time to reach him. Tight hips limit his ability to change direction and close in space. Struggles to disengage from blocks once engaged, often getting washed. Eyes drop to the blocker's chest, losing sight of the ball carrier. Missed tackle rate climbed significantly as a senior. Limited as a pass rusher, showing hesitation and marginal burst off the edge. Cannot handle man coverage responsibilities against tight ends or athletic backs.
player_comparisons: SteeleChambers - Ohio State (88%), DallasGant - Toledo (87%), MaristLiufau - Notre Dame (75%)

bio: Will Lee III starred at Kirkwood High School in St. Louis, posting 61 tackles, 14 for loss, and four sacks as a junior while earning first-team all-state honors. His first scholarship offer came after an Iowa coach spotted him at a basketball game while recruiting a teammate. No star rating, no traditional path. Lee started at Iowa Western Community College, redshirting in 2021 before becoming a starter on the Reivers' 2022 NJCAA National Championship team. He tallied 34 tackles and 10 passes defended, earning First Team All-America honors. Kansas State signed him, and he started nine of 11 games in 2023, finishing with 42 tackles, two interceptions, and All-Big 12 Honorable Mention recognition. He transferred to Texas A&M for 2024 and made an immediate impact: 42 tackles, two interceptions, and a national-best 10 pass breakups earned him second-team All-SEC honors. His 93-yard pick-six against Texas was one of the season's signature plays. In 2025 he started all 13 games with 50 tackles, eight pass breakups, and a sack while helping A&M's pass defense climb from 13th to 3rd in the SEC.
strengths: Those long arms change the math at the catch point. Receivers think they have a window and he closes it. Man coverage is where he earns his money. Stays in phase through breaks and trusts his feet one-on-one. Explosive lower half confirmed at the Combine. You see it on tape when he drives downhill on short throws. First step is quick for his size. Gets into the receiver's chest at the line before the route even starts. Hands at the line are active and disruptive. Jams receivers off their stems and throws timing off early. Competes for the football at the high point. Goes up and plays the ball rather than just the body. Ball production follows him everywhere. The picks and breakups come because he trusts his eyes and jumps routes.
weaknesses: Does not have the long speed to recover when a receiver gets on top of him on nine routes. Zone tape is noticeably worse than his man work. Eyes drift to the quarterback and he loses receivers underneath. Avoids coming downhill in the run game. Takes poor angles and gets washed on the perimeter too often. Missed tackles became a real issue in his final season. Finishing through contact was cleaner at Kansas State. Thin frame at 189 shows up when bigger receivers try to body him through press at the line of scrimmage. Grabs when he is late to turn and run. The hand goes to the jersey before he flips his hips.
player_comparisons: Kyu BluKelly - Stanford (82%), EliRicks - Alabama (82%), JoeyPorter Jr. - Penn State (80%)

bio: Sixteen interceptions across four varsity seasons at Southeast Polk High School made Xavier Nwankpa the most decorated prep player in Iowa's recent memory. He earned MaxPreps Iowa Player of the Year honors as a senior, helped his team to a state championship, and both 247Sports and Rivals rated him a five-star prospect, making him the highest-rated recruit Iowa had ever signed. Nwankpa contributed immediately as a true freshman in 2022, playing in all 13 games and earning a start in the Music City Bowl against Kentucky, where he returned an interception 52 yards for a touchdown. He stepped into the starting lineup full-time as a sophomore in 2023, recording 42 tackles, a sack, two tackles for loss, and an interception across 14 games. That season, Iowa's defense was among the best in the country, and Nwankpa was a key piece of it. His junior year in 2024 saw a position shift to strong safety and 10 starts in 13 games, with 41 tackles and a pass breakup. As a senior in 2025, he moved to free safety and started all 13 games, finishing with 76 tackles, an interception, and two forced fumbles while earning third-team All-Big Ten recognition. He also picked up the Big Ten Defensive Player of the Week award for his performance in a win over Penn State. In four years at Iowa, Nwankpa logged 42 games and 36 starts.
strengths: Well-built frame at 6-2 with long arms and room to add functional mass. Good straight-line speed and range to cover ground from a deep alignment. Alignment versatility is real; logged meaningful snaps at box, free safety, and slot. Willing and physical in run support; engages blockers and holds his ground. Reliable wrap-up tackler who runs his feet through contact on most reps. Effective working downhill against short and intermediate routes in the alley. Strong special teams contributor with the speed and effort to be a core four player.
weaknesses: Coverage production dropped sharply from his sophomore year through his senior season. Slow to diagnose route concepts; eyes get stuck on the quarterback too long. Tight hips limit his ability to stay with quicker receivers in man coverage. Pursuit angles in run support are inconsistent, giving up cutback lanes. Allowed too many completions and touchdowns relative to his interception totals. Play strength does not consistently match his frame and measurables.

bio: Zach Calzada from Lanier High School was rated a 4-star recruit by ESPN and handed a 3-star grade by 247 Sports. After high school, Calzada opted to join Texas A&M. In 2019 as a freshman Calzada saw action in just 3 games for the Aggies as he threw for only 133 yards on 24 attempts with a completion percent of 50 for an average of 5.5 yards per pass, with only 2 TDs, while taking 2 sacks, and QB rating of 77.3. In 2021 as a sophomore he played in 12 games as he passed for 2185 yards on 329 attempts with a completion percent of 55.9 for an average of 6.6 yards per pass, with 17 TDs, while also taking 17 sacks, and putting up QB rating of 82.2. After season's end Calzada transferred to Incarnate Word where as a junior in 2023 Calzada played a total of 8 games. He passed for 2597 yards on 287 attempts and put up a completion percentage of 65.5 for an average of 9.0 yards per attempt, with 19 TDs, and allowing 15 sacks, with QB rating on the year of 103.4. As a runner he logged 23 runs for a total of 141 yards at an average of 6.1 per run.
strengths: Good (not elite) arm strength and can easily make all the NFL throws with effortless deep passes. He’s an athlete who runs well and must be accounted for in the read-option game. He’s enough of a threat as a runner to make the read-option game effective. Prototypical height for the position with the frame to easily add another 15 pounds of muscle mass without a significant loss in quickness. Gets the ball out quickly, making ·splitsecond decisions. Good short to intermediate level accuracy, demonstrating the ball placement to allow receivers to run after the catch. A tall, lean pocket passer with room to add weight, Calzada is an aggressive, confident downfield thrower.
weaknesses: Lacks poise in the pocket and abandons his reads too quickly. Takes too many chances. Inconsistent downfield passer and puts too much air under his throws. Calzada often holds the ball too long. While Calzada keeps his eyes up while moving, his mechanics get increasingly sloppy once he’s pushed off his spot.

bio: Zach Rice, an offensive guard from the University of North Carolina, entered college football as a highly touted five-star recruit. Ranked as the top offensive tackle and player in Virginia by multiple recruiting services, Rice garnered national attention, including invitations to prestigious all-star games like the Under Armour All-American Bowl and the Polynesian Bowl. At Liberty Christian Academy, Rice's dominance was evident. He anchored the offensive line for four years, leading his team to a state championship appearance in his senior season. His high school accolades include being named Virginia's Gatorade Player of the Year and MaxPreps Virginia Player of the Year. Rice's senior year was particularly impressive, as he tallied an astounding 219 pancake blocks and didn't allow a single sack in his final two seasons. Rice's college career at UNC has been a gradual progression. After redshirting his true freshman year in 2022, where he appeared in two games for a total of 16 snaps at right guard, he saw increased playing time in his redshirt freshman season in 2023. He appeared in 12 games, primarily on special teams, while also providing depth along the offensive line.
strengths: Prototype size and length for the position with a frame that can easily add functional mass. Rare movement skills for a big man, displaying excellent foot quickness and lateral agility. Shows good anchor ability in pass pro, maintaining a solid base against power rushers. High football IQ with the ability to recognize and pick up stunts and blitzes effectively. Positional versatility to kick out to tackle in a pinch, enhancing his value as a swing lineman. Blue-chip pedigree with a family tree rooted in high-level football, suggesting natural feel for the game. Natural leader who commanded respect in high school, indicating potential to be a locker room presence. Raw talent with sky-high ceiling, barely scratching the surface of his potential.
weaknesses: Extremely limited game tape at the collegiate level, leaving many questions about his true capabilities. Lacks pop and drive in the run game, often failing to generate consistent movement at the point of attack. Still adapting to inside techniques after transitioning from high school tackle to college guard. Minimal experience against high-level competition, raising concerns about his ability to handle NFL-caliber defenders. Draft stock heavily reliant on projection rather than proven on-field production.
player_comparisons: NickFord - Utah (89%), EliCox - Kentucky (76%), WillPutnam - Clemson (75%)

bio: Pro Football Hall of Famer Cliff Branch's nephew, Zachariah Branch starred at Bishop Gorman High School in Las Vegas as a two-sport standout. He ran a 10.33-second 100 meters as a sophomore and caught 48 passes for 1,094 yards and 14 touchdowns as a junior, earning MaxPreps Nevada Player of the Year. As a senior he posted 45 receptions, 753 yards, and 11 scores while helping Bishop Gorman capture the 5A state title. He left as a consensus five-star and the No. 1 receiver in the 2023 class. Branch signed with USC but made more noise on special teams than as a receiver. He won the Jet Award as the nation's top return man as a freshman, posting return touchdowns on both punts and kicks, though his receiving totals were modest at 31 catches for 320 yards. A bump to 47 catches and 503 yards as a sophomore still fell short of his billing, and he entered the transfer portal before landing at Georgia. The move paid off. Branch set a Georgia single-season record with 81 receptions, totaling 811 yards and six touchdowns while leading the SEC in catches. He earned All-SEC honors at receiver and return specialist before declaring for the 2026 Draft.
strengths: Top-end speed is confirmed; his acceleration from a standstill to full stride is rare at any position. YAC ability is the calling card, with roughly 80% of career receiving yards coming after the catch. Processes angles and finds creases through traffic with a running back's vision once the ball arrives. Tougher than the frame suggests; runs through arm tackles and keeps his legs moving on contact. Attacks leverage on route stems with savvy, snapping off breaks without losing much speed at the top. Reliable hands and real focus to pluck throws through contact and adjust to off-target balls. Deployment versatility is a plus: slot, bunch, stacks, jet motions, and backfield handoff reps all look natural. Two career return touchdowns with patience and feel for setting up blocks before hitting the crease.
weaknesses: Undersized frame with short arms limits his catch radius and ability to win at contested catch points. College route tree leaned heavily on manufactured touches; 49 of 81 catches in 2025 came at or behind the line. Routes can look rushed and rounded, especially at the top of breaks where physical corners can disrupt timing. Has a habit of unnecessarily jumping to catch, leaving him vulnerable to hits and off-balance after the grab. Blocking effort and ability are both lacking; functional strength to sustain in the run game is not there. Bad body language shows up on tape when the ball is not headed his way, and NFL coaches will notice.
player_comparisons: MalikWashington - Virginia (84%), ElijahMoore - Ole Miss (84%), AiniasSmith - Texas A&M (83%)
name: Zachariah Branch
scouting_report:
Background:
Pro Football Hall of Famer Cliff Branch's nephew, Zachariah Branch starred at Bishop Gorman High School in Las Vegas as a two-sport standout. He ran a 10.33-second 100 meters as a sophomore and caught 48 passes for 1,094 yards and 14 touchdowns as a junior, earning MaxPreps Nevada Player of the Year. As a senior he posted 45 receptions, 753 yards, and 11 scores while helping Bishop Gorman capture the 5A state title. He left as a consensus five-star and the No. 1 receiver in the 2023 class. Branch signed with USC but made more noise on special teams than as a receiver. He won the Jet Award as the nation's top return man as a freshman, posting return touchdowns on both punts and kicks, though his receiving totals were modest at 31 catches for 320 yards. A bump to 47 catches and 503 yards as a sophomore still fell short of his billing, and he entered the transfer portal before landing at Georgia. The move paid off. Branch set a Georgia single-season record with 81 receptions, totaling 811 yards and six touchdowns while leading the SEC in catches. He earned All-SEC honors at receiver and return specialist before declaring for the 2026 Draft.

Strengths:
Top-end speed is confirmed; his acceleration from a standstill to full stride is rare at any position. YAC ability is the calling card, with roughly 80% of career receiving yards coming after the catch. Processes angles and finds creases through traffic with a running back's vision once the ball arrives. Tougher than the frame suggests; runs through arm tackles and keeps his legs moving on contact. Attacks leverage on route stems with savvy, snapping off breaks without losing much speed at the top. Reliable hands and real focus to pluck throws through contact and adjust to off-target balls. Deployment versatility is a plus: slot, bunch, stacks, jet motions, and backfield handoff reps all look natural. Two career return touchdowns with patience and feel for setting up blocks before hitting the crease.

Weaknesses:
Undersized frame with short arms limits his catch radius and ability to win at contested catch points. College route tree leaned heavily on manufactured touches; 49 of 81 catches in 2025 came at or behind the line. Routes can look rushed and rounded, especially at the top of breaks where physical corners can disrupt timing. Has a habit of unnecessarily jumping to catch, leaving him vulnerable to hits and off-balance after the grab. Blocking effort and ability are both lacking; functional strength to sustain in the run game is not there. Bad body language shows up on tape when the ball is not headed his way, and NFL coaches will notice.

Trait Scores (NFLDraftBuzz):
  Defense Faced Rating: 76%
  QB Rating When targeted: 109.7
  Hands: 91%
  Short Receiving: 86%
  Intermediate Routes: 90%
  Deep Threat: 90%
  Blocking: 50%

NFL Player Comparisons: MalikWashington - Virginia (84%), ElijahMoore - Ole Miss (84%), AiniasSmith - Texas A&M (83%)

bio: Every SEC program knew about Zxavian Harris by the time he was tearing through 6A competition at Canton High School in Mississippi, where he racked up 62 tackles and 8.5 sacks as a junior and added a blocked field goal and punt for good measure. He earned MHSAA 6A Defensive Player of the Year honors as a senior, and the recruiting services confirmed what the tape already showed: a consensus four-star ranked No. 2 in Mississippi by 247Sports (0.94 grade) and the No. 18 defensive lineman nationally. Harris chose Ole Miss over scholarship offers from Alabama, Georgia, LSU, Auburn, and Arkansas. He arrived in Oxford in 2022 ready to compete, though nobody could have predicted just how winding his path to the starting lineup would be. Harris saw limited action as a true freshman, logging just 90 defensive snaps across 13 appearances. His sophomore year told a different story. He started seven games, put up 30 tackles and 3.5 TFLs, and became the most feared kick-blocker in the entire SEC, swatting three kicks that season alone, including a game-winning field goal block against Texas A&M that earned him SEC Co-Defensive Lineman of the Week. He capped 2023 with another blocked field goal in the Peach Bowl victory over Penn State, bringing his career total to five at that point. As a junior in 2024, the arrival of Texas A&M transfer Walter Nolen and the presence of JJ Pegues pushed Harris back into a reserve role, but he still contributed 32 tackles, 4.5 TFLs, and 1.5 sacks off the bench across all 13 games. His best single-game performance came against No. 2 Georgia, where he posted the highest defensive grade on the team and forced a fumble. The 2025 season brought everything full circle. With Nolen and Pegues off to the NFL, Harris reclaimed his starting spot and delivered a career year: 56 tackles, nine TFLs, three sacks, an interception, and three passes defended across a full workload. When Lane Kiffin left for LSU on the eve of the College Football Playoff, Harris became one of the loudest voices in the locker room, calling Kiffin's departure "a slap in the face" and rallying behind interim head coach Pete Golding. Ole Miss rode that energy all the way to a Sugar Bowl victory over Georgia and a semifinal appearance. Harris earned Preseason All-SEC recognition from coaches and media heading into the season and received a Senior Bowl Top-300 invitation. He finishes his Ole Miss career with 50 games played, 1,400-plus defensive snaps, six blocked kicks, and a reputation as one of the biggest human beings to ever line up in the SEC.
strengths: At 6'7" with arms that seem to go on forever, Harris owns a frame that offensive linemen simply cannot get around or work over the top of easily. His run defense took a real leap in his final two seasons; when he gets his hands placed right, he can stack blockers and hold gaps with surprising authority. Versatile enough to line up at 0-tech, 3-tech, 4i, or 5-tech, giving a defensive coordinator multiple lineup options from one roster spot on game day. Six career blocked kicks is absurd production, and that unique combination of length and timing on special teams translates to immediate NFL value the moment he's active. His swim move is a legitimate weapon, and when he times it right off the snap, guards have no answer for those long arms clearing the block. Late-arriving power is real on film. He builds momentum going downhill and generates knockback that can collapse the pocket or blow up pulling schemes. Showed clear year-over-year growth across his career, cutting his missed tackle rate nearly in half from junior to senior year while nearly doubling his snap count. Long lateral stride allows him to chase down seal blocks and reach plays from the backside, covering ground you would not expect from a man his size.
weaknesses: Bottom-of-the-barrel timed speed and a noticeable lack of first-step quickness off the snap leave him vulnerable to reach blocks and scoop combos at the line. Plays too tall too often, and that high pad level invites double teams to walk him off the ball; his waist bending is a consistent issue on film. Pass rush toolkit is shallow beyond the swim move and occasional bull rush. He lacks counters, and his hands are frequently late arriving to the chest plate. Tackling technique remains a work in progress. He struggles to break down in space and too often arrives off-balance, leading to arm tackles and whiffs near the line of scrimmage. Two arrests during his college career, a DUI with felony fleeing in 2023 and a domestic violence charge with obstructing arrest in 2024, will demand thorough vetting and could remove him from multiple draft boards entirely.
player_comparisons: BrodricMartin - Western Kentucky (70%), CamJackson - Florida (62%), JerrodClark - Coastal Carolina (62%)
